> 6. **Inglis derivation shortcuts:** The KTU examiner will not award full marks for merely writing $K_t = 1 + 2a/b$ without showing the conformal map and stress-function steps. The 7-mark sub-part on Inglis demands the full derivation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- [ ] **$K_t = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_{nom}$** — Definition, depends ONLY on geometry.
- [ ] **$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$** — Fatigue SCF; always $\le K_t$ for ductile materials.
- [ ] **Inglis equation:** $K_t = 1 + 2a/b$ for an elliptical hole.
- [ ] **Circular hole:** $K_t = 3$ (memorise — most-tested single result).
- [ ] **Neuber's equation:** $q = 1/(1 + \sqrt{a/r})$ — apply with $a$ in **same units** as $r$.
- [ ] **$q \to 1$** for brittle materials (use $K_t$ directly).
- [ ] **$q \to 0$** for very ductile, fine-grained materials (use $K_f \to 1$).
- [ ] **Larger fillet radius $r$** → lower $K_t$ → higher fatigue life.
- [ ] **Larger diameter ratio $D/d$** → higher $K_t$ → potential failure site.
- [ ] **Peak stress location:** at the tip of the major axis (perpendicular to load) for an elliptical hole.
- [ ] **Common exam values:** $K_t$ for stepped shafts: $1.4$ to $2.5$; for keyways: $\approx 2.0$; for threads: $1.5$ (rolled) to $3.0$ (sharp-cut).
- [ ] **Design remedies:** increase $r$, use relief grooves, use multiple radii, use generous transitions, place holes away from high-stress zones, prefer rolled threads over cut threads.
- [ ] **Neuber's constant $a$ values to remember:** Mild steel $0.25 \text{ mm}$, Med. carbon steel $0.5 \text{ mm}$, High-strength steel $1.0 \text{ mm}$, Al alloy $0.62 \text{ mm}$, Cast iron $> 4 \text{ mm}$.
- [ ] **Two distinct $K_t$ definitions exist** — Inglis (elliptical hole) and chart-based (Peterson/Pilkey for shafts). Don't mix them.
- [ ] **Always compute the peak stress** $\sigma_{max} = K_f \times \sigma_{nom}$ before applying any failure criterion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theories of Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Theories of Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of machine design and automotive component engineering, a **Theory of Failure** (also called a **Strength Theory** or **Failure Criterion**) is a mathematically formulated hypothesis that predicts the conditions under which a material subjected to a complex multi-axial state of stress will fail (yield or fracture) when compared to its known behavior under a simple uniaxial tensile test.

For an automotive component (such as a crankshaft, connecting rod, axle, or piston pin), the actual stress state at any critical point is generally **biaxial** or **triaxial** (multi-dimensional), unlike the simple uniaxial tension test used to determine material properties in the laboratory. The theories of failure bridge this gap by allowing the designer to equate a complex stress state to an equivalent simple uniaxial stress, which can then be compared directly against the allowable stress obtained from standard material tests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The theories of failure provide the necessary analytical foundation for the safe design of automotive components under combined loading (e.g., a vehicle stub axle subjected to bending + torsion + axial load simultaneously). Mastery of these theories is **mandatory** under Course Outcome CO1 of PCMUT601.

## 1.2 Physical Meaning & Conceptual Analogy

To understand the *necessity* of failure theories, imagine the following real-world scenario:

**Analogy — The Inflated Rubber Balloon Inside a Steel Cage:**
- Consider a pressurized gas cylinder (like a CNG tank in a CNG-powered car). The inner wall experiences **hoop stress** (tangential, trying to split the cylinder) and **longitudinal stress** (axial, trying to stretch it lengthwise) — a **biaxial stress** state.
- Now, the material property you measured in the lab is the **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** obtained from a simple rod pulled in one direction only.
- **Question:** Can you directly compare the biaxial stresses in the cylinder wall with the UTS from the simple test? **NO.**
- **Answer:** The failure theories act as a **translator** — they convert the complex biaxial stresses into an *equivalent uniaxial stress* ($\sigma_{eq}$), which can then be safely compared with the yield strength ($\sigma_y$) or ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_u$) from the simple tensile test.

**Plain-English Interpretation for a First-Time Reader:**
> Every material "fails" in its own characteristic way — brittle materials crack suddenly (like cast iron), while ductile materials deform permanently before breaking (like mild steel). A failure theory captures this "characteristic way" of failure in a mathematical equation, so that an engineer designing an automotive part can confidently answer: *"Given the actual complex loading, will this part yield? Will it fracture? Will it deform excessively?"*

## 1.3 Standard Material Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> The following material properties are repeatedly referenced in KTU examination problems on theories of failure. Always commit them to memory.

| Property | Symbol | Unit | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Typical Value (Cast Iron) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength in Tension | $\sigma_{yt}$ | **MPa** (or **N/mm²**) | **250** | — (not defined) |
| Yield Strength in Compression | $\sigma_{yc}$ | **MPa** | **250** | — |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_u$ or $S_{ut}$ | **MPa** | **410** | **200** |
| Ultimate Compressive Strength | $\sigma_{uc}$ or $S_{uc}$ | **MPa** | — | **700** |
| Factor of Safety | $F.O.S.$ or $n$ | dimensionless | 2 – 4 | 4 – 8 |

## 1.4 Classification of Theories of Failure

The classical theories are broadly categorized based on the **type of material** they apply to:

**A. Theories for Brittle Materials** (e.g., Cast Iron, Concrete, Ceramics):
1. **Maximum Principal Stress Theory** (Rankine's Theory)
2. **Maximum Principal Strain Theory** (Saint-Venant's Theory)
3. **Mohr's Theory** (Modified Mohr's Theory)

**B. Theories for Ductile Materials** (e.g., Mild Steel, Aluminium Alloys):
1. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** (Tresca / Guest's Theory)
2. **Maximum Distortion Energy Theory** (von Mises / Hencky Theory)
3. **Maximum Strain Energy Theory** (Haigh's Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Selecting the correct theory for the given material is the **single most critical first step**. Wrong selection = **wrong answer = 0 marks**, even if calculations are perfect.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Setup of a Theory of Failure

Every classical theory of failure can be expressed in the canonical form:

$$\sigma_{eq} \leq \frac{\sigma_{limit}}{F.O.S.} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sigma_{eq} \leq \frac{\sigma_{limit}}{n}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{eq}$ = **Equivalent Stress** (calculated from the actual stress state $\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$, $\sigma_3$)
- $\sigma_{limit}$ = Material property from uniaxial test (usually $\sigma_y$ or $\sigma_u$)
- $n$ = **Factor of Safety**

The goal of each theory is to prescribe a unique mathematical formula for $\sigma_{eq}$.

## 2.2 The Six Theories — Systematic Analysis

### **Theory 1: Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine's Theory) — 1858**

- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **maximum principal stress** in a complex stress state equals the strength in a simple tension test.
- **Assumption:** Material is **brittle**, homogeneous, and isotropic.
- **Equivalent Stress Formula:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 \quad (\text{if } \sigma_1 > \vert \sigma_3 \vert)$$
$$\sigma_{eq} = -\sigma_3 \quad (\text{if } \vert \sigma_3 \vert > \sigma_1)$$
- **Failure Criterion:**
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_u}{n} \quad \text{(tensile)} \qquad \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{n} \quad \text{(compressive)}$$
- **Real-World Utility:** Used in design of **cast iron flywheels, concrete columns, ceramic components**.
- **Limitation:** Ignores the effect of the *other* principal stresses. Inconsistent with experimental observations in biaxial tension-tension.

### **Theory 2: Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant's Theory) — 1857**

- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **maximum principal strain** reaches the strain at failure in a simple tension test.
- **Equivalent Stress Formula:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$$
- **Failure Criterion:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$
- **Real-World Utility:** Used for brittle materials where strain-controlled fracture is dominant.
- **Limitation:** Predicts *higher* strength than actual for biaxial tension cases (unsafe design).

### **Theory 3: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca's Theory) — 1864**

- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **maximum shear stress** in the complex state equals the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tension test.
- **Equivalent Stress Formula:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \quad \text{(taking }\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3\text{)}$$
- **Failure Criterion:**
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$
- **Real-World Utility:** Used for **ductile materials** in design of **pressure vessels, shafts under combined bending and torsion, automotive axles**.
- **Limitation:** Ignores the influence of the intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$. Less accurate than von Mises.

### **Theory 4: Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises / Hencky Theory) — 1913**

- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **distortion strain energy** (energy associated with shape change only, excluding volumetric change) reaches the distortion strain energy at yield in a simple tension test.
- **Equivalent Stress Formula:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$
- **Failure Criterion:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$
- **Real-World Utility:** **Most widely used theory in modern automotive design.** Used for design of **crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, suspension components**. Gives the most accurate results for ductile materials.
- **Limitation:** More complex mathematically; gives a slightly more permissive (less conservative) result than Tresca.

### **Theory 5: Maximum Strain Energy Theory (Haigh's Theory) — 1921**

- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **total strain energy** in the complex state equals the strain energy at failure in a simple tension test.
- **Equivalent Stress Formula:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)}$$
- **Failure Criterion:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$
- **Real-World Utility:** Generally not used in industrial practice for ductile materials; historically used for brittle materials but largely superseded by Mohr's theory.

### **Theory 6: Mohr's Theory (Modified Mohr's Theory)**

- **Hypothesis:** Extends Rankine's theory by considering different strengths in tension and compression; failure is governed by shear on a specific plane.
- **Equivalent Stress Formula:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\sigma_u}\sigma_3$$
- **Failure Criterion:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{\sigma_u}{n}$$
- **Real-World Utility:** The most accurate theory for **brittle materials** with different tensile and compressive strengths (e.g., cast iron, concrete).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FOR EXAM:** The following table contains every formula you will need to solve KTU university exam problems on theories of failure. Memorize the formula column.

| Theory # | Theory Name | Equivalent Stress $\sigma_{eq}$ | Suitable Material | Governing Material Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max Principal Stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_1$ (or $-\sigma_3$) | Brittle | $\sigma_u$ or $\sigma_{uc}$ |
| 2 | Max Principal Strain (Saint-Venant) | $\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2+\sigma_3)$ | Brittle | $\sigma_y$ or $\sigma_u$ |
| 3 | Max Shear Stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | Ductile | $\sigma_y$ |
| 4 | Max Distortion Energy (von Mises) | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]}$ | Ductile | $\sigma_y$ |
| 5 | Max Strain Energy (Haigh) | $\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)}$ | Brittle/Ductile | $\sigma_y$ or $\sigma_u$ |
| 6 | Mohr's Theory | $\sigma_1 - \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\sigma_u}\sigma_3$ | Brittle | $\sigma_u$ or $\sigma_{uc}$ |

## 2.4 Comparison Between Theories (Engineering Insight)

**Ordering of Equivalent Stress for a Given Stress State (with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$):**

$$\sigma_{eq}(\text{Distortion Energy}) \geq \sigma_{eq}(\text{Max Shear}) \geq \sigma_{eq}(\text{Max Principal Strain})$$

**Practical Implication:** The Distortion Energy theory gives the **highest** equivalent stress (most accurate prediction for ductile materials); the Tresca theory is the **most conservative** (safe design choice). Von Mises gives ~**15% higher** allowable stress than Tresca in pure torsion cases.

## 2.5 Special Case: 2-D / Biaxial Stress (Most Common in KTU Problems)

For an automotive component with $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$ on its critical plane, and assuming **plane stress** ($\sigma_z = \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zx} = 0$):

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

The principal stresses are computed first, then substituted into the chosen theory's formula for $\sigma_{eq}$.

**For Maximum Shear Stress Theory (2-D):**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$$

**For Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (2-D):**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)

### Starting Point: Simple Tension Test
Consider a cylindrical specimen under uniaxial tensile stress $\sigma_y$ (yield stress) at the point of yielding. The principal stresses in this state are:
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_y, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

The maximum shear stress in a uniaxial tension test is given by:
$$\tau_{max,\text{tension}} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y - 0}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

### Complex Stress State
For a general 3-D stress state with principal stresses $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$, the maximum shear stress occurs on a plane oriented at $45°$ to the $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ axes:
$$\tau_{max,\text{complex}} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

### Equating the Two (Failure Hypothesis)
According to Tresca's hypothesis, failure occurs when:
$$\tau_{max,\text{complex}} = \tau_{max,\text{tension}}$$

$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y}$$

### Generalized Form (with Factor of Safety):
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$$

**Why $\sigma_2$ is excluded:** The Tresca criterion only looks at the *extremes* of the principal stress triad. The intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$ has no effect on the maximum shear plane, which is oriented between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)

### Step 1: Total Strain Energy in 3-D
The total strain energy per unit volume stored in an elastic body under principal stresses $\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$, $\sigma_3$ is:
$$U_{total} = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- The term $\sigma_i^2$ represents energy stored along each principal axis.
- The cross terms $2\nu(\sigma_i\sigma_j)$ account for **Poisson's effect** — lateral contraction caused by axial extension.

### Step 2: Dilatation Energy (Volumetric Change)
The hydrostatic (mean) stress that causes only volume change without shape distortion is:
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$$

The volumetric strain energy (energy stored due to pure dilation, no shape change) is:
$$U_{vol} = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2 = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{2E}\sigma_m^2$$

### Step 3: Distortion Energy (Shape Change Only)
By definition of distortion energy:
$$U_{dist} = U_{total} - U_{vol}$$

After algebraic simplification (full expansion given below):

$$U_{dist} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$$

**Detailed Algebraic Expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{dist} &= U_{total} - U_{vol} \\
&= \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right] \\
&\quad - \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{6E}\left[3(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2) - 6\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \right. \\
&\quad \left. - (1 - 2\nu)(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 + 2\sigma_1\sigma_2 + 2\sigma_2\sigma_3 + 2\sigma_3\sigma_1)\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{6E}\left[2(1 + \nu)(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2) - 2(1 + \nu)(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right] \\
&= \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}\left[(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2) - (\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

The identity $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1)$ gives the **final compact form**:

$$U_{dist} = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$$

### Step 4: Apply the Failure Hypothesis
For a uniaxial tension test at yield ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$):
$$U_{dist,\text{tension}} = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[\sigma_y^2 + \sigma_y^2\right] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2$$

According to von Mises, failure occurs when:
$$U_{dist,\text{complex}} = U_{dist,\text{tension}}$$

$$\frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}} = \sigma_y}$$

### Step 5: Generalized Design Equation
$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

### Problem Statement (Biaxial Stress with Torsion):
A steel shaft of a vehicle gearbox is subjected to:
- Bending stress: $\sigma_x = 80$ **MPa** (tensile)
- Axial stress: $\sigma_y = -40$ **MPa** (compressive)
- Torsional shear stress: $\tau_{xy} = 60$ **MPa**

Given: Yield strength $\sigma_y = 320$ **MPa**, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$, Factor of Safety $n = 2.5$.
Determine the **Factor of Safety** according to the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory**.

### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Compute Principal Stresses.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
&= \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + (60)^2} \\
&= \frac{40}{2} \pm \sqrt{(60)^2 + (60)^2} \\
&= 20 \pm \sqrt{3600 + 3600} \\
&= 20 \pm \sqrt{7200} \\
&= 20 \pm 84.853 \\
\sigma_1 &= 20 + 84.853 = 104.853 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2 &= 20 - 84.853 = -64.853 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_3 &= 0 \quad \text{(plane stress condition)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Order Principal Stresses.

$$\sigma_1 = 104.853 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = -64.853 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Maximum Shear Stress Theory.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{eq} &= \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \\
&= 104.853 - (-64.853) \\
&= 169.706 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute Factor of Safety.

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq}} \\
&= \frac{320}{169.706} \\
&= 1.886
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $n \approx 1.89$. Since the required Factor of Safety is 2.5, the shaft is **UNSAFE**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table — Same Problem, Different Theories

| Theory | Equivalent Stress $\sigma_{eq}$ | Factor of Safety $n = \sigma_y / \sigma_{eq}$ | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Shear Stress (Tresca) | $169.71$ **MPa** | $1.89$ | UNSAFE |
| Max Distortion Energy (von Mises) | $157.46$ **MPa** | $2.03$ | UNSAFE |
| Max Principal Stress (Rankine) | $104.85$ **MPa** | $3.05$ | SAFE (overly optimistic for ductile material) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the Tresca theory gives the **most conservative (smallest) factor of safety**. Von Mises is slightly less conservative. Rankine (suitable for brittle materials) gives an unrealistically high safety factor — which is why applying the wrong theory to the wrong material leads to **dangerously unsafe designs**.

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Selection of Appropriate Failure Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B -- "Ductile: Mild Steel, Aluminium, Brass" --> D{"Which Ductile Theory?"}
    C --> C1["Rankine: sigma_eq = sigma1"]
    C --> C2["Saint-Venant: sigma_eq = sigma1 - nu sigma2 + sigma3"]
    C --> C3["Mohr: sigma_eq = sigma1 - sigma_uc/sigma_u times sigma3"]
    D -- "Conservative Design" --> E["Tresca: sigma_eq = sigma1 - sigma3"]
    D -- "Accurate / Modern Practice" --> F["Von Mises: Distortion Energy"]
    D -- "Historical / Rare" --> G["Haigh: Max Strain Energy"]

    C3 --> H
    H --> I{"n greater than n_required?"}

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** State the **Maximum Principal Stress Theory** of failure. For what type of material is it applicable?

**Model Answer:**
The Maximum Principal Stress Theory, proposed by **W.J.M. Rankine (1858)**, states that failure of a material occurs when the **maximum principal stress** developed in a complex loading state equals the ultimate strength of the material as obtained from a simple uniaxial tensile test.

The design equation is given by:

$$\sigma_1 \leq \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{n} \quad \text{(for tensile principal stress)}$$

$$\vert \sigma_3 \vert \leq \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{n} \quad \text{(for compressive principal stress)}$$

It is applicable to **brittle materials** such as cast iron, concrete, and ceramics, which fail predominantly by fracture rather than yielding.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **Tresca Theory** and **Von Mises Theory** of failure for ductile materials.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Tresca Theory | Von Mises Theory |
|---|---|---|
| Also known as | Maximum Shear Stress Theory | Maximum Distortion Energy Theory |
| Equivalent Stress | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]}$ |
| Effect of $\sigma_2$ | Ignored | Considered |
| Yield locus shape | Hexagonal | Elliptical |
| Conservativeness | More conservative | Less conservative (~15% higher stress) |
| Accuracy | Approximate | More accurate |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Choice Pattern]`

**Q (a)** State and explain the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** and **Maximum Distortion Energy Theory** of failure for ductile materials. Derive their respective equivalent stress expressions. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A mild steel shaft of diameter **50 mm** transmits a torque of **2.5 kN·m** while simultaneously subjected to a bending moment of **3 kN·m** and an axial tensile load of **50 kN**. Determine the factor of safety using **(i)** Maximum Shear Stress Theory and **(ii)** Maximum Distortion Energy Theory. Given: $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca, 1864):**
- **Statement:** Failure occurs when the maximum shear stress in a complex stress state equals the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tension test.
- **Derivation:** In uniaxial tension at yield, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$. The maximum shear stress is:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$
- In complex 3-D state:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \quad (\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3)$$
- Equating:
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$$

**Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises, 1913):**
- **Statement:** Failure occurs when the distortion strain energy per unit volume in the complex stress state equals the distortion strain energy at yield in simple tension.
- **Distortion Energy Formula:**
$$U_{dist} = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]$$
- At yield in simple tension ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, others zero):
$$U_{dist,y} = \frac{(1+\nu)\sigma_y^2}{3E}$$
- Equating and simplifying:
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 50$ mm, Area $A = \frac{\pi}{4}(50)^2 = 1963.5$ mm²
- Torque $T = 2.5$ kN·m $= 2.5 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- Bending moment $M = 3$ kN·m $= 3 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- Axial load $P = 50$ kN $= 50 \times 10^3$ N
- $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa

**Step 1: Calculate Stresses**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Axial (tensile)} \quad \sigma_x &= \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{1963.5} = 25.46 \text{ MPa} \\
\text{Bending (tensile)} \quad \sigma_b &= \frac{32M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 3 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (50)^3} = 244.46 \text{ MPa} \\
\text{Total normal stress} \quad \sigma_x &= 25.46 + 244.46 = 269.92 \text{ MPa} \\
\text{Shear (torsion)} \quad \tau &= \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (50)^3} = 101.86 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Principal Stresses**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} \quad (\sigma_y = 0) \\
&= 134.96 \pm \sqrt{(134.96)^2 + (101.86)^2} \\
&= 134.96 \pm \sqrt{18214.2 + 10375.5} \\
&= 134.96 \pm \sqrt{28589.7} \\
&= 134.96 \pm 169.08 \\
\sigma_1 &= 304.04 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = -34.12 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Factor of Safety — Tresca Theory**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{eq,T} &= \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 304.04 - (-34.12) = 338.16 \text{ MPa} \\
n_T &= \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq,T}} = \frac{320}{338.16} = 0.946
\end{aligned}$$

**Result: $n_T = 0.95$ — UNSAFE (the shaft will fail)**

**Step 4: Factor of Safety — von Mises Theory**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{eq,VM} &= \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3\tau^2} = \sqrt{(269.92)^2 + 3(101.86)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{72857.0 + 31126.8} \\
&= \sqrt{103983.8} \\
&= 322.46 \text{ MPa} \\
n_{VM} &= \frac{320}{322.46} = 0.992
\end{aligned}$$

**Result: $n_{VM} = 0.99$ — UNSAFE (the shaft will fail)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Pattern]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **Maximum Principal Strain Theory** and **Mohr's Theory** of failure. Derive their equivalent stress equations and identify the type of materials they are best suited for. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A cast iron component is subjected to biaxial stresses: $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa (compressive), $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa. Given $\sigma_u = 200$ MPa, $\sigma_{uc} = 700$ MPa, $\nu = 0.25$. Find the Factor of Safety using **(i)** Maximum Principal Stress Theory, **(ii)** Maximum Principal Strain Theory, and **(iii)** Mohr's Theory. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant, 1857):**
- **Statement:** Failure occurs when the maximum principal strain in the complex stress state reaches the strain at failure in simple tension.
- **For plane stress:**
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2\right]$$
- At yield/failure in simple tension: $\varepsilon_{1,\text{tension}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{E}$ or $\frac{\sigma_u}{E}$
- Equating:
$$\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2 = \sigma_{\text{limit}}$$
- **Equivalent Stress:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \quad \text{(3-D form)}$$
- **Application:** Brittle materials, especially where strain measurement is critical.

**Mohr's Theory (Modified Mohr, 1900):**
- **Statement:** Failure occurs when the maximum shear stress on any plane reaches a critical value that depends on the normal stress on that plane. Different tensile and compressive strengths are accounted for.
- **Equivalent Stress:**
$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\sigma_{ut}}\sigma_3$$
- **Application:** Best suited for **brittle materials with different tensile and compressive strengths** (cast iron, concrete).

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Principal Stresses**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{60 + (-30)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{60-(-30)}{2}\right)^2 + 25^2} \\
&= 15 \pm \sqrt{45^2 + 25^2} \\
&= 15 \pm \sqrt{2025 + 625} \\
&= 15 \pm \sqrt{2650} \\
&= 15 \pm 51.48 \\
\sigma_1 &= 66.48 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = -36.48 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Rankine (Max Principal Stress) Theory**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 = 66.48 \text{ MPa}$$
$$n_R = \frac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_{eq}} = \frac{200}{66.48} = 3.01$$

**Step 3: Saint-Venant (Max Principal Strain) Theory**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = 66.48 - 0.25(0 + (-36.48)) = 66.48 + 9.12 = 75.60 \text{ MPa}$$
$$n_{SV} = \frac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_{eq}} = \frac{200}{75.60} = 2.65$$

**Step 4: Mohr's Theory**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\sigma_u}\sigma_3 = 66.48 - \frac{700}{200}(-36.48) = 66.48 + 127.68 = 194.16 \text{ MPa}$$
$$n_M = \frac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_{eq}} = \frac{200}{194.16} = 1.03$$

**Comparison Summary:**

| Theory | $\sigma_{eq}$ | FOS | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rankine | 66.48 MPa | 3.01 | SAFE |
| Saint-Venant | 75.60 MPa | 2.65 | SAFE |
| Mohr | 194.16 MPa | 1.03 | **CRITICAL — UNSAFE** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — torque in kN·m must be converted to N·mm before substitution (multiply by $10^6$). Students who write torque as 2.5 lose **2–3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Wrong ordering of principal stresses** — Always ensure $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ before applying Tresca's $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$. Wrong order gives wrong (often positive) factor of safety — **3 marks lost**.
> 3. **Mixing up $\sigma_y$ vs $\sigma_u$** — For ductile materials use $\sigma_y$ (yield); for brittle use $\sigma_u$ (ultimate). Substituting the wrong value = **entire answer wrong**.
> 4. **Sign errors in Mohr's equation** — The term is $\frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\sigma_u} \cdot \sigma_3$, where $\sigma_3$ is **algebraically negative**. Forgetting the negative sign makes $\sigma_{eq}$ smaller and factor of safety artificially high — **dangerous in real engineering**.
> 5. **Ignoring axial load** — In Part B question above, students often forget to add $\sigma_x = P/A$ to the bending stress. This error alone costs **2 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Before Exam — Memorize These:**

- [x] **Always classify the material first** (Ductile → von Mises / Tresca; Brittle → Mohr / Rankine).
- [x] **Principal stress ordering convention:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ — without this, Tresca formula fails.
- [x] **Tresca is more conservative** than von Mises — use Tresca when in doubt.
- [x] **Von Mises formula in 2-D:** $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ — most commonly tested.
- [x] **Tresca formula in 2-D:** $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$ — derived form.
- [x] **Poisson's ratio** is needed ONLY for Saint-Venant and Haigh theories — NOT for Tresca, von Mises, Rankine, or Mohr.
- [x] **Factor of Safety** $n = \sigma_{\text{limit}} / \sigma_{eq}$ — always positive; design is safe if $n_{actual} \geq n_{required}$.
- [x] **Units consistency:** MPa (or N/mm²) for stress, mm for dimensions, N·mm for moments — KTU strictly enforces this.
- [x] **Distortion energy** = Total strain energy − Volumetric (dilatation) energy — this is the **physical origin** of von Mises criterion.
- [x] **Mohr's theory requires both** $\sigma_u$ and $\sigma_{uc}$ — if only $\sigma_u$ is given, Rankine must be used.
- [x] **Engineering safety:** Never use Rankine's theory for ductile materials; it gives overly optimistic (unsafe) designs.
- [x] **Von Mises vs Tresca percentage:** Von Mises allows ~15% higher stress than Tresca in pure torsion — remember this for comparison questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Coverage Complete.** The next topic in Module 1 typically covers **Design for Fluctuating Loads** (Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber, ASME-Elliptic methods), which builds directly on these failure theories by adding the time-varying (fatigue) component.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Guest's Theory (1900):** *"Failure of a ductile material under complex (combined/multi-axial) loading occurs when the maximum shear stress developed in the element equals the maximum shear stress produced at the yield point in a simple uniaxial tensile test."*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\tau_{\max,\text{complex}} = \tau_{\max,\text{tension at yield}}$$

In terms of principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$$

where:
- $\sigma_1$ = major principal stress (algebraically largest)
- $\sigma_3$ = minor principal stress (algebraically smallest)
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress in simple tension
- $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms / Equivalent Theories:** Guest's Theory is mathematically identical to **Tresca's Criterion (1864)** and **Saint-Venant's Maximum Shear Stress Theory**. All three yield the same governing equation — only the historical attribution differs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Squashed Watermelon Seed":**
> Hold a watermelon seed between your thumb and index finger and squeeze. The seed does *not* crush directly — it shoots out at high velocity. Why? Because the **shear** on the diagonal planes becomes so intense that the seed fails along the plane of maximum shear, not the plane of maximum compression.
>
> A ductile metal behaves similarly. When a crankshaft is simultaneously bent and twisted, the metal "gives way" along the plane of greatest shear distortion, not necessarily where the normal stress is largest. **Guest's Theory predicts exactly which plane will slip and at what load it slips.**

**Picture this:** Take a piece of modeling clay and twist it. It doesn't crack along a face — it shears off obliquely along a 45° helical line. That 45° plane is where the **maximum shear stress** lives. Guest's Theory says: *"Yielding starts the moment this shear stress reaches the shear yield strength of the material."*

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_y$ | Tensile Yield Strength | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| $S_{sy}$ | Shear Yield Strength ≈ $0.577\,\sigma_y$ | **MPa** |
| $\sigma_u$ | Ultimate Tensile Strength | **MPa** |
| $\nu$ | Poisson's Ratio (steel ≈ **0.3**) | dimensionless |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus (steel ≈ **200 GPa**) | **GPa** |
| $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ | Principal Stresses (ordered) | **MPa** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Format for Design:** In KTU board problems, Guest's criterion is conventionally written as:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2 \, n}}$$

> where **n = Factor of Safety (FoS)**. This is the form examiners expect in design calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Shear Cause Yield in Ductile Materials?

Ductile materials fail by **slip** along crystallographic planes (the Schmid factor in metallurgy). Slip is fundamentally a **shear phenomenon**. Even in a pure tensile test, the slip plane orients at ~45° to the loading axis — precisely where the shear stress is maximum. Guest recognized that this *same* mechanism controls yielding in *combined* loading.

> **Key insight:** Slip in a ductile metal is governed by **shear**, not by **normal stress**. Hence, *maximum shear stress* (not maximum normal stress) is the correct physical criterion for the onset of yielding.

---

## 2.2 Logical Steps of the Theory

1. **Start with the general 3-D stress state** at a point: six components ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$).
2. **Transform to principal stresses** $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ (the planes where shear vanishes).
3. **Compute the maximum shear stress** — it acts on a plane oriented at 45° between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$:

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

4. **Compute the maximum shear stress in a simple tension specimen at yielding** ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$):

$$\tau_{y,\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_y - 0}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

5. **Equate the two** (failure criterion):

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$$

6. **For design with factor of safety n:**

$$\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2n}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for any Guest's Theory problem in the KTU board exam. Memorize every row.

| # | Situation | Governing Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General 3-D principal state (yielding) | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$ | Core formula |
| 2 | With Factor of Safety | $\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2n}$ | Always use this for design |
| 3 | Pure tension ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_t$, rest = 0) | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | Sanity check |
| 4 | Pure torsion (shear $\tau$) | $\tau = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2n}$ | Gives shear yield strength |
| 5 | Plane stress (2-D) — $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Roots of Mohr's circle |
| 6 | Plane stress with $\sigma_3 = 0$ | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\max(\vert \sigma_1 \vert, \vert \sigma_3 \vert)}{2}$ when both $\sigma_1,\sigma_3$ same sign; else $\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | Watch sign convention! |
| 7 | Combined bending + torsion (shaft design) | $\dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | ASME / Shigley style |
| 8 | Equivalent / Reduced stress | $\sigma_{\text{eq,Guest}} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | Compare directly to $\sigma_y$ |
| 9 | Strain energy stored at yield (Guest) | $U_{\text{Guest}} = \dfrac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$ | Useful for comparison with VM theory |
| 10 | Orthogonal shear planes (45° to axes) | $\tau$ on plane = $\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | Mohr's circle geometry |

> **Convention used in this sheet:** Compressive stresses are **negative**, tensile stresses are **positive**, following the KTU / Shigley convention.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Guest's Theory Works Here |
|---|---|
| **Automotive crankshafts** | Subjected to bending + torsion; both are ductile failure modes. |
| **Drive shafts (propeller shafts)** | Pure torsion → Guest's gives a clean shear-yield strength. |
| **Connecting rods** | Combined axial + bending (compression dominant). |
| **Bolts under tension + shear** | Mixed-mode fastener loading in chassis. |
| **Pressure vessel nozzles** | Membrane + bending stresses at junctions. |

> Guest's Theory is **conservative** for ductile materials (compared to Von Mises) — it predicts yielding *earlier* and is therefore **safe-side** for design. This is one of the reasons it remains popular in KTU-style problems and design textbooks (Shigley, Khurmi & Gupta, V.B. Bhandari).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Shear Stress in a 3-D Principal State

**Given:** A material element is subjected to three mutually orthogonal principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.

**To find:** The maximum shear stress acting on any plane inside the element.

**Step 1 — Consider an inclined plane.** Take a plane whose normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the $\sigma_1$-axis. The traction vector on this plane can be resolved into a normal component $\sigma_n$ and a shear component $\tau_n$.

**Step 2 — Resolve using equilibrium / Mohr's circle.** On a plane whose normal lies in the $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_3$ plane and makes angle $\theta$ with $\sigma_1$:

$$\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\cos 2\theta$$

$$\tau_n = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\sin 2\theta$$

**Step 3 — Maximize $\tau_n$ with respect to $\theta$.**

$$\dfrac{d\tau_n}{d\theta} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)\cos 2\theta = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \cos 2\theta = 0 \Rightarrow 2\theta = 90^\circ \Rightarrow \theta = 45^\circ$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\theta = 45^\circ$ into $\tau_n$:**

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\sin(90^\circ) = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}}$$

> **Insight:** The maximum shear stress is *independent* of $\sigma_2$ (the intermediate principal stress). This is why Guest's Theory is often called a **2-D criterion** in plane stress problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Guest's Yield Criterion (Comparison with Simple Tension)

**Given:** A simple tensile specimen of the *same* ductile material.

**In a uniaxial tensile test at the moment of yielding:** $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$ and $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$.

**Maximum shear stress at yield:**

$$\tau_{y,\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_y - 0}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

**Equating with the complex loading case (Guest's postulate):**

$$\tau_{\max,\text{complex}} = \tau_{\max,\text{yield}}$$

$$\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y \quad \text{(Guest's yield criterion)}}$$

**With factor of safety $n$:**

$$\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2n} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Principal Stresses in a Plane-Stress Element (For Solving KTU Problems)

**Given:** A 2-D element with $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ acting on its faces; $\sigma_z = \tau_{xz} = \tau_{yz} = 0$.

**Step 1 — Mohr's circle center.**

$$C = \sigma_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Mohr's circle radius.**

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Principal stresses** (read off Mohr's circle):

$$\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 4 — For plane stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$. Apply Guest's criterion:**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_1 \quad \text{(if } \sigma_1 > 0 \text{ and } \sigma_2 < 0\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case logic for $\sigma_3$ in 2-D problems:**
> - If $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are **both positive** → $\sigma_3 = 0$ (out-of-plane).
> - If $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are **both negative** → $\sigma_3 = \sigma_2$ (more negative of the two).
> - If they have **opposite signs** → $\sigma_3 = 0$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Derivation of Principal Stress Formula (Stress Transformation)

Starting from the 2-D stress transformation equation on a plane at angle $\theta$:

$$\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

**Set** $\dfrac{d\sigma_n}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$-\left(\sigma_x - \sigma_y\right)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

Substitute back into $\sigma_n$ and simplify:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}$$

The square-root term is the **Mohr's circle radius** $R$. The two roots give $\sigma_1$ (with the $+$ sign) and $\sigma_2$ (with the $-$ sign).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Guest vs Other Theories

| Theory | Yield Criterion (2-D) | Conservatism | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Normal Stress (Rankine)** | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$ | Over-conservative in shear | **Brittle** materials |
| **Max Normal Strain (Saint-Venant)** | $\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = \sigma_y$ | Moderately conservative | Ductile (rarely used) |
| **Guest / Tresca / Max Shear** | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$ | Moderately conservative | **Ductile** materials |
| **Von Mises (Distortion Energy)** | $\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_3 + \sigma_3^2 = \sigma_y^2$ | Most accurate (least conservative) | **Ductile** materials |
| **Coulomb–Mohr** | $\sigma_1 \dfrac{1}{S_t} - \sigma_3 \dfrac{1}{S_c} = 1$ | — | **Brittle** materials |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ratio check (pure shear):** For pure shear $\tau$, Guest predicts yield at $\tau = \sigma_y/2 = 0.500\,\sigma_y$, while Von Mises predicts $\tau = \sigma_y/\sqrt{3} = 0.577\,\sigma_y$. Therefore, **Guest is ~15.5% more conservative** than Von Mises.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (For Examiner's Reference)

**Problem:** A shaft carries a bending moment $M = 1.5$ kN·m and a torque $T = 4$ kN·m. The shaft diameter is $d = 60$ mm. The material has $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa. Find the factor of safety by Guest's Theory.

**Solution:**

Bending stress:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{32M}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{32 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} = \dfrac{48 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000} \approx 70.74 \text{ MPa}$$

Torsional shear stress:

$$\tau = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16 \times 4 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} \approx \dfrac{64 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000} \approx 94.32 \text{ MPa}$$

Principal stresses (using Mohr's circle):

$$\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} = \dfrac{70.74}{2} + \sqrt{(35.37)^2 + (94.32)^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 35.37 + \sqrt{1250.94 + 8896.27} = 35.37 + \sqrt{10147.21} = 35.37 + 100.73 \approx 136.10 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_3 = 0 \text{ (plane stress)}$$

Guest's criterion:

$$n = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \dfrac{320}{136.10 - 0} \approx 2.35$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 2.35}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Set A]**
**State Guest's Theory of failure. Why is it preferred for ductile materials?**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Guest's Theory (1900):** Failure of a ductile material under combined loading occurs when the maximum shear stress in the complex stress system equals the maximum shear stress at yielding in a simple tensile test.
>
> $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y \quad \text{(yield)} \qquad \text{or} \qquad \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2n} \quad \text{(design)}$$
>
> **Reasons preferred for ductile materials:**
> 1. Ductile materials fail by **shear slip** along planes oriented at ~45° to the maximum tensile stress — Guest's criterion correctly captures this physical mechanism.
> 2. It is **simple to apply** (only $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ needed; $\sigma_2$ is ignored).
> 3. It is **conservative** (predicts yielding earlier than Von Mises), providing a safe design margin.
> 4. Matches **Tresca's criterion** and **Saint-Venant's** maximum shear stress theory. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Set B]**
**A 2-D element is subjected to $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Calculate the principal stresses and the maximum shear stress as per Guest's Theory.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> Using the principal stress formula:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \sigma_{1,2} &= \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\[4pt]
> &= \dfrac{80 + (-40)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} \\[4pt]
> &= \dfrac{40}{2} \pm \sqrt{(60)^2 + 30^2} \\[4pt]
> &= 20 \pm \sqrt{3600 + 900} \\[4pt]
> &= 20 \pm \sqrt{4500} = 20 \pm 67.08
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$\sigma_1 = 87.08 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -47.08 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0 \text{ (plane stress)}$$
>
> Maximum shear stress (Guest):
>
> $$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{87.08 - (-47.08)}{2} = \dfrac{134.16}{2} = 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> *(Note: when $\sigma_3$ becomes negative, take it as the in-plane minor principal stress.)* **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice

---

### Q3A. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 marks]**
**A transmission shaft of a truck is subjected to a bending moment of 1.2 kN·m and a torsional moment of 3 kN·m. The shaft is made of steel having yield strength 350 MPa. Take a factor of safety of 2.5. Design the shaft diameter using:**
**(a)** **Guest's (Maximum Shear Stress) Theory — 7 marks**
**(b)** **Maximum Normal Stress (Rankine's) Theory — 7 marks**
**Compare the two diameters obtained.**

> **Model Answer (Part a — Guest's Theory):**
>
> The shaft experiences simultaneous bending and torsion. The bending stress is tensile at one extreme fibre and compressive at the opposite fibre:
>
> $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{32M}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{32 \times 1.2 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{38.197 \times 10^6}{d^3} \text{ MPa}$$
>
> Torsional shear stress:
>
> $$\tau = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16 \times 3 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{15.279 \times 10^6}{d^3} \text{ MPa}$$
>
> Principal stresses at the critical fibre (tensile bending + shear):
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \sigma_1 &= \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} \\[4pt]
> \sigma_2 &= \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> For Guest's Theory with FoS $n = 2.5$:
>
> $$\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2n}$$
>
> Note: $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = 2\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$
>
> $$\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2n} = \dfrac{350}{2 \times 2.5} = 70 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> Substituting the expressions:
>
> $$\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{19.098 \times 10^6}{d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{15.279 \times 10^6}{d^3}\right)^2} = 70$$
>
> $$\dfrac{10^6}{d^3}\sqrt{(19.098)^2 + (15.279)^2} = 70$$
>
> $$\dfrac{10^6}{d^3} \times \sqrt{364.73 + 233.45} = 70$$
>
> $$\dfrac{10^6}{d^3} \times \sqrt{598.18} = \dfrac{10^6}{d^3} \times 24.46 = 70$$
>
> $$d^3 = \dfrac{24.46 \times 10^6}{70} = 349.43 \times 10^3$$
>
> $$d = \sqrt[3]{349430} \approx 70.4 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$\boxed{d_{\text{Guest}} \approx 70.4 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{standardize to } 75 \text{ mm}}$$
>
> **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (Part b — Rankine's Theory):**
>
> Rankine's criterion: $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$
>
> Using $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} = \dfrac{350}{2.5} = 140$ MPa
>
> We already computed the radical term's structure. Instead, substitute the expressions and equate directly:
>
> $$\dfrac{19.098 \times 10^6}{d^3} + 70 = 140$$
>
> $$\dfrac{19.098 \times 10^6}{d^3} = 70$$
>
> $$d^3 = \dfrac{19.098 \times 10^6}{70} = 272.83 \times 10^3$$
>
> $$d = \sqrt[3]{272830} \approx 64.86 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$\boxed{d_{\text{Rankine}} \approx 64.86 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{standardize to } 65 \text{ mm}}$$
>
> **Comparison:** $d_{\text{Guest}} \approx 70.4$ mm $> d_{\text{Rankine}} \approx 64.86$ mm. **Guest's Theory gives a more conservative (larger) shaft diameter** as expected for ductile materials under combined loading. **(7 marks)**

---

### Q3B. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14 marks — Alternative Choice]**
**(a) Define principal stresses. Derive the expressions for principal stresses and maximum shear stress for a 2-D stress element. — 7 marks**
**(b) A ductile steel component has $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa. The stress state at the critical point is $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 60$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa. Determine the factor of safety by (i) Guest's Theory and (ii) Von Mises Theory, and comment on the difference. — 7 marks**

> **Model Answer (Part a):**
>
> **Definition:** Principal stresses are the normal stresses acting on planes (called principal planes) on which the shear stress is **zero**. There are three principal stresses in 3-D: $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ (ordered $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$).
>
> **Derivation:**
>
> Consider an inclined plane at angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis. The normal stress on this plane:
>
> $$\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$
>
> The shear stress on this plane:
>
> $$\tau_n = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$
>
> For principal stresses, set $\tau_n = 0$:
>
> $$\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$
>
> Substituting back and simplifying using $\sin^2 2\theta + \cos^2 2\theta = 1$:
>
> $$\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$
>
> The maximum shear stress is obtained when $\theta = \theta_p \pm 45^\circ$:
>
> $$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$
>
> **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (Part b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Principal stresses:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \sigma_{1,2} &= \dfrac{120 + 60}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{120 - 60}{2}\right)^2 + 40^2} \\[4pt]
> &= 90 \pm \sqrt{(30)^2 + (40)^2} = 90 \pm \sqrt{900 + 1600} = 90 \pm 50
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> $$\sigma_1 = 140 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 40 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$
>
> **(i) Guest's Theory:**
>
> $$\sigma_{\text{eq,Guest}} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 140 - 0 = 140 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> $$n_{\text{Guest}} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \dfrac{400}{140} \approx 2.857$$
>
> $$\boxed{n_{\text{Guest}} \approx 2.86}$$
>
> **(ii) Von Mises Theory:**
>
> $$\sigma_{\text{eq,VM}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$
>
> $$\sigma_{\text{eq,VM}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(140 - 40)^2 + (40 - 0)^2 + (0 - 140)^2}{2}}$$
>
> $$\sigma_{\text{eq,VM}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(100)^2 + (40)^2 + (-140)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10000 + 1600 + 19600}{2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{31200}{2}} = \sqrt{15600}$$
>
> $$\sigma_{\text{eq,VM}} \approx 124.90 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> $$n_{\text{VM}} = \dfrac{400}{124.90} \approx 3.203$$
>
> $$\boxed{n_{\text{VM}} \approx 3.20}$$
>
> **Comment:** $n_{\text{Guest}} = 2.86 < n_{\text{VM}} = 3.20$. Guest's Theory gives a **lower (more conservative)** factor of safety. This is consistent with theory — Guest predicts yielding earlier than Von Mises for ductile materials. In practice, both are acceptable; Von Mises agrees better with experimental data for ductile steels, but Guest is mathematically simpler. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Guest's Theory problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to order principal stresses**: Many students write $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ using $\sigma_3$ as the intermediate root. **Always ensure $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.** Mixing up $\sigma_2$ and $\sigma_3$ alone can cost **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
>
> 2. **Sign convention mistakes**: When all principal stresses are compressive (negative), some students subtract the wrong pair. **Rule:** always compute (algebraically largest) $-$ (algebraically smallest), regardless of sign.
>
> 3. **Using $\sigma_3 = 0$ blindly**: In plane stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$ is correct **only if $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ have the same sign**. If they have opposite signs, the minor principal *is* $\sigma_2$ and may itself be negative — then $\sigma_3 = \sigma_2$, not 0.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of safety**: Many board answers write $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$ for a *design* problem where $n$ is specified. **Always write $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y / n$ in design problems.**
>
> 5. **Unit errors**: Forgetting to convert kN·m → N·mm when computing bending and torsional stresses. Diameter in mm + moment in kN·m → catastrophic numerical error.
>
> 6. **Not stating the assumption**: Examiners reward stating "ductile material, plane stress assumed, $\sigma_3 = 0$" at the start. Costs nothing, looks professional, and may earn an "impression" mark.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Guest's Theory**

- **Core equation (yield):** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$
- **Core equation (design with FoS $n$):** $\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2n}$
- **Always** order principal stresses: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.
- **Intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$ does NOT appear** in the criterion — a key distinguishing feature of Guest/Tresca.
- **Plane stress default**: $\sigma_3 = 0$ **only** if the two in-plane principals are same-sign.
- **Pure torsion sanity check**: $\tau_{\text{yield}} = \sigma_y / 2 = 0.500 \, \sigma_y$ (Guest) vs $\sigma_y / \sqrt{3} = 0.577 \, \sigma_y$ (Von Mises).
- **Combined bending + torsion shaft**: use equivalent moment $M_{\text{eq}} = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ approach (when von Mises) or principal-stress approach (when Guest).
- **Applicability**: Ductile materials (steel, aluminum, copper). **Not** for brittle materials (cast iron, ceramics) — use Rankine or Coulomb–Mohr instead.
- **Conservatism**: Guest is more conservative than Von Mises (~15.5% in pure shear) — safe-side for design.
- **Equivalences**: Guest ≡ Tresca ≡ Saint-Venant's Max Shear Stress Theory (all mathematically identical in 2-D).
- **Plane-stress principal stress formula** (memorize!):

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

- **Maximum shear stress formula** (memorize!):

$$\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

- **Failure plane orientation**: 45° to the major principal stress direction (between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$).
- **Always** write the equation **and** substitute the numerical values — partial-mark hunting requires every algebraic step.
- **Units**: keep consistent — diameter in mm, moment in N·mm, stress in MPa (N/mm²).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine's Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rankine's Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rankine's Theory (Rankine-Gordon Formula)** is an empirical column-strength theory that provides a unified, continuous expression for predicting the crippling (failure) load of columns, valid for **all slenderness ratios** — spanning the entire spectrum from short (stocky) columns that fail by direct crushing, through intermediate columns, to long (slender) columns that fail by elastic buckling.

Mathematically, the **Rankine crippling load** $P$ for a column of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, least radius of gyration $k$, made of a material with ultimate crushing stress $\sigma_c$ and Rankine's empirical constant $a$, is given by:

$$P = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2}$$

The term $\dfrac{L}{k}$ is called the **slenderness ratio** $(\lambda)$ — a dimensionless measure of the column's tendency to buckle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw vs. Steel Rod" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine holding a thin drinking straw vertically and pressing down on its top. The straw is short — it simply **crushes** at the top with a dull *crunch*. Now, take the same straw and make it very long (say, 1 meter). Press down again — the straw does **not crush**; instead, it suddenly snaps sideways in a violent *snap*. This is **buckling**.

> **Where does Rankine's Theory fit?**
> There exists a **"Goldilocks zone"** of length — neither very short nor very long — where the column can fail by **either** crushing **or** buckling, and we cannot predict which. Rankine brilliantly crafted a single equation that **smoothly transitions** from crushing behavior (Euler's regime fails here) to buckling behavior (pure crushing theory fails here) by mathematically *bridging* the two failure modes.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Rankine's Constant ($a$)** — an empirical dimensionless coefficient that depends solely on the material:
  - **Mild Steel:** $a = \dfrac{1}{7500}$ *(most common in KTU problems)*
  - **Wrought Iron:** $a = \dfrac{1}{9000}$
  - **Cast Iron:** $a = \dfrac{1}{1600}$
  - **Timber:** $a = \dfrac{1}{750}$

- **Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$)** — always expressed as a **pure number** (no units):
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L}{k} = \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$

- **Factor of Safety (FoS):** typically **3 to 4** for column design as per IS:800 and standard automotive design codes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Rankine's Theory is taught as the *go-to* design formula for **intermediate slenderness ratio columns** because Euler's formula catastrophically over-predicts strength for short columns, while pure crushing equations ignore buckling entirely. The Rankine-Gordon formula is the **engineering compromise** that works for *all* lengths.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Two Failure Regimes

A structural column under axial compressive load $P$ can fail in one of two fundamentally different ways depending on its slenderness ratio $\lambda$:

### Regime 1: Short Column Failure (Crushing)
- **Mechanism:** Direct material compression failure.
- **Applicable when:** $\lambda < \lambda_c$ (below the critical slenderness ratio, typically $\lambda_c \approx 80$ for mild steel).
- **Failure load:** $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$
- **Governed by:** Material strength.

### Regime 2: Long Column Failure (Euler Buckling)
- **Mechanism:** Lateral elastic instability.
- **Applicable when:** $\lambda > \lambda_c$ (above critical slenderness ratio).
- **Failure load:** $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2}$
- **Governed by:** Geometry and stiffness (modulus of elasticity).

### Regime 3: The Intermediate Gap
- Neither pure crushing nor pure Euler buckling accurately predicts the failure load.
- **Rankine's genius:** Empirically blends both with the constant $a$ such that the Rankine load $P$ approaches $P_c$ for short columns and $P_E$ for long columns.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation Outline

1. **Assume** the column deflection consists of two additive components: bending deflection + direct shortening.
2. **Apply** the moment-curvature relationship considering the additional moment due to deflection.
3. **Empirically fit** the resulting transcendental equation using a constant $a$ such that:
   - As $L/k \to 0$ (short column): $P \to \sigma_c A = P_c$ ✓
   - As $L/k \to \infty$ (long column): $P \to \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2} = P_E$ ✓
4. The resulting empirical form is the **Rankine-Gordon equation**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam!**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a(L/k)^2}$ | Rankine crippling load | N (or kN) | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{1 + a(L/k)^2}$ | Rankine design/working stress | N/mm² | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{F.S.}$ | Safe design load | N (or kN) | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $A = \dfrac{P_d [1 + a(L/k)^2]}{\sigma_c}$ | Required area (direct design) | mm² | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L}{k} = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{I/A}}$ | Slenderness ratio | dimensionless | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | $k = \sqrt{I/A} = \dfrac{D}{4}$ (solid circular) | Radius of gyration | mm | ★★★★ |
| 7 | $k = \dfrac{D_o}{4}\sqrt{1 + (D_i/D_o)^2}$ (hollow circular) | Hollow shaft radius of gyration | mm | ★★★★ |
| 8 | $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2}$ | Euler buckling load | N (or kN) | ★★★★ |
| 9 | $L_{eff} = L \cdot (\text{effective length factor})$ | Effective length | mm | ★★★★★ |
| 10 | $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ | Pure crushing load | N (or kN) | ★★★ |

**Effective Length Factors (End Conditions):**

| End Condition | $L_{eff}$ |
|--------------|-----------|
| Both ends hinged | $L$ |
| Both ends fixed | $0.5L$ |
| One end fixed, one end free | $2L$ |
| One end fixed, one end hinged | $0.707L$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Automotive Design

Rankine's Theory is **mission-critical** in automotive engineering for designing:

- 🚗 **Drive Shafts** — long rotating shafts transmitting torque that experience combined bending + torsion.
- 🚗 **Axles** — particularly rear dead axles in trucks.
- 🚗 **Steering Columns** — must resist buckling under driver's compressive load.
- 🚗 **Connecting Rods** — operate as short-to-intermediate columns under combustion loading.
- 🚗 **Piston Pins (Gudgeon Pins)** — short columns under cyclic combustion force.
- 🚗 **Push Rods** (in OHV engines) — long slender columns under valve load.
- 🚗 **Suspension Struts and Shock Absorber Rods.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just use Euler's formula for everything?** Because Euler's formula gives **infinite strength** as length decreases — physically absurd. Rankine's formula is the **designer's practical tool** that gracefully degrades to material crushing strength for short columns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Rankine-Gordon Formula from First Principles

### Setup and Assumptions

Consider a column of length $L$, hinged at both ends, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$. Let the column deflect laterally by an amount $y$ at a distance $x$ from the bottom.

**Assumption (Rankine):** The column is initially slightly curved, and the *deflection* $y$ at any point is the sum of the initial crookedness $y_0$ and an additional deflection $y_1$ caused by the bending moment $P \cdot y$.

### Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1: Governing differential equation.**

For an initially slightly curved column, the bending moment at any section is:
$$M = P \cdot y$$

Using the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -M$:
$$EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -P \cdot y$$

**Step 2: Solving the differential equation.**

This is a standard second-order linear ODE with the solution:
$$y = C_1 \cos\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{P}{EI}} \cdot x\right) + C_2 \sin\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{P}{EI}} \cdot x\right)$$

**Step 3: Apply boundary conditions.**

For both ends hinged: $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$.
- At $x = 0$: $C_1 = 0$
- At $x = L$: $C_2 \sin\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{P}{EI}} \cdot L\right) = 0$

For a non-trivial solution:
$$\sqrt{\dfrac{P}{EI}} \cdot L = \pi$$
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

This recovers **Euler's formula** for a perfectly straight column. However, Rankine recognized that **real columns are never perfectly straight**, so he introduced a *corrected* approach.

**Step 4: Rankine's empirical correction.**

Rankine assumed the **maximum deflection** $y_{max}$ at the mid-height of the column. The bending stress at the extreme fiber is:
$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{P \cdot y_{max} \cdot c}{I}$$

**Step 5: Add direct compressive stress.**

The total maximum stress is the sum of direct compressive stress and bending stress:
$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{P \cdot y_{max} \cdot c}{I}$$

**Step 6: Empirically relate $y_{max}$ to geometry.**

Rankine proposed:
$$y_{max} = c_0 \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{k^2}$$
where $c_0$ is an empirical coefficient. After substitution and collecting terms, and defining $a = c_0 \cdot c / k^2$, we obtain:
$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left[1 + a \left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 7: Set maximum stress equal to crushing stress.**

At incipient failure, $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_c$:
$$\dfrac{P}{A}\left[1 + a \left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2\right] = \sigma_c$$

**Step 8: Solve for the Rankine crippling load.**

$$\boxed{P = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Reciprocal Form (KTU Favorite)

The Rankine formula can be elegantly rewritten in **reciprocal form** that beautifully shows the dual failure mechanism:

Starting from:
$$\dfrac{1}{P} = \dfrac{1 + a(L/k)^2}{\sigma_c \cdot A} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_c A} + \dfrac{a L^2}{\sigma_c A k^2}$$

Recognizing that $P_c = \sigma_c A$ and $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$:

Since $a$ is empirically chosen such that $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$, we get:

$$\dfrac{1}{P} = \dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}$$

This is the **reciprocal Rankine formula**:
$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{P} = \dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** The Rankine crippling load is the *harmonic-mean-like* combination of crushing load and Euler buckling load. It is *always less than both* $P_c$ and $P_E$, meaning the column is **always weaker** than predicted by either pure theory alone — a safety-favoring result.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A mild steel column of circular cross-section is **3 m long**, **hinged at both ends**, and is to carry an axial compressive load of **100 kN**. Find the diameter of the column using Rankine's formula. Take $\sigma_c = 330$ N/mm², $a = 1/7500$, and Factor of Safety = 4.

### Given Data

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 3000 \text{ mm} \\
P &= 100{,}000 \text{ N} \\
\sigma_c &= 330 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
a &= \dfrac{1}{7500} \\
F.S. &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 1: Compute Design Load

$$
P_d = \dfrac{P}{F.S.} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{4} = 25{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Express Cross-Sectional Properties for Solid Circular Section

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}, \quad I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}, \quad k^2 = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{D^2}{16}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Substitute into Rankine's Design Equation

The design equation is:
$$P_d = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a(L/k)^2}$$

Substituting $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ and $L/k = L/(D/4) = 4L/D$:

$$
25{,}000 = \dfrac{330 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}}{1 + \dfrac{1}{7500} \cdot \left(\dfrac{4 \times 3000}{D}\right)^2}
$$

### Solution — Step 4: Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
25{,}000 &= \dfrac{330 \cdot \pi D^2 / 4}{1 + \dfrac{(12000)^2}{7500 \cdot D^2}} \\[6pt]
25{,}000 &= \dfrac{259.18 \cdot D^2}{1 + \dfrac{1.44 \times 10^8}{7500 \cdot D^2}} \\[6pt]
25{,}000 &= \dfrac{259.18 \cdot D^2 \cdot 7500 \cdot D^2}{7500 \cdot D^2 + 1.44 \times 10^8}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 5: Cross-Multiply

$$
25{,}000 \cdot (7500 \cdot D^2 + 1.44 \times 10^8) = 259.18 \cdot 7500 \cdot D^4
$$

$$
1.875 \times 10^8 \cdot D^2 + 3.6 \times 10^{12} = 1.9439 \times 10^6 \cdot D^4
$$

### Solution — Step 6: Rearrange as Polynomial in $D^2$

$$
1.9439 \times 10^6 \cdot D^4 - 1.875 \times 10^8 \cdot D^2 - 3.6 \times 10^{12} = 0
$$

Let $x = D^2$:

$$
1.9439 \times 10^6 \cdot x^2 - 1.875 \times 10^8 \cdot x - 3.6 \times 10^{12} = 0
$$

### Solution — Step 7: Solve Quadratic Equation

Using the quadratic formula $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$
x = \dfrac{1.875 \times 10^8 \pm \sqrt{(1.875 \times 10^8)^2 + 4 \cdot 1.9439 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{12}}}{2 \cdot 1.9439 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{1.875 \times 10^8 \pm \sqrt{3.5156 \times 10^{16} + 2.7992 \times 10^{19}}}{3.8878 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{1.875 \times 10^8 \pm \sqrt{2.8027 \times 10^{19}}}{3.8878 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{1.875 \times 10^8 \pm 5.2940 \times 10^9}{3.8878 \times 10^6}
$$

Taking the **positive root** (since $x = D^2 > 0$):

$$
x = \dfrac{5.2940 \times 10^9 + 1.875 \times 10^8}{3.8878 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{5.4815 \times 10^9}{3.8878 \times 10^6} = 1410 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Solution — Step 8: Calculate Diameter

$$
D = \sqrt{1410} \approx 37.55 \text{ mm}
$$

**Standardized diameter (KTU convention): $D = 40$ mm** ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** The slenderness ratio is $\lambda = 4L/D = 4(3000)/40 = 300$. This is an *intermediate* column — perfect Rankine territory. Euler's formula would have been invalid here.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Rankine's Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Column Design Problem] --> B[Identify End Conditions]
    B --> C[Compute Effective Length L_eff]
    C --> D[Assume Cross-Section Geometry]
    D --> E[Compute Area A and Radius of Gyration k]
    E --> F[Calculate Slenderness Ratio lambda = L_eff / k]
    F --> G{Is lambda less than Critical?}
    G -->|Yes, short column| H[Use Pure Crushing Formula P_c = sigma_c x A]
    G -->|No, long column| I[Use Euler Buckling Formula P_E = pi squared E I / L_eff squared]
    G -->|Intermediate| J[Use Rankine Formula P = sigma_c A / 1 + a times L/k squared]
    H --> K[Apply Factor of Safety to get P_d]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Check Deflection and Buckling Safety]
    L --> M{Safe Design?}
    M -->|No| N[Revise Cross-Section: Increase A or k]
    N --> D
    M -->|Yes| O[Finalize: Standardize Dimensions]
    O --> P[End: Document Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure Regime Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slenderness Ratio lambda] --> B[lambda less than 30]
    A --> C[30 to 80]
    A --> D[lambda greater than 80]
    B --> E[Short Column: Crushing Failure]
    C --> F[Intermediate: Rankine Theory Applies]
    D --> G[Long Column: Euler Buckling]
    E --> H[Formula: P_c = sigma_c times A]
    F --> I[Formula: P = sigma_c A over 1 plus a L over k squared]
    G --> J[Formula: P_E = pi squared E I over L_eff squared]
    H --> K[Automotive Example: Piston Pin]
    I --> L[Automotive Example: Connecting Rod]
    J --> M[Automotive Example: Drive Shaft]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Three Column Theories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ShortColumn
        SC1[Crushing Theory] --> SC2[P_c = sigma_c times A]
        SC2 --> SC3[Valid for lambda less than 30]
    end
    subgraph IntermediateColumn
        IC1[Rankine Theory] --> IC2[P = sigma_c A over 1 plus a L over k squared]
        IC2 --> IC3[Valid for lambda between 30 and 80]
        IC2 --> IC4[Empirical Constant a]
    end
    subgraph LongColumn
        LC1[Euler Theory] --> LC2[P_E = pi squared E I over L_eff squared]
        LC2 --> LC3[Valid for lambda greater than 80]
    end
    ShortColumn -.->|Transition Zone| IntermediateColumn
    IntermediateColumn -.->|Transition Zone| LongColumn
```

## 4.4 Free-Body Schematic Description (For Student Visualization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Picture:** A vertical column of length $L$ is hinged at top and bottom. An axial compressive load $P$ acts at the centroid of the top cross-section. The column deflects laterally by an amount $\delta$ at the mid-height. The bending moment at mid-height is $M = P \cdot \delta$. The maximum compressive stress occurs at the **concave side** of the buckled column, where direct stress and bending stress add up. The cross-section (for a circular column) has outer diameter $D$, cross-sectional area $A = \pi D^2/4$, second moment of area $I = \pi D^4/64$, and radius of gyration $k = D/4$.

## 4.5 Mermaid Sequence — Problem-Solving Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Student
    student->>Problem: Read Given Data
    Problem->>Student: Return L, P, Material, FoS
    Student->>Student: Compute L_eff from End Conditions
    Student->>Student: Express A and k in terms of D
    Student->>Rankine: Substitute into P_d = sigma_c A / 1 + a L over k squared
    Rankine->>Student: Rearrange as Quadratic in D squared
    Student->>Student: Solve Quadratic Equation
    Student->>Student: Compute D and Standardize
    Student->>Verifier: Check Slenderness Ratio is in Rankine Range
    Verifier->>Student: Confirm Design
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q: State Rankine's formula for the crippling load of a column. What is the significance of Rankine's constant?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rankine's Formula:** $P = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2}$

where:
- $P$ = Rankine crippling load (N)
- $\sigma_c$ = ultimate crushing stress of column material (N/mm²)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (mm²)
- $L$ = length of column (mm)
- $k$ = least radius of gyration (mm)
- $a$ = **Rankine's empirical constant** (dimensionless)

**Significance of Rankine's Constant $a$:**
- It is a material-dependent empirical coefficient that quantifies the initial imperfection/inhomogeneity of the column material.
- For **mild steel**, $a = 1/7500$; for **cast iron**, $a = 1/1600$; for **wrought iron**, $a = 1/9000$.
- It is mathematically related to material properties: $a = \sigma_c / (\pi^2 E)$, where $E$ is Young's modulus.
- It ensures the formula correctly transitions from short-column crushing to long-column Euler buckling.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Q: Compare Euler's theory and Rankine's theory for column design. When is Rankine's formula preferred over Euler's formula?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | Euler's Theory | Rankine's Theory |
> |--------|---------------|------------------|
> | Validity | Long columns ($\lambda > 80$ for steel) | All slenderness ratios |
> | Failure mode | Elastic buckling only | Combined crushing + buckling |
> | Material constant | Uses $E$ only | Uses $\sigma_c$ and $a$ |
> | Formula | $P_E = \pi^2 E I / L_{eff}^2$ | $P = \sigma_c A / [1 + a(L/k)^2]$ |
> | Limitation | Fails for short columns (over-predicts) | Empirically valid for all $\lambda$ |

**Rankine's formula is preferred when:**
- The column has an **intermediate slenderness ratio** (typically $30 < \lambda < 80$ for mild steel).
- Euler's formula would **grossly over-predict** the crippling load.
- A unified formula valid for **all** column lengths is desired.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the Rankine-Gordon formula for the crippling load of a column. State clearly the assumptions made in the derivation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
