## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Von Mises Failure Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: 3D Stress State sigma1, sigma2, sigma3] --> B[Compute Mean Hydrostatic Stress sigma_m]
    B --> C[Compute Distortion Energy U_d actual]
    C --> D[Compute Uniaxial Yield Distortion Energy U_d yield]
    D --> E{Compare U_d actual vs U_d yield}
    E -- Less --> F[Safe: Component OK]
    E -- Equal --> G[Yield Onset: Boundary]
    E -- Greater --> H[FAILURE: Redesign Required]
    F --> I[Compute FOS = S_yt / sigma_VM]
    I --> J[Output: FOS value to Designer]
    H --> K[Increase Cross Section OR Change Material]
    K --> L[Re-enter Loop at A]
```

### 4.2 2-D Yield Surface Comparison (Von Mises vs Tresca vs Rankine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plane Stress State sigma1, sigma2] --> B{Identify Material}
    B -- Ductile Steel --> C[Apply Von Mises Ellipse: sigma1^2 - sigma1 sigma2 + sigma2^2 = Sy^2]
    B -- Conservative Ductile --> D[Apply Tresca Hexagon]
    B -- Brittle Cast Iron --> E[Apply Rankine Square]
    C --> F[Result: Elliptical Safe Zone]
    D --> G[Result: Hexagonal Safe Zone]
    E --> H[Result: Square Safe Zone]
    F --> I[KTU Standard for Automotive Ductile Components]
    G --> J[Conservative Estimate]
    H --> K[For Brittle Materials Only]
```

### 4.3 Application Mapping in an Automotive Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Automotive Powertrain] --> B[Engine Block]
    A --> C[Crankshaft]
    A --> D[Connecting Rod]
    A --> E[Drive Axle]
    A --> F[Transmission Gears]
    C --> C1[Bending Moment M]
    C --> C2[Torsion T]
    C1 --> G[Combined Stress State]
    C2 --> G
    G --> H[Von Mises Equivalent Stress: sqrt sigma^2 + 3 tau^2]
    H --> I{sigma_VM less than Sy by N}
    I -- Yes --> J[Safe Design]
    I -- No --> K[Increase Diameter or Use Higher Grade Steel]
    D --> L[Axial Compression + Buckling]
    L --> M[Von Mises Biaxial Check]
    E --> N[Pure Torsion Dominant]
    N --> O[Von Mises: tau_max less than 0.577 Sy]
```

### 4.4 Stress State Decoding Flowchart for a Solid Circular Shaft

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solid Circular Shaft in Automotive] --> B[Loading Type?]
    B -- Pure Bending M --> C[Bending Stress: sigma = 32M pi d^3]
    B -- Pure Torsion T --> D[Shear Stress: tau = 16T pi d^3]
    B -- Combined Bending and Torsion --> E[Bending: sigma_x = 32M pi d^3]
    E --> F[Torsional Shear: tau_xy = 16T pi d^3]
    F --> G[Compute Von Mises: sigma_VM = sqrt sigma_x^2 + 3 tau_xy^2]
    G --> H[Apply: sigma_VM less than Sy divided by N]
    H --> I{Solve for Diameter d}
    I -- Design OK --> J[Final d]
    I -- Inadequate --> K[Increase d and Recompute]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Von Mises–Hencky distortion energy failure theory for a ductile material under complex 3-D loading.*

**Model Answer:**

According to the **Von Mises–Hencky (Distortion Energy) Theory**, a ductile component is safe under a complex three-dimensional stress state $(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3)$ when the total **distortion strain energy per unit volume** at the critical point does not exceed the distortion strain energy at the yield point in a uniaxial tensile test.

Mathematically:

$$
\dfrac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] \le \left(\dfrac{S_{yt}}{N}\right)^2
$$

This theory is the most accurate failure criterion for **ductile materials** such as structural and automotive steels, and is used as the **default theory** in KTU 2024 automotive component design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between volumetric strain energy and distortion strain energy. Why is the latter responsible for yielding in ductile materials?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Volumetric Strain Energy ($U_v$) | Distortion Strain Energy ($U_d$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Hydrostatic / mean stress $\sigma_m$ | Deviatoric / shear components |
| **Physical Effect** | **Volume change** (dilatation) | **Shape change** (distortion) |
| **Formula** | $U_v = \dfrac{3(1-2\nu)}{2E}\sigma_m^2$ | $U_d = \dfrac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]$ |
| **Causes Yield?** | ❌ No (in ductile metals) | ✅ Yes |

**Reason:** In ductile materials, atomic sliding occurs along slip planes when **shear distortion** is present. Hydrostatic compression/tension only brings atoms closer/farther — it does not initiate slip. Therefore, only $U_d$ is responsible for plastic yielding.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** *A solid circular shaft of an automobile transmits a torque of 1.2 kN·m and is simultaneously subjected to a bending moment of 0.9 kN·m. The shaft is made of 45C8 steel with yield strength $S_{yt} = 380 \text{ MPa}$. Using the Von Mises–Hencky theory, determine the required shaft diameter for a factor of safety of 3. Assume a small keyway factor of 1.0.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain with neat sketch the physical meaning of distortion energy. How is it different from total strain energy? Prove that the total strain energy is the sum of volumetric and distortion strain energies.* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Given:**
- Torque, $T = 1.2 \text{ kN·m} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$
- Bending moment, $M = 0.9 \text{ kN·m} = 0.9 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$
- Yield strength, $S_{yt} = 380 \text{ MPa}$
- Factor of safety, $N = 3$

**Step 1: Bending Stress**

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 0.9 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} = \frac{9.167 \times 10^6}{d^3} \text{ MPa (with } d \text{ in mm)}
$$

**Step 2: Torsional Shear Stress**

$$
\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 1.2 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} = \frac{6.111 \times 10^6}{d^3} \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Von Mises Equivalent Stress (Combined Bending + Torsion)**

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{9.167 \times 10^6}{d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{6.111 \times 10^6}{d^3}\right)^2}
$$

Factor out $\dfrac{1}{d^3}$:

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \frac{1}{d^3}\sqrt{(9.167)^2 + 3(6.111)^2} \times 10^6
$$

$$
(9.167)^2 = 84.034 \quad ; \quad 3(6.111)^2 = 3 \times 37.344 = 112.032
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 196.066 \quad ; \quad \sqrt{196.066} = 14.002
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \frac{14.002 \times 10^6}{d^3} \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Von Mises–Hencky Failure Criterion**

$$
\sigma_{VM} \le \frac{S_{yt}}{N}
$$

$$
\frac{14.002 \times 10^6}{d^3} \le \frac{380}{3}
$$

$$
\frac{14.002 \times 10^6}{d^3} \le 126.667
$$

$$
d^3 \ge \frac{14.002 \times 10^6}{126.667} = 110{,}550.1
$$

$$
d \ge \sqrt[3]{110{,}550.1} = 47.97 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{d \approx 48 \text{ mm}}
$$

> **Designer's Standard Practice:** Round up to next standard size — **d = 50 mm**.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Physical Meaning of Distortion Energy:**
Distortion energy is the portion of total elastic strain energy per unit volume that is responsible for **changing the shape** (angular deformation) of a material element, without changing its volume. It is the energy absorbed by **deviatoric (shear) stresses**.

**Mathematical Statement:**

The total strain energy per unit volume in a stressed element can be uniquely decomposed as:

$$
U_{total} = U_{volumetric} + U_{distortion}
$$

**Proof:**

Total strain energy (3-D elastic, principal stresses):

$$
U_{total} = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]
$$

Volumetric strain energy (under hydrostatic stress $\sigma_m$):

$$
U_v = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{2E}\sigma_m^2, \quad \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3}{3}
$$

Expanding:

$$
U_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)^2\right]
$$

$$
U_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 + 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]
$$

Now, $U_d = U_{total} - U_v$:

$$
U_d = \frac{1}{2E}(\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2) - \frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1) - \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2) - \frac{2(1-2\nu)}{6E}(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)
$$

Simplifying coefficients (as in the earlier derivation):

$$
U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]
$$

Hence **proved**:

$$
U_{total} = U_v + U_d
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Sketch Description (for answer sheet):**
> Draw a small cube with three principal stresses $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ acting on the faces. Show a copy of the same cube with **equal stress** $\sigma_m$ on all faces (pure volume change). Show the **difference cube** with deviatoric stresses causing shape change (shear distortion). Label: $U_{total}$ = (1st cube), $U_v$ = (2nd cube), $U_d$ = (3rd cube).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** *A connecting rod of an IC engine is subjected to a tensile load of 50 kN. The cross-section at the critical location is $40 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ (rectangular). Due to the dynamic nature, the rod also experiences a transverse shear of 20 kN. If the material has $S_{yt} = 320 \text{ MPa}$, find the factor of safety using the Von Mises theory. Take Poisson's ratio = 0.3.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the Von Mises–Hencky theory with the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca) in terms of (i) accuracy, (ii) safety zone in $\sigma_1-\sigma_2$ plane, and (iii) preferred application. Show the elliptical and hexagonal yield loci in a neat sketch.* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Given:**
- Axial tensile load, $P = 50 \text{ kN} = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$
- Cross-section: $40 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ → Area $A = 800 \text{ mm}^2$
- Transverse shear force, $V = 20 \text{ kN} = 20{,}000 \text{ N}$
- Yield strength, $S_{yt} = 320 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1: Normal Stress (Tension along y-axis)**

$$
\sigma_y = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{800} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Average Shear Stress (Transverse)**

$$
\tau_{xy} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{20{,}000}{800} = 25 \text{ MPa}
$$

(For a rectangular section, max shear at neutral axis = $\frac{3V}{2A}$, but average is used in introductory problems.)

**Step 3: Identify Principal Stresses**

In plane stress, $\sigma_x = 0, \sigma_y = 62.5 \text{ MPa}, \tau_{xy} = 25 \text{ MPa}$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{0 + 62.5}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{0 - 62.5}{2}\right)^2 + 25^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{1,2} = 31.25 \pm \sqrt{976.5625 + 625} = 31.25 \pm \sqrt{1601.5625}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1601.5625} = 40.020
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = 31.25 + 40.020 = 71.27 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = 31.25 - 40.020 = -8.77 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_3 = 0 \text{ (plane stress)}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Von Mises Criterion (Factor of Safety form)**

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] = \left(\frac{S_{yt}}{N}\right)^2
$$

Computing terms:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 = (71.27 - (-8.77))^2 = (80.04)^2 = 6406.40
$$

$$
(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 = (-8.77 - 0)^2 = 76.91
$$

$$
(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = (0 - 71.27)^2 = 5079.41
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 6406.40 + 76.91 + 5079.41 = 11{,}562.72
$$

$$
\frac{1}{2}(11{,}562.72) = 5781.36
$$

**Step 5: Solve for N**

$$
5781.36 = \left(\frac{320}{N}\right)^2
$$

$$
\frac{320}{N} = \sqrt{5781.36} = 76.04
$$

$$
N = \frac{320}{76.04} = 4.21
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{N \approx 4.2}
$$

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

| Aspect | Von Mises–Hencky (Distortion Energy) | Tresca (Maximum Shear Stress) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Accuracy** | **Most accurate** for ductile materials (closely matches experimental data) | Conservative — over-predicts failure by ~15% |
| **(ii) Safe Zone in $\sigma_1$-$\sigma_2$ Plane** | **Elliptical curve** $ \sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2 = S_y^2/N^2 $ | **Hexagonal curve** $\max(\vert \sigma_1-\sigma_2 \vert, \vert \sigma_1 \vert, \vert \sigma_2 \vert) = S_y/N$ |
| **(iii) Preferred Application** | **Automotive ductile components** (crankshafts, axles, connecting rods) — weight-optimized | Quick conservative check; used when load data is uncertain |
| **Geometric Relation** | Ellipse is **inscribed inside** the Tresca hexagon | Hexagon **encloses** the Von Mises ellipse |
| **Mathematical Form** | Involves squared terms → smooth curve | Involves absolute values → piecewise linear |
| **Engineering Justification** | Physical: distortion energy causes slip | Physical: max shear on slip plane causes slip |

> **Neat Sketch Description (for answer sheet):**
> Draw a $\sigma_1$ (horizontal) vs $\sigma_2$ (vertical) plot. Draw an **equilateral hexagon** with vertices at $(\pm S_y/N, 0)$, $(0, \pm S_y/N)$, and $(S_y/N, S_y/N)$, $(-S_y/N, -S_y/N)$ — this is the Tresca locus. **Inscribe an ellipse** inside it, passing through the same six points — this is the Von Mises locus. The ellipse bulges **outward** at the corners of the hexagon, giving a larger safe area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Von Mises stress" formula for bending + torsion:**
>    ❌ Wrong: $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$ (this is for thin-walled pressure vessels)
>    ✅ Correct: $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ (solid shaft, 3-D state)
> 2. **Forgetting to include 3 in "3τ²"** — the 3 comes from the third principal stress being zero in plane stress. Missing it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using $S_{ut}$ instead of $S_{yt}$** for ductile static design — use $S_{yt}$ (yield), not ultimate.
> 4. **In Tresca, students forget to check 3 cases** — not just $\vert \sigma_1-\sigma_2 \vert$, but also $\vert \sigma_1 \vert$ and $\vert \sigma_2 \vert$ when one stress is negative.
> 5. **Units mismatch** — always convert kN·m to N·mm before plugging into $32M/\pi d^3$ formula. Common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Final diameter not rounded up to next standard size** — examiners expect 50 mm, 55 mm, etc., not 47.97 mm.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Von Mises–Hencky** = **Distortion Energy Theory** = most accurate for **ductile** materials.
- ✅ **Core Hypothesis:** Yielding depends only on **shape change (distortion) energy**, not **volume change (volumetric) energy**.
- ✅ **Master Formula (3-D):**
  $$ \tfrac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right] \le (S_{yt}/N)^2 $$
- ✅ **Automotive Shortcut (Bending + Torsion):**
  $$ \sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2} \le S_{yt}/N $$
- ✅ **Plane Stress Biaxial Form:** $\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2 = (S_{yt}/N)^2$ — elliptical yield locus.
- ✅ **Octahedral Shear Stress Relation:** $\tau_{oct} = \dfrac{1}{3}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3}\sigma_{VM}$
- ✅ **Why Von Mises is preferred over Tresca in automotive:** Ellipse encloses hexagon's interior at non-axis points → ~15% better material utilization → lighter vehicles → better fuel economy.
- ✅ **Failure Theories Comparison Order (brittle → ductile):** Rankine (brittle) → Mohr (brittle/ductile) → Tresca (conservative ductile) → **Von Mises (accurate ductile)**.
- ✅ **Automotive Applications:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, axles, half-shafts, transmission shafts, suspension links, gear tooth roots.
- ✅ **Use $S_{yt}$ for static/dynamic ductile design; use $S_{ut}$ only for brittle or ultimate-strength check.**
- ✅ **Always include the 3 in 3τ² for solid shafts** — this is the #1 KTU 2024 valuation pitfall.
- ✅ **Round up final diameters to next standard ISO/IS size** (e.g., 50 mm, 55 mm, 60 mm).
- ✅ **Recall the key physical constants:** $E$ = 200–210 GPa (steel), $\nu$ ≈ 0.3, $\frac{1+\nu}{E} \approx 6.19 \times 10^{-3}$ /GPa.
- ✅ **Hencky (1924)** is the **mathematical formulator**; **Von Mises (1913)** is the **physical originator** — cite both names in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shock and Impact Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shock and Impact Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Shock and Impact Loads — Foundations & Physical Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shock Loading** refers to a condition in which a load is applied to a machine element **instantaneously** or over a very short duration, such that the element does not have time to deform gradually before the full magnitude of the load acts upon it. The resulting stresses are called **dynamic stresses** or **shock stresses**, and they can be **several times higher** than the static stress produced by the same nominal load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Impact Loading** is a special, more severe form of shock loading in which a moving mass collides with a structural member, transferring its **kinetic energy** along with the work done by gravity during the fall. The classic example is a weight dropped from a certain height onto the component being designed.

In automotive design, these loads govern components such as **connecting rods** (combustion pressure spikes), **valve springs** (camshaft impact), **piston pins**, **crankshafts** (torsional shock during firing), and **chassis members** (stone impacts).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a couch across a room:

- **Gradual Loading** — You push gently, the couch moves smoothly. The force builds up over seconds.
- **Sudden (Shock) Loading** — Someone yanks the couch suddenly while you're not ready. Your arms feel a sudden jolt — the same weight, but a much higher effective force.
- **Impact Loading** — A heavy box falls from a shelf onto the couch. The couch not only carries the box's weight but also absorbs the energy of the fall.

> [!TIP]
> **The Key Insight:** The *internal force* in a member is not just equal to the applied load — it is proportional to the **energy the load can deliver**. The faster you apply it, the less time the member has to stretch and dissipate energy, and the higher the internal stress becomes.

## 1.3 Three Reference Loading Cases (KTU Standard Classification)

The KTU 2024 scheme formally recognises three reference cases against which all dynamic problems are benchmarked:

| Case | Description | Typical Engineering Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gradual (Static)** | Load applied from zero to full value without acceleration | Hydraulic press stamping slowly |
| **Sudden (Shock)** | Load applied from zero to full value *instantaneously*, with zero initial velocity | Engagement of a clutch at standstill |
| **Impact** | A moving body of weight W collides from a height h | Drop-hammer forging, drop test of a chassis component |

## 1.4 Physical Constants Used Throughout

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Use |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** | Converts mass (kg) to weight (N): W = mg |
| Modulus of Elasticity (Steel) | **E** | **2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm² (210 GPa)** | Relates stress and strain in the elastic region |
| Strain energy density | **u** | **σ²/(2E)** | Energy stored per unit volume |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle: Strain Energy Method

The unified analytical framework for **all** dynamic loading problems in the KTU syllabus is the **Law of Conservation of Energy** applied between the instant of load application and the instant of maximum deformation:

$$
\text{Work done by external loads} = \text{Strain energy stored in the member}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation, when applied to each loading case, generates the entire family of dynamic stress formulas. The *only thing that changes* between cases is the **external work term** on the left-hand side.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Strain-Energy Method

- **Step 1 — Identify the loading case.** Is the load gradual, sudden, or impact?
- **Step 2 — Write the external work equation.** Include the work done *during* the deformation.
  - Gradual: external force is zero when $\delta = 0$ and grows linearly to P. Average force = P/2.
  - Sudden: full load P is already acting from the start. Work = P × $\delta_d$.
  - Impact: weight W falls through (h + $\delta_d$). Work = W(h + $\delta_d$).
- **Step 3 — Write the strain energy.** For a uniform bar of length L, area A, modulus E:
  - $U = \dfrac{\sigma_d^2}{2E} \times A \times L$
- **Step 4 — Equate work and strain energy.** Solve for the unknown $\sigma_d$.
- **Step 5 — Introduce the static stress** $\sigma_s = P/A$ to obtain the **load factor (impact factor)**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Loading Case | External Work Equation | Maximum Dynamic Stress $\sigma_d$ | Impact Factor $K$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | **Gradual (Static)** | $\dfrac{1}{2}P\,\delta$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}$ | **1** |
| 2 | **Sudden (no height)** | $P\,\delta_d$ | $\dfrac{2P}{A}$ | **2** |
| 3 | **Sudden + height $h$** | $P\left(h + \delta_d\right)$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{PL}}\right]$ | $1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{PL}}$ |
| 4 | **Impact from height $h$** | $W\left(h + \delta_d\right)$ | $\dfrac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]$ | $1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}$ |
| 5 | **Impact, large $h$** (approx.) | $W\,h$ (dominant) | $\dfrac{W}{A}\sqrt{\dfrac{2hAE}{WL}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2hAE}{WL}} \cdot \dfrac{W}{A}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:**
> - $P$ = applied static load (N), $W$ = weight of falling mass (N)
> - $A$ = cross-sectional area (mm²), $L$ = length of bar (mm)
> - $E$ = Young's Modulus (N/mm²), $h$ = height of fall (mm)
> - $\sigma_s = P/A$, $\delta_s = PL/AE$ = static deflection
> - $\sigma_d$ = **maximum dynamic stress** during the loading event

## 2.4 Derivation of the "Impact Factor"

A cleaner form of the impact case is obtained by substituting $\delta_s = PL/(AE)$:

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_s \cdot (W/P)}}\right]
$$

For the **common KTU sub-case** where $W = P$ (impact body is itself the load):

$$
\sigma_d = \sigma_s \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_s}}\right]
$$

## 2.5 Practical Significance in Automotive Design

- **Factor of Safety under Shock:** KTU design problems typically use a **higher factor of safety (n ≈ 2 to 4)** for shock/impact-loaded components versus static (n ≈ 1.5 to 2.5), because the analytical formulas assume uniform stress — real components have stress concentrations.
- **Modulus matters:** Since $E$ appears inside the radical, **stiffer materials experience *higher* dynamic stresses** for the same impact energy. This is why rubber mounts and bumpers use **low-E materials** — they absorb energy through large deflection rather than high stress.
- **Local deformation correction:** Marin's equation modifies the simple formula by replacing $h$ with $h + \delta$ and dividing by an **effective stress concentration factor**, accounting for localised indentation at the contact point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Sudden Loading Formula ($\sigma_d = 2P/A$)

### Setup
A vertical bar of length $L$, area $A$, modulus $E$ hangs from a rigid ceiling. A load $P$ is applied instantaneously at the lower end. Let $\delta_d$ be the dynamic elongation and $\sigma_d$ the corresponding dynamic stress.

### Step 1: External Work Done
Since the full load P is acting throughout the deformation from $\delta = 0$ to $\delta = \delta_d$, the external work is:

$$
W_{ext} = P \cdot \delta_d
$$

### Step 2: Strain Energy Stored
For a uniform bar stressed uniformly up to $\sigma_d$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \varepsilon_d \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_d^2}{E} \cdot A \cdot L
$$

### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Energy

$$
P \cdot \delta_d = \frac{\sigma_d^2 \cdot A \cdot L}{2E}
$$

### Step 4: Hooke's Law for Dynamic Deformation

$$
\delta_d = \frac{\sigma_d \cdot L}{E}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute $\delta_d$ into the Energy Equation

$$
P \cdot \frac{\sigma_d \cdot L}{E} = \frac{\sigma_d^2 \cdot A \cdot L}{2E}
$$

### Step 6: Cancel $L/E$ from Both Sides

$$
P \cdot \sigma_d = \frac{\sigma_d^2 \cdot A}{2}
$$

### Step 7: Solve for $\sigma_d$

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{2P}{A} = 2\sigma_{static}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** A sudden load produces **exactly twice** the static stress. This is a board-exam favourite and must be memorised.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Impact Loading Formula (Weight W Falling from Height h)

### Setup
A weight W strikes a vertical bar of length $L$, area $A$ from a height $h$. The bar compresses by $\delta_d$ and the dynamic stress is $\sigma_d$.

### Step 1: External Work Done by Weight
The weight W falls through a total distance of $h + \delta_d$ (initial drop + bar's compression):

$$
W_{ext} = W \cdot (h + \delta_d)
$$

### Step 2: Strain Energy Stored in the Bar
Same as before:

$$
U = \frac{\sigma_d^2 \cdot A \cdot L}{2E}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Conservation of Energy

$$
W(h + \delta_d) = \frac{\sigma_d^2 \cdot A \cdot L}{2E}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $\delta_d = \sigma_d L / E$

$$
W \cdot h + W \cdot \frac{\sigma_d L}{E} = \frac{\sigma_d^2 \cdot A \cdot L}{2E}
$$

### Step 5: Multiply Throughout by $2E$

$$
2WhE + 2W \sigma_d L = \sigma_d^2 \cdot A \cdot L
$$

### Step 6: Rearrange into Standard Quadratic Form

$$
\sigma_d^2 \cdot A \cdot L - 2W L \cdot \sigma_d - 2WhE = 0
$$

### Step 7: Apply the Quadratic Formula
This is a quadratic in $\sigma_d$. Taking the positive root (stress cannot be negative):

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{2WL + \sqrt{(2WL)^2 + 4 \cdot AL \cdot 2WhE}}{2 \cdot AL}
$$

### Step 8: Simplify the Discriminant

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{2WL + \sqrt{4W^2 L^2 + 8AWLhE}}{2AL} = \frac{2WL + 2L\sqrt{W^2 + \dfrac{2WhEA}{L}}}{2AL}
$$

### Step 9: Factor out $W$ inside the radical and divide by $2AL$

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (Impact Formula):**
> $$\boxed{\;\sigma_d = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]\;}$$
> This is the **single most important equation** in the shock and impact module.

### Step 10: Sanity Check — Set h = 0
For pure sudden loading without any drop height, set $h = 0$:

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 0}\right] = \frac{W}{A}[1 + 1] = \frac{2W}{A} \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 11: Large-h Approximation
For $h \gg \delta_s$, the "1" inside the square root becomes negligible:

$$
\sigma_d \approx \frac{W}{A}\sqrt{\frac{2hAE}{WL}} = \sqrt{\frac{2hAE}{WL}} \cdot \frac{W}{A}
$$

This reduces to a direct proportionality between $\sigma_d$ and $\sqrt{h}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Sudden Load Combined with Initial Height h

The general case: a load P is applied suddenly *after* falling a small height $h$ (e.g., a slack chain pulled taut). External work is $P(h + \delta_d)$:

$$
P(h + \delta_d) = \frac{\sigma_d^2 A L}{2E}, \quad \delta_d = \frac{\sigma_d L}{E}
$$

Following identical algebraic manipulation (identical to Section 3.2 with W replaced by P):

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{P}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2hAE}{PL}}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that when $h = 0$, this correctly degenerates to $\sigma_d = 2P/A$, the pure sudden-load result.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Apply?

| Given in Problem | Formula to Use | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Load P applied gradually | $\sigma_d = P/A$ | Trivial static case |
| Load P suddenly, $h = 0$ | $\sigma_d = 2P/A$ | Apply factor of safety |
| Load P suddenly, height $h$ | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{P}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{PL}}\right]$ | Slack-chain / pulley case |
| Weight W drops from height $h$ | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]$ | Most common KTU question |
| Given $\delta_s$, find dynamic | $\sigma_d = \sigma_s\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_s}}\right]$ | Use when $W = P$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Loading Case Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: External Load Applied] --> B{Is there a Drop Height h greater than 0?}
    B -- No --> C{Is the Load Applied Instantaneously?}
    B -- Yes --> D{Is the Falling Mass the Same as the Applied Load?}
    C -- No --> E[Gradual Loading Case]
    C -- Yes --> F[Sudden Loading Case]
    D -- Yes --> G[Impact Loading with Weight W]
    D -- No --> H[Sudden Load Plus Initial Drop]
    E --> I[sigma d equals P over A]
    F --> J[sigma d equals 2P over A]
    G --> K[sigma d equals W over A times one plus sqrt one plus 2hAE over WL]
    H --> L[sigma d equals P over A times one plus sqrt one plus 2hAE over PL]
    I --> M[Apply Factor of Safety]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Design Check]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Conservation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ExtWork[External Work Done by Load] --> EnergyEq[Conservation of Energy Equation]
    StrainEnergy[Strain Energy Stored in Bar] --> EnergyEq
    EnergyEq --> Quadratic[Solve Quadratic for sigma d]
    Quadratic --> PositiveRoot[Take Positive Root]
    PositiveRoot --> DynamicStress[Maximum Dynamic Stress sigma d]
    DynamicStress --> SafetyCheck[Compare sigma d with Allowable Stress]
    SafetyCheck --> DesignOK[Design Acceptable]
    SafetyCheck --> DesignFail[Redesign: Increase A or Reduce L]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Typical Impact Problem Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User/Designer
    participant P as Problem Statement
    participant E as Energy Equation
    participant Q as Quadratic Solver
    participant V as Verification Step
    U->>P: Identify W, h, A, L, E
    P->>E: Write W times h plus delta d equals sigma d squared AL over 2E
    E->>E: Substitute delta d equals sigma d L over E
    E->>Q: Rearrange to quadratic in sigma d
    Q->>V: Compute positive root
    V->>U: Return sigma d and impact factor K
    U->>U: Compare with allowable stress times factor of safety
```

## 4.4 Schematic Representation of the Three Loading Cases

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph StaticCase[Gradual Loading Case]
        S1[Rigid Support] --- S2[Bar of Length L and Area A]
        S2 --- S3[Load P Increases Linearly from 0 to P]
    end
    subgraph SuddenCase[Sudden Loading Case]
        SU1[Rigid Support] --- SU2[Bar of Length L and Area A]
        SU2 --- SU3[Full Load P Applied Instantaneously]
    end
    subgraph ImpactCase[Impact Loading Case]
        I1[Rigid Support] --- I2[Bar of Length L and Area A]
        I2 --- I3[Weight W Falls from Height h]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define shock loading and impact loading. How do they differ in the manner in which energy is transferred to the component?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Shock Loading (1.5 marks):** Shock loading is a condition in which a load is applied to a machine member **instantaneously** such that the member does not have sufficient time to deform gradually. The defining feature is that the full magnitude of the load acts from the very beginning of the deformation event, and the energy is delivered through the *work done by the load during deformation*. A typical example is the engagement of a clutch at standstill.

**Impact Loading (1.5 marks):** Impact loading occurs when a moving mass collides with the member. Here, the energy delivered equals the **kinetic energy of the moving mass** *plus* the *work done by gravity during the fall*. Impact loading is therefore more severe than pure shock loading because the moving body carries additional kinetic energy. A drop-hammer forging operation is a textbook example.

**Key Difference:** In shock loading, energy is transferred only via the displacement of the applied load; in impact loading, energy is transferred additionally via the kinetic energy of the falling mass.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**A vertical bar is subjected to a load that is applied suddenly. Show that the maximum stress induced is twice the static stress.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consider a bar of length $L$, area $A$, modulus $E$ subjected to a sudden load $P$ (1 mark).

Energy equation (1 mark):
$$
P \cdot \delta_d = \frac{\sigma_d^2 \cdot A \cdot L}{2E}
$$

Substitute $\delta_d = \sigma_d L / E$ and simplify (0.5 marks):

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{2P}{A} = 2\sigma_{static}
$$

Since $\sigma_{static} = P/A$, we have $\sigma_d = 2\sigma_{static}$, which proves the result (0.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**A copper rod of length 1.2 m and cross-sectional area 480 mm² hangs vertically from a rigid support. A weight of 5 kN is dropped onto a flange at the bottom of the rod from a height of 80 mm. Determine:**

**(a)** The maximum stress induced in the rod, the maximum elongation, and the impact factor.  
**(b)** If a factor of safety of 5 is required, check whether the design is safe. Take $E = 1.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² for copper.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Length $L = 1.2$ m $= 1200$ mm
- Area $A = 480$ mm²
- Weight $W = 5$ kN $= 5000$ N
- Height $h = 80$ mm
- $E = 1.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Static Stress (for reference)**

$$
\sigma_s = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{5000}{480} = 10.417 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Impact Formula (3 marks)**

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]
$$

**Step 3: Compute the term under the radical**

$$
\frac{2hAE}{WL} = \frac{2 \times 80 \times 480 \times 1.0 \times 10^5}{5000 \times 1200}
$$

$$
= \frac{7.68 \times 10^9}{6.0 \times 10^6} = 1280
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and Solve (2 marks)**

$$
\sigma_d = 10.417 \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 1280}\right] = 10.417\left[1 + \sqrt{1281}\right]
$$

$$
\sqrt{1281} \approx 35.79
$$

$$
\sigma_d = 10.417 \times [1 + 35.79] = 10.417 \times 36.79 \approx 383.2 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5: Maximum Elongation (0.5 mark)**

$$
\delta_d = \frac{\sigma_d L}{E} = \frac{383.2 \times 1200}{1.0 \times 10^5} = 4.598 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Impact Factor (0.5 mark)**

$$
K = \frac{\sigma_d}{\sigma_s} = \frac{383.2}{10.417} \approx 36.79
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Allowable Stress (2 marks)**

For copper, ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u \approx 200$ to 400 N/mm² (assume $\sigma_u = 220$ N/mm² as standard).

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_u}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{220}{5} = 44 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Comparison (2 marks)**

$$
\sigma_d = 383.2 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{vs} \quad \sigma_{allow} = 44 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Conclusion (1 mark)**

Since $\sigma_d = 383.2 \text{ N/mm}^2 \gg \sigma_{allow} = 44 \text{ N/mm}^2$, **the design is NOT safe**. The rod will fail.

**Step 4: Redesign Suggestion (2 marks)**

To make the design safe, we require $\sigma_d \leq 44$ N/mm². For very large $h$ (here $h/\delta_s$ is huge), use the approximation:

$$
\sigma_d \approx \frac{W}{A}\sqrt{\frac{2hAE}{WL}} = \sqrt{\frac{2WhE}{LA}}
$$

Setting $\sigma_d = 44$ and solving for $A$:

$$
44 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 80 \times 1.0 \times 10^5}{1200 \times A}}
$$

$$
44^2 = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{10}}{1200 \times A}
$$

$$
A = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{10}}{1200 \times 1936} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{10}}{2.323 \times 10^6} \approx 34{,}434 \text{ mm}^2
$$

A rod of cross-section **about 34,000 mm²** (roughly 185 mm × 185 mm square) would be required — clearly, the original design needs to be drastically revised.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**A steel hoist rope of length 8 m and diameter 24 mm is used to lift a load of 20 kN. The rope is initially slack and the load falls freely through 60 mm before the rope becomes taut. Determine:**

**(a)** The maximum instantaneous stress and elongation in the rope.  
**(b)** Compare this with the static stress and comment on the design implications. Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² for steel.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Length $L = 8$ m $= 8000$ mm
- Diameter $d = 24$ mm, so area $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(24)^2 = 452.4$ mm²
- Load $P = 20$ kN $= 20{,}000$ N
- Drop height $h = 60$ mm
- $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Identify the Loading Case (1 mark)**

This is a **sudden load with initial drop** case (slack-rope problem), since the load falls through 60 mm *before* the rope picks up the load. The relevant formula is:

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{P}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2hAE}{PL}}\right]
$$

**Step 2: Static Stress (1 mark)**

$$
\sigma_s = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{20{,}000}{452.4} = 44.21 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Term Under the Radical (2 marks)**

$$
\frac{2hAE}{PL} = \frac{2 \times 60 \times 452.4 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{20{,}000 \times 8000}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.0858 \times 10^{10}}{1.6 \times 10^8} = 67.86
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Dynamic Stress (1 mark)**

$$
\sigma_d = 44.21 \left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 67.86}\right] = 44.21\left[1 + \sqrt{68.86}\right]
$$

$$
\sqrt{68.86} \approx 8.298
$$

$$
\sigma_d = 44.21 \times [1 + 8.298] = 44.21 \times 9.298 \approx 411.0 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5: Maximum Elongation (1 mark)**

$$
\delta_d = \frac{\sigma_d \cdot L}{E} = \frac{411.0 \times 8000}{2.0 \times 10^5} = 16.44 \text{ mm}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compare Stresses (2 marks)**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Static stress | 44.21 N/mm² |
| Dynamic stress | 411.0 N/mm² |
| **Impact Factor** | **9.30** |

The dynamic stress is roughly **9.3 times** the static stress.

**Step 2: Comment on the Design (2 marks)**

For mild steel, $\sigma_u \approx 410$ to 460 N/mm². The computed dynamic stress of **411 N/mm² is dangerously close to the ultimate strength** — there is virtually no safety margin. Under normal lifting, the rope is highly stressed; any slack in the hoist mechanism will precipitate failure.

**Step 3: Design Implications (3 marks)**

- A hoist rope must always be kept **taut** during operation, and the slack-take-up mechanism must be regularly maintained.
- A **higher factor of safety (5 to 6)** must be adopted for hoist ropes compared to ordinary tension members.
- A **shock absorber or spring-loaded tensioning device** can be used to reduce the effective drop height $h$.
- Increasing rope diameter drastically increases $A$ in the radical denominator — a larger rope reduces $\sigma_d$ but at the cost of self-weight.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong formula chosen** — Students often confuse the "sudden load" formula ($\sigma_d = 2P/A$) with the "impact" formula. **If the problem states a height $h > 0$, you must use the impact formula, not $2P/A$.**
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — Convert $h$, $L$, $A$, $E$ to **consistent units (mm and N/mm²)** before plugging into the formula. Mixing metres and millimetres is a guaranteed mark-loss.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of safety** — KTU Part B questions almost always require a safety check. Quoting only $\sigma_d$ without comparing it to $\sigma_{allow}$ loses 1 to 2 marks.
> 4. **Taking the negative root of the quadratic** — The negative root gives a meaningless negative stress. Always write **"taking the positive root since stress is compressive/tensile in nature."**
> 5. **Wrong value of g** — Use **$g = 9.81$ m/s²** when converting mass to weight. Using $g = 10$ m/s² is acceptable but should be stated as an approximation.
> 6. **Ignoring self-weight of the bar** — In KTU problems, self-weight of the member is usually neglected unless explicitly mentioned. Do not introduce it spontaneously.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise:**

- [ ] **Three reference cases:** Gradual, Sudden, Impact — and the *exact* formula for each.
- [ ] **Sudden load result:** $\sigma_d = 2P/A$ — **exactly double** the static stress.
- [ ] **Impact formula:** $\sigma_d = \dfrac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}\right]$ — the *single most important equation* in the module.
- [ ] **Large-h approximation:** $\sigma_d \approx \dfrac{W}{A}\sqrt{\dfrac{2hAE}{WL}}$ — valid when $h \gg \delta_s$.
- [ ] **Energy conservation principle:** External work = Strain energy stored. This is the *only* tool required.
- [ ] **Consistent units:** Use mm for $L$, $h$, $\delta$; N/mm² for $E$, $\sigma$; N for $P$, $W$.
- [ ] **Strain energy density:** $u = \sigma^2/(2E)$ — useful for 3-mark derivation sub-parts.
- [ ] **Impact factor $K = \sigma_d/\sigma_s$** — quote it explicitly in every numerical answer.
- [ ] **Higher factor of safety** (≈ 2 to 4) is used for shock/impact-loaded components.
- [ ] **Slack-chain / hoist-rope problem:** use the "sudden load + height" variant: $\sigma_d = \dfrac{P}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2hAE}{PL}}\right]$.
- [ ] **Sanity check:** when $h = 0$, the impact formula must reduce to the sudden-load formula $\sigma_d = 2P/A$. Always verify this in the exam.
- [ ] **Design modification logic:** to reduce $\sigma_d$ — increase $A$ (largest effect, square-root inside radical), reduce $h$ (use snubbers/buffers), reduce $L$ (stiffer structure), or use lower-$E$ material (rubber mounts).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fatigue Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Fatigue Loading

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue Loading** is defined as the application of **dynamic, fluctuating, or cyclic stresses** to a machine element or structural component, which ultimately causes failure of the material at stress levels **significantly below** its static yield strength or ultimate tensile strength.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMUT601)**, fatigue loading refers to the time-varying stress history experienced by critical automotive components such as **crankshafts, connecting rods, suspension springs, leaf springs, axle shafts, valve springs, and gears**. These components are subjected to millions of stress cycles during their service life, making fatigue analysis a non-negotiable design criterion.

### Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> **Fatigue** is the progressive, localized structural damage caused by cyclic loading at stress amplitudes lower than the static strength of the material. The failure occurs due to the **initiation, propagation, and final fracture** of microscopic cracks over a period of time.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Paperclip Test:**
> Take a standard metal paperclip and bend it back and forth repeatedly. On the first bend, nothing visible happens. After perhaps 8–10 bends, you hear a faint *crack* — a tiny crack has initiated on the outer surface where the bending stress is highest. After another few bends, the crack grows larger, and eventually, the paperclip snaps cleanly in two.
>
> **Key Insight:** The paperclip never experienced a stress higher than its yield strength. It failed because of **repeated, low-magnitude cyclic loading**. The applied stress was small, but the **number of cycles** was large — and the **localized damage accumulated** until catastrophic failure. This is *exactly* what happens in crankshafts, connecting rods, and automotive springs.

### Why Fatigue Loading is Critical in Automotive Design

- Over **90% of all mechanical failures** in automotive components are attributed to **fatigue**.
- A connecting rod may experience **~3000 stress cycles per minute** at 3000 RPM in a typical passenger car engine.
- A standard vehicle axle undergoes **~10⁸ to 10⁹ stress cycles** over its 10-year design life.
- Standard static strength calculations (using yield or ultimate stress) are **inadequate** and dangerously optimistic for such components.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Endurance Limit (Se):** The maximum completely reversed bending stress that a material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (conventionally taken as **N = 10⁶ or 3×10⁶ cycles** for steels). The value of Se is approximately **0.5 × Sut** for ferrous metals.
- **Fatigue Strength (Sf):** The maximum cyclic stress a material can withstand for a **finite, specified number of cycles** (typically N = 10³, 10⁴, 10⁵).
- **Fatigue Life (Nf):** The total number of stress cycles to failure at a given stress level.
- **Basquin's Exponent (b):** Typically lies between **-0.05 and -0.12** for most steels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fatigue Loading (Cyclic Stress Patterns)

A completely reversed stress cycle is characterized by equal magnitudes of tension and compression. In reality, automotive components experience a wide spectrum of cyclic stress patterns:

| Type | Description | Engineering Example |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Fully Reversed** | σmax = −σmin (zero mean stress) | Rotating shaft in pure bending |
| **Repeated (Pulsating)** | σmin = 0 (mean = amplitude) | Press-fit operations, notched bar in tension |
| **Fluctuating (Asymmetric)** | σmax ≠ σmin ≠ 0 (non-zero mean) | Crankshaft, connecting rod, leaf spring |
| **Random / Variable Amplitude** | Irregular, stochastic history | Vehicle suspension on rough road |

## 2.2 Fundamental Parameters of a Cyclic Stress

For any fluctuating stress cycle, four key parameters define the loading:

1. **Maximum Stress:** σmax = σm + σa
2. **Minimum Stress:** σmin = σm − σa
3. **Mean Stress:** σm = (σmax + σmin) / 2
4. **Stress Amplitude:** σa = (σmax − σmin) / 2

Additional derived parameters used extensively in KTU problems:
- **Stress Range:** σr = σmax − σmin = 2σa
- **Amplitude Ratio:** A = σa / σm
- **Stress Ratio (R):** R = σmin / σmax

## 2.3 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)

The **Stress-Number of cycles (S-N) curve** is the fundamental graphical tool for fatigue analysis. It is plotted on a **semi-logarithmic scale** (log N on the abscissa, linear σ on the ordinate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - For **ferrous metals (steels)**: S-N curve becomes **horizontal** at N = 10⁶ cycles, defining the **Endurance Limit (Se)**.
> - For **non-ferrous metals (aluminium, copper alloys)**: S-N curve **continues to drop** — no true endurance limit exists. A **Fatigue Strength at 5×10⁸ or 10⁹ cycles** is used as a design reference.

## 2.4 Basquin's Equation (The Core S-N Equation)

For the finite-life region (10³ < N < 10⁶), the S-N relationship is mathematically expressed as:

$$\sigma_{a} = \sigma'_{f} \left(2N\right)^{b}$$

Where:
- σ'a = fatigue strength coefficient (≈ Sut for steels)
- b = fatigue strength exponent (Basquin's exponent, negative)
- N = number of cycles to failure

## 2.5 Endurance Limit Modifying Factors (Marin Factors)

The theoretical endurance limit (Se') obtained from test specimens must be modified for real-world automotive components. The modified endurance limit is:

$$S_{e} = k_{a} \cdot k_{b} \cdot k_{c} \cdot k_{d} \cdot k_{e} \cdot k_{f} \cdot S'_{e}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **steels**: S'e = 0.5 × Sut (when Sut < 1400 MPa)
> For **non-ferrous metals**: S'e is taken from the S-N curve at the specified design life.

## 2.6 Mean Stress Correction Theories

When the mean stress is non-zero (the common automotive case), the allowable stress amplitude must be reduced. Three classical theories are used:

| Theory | Equation | Failure Locus | Conservatism |
|--------|----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Soderberg** | σa/Se + σm/Sy = 1/n | Most conservative, uses Yield | Highest safety |
| **Goodman** | σa/Se + σm/Sut = 1/n | Straight line to Sut | Moderately conservative |
| **Gerber** | (σa/Se) + (σm/Sut)² = 1/n | Parabolic curve | Least conservative (closer to experimental) |

Where **n** is the factor of safety.

## 2.7 Cumulative Fatigue Damage — Miner's Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Palmgren-Miner Linear Damage Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the cumulative damage fraction reaches unity.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_{i}}{N_{i}} = 1$$

Where:
- ni = number of stress cycles applied at stress level i
- Ni = number of cycles to failure at stress level i (from S-N curve)

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | σm = (σmax + σmin) / 2 | Mean stress | MPa |
| 2 | σa = (σmax − σmin) / 2 | Stress amplitude | MPa |
| 3 | R = σmin / σmax | Stress ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | σa = σ'f (2N)^b | Basquin's S-N equation | MPa |
| 5 | Se = ka·kb·kc·kd·ke·kf·S'e | Modified endurance limit | MPa |
| 6 | ka = a·Sut^b (Shigley) | Surface factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | kb = 0.879·d^(-0.107) | Size factor (d in mm) | dimensionless |
| 8 | kc = 1 (bending/torsion), 0.85 (axial) | Load factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | kd = 1 (T < 450°C), else reduce | Temperature factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | ke = 0.814 (90%), 0.753 (99%) | Reliability factor | dimensionless |
| 11 | kf = 1/(1 − q(Kt − 1)) | Notch sensitivity factor | dimensionless |
| 12 | σa/Se + σm/Sut = 1/n | Goodman line | dimensionless |
| 13 | σa/Se + (σm/Sut)² = 1/n | Gerber parabola | dimensionless |
| 14 | σa/Se + σm/Sy = 1/n | Soderberg line | dimensionless |
| 15 | Σ(ni/Ni) = 1 | Miner's cumulative damage | dimensionless |

> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:** The Goodman criterion is the **industry standard for automotive crankshafts and connecting rods** because of its simplicity and reasonable conservatism. Gerber is used when **weight optimization is critical** (e.g., Formula 1 connecting rods). Soderberg is reserved for **ultra-conservative designs** like pressure vessels and nuclear components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fundamental Cyclic Stress Parameters

**Given:** σmax and σmin for any general fluctuating stress cycle.

**Step 1 — Express σmax and σmin in terms of mean and amplitude:**

By definition of the mean stress, the stress oscillates symmetrically about σm. Therefore:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_{m} + \sigma_{a}$$

$$\sigma_{\min} = \sigma_{m} - \sigma_{a}$$

**Step 2 — Add the two equations to solve for σm:**

$$\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min} = 2\sigma_{m}$$

$$\sigma_{m} = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations to solve for σa:**

$$\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min} = 2\sigma_{a}$$

$$\sigma_{a} = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the stress ratio R:**

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}}$$

**Step 5 — Express stress amplitude in terms of mean and R:**

Substituting σmax = σm + σa and σmin = R·σmax = R(σm + σa):

$$R(\sigma_{m} + \sigma_{a}) = \sigma_{m} - \sigma_{a}$$

$$R\sigma_{m} + R\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{m} - \sigma_{a}$$

$$R\sigma_{a} + \sigma_{a} = \sigma_{m} - R\sigma_{m}$$

$$\sigma_{a}(1 + R) = \sigma_{m}(1 - R)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{m} \cdot \frac{1 - R}{1 + R}}$$

This is the canonical relationship used in every KTU Part B numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation: Basquin's Equation (S-N Curve)

**Given:** Two known points on the S-N curve:
- (N₁, σa₁) = (10³, σa at 10³ cycles ≈ 0.9·Sut)
- (N₂, σa₂) = (10⁶, σa at 10⁶ cycles = Se)

**Step 1 — Apply the power-law form:**

$$\sigma_{a1} = \sigma'_{f}(2N_{1})^{b}$$

$$\sigma_{a2} = \sigma'_{f}(2N_{2})^{b}$$

**Step 2 — Take the ratio to eliminate σ'f:**

$$\frac{\sigma_{a1}}{\sigma_{a2}} = \left(\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}\right)^{b}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma_{a1}}{\sigma_{a2}}\right) = b \cdot \ln\left(\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the fatigue strength exponent b:**

$$\boxed{b = \frac{\log(\sigma_{a1}/\sigma_{a2})}{\log(N_{1}/N_{2})}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the fatigue strength coefficient σ'f:**

Substituting back into the first equation:

$$\sigma'_{f} = \frac{\sigma_{a1}}{(2N_{1})^{b}}$$

**Numerical Verification (typical KTU problem values):**
- σa1 = 0.9 × 600 = 540 MPa at N1 = 10³
- σa2 = 0.5 × 600 = 300 MPa at N2 = 10⁶

$$b = \frac{\log(540/300)}{\log(10^{3}/10^{6})} = \frac{\log(1.8)}{-3} = \frac{0.2553}{-3} = -0.0851$$

$$\sigma'_{f} = \frac{540}{(2 \times 10^{3})^{-0.0851}} = \frac{540}{(2000)^{-0.0851}} = 898.4 \text{ MPa}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Goodman Mean Stress Correction

**Given:** For infinite life, σa ≤ Se when σm = 0 (fully reversed).
For static loading, σm ≤ Sut when σa = 0.

**Step 1 — Define the failure envelope as a straight line in the σm-σa plane:**

A linear relationship between σm and σa at the boundary of safe design gives:

$$\frac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the boundary conditions:**

- When σm = 0: σa/Se = 1/n → σa = Se/n ✓ (matches fully reversed condition)
- When σa = 0: σm/Sut = 1/n → σm = Sut/n ✓ (matches static condition)

**Step 3 — Solve for allowable σa at any given σm:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{a} = \frac{S_{e}}{n}\left[1 - \left(\frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}\right)\right]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to factor of safety form (for given σm and σa):**

$$n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Gerber Parabolic Equation

**Given:** Experimental data shows the actual fatigue boundary is parabolic, not linear.

**Step 1 — Propose a parabolic relationship:**

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}}\right) + \left(\frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the allowable stress amplitude:**

$$\sigma_{a} = \frac{S_{e}}{n}\left[1 - \left(\frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Form the factor of safety equation:**

For given applied σa and σm, the factor of safety n satisfies:

$$\frac{n^{2}\sigma_{a}^{2}}{S_{e}^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}\sigma_{m}^{2}}{S_{ut}^{2}} + \frac{2n\sigma_{a}\sigma_{m}^{2}}{S_{e}S_{ut}^{2}} - 1 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Practical simplification (KTU board uses this form):**

$$\boxed{n = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{S_{ut}}{S_{e}}\right)\left[-1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2S_{e}\sigma_{a}}{S_{ut}\sigma_{m}}\right)^{2}}\right]}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule

**Given:** A component is subjected to k different stress levels. At each level i, the component survives Ni cycles and is subjected to ni cycles.

**Step 1 — Define damage fraction at level i:**

If the entire life Ni corresponds to a damage of 1, then one cycle causes damage:

$$D_{i} = \frac{1}{N_{i}}$$

**Step 2 — Total damage from ni cycles at level i:**

$$D_{i}^{\text{total}} = \frac{n_{i}}{N_{i}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Palmgren-Miner linear superposition:**

The total accumulated damage is the sum of all individual damages:

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_{i}}{N_{i}} = 1}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

- If Σ(ni/Ni) < 1: The component is **safe** (under-utilized).
- If Σ(ni/Ni) = 1: **Failure** is expected.
- If Σ(ni/Ni) > 1: The component has **failed** before reaching the design spectrum.

**Numerical KTU Example:**
A shaft undergoes:
- 50,000 cycles at σa = 500 MPa → N1 = 100,000 cycles
- 30,000 cycles at σa = 400 MPa → N2 = 200,000 cycles
- 100,000 cycles at σa = 300 MPa → N3 = 500,000 cycles

$$\text{Damage} = \frac{50{,}000}{100{,}000} + \frac{30{,}000}{200{,}000} + \frac{100{,}000}{500{,}000}$$

$$\text{Damage} = 0.500 + 0.150 + 0.200 = 0.850$$

Since 0.850 < 1.0, the shaft is **safe** with a remaining life of **15%**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Theory

| Scenario | Recommended Theory | Reason |
|----------|--------------------|--------|
| Ductile steel, conservative automotive | **Goodman** | Linear, simple, industry-standard |
| Ductile material, weight-critical (F1) | **Gerber** | Matches experimental data best |
| Brittle material, ultra-conservative | **Soderberg** | Uses Sy as ultimate limit |
| Completely reversed loading (R = −1) | **Se directly** | Mean = 0, no correction needed |
| Variable amplitude loading | **Miner's Rule** | Cumulative damage analysis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fatigue Analysis Flow Diagram

The following block diagram maps the standard KTU-approved methodology for fatigue design of automotive components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Component and Loading] --> B[Determine Sut and Sy from Material Data]
    B --> C[Establish Stress Cycle sigma_max and sigma_min]
    C --> D[Compute Mean Stress sigma_m and Amplitude sigma_a]
    D --> E{Is Loading Fully Reversed R = -1?}
    E -- YES --> F[Use S-e directly with Marin Factors]
    E -- NO --> G[Apply Mean Stress Correction Goodman or Gerber]
    F --> H[Compute Modified Endurance Limit S-e]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Is Life Finite 10 to power 3 N 10 to power 6?}
    I -- YES --> J[Apply Basquins Equation for N cycles]
    I -- NO --> K{Infinite Life Required N greater than 10 to power 6?}
    K -- YES --> L[Use S-e as Allowable Stress Amplitude]
    J --> M[Compute Factor of Safety n]
    L --> M
    M --> N{n greater than or equal to Design Factor?}
    N -- YES --> O[DESIGN ACCEPTABLE]
    N -- NO --> P[ITERATE: Change Material, Geometry, or Surface Treatment]
    P --> B
    M --> Q{Variable Amplitude Loading?}
    Q -- YES --> R[Apply Miners Rule Sigma n_i slash N_i]
    R --> S{Damage less than 1.0?}
    S -- YES --> O
    S -- NO --> P
    Q -- NO --> N
```

## 4.2 S-N Curve Schematic Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    AX[Y axis Stress Amplitude sigma_a] --> P1[Region 1 Low Cycle N 10 to power 1 to 10 to power 3 Static Failure]
    P1 --> P2[Region 2 Finite Life N 10 to power 3 to 10 to power 6 Basquin Region]
    P2 --> P3[Region 3 Infinite Life N greater than 10 to power 6 Endurance Limit S-e]
    AX2[X axis Log N Cycles] --> P1
    AX2 --> P2
    AX2 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Mean Stress Correction Diagram (Haigh Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Origin sigma_a and sigma_m] --> A1[Point A Fully Reversed sigma_m equals 0 sigma_a equals S-e]
    O --> A2[Point B Static sigma_a equals 0 sigma_m equals S-ut]
    A1 --> A3[Goodman Line Straight from A to B]
    A1 --> A4[Gerber Curve Parabolic A to B]
    A1 --> A5[Soderberg Line Straight from A to Sy]
    A3 --> SAFE[Safe Region Below the Line]
    A4 --> SAFE
    A5 --> SAFE
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fatigue Design System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT MODULE]
        A1[Material Database Sut Sy]
        A2[Loading Spectrum sigma_max sigma_min]
        A3[Geometry Data Diameter Fillets]
        A4[Service Conditions Temperature Reliability]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[PROCESSING MODULE]
        B1[Cyclic Parameter Calculator]
        B2[Marin Factor Engine]
        B3[S-N Curve Interpolator]
        B4[Mean Stress Corrector]
        B5[Miner Damage Accumulator]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT MODULE]
        C1[Factor of Safety n]
        C2[Fatigue Life N-f]
        C3[Cumulative Damage Index]
        C4[Design Modification Report]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    A2 --> B5
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Fatigue Loading. Explain the significance of the Endurance Limit in the design of automotive components.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Fatigue loading is the application of **dynamic, fluctuating, or cyclic stresses** to a machine element, which causes failure at stress levels **below the material's yield strength**. The failure occurs through the **progressive initiation and propagation of cracks** over a large number of stress cycles.

**Significance of Endurance Limit (1.5 Marks):**
The endurance limit (Se) is the maximum completely reversed stress that a material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (N ≥ 10⁶) without failure. For steels, Se ≈ **0.5 × Sut**. Its significance in automotive design is:

1. It serves as the **fundamental design stress** for infinite-life components such as crankshafts, connecting rods, and axle shafts.
2. It allows designers to specify a safe operating stress that guarantees the component will **not fail due to cyclic loading** during its entire service life.
3. It forms the basis of the modified endurance limit (S_e = k_a · k_b · k_c · S_e'), accounting for real-world factors like surface finish, size, and reliability.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Soderberg, Goodman, and Gerber mean stress correction criteria.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Equation | Key Feature |
|-----------|----------|-------------|
| **Soderberg** | σa/Se + σm/Sy = 1/n | Uses yield strength Sy; most conservative |
| **Goodman** | σa/Se + σm/Sut = 1/n | Uses ultimate strength Sut; linear; industry standard |
| **Gerber** | σa/Se + (σm/Sut)² = 1/n | Parabolic; least conservative; matches ductile materials |

**Key Distinction:** Soderberg is the most conservative because it uses the **lower yield strength** as the failure criterion for the static portion. Goodman provides a **linear, simple, and moderately conservative** estimate, making it the **default choice in KTU problems and the automotive industry**. Gerber's parabolic curve best matches **experimental fatigue data for ductile metals** but is mathematically more complex.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A steel shaft has Sut = 600 MPa and Sy = 420 MPa. It is subjected to a fluctuating bending stress ranging from σmin = −150 MPa to σmax = 350 MPa. The shaft has a diameter of 50 mm and a machined surface finish. Using the **Goodman criterion** and Marin factors, determine the factor of safety for an **infinite life**. Assume the loading type is bending with 90% reliability. `(7 Marks)`

**(b)** Using **Basquin's equation**, find the fatigue life of the same shaft if the stress amplitude is increased to σa = 280 MPa with mean stress σm = 100 MPa. Assume the modified endurance limit is Se = 210 MPa. `(7 Marks)`

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute mean stress and amplitude (1 Mark)**

$$\sigma_{m} = \frac{350 + (-150)}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{a} = \frac{350 - (-150)}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute Marin factors (2.5 Marks)**

Theoretical endurance limit for steel:

$$S'_{e} = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 600 = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

- **Surface factor (machined):** ka = a × Sut^b = 4.51 × (600)^(−0.265) = 0.834
- **Size factor (d = 50 mm, 2.79 < d ≤ 51 mm):** kb = 1.51 × d^(−0.157) = 1.51 × (50)^(−0.157) = 0.827
- **Load factor (bending):** kc = 1.0
- **Temperature factor (T < 450°C):** kd = 1.0
- **Reliability factor (90%):** ke = 0.897

**Step 3: Compute modified endurance limit (1 Mark)**

$$S_{e} = 0.834 \times 0.827 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 300 = 185.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Apply Goodman criterion for factor of safety (2.5 Marks)**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_{a}}{S_{e}} + \frac{\sigma_{m}}{S_{ut}}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{250}{185.6} + \frac{100}{600} = 1.347 + 0.167 = 1.514$$

$$\boxed{n = \frac{1}{1.514} = 0.661}$$

Since **n < 1**, the shaft will **fail** under infinite life conditions. The design is inadequate.

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Basquin's exponent b (2 Marks)**

Using two reference points:
- (N₁ = 10³, σa1 = 0.9 × Sut = 0.9 × 600 = 540 MPa)
- (N₂ = 10⁶, σa2 = Se = 185.6 MPa)

$$b = \frac{\log(540/185.6)}{\log(10^{3}/10^{6})} = \frac{\log(2.910)}{-3} = \frac{0.4640}{-3} = -0.1547$$

**Step 2: Compute fatigue strength coefficient σ'f (2 Marks)**

$$\sigma'_{f} = \frac{\sigma_{a1}}{(2N_{1})^{b}} = \frac{540}{(2000)^{-0.1547}} = \frac{540}{0.3455} = 1563 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Apply mean stress correction (Goodman) to find equivalent fully reversed σa (1.5 Marks)**

$$\sigma_{ar} = \frac{\sigma_{a}}{1 - \sigma_{m}/S_{ut}} = \frac{280}{1 - 100/600} = \frac{280}{0.8333} = 336 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Solve for fatigue life N using Basquin (1.5 Marks)**

$$336 = 1563 \times (2N)^{-0.1547}$$

$$\left(\frac{336}{1563}\right) = (2N)^{-0.1547}$$

$$\log(0.2150) = -0.1547 \times \log(2N)$$

$$-0.6676 = -0.1547 \times \log(2N)$$

$$\log(2N) = 4.315$$

$$2N = 10^{4.315} = 20{,}658$$

$$\boxed{N = 10{,}329 \text{ cycles}}$$

The shaft has a finite fatigue life of approximately **10,329 cycles** under the increased loading.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Marin factors** used to modify the endurance limit of a machine element subjected to fatigue loading. For each factor, state its physical significance and typical numerical range. `(7 Marks)`

**(b)** A connecting rod is subjected to the following stress spectrum:
- 5,000 cycles at σmax = 500 MPa, σmin = 100 MPa
- 15,000 cycles at σmax = 400 MPa, σmin = 50 MPa
- 80,000 cycles at σmax = 300 MPa, σmin = 0 MPa

The S-N curve for the material is given by: σa = 1200 × (N)^(−0.10) MPa. Using **Miner's rule**, determine whether the connecting rod will survive. `(7 Marks)`

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step (7 Marks):**

The Marin factors account for the deviation of real engineering components from ideal laboratory test specimens. The modified endurance limit is:

$$S_{e} = k_{a} \cdot k_{b} \cdot k_{c} \cdot k_{d} \cdot k_{e} \cdot k_{f} \cdot S'_{e}$$

| Factor | Name | Physical Significance | Range |
|--------|------|----------------------|-------|
| **ka** | Surface factor | Accounts for **surface roughness** (machined, ground, polished). Higher roughness → lower fatigue life. | 0.4 – 1.0 |
| **kb** | Size factor | Larger components have a **higher probability of internal defects** and a steeper stress gradient. | 0.6 – 1.0 |
| **kc** | Load factor | Corrects for **type of loading** (bending, torsion, axial). | 0.85 – 1.0 |
| **kd** | Temperature factor | Elevated temperatures **reduce yield strength** and fatigue resistance. | 0.4 – 1.0 |
| **ke** | Reliability factor | Higher reliability requirement → **lower allowable stress**. | 0.753 – 1.0 |
| **kf** | Miscellaneous factor | Accounts for **residual stresses, corrosion, plating, and fretting**. | 0.5 – 1.0 |

**Step-by-step application in KTU problems (2 Marks demonstration):**

- Compute S'e from Sut: S'e = 0.5 × Sut (for steels).
- Look up each factor from standard Shigley/Mott tables using the given conditions.
- Multiply all factors with S'e to obtain Se.

This systematic approach ensures the modified endurance limit reflects the **actual operating conditions** of the automotive component.

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute stress amplitudes at each level (1.5 Marks)**

- Level 1: σa1 = (500 − 100)/2 = **200 MPa**
- Level 2: σa2 = (400 − 50)/2 = **175 MPa**
- Level 3: σa3 = (300 − 0)/2 = **150 MPa**

**Step 2: Compute cycles to failure Ni from S-N equation (2 Marks)**

Given: σa = 1200 × (N)^(−0.10)

Solving for N: N = (1200/σa)^(1/0.10) = (1200/σa)^10

- N1 = (1200/200)^10 = (6)^10 = 60,466,176 cycles
- N2 = (1200/175)^10 = (6.857)^10 = 195,308,925 cycles
- N3 = (1200/150)^10 = (8)^10 = 1,073,741,824 cycles

**Step 3: Apply Miner's Rule (2.5 Marks)**

$$\sum \frac{n_{i}}{N_{i}} = \frac{5{,}000}{60{,}466{,}176} + \frac{15{,}000}{195{,}308{,}925} + \frac{80{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824}$$

$$\sum = 8.27 \times 10^{-5} + 7.68 \times 10^{-5} + 7.45 \times 10^{-5} = 2.34 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\sum \frac{n_{i}}{N_{i}} = 0.000234 \ll 1.0}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 Mark)**

Since the cumulative damage index is **far less than 1.0**, the connecting rod will **easily survive** the applied spectrum. The component is significantly under-utilized, and either the **loading can be increased** or a **lighter, more economical material** can be selected to optimize the design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Marin Factor Errors:** Students frequently forget to multiply ALL six factors or use the wrong equation for size factor. For d in mm and d > 51 mm, the formula is kb = 0.91 × d^(−0.157) — NOT 0.879 × d^(−0.107) (which is for d in inches).
> 2. **Mean Stress Sign Convention:** Always ensure σm is computed with the **correct algebraic sign**. A negative σmin inverts the calculation completely.
> 3. **Goodman vs Gerber Confusion:** KTU most frequently asks Goodman. If the question does not specify, default to **Goodman**. Use Gerber only if explicitly asked.
> 4. **Endurance Limit for Non-Ferrous:** Do NOT apply Se = 0.5 × Sut for aluminium or copper alloys. They have **no true endurance limit** — use the S-N curve directly.
> 5. **Miner's Rule Threshold:** A damage index of 0.99 does NOT mean 99% safe. The linear hypothesis is **approximate** and has a typical scatter of ±30%.
> 6. **Units:** Always keep MPa consistent. Mixing ksi and MPa is the most common arithmetic error in KTU scripts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fatigue Loading**

- [ ] **Fatigue** = failure under **cyclic stress below yield strength**. Over 90% of automotive failures are fatigue-related.
- [ ] **Three components of cyclic stress:** mean stress (σm), stress amplitude (σa), and stress ratio (R = σmin/σmax).
- [ ] **Fully reversed loading** (R = −1): σa = Se, no mean stress correction needed.
- [ ] **Endurance limit Se ≈ 0.5 × Sut** for steels; non-ferrous metals have **no true Se**.
- [ ] **S-N curve** is plotted on semi-log scale; horizontal plateau for steels, continuously decreasing for aluminium.
- [ ] **Basquin's equation** governs the finite-life region: σa = σ'f (2N)^b.
- [ ] **Marin factors** must always be applied: Surface (ka), Size (kb), Load (kc), Temperature (kd), Reliability (ke), Miscellaneous (kf).
- [ ] **Mean stress corrections:** Goodman is the **default** for KTU. Use Gerber for weight-critical, Soderberg for ultra-conservative.
- [ ] **Goodman equation:** σa/Se + σm/Sut = 1/n → solve for n in factor-of-safety problems.
- [ ] **Miner's rule:** Σ(ni/Ni) = 1 at failure; safe if Σ < 1.
- [ ] **Cumulative damage < 1** ⇒ safe; **≥ 1** ⇒ failed.
- [ ] **Always quote units** in MPa; **always show step-by-step** Marin factor evaluation in answers.
- [ ] **For KTU Part B:** Internal choice is mandatory — practice BOTH Goodman-based and Miner's-rule-based numericals.
- [ ] **Common automotive fatigue-critical parts:** Crankshaft, connecting rod, leaf spring, valve spring, axle, gear tooth, piston pin, steering knuckle.
- [ ] **Surface treatment improves fatigue life:** Shot peening, carburizing, nitriding, induction hardening all increase the effective endurance limit by introducing compressive residual stresses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Endurance Limit Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Endurance Limit Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endurance Limit (Se)** is defined as the maximum completely reversed bending stress that a standard polished specimen of a material can withstand for an **infinite number of stress cycles (typically 10⁶ to 10⁸ cycles)** without failure.

For **ferrous metals** (e.g., steels), the endurance limit exists and is approximated as:

$$\sigma_e' \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_{ut}$$

For **non-ferrous metals** (e.g., aluminium, copper alloys), endurance limit does **not** truly exist. Instead, designers use the **Fatigue Strength** at a finite number of cycles (commonly $N = 5 \times 10^8$).

**Symbol Glossary (KTU Convention):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S_e'$ | Uncorrected (rotating beam) endurance limit | MPa |
| $S_e$ | Corrected endurance limit at the design point | MPa |
| $\sigma_{ut}$ | Ultimate Tensile Strength | MPa |
| $\sigma_{sy}$ | Yield Strength | MPa |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bending a Wire" Mental Model

Imagine you have a piece of **steel wire (paper clip)** in your hand. If you pull it straight with great force **once**, it may not break — but if you keep bending it back and forth (forward, then reverse, forward, then reverse...), even with a force far smaller than the breaking force, it eventually **snaps** at one bend.

This "snapping after many small bends" is called **fatigue failure**, and the stress level below which the wire could bend *forever* without breaking is its **Endurance Limit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** A shaft running at 1500 RPM does 75 reversals per second. In just **6 days of continuous running**, the shaft completes 10⁸ cycles. Without endurance limit consideration, this is a guaranteed failure waiting to happen!

---

### 1.3 Stress Cycles & Terminology

A fluctuating stress cycle is described by two key parameters:

- **Maximum Stress** ($\sigma_{max}$): Peak tensile stress in the cycle
- **Minimum Stress** ($\sigma_{min}$): Peak compressive (or lowest tensile) stress

From these, we derive:

**Mean Stress:**
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

**Stress Amplitude:**
$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

**Stress Ratio (R):**
$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Special Cases (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):
> - **Fully Reversed Cycle:** $R = -1$ → used to define the endurance limit itself
> - **Pulsating (Zero to Max):** $R = 0$
> - **Static:** $R = +1$
> - **Repeated (Tension-Tension):** $0 < R < +1$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Endurance Limit Must Be Corrected

The value $\sigma_e' = 0.5 \sigma_{ut}$ applies only to the **ideal Shigley's rotating-beam specimen** (polished, 8 mm diameter, room temperature, axial loading, 50% reliability).

A real automotive component — a **crankshaft, axle, leaf spring, connecting rod** — differs from this specimen in **six practical aspects**. Each aspect **reduces** the effective endurance limit, and is accounted for using **Marin's Modification Factors**.

---

### 2.2 The Marin Modification Equation (Most Tested Formula in KTU)

The corrected endurance limit at the critical point of a real machine element is:

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$

Where each factor is **individually ≤ 1** (since they all reduce the limit).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Factor | Symbol | Purpose | Governing Equation (Engineering Units) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Surface Finish | $k_a$ | Accounts for machining roughness | $k_a = a \cdot \sigma_{ut}^{\,b}$ |
| Size | $k_b$ | Larger volume → lower endurance | $k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$ (for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm) |
| Loading | $k_c$ | Bending vs Axial vs Torsion | $k_c = 1$ (bending), $0.85$ (axial), $0.59$ (torsion) |
| Temperature | $k_d$ | High-T operation | $k_d = \dfrac{S_T}{S_{RT}}$ (from charts) |
| Reliability | $k_e$ | Statistical scatter | $k_e = 0.897$ (90%), $0.868$ (95%), $0.814$ (99%) |
| Miscellaneous | $k_f$ | Residual stresses, corrosion, plating | $k_f$ = product of sub-factors |

**The Six Formulas In Detail:**

**1. Surface Finish Factor ($k_a$):**

$$k_a = a \, \sigma_{ut}^{\,b}$$

- **Ground:** $a = 1.58$, $b = -0.085$
- **Machined / Cold-drawn:** $a = 4.51$, $b = -0.265$
- **Hot-rolled:** $a = 57.7$, $b = -0.718$
- **As-forged:** $a = 272$, $b = -0.995$

**2. Size Factor (Rotating Round Shaft, $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm):**

$$k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$$

For non-rotating or rectangular sections, use the **effective diameter** $d_e = 0.808 \sqrt{D \cdot d}$ (for hollow shafts).

**3. Loading Factor ($k_c$):**

$$k_c = \begin{cases} 1.00 & \text{bending} \\ 0.85 & \text{axial} \\ 0.59 & \text{torsion} \end{cases}$$

**4. Temperature Factor (Empirical Interpolation):**

$$k_d = \begin{cases} 1.00 & T \le 50^\circ C \\ \dfrac{S_T}{S_{RT}} & T > 50^\circ C \end{cases}$$

**5. Reliability Factor:**

$$k_e = \begin{cases} 1.000 & 50\% \\ 0.897 & 90\% \\ 0.868 & 95\% \\ 0.814 & 99\% \\ 0.753 & 99.9\% \end{cases}$$

**6. Miscellaneous Factor:**

$$k_f = k_{f,\text{reliability-residual}} \cdot k_{f,\text{corrosion}} \cdot k_{f,\text{plating}} \cdot k_{f,\text{electrochemical}}$$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Automotive Design

- **Crankshaft:** Fatigue is the #1 failure mode. Endurance limit governs the fillet radius design at the journal-to-web transition.
- **Coil Springs:** Subjected to fully reversed torsional loading → uses $k_c = 0.59$.
- **Connecting Rods:** Combined bending + axial → requires superposition of $k_c$ values.
- **Gears (Bending Fatigue):** Tooth root is treated as a cantilever beam, and the corrected $S_e$ is used in the AGMA Lewis equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Marin factors are *multiplicative*, not additive. Forgetting even one factor can lead to a 30–60% over-estimation of component life, which is precisely the kind of design error KTU examiners test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Rotating-Beam Specimen to Corrected Endurance Limit

**Stage 1: Specimen Limit**

Consider a rotating cylindrical beam under a constant bending load $W$. The outer fibre stress alternates between $+\sigma$ and $-\sigma$ every half revolution. By definition:

$$S_e' = 0.5 \, \sigma_{ut}$$

This is the *uncorrected* endurance limit, valid for a **polished, 8 mm diameter, room-temperature rotating steel specimen**.

---

**Stage 2: Surface Finish Correction**

The actual component surface has micro-notches that act as stress concentrators. Fettling and machining tests show that a power-law relationship best fits the reduction in fatigue strength:

$$\frac{S_e^{\,\text{surface}}}{S_e'} = a \, \sigma_{ut}^{\,b}$$

Solving for the surface factor:

$$k_a = a \, \sigma_{ut}^{\,b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Units:** The empirical constants $a$ and $b$ are calibrated for $\sigma_{ut}$ in **MPa**. If $\sigma_{ut}$ is in **ksi**, the constants $a$ and $b$ take different numerical values (e.g., machined: $a = 2.70$, $b = -0.265$).

---

**Stage 3: Size Effect**
