# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six causes of failure in automotive shafts.**

**Model Answer:**

The various causes of failure in automotive shafts are:

1. **Static yielding** due to bending and/or torsion exceeding the yield strength.
2. **Fatigue failure** due to fluctuating (often fully reversed) stresses from rotation.
3. **Stress concentration** at geometric discontinuities like keyways, fillets, and oil holes.
4. **Excessive deflection** causing misalignment of gears, pulleys, or couplings.
5. **Excessive angular twist** causing functional/operational issues.
6. **Lateral or torsional vibration** at critical speeds leading to resonance.
7. **Buckling** for slender shafts under axial compression.
8. **Wear and corrosion** at bearing seats and splines.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is fatigue considered the most critical cause of failure in rotating shafts?**

**Model Answer:**

Fatigue is the most critical cause of failure in rotating shafts because:

1. A **rotating shaft under stationary bending load** experiences a **fully reversed stress cycle** ($r = -1$) at every revolution, meaning even a small alternating stress contributes to damage.
2. Automotive shafts operate for **millions of cycles** (a driveshaft turning at 3000 RPM for 1 hour = 180,000 cycles), so the **endurance limit** (not the yield strength) governs the design.
3. **Cracks initiate at the surface** (often at stress concentration sites) and propagate slowly, making detection difficult before sudden catastrophic fracture.
4. The shaft can fail even though the **peak stress is well below the yield strength** — failure is by cyclic, not static, loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention "fully reversed" + "endurance limit" + "surface crack initiation" for full marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Module 1 Internal Choice — Attempt Any One]**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCMUT601]**
> A solid circular shaft of an automobile is required to transmit **5 kW** power at **1500 rpm**. The shaft is supported between two bearings placed **600 mm apart** and carries a **gear at the mid-span** that exerts a tangential force of **2 kN** and a radial force of **0.8 kN**. The shaft is made of **AISI 1040 steel** with $S_{ut} = 570$ MPa and $S_y = 380$ MPa. Assume a **factor of safety of 2** and check the shaft for:
> **(a)** Static failure using the distortion-energy theory. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Deflection and angular twist. **(7 marks)**
> Take $E = 207 \times 10^{3}$ MPa and $G = 80 \times 10^{3}$ MPa.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Static Failure Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Torque:**

$$
T = \frac{60 \, P}{2 \pi \, N} = \frac{60 \times 5000}{2 \pi \times 1500}
$$

$$
T = \frac{300{,}000}{9424.78} = 31.83 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment at Mid-Span:**
Total transverse force on shaft: $F = \sqrt{F_t^{2} + F_r^{2}} = \sqrt{2000^{2} + 800^{2}} = 2154$ N

For a simply supported beam of length $L = 0.6$ m with a central load:

$$
M = \frac{F \cdot L}{4} = \frac{2154 \times 0.6}{4} = 323.1 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Bending Stress and Torsional Shear Stress (assume a trial diameter of $d = 30$ mm = 0.03 m):**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^{3}} = \frac{32 \times 323.1}{\pi \times (0.03)^{3}}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{10{,}339.2}{8.482 \times 10^{-5}} = 121.88 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 31.83}{\pi \times (0.03)^{3}}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{509.28}{8.482 \times 10^{-5}} = 6.00 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — von Mises Equivalent Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^{\,2} + 3 \, \tau^{2}} = \sqrt{(121.88)^{2} + 3 \times (6.00)^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{14{,}854.7 + 108.0} = \sqrt{14{,}962.7} = 122.32 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Allowable Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{S_y}{n} = \frac{380}{2} = 190 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

$$
\sigma_{VM} = 122.32 \text{ MPa} \le \sigma_{\text{allow}} = 190 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **The shaft is SAFE against static failure with $d = 30$ mm.**

---

#### **Part (b) — Deflection and Angular Twist Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Deflection at Mid-Span:**

$$
I = \frac{\pi \, d^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (0.03)^{4}}{64} = \frac{2.545 \times 10^{-7}}{64} = 3.976 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

$$
y = \frac{F \, L^{3}}{48 \, E \, I} = \frac{2154 \times (0.6)^{3}}{48 \times (207 \times 10^{9}) \times (3.976 \times 10^{-8})}
$$

$$
y = \frac{465.26}{3.951 \times 10^{5}} = 1.178 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.178 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Allowable Deflection:**
KTU standard limit: $y_{\text{allow}} = 0.001 \, L = 0.001 \times 600 = 0.6$ mm.

**Step 3 — Conclusion on Deflection:**

$$
y = 1.178 \text{ mm} > y_{\text{allow}} = 0.6 \text{ mm} \quad \times
$$

> **Shaft FAILS the deflection criterion with $d = 30$ mm.**

**Step 4 — Angular Twist:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi \, d^{4}}{32} = 2I = 7.952 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{T \, L}{G \, J} = \frac{31.83 \times 0.6}{(80 \times 10^{9}) \times (7.952 \times 10^{-8})}
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{19.10}{6361.6} = 3.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = 0.172^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 — Allowable Twist (0.25°/m):**

$$
\theta_{\text{allow}} = 0.25 \times 0.6 = 0.15^{\circ}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion on Twist:**

$$
\theta = 0.172^{\circ} > \theta_{\text{allow}} = 0.15^{\circ} \quad \times
$$

> **Shaft FAILS the twist criterion as well.**

**Step 7 — Final Verdict:**
Although the shaft is safe against static failure, it fails both **deflection and twist** checks. The diameter must be **increased** to satisfy all criteria. A larger diameter (e.g., $d = 35$ mm) would typically be required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCMUT601]**
> A **propeller shaft of a truck** transmits **60 kW at 1000 rpm** and is subjected to a **fluctuating torque** with mean value $T_m = 600$ N·m and alternating component $T_a = 200$ N·m. The bending moment is also fluctuating with $M_m = 200$ N·m and $M_a = 400$ N·m. The shaft material has $S_{ut} = 650$ MPa, $S_y = 400$ MPa, and endurance limit $S_e = 280$ MPa. The fatigue stress concentration factors are $K_f = 2.0$ and $K_{fs} = 1.6$. Using the **ASME-elliptic combined equation**, find the **required shaft diameter** for a **factor of safety of 2**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Step 1 — Identify Shear Strength Properties:**

$$
S_{se} = 0.577 \, S_e = 0.577 \times 280 = 161.56 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
S_{sy} = 0.577 \, S_{ut} = 0.577 \times 650 = 375.05 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the ASME Combined Equation:**

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{16}{\pi d^{3}} \sqrt{ 4 \left[ \frac{K_f M_a}{S_e} + \frac{M_m}{S_{ut}} \right]^{2} + 3 \left[ \frac{K_{fs} T_a}{S_{se}} + \frac{T_m}{S_{sy}} \right]^{2} }
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values:**

- Bending term inside bracket:

$$
\frac{K_f M_a}{S_e} = \frac{2.0 \times 400}{280} = 2.857
$$

$$
\frac{M_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{200}{650} = 0.308
$$

Sum: $2.857 + 0.308 = 3.165$

- Torsion term inside bracket:

$$
\frac{K_{fs} T_a}{S_{se}} = \frac{1.6 \times 200}{161.56} = 1.981
$$

$$
\frac{T_m}{S_{sy}} = \frac{600}{375.05} = 1.600
$$

Sum: $1.981 + 1.600 = 3.581$

**Step 4 — Square and Combine:**

$$
4 \times (3.165)^{2} = 4 \times 10.017 = 40.07
$$

$$
3 \times (3.581)^{2} = 3 \times 12.823 = 38.47
$$

Total: $40.07 + 38.47 = 78.54$

Square root: $\sqrt{78.54} = 8.862$

**Step 5 — Solve for $d$:**

$$
\frac{1}{2} = \frac{16}{\pi d^{3}} \times 8.862
$$

$$
\frac{1}{2} = \frac{141.79}{d^{3}}
$$

$$
d^{3} = 2 \times 141.79 = 283.58
$$

$$
d = (283.58)^{1/3} = 6.567 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 65.67 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{d \approx 66 \text{ mm (standard size)}}
$$

> The propeller shaft diameter should be at least **66 mm** to ensure safety against combined fluctuating bending and torsion with a factor of safety of 2.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the factor of safety $n$** in the ASME equation: students often write $1 = ...$ instead of $1/n = ...$. **−3 marks** typical penalty.
> 2. **Mixing up $S_e$ and $S_{se}$**: Bending uses $S_e$ (tension-compression), torsion uses $S_{se} = 0.577 \, S_e$. **−2 marks** for this error.
> 3. **Confusing mean and alternating stresses**: $\sigma_a$ is the amplitude, $\sigma_m$ is the average. Reversing them gives the wrong result. **−3 marks**.
> 4. **Not squaring and square-rooting** the combined terms properly. The ASME equation has a **square root** outside the **sum of squares**. **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Using wrong units**: Convert N·m to N·mm and m to mm consistently. Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong answers in KTU exams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ⬜ **Shaft** is a rotating power-transmission element. The **5 KTU failure causes** to memorize: **static yielding, fatigue, stress concentration, deflection, twist** (+ vibration, buckling, wear, corrosion).
- ⬜ **Bending stress in a solid shaft:** $\sigma_b = 32 M / (\pi d^{3})$.
- ⬜ **Torsional shear stress in a solid shaft:** $\tau = 16 T / (\pi d^{3})$.
- ⬜ **von Mises combined stress:** $\sigma_{VM} = (16/\pi d^{3}) \sqrt{4 M^{2} + 3 T^{2}}$.
- ⬜ **Endurance limit (steel):** $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa.
- ⬜ **Modified endurance limit:** $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, S_e'$.
- ⬜ **Shear endurance limit:** $S_{se} = 0.577 \, S_e$.
- ⬜ **Fatigue stress concentration factor:** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$.
- ⬜ **ASME combined equation** is the **single most important formula** for KTU Part B (14-mark) shaft design questions.
- ⬜ **Deflection limit:** typically $0.001 L$ for line shafts; $y = F L^{3} / (48 E I)$.
- ⬜ **Twist limit:** typically $0.25^{\circ}/\text{m}$; $\theta = T L / (G J)$.
- ⬜ **Critical speed** must be at least **2× the operating speed** (or operating speed $\le 0.5 \, \omega_{cr}$) to avoid resonance.
- ⬜ **Polar moment of inertia** $J = 2I = \pi d^{4}/32$.
- ⬜ **Units, units, units:** Convert all to **N·mm and MPa** for consistency before substituting.
- ⬜ **Always state factor of safety** in your final design.
- ⬜ **Practice handwriting** the ASME equation at least 5 times — it is long, and you will lose marks if you miswrite it under exam pressure.
- ⬜ **Mention "fully reversed bending"** when a shaft is rotating under a stationary transverse load — this is the most important KTU conceptual point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Based on Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Design Based on Strength ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Design Based on Strength** is the foundational methodology in mechanical and automotive component design wherein the dimensions of a machine member are determined such that the **induced stress** (the stress generated internally by external service loads) remains **strictly below** the **allowable stress** (the maximum stress the material can safely tolerate under the chosen loading condition). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Design based on strength* is the classical deterministic sizing approach used to proportion automotive components (axles, crankshafts, connecting rods, piston pins, springs, brackets) such that the **maximum induced stress** under expected service loading does not exceed the **permissible stress** of the selected material. Mathematically, the governing design inequality is expressed as: $$ \sigma_{induced} \;\leq\; \sigma_{permissible} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{n \cdot K} $$ where $\sigma_{ultimate}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the parent material, $n$ is the **factor of safety**, and $K$ is the **service factor / load factor** that accounts for dynamic or unpredictable loading conditions. ### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Strength-Based Design | Pillar | Symbol | Role in Design | |---|---|---| | **Induced Stress** | $\sigma_{ind}$ or $\tau_{ind}$ | Stress caused by applied loads (calculated via mechanics of materials) | | **Material Strength** | $\sigma_{ut}$, $\sigma_{y}$ | Inherent resistance of the chosen engineering material | | **Factor of Safety** | $FOS$ or $n$ | Reliability multiplier bridging the gap between theory and real-world uncertainty | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bridge Analogy" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Rope Bridge:** > Imagine you are designing a rope bridge to carry **at most 100 people**, but you only expect **20 people** to ever cross at once. > > - The **rope's breaking strength** = Material ultimate strength ($\sigma_{ut}$). > - The **rope's rated capacity** = Allowable stress ($\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_{ut}/n$). > - The **actual crowd pull** = Induced stress ($\sigma_{ind}$). > > You would never use a rope rated exactly for 20 people. You select a much thicker rope (say, rated for 100 people) to account for sudden weight surges, dynamic swaying, rope aging, manufacturing defects, and corrosion. **This "over-design ratio" is exactly the Factor of Safety.** ### 1.4 Why Strength-Based Design is Critical in Automotive Engineering In the automotive industry, **failure is not an option**. A crankshaft that yields, a connecting rod that buckles, or a steering knuckle that fractures directly endangers human life. Strength-based design ensures: 1. **Static Load Safety** — Resistance to yielding under steady loads. 2. **Predictable Performance** — Dimensions and material choice are mathematically traceable. 3. **Cost Optimization** — Avoids over-engineering while maintaining reliability. 4. **Regulatory Compliance** — Meets AIS (Automotive Industry Standards), BIS, and OEM design protocols. ### 1.5 Key Terminology Snapshot > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{ut}$):** The maximum stress a material can withstand before fracture. > - **Yield Strength ($\sigma_{y}$):** The stress at which a material begins to deform plastically. > - **Allowable / Permissible Stress ($\sigma_{perm}$):** The design stress limit, set as $\sigma_y/n$ or $\sigma_{ut}/n$. > - **Factor of Safety ($n$):** A dimensionless multiplier representing design conservatism. > - **Stress Concentration Factor ($K_t$):** A geometric multiplier (always $\geq 1$) accounting for discontinuities like holes, fillets, and keyways. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Design Philosophy — A Stepwise Framework Strength-based design follows a **sequential decision pipeline**. Each step builds upon the previous, and a flaw in any stage propagates into the final component. #### Step 1 — Load Estimation Identify **all forces** acting on the component. For an automotive component, this includes: - Static dead-weight loads - Dynamic inertial loads (e.g., reciprocating mass in an engine) - Shock and impact loads (road-induced vibrations) - Thermal loads (expansion/contraction) #### Step 2 — Free-Body Diagram (FBD) Construction Resolve all loads into **normal ($F$)**, **shear ($V$)**, and **bending ($M$) / torsional ($T$)** components. #### Step 3 — Induced Stress Computation Using mechanics of materials: - **Normal stress:** $\sigma = F/A$ - **Bending stress:** $\sigma_b = Mc/I$ - **Torsional shear stress:** $\tau = Tr/J$ - **Combined (principal) stress** evaluated via Mohr's circle or transformation equations. #### Step 4 — Material Selection & Permissible Stress Determination Choose a material from the **automotive materials database** (e.g., 40C8, 45C8, EN8, EN24, alloy steels, Al-Si alloys). Establish $\sigma_{perm}$ based on failure theory. #### Step 5 — Dimension Determination Solve the strength inequality for the unknown geometric variable (diameter, thickness, cross-section). #### Step 6 — Verification Against Deflection, Stiffness, Buckling, Fatigue Even after strength is satisfied, **deformation** and **buckling** criteria must be rechecked. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the master reference for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 numericals.** | # | Design Scenario | Governing Equation | Permissible Stress Criterion | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Simple axial tensile member | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A} \le \sigma_{perm}$ | $\sigma_{perm} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | | 2 | Pure bending beam | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3} \le \sigma_{perm}$ | $\sigma_{perm} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | | 3 | Pure torsion (circular shaft) | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \le \tau_{perm}$ | $\tau_{perm} = \dfrac{0.5 \sigma_y}{n}$ | | 4 | Combined bending + torsion | $\sigma_{eq,vM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2} \le \sigma_{perm}$ | Von Mises criterion | | 5 | Combined bending + torsion (Tresca) | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} \le \sigma_{perm}$ | Maximum shear stress theory | | 6 | Axial + bending (eccentric load) | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{F}{A} + \dfrac{M c}{I}$ | Direct summation (no interaction term) | | 7 | Stressed member with stress concentration | $\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nominal} \le \sigma_{perm}$ | $\sigma_{perm} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n \cdot K_t}$ | | 8 | Thin cylindrical pressure vessel | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ (hoop) and $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ (longitudinal) | $\sigma_{perm} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | | 9 | Helical spring (shear) | $\tau = \dfrac{8 F D}{\pi d^3} K_w \le \tau_{perm}$ | Wahl's factor correction | | 10 | Knuckle / crank-pin (general) | $\dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \le \sigma_{perm}$ | Equivalent bending moment approach | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention Reminder:** For static ductile material design, KTU examiners most commonly accept the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST / Tresca)** or the **Distortion Energy Theory (DET / Von Mises)**. Both are valid; the choice depends on the specific question wording. ### 2.3 The Factor of Safety — Engineering Judgment in Numbers The Factor of Safety ($n$) is **never arbitrary**. It is selected based on: | Factor | High $n$ Required When | Low $n$ Acceptable When | |---|---|---| | **Load uncertainty** | Dynamic, shock, or impact loads | Static, well-known loads | | **Material uniformity** | Cast, forged, or welded parts | Wrought, machined stock | | **Failure consequence** | Risk to human life | Non-critical brackets | | **Inspection access** | Cannot be inspected in service | Easy inspection possible | | **Stress concentration** | Sharp notches, keyways, threads | Smooth geometry | | **Environmental degradation** | Corrosive, high-temperature service | Mild ambient conditions | **Typical KTU Recommended Values:** - **Ductile material under steady load:** $n = 1.5$ to $2.0$ - **Brittle material under steady load:** $n = 2.0$ to $4.0$ - **Ductile material with dynamic load:** $n = 2.0$ to $3.0$ - **Automotive safety-critical parts (crankshaft, axle):** $n = 3.0$ to $4.0$ ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Automotive Engineering Strength-based design is the **backbone of every automotive sub-system**: - **Crankshaft Sizing:** Combines bending (from cylinder pressure) and torsion (from torque transmission). Uses the **equivalent bending moment** or **equivalent torque** approach. - **Connecting Rod:** Subject to axial tension/compression from piston force plus bending from inertia. Combined loading formulation applied. - **Axle Shaft:** Pure torsion (driving torque) plus bending (vehicle weight transfer). - **Piston Pin:** Bending stress from gas force and bearing reactions. - **Frame Members:** Combined bending + axial (during cornering or braking). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Bending Moment for Combined Loading (Shafts) When a shaft is subjected to **simultaneous bending moment $M$** and **torsional moment $T$**, the principal stresses at the critical fiber (surface) are non-zero and must be combined via a failure theory. **Step 1 — Identify the stress state at the critical surface element.** At the top fiber of a circular shaft in bending + torsion, the stress tensor has only: - Normal stress: $\sigma_x = \sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ - Shear stress: $\tau_{xy} = \tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ **Step 2 — Compute the principal stresses using the plane-stress transformation.** $$ \sigma_{1,2} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \;\pm\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca).** The maximum shear stress is half the difference of the principal stresses: $$ \tau_{max} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} \;=\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ Setting $\tau_{max} = \tau_{perm}$ (or equivalently, maximum principal stress = $\sigma_{perm}$): $$ \sigma_{perm} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $\sigma_x$ and $\tau_{xy}$.** $$ \sigma_{perm} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}\right) + \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} $$ **Step 5 — Factor out $\dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}$.** $$ \sigma_{perm} \;=\; \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left[\;M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\;\right] $$ **Step 6 — Define the Equivalent Bending Moment.** $$ M_{eq} \;=\; M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} $$ **Final governing equation for shaft diameter:** $$ d \;=\; \left(\frac{16 \, M_{eq}}{\pi \, \sigma_{perm}}\right)^{1/3} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Note:** This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE. Memorize the substitution steps and the final compact form of $M_{eq}$. ### 3.2 Derivation: Von Mises Equivalent Stress for Combined Loading **Step 1 — Start with the Von Mises yield criterion.** $$ \sigma_{vM} \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3 \tau_{xy}^2} \;\leq\; \sigma_{perm} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the bending and torsion stress components.** $$ \sigma_{vM} \;=\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} \;\leq\; \sigma_{perm} $$ **Step 3 — Factor out $\dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}$.** $$ \sigma_{vM} \;=\; \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{(2M)^2 + 3 T^2} $$ **Step 4 — Define the equivalent moment (Von Mises form).** $$ M_{eq,vM} \;=\; \sqrt{M^2 + \frac{3}{4}T^2} \quad \text{(when written for the moment formulation, some texts use } \frac{3}{4} \text{)} $$ Wait — correction: the cleanest equivalent moment form using the Von Mises convention is: $$ T_{eq,vM} \;=\; \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \quad \text{is for Tresca.} $$ For Von Mises: $$ T_{eq,vM} \;=\; \sqrt{M^2 + \frac{3}{4}T^2} $$ And the diameter equation: $$ d \;=\; \left(\frac{16}{\pi \sigma_{perm}}\sqrt{M^2 + \frac{3}{4}T^2}\right)^{1/3} $$ ### 3.3 Worked Example: Solid Circular Shaft Under Bending + Torsion **Problem Statement:** A solid circular shaft transmits a torque of $T = 1200$ N·m and is subjected to a bending moment of $M = 900$ N·m. The shaft material has a yield strength of $\sigma_y = 360$ MPa. Using a factor of safety of $n = 3$ and the **maximum shear stress theory**, determine the required shaft diameter. **Given Data:** - $T = 1200 \; \text{N·m} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}$ - $M = 900 \; \text{N·m} = 9.0 \times 10^5 \; \text{N·mm}$ - $\sigma_y = 360 \; \text{MPa} = 360 \; \text{N/mm}^2$ - $n = 3$ **Step 1 — Determine the permissible normal stress.** $$ \sigma_{perm} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y}{n} \;=\; \frac{360}{3} \;=\; 120 \; \text{N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the equivalent bending moment (Tresca-based).** $$ M_{eq} \;=\; M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} $$ **Step 2a — Compute $M^2$:** $$ M^2 \;=\; (9.0 \times 10^5)^2 \;=\; 8.1 \times 10^{11} \; \text{(N·mm)}^2 $$ **Step 2b — Compute $T^2$:** $$ T^2 \;=\; (1.2 \times 10^6)^2 \;=\; 1.44 \times 10^{12} \; \text{(N·mm)}^2 $$ **Step 2c — Sum and square root:** $$ M^2 + T^2 \;=\; 8.1 \times 10^{11} + 1.44 \times 10^{12} \;=\; 2.25 \times 10^{12} $$ $$ \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \;=\; \sqrt{2.25 \times 10^{12}} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm} $$ **Step 2d — Final $M_{eq}$:** $$ M_{eq} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^5 + 1.5 \times 10^6 \;=\; 2.4 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the bending equation solved for $d$.** $$ \sigma_{perm} \;=\; \frac{32 M_{eq}}{\pi d^3} $$ $$ d^3 \;=\; \frac{32 M_{eq}}{\pi \sigma_{perm}} $$ $$ d^3 \;=\; \frac{32 \times 2.4 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 120} $$ **Step 3a — Compute numerator:** $$ 32 \times 2.4 \times 10^6 \;=\; 76.8 \times 10^6 $$ **Step 3b — Compute denominator:** $$ \pi \times 120 \;=\; 376.99 $$ **Step 3c — Ratio:** $$ d^3 \;=\; \frac{76.8 \times 10^6}{376.99} \;=\; 203{,}719.6 \; \text{mm}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Cube root to find $d$.** $$ d \;=\; (203{,}719.6)^{1/3} $$ $$ d \;\approx\; 58.81 \; \text{mm} $$ **Step 5 — Round up to a standard size.** $$ \boxed{d \;=\; 60 \; \text{mm}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why do we round UP?** In KTU board examinations, examiners award **full marks only if the final diameter is rounded up** to the next standard integer. Selecting a smaller diameter, even one that "satisfies" the equation, is technically unsafe in design philosophy and may attract partial deductions. ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Failure Theory | Scenario | Preferred Theory | Justification | |---|---|---| | **Ductile material, static loading** | Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca) or Von Mises | Both accurate; Tresca is simpler & conservative | | **Brittle material, static loading** | Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine) or Modified Mohr | Brittle materials fracture without yielding | | **Ductile material, fatigue loading** | Goodman / Soderberg criterion | Mean + alternating stress matters | | **Uniaxial stress state** | Any theory yields the same result | No need to invoke advanced criteria | | **Pure torsion** | Maximum Shear Stress Theory is exact | Shear failure is dominant | | **Combined bending + torsion on shaft** | Von Mises (more accurate) or Tresca (simpler) | Both valid in KTU 2024 syllabus | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Strength-Based Design Workflow (Functional Architecture Flow) The following diagram maps the complete logical pipeline a designer follows from initial load identification to final dimension verification. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Step 1: Define Service Loads
Static, Dynamic, Shock, Thermal"] nodeB["Step 2: Construct Free Body Diagram
Resolve into F, V, M, T"] nodeC["Step 3: Compute Induced Stress
sigma = F/A, Mc/I, Tr/J"] nodeD["Step 4: Identify Failure Mode
Yield, Fracture, Buckling, Fatigue"] nodeE["Step 5: Select Failure Theory
Tresca, Von Mises, Rankine"] nodeF["Step 6: Choose Material
Database Lookup, Cost Analysis"] nodeG["Step 7: Compute Permissible Stress
sigma_perm = sigma_y / n"] nodeH["Step 8: Solve Strength Inequality
Determine Unknown Dimension"] nodeI["Step 9: Check Stress Concentration
Apply Kt, Notch Sensitivity"] nodeJ["Step 10: Verify Deflection and Stiffness
Ensure Acceptable Deformation"] nodeK["Step 11: Final Standardized Dimension
Round Up to Next Standard Size"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeK ``` ### 4.2 Decision Branch: Selection of Failure Theory ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeP["Material and Loading Type?"] nodeQ["Ductile Material
Static Load"] nodeR["Brittle Material
Static Load"] nodeS["Ductile Material
Dynamic/Fatigue Load"] nodeT["Use Tresca or
Von Mises Theory"] nodeU["Use Maximum Normal Stress
or Modified Mohr Theory"] nodeV["Use Goodman or
Soderberg Criterion"] nodeP --> nodeQ --> nodeT nodeP --> nodeR --> nodeU nodeP --> nodeS --> nodeV ``` ### 4.3 Schematic: Stress State at a Critical Point on a Shaft Under Combined Bending and Torsion ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeL["Cross-section of Shaft"] nodeM["Top Fiber: Maximum Bending Stress"] nodeN["sigma_x = 32M over pi d cubed"] nodeO["Surface Shear: tau_xy = 16T over pi d cubed"] nodeL --> nodeM --> nodeN nodeL --> nodeO ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visualization Note:** On the top fiber of a shaft under combined bending and torsion, the stress element experiences a **uniaxial normal stress $\sigma_x$** in the axial direction and a **shear stress $\tau_{xy}$** in the transverse plane. This 2D stress state is the canonical case for applying the Tresca and Von Mises criteria. ### 4.4 Factor of Safety Selection Roadmap ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeFA["Failure Consequence?"] nodeFB["Human Life at Risk"] nodeFC["Equipment Damage Only"] nodeFD["Choose High Factor of Safety
n = 3 to 5"] nodeFE["Choose Moderate Factor of Safety
n = 1.5 to 3"] nodeFA --> nodeFB --> nodeFD nodeFA --> nodeFC --> nodeFE ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *Define permissible stress and factor of safety. Explain their significance in the design of automotive components.* **Model Answer:** **Permissible Stress** ($\sigma_{perm}$) is the maximum stress to which a machine element can be subjected under design conditions. It is obtained by dividing the material's yield (or ultimate) strength by the factor of safety: $$ \sigma_{perm} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y}{n} $$ **Factor of Safety (FOS)** is the numerical ratio between the material's strength and the allowable working stress. It accounts for uncertainties in: - Load magnitude - Material properties - Manufacturing quality - Operating environment **Significance in Automotive Design:** - Prevents in-service failure (yielding, fracture) - Accommodates overloads and impact conditions - Compensates for stress concentrations at notches and fillets - Provides margin against corrosion, wear, and fatigue degradation In automotive practice, components like crankshafts and axles use FOS of **3 to 4**, while non-critical brackets use **1.5 to 2**. --- **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] *Distinguish between design based on strength and design based on stiffness. State one situation where each is preferred.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Design Based on Strength | Design Based on Stiffness | |---|---|---| | **Primary concern** | Avoiding material failure (yield/fracture) | Limiting deformation under load | | **Governing quantity** | Induced stress vs. permissible stress | Deflection vs. allowable deflection | | **Mathematical form** | $\sigma_{ind} \le \sigma_{perm}$ | $\delta_{actual} \le \delta_{allowable}$ | | **Design variable** | Cross-section area, diameter | Second moment of area $I$ | | **Preferred for** | Shafts, axles, connecting rods | Leaf springs, frame rails, long shafts | | **Typical output** | Diameter $d$ satisfies stress criterion | Diameter $d$ satisfies deflection criterion | In practice, **automotive designers check BOTH** criteria and select the **larger** of the two diameters. For example, a propeller shaft is often sized by **stiffness** (to avoid whirling vibration) even though its strength margin is already adequate. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) #### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – June 2023] A mild steel shaft transmits 7.5 kW power at 200 rpm. The shaft is also subjected to a bending moment of 600 N·m due to gear reactions. The material has an ultimate tensile strength of 540 MPa and yield strength of 380 MPa. Using a factor of safety of 4 and **maximum shear stress theory (Tresca)**, design the shaft diameter. Assume the shaft is solid and circular. **(a) Compute the torque transmitted by the shaft.** **(7 Marks)** **(b) Determine the required diameter and select the standard size.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Torque Calculation:** Given: - Power $P = 7.5$ kW $= 7500$ W - Speed $N = 200$ rpm The relationship between power, torque, and angular velocity is: $$ P \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} $$ Solving for torque: $$ T \;=\; \frac{P \times 60}{2 \pi N} $$ $$ T \;=\; \frac{7500 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 200} $$ $$ T \;=\; \frac{450{,}000}{400 \pi} $$ $$ T \;=\; \frac{450{,}000}{1256.64} $$ $$ T \;\approx\; 358.1 \; \text{N·m} $$ Converting to N·mm: $$ T \;=\; 358.1 \times 10^3 \; \text{N·mm} \;=\; 3.581 \times 10^5 \; \text{N·mm} $$ Bending moment given: $$ M \;=\; 600 \; \text{N·m} \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^5 \; \text{N·mm} $$ --- **Part (b) — Diameter Calculation:** **Step 1 — Permissible stress:** $$ \sigma_{perm} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y}{n} \;=\; \frac{380}{4} \;=\; 95 \; \text{N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Equivalent bending moment (Tresca):** $$ M_{eq} \;=\; M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} $$ Computing $M^2$: $$ M^2 \;=\; (6.0 \times 10^5)^2 \;=\; 3.6 \times 10^{11} \; \text{(N·mm)}^2 $$ Computing $T^2$: $$ T^2 \;=\; (3.581 \times 10^5)^2 \;=\; 1.282 \times 10^{11} \; \text{(N·mm)}^2 $$ Sum: $$ M^2 + T^2 \;=\; 3.6 \times 10^{11} + 1.282 \times 10^{11} \;=\; 4.882 \times 10^{11} $$ Square root: $$ \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \;=\; \sqrt{4.882 \times 10^{11}} \;=\; 6.987 \times 10^5 \; \text{N·mm} $$ Equivalent bending moment: $$ M_{eq} \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^5 + 6.987 \times 10^5 \;=\; 12.987 \times 10^5 \; \text{N·mm} \;=\; 1.2987 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm} $$ **Step 3 — Apply bending equation:** $$ \sigma_{perm} \;=\; \frac{32 M_{eq}}{\pi d^3} $$ $$ d^3 \;=\; \frac{32 M_{eq}}{\pi \sigma_{perm}} \;=\; \frac{32 \times 1.2987 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 95} $$ Numerator: $$ 32 \times 1.2987 \times 10^6 \;=\; 41.558 \times 10^6 $$ Denominator: $$ \pi \times 95 \;=\; 298.45 $$ Ratio: $$ d^3 \;=\; \frac{41.558 \times 10^6}{298.45} \;=\; 139{,}262 \; \text{mm}^3 $$ Cube root: $$ d \;=\; (139{,}262)^{1/3} \;\approx\; 51.84 \; \text{mm} $$ **Step 4 — Round up to standard size:** $$ \boxed{d \;=\; 55 \; \text{mm}} $$ --- #### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] A connecting rod of an IC engine is made of steel with ultimate tensile strength 600 MPa and yield strength 420 MPa. It is subjected to a maximum axial compressive load of 45 kN. Using a factor of safety of 5 and **Rankine's formula** with constants $a = \dfrac{1}{750}$ and $b = \dfrac{1}{3000}$, determine the diameter of the connecting rod. Assume the rod is solid and circular. **(a) State Rankine's formula and explain the role of constants $a$ and $b$.** **(7 Marks)** **(b) Solve for the diameter and recommend the standard size.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Rankine's Formula Theory:** Rankine's formula is an empirical design equation for **short columns / compression members** that accounts for both direct crushing failure and buckling tendency: $$ \sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{P}{A}\left(1 + a \left(\frac{L}{k}\right)^2\right) \;\leq\; \sigma_{c} $$ Alternative rearranged form: $$ P \;=\; \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\frac{L}{k}\right)^2} $$ Or, using the form with $a$ and $b$: $$ \sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{P}{A} + b \cdot \frac{L^2}{k^2} $$ where: - $\sigma_c = $ permissible crushing stress = $\sigma_y / n$ - $L = $ length of the rod - $k = $ radius of gyration $= \dfrac{d}{4}$ for a solid circular cross-section - $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants depending on material and end conditions For mild steel with both ends pinned, $a = 1/7500$ to $1/1600$; for both ends fixed, $a = 1/3000$ approximately. --- **Part (b) — Diameter Calculation:** **Given:** - $P = 45$ kN $= 45{,}000$ N (compressive) - $\sigma_y = 420$ MPa - $n = 5$ - $a = 1/750$ - $b = 1/3000$ - Assume $L = 300$ mm (typical connecting rod length for small IC engine) **Step 1 — Permissible crushing stress:** $$ \sigma_c \;=\; \frac{\sigma_y}{n} \;=\; \frac{420}{5} \;=\; 84 \; \text{N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of solid circular rod:** $$ A \;=\; \frac{\pi d^2}{4} $$ **Step 3 — Radius of gyration:** $$ k \;=\; \frac{d}{4} $$ Therefore: $$ \left(\frac{L}{k}\right)^2 \;=\; \left(\frac{300}{d/4}\right)^2 \;=\; \left(\frac{1200}{d}\right)^2 \;=\; \frac{1{,}440{,}000}{d^2} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Rankine's formula:** $$ \sigma_c \;=\; \frac{P}{A}\left(1 + a \left(\frac{L}{k}\right)^2\right) $$ $$ 84 \;=\; \frac{45{,}000}{\pi d^2 / 4}\left(1 + \frac{1}{750} \cdot \frac{1{,}440{,}000}{d^2}\right) $$ $$ 84 \;=\; \frac{4 \times 45{,}000}{\pi d^2}\left(1 + \frac{1920}{d^2}\right) $$ $$ 84 \;=\; \frac{180{,}000}{\pi d^2}\left(1 + \frac{1920}{d^2}\right) $$ $$ 84 \;=\; \frac{180{,}000}{\pi d^2} + \frac{180{,}000 \times 1920}{\pi d^4} $$ $$ 84 \;=\; \frac{57{,}295.78}{d^2} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{d^4} $$ **Step 5 — Solve iteratively:** Try $d = 30$ mm: $$ \frac{57{,}295.78}{900} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{810{,}000} \;=\; 63.66 + 135.8 \;=\; 199.5 \; \text{(too high)} $$ Try $d = 40$ mm: $$ \frac{57{,}295.78}{1600} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{2{,}560{,}000} \;=\; 35.81 + 42.97 \;=\; 78.78 \; \text{(too low)} $$ Try $d = 41$ mm: $$ \frac{57{,}295.78}{1681} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{2{,}825{,}761} \;=\; 34.08 + 38.93 \;=\; 73.01 \; \text{(too low)} $$ Try $d = 42$ mm: $$ \frac{57{,}295.78}{1764} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{3{,}111{,}696} \;=\; 32.48 + 35.35 \;=\; 67.83 \; \text{(too low)} $$ Try $d = 39$ mm: $$ \frac{57{,}295.78}{1521} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{2{,}312{,}481} \;=\; 37.67 + 47.57 \;=\; 85.24 \; \text{(slightly high)} $$ Try $d = 39.5$ mm: $$ \frac{57{,}295.78}{1560.25} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{2{,}434{,}296} \;=\; 36.72 + 45.19 \;=\; 81.91 $$ Try $d = 39.7$ mm: $$ \frac{57{,}295.78}{1576.09} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{2{,}485{,}209} \;=\; 36.35 + 44.26 \;=\; 80.61 $$ Try $d = 39.9$ mm: $$ \frac{57{,}295.78}{1592.01} + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{2{,}537{,}088} \;=\; 35.99 + 43.35 \;=\; 79.34 $$ Closest match at $d \approx 40$ mm (where value is 78.78 MPa, less than 84 MPa permissible — meaning the design is **safe**). **Step 6 — Round up to standard size:** $$ \boxed{d \;=\; 40 \; \text{mm}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative Solution Check:** Using the simplified form $\sigma_c = P/A + bL^2/k^2$: > > $84 = (45{,}000 / (\pi d^2/4)) + (1/3000)(300^2)/(d/4)^2$ > > $84 = 57{,}295.78/d^2 + 48{,}000/d^2$ > > $84 = 105{,}295.78/d^2$ > > $d^2 = 1253.5$ > > $d = 35.4$ mm → round up to 36 mm > > This simplified form yields a smaller diameter because it neglects the interaction term. Always use the form specified in the question. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Design-Based-on-Strength Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to round up** the final diameter — selecting the calculated value even when it's below a standard size (–1 mark). > 2. **Mixing units** between N·m and N·mm — a single unit error propagates through the entire calculation (–2 to –4 marks). > 3. **Using $\sigma_{ut}$ instead of $\sigma_y$** in the permissible stress formula — for static ductile design, KTU expects $\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y/n$, not $\sigma_{ut}/n$ (–1 mark). > 4. **Wrong failure theory** — applying Tresca when Von Mises is asked, or vice versa (–1 mark). > 5. **Neglecting stress concentration** — when a shaft has a shoulder fillet, keyway, or hole, the actual stress is $K_t$ times the nominal value. Missing this incurs heavy deductions in design-problem questions (–2 marks). > 6. **Not verifying the buckling or deflection criterion** after strength is satisfied — examiners expect a one-line statement confirming stiffness check (–1 mark). > 7. **No FBD or assumption list** — KTU valuation keys require a clearly labeled free-body diagram and an explicit listing of given data and assumptions (–2 marks if absent). ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Design Based on Strength** > > ✅ **Core Inequality:** $\sigma_{induced} \le \sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y / n$ > > ✅ **Factor of Safety:** $n = 1.5$–$2.0$ (static, ductile); $n = 3$–$4$ (automotive critical); $n = 5+$ (uncertain dynamic loads) > > ✅ **Bending Stress (circular):** $\sigma_b = 32M / (\pi d^3)$ > > ✅ **Torsional Stress (circular):** $\tau = 16T / (\pi d^3)$ > > ✅ **Equivalent Bending Moment (Tresca):** $M_{eq} = M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ > > ✅ **Equivalent Moment (Von Mises):** $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2}$ > > ✅ **Permissible Shear Stress:** $\tau_{perm} = 0.5 \sigma_y / n$ (for direct shear) or $\tau_{perm} = 0.577 \sigma_y / n$ (for torsion) > > ✅ **Pressure Vessel Hoop Stress:** $\sigma_h = pd / (2t)$ — always twice the longitudinal stress > > ✅ **Stress Concentration:** $\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nominal}$; always $\ge$ the nominal stress > > ✅ **Always round UP** the final diameter to the next integer or standard available size > > ✅ **Always check** both strength AND stiffness/buckling criteria before finalizing dimensions > > ✅ **Document everything:** FBD, assumptions, material choice, failure theory, factor of safety — KTU valuation rewards structured presentation

topics.title: Rigidity and Critical Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rigidity and Critical Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rigidity of a Shaft

**Rigidity** is the ability of a shaft to resist deformation (bending or twisting) under the action of external loads. In the context of automotive component design, a shaft must possess adequate rigidity to:

- Maintain accurate alignment of mounted components (gears, pulleys, bearings).
- Prevent excessive **lateral deflection** (sagging) which would cause misalignment, vibration, and premature bearing failure.
- Resist **torsional twist** which would cause angular lag between the driving and driven members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *Rigidity refers to the stiffness of the shaft against bending and torsion. For bending (lateral rigidity), the shaft is treated as a simply supported beam, and its deflection is calculated using standard beam equations. For torsion (torsional rigidity), the angular twist is computed using the torsion equation.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel Ruler" Analogy

Imagine holding a thin steel ruler (a beam) on two fingers (supports) and pressing down on it with your thumb (load). The ruler bends slightly. If the ruler were:
- **Thicker** → less bend → **more rigid**.
- **Shorter** → less bend → **more rigid**.
- **Made of a stiffer material** (steel vs. plastic) → less bend → **more rigid**.

Now imagine the ruler spinning rapidly between your fingers like a propeller. At some spin speed, the ruler will start to wobble violently sideways — that wobble is called **whirling**, and the speed at which it happens is the **critical speed**. A more rigid ruler will whirling at a much higher speed.

## 1.2 Critical Speed (Whirling Speed)

**Critical speed** is the rotational speed at which the natural frequency of lateral (transverse) vibration of a shaft coincides with its rotational frequency, causing **resonance**. At this speed, the amplitude of vibration becomes theoretically infinite (in an ideal undamped system), leading to catastrophic failure of the shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> The critical speed of a shaft is the speed at which the shaft, when slightly deflected, will continue to deflect with continuously increasing amplitude, i.e., the speed at which the shaft becomes dynamically unstable. It is also called the **whirling speed** because the shaft appears to "whirl" around its geometric axis.

### Key Terms at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Lateral Deflection (y) | Sideways sag of the shaft under load | **mm** |
| Angular Twist (θ) | Rotation of one end relative to the other | **radians** |
| Natural Frequency (ωn) | Frequency at which shaft vibrates freely | **rad/s** |
| Critical Speed (Nc) | Rotational speed = natural frequency | **rpm** |
| Rigidity Modulus (k) | Force per unit deflection | **N/mm** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In automotive shafts (propeller shafts, drive shafts, crankshafts), designers must ensure that the **operating speed** is always at least **20–25% below** the calculated critical speed, OR **20–25% above** it (super-critical operation). Operating *at* critical speed is **strictly forbidden**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lateral Rigidity — Deflection of Shafts

The shaft is idealized as a **simply supported beam** with bearings at the ends. Standard beam-deflection formulas are used.

### Case 1: Single Concentrated Load (W) at Mid-span

The maximum deflection occurs at the centre of the span:

$$y = \frac{W \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

Where:
- $W$ = concentrated load (in **N**)
- $L$ = span length between bearings (in **mm**)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the shaft material (in **N/mm²**)
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the shaft cross-section (in **mm⁴**)
- $y$ = Maximum lateral deflection at mid-span (in **mm**)

### Case 2: Uniformly Distributed Load (w) over the Entire Span

$$y = \frac{5 \cdot w \cdot L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

### Case 3: Multiple Concentrated Loads (W₁, W₂, ...) at Various Positions

Deflection is calculated by **superposition** of deflections due to individual loads. For a simply supported beam with a load $W_i$ at distance $a_i$ from the left support (with $b_i = L - a_i$):

$$y_i = \frac{W_i \cdot a_i^2 \cdot b_i^2}{3 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot L}$$

The total deflection at any point is the algebraic sum of individual contributions.

## 2.2 Torsional Rigidity — Angular Twist

A shaft transmitting torque $T$ over length $L$ undergoes an angular twist:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

Where:
- $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus), for steel ≈ **79.3 GPa** (or 80 kN/mm²)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$:

$$J = \frac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32}$$

For a hollow shaft of outer diameter $d_o$ and inner diameter $d_i$:

$$J = \frac{\pi \cdot (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{32}$$

## 2.3 Critical Speed — Rayleigh's Method

The most widely used method (and the one asked repeatedly in KTU exams) is **Rayleigh's Energy Method**.

### Concept

When a shaft rotates with a small eccentricity (or carries a rotor with its centre of mass slightly off the geometric axis), the centrifugal force acts as a transverse load. The shaft deflects until the elastic restoring force balances the centrifugal force. Equating these two forces gives the critical speed.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Rayleigh's Method

1. Assume a small **static deflection** $\delta$ of the shaft at the rotor location due to the weight of the rotor (treated as a transverse load).
2. When the shaft rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, the centrifugal force on the rotor is $m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (\delta + y)$, where $y$ is the additional dynamic deflection.
3. The elastic restoring force from the shaft is $k \cdot y$, where $k$ is the lateral stiffness.
4. At the threshold of instability, set centrifugal force = elastic restoring force, giving the critical angular velocity:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$$

> Where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 9810 \text{ mm/s}^2$ is gravitational acceleration.

5. Converting to rotational speed in **rpm**:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$$

6. Simplifying with $g = 9810 \text{ mm/s}^2$:

$$N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} \quad \text{rpm, where } \delta \text{ is in mm}$$

7. If $\delta$ is in **metres**:

$$N_c = \frac{29.88}{\sqrt{\delta}} \quad \text{rpm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The critical speed depends **only on the static deflection** of the shaft at the rotor location, NOT on the mass of the rotor (the mass cancels out in the derivation). However, the deflection itself depends on the load (weight of rotor).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All table entries below use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y = \frac{W L^3}{48 E I}$ | Deflection at centre, point load at mid-span | $y$ in **mm** |
| 2 | $y = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | Deflection at centre, UDL over full span | $y$ in **mm** |
| 3 | $y_i = \frac{W_i a_i^2 b_i^2}{3 E I L}$ | Deflection due to point load at $a_i$ from left support | $y$ in **mm** |
| 4 | $\theta = \frac{T L}{G J}$ | Angular twist under torque $T$ | $\theta$ in **rad** |
| 5 | $J_{solid} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia, solid shaft | **mm⁴** |
| 6 | $J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia, hollow shaft | **mm⁴** |
| 7 | $N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$ | Critical speed (Rayleigh) | $N_c$ in **rpm**, $\delta$ in same unit as $g$ |
| 8 | $N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}}$ | Critical speed (simplified, $\delta$ in mm) | **rpm** |
| 9 | $N_c = \frac{29.88}{\sqrt{\delta}}$ | Critical speed (simplified, $\delta$ in m) | **rpm** |
| 10 | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$ | Critical angular velocity | **rad/s** |
| 11 | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{N_c}} = \sum \frac{1}{\sqrt{N_{ci}}}$ | Dunkerley's formula (multiple rotors) | $N_c$ in **rpm** |
| 12 | $k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{48 E I}{L^3}$ | Lateral stiffness of simply supported beam | **N/mm** |

## 2.5 Real-World Automotive Applications

| Automotive Shaft Type | Typical Operating Speed | Critical Speed Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Propeller Shaft** (rear-wheel drive cars) | 1000–5000 rpm | Must be designed for sub-critical operation |
| **Crankshaft** (engine) | 800–7000 rpm | Critical speed often above max RPM |
| **Drive Shaft (Half Shaft)** | Up to 8000 rpm | Designed with large safety margin |
| **Steering Column Shaft** | Low RPM | Rigidity more critical than critical speed |
| **Turbocharger Shaft** | 100,000–250,000 rpm | Super-critical operation; uses tilting-pad bearings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Rigidity Matters in Automobiles:** Excessive shaft deflection causes gear-tooth misalignment, uneven loading, noise, vibration, harshness (NVH) issues, and premature bearing wear. The 2024 KTU syllabus explicitly links rigidity to **shaft design and selection**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Critical Speed (Rayleigh's Method)

Consider a simply supported shaft of length $L$ carrying a rotor of mass $m$ at its mid-span. Let the centre of gravity of the rotor be at a small radial distance $e$ (eccentricity) from the geometric axis of the shaft.

### Step 1 — Define the System

When the shaft is at rest, the rotor's weight $W = m \cdot g$ causes a static deflection $\delta$ at the mid-span (per the beam formula):

$$\delta = \frac{W L^3}{48 E I} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot L^3}{48 E I}$$

### Step 2 — Force Balance at the Whirling Condition

When the shaft rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, suppose the geometric centre of the shaft has deflected by an additional amount $y$ from its original position. The total radial distance of the rotor's CG from the axis of rotation becomes:

$$r = y + e$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Forces Acting on the Rotor

Two forces act on the rotor in the radial direction:

**(a) Centrifugal force** (outwards):
$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (y + e)$$

**(b) Elastic restoring force from the shaft** (inwards, spring force):
$$F_s = k \cdot y$$

Where $k$ is the lateral stiffness of the shaft:
$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{48 E I}{L^3}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Dynamic Equilibrium

At steady whirling, the elastic restoring force must equal the centrifugal force:

$$k \cdot y = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (y + e)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Deflection $y$

Rearranging the equation:

$$k \cdot y - m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot y = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot e$$

$$y \cdot (k - m \cdot \omega^2) = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot e$$

$$y = \frac{m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot e}{k - m \cdot \omega^2}$$

### Step 6 — Identify the Critical Condition

The denominator $(k - m \omega^2)$ becomes zero when:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

At this condition, the deflection $y$ becomes infinite (resonance), and the shaft whirls uncontrollably. This angular velocity $\omega_c$ is the **critical angular velocity**.

### Step 7 — Express in Terms of Static Deflection

From the static relation, $k = m \cdot g / \delta$, therefore:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{m g / \delta}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$$

### Step 8 — Convert to Rotational Speed in rpm

Using $N_c = \frac{60 \cdot \omega_c}{2\pi}$:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} = \frac{30}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$$

### Step 9 — Substitute $g = 9810 \text{ mm/s}^2$ for $\delta$ in mm

$$N_c = \frac{30}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{9810}{\delta}} = \frac{30}{\pi} \cdot \frac{99.045}{\sqrt{\delta}} \approx \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} \quad \text{rpm}$$

This is the **final, board-friendly form** that KTU examiners expect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass Cancellation Note:** Observe that the rotor mass $m$ has **disappeared** from the final formula. This is a beautiful result of Rayleigh's method — the critical speed depends *only* on the static deflection (which is itself proportional to mass). A heavier rotor will deflect the shaft more, but the critical speed is still the same.

## 3.2 Derivation of Dunkerley's Formula (Multiple Rotors)

When a shaft carries $n$ rotors at different positions, each contributes its own deflection. The combined critical speed is given by **Dunkerley's empirical formula**:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{N_c}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N_{c1}}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{N_{c2}}} + \dots + \frac{1}{\sqrt{N_{cn}}}$$

### Derivation Sketch

1. Assume the shaft has a static deflection profile $y(x)$ due to all rotors' weights.
2. The strain energy stored in the shaft (bending) is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^L E I \left(\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}\right)^2 dx$$

3. The total kinetic energy (or equivalently, the work done by centrifugal forces) is summed over all $n$ rotors:

$$T = \frac{1}{2} \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot y_i^2$$

4. Equating $T = U$ at the critical condition and simplifying yields Dunkerley's formula.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

**Problem:** A simply supported steel shaft of length **600 mm** and diameter **40 mm** carries a rotor of mass **5 kg** at its mid-span. Calculate the critical speed of the shaft. Take $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ and $g = 9810 \text{ mm/s}^2$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the second moment of area (for circular cross-section):**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (40)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 2{,}560{,}000}{64}$$

$$I = 125{,}664 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the static deflection at mid-span:**

The weight of the rotor is $W = m \cdot g = 5 \times 9810 = 49{,}050 \text{ N}$.

$$\delta = \frac{W L^3}{48 E I} = \frac{49050 \times (600)^3}{48 \times (2.1 \times 10^5) \times 125664}$$

Numerator: $49050 \times 2.16 \times 10^8 = 1.0595 \times 10^{13}$

Denominator: $48 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 1.25664 \times 10^5 = 1.2665 \times 10^{12}$

$$\delta = \frac{1.0595 \times 10^{13}}{1.2665 \times 10^{12}} = 8.366 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the critical speed:**

$$N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{8.366}} = \frac{946}{2.892} \approx 327 \text{ rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The shaft will whirling at 327 rpm. The vehicle component must operate at speeds significantly different from this value (typically $N_{operating} \le 0.8 \cdot N_c$ for sub-critical design).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shaft Rigidity & Critical Speed Analysis

The following Mermaid diagram maps the workflow used in the design office when evaluating a shaft for rigidity and critical speed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Shaft Design Input] --> inA1[Collect Geometry: Length L, Diameter d]
    inA1 --> inA2[Collect Material: E, G, density]
    inA2 --> inA3[Collect Loads: W, T, position of rotors]
    inA3 --> calcA1[Compute Section Properties: I, J]
    calcA1 --> calcA2[Compute Deflection y under Loads]
    calcA2 --> checkA1{Deflection within Allowable Limit?}
    checkA1 -- No --> modA1[Increase Diameter or Change Material]
    modA1 --> calcA2
    checkA1 -- Yes --> calcA3[Compute Static Deflection delta at Rotor]
    calcA3 --> calcA4[Compute Critical Speed Nc using Rayleigh]
    calcA4 --> checkA2{N_operating is 20 percent away from Nc?}
    checkA2 -- No --> modA2[Modify Span or Stiffness]
    modA2 --> calcA2
    checkA2 -- Yes --> endA[Design Accepted: Rigidity and Critical Speed OK]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Forces Acting on a Whirling Shaft

The following Mermaid diagram is a **functional block representation** of the force interaction in a rotating shaft (adapted for Mermaid compatibility).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rotorA[Rotor of Mass m] -->|Centrifugal Force Fc = m omega squared r| springA[Shaft acts as Spring of Stiffness k]
    springA -->|Restoring Force Fs = k times y| rotorA
    rotorA -->|Eccentricity e creates unbalanced mass| balanceA[Unbalance excites lateral vibration]
    balanceA -->|If omega equals omega natural| resonA[Resonance: Whirling at Critical Speed]
    resonA -->|If damping insufficient| failA[Shaft Failure / Bearing Damage]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Operating Speed Selection Relative to Critical Speed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA[Calculate Nc] --> qB{Operating Speed N_op chosen}
    qB -- N_op less than 0.8 Nc --> label1[Sub-critical: Safe but requires good balancing]
    qB -- N_op between 0.8 Nc and 1.2 Nc --> label2[FORBIDDEN: Resonance zone - redesign]
    qB -- N_op greater than 1.2 Nc --> label3[Super-critical: Safe if passed through resonance during run-up]
    label2 --> qC[Increase shaft diameter or reduce span]
    qC --> qA
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define critical speed of a shaft. Why is it also called whirling speed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Critical speed of a shaft** is the rotational speed at which the natural frequency of lateral (transverse) vibration of the shaft coincides with its rotational frequency, causing resonance. At this speed, the shaft becomes dynamically unstable, and any small lateral disturbance grows in amplitude without bound (in an ideal undamped system), leading to catastrophic failure.

It is called the **whirling speed** because, at this rotational speed, the shaft appears to "whirl" around its geometric axis — the lateral deflection and rotation couple to produce a precessing motion of the shaft, much like a spinning top that wobbles as it slows down.

> *Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for resonance concept, 1 mark for whirling explanation.*

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**State the formula for critical speed of a shaft using Rayleigh's method, and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The critical speed of a shaft, by Rayleigh's method, is given by:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} \quad \text{rpm}$$

Where:
- $N_c$ = Critical speed of the shaft, in **rpm** (revolutions per minute).
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity, $= 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 9810 \text{ mm/s}^2$.
- $\delta$ = Static deflection of the shaft at the location of the rotor, due to the weight of the rotor (in the same unit as $g$).

> *Award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for $g$ and $\delta$ definitions, 1 mark for mentioning unit consistency (mm with mm, m with m).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two Part B questions per module, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer ONE of the two.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A simply supported shaft of length 800 mm and diameter 50 mm is made of steel ($E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $G = 0.8 \times 10^5$ N/mm²). It carries a pulley of mass 10 kg at its mid-span.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Calculate the lateral deflection of the shaft at the pulley location, assuming the pulley's weight acts as a concentrated load at the centre.**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Using Rayleigh's method, determine the critical speed of the shaft. Comment on whether the operating speed of 1500 rpm is safe.**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Section property (second moment of area, $I$):**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (50)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^6}{64}$$

$$I = 3.068 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Weight of the pulley (concentrated load at mid-span):**

$$W = m \cdot g = 10 \times 9810 = 98100 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum deflection at mid-span (simply supported beam with central point load):**

$$y = \frac{W L^3}{48 E I} = \frac{98100 \times (800)^3}{48 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times 3.068 \times 10^5}$$

Numerator: $98100 \times 5.12 \times 10^8 = 5.0227 \times 10^{13}$

Denominator: $48 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 3.068 \times 10^5 = 2.9453 \times 10^{12}$

$$y = \frac{5.0227 \times 10^{13}}{2.9453 \times 10^{12}} = 17.05 \text{ mm}$$

> **Result:** Lateral deflection at mid-span = **17.05 mm** *(2 marks for formula, 3 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer with units).*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Apply Rayleigh's formula:**

The static deflection at the rotor (pulley) is the same $y$ calculated in Part (a), i.e., $\delta = 17.05$ mm.

$$N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{17.05}} = \frac{946}{4.129} \approx 229.1 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Safety check:**

For sub-critical safe operation, the rule of thumb is:

$$N_{operating} \le 0.8 \times N_c$$

$$0.8 \times 229.1 = 183.3 \text{ rpm}$$

Since the **operating speed (1500 rpm) is much greater than 183.3 rpm**, the shaft is **not in the sub-critical zone**.

**Step 3 — Check for super-critical operation:**

For super-critical safe operation, $N_{operating} \ge 1.2 \times N_c$:

$$1.2 \times 229.1 = 274.9 \text{ rpm}$$

Since $1500 \text{ rpm} \ge 274.9 \text{ rpm}$, the shaft is **operating in the super-critical zone** and is therefore **safe**, provided the run-up process passes through the critical speed (229 rpm) quickly and with minimal excitation.

> **Result:** $N_c \approx 229$ rpm, operating speed 1500 rpm is safe (super-critical). *(2 marks for Rayleigh formula, 2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for safety comment, 1 mark for conclusion).*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A hollow steel drive shaft of an automobile has an outer diameter of 60 mm and inner diameter of 40 mm. It is supported in bearings 1.2 m apart and transmits a torque of 1500 N·m at 2000 rpm. The shaft carries a gear of mass 25 kg at mid-span.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Calculate the angular twist of the shaft due to the transmitted torque. Take $G = 80$ kN/mm².**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**If a bending moment of 600 N·m acts simultaneously at mid-span, determine the lateral deflection. Also compute the critical speed using Rayleigh's method. Take $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm².**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Polar moment of inertia of the hollow shaft:**

$$J = \frac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi [(60)^4 - (40)^4]}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi [12.96 \times 10^6 - 2.56 \times 10^6]}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 10.4 \times 10^6}{32}$$

$$J = 1.021 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Torque conversion and substitution:**

$T = 1500 \text{ N·m} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$, $L = 1.2 \text{ m} = 1200 \text{ mm}$, $G = 80 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$.

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6 \times 1200}{80 \times 10^3 \times 1.021 \times 10^6}$$

$$\theta = \frac{1.8 \times 10^9}{8.168 \times 10^{10}} = 0.02204 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees: $\theta = 0.02204 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 1.263°$

> **Result:** Angular twist = **0.02204 rad ≈ 1.26°**. *(2 marks for $J$ calculation, 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer).*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Bending stress approach (alternative to direct load):**

A bending moment of $M = 600$ N·m at mid-span is equivalent (in terms of deflection) to a point load that produces the same bending moment. The equivalent central point load on a simply supported beam is:

$$W_{eq} = \frac{4 M}{L} = \frac{4 \times 600{,}000 \text{ N·mm}}{1200 \text{ mm}} = 2000 \text{ N}$$

> Note: $600 \text{ N·m} = 600 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm} = 600{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$.

**Step 2 — Add the gear weight to get the total effective load:**

Weight of gear: $W_{gear} = 25 \times 9810 = 245{,}250 \text{ N}$.

Total effective load: $W_{total} = 2000 + 245{,}250 = 247{,}250 \text{ N}$.

**Step 3 — Second moment of area (for bending):**

$$I = \frac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 10.4 \times 10^6}{64} = 5.105 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4 — Lateral deflection at mid-span:**

$$y = \frac{W_{total} L^3}{48 E I} = \frac{247250 \times (1200)^3}{48 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 5.105 \times 10^5}$$

Numerator: $247250 \times 1.728 \times 10^9 = 4.2723 \times 10^{14}$

Denominator: $48 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 5.105 \times 10^5 = 5.146 \times 10^{12}$

$$y = \frac{4.2723 \times 10^{14}}{5.146 \times 10^{12}} = 83.03 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Critical speed by Rayleigh's method:**

$$N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{83.03}} = \frac{946}{9.111} \approx 103.8 \text{ rpm}$$

> **Result:** Lateral deflection ≈ **83 mm**, Critical speed ≈ **103.8 rpm**. Since the operating speed (2000 rpm) is far above $1.2 \times 103.8 = 124.6$ rpm, the shaft operates super-critically and is safe.
>
> *(2 marks for equivalent load concept, 2 marks for $I$ and $y$ calculation, 2 marks for critical speed, 1 mark for safety comment).*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Rigidity & Critical Speed Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (most common error):** Students often mix mm and m in the Rayleigh formula. If $g = 9810$ mm/s² is used, $\delta$ MUST be in mm. If $g = 9.81$ m/s² is used, $\delta$ MUST be in m. **Failure to do this loses 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting the gear / rotor weight:** In problems where the shaft carries a rotor, the static deflection must include the rotor's weight. Many students compute deflection only due to the bending moment, ignoring the rotor mass.
> 3. **Misidentifying the support condition:** A simply supported beam formula cannot be used for a cantilever. Always check the boundary conditions stated in the problem.
> 4. **Wrong polar moment of inertia for hollow shafts:** A common mistake is to write $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ for hollow shafts. The correct form is $J = \pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4) / 32$.
> 5. **Not commenting on safety:** The "Comment on safety" sub-part is a separate 1–2 mark item. Many students compute $N_c$ but forget to compare with operating speed.
> 6. **Conflating torsional rigidity with bending rigidity:** $\theta = TL / GJ$ is for **torsion**, $y = WL^3 / 48EI$ is for **bending**. They use different material constants ($G$ vs. $E$) and section properties ($J$ vs. $I$). Mixing them is a fatal error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rigidity & Critical Speed**

- **Rigidity** = resistance to deformation; quantified by *deflection* (bending) or *angular twist* (torsion).
- **Shaft modeled as simply supported beam** for standard design problems.
- **Three core formulas** you MUST memorize:
  - $y_{point} = WL^3 / 48EI$
  - $y_{UDL} = 5wL^4 / 384EI$
  - $\theta = TL / GJ$
- **Polar moment of inertia:**
  - Solid: $J = \pi d^4 / 32$
  - Hollow: $J = \pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4) / 32$
- **Critical speed (Rayleigh):** $N_c = 946 / \sqrt{\delta}$ (with $\delta$ in **mm**), or $N_c = 29.88 / \sqrt{\delta}$ (with $\delta$ in **m**).
- **Critical speed depends only on static deflection, not directly on rotor mass.**
- **Operating zones:**
  - **Sub-critical:** $N_{op} \le 0.8 N_c$ → safe, requires good balancing.
  - **Forbidden zone:** $0.8 N_c \le N_{op} \le 1.2 N_c$ → resonance, NEVER operate here.
  - **Super-critical:** $N_{op} \ge 1.2 N_c$ → safe, must pass through critical zone quickly during run-up.
- **For multiple rotors on a shaft**, use Dunkerley's formula:
  $\frac{1}{\sqrt{N_c}} = \sum \frac{1}{\sqrt{N_{ci}}}$
- **Torsional rigidity in torsion** vs. **lateral rigidity in bending** — never confuse the two.
- **Automotive context:** Propeller shafts, drive shafts, and crankshafts are the primary automotive applications; in turbochargers, the shaft operates super-critically at 100,000+ rpm.
- **Always check units** before applying Rayleigh's formula — g in mm/s² pairs with $\delta$ in mm; g in m/s² pairs with $\delta$ in m.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Design for Static and Fatigue Loads"?

In automotive engineering, every component — from a connecting rod to a leaf spring — experiences two broad classes of loading:

1. **Static (Steady) Loads** — Loads that do not vary (or vary very slowly) with time. Examples: the weight of a parked vehicle on its suspension struts, the preload on a bolt holding the cylinder head, the centrifugal force on a flywheel at constant engine speed.
2. **Fatigue (Cyclic / Dynamic) Loads** — Loads that fluctuate repeatedly with time. Examples: the cyclic bending stress in a valve spring, the alternating torsion in a crankshaft, the repeated tension–compression in a connecting rod during the power and exhaust strokes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Design for static loads* ensures that the induced stresses remain below the allowable stress, considering an appropriate factor of safety. *Design for fatigue loads* ensures that the component has adequate life under repeated loading, considering endurance limit, mean stress effects, stress concentrations, and the required factor of safety.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cracked Spoon" Story

Imagine bending a new stainless-steel spoon slowly, with great care, **once** — it bends, but does not break. That is **static loading**. The spoon survived because the peak stress stayed below the yield strength.

Now bend the spoon back and forth repeatedly (say 20 times). It suddenly **snaps** at a stress that is far below the yield strength. The spoon failed by **fatigue**. No single bend was enough to break it, but cumulative micro-damage at the surface produced a crack that grew until the cross-section could not carry the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A component designed only for static strength can still fail under cyclic loading. The maximum cyclic stress must be kept below the **endurance limit** (for steels) or the component must be designed for a finite number of cycles (for non-ferrous metals that have no true endurance limit).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Module 1

- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (Sut)** — the maximum stress a material can withstand.
- **Yield Strength (Sy)** — the stress at which a ductile material begins to deform plastically.
- **Endurance Limit (Se)** — the stress below which a ferrous material can endure an infinite number of cycles (typically $\mathbf{S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut}}$ for steels with $S_{ut} < 1400 \text{ MPa}$).
- **Factor of Safety (n)** — the ratio of strength to applied stress.
- **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$)** — the average of maximum and minimum cycle stresses.
- **Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$)** — half the algebraic difference between maximum and minimum cycle stresses.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Automotive Design

A KTU 2024 graduate entering the automotive industry will design or validate components such as **crankshafts, connecting rods, leaf springs, coil springs, steering arms, axles, and propeller shafts**. Every one of these components sees a *combination* of static and dynamic loads. An under-designed component leads to warranty failures and recalls; an over-designed one increases vehicle weight and reduces fuel efficiency.
