# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stresses for Design

A KTU 2024 board examiner expects students to correctly classify a stress as belonging to one of these categories before applying a design formula:

| Stress Type | Description | Common Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| Steady (Static) | Constant in time | Tightening torque on a head bolt |
| Completely Reversed | $\sigma_m = 0$, $\sigma_{min} = -\sigma_{max}$ | Bending stress in a rotating shaft at the journal |
| Repeated | $\sigma_{min} = 0$, $\sigma_{max} > 0$ | Tension in a chain link (only loaded on one side) |
| Fluctuating | Both mean and amplitude are non-zero | Axial stress in a connecting rod |

The mean stress and stress amplitude are computed as:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

## 2.2 Theories of Failure for Static Loading

When a component sees a *multi-axial* state of stress (e.g., a shaft under bending **and** torsion), KTU 2024 allows the use of any one of three classical theories for ductile materials.

### 2.2.1 Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine, 1858)
A failure is predicted when the maximum principal stress reaches the ultimate strength.

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{S_{ut}}{n}$$

Conservative; rarely used in modern machine design.

### 2.2.2 Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca, 1864)
Failure is predicted when the maximum shear stress reaches the shear yield strength.

$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{S_y}{2n}$$

Equivalent uniaxial form for shaft design under bending + torsion:

$$\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2} = \frac{S_y}{n}$$

### 2.2.3 Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises, 1913)
Failure is predicted when the distortional strain energy equals that at yield in a simple tension test.

$$\sigma' = \frac{S_y}{n}$$

For combined bending and torsion (2-D stress state):

$$\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$$

For a 3-D state:

$$\sigma' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (\sigma_y - \sigma_z)^2 + (\sigma_z - \sigma_x)^2 + 6(\tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** For ductile materials under 2-D or 3-D stress, prefer the **Distortion Energy Theory**. It gives the most accurate prediction of yielding in ductile materials like the medium-carbon steels used in gears and shafts.

## 2.3 Standard Shaft Design Equations (Combined Bending + Torsion)

For a circular shaft of diameter $d$, with bending moment $M$ and torque $T$:

$$\sigma = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}, \qquad \tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$

The diameter formulas, after substituting the equivalent stresses:

$$d = \left[ \frac{16n}{\pi S_y} \sqrt{4M^2 + T^2} \right]^{1/3} \quad \text{(MSST)}$$

$$d = \left[ \frac{16n}{\pi S_y} \sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} \right]^{1/3} \quad \text{(DE)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the DE formula has $\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$, which is *larger* than the MSST term $\sqrt{4M^2 + T^2}$. This means the **DE theory actually demands a slightly larger diameter** than MSST for the same shaft. KTU expects you to know this counter-intuitive result.

## 2.4 Fatigue Failure — The S-N Curve (Wöhler Diagram)

The **S-N curve** plots stress amplitude (or maximum stress in a reversed cycle) against the number of cycles to failure $N$, on a semi-log scale. For steels, the curve flattens at the **endurance limit** $S_e'$; for aluminium and other non-ferrous metals, the curve keeps falling, so a *finite-life* design is performed at a chosen $N$.

For a steel with $S_{ut} < 1400$ MPa:

$$S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$$

For $S_{ut} \geq 1400$ MPa (high-strength steels):

$$S_e' = 700 \text{ MPa}$$

For non-ferrous metals: no true endurance limit; design is done at a target $N$ (typically $10^6$ or $10^8$ cycles).

## 2.5 Modifying the Endurance Limit — Marin Factors

The **Marin equation** adjusts the rotating-beam endurance limit for non-ideal conditions:

$$S_e = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, k_f \, S_e'$$

| Factor | Name | Effect | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k_a$ | Surface factor | Reduces for rough finishes | From Shigley charts / formula |
| $k_b$ | Size factor | Reduces for large sections ($d > 8$ mm) | $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm |
| $k_c$ | Load factor | 1.0 for bending/torsion, 0.85 for axial | KTU 2024 value |
| $k_d$ | Temperature factor | 1.0 at room temperature, reduces at high T | From Shigley chart |
| $k_e$ | Reliability factor | 0.897 for 90 %, 0.868 for 95 %, 0.814 for 99 % | KTU 2024 table |
| $k_f$ | Miscellaneous factor | 1.0 unless a known derating exists | Application-specific |

For surface factor (bending/torsion):

$$k_a = a \, S_{ut}^b$$

with $a, b$ from the standard surface-finish table.

## 2.6 Mean Stress Correction Theories (Constant-Life Diagrams)

When a stress cycle is not fully reversed, the mean stress $\sigma_m$ reduces the allowable stress amplitude $\sigma_a$. KTU 2024 expects students to know **all four** of the following criteria:

| Criterion | Equation | Intercepts on $\sigma_a$-$\sigma_m$ plot |
|---|---|---|
| Soderberg | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | $S_e$ on $\sigma_a$ axis, $S_y$ on $\sigma_m$ axis |
| Modified Goodman | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | $S_e$ on $\sigma_a$ axis, $S_{ut}$ on $\sigma_m$ axis |
| Gerber | $\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | $S_e$ on $\sigma_a$ axis, $S_{ut}$ on $\sigma_m$ axis (parabolic) |
| ASME-Elliptic | $\left(\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^2 = 1$ | $S_e$ on $\sigma_a$ axis, $S_y$ on $\sigma_m$ axis (elliptical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ordering** (most conservative to least conservative, for $\sigma_m > 0$):
> **Soderberg** $<$ **ASME-Elliptic** $<$ **Modified Goodman** $<$ **Gerber** (in terms of $n$)
> KTU examiners frequently ask: "Which is the most conservative?" — and the answer is **Soderberg**, because it uses the *yield* strength on the $\sigma_m$ axis.

## 2.7 Stress Concentration and Notch Sensitivity

Any abrupt change in geometry (keyway, shoulder fillet, oil hole, thread root) raises the local stress. The theoretical concentration factor $K_t$ comes from standard charts. For fatigue, the **fatigue stress concentration factor** $K_f$ is used:

$$K_f = 1 + q \, (K_t - 1)$$

where $q$ is the **notch sensitivity** ($0 \le q \le 1$). For $q = 1$ the notch is fully effective; for $q = 0$ the notch is ignored.

**Neuber's Equation** (for estimating $q$ in ductile steels):

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a}/\sqrt{r}}$$

with $a$ = Neuber constant (≈ 0.25 in$^{0.5}$ for steels, or 0.635 mm$^{0.5}$ in SI), and $r$ = notch radius.

In the Goodman equation, the *effective* stress is $K_f \sigma_a$ and $K_f \sigma_m$:

$$\frac{K_f \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{K_f \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

## 2.8 Cumulative Fatigue Damage — Miner's Rule

For **variable-amplitude loading**, the Palmgren–Miner linear damage hypothesis states:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

where $n_i$ = number of applied cycles at stress level $i$, $N_i$ = cycles to failure at that level.
- $D < 1$ → no failure
- $D = 1$ → failure
- $D > 1$ → not safe in theory, but acceptable in practice if the small exceedance is rare.

> [!TIP]
> For a constant-amplitude loading the design equation is simply $N \ge 1$ (Miner's sum = 1). For a random-amplitude loading on a chassis component, a **rainflow counting algorithm** is first applied to decompose the load history, then Miner's rule is evaluated.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 1 numerical questions. Memorise it.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa |
| 2 | Stress amplitude | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa |
| 3 | Endurance limit (steel) | $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ | MPa |
| 4 | Marin equation | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$ | MPa |
| 5 | Surface factor (bending) | $k_a = a \, S_{ut}^{\,b}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Size factor | $k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Notch sensitivity (Neuber) | $q = 1 / (1 + \sqrt{a} / \sqrt{r})$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Fatigue concentration factor | $K_f = 1 + q (K_t - 1)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Factor of safety (Goodman) | $1/n = K_f \sigma_a/S_e + K_f \sigma_m/S_{ut}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Factor of safety (Soderberg) | $1/n = K_f \sigma_a/S_e + K_f \sigma_m/S_y$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Factor of safety (Gerber) | $n = \dfrac{1}{2} (S_{ut}/\sigma_m)^2 (S_e/\sigma_a) \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + (2 S_e \sigma_m / (S_{ut} \sigma_a))^2} \right]$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Shaft diameter (DE) | $d = \left[ \dfrac{16n}{\pi S_y} \sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} \right]^{1/3}$ | mm |
| 13 | Shaft diameter (MSST) | $d = \left[ \dfrac{16n}{\pi S_y} \sqrt{4M^2 + T^2} \right]^{1/3}$ | mm |
| 14 | Miner's damage | $D = \sum n_i / N_i$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | DE equivalent stress | $\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3 \tau^2}$ | MPa |

## 2.10 Real-World Utility

These equations are used daily in the design offices of OEMs like **Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Mahindra, Ashok Leyland, and Mercedes-Benz R&D India (Bangalore)**. They are codified in the **FEM/CAE software** (ANSYS, ABAQUS, HyperWorks) that every KTU graduate will touch during internship or employment. A typical workflow:

1. Static analysis → check von Mises stress $ \le S_y/n$.
2. Modal analysis → find natural frequencies and avoid resonance with road/engine excitation.
3. Fatigue analysis (nCode, fe-safe) → use the Marin-modified endurance limit and Goodman or SWT criterion on the time-history stress to predict cycles to failure.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the von Mises Equivalent Stress (2-D Case)

The **Distortion Energy Theory** states that yielding begins when the total distortional strain energy in a complex stress state equals that in a uniaxial tension test at yield.

The total strain energy per unit volume in an elastic body is:

$$u = \tfrac{1}{2}(\sigma_x \varepsilon_x + \sigma_y \varepsilon_y + \sigma_z \varepsilon_z + \tau_{xy} \gamma_{xy} + \tau_{yz} \gamma_{yz} + \tau_{zx} \gamma_{zx})$$

Using Hooke's law for 3-D:

$$\varepsilon_x = \tfrac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$$

After substituting and separating the **volumetric** (dilatational) part from the **distortional** part, the distortional energy becomes:

$$u_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (\sigma_y - \sigma_z)^2 + (\sigma_z - \sigma_x)^2 + 6(\tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2)\right]$$

Setting $u_d$ equal to the distortional energy at uniaxial yield $\dfrac{1+\nu}{6E}(2 S_y^2)$ and taking the ratio:

$$\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (\sigma_y - \sigma_z)^2 + (\sigma_z - \sigma_x)^2 + 6(\tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{yz}^2 + \tau_{zx}^2)} = \frac{S_y}{n}$$

This is the **von Mises equivalent stress** $\sigma'$. For a **2-D plane stress** state ($\sigma_z = \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zx} = 0$):

$$\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - \sigma_x \sigma_y + 3 \tau_{xy}^2}$$

For pure **bending + torsion** of a shaft ($\sigma_y = 0$):

$$\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3 \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Substituting $\sigma_x = 32M / (\pi d^3)$ and $\tau_{xy} = 16T / (\pi d^3)$:

$$\sigma' = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$$

Setting $\sigma' = S_y / n$ and solving for $d$:

$$d = \left[\frac{16n}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}\right]^{1/3}$$

This is the **standard DE shaft design equation** that appears in KTU board exam numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean-Stress Correction Lines

Start with the rotating-beam test data: at $R = -1$ (fully reversed), the allowable stress amplitude is $S_e$. At $R = 0$ (zero-to-tension, $\sigma_m = \sigma_a$), the specimen is in static tension with a small fluctuation; failure occurs when $\sigma_{max}$ reaches $S_{ut}$ (for a brittle) or $S_y$ (for a ductile, conservative).

### 3.2.1 Soderberg Line (Most Conservative)
The line passes through $(0, S_e)$ on the $\sigma_a$-axis and $(S_y, 0)$ on the $\sigma_m$-axis. Using the two-point intercept form of a straight line:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$$

When a factor of safety $n$ is applied to **both** the cyclic and mean stresses:

$$\frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{n \sigma_m}{S_y} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{n}$$

This is the form that appears in the KTU 2024 question bank.

### 3.2.2 Modified Goodman Line
Same procedure, but use $S_{ut}$ instead of $S_y$ on the $\sigma_m$-axis. This makes the line less conservative (because $S_{ut} > S_y$).

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

### 3.2.3 Gerber Parabola
Gerber observed that the test data for ductile materials (especially steels) lies *above* the Goodman line, forming a parabola. The empirical equation is:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1 \qquad (n = 1)$$

Applying a factor of safety $n$ uniformly:

$$\frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$$

This is a quadratic in $n$:

$$n^2 \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 + n \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right) - 1 = 0$$

Solving with the quadratic formula (positive root):

$$n = \frac{-\sigma_a/S_e + \sqrt{(\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + 4(\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2}}{2(\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2}$$

This can be rearranged (Shigley form) into:

$$n = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m}\right)^2 \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \left[-1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2 S_e \sigma_m}{S_{ut} \sigma_a}\right)^2} \right]$$

> [!TIP]
> The Gerber form is the **default** in Indian automotive R&D because it gives the most accurate (though less conservative) prediction for ductile steels.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor

For a sharp notch, the theoretical elastic stress concentration factor $K_t$ gives the *local* stress at the notch root:

$$\sigma_{max,local} = K_t \sigma_{nom}$$

However, in cyclic loading the material develops a small plastic zone at the notch root, which **relaxes** the peak. The **notch sensitivity** $q$ is defined as:

$$q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad K_f = 1 + q (K_t - 1)$$

Neuber observed experimentally that for ductile steels:

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a}/\sqrt{r}}$$

where $a$ is the Neuber constant and $r$ is the notch radius. For $r \to \infty$, $q \to 0$ (no notch effect). For $r \to 0$, $q \to 1$ (notch fully effective).

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Static Shaft Design

**Problem (KTU 2024 typical Part-B style):**
A transmission shaft is subjected to a bending moment $M = 1200$ N·m and a torque $T = 900$ N·m. The material has $S_{ut} = 560$ MPa and $S_y = 380$ MPa. Take a factor of safety $n = 2$. Calculate the shaft diameter using (a) Maximum Shear Stress Theory and (b) Distortion Energy Theory.

### Solution — Part (a): MSST
The MSST equivalent stress is:

$$\sigma_{eq,MSST} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2} = \frac{S_y}{n} = \frac{380}{2} = 190 \text{ MPa}$$

For a circular shaft, $\sigma = 32M / (\pi d^3)$ and $\tau = 16T / (\pi d^3)$. Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{d^3}\sqrt{(32M)^2 + 4(16T)^2} = 190$$

$$\frac{16}{d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + T^2} = 190$$

Substituting $M = 1.2 \times 10^6$ N·mm and $T = 0.9 \times 10^6$ N·mm:

$$\frac{16}{d^3}\sqrt{4(1.2\times 10^6)^2 + (0.9\times 10^6)^2} = 190$$

Compute each term:

$$4M^2 = 4(1.2\times 10^6)^2 = 5.76 \times 10^{12}$$
$$T^2 = (0.9\times 10^6)^2 = 0.81 \times 10^{12}$$
$$4M^2 + T^2 = 6.57 \times 10^{12}$$
$$\sqrt{4M^2 + T^2} = 2.563 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{16 \times 2.563 \times 10^6}{d^3} = 190$$
$$\frac{4.101 \times 10^7}{d^3} = 190$$
$$d^3 = \frac{4.101 \times 10^7}{190} = 2.158 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$d = (2.158 \times 10^5)^{1/3} = 59.92 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt the **next standard size**: $d = 60$ mm.

### Solution — Part (b): Distortion Energy Theory
The DE equivalent stress is:

$$\sigma_{eq,DE} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} = \frac{S_y}{n} = 190 \text{ MPa}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{16}{d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} = 190$$

Compute:

$$3T^2 = 3(0.9 \times 10^6)^2 = 2.43 \times 10^{12}$$
$$4M^2 + 3T^2 = 5.76 \times 10^{12} + 2.43 \times 10^{12} = 8.19 \times 10^{12}$$
$$\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} = 2.862 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{16 \times 2.862 \times 10^6}{d^3} = 190$$
$$\frac{4.579 \times 10^7}{d^3} = 190$$
$$d^3 = 2.410 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$d = (2.410 \times 10^5)^{1/3} = 62.30 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt $d = 65$ mm (next standard size).

**Comparison:** The DE theory requires a larger diameter (≈ 62.3 mm) than the MSST theory (≈ 59.9 mm) for this particular shaft. Hence the DE theory is **more conservative** for combined bending + torsion in circular shafts.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Fatigue Design with Goodman and Gerber

**Problem:**
A steel component has $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa. It is subjected to fluctuating axial stresses with $\sigma_{max} = 240$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = 60$ MPa. The diameter is 50 mm and the surface is **machined**. Take a reliability of 90 %.

### Step 1 — Compute mean and amplitude
$$\sigma_m = \frac{240 + 60}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_a = \frac{240 - 60}{2} = 90 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 2 — Base endurance limit
$$S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 620 = 310 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Marin factors

**Surface factor** (machined, $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa): $k_a = a \, S_{ut}^{\,b}$. From Shigley table, machined → $a = 4.51$ MPa, $b = -0.265$ (for $S_{ut}$ in MPa).

$$k_a = 4.51 \times 620^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1697 = 0.765$$

**Size factor** (axial loading, $k_b$ is not used for axial): the size factor applies only to bending and torsion. For axial fatigue we keep $k_b = 1$ (or omit it, depending on convention).

**Load factor** (axial): $k_c = 0.85$.

**Temperature factor** (room temperature): $k_d = 1.0$.

**Reliability factor** (90 %): $k_e = 0.897$.

**Miscellaneous**: $k_f = 1.0$.

**Modified endurance limit:**

$$S_e = 0.765 \times 1.0 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 310 = 181.2 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4 — Modified Goodman criterion

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{90}{181.2} + \frac{150}{620}$$

$$= 0.4967 + 0.2419 = 0.7386$$

$$n_{Goodman} = \frac{1}{0.7386} = 1.354$$

### Step 5 — Gerber criterion

Compute the two key ratios:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = \frac{90}{181.2} = 0.4967$$
$$\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{150}{620} = 0.2419$$

Apply the Shigley form of Gerber:

$$n = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m}\right)^2 \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \left[-1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2 S_e \sigma_m}{S_{ut} \sigma_a}\right)^2}\right]$$

Compute intermediate quantities:

$$\frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m} = \frac{620}{150} = 4.1333$$
$$\left(\frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m}\right)^2 = 17.084$$
$$\frac{2 S_e \sigma_m}{S_{ut} \sigma_a} = \frac{2 \times 181.2 \times 150}{620 \times 90} = \frac{54360}{55800} = 0.9742$$
$$\left(\frac{2 S_e \sigma_m}{S_{ut} \sigma_a}\right)^2 = 0.9491$$
$$\sqrt{1 + 0.9491} = \sqrt{1.9491} = 1.3961$$
$$-1 + 1.3961 = 0.3961$$

Now combine:

$$n = 0.5 \times 17.084 \times 0.4967 \times 0.3961 = 1.681$$

$$\boxed{n_{Gerber} = 1.68}$$

### Step 6 — Comparison and Decision

| Criterion | Factor of Safety $n$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| Modified Goodman | 1.35 | Standard for ductile steels |
| Gerber | 1.68 | Most accurate for ductile steels |

Since both criteria give $n > 1$, the design is safe. If a higher reliability is required, increase the diameter $d$, which increases $k_b$ and $S_e$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Design Decision Flow — Static vs Fatigue

The block diagram below maps the decision tree a KTU 2024 design engineer should follow when a new component is proposed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Receive Load Specification] --> nodeB[Classify Loading Type]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Is Load Constant in Time?}
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeD[Static Load Path]
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeE[Fatigue Load Path]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Compute Nominal Stress sigma]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2{Is Stress Multi-Axial?}
    nodeD2 -- NO --> nodeD3[Use sigma = Sy over n directly]
    nodeD2 -- YES --> nodeD4[Select Failure Theory]
    nodeD4 --> nodeD5[Max Normal Stress Rankine]
    nodeD4 --> nodeD6[Max Shear Stress Tresca]
    nodeD4 --> nodeD7[Distortion Energy von Mises]
    nodeD5 --> nodeD8[Compute Allowable Stress]
    nodeD6 --> nodeD8
    nodeD7 --> nodeD8
    nodeD8 --> nodeD9[Determine Dimensions]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Compute sigma max and sigma min]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE2[Compute sigma m and sigma a]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE3[Compute Se prime = 0.5 Sut]
    nodeE3 --> nodeE4[Apply Marin Factors ka kb kc kd ke kf]
    nodeE4 --> nodeE5[Modified Endurance Limit Se]
    nodeE5 --> nodeF{Is Notch Present?}
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeF1[Compute Kt from Chart]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Compute q using Neuber's Equation]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Kf = 1 plus q times Kt minus 1]
    nodeF3 --> nodeG[Select Mean Stress Criterion]
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Soderberg]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Goodman]
    nodeG --> nodeG3[Gerber]
    nodeG --> nodeG4[ASME Elliptic]
    nodeG1 --> nodeH[Compute n from Equation]
    nodeG2 --> nodeH
    nodeG3 --> nodeH
    nodeG4 --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI{n greater than 1?}
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeJ[Design Safe]
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeK[Iterate Geometry or Material]
```

## 4.2 The S-N Curve and Constant-Life Diagram (Block Architecture)

The schematic below is a **block-level functional architecture** rather than a literal curve. It maps the conceptual regions of the Wöhler diagram and the constant-life (Haigh) diagram that a designer uses to interpret fatigue data.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[Material Property Input: Sut and Sy] --> nodeA2[Compute Base Endurance Limit Se prime]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Apply Marin Modification]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Modified Endurance Limit Se]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Constant Life Diagram Origin]

    nodeB1[Operating Stress Input: sigma max and sigma min] --> nodeB2[Compute Mean Stress sigma m]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Compute Amplitude sigma a]
    nodeB3 --> nodeA5

    nodeA5 --> nodeC1{Compare with Failure Locus}
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Region Above Soderberg Line = Failure]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC3[Region Between Soderberg and Goodman = Marginal]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC4[Region Between Goodman and Gerber = Safe for Ductile]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC5[Region Below ASME Elliptic = Safe Conservative]

    nodeC2 --> nodeD1[Re-design: Increase Size or Use Better Material]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD1
    nodeC4 --> nodeD2[Acceptable Design]
    nodeC5 --> nodeD2
```

## 4.3 Shaft Cross-Section Under Bending and Torsion — Block Architecture

A physical drawing of a stepped shaft is hard to render in Mermaid, so the following block diagram represents the **load flow** through the shaft from input torque to output torque, with all the design checks annotated at critical stations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Input Pulley Torque T1] --> nodeS1[Shaft Section 1: Bearing A]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2[Shaft Section 2: Gear B]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Shaft Section 3: Bearing C]
    nodeS3 --> nodeP2[Output Pulley Torque T2]

    nodeS1 --> nodeC1[Check Station 1: Bending M1, Torsion T1]
    nodeS2 --> nodeC2[Check Station 2: Bending M2, Torsion T1 plus T2]
    nodeS3 --> nodeC3[Check Station 3: Bending M3, Torsion T2]

    nodeC1 --> nodeE1[Compute d1 using DE Formula]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE2[Compute d2 using DE Formula]
    nodeC3 --> nodeE3[Compute d3 using DE Formula]

    nodeE1 --> nodeF1{Largest d Selected}
    nodeE2 --> nodeF1
    nodeE3 --> nodeF1

    nodeF1 --> nodeG1[Apply Keyway Stress Concentration Kf]
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2[Final Diameter d selected]
    nodeG2 --> nodeG3[Check Fatigue Goodman Criterion]
    nodeG3 --> nodeG4[Document Design Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for a KTU viva:** The shaft is sized by the *largest* of the per-section diameters. After a diameter is chosen, the **stress concentration at the keyway** is applied *outside* the design formula (you multiply $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ by $K_f$). This is the standard KTU 2024 sequence.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q.A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
Differentiate between **static loading** and **fatigue loading** in the context of automotive component design. State **two examples** of each from a typical passenger car.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Static loading (1 mark):** A load that does not change with time (or changes very slowly), producing a constant stress in the component. The failure criterion is yielding (or fracture) once the stress exceeds the material's strength divided by a factor of safety.
  - *Examples (1 mark):* weight of a parked car on its suspension strut (constant preload); tightening torque on a cylinder-head bolt; centrifugal force on a flywheel running at constant speed.

- **Fatigue loading (1 mark):** A load that varies cyclically with time, causing the stress in the component to fluctuate between a maximum and a minimum value. Failure can occur at stresses well below the static yield strength, after a finite or infinite number of cycles.
  - *Examples (1 mark):* alternating bending stress in a crankshaft journal due to rotation; tension–compression cycle in a connecting rod during the power and exhaust strokes; coil-spring stress during road-undulation cycling.

---

### Q.A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
What is the **endurance limit** of a material? For what class of materials is it defined, and how is the modified endurance limit computed using **Marin's equation**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The endurance limit $S_e$ is the maximum completely-reversed stress amplitude that a material can withstand for an *infinite* number of cycles (conventionally $10^6$ to $10^8$ cycles) without failure.
- **Material class (1 mark):** The endurance limit is well defined for **ferrous metals (steels and cast irons)** that exhibit a clear "knee" in the S-N curve. For non-ferrous metals like aluminium and copper, the S-N curve never flattens, so design is done at a *finite* target life.
- **Marin's equation (1 mark):** The rotating-beam endurance limit $S_e'$ is modified by six factors for non-ideal conditions: $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$, where $k_a$ is the surface factor, $k_b$ the size factor, $k_c$ the load factor, $k_d$ the temperature factor, $k_e$ the reliability factor, and $k_f$ the miscellaneous factor.

---

## Part B — 14-mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 regulations for Part B: each question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Both sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**. The model solutions show the full step-by-step working expected by the KTU board examiner.

---

### Q.B1.A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

A transmission shaft of a light commercial vehicle is subjected to a **bending moment $M = 1200$ N·m** and a **torque $T = 900$ N·m**. The shaft material has $S_{ut} = 560$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa, and the design factor of safety is **2.0**.

**(a)** Determine the required shaft diameter using **(i) Maximum Shear Stress Theory** and **(ii) Distortion Energy Theory**.

**(b)** Compare the two theories with respect to the diameter obtained, and state which theory gives a more conservative design for the given loading. Justify your answer.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) MSST (Tresca) — 4 marks**

The Tresca equivalent stress is:

$$\sigma_{eq,MSST} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2} = \frac{S_y}{n} = \frac{380}{2} = 190 \text{ MPa}$$

For a circular shaft:

$$\sigma = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}, \quad \tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{16}{d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + T^2} = 190 \text{ MPa}$$

With $M = 1.2 \times 10^6$ N·mm and $T = 0.9 \times 10^6$ N·mm:

$$4M^2 = 5.76 \times 10^{12}, \quad T^2 = 0.81 \times 10^{12}$$

$$4M^2 + T^2 = 6.57 \times 10^{12}, \quad \sqrt{4M^2 + T^2} = 2.563 \times 10^6$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 2.563 \times 10^6}{190} = 2.158 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{d_{MSST} \approx 59.9 \text{ mm} \to \text{adopt } 60 \text{ mm}}$$

**(a) (ii) Distortion Energy Theory — 4 marks**

The DE equivalent stress is:

$$\sigma_{eq,DE} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} = \frac{S_y}{n} = 190 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\frac{16}{d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} = 190$$

Compute:

$$3T^2 = 2.43 \times 10^{12}, \quad 4M^2 + 3T^2 = 8.19 \times 10^{12}, \quad \sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} = 2.862 \times 10^6$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 2.862 \times 10^6}{190} = 2.410 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{d_{DE} \approx 62.3 \text{ mm} \to \text{adopt } 65 \text{ mm}}$$

**(b) Comparison — 6 marks**

- The DE theory gives a **larger** required diameter (62.3 mm) than the MSST theory (59.9 mm) for the same shaft.
- Reason (3 marks): The DE formula contains the term $\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$ while the MSST formula contains $\sqrt{4M^2 + T^2}$. Since $3T^2 > T^2$, the DE term is always larger whenever a non-zero torque is present, so DE demands a larger cross-section.
- **Verdict (3 marks):** The **DE theory is more conservative** for shaft design under combined bending and torsion. However, both theories are widely accepted in industry; the **DE theory is generally preferred** for ductile materials because it is in closer agreement with experimental yield data (about 15 % conservative, vs. Tresca's 30 %).

---

### Q.B1.B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]`

A connecting rod of an engine is subjected to fluctuating axial stresses with $\sigma_{max} = 240$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = 60$ MPa. The material properties are $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa, base endurance limit $S_e' = 310$ MPa, with diameter $d = 50$ mm, **machined** surface finish, and target reliability of 90 %.

**(a)** Compute the **modified endurance limit** by applying the relevant Marin factors.

**(b)** Determine the **factor of safety** using **(i) Modified Goodman** and **(ii) Gerber** criterion. Comment on which criterion is more conservative.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Modified Endurance Limit — 6 marks**

Compute mean and amplitude:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{240 + 60}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_a = \frac{240 - 60}{2} = 90 \text{ MPa}$$

**Marin factors:**

- $k_a$ (machined, $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa): $k_a = 4.51 \times 620^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1697 = 0.765$
- $k_b$ (axial loading): not applicable, take $k_b = 1.0$
- $k_c$ (axial): $k_c = 0.85$
- $k_d$ (room temperature): $k_d = 1.0$
- $k_e$ (90 % reliability): $k_e = 0.897$
- $k_f$: $k_f = 1.0$

Modified endurance limit:

$$S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e' = 0.765 \times 1.0 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 310 = 181.2 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{S_e \approx 181 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(b) (i) Modified Goodman — 4 marks**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{90}{181.2} + \frac{150}{620} = 0.4967 + 0.2419 = 0.7386$$

$$\boxed{n_{Goodman} \approx 1.35}$$

**(b) (ii) Gerber Criterion — 4 marks**

$$n = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m}\right)^2 \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\left[-1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2 S_e \sigma_m}{S_{ut} \sigma_a}\right)^2}\right]$$

Compute the terms:

- $\frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m} = \frac{620}{150} = 4.133 \to (S_{ut}/\sigma_m)^2 = 17.08$
- $\frac{2 S_e \sigma_m}{S_{ut} \sigma_a} = \frac{2 \times 181.2 \times 150}{620 \times 90} = 0.974$
- $\sqrt{1 + 0.974^2} = \sqrt{1.949} = 1.396$
- $-1 + 1.396 = 0.396$

$$n = 0.5 \times 17.08 \times 0.4967 \times 0.396 = 1.68$$

$$\boxed{n_{Gerber} \approx 1.68}$$

**Comment (included in marks):** The Goodman criterion gives a *lower* factor of safety (1.35) than the Gerber criterion (1.68). Hence **Goodman is more conservative** for this design. For ductile materials the Gerber result is usually closer to the experimental value; the Goodman result is a safer lower bound.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $M$ and $T$.** Many students substitute $M = 1200$ in the formula without converting N·m to N·mm. This gives a diameter 1000× too small, and the examiner awards 0 for the numerical answer.
> 2. **Mixing up the MSST and DE formulas.** The MSST formula has $\sqrt{4M^2 + T^2}$ and the DE formula has $\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$. Interchanging them costs 4 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply all six Marin factors** in fatigue problems. Each factor is a separate "check box" for the examiner; missing one is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Using $S_{ut}$ instead of $S_y$ in the Soderberg equation.** This is the most common mean-stress mistake. The Soderberg line uses $S_y$ on the $\sigma_m$ axis; using $S_{ut}$ converts the line into the Goodman line.
> 5. **Not rounding up the diameter to a standard size** (e.g., 62.3 mm → 65 mm). The component must be manufacturable, so always adopt the next higher standard size.
> 6. **Conflating $K_t$ and $K_f$.** $K_t$ is the *theoretical* concentration factor (elastic, from charts). $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ is the *fatigue* concentration factor, which accounts for local plastic relaxation. Using $K_t$ instead of $K_f$ in a fatigue problem over-predicts damage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the KTU 2024 exam.**

- **Static Loading (Module 1 core):**
  - Three theories — Rankine (Normal), Tresca (Shear), von Mises (Distortion Energy).
  - For ductile materials use **DE theory**: $\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ for 2-D bending + torsion.
  - Shaft design formulas — **MSST**: $d = \left[ \frac{16n}{\pi S_y} \sqrt{4M^2 + T^2} \right]^{1/3}$; **DE**: $d = \left[ \frac{16n}{\pi S_y} \sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} \right]^{1/3}$.
  - DE gives a *larger* diameter for shafts under combined bending + torsion → more conservative.

- **Fatigue Loading (Module 1 core):**
  - $S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut}$ (for steels with $S_{ut} < 1400$ MPa).
  - **Marin equation:** $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$ — six factors, all multiplicative.
  - For surface factor: $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{\,b}$ (read $a, b$ from the surface-finish table).
  - For size factor in bending/torsion: $k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$ (for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm).
  - For axial loading: $k_c = 0.85$.

- **Mean Stress Correction Criteria (in order of conservativeness, for $\sigma_m > 0$):**
  1. Soderberg (most conservative — uses $S_y$ on $\sigma_m$ axis)
  2. ASME-Elliptic (uses $S_y$ and $S_e$)
  3. Modified Goodman (uses $S_{ut}$ on $\sigma_m$ axis — *default in industry*)
  4. Gerber (parabolic — most accurate for ductile, *least conservative*)

- **Stress Concentration:**
  - $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, where $q$ is notch sensitivity.
  - Neuber: $q = 1 / (1 + \sqrt{a}/\sqrt{r})$.
  - Always apply $K_f$ in the Goodman equation by multiplying **both** $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ by $K_f$ (i.e., $K_f \sigma_a / S_e + K_f \sigma_m / S_{ut} = 1/n$).

- **Cumulative Damage (Miner's Rule):**
  - $D = \sum n_i / N_i$. Failure at $D = 1$.
  - Used only for variable-amplitude loading (e.g., road-load data on a chassis component).

- **Unit Traps to Avoid:**
  - Convert N·m → N·mm before using in $\sigma = 32M/(\pi d^3)$ formula (multiply by $10^3$).
  - Convert mm → m if you prefer SI throughout; just stay consistent.
  - All stresses should be in **MPa** (N/mm²).

- **KTU 2024 "Must-Know" Insight:**
  - For a component under **static** load use DE theory.
  - For a component under **fluctuating** load with $\sigma_m > 0$ use the **Modified Goodman** line as the design line; the Gerber result is reported as a "calculated" value.
  - For a component under **fluctuating** load with a notch, the *effective* stress is $K_f \sigma$ — never forget the $K_f$ multiplication.
  - For multi-axial fatigue (combined bending + torsion), use the **Sines** or **Findley** criterion — not in KTU Module 1, but know the limit.

**End of Module 1 notes on Design for Static and Fatigue Loads — PCMUT601, KTU 2024 Scheme.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeated Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Repeated Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Repeated Loading** (also called **Cyclic Loading** or **Fluctuating Loading**) is a type of mechanical loading in which the magnitude and/or direction of the applied stress varies cyclically with time. Unlike static loading where the stress remains constant, repeated loading subjects an automotive component to a continuous spectrum of stresses that fluctuate between a maximum and minimum value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *"Repeated loading is the cyclic application of stresses in a component, characterized by a maximum stress (σ_max), minimum stress (σ_min), mean stress (σ_m), and alternating stress (σ_a), which leads to fatigue failure at stress levels considerably below the ultimate tensile strength of the material after a sufficient number of cycles."*

In the context of **automotive components**, repeated loading is encountered in virtually every moving part:
- Crankshafts (cyclic bending and torsion)
- Connecting rods (cyclic tension-compression)
- Suspension springs (cyclic fluctuating stresses)
- Axles and gears (rotating bending)
- Valve springs (high-frequency cyclic loading)

### 1.2 The Four Characteristic Stresses of a Cycle

Any repeated stress cycle is uniquely described by four parameters:

1. **Maximum Stress (σ_max)**: The highest algebraic value of stress during one cycle.
2. **Minimum Stress (σ_min)**: The lowest algebraic value of stress during one cycle.
3. **Mean Stress (σ_m)**: The arithmetic average of maximum and minimum stress.
4. **Alternating Stress (σ_a)**: Half the algebraic difference between maximum and minimum stress.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Paperclip Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Take a standard paperclip and bend it back and forth repeatedly. You will notice that the paperclip eventually breaks — but the force you apply during each bend is *far less* than the force required to snap it in a single pull. The paperclip fails at a much lower applied force simply because the stress is being **repeated**. This is **fatigue failure** — the hallmark phenomenon of components subjected to repeated loading.

In an automobile, a connecting rod experiences millions of stress cycles per hour of operation. Even though the peak stress in the rod may be only **30% to 40%** of the material's ultimate tensile strength, failure can occur after a finite number of cycles. This is why understanding repeated loading is **fundamental to automotive design**.

### 1.4 Types of Stress Cycles

There are three fundamental classifications of repeated stress cycles:

| Cycle Type | Description | σ_min / σ_max Ratio | Automotive Example |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Reversed Cycle** | Stress alternates between equal and opposite values | σ_min = −σ_max | Rotating shaft (pure bending) |
| **Repeated Cycle (Pulsating)** | Stress varies from zero to a maximum tensile value | σ_min = 0 | Tooth of a one-way driven gear |
| **Fluctuating (Random) Cycle** | Stress varies between two values of same sign but different magnitude | 0 < σ_min < σ_max | Press-fitted shaft, pre-loaded bolts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Nomenclature:** Tensile stresses are taken as **positive (+)** and compressive stresses as **negative (−)**. This sign convention is mandatory in KTU board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Parameters of a Stress Cycle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum and minimum stresses** from the loading diagram of the component.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean stress** as the arithmetic center of the cycle:
- Why? Because mean stress represents the *static* (time-invariant) component of the load, which determines whether the cycle is biased toward tension or compression.

**Step 3 — Compute the alternating (amplitude) stress**:
- Why? Because alternating stress is the *dynamic* component that actually drives fatigue damage. A high amplitude with low mean stress is just as dangerous as a moderate amplitude with high mean stress.

**Step 4 — Compute the stress ratio (R)** to classify the cycle:
- Why? R is the most compact way to describe the *nature* of the cycle in a single number.

### 2.2 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Engineering Backbone

The **S-N Curve** (also called the **Wöhler Curve** after August Wöhler, 1860) is the fundamental graphical tool that links the applied stress amplitude (S) to the number of cycles to failure (N).

**Reading the curve:**
- The **y-axis** is the stress amplitude σ_a (or σ_max) on a **linear scale**.
- The **x-axis** is the number of cycles N on a **logarithmic scale**.
- The curve **drops** as N increases — meaning the higher the stress, the fewer the cycles to failure.
- For **ferrous metals (steel, iron)**, the curve becomes **horizontal** after a certain N (typically 10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles). This horizontal plateau is the **Endurance Limit (σ_e or S_e')**.
- For **non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper)**, the curve keeps sloping downward — there is **no true endurance limit**. Designers use the **Fatigue Strength at N = 5×10⁸** (or 10⁹) cycles as a design reference.

### 2.3 Endurance Limit vs. Fatigue Strength

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endurance Limit (σ_e):** The maximum completely reversed stress that a material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (typically defined at N = 10⁶ for steel).
>
> **Fatigue Strength (S_f or σ_f'):** The maximum stress a material can withstand for a **specified finite number of cycles** when no true endurance limit exists (typical for aluminum alloys).

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Fatigue Life — The Marin Factors

In practice, the *theoretical* endurance limit from a polished specimen (S_e') must be modified to account for real-world conditions. **Marin's Equation** (1947) is universally used:

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$

| Factor | Symbol | Accounts For | Range |
|--------|--------|--------------|-------|
| **Surface Finish Factor** | k_a | Surface roughness (machined, ground, polished, forged) | 0.5 to 1.0 |
| **Size Factor** | k_b | Component diameter / cross-section size | 0.6 to 1.0 |
| **Loading Factor** | k_c | Type of loading (bending, axial, torsion) | 0.58 to 1.0 |
| **Temperature Factor** | k_d | Operating temperature | 0.2 to 1.0 |
| **Reliability Factor** | k_e | Statistical reliability requirement (50%, 90%, 99%, 99.9%) | 0.62 to 1.0 |
| **Miscellaneous Factor** | k_f | Residual stresses, corrosion, etc. | 0.5 to 1.0 |

### 2.5 Stress Concentration and Notch Sensitivity

Automotive components invariably contain geometric discontinuities — **fillets, keyways, holes, threads, splines**. These create localized stress peaks described by the **Theoretical Stress Concentration Factor (K_t)**.

Because materials do not respond perfectly elastically to these peaks, the actual peak stress is reduced. The **Notch Sensitivity (q)** quantifies this:

$$K_f = 1 + q \cdot (K_t - 1)$$

Where:
- K_f = **Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor** (used for fatigue design)
- K_t = **Theoretical (geometric) Stress Concentration Factor**
- q = **Notch Sensitivity Index** (0 ≤ q ≤ 1)

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|----------------------|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | σ_m = (σ_max + σ_min) / 2 | Mean stress | Always show sign |
| 2 | σ_a = (σ_max − σ_min) / 2 | Alternating stress amplitude | Magnitude only |
| 3 | R = σ_min / σ_max | Stress ratio (cycle nature) | R = −1 → reversed |
| 4 | A = σ_a / σ_m = (1 − R) / (1 + R) | Amplitude ratio | Used in Shigley's method |
| 5 | S_e = k_a · k_b · k_c · k_d · k_e · k_f · S_e' | Modified endurance limit (Marin) | Multiply all factors |
| 6 | S_e' = 0.5 · S_ut (for S_ut ≤ 1400 MPa) | Approx. endurance limit of specimen | Only for steels |
| 7 | S_e' = 700 MPa (for S_ut > 1400 MPa) | Ceiling value for endurance limit | Empirical cap |
| 8 | K_f = 1 + q · (K_t − 1) | Fatigue stress concentration factor | q from charts |
| 9 | n_f = σ_e / σ_a (for σ_m = 0) | Fatigue factor of safety (pure reversed) | |
| 10 | 1/n_f = 16·S_a / (3·π·d³·σ_e) | Fatigue FOS for rotating shaft | KTU favourite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the automotive industry, these formulas are embedded in **Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE)** software like ANSYS, nCode, and FE-SAFE. The formulas form the analytical foundation; the software just automates the computation for millions of elements in a finite element mesh.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Mean and Alternating Stress Components

Let σ_max and σ_min be the algebraic maximum and minimum stresses in one cycle of repeated loading.

**Step 1:** The mean stress is, by definition, the arithmetic center of the cycle:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

*Logic:* Taking the arithmetic mean gives the constant (DC) component of the stress signal, analogous to the DC offset of an AC waveform in electrical engineering.

**Step 2:** The alternating stress is, by definition, half the range:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

*Logic:* The "range" σ_max − σ_min is the peak-to-peak swing; dividing by 2 gives the peak deviation from the mean.

**Step 3:** Verify by addition:

$$\sigma_m + \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} + \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \sigma_{max}$$

*Logic:* Adding mean + alternating gives back the maximum — confirming the decomposition is mathematically consistent. By symmetry, σ_m − σ_a = σ_min.

### 3.2 Derivation of Stress Ratio (R) and Amplitude Ratio (A)

**Step 1:** Divide σ_min by σ_max:

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$$

*Logic:* R is dimensionless and lies in the range −1 ≤ R ≤ 1 for most engineering cases.

**Step 2:** Express R in terms of σ_m and σ_a:

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{\sigma_m - \sigma_a}{\sigma_m + \sigma_a}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the ratio σ_a / σ_m:

$$\begin{aligned}
R(\sigma_m + \sigma_a) &= \sigma_m - \sigma_a \\
R \cdot \sigma_m + R \cdot \sigma_a &= \sigma_m - \sigma_a \\
R \cdot \sigma_a + \sigma_a &= \sigma_m - R \cdot \sigma_m \\
\sigma_a \cdot (R + 1) &= \sigma_m \cdot (1 - R) \\
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m} &= \frac{1 - R}{1 + R} = A
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* This expresses the *amplitude ratio A* purely as a function of R, which is convenient when only the cycle ratio is known.

### 3.3 Derivation of Endurance Limit for Steel Specimens (Empirical)

For a rotating-beam specimen of standard size, polished surface, tested at room temperature:

**Step 1:** For steels with S_ut ≤ 1400 MPa:

$$S_e' = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}$$

*Logic:* This 50% rule is an empirical observation first reported by the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) and has been verified for thousands of ferrous alloys.

**Step 2:** For ultra-high-strength steels (S_ut > 1400 MPa), the ratio S_e'/S_ut drops below 0.5:

$$S_e' = 700 \text{ MPa}$$

*Logic:* Experimental data show that the endurance limit saturates around 700 MPa for the strongest steels because internal defects and inclusions become the limiting factor at very high strengths.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor (K_f)

Consider a notched specimen under axial load P, with nominal stress σ_0 = P/A_nom.

**Step 1:** The theoretical (elastic) peak stress at the notch root is:

$$\sigma_{peak} = K_t \cdot \sigma_0$$

*Logic:* K_t is purely geometric and assumes perfectly elastic, isotropic behavior.

**Step 2:** In reality, local yielding and micro-plasticity reduce the peak stress. Define the **fatigue notch sensitivity q**:

$$q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$$

*Logic:* q = 0 means the material is fully notch-insensitive (no fatigue penalty), q = 1 means fully notch-sensitive.

**Step 3:** Rearrange to obtain the design equation:

$$K_f = 1 + q \cdot (K_t - 1)$$

*Logic:* This is the equation used in every KTU board problem involving fatigue design of notched shafts, gears, and keyways.

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Standard Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A rotating shaft of 50 mm diameter is subjected to a bending moment that fluctuates between +600 N·m and −200 N·m. The material has S_ut = 650 MPa, S_y = 480 MPa, surface finish is machined, and operating temperature is 50 °C. Determine the factor of safety against fatigue failure using the Soderberg criterion.

**Solution Outline:**

Step 1: Bending stresses:
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 M_{max}}{\pi d^3}, \quad \sigma_{min} = \frac{32 M_{min}}{\pi d^3}$$

Step 2: Mean and alternating:
$$\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2, \quad \sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$$

Step 3: Apply Marin factors (S_ut = 650 MPa → S_e' = 325 MPa; k_a, k_b, k_c, k_d, k_e from standard charts).

Step 4: Soderberg line:
$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y}$$

Step 5: Solve for n. **(Full numerical values are tabulated in question bank below.)**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Repeated Loading Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Loading on Component] --> B[Extract Sigma_max and Sigma_min from Service Cycle]
    B --> C[Compute Sigma_m and Sigma_a]
    C --> D{Is Sigma_m zero?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Reversed Cycle: Use Sigma_e directly]
    D -- No --> F[Compute R and A ratios]
    F --> G[Apply Marin Modification: S_e Prime to S_e]
    G --> H{Notch Present?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply K_f Correction]
    H -- No --> J[Use K_f equals 1]
    I --> K[Choose Failure Criterion]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Soderberg / Goodman / Gerber / ASME]
    L --> M[Compute Factor of Safety n]
    M --> N{n greater than n_design?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Design is SAFE]
    N -- No --> P[Design FAILS - Revise]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Stress Cycle Types and their R-values

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REVERSED[Reversed Cycle: R equals -1]
        R1[Sigma_max positive] --> R2[Sigma_m equals 0]
        R2 --> R3[Sigma_min negative]
    end
    subgraph PULSATING[Pulsating Cycle: R equals 0]
        P1[Sigma_max positive] --> P2[Sigma_m equals Sigma_a]
        P2 --> P3[Sigma_min equals 0]
    end
    subgraph FLUCTUATING[Fluctuating Cycle: 0 less than R less than 1]
        F1[Sigma_max positive] --> F2[Sigma_m positive]
        F2 --> F3[Sigma_min positive but smaller]
    end
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Marin's Modification Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUT[Material S_ut] --> SE_PRIME[Unmodified S_e Prime equals 0.5 times S_ut]
    SE_PRIME --> KA[k_a: Surface Finish]
    SE_PRIME --> KB[k_b: Size]
    SE_PRIME --> KC[k_c: Load Type]
    SE_PRIME --> KD[k_d: Temperature]
    SE_PRIME --> KE[k_e: Reliability]
    KA --> MULT[Multiply All Factors]
    KB --> MULT
    KC --> MULT
    KD --> MULT
    KE --> MULT
    MULT --> SE[Modified Endurance Limit S_e]
    SE --> CRITERIA[Apply to Failure Criterion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowcharts above map the analytical sequence a designer must follow when sizing an automotive component (e.g., a crankshaft) for repeated loading. The first stage is *stress extraction* from the duty cycle, the second stage is *fatigue property modification* via Marin, and the third stage is *failure criterion application*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the terms: (i) Mean stress, (ii) Alternating stress, (iii) Stress ratio. Illustrate the reversed stress cycle with a sketch.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Mean Stress (σ_m):** It is the arithmetic average of the maximum and minimum stresses during one complete cycle of repeated loading, given by σ_m = (σ_max + σ_min) / 2. It represents the *static* component of the cyclic load.
>
> **Alternating Stress (σ_a):** It is half the algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum stresses, given by σ_a = (σ_max − σ_min) / 2. It represents the *dynamic* component that drives fatigue damage.
>
> **Stress Ratio (R):** It is the ratio of minimum to maximum stress, R = σ_min / σ_max. For a **reversed cycle**, σ_min = −σ_max, hence R = −1. The cycle is sinusoidal about the zero-stress axis, and σ_m = 0.

*(Sketch: A sine wave symmetric about the time axis, peak at +σ_max and trough at −σ_max.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the endurance limit? Why does it exist for steels but not for aluminum alloys?*

**Model Answer:**

> The **endurance limit (S_e or σ_e)** is defined as the maximum value of completely reversed stress that a material can withstand for an *infinite number of cycles* (conventionally taken as 10⁶ cycles for steels) without experiencing fatigue failure.
>
> For **ferrous metals (steels)**, the S-N curve becomes *horizontal* beyond ~10⁶ cycles, indicating a true endurance limit. This is attributed to the strain-aging phenomenon in BCC iron lattices, where solute atoms lock dislocations in place indefinitely below a threshold stress.
>
> For **non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper)**, the S-N curve continues to slope downward indefinitely — there is no stress below which the material can survive infinitely. Hence, designers use the **fatigue strength at a specified large N (e.g., 5×10⁸ cycles)** as a design reference rather than a true endurance limit.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** A steel shaft of diameter 60 mm is subjected to a fluctuating torque that varies from 800 N·m to 200 N·m, simultaneously with a bending moment varying from 1200 N·m to 400 N·m. The material properties are: S_ut = 700 MPa, S_y = 480 MPa, S_e' = 350 MPa. The shaft is machined, operates at 40 °C, and requires 90% reliability. Use the Soderberg criterion to find the factor of safety. Assume notch-free. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** What is the significance of stress concentration in fatigue design? With relevant equations, explain how notch sensitivity affects fatigue strength. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Compute bending stresses (M = bending moment, d = 60 mm = 0.06 m):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{32 M_{max}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 1200}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} \\
&= \frac{38400}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{38400}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 56.6 \text{ MPa (at M = 1200 N·m)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{min} &= \frac{32 \times 400}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} \approx 18.87 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute mean and alternating bending stresses:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_m &= (56.6 + 18.87) / 2 = 37.74 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_a &= (56.6 - 18.87) / 2 = 18.87 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute shear stresses from torque (T varies 800 to 200 N·m):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{16 T_{max}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 800}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} \approx 18.87 \text{ MPa} \\
\tau_{min} &= \frac{16 \times 200}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} \approx 4.72 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\tau_m = 11.79 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_a = 7.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Compute equivalent von-Mises amplitudes:

$$\sigma_{m,eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_m^2 + 3\tau_m^2} = \sqrt{(37.74)^2 + 3(11.79)^2} \approx 42.16 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{a,eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_a^2 + 3\tau_a^2} = \sqrt{(18.87)^2 + 3(7.08)^2} \approx 22.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Marin factors:
- k_a (machined, S_ut = 700 MPa) = 0.83 (from standard chart)
- k_b (d = 60 mm, axial/bending) = 0.85 (from chart)
- k_c (combined bending + torsion; we already use von-Mises, so k_c = 1 for equivalent stress)
- k_d (T = 40 °C) = 1.0
- k_e (90% reliability) = 0.897

$$S_e = 0.83 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 350 = 221.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6:** Apply Soderberg:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_{a,eq}}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_{m,eq}}{S_y} = \frac{22.65}{221.5} + \frac{42.16}{480}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = 0.1023 + 0.0878 = 0.1901$$

$$\boxed{n = 5.26}$$

The shaft has a factor of safety of approximately **5.26** against fatigue failure by Soderberg.

**Model Solution to (b):**

> In any automotive component, geometric discontinuities such as fillets, keyways, holes, and threads cause a **local elevation of stress** far above the nominal stress calculated from simple strength-of-materials formulas. This is called **stress concentration**.
>
> The theoretical concentration factor is K_t = σ_peak / σ_nom. In static design, ductile materials yield locally and redistribute the load, so the *actual* peak stress is lower than K_t · σ_nom. In **fatigue**, however, the localized cyclic plastic strain initiates a crack at the notch root, and the entire fatigue life is governed by this peak.
>
> **Notch Sensitivity (q)** is a material property (0 ≤ q ≤ 1) that quantifies how much a material responds to a stress concentration in *fatigue*. For a fully sensitive material (q = 1), the fatigue strength at the notch equals the un-notched strength divided by K_t. For an insensitive material (q = 0), the notch has no effect.
>
> The **fatigue stress concentration factor** is therefore:
> $$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$
>
> Ne ductile steels at typical hardness show q ≈ 0.8 to 0.95. Cast irons and very hard steels show q close to 1. Aluminum alloys typically show q ≈ 0.6 to 0.8. The fatigue design stress becomes σ_design = K_f · σ_nom.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the S-N curve in detail. Discuss the factors affecting fatigue strength with the Marin equation. List the values of various modifying factors for a typical automotive alloy steel component. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A connecting rod is made of alloy steel with S_ut = 900 MPa, S_y = 700 MPa, and S_e' = 450 MPa. The connecting rod is subjected to an axial load fluctuating between 50 kN (tension) and 15 kN (compression). The rod has a cross-section of 30 mm × 15 mm with a fillet radius of 3 mm. Surface is machined, k_b = 0.85, k_c = 0.85, k_d = 1.0, k_e = 0.897, q = 0.85, K_t = 2.1. Determine the factor of safety using the Goodman criterion. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution to (a):**

> The **S-N curve** (Wöhler curve) is the experimentally determined graph of stress amplitude (S) versus number of cycles to failure (N) for a standardized specimen under a specified loading mode. It is the *primary design tool* for fatigue.
>
> **Construction:**
> 1. Multiple identical specimens are tested at different stress amplitudes.
> 2. For each specimen, the number of cycles to failure is recorded.
> 3. Stress amplitude is plotted on the linear y-axis; N is plotted on the logarithmic x-axis.
>
> **Three Regions of the S-N Curve:**
> - **Low-cycle fatigue (N < 10³):** Plastic strain dominates; the curve is steep.
> - **High-cycle fatigue (10³ < N < 10⁶):** Elastic strain dominates; curve is approximately linear on log-N scale.
> - **Infinite-life region (N > 10⁶, for steel):** Curve is horizontal at the endurance limit.
>
> **Marin's Equation for Modified Endurance Limit:**
> $$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$
>
> **Typical values for an alloy steel automotive component (e.g., crankshaft):**
> - k_a (machined, S_ut = 900 MPa) ≈ 0.72
> - k_b (d = 50 mm, rotating bending) ≈ 0.81
> - k_c (bending) = 1.0
> - k_d (T < 100 °C) = 1.0
> - k_e (90% reliability) = 0.897
> - k_f (no corrosion) = 1.0
>
> $$S_e = 0.72 \times 0.81 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 450 \approx 235 \text{ MPa}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Cross-section area A = 30 × 15 = 450 mm² = 450 × 10⁻⁶ m²

**Step 2:** Compute nominal stresses (tension positive):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{+50000}{450 \times 10^{-6}} = +111.11 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{-15000}{450 \times 10^{-6}} = -33.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Mean and alternating:

$$\sigma_m = (111.11 - 33.33) / 2 = 38.89 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = (111.11 + 33.33) / 2 = 72.22 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Fatigue concentration factor:

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.85(2.1 - 1) = 1 + 0.935 = 1.935$$

**Step 5:** Corrected alternating stress:

$$\sigma_{a,corrected} = K_f \cdot \sigma_a = 1.935 \times 72.22 = 139.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6:** Modified endurance limit:

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot S_e'$$

We need k_a for S_ut = 900 MPa (machined). From Shigley's table: k_a = a · S_ut^b, with a = 4.51, b = −0.265 (for MPa):

$$k_a = 4.51 \times 900^{-0.265} \approx 4.51 \times 0.175 \approx 0.79$$

$$S_e = 0.79 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 450 \approx 230.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7:** Apply Goodman:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_{a,corrected}}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{139.75}{230.0} + \frac{38.89}{900}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = 0.6076 + 0.0432 = 0.6508$$

$$\boxed{n = 1.537}$$

The factor of safety is approximately **1.54**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Repeated Loading Problems:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Forgetting that compression is negative. This flips σ_m and σ_a values entirely — a single sign mistake can cost **3 to 4 marks**.
> 2. **Ignoring Combined Loading:** A shaft under combined bending + torsion requires von-Mises equivalent stresses, NOT direct addition. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Using S_ut instead of S_e:** Many students confuse static and fatigue strengths. Always use **endurance limit** in the fatigue criterion equation.
> 4. **Forgetting Marin factors:** Not multiplying by k_a, k_b, k_c, k_d, k_e leads to an over-optimistic design. Common KTU board penalty: **2 marks**.
> 5. **Wrong Failure Criterion:** Mixing up Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber, and ASME-Elliptic. The question will explicitly state the criterion. Use the correct one.
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in stress calculations. Always convert to SI units consistently before substitution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Repeated Loading = Cyclic Stress** applied to a component; characterized by **σ_max, σ_min, σ_m, σ_a, and R**.
- **Mean stress** σ_m = (σ_max + σ_min) / 2 → static component.
- **Alternating stress** σ_a = (σ_max − σ_min) / 2 → dynamic (fatigue) component.
- **Stress ratio R = σ_min / σ_max**: R = −1 is reversed, R = 0 is pulsating, 0 < R < 1 is fluctuating.
- **S-N Curve (Wöhler)** is the foundation of fatigue design.
- **Endurance Limit S_e'** exists for steels (~0.5 × S_ut), not for aluminum.
- **Marin's Equation**: S_e = k_a · k_b · k_c · k_d · k_e · k_f · S_e'.
- **Stress Concentration**: K_f = 1 + q (K_t − 1) is mandatory for notched components.
- **Failure Criteria** (in order of conservatism): Soderberg > Goodman > Gerber > ASME-Elliptic.
- **Soderberg** uses S_y → most conservative → preferred for ductile, safety-critical parts.
- **Goodman** uses S_ut → standard for general machine design.
- **Gerber** uses parabolic curve → less conservative, good for ductile materials.
- **ASME-Elliptic** uses quadratic with both S_y and S_ut → balanced approach.
- **Always** include Marin factors and von-Mises equivalents in KTU board problems.
- **Rotating shaft under bending** gives a fully reversed cycle automatically (R = −1).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversed Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reversed Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reversed Bending** is a phenomenon encountered in the design of flexural members (especially leaf springs and laminated springs used in automotive suspensions) wherein the fibre stresses at the top and bottom surfaces alternate between tensile and compressive states during a complete load-unload cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Reversed bending refers to the change in the sign of the bending stress on the surface fibres of a leaf spring during the rebound phase after the load is suddenly released. The fibre which was initially in compression becomes tension, and vice-versa, *provided the initial radius of curvature is less than a critical value.*

Mathematically, reversed bending occurs when the **initial radius of curvature of the spring (1/C)** is such that during full deflection and subsequent rebound, the curvature of the leaf spring crosses through the neutral straight configuration multiple times.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thin curved strip of steel (like a measuring tape slightly bent). When you press down on it (loading), it bends more. When you suddenly release it, it doesn't just return to its original curved shape — it **overshoots** and momentarily bends in the *opposite direction* (like a diving board snapping back up). This overshoot causes the fibres that were previously stretched (tension) to be squeezed (compression). That overshoot moment is **reversed bending**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Manifestation in Automotives:**
> - **Leaf Springs** of trucks/jeeps hitting a pothole and rebounding.
> - **Valve Springs** of an IC engine during rapid opening/closing cycles.
> - **Torsion Bar Suspensions** undergoing alternating twist.
> - **Connecting Rods** under fluctuating bending loads between power and return strokes.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards

| Constant / Symbol | Value / Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **σ_b** | Bending stress | **MPa (N/mm²)** |
| **W** | Maximum load on spring | **N** |
| **C** | Initial curvature of leaf spring (deflection equivalent) | **mm** |
| **δ (W)** | Maximum deflection under load W | **mm** |
| **n** | Number of full-length leaves | — |
| **l** | Effective length of spring (span) | **mm** |
| **b** | Width of leaf | **mm** |
| **t** | Thickness of leaf | **mm** |
| **E** | Young's Modulus of spring steel | **MPa** (~2 × 10⁵) |
| **K** | Bending stress factor (due to reversed bending) | **2.0** typical |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Reversed Bending in a Leaf Spring

A leaf spring is pre-curved (cambered) so that when fully loaded, the spring becomes approximately **straight**, neutralizing the initial curvature. During rebound, however, the dynamics can cause the spring to flex past the neutral position, flipping the stress sign.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Initial State:** Spring has pre-curvature equivalent to deflection **C**.
2. **Loaded State (full load W applied):** Spring deflects by **W**, becomes *nearly straight* if **C ≈ 2W**. Stress: $\sigma_b$ at top fibre.
3. **Rebound State:** Load is suddenly removed. Due to stored strain energy, the spring momentarily curves *beyond* the initial shape.
4. **Stress Reversal:** The top fibre now experiences the *opposite sign* of stress → this is **reversed bending**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Engineers Care:** In reversed bending, fatigue failure dominates (the spring is subjected to a **fully reversed stress cycle** with mean stress ≈ 0). The allowable bending stress must be reduced using a fatigue/factor-of-safety correction.

## 2.2 Condition for Avoiding Reversed Bending

To **eliminate** reversed bending (i.e., design for **unidirectional bending**), the designer ensures:

$$C \geq 2W$$

where **C** = Initial camber/deflection equivalent, **W** = working deflection.

If **C < 2W**, then during rebound, the spring crosses the neutral axis → **reversed bending is unavoidable**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 W l}{2 n b t^2}$ | Maximum bending stress (no reversed bending) | N/mm² |
| 2 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 W l}{2 n b t^2} \times K$ | Bending stress when reversed bending is considered. **K = 2 (V.B. Bhandari convention)** for full-length leaves | N/mm² |
| 3 | $\delta = \dfrac{6 W l^3}{2 n E b t^3} = \dfrac{W l^3}{2 n E b t^3} \times 6$ | Maximum deflection of leaf spring (corrected) | mm |
| 4 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{18 W l}{2 n b t^2} = \dfrac{9 W l}{n b t^2}$ | Bending stress when **C = W** (rebound causes 1.5× curvature flip) | N/mm² |
| 5 | $l = \text{span between eyes} - 2 \times \text{bushing length}$ | Effective length of spring | mm |
| 6 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 W (l - t)}{2 n b t^2}$ | Stress with leaf thickness correction at the eye | N/mm² |
| 7 | $K = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{max}}}{\sigma_{\text{mean}}} = 2$ | Stress concentration factor (reversed bending ratio) | — |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{3 W}{2 n b t}$ | Maximum shear stress in leaves | N/mm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠️ Markdown Rendering Safety:** All absolute values and vertical bars in math have been escaped to avoid breaking the table syntax. Use `\vert` in LaTeX rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Suspensions:** Determines fatigue life of leaf springs in trucks, jeeps, and commercial vehicles.
- **Railway Bogies:** Governs the design of laminated springs used in train suspensions.
- **Clutch & Brake Systems:** Reverse bending dictates spring life in return springs.
- **Aerospace Landing Gear:** Critical for fatigue-certified laminated springs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bending Stress in a Single Leaf with Initial Curvature

**Given:**
- A leaf of length $l$, width $b$, thickness $t$, initially curved with camber equivalent to deflection $C$.
- A transverse load $W$ applied at the centre.
- Number of full-length leaves = $n$.

**Step 1 — Bending Moment at the centre:**
$$M = \frac{W \cdot l}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus of rectangular section:**
$$Z = \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress (without considering initial curvature reversal):**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{W l / 2}{b t^2 / 6} = \frac{3 W l}{b t^2}$$

**Step 4 — For a multi-leaf spring (n full-length leaves):**

The load is shared equally among $n$ leaves, so effective load per leaf = $W/n$. Substituting:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{3 W l}{2 n b t^2}$$

**Step 5 — Considering the effect of initial curvature:**

If the leaf spring is pre-curved such that the initial camber is less than $2W$, the spring will **deflect further** than $W$ on the loaded side. The effective stress becomes:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{3 W l}{2 n b t^2} \times K$$

where $K = 2$ when **reversed bending is allowed** (per V.B. Bhandari convention for full-length leaves).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Deflection of a Leaf Spring

**Given:** Cantilever-equivalent with bending moment $M(x) = \dfrac{W x}{2}$ for $0 \leq x \leq l$.

**Step 1 — Equation of Elastic Curve:**
$$E I \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = \frac{W x}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once (slope):**
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{W x^2}{4 E I} + C_1$$

Applying boundary condition at $x = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ → $C_1 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Integrate twice (deflection):**
$$y = \frac{W x^3}{12 E I} + C_2$$

Applying $y(0) = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Maximum deflection at $x = l$:**
$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{W l^3}{12 E I}$$

**Step 5 — Moment of inertia:**
$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute I:**
$$\delta = \frac{W l^3}{12 E \cdot (b t^3 / 12)} = \frac{W l^3}{E b t^3}$$

**Step 7 — For multi-leaf spring (n leaves, tapered share):**
$$\delta = \frac{W l^3}{2 n E b t^3} \times \frac{6}{6} = \frac{6 W l^3}{12 n E b t^3}$$

Simplified:

$$\delta = \frac{W l^3}{2 n E b t^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on factor:** In some textbooks (e.g., Shigley, Khurmi), the deflection formula carries a factor of **6** in the numerator when $n$ leaves of varying lengths are accounted for. KTU typically follows the **V.B. Bhandari convention** with a coefficient consistent with the stress equation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stress with Reversed Bending (C = W)

When initial camber **C = W**, the spring is loaded until deflection = W, then rebounds past initial camber and goes back by an additional amount equal to $\dfrac{W}{2}$. This rebound causes the curvature to momentarily reverse, doubling the effective stress change.

**Step 1 — Initial camber equivalent deflection:**
$$C = W$$

**Step 2 — Maximum curvature change during rebound:**
The fibre stress alternation is from $\sigma_{\max}$ (loaded) to $-\sigma_{\max}$ (rebound) → stress range = $2 \sigma_{\max}$.

**Step 3 — Mean stress:**
$$\sigma_m = 0$$

**Step 4 — Amplitude of reversed stress cycle:**
$$\sigma_a = \sigma_{\max}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent stress (using Goodman or Soderberg with K = 2):**
$$\sigma_b = K \cdot \frac{3 W l}{2 n b t^2} = 2 \times \frac{3 W l}{2 n b t^2} = \frac{3 W l}{n b t^2}$$

Hence, when **reversed bending is dominant**, the **permissible stress must be halved** (since K = 2 effectively doubles the working stress).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table

| Parameter | Unidirectional Bending ($C \geq 2W$) | Reversed Bending ($C < 2W$) |
|---|---|---|
| Stress Equation | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3Wl}{2nbt^2}$ | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3Wl}{nbt^2}$ (K=2) |
| Deflection Equation | $\delta = \dfrac{Wl^3}{2nEbt^3}$ | $\delta = \dfrac{Wl^3}{2nEbt^3}$ (same) |
| Fatigue Life | Higher (partial cycle) | Lower (fully reversed cycle) |
| Safety Factor | ~1.5 – 2 | ~2.5 – 4 |
| Application | Stationary leaf springs | Automotive rebound-loaded springs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Reversed Bending Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Spring at Initial Camber C] --> B[Apply Vertical Load W]
    B --> C[Spring Deflects by amount W]
    C --> D{Spring becomes straight?}
    D -- Yes if C equals 2W --> E[No Reversed Bending]
    D -- No if C is less than 2W --> F[Curvature Increases Beyond Straight]
    F --> G[Sudden Release of Load W]
    G --> H[Spring Rebounds Past Initial Camber]
    H --> I[Curvature Reverses Sign]
    I --> J[Stress Reversal on Surface Fibres]
    J --> K[Fully Reversed Stress Cycle]
    K --> L[Apply K equals 2 Factor in Design]
    L --> M[Reduced Permissible Stress for Fatigue]
    M --> N[Final Design with Higher FOS]
    E --> O[Unidirectional Stress Cycle]
    O --> P[Standard Permissible Stress]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Leaf Spring Loading Stages

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialCamber
    InitialCamber: Initial Curvature equals C
    LoadedState: Loaded State Stress is sigma_b
    InitialCamber --> LoadedState: Apply Load W
    LoadedState --> ReboundPhase: Sudden Release
    ReboundPhase: Rebound Past Initial Camber
    ReboundPhase --> StressReversal: Curvature Sign Flips
    StressReversal: Stress becomes minus sigma_b
    StressReversal --> SteadyState: Damping Settles Spring
    SteadyState: Returns to Initial Camber C
    SteadyState --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Function-Block Diagram: Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Loading Type] --> B{Static or Dynamic?}
    B -- Static --> C[Use Unidirectional Formula]
    B -- Dynamic --> D{Is C is greater than 2W?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Unidirectional Formula]
    D -- No --> F[Apply K equals 2 for Reversed Bending]
    F --> G[Apply Fatigue Correction]
    G --> H[Calculate Permissible Stress]
    H --> I[Determine Thickness t and Width b]
    I --> J[Check Deflection Constraint]
    J --> K{Deflection within Limit?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Design Accepted]
    K -- No --> M[Iterate Design Parameters]
    M --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid blocks depict the *decision flow* of the reversed bending design logic. They are NOT physical drawings of leaf springs; rather, they map the state transitions and design choices governed by the reversed bending condition $C < 2W$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define reversed bending in the context of leaf spring design. Under what condition is it avoided?**

**Model Answer:**
Reversed bending is the phenomenon in which the bending stress on the surface fibres of a leaf spring alternates between tension and compression during a complete load-rebound cycle. This occurs when the initial camber **C** is less than twice the working deflection **W** (i.e., $C < 2W$), causing the spring to deflect past its neutral straight position during rebound.

**Reversed bending is avoided when:** $C \geq 2W$ (the spring becomes straight at full load and does not flex further).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the design equation for the bending stress in a multi-leaf spring when reversed bending is considered. What is the value of the stress factor K?**

**Model Answer:**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{3 W l}{2 n b t^2} \times K$$

The bending stress factor **K = 2** when reversed bending is considered (as per V.B. Bhandari's convention for full-length leaf springs). This effectively doubles the calculated stress, requiring the use of a reduced permissible bending stress in design.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

A laminated leaf spring for a truck has the following specifications:
- Maximum load on spring: $W = 60{,}000 \text{ N}$
- Span of spring: $l = 1200 \text{ mm}$
- Number of full-length leaves: $n = 8$
- Width of leaf: $b = 75 \text{ mm}$
- Thickness of leaf: $t = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Modulus of Elasticity: $E = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$
- Initial camber: $C = 75 \text{ mm}$

### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Calculate the maximum bending stress in the spring considering reversed bending. Comment on whether reversed bending will occur.**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify loading condition:**
Since initial camber $C = 75 \text{ mm}$, we must compute working deflection $W$ (in mm equivalent).

**Step 2 — Compute deflection under load W:**
$$\delta = \frac{W l^3}{2 n E b t^3}$$

Substituting values (note: $W$ here is the load in N, not deflection in mm):

$$\delta = \frac{60000 \times 1200^3}{2 \times 8 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 75 \times 12^3}$$

$$\delta = \frac{60000 \times 1.728 \times 10^9}{2 \times 8 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 75 \times 1728}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1.0368 \times 10^{14}}{4.35456 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\delta \approx 238.13 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with initial camber C:**
$$C = 75 \text{ mm}, \quad \delta = 238.13 \text{ mm}$$

Clearly $\delta \gg C$, hence **$C < 2W$ condition is violated** → **Reversed bending will occur**.

**Step 4 — Compute maximum bending stress with K = 2:**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{3 W l}{2 n b t^2} \times K$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{3 \times 60000 \times 1200}{2 \times 8 \times 75 \times 12^2} \times 2$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{2.16 \times 10^8}{172800} \times 2$$
