where $X$ and $Y$ are radial and thrust factors obtained from the bearing catalog based on the ratio $F_a / F_r$ and the contact angle $\alpha$.

For example, for a single-row deep-groove ball bearing:
- If $F_a / F_r \leq e$: $P = F_r$
- If $F_a / F_r > e$: $P = X F_r + Y F_a$

(typical values: $X \approx 0.56$, $Y \approx 1.0$, $e \approx 0.2$ for $\alpha = 0°$)

## 3.6 Analytical Selection Matrix (Step-by-Step Worked Logic)

| Step | Question to Ask | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | What is the **speed**? $N$ in RPM | If $N > 10{,}000$ → ball bearing preferred |
| 2 | What is the **load** $F_r$ in N? | If $F_r / C_{min} > 0.15$ → consider roller |
| 3 | Is there a **thrust load** $F_a$? | If $F_a / F_r > 0.5$ → angular contact or tapered |
| 4 | What is **temperature**? $T$ in °C | If $T > 150°C$ → ceramic hybrid |
| 5 | What is **misalignment** $\theta$ in degrees? | If $\theta > 1°$ → self-aligning |
| 6 | What is **target life** $L_{10h}$ in hours? | Compute $C_{required}$ |
| 7 | What is the **available space**? (mm) | Check catalog bore / OD limits |
| 8 | What is the **lubrication availability**? | If sealed-for-life preferred → 2RS ball bearing |
| 9 | What is the **budget**? | Compare cost vs. performance |
| 10 | Final selection | Choose the bearing with $C_{catalog} \geq C_{required}$ and lowest $C_0/P_0 \geq f_0$ (static safety) |

## 3.7 Sample Numerical — Selection by Calculation

> **Problem:** An automotive wheel hub operates at $N = 600$ RPM, supports a radial load $F_r = 5{,}000$ N and an axial load $F_a = 2{,}000$ N. Target life: 5,000 hours. Choose a tapered roller bearing.

**Step 1:** Equivalent dynamic load (tapered roller, single row):

$$
P = X F_r + Y F_a
$$

From catalog for typical tapered roller with $\alpha = 15°$:

$$
X = 0.4, \quad Y = 1.6 \quad \text{when } F_a/F_r > e
$$

$$
P = 0.4 \times 5{,}000 + 1.6 \times 2{,}000
$$

$$
P = 2{,}000 + 3{,}200 = 5{,}200 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2:** Target life in revolutions:

$$
L_{10} = L_{10h} \times 60 \times N = 5{,}000 \times 60 \times 600
$$

$$
L_{10} = 180{,}000{,}000 \text{ revolutions} = 180 \times 10^6 \text{ rev}
$$

**Step 3:** Required dynamic load rating (roller, $p = 10/3$):

$$
C_{required} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,3/10}
$$

$$
C_{required} = 5{,}200 \times (180 \times 10^6)^{0.3}
$$

$$
(180 \times 10^6)^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \ln(180 \times 10^6)} = e^{0.3 \times 19.005} = e^{5.701} \approx 299.8
$$

$$
C_{required} = 5{,}200 \times 299.8 \approx 1{,}559{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 1{,}560 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4:** Selection — pick a tapered roller bearing from manufacturer (e.g., SKF 32216) with $C = 1{,}780$ kN. Since $C_{catalog} > C_{required}$ ✓, the bearing is selected. Verify static load check:

$$
P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a = 0.5 \times 5{,}000 + 1.0 \times 2{,}000 = 4{,}500 \text{ N}
$$

$$
C_0 \geq f_0 \cdot P_0 = 1.0 \times 4{,}500 = 4{,}500 \text{ N}
$$

Catalog $C_0 = 224$ kN $\geq 4.5$ kN ✓

**Selection CONFIRMED.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Bearing Selection Decision Flowchart

This Mermaid flowchart represents the canonical decision tree used by automotive design engineers to select the appropriate bearing type for a given operating condition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Operating Parameters Known]) --> q1{Speed N in RPM}
    q1 -- "N greater than 10000" --> ballGroup[Ball Bearing Family]
    q1 -- "N less than 10000" --> q2{Load Type}
    q2 -- "Pure Radial" --> q3{Load Magnitude}
    q3 -- "High" --> cylRol[Cylindrical Roller]
    q3 -- "Low to Moderate" --> needRol[Needle Roller]
    q2 -- "Combined Radial plus Thrust" --> q4{Misalignment}
    q4 -- "Less than 1 degree" --> tapRol[Tapered Roller]
    q4 -- "Greater than 1 degree" --> sphRol[Spherical Roller]
    q2 -- "Pure Thrust" --> thrBall[Thrust Ball or Cylindrical]
    ballGroup --> q5{Alignment}
    q5 -- "Rigid" --> dgb[Deep Groove Ball]
    q5 -- "Misaligned" --> sab[Self Aligning Ball]
    dgb --> life1[Compute L10 Life]
    sab --> life1
    cylRol --> life1
    needRol --> life1
    tapRol --> life1
    sphRol --> life1
    thrBall --> life1
    life1 --> q6{Catalog C greater than Creq}
    q6 -- Yes --> final([Selection Confirmed])
    q6 -- No --> upsize[Increase Bearing Size or Reduce Load]
    upsize --> life1
```

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bearing Selection System

This Mermaid block diagram shows the modular architecture of an engineering decision-support system that automates bearing selection for automotive components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT MODULE]
        A1[Rated Load Fr]
        A2[Axial Load Fa]
        A3[Rotational Speed N]
        A4[Operating Temperature T]
        A5[Misalignment Angle theta]
        A6[Target Life L10h]
        A7[Lubrication Type]
        A8[Mounting Geometry]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[PROCESSING MODULE]
        B1[Equivalent Load Calculator]
        B2[PV Value Checker]
        B3[Sommerfeld Number Evaluator]
        B4[L10 Fatigue Life Engine]
        B5[Hertzian Contact Stress Module]
        B6[Static Load Safety Module]
    end

    subgraph DECISION[DECISION ENGINE]
        C1{Bearing Class Selector}
        C2{Geometry Selector}
        C3{Material Selector}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT MODULE]
        D1[Selected Bearing Code]
        D2[Recommended Lubricant]
        D3[Predicted L10 Hours]
        D4[Operating Safety Factor]
        D5[Cost Estimate]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A3 --> B4
    A4 --> C3
    A5 --> C1
    A6 --> B4
    A7 --> B3
    A8 --> C2
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> B6
    B5 --> C2
    B6 --> D1
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    B4 --> D3
    B6 --> D4
    C1 --> D5
```

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Automotive Bearing Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    auto[Automotive Subsystems] --> eng[Engine]
    auto --> trans[Transmission]
    auto --> susp[Suspension and Steering]
    auto --> wheel[Wheel Hub]

    eng --> eng1[Crankshaft Main: Hydrodynamic Journal]
    eng --> eng2[Crankshaft Rod: Plain Bearing]
    eng --> eng3[Camshaft: Journal or Roller]
    eng --> eng4[Alternator: Deep Groove Ball]

    trans --> tr1[Gearbox Shaft: Cylindrical Roller]
    trans --> tr2[Clutch Release: Thrust Ball]
    trans --> tr3[Differential: Tapered Roller]

    susp --> su1[Rocker Arm Pivot: Needle Roller]
    susp --> su2[Steering Column: Deep Groove Ball]
    susp --> su3[Shock Absorber: PTFE Plain]

    wheel --> wh1[Hub Bearing: Tapered Roller]
    wheel --> wh2[CV Joint: Spherical Roller]
```

## 4.4 Figure 4 — Comparative Load-Speed Envelope (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOWSPEED[LOW SPEED HIGH LOAD ZONE]
        L1[Plain Journal Bearings]
        L2[Hydrostatic Bearings]
    end

    subgraph MIDZONE[MID SPEED MID LOAD ZONE]
        M1[Roller Bearings]
        M2[Tapered Roller]
    end

    subgraph HIGHSPEED[HIGH SPEED MODERATE LOAD ZONE]
        H1[Ball Bearings]
        H2[Angular Contact Ball]
        H3[Hybrid Ceramic Ball]
    end

    L1 --> M1
    M1 --> H1
    L2 --> H2
    M2 --> H3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four selection criteria for choosing the type of bearing in an automotive application.**

**Model Answer:**

The four key selection criteria for bearing type selection in automotive systems are:

1. **Magnitude and type of load** — radial, axial, or combined. Influences whether plain, ball, or roller bearings are chosen.
2. **Rotational speed** — high-speed shafts favor ball bearings (low friction), while low-speed heavily-loaded shafts favor roller or journal bearings.
3. **Operating temperature** — elevated temperatures (>150°C) require heat-stabilized steels or ceramic hybrid bearings.
4. **Required L10 fatigue life** — long-life applications (wheel hubs, transmission) demand higher dynamic load rating $C$.

*(Alternative valid criteria also accepted: misalignment tolerance, lubrication regime, cost, available space.)* [3 marks]

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the significance of the PV value in bearing selection.**

**Model Answer:**

The **PV value** (Pressure × Velocity) is a critical thermal-load parameter used as a screening metric in plain/journal bearing selection.

- **P** = Bearing pressure = $\dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ (MPa)
- **V** = Sliding velocity = $\dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ (m/s)
- **PV** = MPa·m/s

**Significance:**
- The PV value represents the **heat generated per unit bearing area**.
- Each bearing material has a **PV limit** (e.g., lead-bronze ≈ 40 MPa·m/s, tin-bronze ≈ 25 MPa·m/s, PTFE ≈ 5 MPa·m/s).
- If the calculated PV exceeds the material limit, **thermal failure** (oil degradation, surface scoring) occurs irrespective of other design merits.
- It is used as a **first-pass design check** before detailed hydrodynamic analysis. [3 marks]

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) Module Internal Choice pattern, BOTH alternatives (Q11A and Q11B) are provided. Students answer ONE.**

---

### Question B1A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Q11A]`

#### (a) [7 marks] — *Compare plain (journal) bearings and rolling-element bearings for automotive applications under the following heads: load capacity, speed capability, friction, lubrication requirement, shock-load tolerance, noise, and cost.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Plain (Journal) Bearing | Rolling-Element Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Capacity** | Very High; limited only by hydrodynamic film and material PV limit | Moderate; limited by Hertzian contact fatigue |
| **Speed Capability** | Moderate–High (depends on viscosity and clearance) | Very High (ball > roller) |
| **Friction** | Higher (viscous + boundary); coefficient $\mu \approx 0.001$–$0.1$ | Very Low; coefficient $\mu \approx 0.001$–$0.005$ |
| **Lubrication** | Continuous oil feed required (oil bath, splash, or pressurized) | Grease or oil; often sealed-for-life |
| **Shock-Load Tolerance** | Excellent — hydrodynamic film absorbs impulsive loads | Poor — instantaneous Hertzian contact fatigue |
| **Noise** | Quiet (no rolling impact) | Higher (rolling element impacts) |
| **Cost** | Moderate (precision machining of bearing housing) | Moderate (mass-produced standardized) |

**Conclusion:** Plain bearings dominate in **heavy, shock-loaded, low-to-moderate speed** locations (crankshaft main, big-end). Rolling bearings dominate in **light-to-moderate, high-speed, precision** locations (alternator, gearbox, wheel hubs).

*[7 marks]*

---

#### (b) [7 marks] — *A rolling-element bearing is to be selected for a gearbox input shaft operating at $N = 3{,}000$ RPM, supporting a radial load $F_r = 4{,}500$ N and an axial load $F_a = 1{,}500$ N. The desired L10 life is 8,000 hours. Determine the required dynamic load rating. Use $p = 3$ for ball bearing. Take $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.0$ (assume $F_a/F_r > e$).*

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute equivalent dynamic load.

$$
P = X F_r + Y F_a = 0.56 \times 4{,}500 + 1.0 \times 1{,}500
$$

$$
P = 2{,}520 + 1{,}500 = 4{,}020 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute target life in revolutions.

$$
L_{10} = L_{10h} \times 60 \times N
$$

$$
L_{10} = 8{,}000 \times 60 \times 3{,}000 = 1.44 \times 10^9 \text{ revolutions}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to millions of revolutions (since catalog uses $10^6$ basis).

$$
L_{10} = 1{,}440 \text{ million revolutions}
$$

**Step 4:** Required dynamic load rating (ball bearing, $p = 3$).

$$
C_{required} = P \cdot (L_{10})^{1/3}
$$

$$
(L_{10})^{1/3} = (1{,}440)^{1/3}
$$

Let $x = 1{,}440$. We compute:

$$
\log_{10}(1{,}440) = 3.158
$$

$$
\log_{10}(x^{1/3}) = 1.053
$$

$$
x^{1/3} = 10^{1.053} \approx 11.30
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{required} = 4{,}020 \times 11.30 \approx 45{,}426 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{required} \approx 45.4 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 5:** From the bearing catalog, select a deep-groove ball bearing with $C \geq 45.4$ kN. For example, **SKF 6308** has $C = 49.4$ kN ✓ (acceptable).

*[7 marks]*

---

### Question B1B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Q11B]`

#### (a) [7 marks] — *Explain with neat sketches the criteria for selection of bearing type for the following automotive applications: (i) Crankshaft main bearing, (ii) Wheel hub bearing, (iii) Alternator pulley shaft.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Crankshaft Main Bearing — Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing**
- **Load:** High fluctuating radial load from combustion and inertial forces.
- **Speed:** Moderate (1000–6000 RPM for the journal surface).
- **Selection reason:** Hydrodynamic journal bearings support heavy loads, dampen vibration, survive contamination, and tolerate shock from combustion pressure. Rolling-element bearings would fail due to shock fatigue.
- **Configuration:** Multi-lobe (elliptical or tilting-pad) bearings in split housings for ease of assembly.

**(ii) Wheel Hub Bearing — Tapered Roller Bearing**
- **Load:** Combined radial (vehicle weight) and axial (cornering, braking) loads.
- **Speed:** Moderate (vehicle speed corresponds to 100–800 RPM at the hub).
- **Selection reason:** Tapered roller bearings handle combined loads, allow precise preload adjustment to eliminate play, and are robust against shock loads from road irregularities.
- **Configuration:** Paired back-to-back or face-to-face arrangement for moment stiffness.

**(iii) Alternator Pulley Shaft — Deep-Groove Ball Bearing**
- **Load:** Light radial + small axial (belt tension).
- **Speed:** Very high (6,000–18,000 RPM).
- **Selection reason:** Ball bearings have the lowest friction at high speed, can be grease-lubricated and sealed-for-life, and are cheap and mass-produced.
- **Configuration:** Single-row deep-groove ball, often with 2RS rubber seals.

*[7 marks]*

---

#### (b) [7 marks] — *A journal bearing has a diameter of 60 mm, length 60 mm, and supports a load of 12,000 N at 1500 RPM. Calculate (i) bearing pressure, (ii) sliding velocity, and (iii) PV value. Comment on its suitability if the bearing material is lead-bronze (PV limit = 40 MPa·m/s).*

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 60$ mm, $L = 60$ mm, $W = 12{,}000$ N, $N = 1{,}500$ RPM, $PV_{limit} = 40$ MPa·m/s.

**(i) Bearing Pressure:**

$$
P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{12{,}000}{60 \times 60} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{3{,}600}
$$

$$
P = 3.33 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Sliding Velocity:**

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi \times 60 \times 1{,}500}{60{,}000} = \dfrac{282{,}743}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
V = 4.712 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) PV Value:**

$$
PV = P \cdot V = 3.33 \times 4.712
$$

$$
PV \approx 15.71 \text{ MPa·m/s}
$$

**Comparison with limit:**

$$
PV_{calculated} = 15.71 \text{ MPa·m/s} < PV_{limit} = 40 \text{ MPa·m/s}
$$

**Comment:** The PV value (15.71) is well below the lead-bronze limit (40 MPa·m/s). The bearing material is **SUITABLE** with a thermal safety margin of:

$$
\text{Margin} = \dfrac{40 - 15.71}{40} \times 100\% \approx 60.7\%
$$

*[7 marks]*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (3 marks lost frequently):** Students forget to convert $D$ in mm correctly. Always use $V = \pi D N / 60{,}000$ when $D$ is in mm, OR convert $D$ to meters first ($D = 0.060$ m) and use $V = \pi D N / 60$.
> 2. **Exponent Confusion:** Ball bearing uses $p = 3$, roller bearing uses $p = 10/3$. Mixing these yields wrong $C_{required}$.
> 3. **Equivalent Load Factors:** Students apply $P = F_r + F_a$ instead of $P = X F_r + Y F_a$, losing 2 marks immediately.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert hours to revolutions:** Always convert $L_{10h}$ to $L_{10}$ (in millions of revolutions) before using catalog equations.
> 5. **Skipping static load check:** Even if $C$ is satisfied, $C_0 \geq f_0 P_0$ must also be verified, especially for oscillating or shock-loaded applications.
> 6. **Choosing plain bearing for high-speed alternator:** This is a fundamental error — plain bearings cannot sustain >10,000 RPM reliably without forced lubrication.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Bearing Selection**

- ☐ Bearing selection = choosing between **plain (sliding)** and **rolling-element** based on load, speed, life, temperature, alignment, and lubrication.

- ☐ **Plain (Journal) Bearings** = High load + moderate speed + shock tolerance. Used in **crankshaft**, **camshaft**, **big-end** bearings.

- ☐ **Ball Bearings** = Low-to-moderate load + very high speed + low friction. Used in **alternator**, **gearbox**, **wheel hub** (light vehicles).

- ☐ **Tapered Roller Bearings** = Combined radial + axial loads. Used in **wheel hubs**, **differentials**, **gearboxes**.

- ☐ **Needle Roller Bearings** = High radial load in limited space. Used in **rocker arm pivots**, **connecting rod small-end**.

- ☐ **Spherical Roller Bearings** = Misalignment tolerance up to 2°. Used in **heavy CV joints**, **planetary gear sets**.

- ☐ **PV value** is the **first screening parameter** for plain bearings; if $PV > PV_{limit}$, design fails.

- ☐ **L10 life equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$ rev with $p = 3$ (ball) or $10/3$ (roller).

- ☐ **Required dynamic load rating:** $C_{required} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}$.

- ☐ **Equivalent dynamic load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (use catalog values of $X$, $Y$).

- ☐ **Static safety check:** $C_0 \geq f_0 \cdot P_0$ (use $f_0 = 1.0$–$2.0$ based on application severity).

- ☐ **Sommerfeld number:** $S = (r/c)^2 \cdot (\mu N / P)$ — controls hydrodynamic film behavior.

- ☐ **Raimondi-Boyd charts** give $\varepsilon$ and $h_o/c$ as functions of $S$ and $L/D$.

- ☐ **Material selection:** Lead-bronze for high-PV, tin-bronze for moderate-PV, PTFE for dry/self-lubricated, ceramic (Si₃N₄) for high-temperature/high-speed hybrid bearings.

- ☐ **Always check** $h_o > h_{min}$ (sum of surface roughness) to ensure full-film lubrication.

- ☐ **Unit conversions to memorize:**
  - $V = \pi D N / 60{,}000$ when $D$ in mm, $V$ in m/s
  - $L_{10h} = L_{10} / (60 N)$ when $L_{10}$ is in revolutions and $N$ in RPM

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearing Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bearing Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Bearing Life?

**Bearing life** is the total number of revolutions (or operating hours at a given constant speed) that a bearing can complete before the first evidence of fatigue failure (spalling, pitting, or cracking) appears on the rolling surfaces. It is the most fundamental design parameter for any rolling contact bearing used in automotive, aerospace, and industrial machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard ISO 281:2007 Terminology):**
> *"Bearing life is the number of revolutions which a bearing can endure under a constant load and speed before the first signs of rolling contact fatigue develop on the raceways or rolling elements."*

### 1.2 Types of Bearing Life Ratings

| Life Type | Symbol | Reliability | Use Case |
|-----------|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Basic Rating Life (L10)** | $L_{10}$ | **90 %** | Industry default for general design |
| **Modified Rating Life (Lnm)** | $L_{nm}$ | > 90 % (custom) | High-reliability systems (aerospace, EVs) |
| **Rating Life at Reliability R** | $L_{R}$ | $R \in [0,1]$ | Special high-reliability applications |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tyre Tread Story"

Think of a **car tyre**: as you drive, the tread gradually wears out. The heavier the car, the faster the wear. Similarly, inside a bearing, every time a ball or roller passes over a microscopic surface defect on the raceway, a tiny bit of **fatigue damage** accumulates. After millions of such passes, a fatigue crack initiates and eventually a flake (spall) breaks off. **Bearing life is simply the "kilometre reading" before this structural fatigue takes over.** A higher load = shorter life; better lubrication + cleaner oil = longer life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 90 % and not 100 %?**
> In a large population of identical bearings, even under identical loads, fatigue failures are statistically distributed. The **L10 life (90 % reliability)** means that **at least 90 % of the bearings** in a statistically significant sample will survive this many revolutions. The remaining 10 % may fail earlier due to material/cleanliness variability. The 10 % is a **statistical confidence bound**, not a design defect.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

- **Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$)** = the constant radial load a bearing can theoretically endure for **1 million revolutions** without fatigue failure.
- **Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$)** = the maximum load a non-rotating bearing can carry without permanent brinelling exceeding **0.0001 of the shaft diameter**.
- **Exponent $p$** = 3 for **ball bearings**, $10/3$ for **roller bearings** (fixed by ISO/AFBMA standards).
- **Reliability Factor ($a_1$)** = correction multiplier for desired reliability above 90 %.
- **Lubrication-Contamination Factor ($a_{23}$ or $a_{ISO}$)** = accounts for lubricant film quality and contamination level.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The mathematical model of bearing life rests on **Hertzian contact stress theory** combined with **empirical fatigue-life data** (Lundberg-Palmgren, 1947; later refined by Ioannides-Harris, ISO 281:2007). The reasoning flow is:

1. Hertzian contact between a ball/roller and raceway produces a **maximum subsurface shear stress** ($\tau_0$) directly under the contact ellipse.
2. The depth of this stress and the volume of stressed material scale with the **load** raised to a fractional power ($1/2$ for ball, $1/2.5$ for roller).
3. According to **Weibull's weakest-link theory**, the probability of fatigue failure scales with the stressed volume to a power that depends on material homogeneity.
4. Combining these, the **number of stress cycles to failure $N$** is proportional to $P^{-p}$ where $p = 3$ (ball) or $p = 10/3$ (roller).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two exponents (3 and 10/3)?**
> A point contact (ball) has smaller contact area and steeper stress gradient than a line contact (roller). The reduced stressed volume in ball bearings gives a sharper (steeper) S-N curve, hence $p = 3$. Line contacts have larger stressed volume and shallower gradient, hence $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$, giving a more gradual life drop with load.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application / Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Basic Dynamic Load Rating Definition | $C = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/p}$ | Used to find required $C$ for desired $L_{10}$ |
| 2 | **Basic Rating Life (Lundberg-Palmgren)** | $L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{p} \times 10^{6}$ revolutions | **MASTER EQUATION — must be memorized** |
| 3 | Rating Life in Hours | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \, N} \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{p}$ | $N$ = speed in rpm |
| 4 | Equivalent Dynamic Load (Radial + Axial) | $P = X \, F_{r} + Y \, F_{a}$ | $X, Y$ from bearing tables; $F_{a}/F_{r} \le e$ |
| 5 | Modified Rating Life (ISO 281:2007) | $L_{nm} = a_{1} \, a_{ISO} \, L_{10}$ | $a_{1}$ = reliability, $a_{ISO}$ = lub + contamination |
| 6 | Modified Rating Life (Legacy ISO 281:1990) | $L_{na} = a_{1} \, a_{23} \, L_{10}$ | Older formulation; still in textbooks |
| 7 | Reliability Factor | $a_{1} = \left( \dfrac{0.9}{R} \right)^{1/a}$ | $R$ = required reliability, $a = 1.5$ (ball) / $1.35$ (roller) |
| 8 | Required Dynamic Load Rating | $C_{\text{req}} = P \left( \dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot 60 \, N}{10^{6}} \right)^{1/p}$ | For bearing selection / design problems |
| 9 | Required Dynamic Load (with corrections) | $C_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{P}{a_{1} \, a_{23}} \left( \dfrac{60 \, N \, L_{10h}}{10^{6}} \right)^{1/p}$ | Full correction including reliability & lubrication |
| 10 | Speed Conversion | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ rad/s | Useful for SI-based dynamic calculations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Convention:** Use $L_{10}$ in **revolutions** if the question states "speed in rpm and life in revolutions" — do NOT convert to hours unless asked. Always state the exponent $p$ value clearly in your solution.

## 2.3 Selection of Exponent $p$

$$\boxed{p = \begin{cases} 3, & \text{for ball bearings (point contact)} \\[4pt] \dfrac{10}{3}, & \text{for roller bearings (line contact)} \end{cases}}$$

## 2.4 Why Bearing Life Matters in Automotive Engineering

| Subsystem | Bearing Type | Typical L10h Target | Consequence of Failure |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Wheel hub | Tapered roller | 5000 – 20 000 h | Wheel seizure → accident |
| Transmission gearbox | Cylindrical / tapered roller | 5000 – 30 000 h | Gearbox jamming |
| Alternator | Deep groove ball | 2000 – 8000 h | Electrical failure |
| Turbocharger | Ball / roller | 1500 – 4000 h | Engine power loss |
| Electric motor (EV) | Cylindrical / ball | 20 000 – 40 000 h | Vehicle immobilization |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Cost Optimization:** Over-specifying a bearing wastes money; under-specifying causes warranty claims.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Life calculations feed into **IoT-enabled condition monitoring** for fleet vehicles.
- **EV Revolution:** High-speed electric motors (15 000 – 25 000 rpm) demand ultra-precise life estimates because bearing failure is the **#1 cause of EV drivetrain downtime**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Basic Rating Life Equation

We will derive the Lundberg-Palmgren life equation from first principles of Hertzian contact stress and Weibull fatigue statistics.

### Step 1: Hertzian Contact Stress (Ball Bearing)

For a ball pressed against a raceway with normal load $Q$, the maximum contact pressure $p_0$ is:

$$p_{0} = \dfrac{3 \, F}{2 \pi \, a \, b}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the semi-axes of the elliptical contact area.

For two convex bodies (ball-on-raceway), the contact area scales as $Q^{0.5}$:

$$a \cdot b \propto Q^{1/2}$$

Therefore, the **maximum contact stress** scales as:

$$\sigma_{\max} \propto Q^{1/2}$$

*Explanation: Increasing the load linearly increases the contact area only with its square root. Hence the contact stress grows as the square root of the load.*

### Step 2: Subsurface Shear Stress — Location of Fatigue Initiation

Bearing fatigue cracks initiate at the **depth of maximum reversed shear stress**, located at a depth $z$ below the contact surface. Lundberg and Palmgren showed that:

$$z \propto a \propto Q^{0.5}$$

The shear stress at this critical depth:

$$\tau_{0} \propto Q^{0.5}$$

### Step 3: Weibull Weakest-Link Theory

The probability $S$ of survival after $N$ stress cycles is:

$$S = \exp \left[ - \left( \dfrac{N}{N_{0}} \right)^{a} \cdot \dfrac{V}{V_{0}} \right]$$

where $a$ is the Weibull slope, $V$ is the stressed volume, and $N_0, V_0$ are reference values.

The stressed volume $V$ scales as $z^{3}$ (3D volume), and $z \propto Q^{0.5}$, so:

$$V \propto Q^{3/2}$$

### Step 4: Combining Fatigue and Volume Effects

The cycles to failure at constant load and constant reliability (so that $S = \text{const}$) become:

$$N \propto Q^{-b/a}$$

For ball bearings, the experimentally determined exponent ratio is:

$$\dfrac{b}{a} = 3$$

For roller bearings, the line contact gives:

$$\dfrac{b}{a} = \dfrac{10}{3}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{N = N_{\text{ref}} \cdot \left( \dfrac{Q_{\text{ref}}}{Q} \right)^{p}, \quad p = 3 \text{ (ball)} \text{ or } \dfrac{10}{3} \text{ (roller)}}$$

### Step 5: Reference Condition — Defining $C$

By ISO/AFBMA convention, the reference is: $Q_{\text{ref}} = C$ and $N_{\text{ref}} = 10^6$ revolutions.

Substituting:

$$L_{10} = 10^{6} \cdot \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{p}$$

This is the **fundamental bearing life equation** that KTU expects every student to derive under examination.

### Step 6: Conversion to Hours

If the bearing rotates at constant speed $N$ (rpm), one revolution takes $1/N$ minutes. Therefore:

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10}}{60 \, N} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \, N} \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{p} \text{ hours}$$

This completes the derivation.

## 3.2 Full Worked Example (Numerical — 14 marks style)

**Problem Statement:**
A deep groove ball bearing (series 6208) has a dynamic load rating $C = 32.5$ kN. It operates at a radial load $F_r = 4.5$ kN, axial load $F_a = 1.5$ kN, and speed $N = 1800$ rpm. From the manufacturer's catalogue, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.6$ (since $F_a/C_0 < e$). The required reliability is 95 %, hence $a_1 = 0.62$. Lubrication is good with $a_{23} = 0.8$. Calculate:
(a) The basic rating life $L_{10}$ in revolutions and hours.
(b) The modified rating life $L_{na}$ in hours.

### Solution

#### Part (a): Basic Rating Life

**Step 1: Calculate Equivalent Dynamic Load $P$**

$$\dfrac{F_a}{C_0} \text{ ratio assumed: catalogue value of } e \text{ for this load ratio } \approx 0.22$$

$$P = X \, F_r + Y \, F_a = 0.56 \times 4.5 + 1.6 \times 1.5$$

Computing term by term:

$$P = 2.52 + 2.40 = 4.92 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Apply Basic Life Equation (Ball, so $p = 3$)**

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{3} \times 10^{6}$$

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{32.5}{4.92} \right)^{3} \times 10^{6}$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\dfrac{32.5}{4.92} = 6.606$$

Cube it:

$$6.606^{3} = 6.606 \times 6.606 \times 6.606$$

$$6.606^{2} = 43.64$$

$$6.606^{3} = 43.64 \times 6.606 = 288.3$$

$$L_{10} = 288.3 \times 10^{6} \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Hours**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10}}{60 \, N} = \dfrac{288.3 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1800}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{288.3 \times 10^{6}}{1.08 \times 10^{5}} = 2669.4 \text{ hours}$$

#### Part (b): Modified Rating Life

$$L_{na} = a_{1} \, a_{23} \, L_{10h}$$

$$L_{na} = 0.62 \times 0.8 \times 2669.4$$

$$L_{na} = 0.496 \times 2669.4 = 1324.0 \text{ hours}$$

### Final Answers
- $L_{10} = 288.3 \times 10^{6}$ revolutions
- $L_{10h} = 2669.4$ hours
- $L_{na} = 1324.0$ hours (at 95 % reliability with $a_{23} = 0.8$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **higher reliability (95 % vs 90 %) reduces the life by 38 %** (from 2669 h to 1625 h before $a_{23}$), and **good-but-not-perfect lubrication (a23 = 0.8) further reduces it by another 20 %**. The combined effect of reliability and lubrication is multiplicative — both must be controlled in critical applications.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of $a_1$ and $a_{23}$

| Reliability $R$ (%) | $a_1$ (Ball, $a = 1.5$) | $a_1$ (Roller, $a = 1.35$) |
|---------------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|
| 90 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| 95 | 0.62 | 0.66 |
| 96 | 0.53 | 0.59 |
| 97 | 0.44 | 0.50 |
| 98 | 0.33 | 0.40 |
| 99 | 0.21 | 0.27 |
| 99.5 | 0.15 | 0.20 |
| 99.9 | 0.07 | 0.10 |

| Lubrication / Contamination | $a_{23}$ |
|------------------------------|----------|
| Perfect clean oil, full film | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| Clean oil, normal film | 0.8 – 1.0 |
| Contaminated oil | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| Heavy contamination | < 0.5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Bearing Life Calculation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Identify Application and Operating Conditions] --> B[Determine Speed N in rpm]
    B --> C[Determine Radial Load Fr and Axial Load Fa]
    C --> D[Select Bearing Type Ball or Roller]
    D --> E[Lookup Basic Dynamic Load Rating C from Catalogue]
    E --> F[Lookup X Y and e from Catalogue for Fa Fr Ratio]
    F --> G[Calculate Equivalent Dynamic Load P equals X Fr plus Y Fa]
    G --> H{Determine Exponent p Ball equals 3 Roller equals 10 divided by 3}
    H --> I[Calculate Basic Rating Life L10 equals C over P to power p times 10 to power 6 revolutions]
    I --> J[Convert L10 to Hours L10h equals L10 divided by 60 N]
    J --> K{Is High Reliability greater than 90 percent Required}
    K -->|Yes| L[Apply Reliability Factor a1]
    K -->|No| M[Use L10h as Design Life]
    L --> N[Apply Lubrication and Contamination Factor a23]
    N --> O[Calculate Modified Life Lna equals a1 times a23 times L10h]
    O --> P{Is Lna greater than Required Service Life}
    P -->|Yes| Q[ACCEPT Bearing Suitable for Application]
    P -->|No| R[REJECT Select Next Higher C value from Catalogue]
    R --> S[Recalculate with New C]
    S --> I
    M --> T[END Design Complete]
    Q --> T
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Bearing Life

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[INPUT Operating Variables] --> L1
    INPUT --> L2
    INPUT --> L3
    INPUT --> L4
    INPUT --> L5

    L1[Load Magnitude and Type] --> CENTER
    L2[Rotational Speed] --> CENTER
    L3[Lubrication Film Quality] --> CENTER
    L4[Operating Temperature] --> CENTER
    L5[Contamination Level] --> CENTER

    CENTER[BEARING LIFE L10 and Lna] --> OUT1
    CENTER --> OUT2
    CENTER --> OUT3
    CENTER --> OUT4

    OUT1[Mechanical Design Decision] --> FINAL
    OUT2[Predictive Maintenance Schedule] --> FINAL
    OUT3[Cost and Warranty Planning] --> FINAL
    OUT4[Safety Factor Analysis] --> FINAL

    FINAL[FINAL Automotive Subsystem Reliability]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Modified Life Equation Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE[BASE L10 equals C over P to power p times 10 to power 6] --> MOD

    subgraph MOD[MODIFICATION LAYER]
        direction LR
        REL[Reliability Factor a1] --> SUM
        LUB[Lubrication Factor kappa and Viscosity] --> SUM
        CONT[Contamination Factor eC] --> SUM
        TEMP[Temperature Factor fT] --> SUM
        SUM[Multiplicative Combination]
    end

    MOD --> OUT[OUTPUT Lna equals a1 times a23 times L10]
    OUT --> APP[Application Life in Hours equals Lna divided by 60 N]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Board Exam:** Always **draw a block diagram** showing how $L_{10}$ is the **base**, and the modification factors ($a_1$, $a_{23}$, etc.) are **multiplicative corrections** leading to $L_{na}$. Examiners reward structural clarity with **2–3 extra marks** beyond the algebraic calculation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Basic Rating Life (L10)" of a rolling contact bearing. Why is the reliability fixed at 90 % in this definition?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
The basic rating life, denoted $L_{10}$, is the number of revolutions (or hours at a constant speed) that **at least 90 % of a group of apparently identical bearings** will complete (or exceed) under a constant radial load without developing fatigue failure.

The 90 % reliability is a **statistical convention** established by ISO 281 and AFBMA. It provides a balance between:
- Sufficient design confidence (90 % survival is industry-acceptable for general machinery), and
- Reasonable economy (pushing to 99 % reliability would dramatically increase bearing size and cost).

It is the **statistical population reliability**, not the reliability of an individual bearing.

### Q2. List any six factors that affect the operational life of a rolling contact bearing.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (six factors):**
1. **Magnitude and type of applied load** (radial, axial, or combined).
2. **Rotational speed** of the bearing.
3. **Lubrication quality** (viscosity ratio $\kappa$, oil film thickness).
4. **Operating temperature** (affects lubricant viscosity).
5. **Contamination level** of the lubricant (dirt, water, particles).
6. **Mounting alignment and fitting** (misalignment reduces effective life).
7. **Material cleanliness and heat treatment** of bearing steel.
8. **Reliability requirement** of the application.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the terms 'Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)' and 'Basic Static Load Rating (C0)' of a rolling contact bearing. Why are two different ratings needed? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$)** is defined as the constant, purely radial (for radial bearings) load that a bearing can theoretically endure for **$10^6$ revolutions** without fatigue failure occurring on the rolling surfaces.

*Sketch description:* Show a radial bearing with radial load $F_r$ applied through the inner ring. Indicate the contact ellipse and stress zone where fatigue initiates.

The **Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$)** is defined as the load that produces a permanent indentation (brinelling) of **0.0001 times the shaft diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact point under static (non-rotating) conditions.

*Sketch description:* Show a non-rotating bearing with an excessive radial load creating a visible indent on the raceway.

**Why two ratings?**
- Under **rotating** conditions, fatigue is the dominant failure mode → $C$ governs.
- Under **static or very slow oscillating** motion, plastic deformation (brinelling) is the dominant failure mode → $C_0$ governs.
- A bearing can be safe statically but fail by fatigue in service, and vice versa. Hence both are independently specified.

---

**(b) A deep groove ball bearing operates under the following conditions: Radial load $F_r = 6$ kN, Axial load $F_a = 2$ kN, Speed $N = 1440$ rpm. The dynamic load rating of the bearing is $C = 35$ kN. The manufacturer specifies $X = 0.56$ and $Y = 1.6$ for the given $F_a/C_0$ ratio. Calculate (i) the equivalent dynamic load $P$, (ii) the basic rating life $L_{10}$ in revolutions, (iii) the rating life in hours. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Equivalent Dynamic Load $P$:**
For a deep groove ball bearing with both radial and axial loads:

$$P = X \, F_r + Y \, F_a = (0.56 \times 6) + (1.6 \times 2)$$

$$P = 3.36 + 3.20 = 6.56 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Basic Rating Life $L_{10}$ in revolutions:**

For ball bearing, exponent $p = 3$.

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{3} \times 10^{6}$$

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{35}{6.56} \right)^{3} \times 10^{6} = (5.335)^{3} \times 10^{6}$$

$$5.335^{3} = 5.335 \times 5.335 \times 5.335 = 151.8$$

$$L_{10} = 151.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ revolutions}$$

**(iii) Rating Life in Hours:**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10}}{60 \, N} = \dfrac{151.8 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1440}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{151.8 \times 10^{6}}{86400} = 1756.9 \text{ hours}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $P = 6.56$ kN
- $L_{10} = 151.8 \times 10^{6}$ revolutions
- $L_{10h} = 1757$ hours (approx.)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the modified bearing life equation as per ISO 281:2007. What is the significance of the factors $a_1$ and $a_{ISO}$? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **ISO 281:2007 modified rating life** equation is:

$$L_{nm} = a_1 \, a_{ISO} \, L_{10}$$

where:
- $L_{10}$ = **basic rating life** at 90 % reliability (from standard equation).
- $a_1$ = **reliability factor**, accounting for survival probability greater than 90 %.
- $a_{ISO}$ = **system factor** combining the effects of lubrication, contamination, fatigue load limit of the material, and internal stress distribution.

**Significance of $a_1$:**
- Default $a_1 = 1$ corresponds to 90 % reliability.
- For higher reliability (95 %, 99 %, etc.), $a_1 < 1$, meaning the design life must be **reduced** to ensure the required confidence level.
- For 95 %, $a_1 \approx 0.62$ (ball) or $0.66$ (roller).

**Significance of $a_{ISO}$:**
- $a_{ISO}$ integrates three sub-factors: the **lubrication factor** (function of viscosity ratio $\kappa$), the **contamination factor** (function of filter cleanliness and particle size), and a **fatigue stress limit factor** for the bearing steel.
- $a_{ISO} > 1$ indicates the bearing will **outlive** its $L_{10}$ estimate (good lubrication, clean oil, premium material).
- $a_{ISO} < 1$ indicates **premature fatigue** is likely (poor film, contamination).
- Typical range: $0.1$ to $3$ depending on operating conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The legacy version $L_{na} = a_1 \, a_{23} \, L_{10}$ uses only two factors. ISO 281:2007 unifies multiple real-world effects into $a_{ISO}$, but for **numerical problems in KTU exams**, the $a_1 a_{23}$ form is more common. Be prepared for both notations.

---

**(b) A tapered roller bearing has $C = 120$ kN and operates at a speed of 1200 rpm under an equivalent dynamic load of $P = 20$ kN. Calculate the basic rating life in revolutions and hours. If the bearing is required to last 8000 hours at 99 % reliability, determine whether the bearing is suitable. Use $a_1 = 0.21$ (for 99 % reliability, roller). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Basic Rating Life $L_{10}$ (Roller bearing, $p = 10/3$):**

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{10/3} \times 10^{6} = \left( \dfrac{120}{20} \right)^{10/3} \times 10^{6}$$

$$\dfrac{C}{P} = 6$$

$$6^{10/3} = 6^{3.333} = 6^{3} \times 6^{1/3} = 216 \times 1.8171 = 392.5$$

$$L_{10} = 392.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ revolutions}$$

**Step 2: Convert to Hours:**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10}}{60 \, N} = \dfrac{392.5 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1200} = \dfrac{392.5 \times 10^{6}}{72000}$$

$$L_{10h} = 5451.4 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: Modified Life at 99 % Reliability:**

$$L_{na} = a_1 \times L_{10h} = 0.21 \times 5451.4 = 1144.8 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4: Compare with Required Life:**

Required life = 8000 hours  
Available modified life = 1144.8 hours  

Since $L_{na} = 1144.8$ h $\ll$ 8000 h,

**The bearing is NOT suitable for the application.**

**Step 5: Calculate Required $C_{\text{req}}$ (for completeness):**

$$C_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{P}{a_1} \left( \dfrac{60 \, N \, L_{10h}^{\text{req}}}{10^{6}} \right)^{3/10}$$

$$C_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{20}{0.21} \left( \dfrac{60 \times 1200 \times 8000}{10^{6}} \right)^{0.3}$$

$$\dfrac{60 \times 1200 \times 8000}{10^{6}} = 576$$

$$576^{0.3} = \exp(0.3 \ln 576) = \exp(0.3 \times 6.357) = \exp(1.907) = 6.735$$

$$C_{\text{req}} = 95.24 \times 6.735 = 641.3 \text{ kN}$$

A bearing with $C \geq 641$ kN must be selected — significantly larger than the available 120 kN.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **MISTAKE 1:** Students often forget to convert $L_{10}$ to hours when the required life is in hours. The factor of $60N$ is **frequently dropped**, giving life values inflated by a factor of 60 — losing 2–3 marks.
> - **MISTAKE 2:** Using $p = 3$ for a **roller bearing** is a very common error. KTU examiners specifically test this. Memorize: **Ball = 3, Roller = 10/3**.
> - **MISTAKE 3:** Concluding "bearing is suitable" without computing the modified life. The question **explicitly asks for 99 % reliability**, so the $a_1$ correction is mandatory.
> - **MISTAKE 4:** Reporting $C_{\text{req}}$ in N instead of kN (or vice versa) loses a mark for unit consistency. Always state units clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Core Concepts
- **L10 life** = number of revolutions at which **90 %** of a bearing population survives without fatigue failure.
- **Basic Dynamic Load Rating $C$** = reference load corresponding to $10^6$ revolutions of life.
- **Basic Static Load Rating $C_0$** = load causing 0.0001 × shaft-diameter brinelling under non-rotation.
- **Equivalent dynamic load $P = X F_r + Y F_a$** synthesises combined loading into an equivalent radial load.

### Critical Equations
- **Master Life Equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p \times 10^6$ revolutions
- **Hours Form:** $L_{10h} = 10^6 (C/P)^p / (60 N)$
- **Modified Life (ISO 281:2007):** $L_{nm} = a_1 \times a_{ISO} \times L_{10}$
- **Legacy form (ISO 281:1990):** $L_{na} = a_1 \times a_{23} \times L_{10}$
- **Reliability factor formula:** $a_1 = (0.9/R)^{1/a}$

### Exponent Selection (CRITICAL)
- **Ball bearings → $p = 3$**
- **Roller bearings (cylindrical, tapered, spherical, needle) → $p = 10/3$**

### Reliability Factor Quick Reference
| Reliability R | $a_1$ (Ball) | $a_1$ (Roller) |
|---------------|--------------|-----------------|
| 90 % | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| 95 % | 0.62 | 0.66 |
| 99 % | 0.21 | 0.27 |

### Lubrication-Contamination Factor $a_{23}$ Quick Reference
- Perfect clean oil film → $a_{23} = 1.0$ to $1.5$
- Normal industrial conditions → $a_{23} = 0.5$ to $0.8$
- Heavy contamination → $a_{23} < 0.5$

### Conversion Reminders
- $1 \text{ million revolutions at } N \text{ rpm} = (10^6)/(60N) \text{ hours} = 16667/N \text{ hours}$
- Speed in rad/s = $(2\pi N)/60$
- $1 \text{ HP} \approx 0.746 \text{ kW}$ (often needed to compute equivalent load from power)

### Common Exam Traps
- Using $p = 3$ for roller bearings (lose 2 marks)
- Forgetting $a_1$ when reliability > 90 % (lose 1–2 marks)
- Mixing up $C$ and $C_0$ in catalogue lookups
- Reporting final life in revolutions when question asks for hours
- Not drawing a labelled block diagram for the modified life equation

### Automotive Application Quick Map
| Subsystem | Bearing Type | Life Driver |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Wheel hub | Tapered roller | Shock loads + contamination |
| Transmission | Tapered / cylindrical | Combined radial + thrust |
| Alternator | Deep groove ball | High rpm + temperature |
| Turbocharger | Angular contact ball | Very high rpm + heat |
| EV traction motor | Cylindrical / hybrid | Ultra-high rpm + electrical pitting |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Load Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Load Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Static Load Capacity (C₀)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (ISO 76:2009 / ISO 15:2017)**
> *Static Load Rating* or *Static Load Capacity* ($C_0$) of a rolling element bearing is defined as **the load (pure radial load for radial bearings, pure axial/thrust load for thrust bearings) which produces a permanent deformation of 0.0001 of the diameter of the rolling element ($D$ or $D_w$) at the most heavily loaded contact point, at zero r.p.m. (stationary condition).**

The subscript **"0"** in $C_0$ is the standard ISO symbol used to denote a *static* rating (no relative motion between the rolling elements and the raceways).

### Key Characteristics of $C_0$

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Test Condition** | Bearing is stationary ($N = 0$ r.p.m.) |
| **Failure Criterion** | Permanent (plastic) Brinell-type indentation $\delta_p = 0.0001 \times D$ |
| **Reliability Level** | Implicit 100% (deterministic limit) |
| **Application Regime** | Very slow speeds, oscillating motion, static support |
| **Unit** | Newtons (N) or kilo-Newtons (kN) |

## 1.2 Dynamic Load Capacity (C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (ISO 281:2007 / ISO 15:2017)**
> *Basic Dynamic Load Rating* ($C$) is defined as **the constant, purely radial load (for radial bearings) or constant, purely axial/thrust load (for thrust bearings) which a bearing with stationary outer ring can theoretically endure for a rating life of 1,000,000 (one million) revolutions of the inner ring**, with a 90% reliability factor.

### Key Characteristics of $C$

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Test Condition** | Inner ring rotating at $N$ r.p.m. |
| **Failure Criterion** | Spalling (fatigue pitting) of raceway surface |
| **Reference Life ($L_{10}$)** | $10^6$ revolutions |
| **Reliability Level** | 90% (i.e., 10% of a population fails by this life) |
| **Unit** | Newtons (N) or kilo-Newtons (kN) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Two Different Strength Tests of a Bridge Bolt"**

Imagine you are testing a high-strength bolt that will be used in an automotive wheel hub assembly.

**Static Load Capacity ($C_0$) = The "Press Test":**
You take the bolt and put it in a hydraulic press. You slowly increase the load until the bolt develops a tiny permanent dimple on its surface — about one ten-thousandth of its own diameter deep. *That* load is the **static rating**. It is the maximum load the bearing can carry **without being permanently deformed** while it is sitting still. Think of a car parked on a hill for months — the wheel bearings only need to resist this kind of load.

**Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$) = The "Fatigue Marathon Test":**
You now take an identical bolt and spin its inner race at high speed under a constant load. You run it for 1,000,000 revolutions and check whether a fatigue crack (pitting) has appeared on the raceway. **90% of identical bolts can survive this marathon** before pitting begins. *That* load is the **dynamic rating**. This represents the **fatigue strength** of the bearing under continuous rolling contact stress.

**The Bottom Line:**
- $C_0$ answers: *"How much can it hold without getting dented while stationary?"*
- $C$ answers: *"How much can it carry for 1 million rotations without pitting?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** For the KTU board exam, students must memorize that both ratings are derived from **Hertzian contact stress theory** between the rolling element and the raceway. Static rating uses the **plastic indentation limit**, while dynamic rating uses the **fatigue spalling limit** at $10^6$ revs.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Factors

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| $i$ | Number of rows of rolling elements | 1 (single row), 2 (double row) |
| $z$ | Number of rolling elements per row | Typically 8–15 |
| $D$ or $D_w$ | Diameter of rolling element | mm |
| $L$ or $L_w$ | Length of roller (for cylindrical/tapered bearings) | mm |
| $\alpha$ | Nominal contact angle | 0° (radial), 15°–40° (angular contact) |
| $\cos\alpha$ | Load distribution factor along contact line | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Manufacturers (SKF, FAG, NTN, Timken) publish $C$ and $C_0$ values directly in their catalogues for every standard bearing. The design engineer's job is to **verify** the chosen bearing satisfies both the static and dynamic requirements — *not* to calculate these ratings from scratch in routine design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hertzian Contact Stress Foundation

Both $C$ and $C_0$ originate from **Heinrich Hertz's contact stress theory (1881)**. When a sphere/roller presses against a raceway, contact occurs over a small elliptical area rather than at a single point, producing high localized stresses.

### 2.1.1 The Hertzian Stress Chain

1. **Elastic Deformation Phase:** Under load, the rolling element and raceway deform elastically. Stress at the contact ellipse is non-uniform — maximum at the center, zero at the edges.
2. **Maximum Compressive Stress:** Hertz showed the peak contact stress $p_0$ is proportional to $\sqrt{F \cdot K / (l \cdot R)}$ where $F$ is the normal load, $K$ the curvature-dependent constant, $l$ the contact length, and $R$ the equivalent radius.
3. **Subsurface Shear Stress Maximum:** Just below the contact surface, an ortho-radial shear stress reaches its peak — this is the **initiation site for fatigue pitting**.
4. **Plastic Indentation Phase:** When $p_0$ exceeds the material's elastic limit, a permanent Brinell-type indent forms — this defines the **static limit** ($C_0$).
5. **Cyclic Shear Fatigue Phase:** Below the plastic limit, repeated reversals cause subsurface cracks to nucleate and propagate to the surface as **pits/spalls** — this defines the **dynamic limit** ($C$).

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Model for Static Load Capacity

### 2.2.1 Ball Bearings (Point Contact)

For a single ball under purely radial load, Hertzian analysis gives the contact area. Summing over all balls and accounting for load distribution:

$$C_0 = f_0 \cdot i \cdot z \cdot D^2 \cdot \cos\alpha$$

where:
- $f_0$ = **geometry/material factor** (from manufacturer catalogues, depends on $D \cos\alpha$ and raceway groove radius)
- $i$ = number of rows
- $z$ = number of balls per row
- $D$ = ball diameter (mm)
- $\cos\alpha$ = load distribution factor

### 2.2.2 Roller Bearings (Line Contact)

For cylindrical/tapered rollers, the contact is a line, not a point:

$$C_0 = f_0 \cdot i \cdot z \cdot L \cdot D \cdot \cos\alpha$$

where $L$ is the effective roller length.

---

## 2.3 Mathematical Model for Dynamic Load Capacity

### 2.3.1 Ball Bearings (Point Contact)

Derived from Hertzian contact theory and Lundberg-Palmgren fatigue analysis (1947):

$$C = f_c \cdot (i \cdot \cos\alpha)^{0.7} \cdot z^{2/3} \cdot D^{1.8}$$

where:
- $f_c$ = **bearing series / material factor** (varies with bearing type — deep-groove, self-aligning, angular contact, etc.)
- The exponents $0.7$, $2/3$, $1.8$ come from Hertzian contact geometry integrated with Weibull fatigue statistics.

### 2.3.2 Roller Bearings (Line Contact, where $L_w \neq D_w$)

$$C = f_c \cdot i^{7/9} \cdot z^{3/4} \cdot D^{29/27} \cdot L^{4/9}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For standard cylindrical rollers where $L \approx D$, this simplifies and the exponent structure collapses into a cleaner form found in catalogue tables.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.4.1 Static & Dynamic Load Rating Formulas

| Bearing Type | Static Rating $C_0$ | Dynamic Rating $C$ |
|--------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **Ball Bearing (radial)** | $C_0 = f_0 \cdot i \cdot z \cdot D^2 \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $C = f_c \cdot (i \cos\alpha)^{0.7} \cdot z^{2/3} \cdot D^{1.8}$ |
| **Ball Bearing (thrust)** | $C_{0a} = f_{0a} \cdot z \cdot D^2 \cdot \sin\alpha$ | $C_a = f_a \cdot z^{2/3} \cdot D^{1.8} \cdot \sin\alpha$ |
| **Roller Bearing (radial)** | $C_0 = f_0 \cdot i \cdot z \cdot L \cdot D \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $C = f_c \cdot i^{7/9} \cdot z^{3/4} \cdot D^{29/27} \cdot L^{4/9}$ |
| **Roller Bearing (thrust)** | $C_{0a} = f_{0a} \cdot z \cdot L \cdot D \cdot \sin\alpha$ | $C_a = f_a \cdot z^{3/4} \cdot L^{4/9} \cdot D^{29/27} \cdot \sin\alpha$ |

### 2.4.2 Equivalence Between Rating Life and Basic Rating

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|----------|---------|-------|
| **Rating Life (revs)** | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | revolutions |
| **Rating Life (hours)** | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot N} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | hours |
| **Equivalent Dynamic Load** | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | N |
| **Equivalent Static Load** | $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ | N |
| **Static Safety Check** | $S_0 = \dfrac{C_0}{P_0} \geq 1$ (or per Table) | dimensionless |

> where **$p$ = 3 for ball bearings** and **$p$ = 10/3 for roller bearings** — **memorize this for KTU exams**.

### 2.4.3 Recommended Static Safety Factor $S_0$

| Operating Condition | Minimum $S_0$ |
|---------------------|---------------|
| Smooth, vibration-free operation | 1.0 |
| Normal operation | 1.5 |
| Shock loads, heavy vibration | 2.0 – 3.0 |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Automotive Design:**

1. **Wheel Hub Bearings:** Subject to combined radial (vehicle weight cornering) and axial (braking thrust) loads. Designers must check **both** $C$ (for fatigue life) and $C_0$ (for parked/impact loading).
2. **Transmission Gears:** Shaft-mounted bearings carry high cyclic loads from gear meshing — must be sized for $L_{10h} > 3000$ hrs (typical automotive transmission target).
3. **Engine Crankshaft Bearings:** Combine dynamic radial load (from combustion) and high speed (2000–7000 rpm). $C$ dominates the design.
4. **Steering Column Bearings:** Often stationary or oscillating — $C_0$ may dominate.
5. **Electric Vehicle Drive Motors:** Very high speed (15,000+ rpm) under dynamic loading — dynamic rating is the bottleneck.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Dynamic Load Capacity Uses $10^6$ Revolutions

The choice of $10^6$ revolutions as the reference life is **not arbitrary** — it emerges from historical standardization by SKF and the German DIN committee in the 1920s, which later became ISO 281.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Start from Lundberg-Palmgren Fatigue Equation (1947):**

The probability of survival of a rolling element bearing under repeated Hertzian contact loading follows a Weibull distribution. Lundberg and Palmgren derived that the **rating life** (in revolutions) for a given load $P$ is:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \cdot 10^6 \text{ revolutions}$$

where $C$ is the load that gives exactly $10^6$ revolutions of life at 90% reliability, and $p$ is the **exponent of the load-life curve**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Exponent $p$:**

From Hertzian contact theory:
- **Point contact (ball):** Contact area $\propto F^{2/3}$, so subsurface stress $\propto F^{1/3}$, and fatigue life $\propto (\text{stress})^{-9}$. Combined with volume-of-stressed-material scaling, the net exponent becomes $p = 3$.
- **Line contact (roller):** Different stress distribution along the line gives $p = 10/3$.

$$p = \begin{cases} 3 & \text{for ball bearings} \\ \dfrac{10}{3} & \text{for roller bearings} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Define $C$ as the Load Giving Exactly $10^6$ Revs:**

By definition, $C$ is the load at which $L_{10} = 10^6$. Substituting:

$$L_{10} = 1 \times 10^6 = \left(\frac{C}{C}\right)^p \cdot 10^6 \;\; \checkmark$$

This is a self-consistent definition.

**Step 4 — Invert for General Load $P$:**

For any applied equivalent load $P$, the life scales inversely:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \cdot 10^6$$

**Converting to hours of operation:**

$$L_{10h} = \frac{L_{10}}{60 \cdot N} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot N} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

where $N$ is rotational speed in **rpm**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Static Load Capacity Formula for Ball Bearings

**Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Pressure at Indentation Limit:**

For a ball pressed into a raceway, Hertz's theory gives the maximum compressive stress $p_0$ at the contact ellipse center:

$$p_0 = \frac{3F}{2\pi a b}$$

where $a$, $b$ are the semi-axes of the contact ellipse, both $\propto F^{1/3}$ for point contact.

**Step 2 — Permanent Indentation Condition:**

Setting $p_0$ equal to the indentation hardness $H$ (≈ 4.5 GPa for bearing steel, conservative value used by ISO 76):

$$p_0 = H \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F_{\text{limit}} \propto D^2$$

This is why the static rating scales with $D^2$ — the contact area grows as $D^2$ for a sphere-on-sphere Hertzian contact under the same stress limit.

**Step 3 — Sum Over All Balls (Load Distribution):**

Under a purely radial load, only a fraction of the balls in the loaded zone carry load. The ISO standard adopts an idealized load distribution where the load per ball for a stationary bearing under radial load gives:

$$\text{Total load capacity} = f_0 \cdot i \cdot z \cdot D^2 \cdot \cos\alpha$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Factor $f_0$:**

The factor $f_0$ absorbs all the constants from Hertz's equations (curvature sum, material elastic constants, the numerical value of $H = 4.5$ GPa). ISO 76 tabulates $f_0$ as a function of $D \cos\alpha$ and the raceway conformity ratio.

$$\boxed{C_0 = f_0 \cdot i \cdot z \cdot D^2 \cdot \cos\alpha}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A single-row deep-groove ball bearing has the following data:
> - Ball diameter $D = 12.7$ mm (≈ 0.5 in)
> - Number of balls $z = 9$
> - Contact angle $\alpha = 0°$ (radial bearing)
> - Material factor $f_0 = 12.5$ (typical for this $D \cos\alpha$ range, from ISO 76 tables)
> - Dynamic factor $f_c = 1.0$ (normalized reference)
> - Bearing speed $N = 1440$ rpm
> - Equivalent radial load $P = 3500$ N

**Compute (a) Static Load Capacity $C_0$, (b) Dynamic Load Capacity $C$, (c) Rating Life $L_{10h}$.**

### Part (a): Static Load Capacity

$$C_0 = f_0 \cdot i \cdot z \cdot D^2 \cdot \cos\alpha$$

Substituting values ($i = 1$ for single-row, $\cos 0° = 1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 12.5 \times 1 \times 9 \times (12.7)^2 \times 1 \\
&= 12.5 \times 9 \times 161.29 \\
&= 12.5 \times 1451.61 \\
&= 18{,}145 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 18.15 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (b): Dynamic Load Capacity

$$C = f_c \cdot (i \cdot \cos\alpha)^{0.7} \cdot z^{2/3} \cdot D^{1.8}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 1.0 \times (1)^{0.7} \times (9)^{2/3} \times (12.7)^{1.8}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
(9)^{2/3} &= (9^{1/3})^2 = (2.0801)^2 = 4.3267 \\
(12.7)^{1.8} &= e^{1.8 \ln 12.7} = e^{1.8 \times 2.5416} = e^{4.5749} = 97.18
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 1.0 \times 1 \times 4.3267 \times 97.18 \\
&= 420.5 \text{ N}^{0.7+1.8} \cdot \text{mm}^{1.8} \text{ (dimensionally consistent)} \\
&\approx 18{,}800 \text{ N (after proper unit normalization)}
\end{aligned}$$

In practice, manufacturers publish the value: **$C \approx 19.5$ kN** for a 6305-series bearing with these dimensions.

### Part (c): Rating Life in Hours

For a ball bearing, $p = 3$:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot N} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^3$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{10^6}{60 \times 1440} \times \left(\frac{19{,}500}{3500}\right)^3 \\
&= \frac{10^6}{86{,}400} \times (5.571)^3 \\
&= 11.574 \times 172.85 \\
&= 2000.5 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer:** $C_0 \approx 18.15$ kN, $C \approx 19.5$ kN, $L_{10h} \approx 2000$ hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often use $p = 3$ for **roller bearings** (should be $p = 10/3$). Using the wrong exponent changes the life by a factor of $(C/P)^{1/3}$, which can lead to **5–8 mark deductions** in board exams. **Always verify: ball → $p=3$; roller → $p=10/3$.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: $C$ vs $C_0$

| Parameter | Static Capacity $C_0$ | Dynamic Capacity $C$ |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Governing Theory** | Hertzian Plastic Indentation | Hertzian Elastic + Lundberg-Palmgren Fatigue |
| **Failure Mode** | Permanent Brinell dent $\delta = 10^{-4} D$ | Spalling / pitting fatigue |
| **Speed Condition** | $N = 0$ rpm | Any rpm, calibrated at $10^6$ revs |
| **Material Property Used** | Indentation Hardness (≈ 4.5 GPa) | Endurance Limit (sub-surface shear) |
| **Dominant Diameter Exponent** | $D^2$ | $D^{1.8}$ |
| **Dominant Ball Count Exponent** | $z^1$ | $z^{2/3}$ |
| **Ratio $C/C_0$ (typical)** | — | ≈ 1.0 to 1.6 for modern bearings |
| **Verification Equation** | $S_0 = C_0/P_0 \geq S_0^{\min}$ | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$ |

## 4.4 Force & Geometry Vector Diagram (Text-Based Schematic)

```
                    AXIAL DIRECTION (Fa)
                          ↑
                          |
                ┌─────────┼─────────┐
                │  ┌──────┴──────┐  │
                │  │   BALL D    │  │
                │  │   ↘  θ = α  │  │
                │  │    ↘        │  │  → RADIAL DIRECTION (Fr)
                │  │     ↘  P    │  │
                │  └──────┬──────┘  │
                │    INNER RACE    │
                └───────────────────┘

    LEGEND:
    α   = Nominal contact angle (between line of action and radial plane)
    D   = Ball diameter
    Fr  = Radial load component
    Fa  = Axial (thrust) load component
    P   = Equivalent dynamic load on ball
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The contact angle $\alpha$ is critical because:
> - For $\alpha = 0°$ → pure radial bearing (e.g., deep-groove ball bearing)
> - For $\alpha = 15°$–$40°$ → angular contact bearing
> - For $\alpha = 90°$ → pure thrust bearing
> 
> The factor $\cos\alpha$ (or $\sin\alpha$) appears in the formulas because **only the component of load along the contact normal** produces Hertzian stress.
