topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Equivalent Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (ISO / IS 6453 Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Equivalent Load (P)** is defined as that **constant, steady, stationary load** which, when applied to a rolling contact bearing, would produce the **same fatigue life** as the actual variable and/or combined (radial + thrust) operating load conditions to which the bearing is subjected in service.

Mathematically, it is the **hypothetical radial load** (for radial bearings) or **hypothetical axial load** (for thrust bearings) used in the rating life equation.

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

where every term carries a precise, standardized meaning defined by **ISO 281** and **IS 6453**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Average Water Bucket"

Imagine a village well where water is drawn at irregular intervals throughout the day — sometimes 5 buckets per hour, sometimes 20. The well's structure (rope, pulley, axle) experiences these **fluctuating loads**. An engineer designing the pulley axle cannot test it under 1,000 different "real" conditions. So, the engineer asks:

> *"What single, constant pulling force on the rope would cause the same wear and fatigue damage as all these fluctuating forces combined?"*

That single constant force is the **Dynamic Equivalent Load**.

- The **fluctuating force** = Actual operating load ($F_r$, $F_a$) in a bearing
- The **pulley axle wear** = Bearing fatigue life ($L_{10}$)
- The **single constant force** = Dynamic equivalent load ($P$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Dynamic"?** — The term *dynamic* is used because the bearing operates under **rotating, cyclic, fatigue-loaded** conditions. A *static equivalent load* (used in the *static* load rating $C_0$) is a completely different concept for non-rotating or very slowly rotating applications.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Standard Description | Typical Magnitude (Automotive) |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $C$ | **Basic Dynamic Load Rating** | 5 kN to 150 kN |
| $C_0$ | **Basic Static Load Rating** | 3 kN to 100 kN |
| $e$ | **Rotation/Characteristic Factor** | 0.1 to 0.6 (dimensionless) |
| $X$ | **Radial Load Factor** | 0.4 to 1.0 (dimensionless) |
| $Y$ | **Axial/Thrust Load Factor** | 0.6 to 2.5 (dimensionless) |
| $p$ | **Life Exponent** | **3** for ball, **10/3** for roller |
| $L_{10}$ | **Rating Life** (millions of revs) | millions |
| $K_a$ | **Application Factor** | 1.0 to 3.0 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Concept

The fatigue life of a rolling element bearing is governed by subsurface shear stresses in the contact zone between the rolling element and the raceway. Since fatigue damage is **cumulative** and depends on the **cube (or 10/3 power)** of the load, we cannot simply *average* the operating loads arithmetically. We must compute a **load-probability-weighted equivalent**.

This is why the equivalent load is derived from the **Miner's cumulative damage rule** (linear damage hypothesis).

## 2.2 Why the Rating Life Exponent Differs (p = 3 vs. p = 10/3)

- **Ball bearings** — Hertzian line contact $\rightarrow$ shear stress $\propto P^{1/3}$ $\rightarrow$ cycles to failure $\propto P^{-3}$ $\rightarrow$ $p = 3$
- **Roller bearings** — Modified line contact with edge loading effects $\rightarrow$ $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU numerical problems, the examiner will explicitly state whether the bearing is **ball** or **roller**. Read the problem carefully — using the wrong exponent costs **all 14 marks**.

## 2.3 Two Distinct Cases the Examiner Tests

### **Case 1: Equivalent Load for Combined Radial + Axial Loading**

For a **single row deep groove ball bearing** subjected simultaneously to:
- A radial load $F_r$
- A thrust (axial) load $F_a$

The equivalent load depends on the **ratio** $F_a / C_0$ (axial load relative to static capacity), which determines the characteristic factor $e$.

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \quad \text{when} \quad \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$$

$$P = F_r \quad \text{when} \quad \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$$

### **Case 2: Equivalent Load for Variable/Stepwise Loading**

When the operating load changes in steps (very common in automotive — e.g., a gearbox shaft under acceleration, cruising, braking):

$$P = \left[ \dfrac{1}{N_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i^{\,p} \cdot n_i \right]^{1/p}$$

This is the **nth-root mean cube (or 10/3 power) load** — a generalized RMS-type formula.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — these are the ONLY 6 formulas that appear in 95% of KTU questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{p}$ | Rating life in millions of revolutions | millions of revs |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot N} \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{p}$ | Rating life in operating hours | hours |
| 3 | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Combined load equivalent (for $F_a/F_r \leq e$) | N or kN |
| 4 | $P = F_r$ | Pure radial loading (or when $F_a/F_r > e$) | N or kN |
| 5 | $P = \left[ \dfrac{1}{L_{total}} \sum P_i^{p} \cdot L_i \right]^{1/p}$ | Variable load equivalent (continuous operation) | N or kN |
| 6 | $P = \left[ \dfrac{\sum P_i^{p} \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i} \right]^{1/p}$ | Variable load equivalent (time-based) | N or kN |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Automotive Application | Why Dynamic Equivalent Load is Computed |
|:---|:---|
| **Wheel hub bearings** | Combined radial (vehicle weight) + axial (cornering forces) |
| **Transmission gear shafts** | Highly fluctuating torque across gear ratios |
| **Alternator bearings** | Belt tension (radial) + axial preload from spring |
| **Crankshaft main bearings** | Combustion pulse + inertial + radial loads |
| **Steering column bearings** | Variable steering torque with road feedback |

The dynamic equivalent load is the **gateway parameter** — once computed correctly, the entire bearing life calculation, selection from catalogue, and reliability estimation flows from it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equivalent Load for Variable Loading (Miner's Hypothesis)

The fatigue damage per cycle at a load $P$ is proportional to $1/L_i$ where $L_i$ is the life at that load.

**Step 1:** Life at constant load $P_i$ (from rating life equation):
$$L_i = \left( \frac{C}{P_i} \right)^{p}$$

**Step 2:** Damage fraction per cycle at load $P_i$ (Miner's rule):
$$D_i = \frac{1}{L_i} = \left( \frac{P_i}{C} \right)^{p}$$

**Step 3:** Total damage over the loading spectrum ($n_i$ cycles at $P_i$):
$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot \left( \frac{P_i}{C} \right)^{p}$$

**Step 4:** For the equivalent constant load $P$ to give the same total damage over $N_{total}$ cycles:
$$N_{total} \cdot \left( \frac{P}{C} \right)^{p} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot \left( \frac{P_i}{C} \right)^{p}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $C^p$ from both sides:
$$N_{total} \cdot P^{\,p} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot P_i^{\,p}$$

**Step 6:** Isolate $P$:
$$P^{\,p} = \dfrac{1}{N_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot P_i^{\,p}$$

**Step 7:** Take the $p^{th}$ root:
$$\boxed{\,P = \left[ \dfrac{1}{N_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot P_i^{\,p} \right]^{1/p}\,}$$

This is the **Cumulative Damage Equivalent Load** — derived rigorously from first principles using Miner's hypothesis. The KTU examiner expects you to **write at least the first 4 steps** in a 14-mark question.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Selecting the Right Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important decision tree** for solving KTU 2024 problems. Memorize the branches.

| Given Condition | Action / Formula to Use |
|:---|:---|
| Only **radial** load on radial bearing | $P = F_r$ |
| Only **axial** load on thrust bearing | $P = F_a$ |
| **Combined** $F_r$ + $F_a$ on single-row ball bearing | Compute $F_a / C_0$ $\rightarrow$ find $e$ $\rightarrow$ use Formula 3 or 4 |
| **Single, constant** load & speed | Use $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ |
| **Stepwise varying** load (automotive cycle) | Use Formula 5 or 6 (Variable load formula) |
| **Rotating** inner race | Standard Formula 3 with $X$ and $Y$ |
| **Rotating** outer race | $P = F_r$ (axial contribution drops out) |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Step-by-Step (For Student Self-Practice)

> **Problem:** A 6208 deep groove ball bearing operates with the following stepped loading cycle:
> - 30% of time: $F_1 = 2$ kN at 1500 rpm
> - 50% of time: $F_2 = 4$ kN at 1500 rpm
> - 20% of time: $F_3 = 1$ kN at 1500 rpm
>
> The basic dynamic load rating $C = 25$ kN. Calculate the rating life in hours. (Ball bearing, $p = 3$.)

**Solution — Step 1: Compute total cycles over a chosen reference period.**

Let the cycle period $T = 1$ hour. Then $N = 1500 \times 60 = 90{,}000$ rev/hour.

$$N_{total} = 90{,}000 \text{ rev/hour}$$

**Step 2: Compute cycles at each load level.**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= 0.30 \times 90{,}000 = 27{,}000 \text{ rev} \\
n_2 &= 0.50 \times 90{,}000 = 45{,}000 \text{ rev} \\
n_3 &= 0.20 \times 90{,}000 = 18{,}000 \text{ rev}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the equivalent dynamic load using $p = 3$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P^{3} &= \frac{1}{N_{total}} \left[ n_1 P_1^{3} + n_2 P_2^{3} + n_3 P_3^{3} \right] \\
P^{3} &= \frac{1}{90{,}000} \left[ 27{,}000 \times 2^{3} + 45{,}000 \times 4^{3} + 18{,}000 \times 1^{3} \right] \\
P^{3} &= \frac{1}{90{,}000} \left[ 27{,}000 \times 8 + 45{,}000 \times 64 + 18{,}000 \times 1 \right] \\
P^{3} &= \frac{1}{90{,}000} \left[ 216{,}000 + 2{,}880{,}000 + 18{,}000 \right] \\
P^{3} &= \frac{3{,}114{,}000}{90{,}000} = 34.6 \text{ (kN)}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Take the cube root.**

$$P = (34.6)^{1/3} = 3.26 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Compute the rating life in hours.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{10^{6}}{60 \cdot N} \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^{p} \\
L_{10h} &= \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1500} \left( \frac{25}{3.26} \right)^{3} \\
L_{10h} &= \frac{10^{6}}{90{,}000} \times (7.67)^{3} \\
L_{10h} &= 11.11 \times 451.3 \\
L_{10h} &\approx 5{,}014 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice that the *arithmetic mean* of the loads would be $(0.3 \times 2 + 0.5 \times 4 + 0.2 \times 1) = 2.6$ kN, which would have **overestimated** the life. The cube-mean (RMS-type) gives the **correct, conservative** result. This is precisely *why* we use the equivalent load formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dynamic Equivalent Load Computation

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision logic** an engineer follows in an automotive bearing selection workflow — from the input operating conditions to the final equivalent load value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Operating Conditions] --> B{Is the Load Constant or Variable?}
    B -->|Constant| C[Single Load F applied]
    B -->|Variable Stepwise| D[Load Spectrum: Pi, ni for i=1 to k]
    C --> E{Is the Load Purely Radial or Combined?}
    D --> E
    E -->|Purely Radial| F[P equals Fr]
    E -->|Combined Fr plus Fa| G[Compute ratio Fa over Fr]
    G --> H{Fa over Fr is less than or equal to e?}
    H -->|Yes| I[P equals X*Fr + Y*Fa]
    H -->|No| J[P equals Fr]
    F --> K[Compute Rating Life: L10 equals C over P to the power p]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    D --> L[Apply Miner Rule: P equals cube root of mean cube]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Output: Equivalent Dynamic Load P and Life L10h]
    M --> N[Select Bearing from Manufacturer Catalogue]
    N --> O[End: Validate against Required L10h]
```

## 4.2 Functional Interaction Schematic — Components of P

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Inputs from Vehicle Dynamics]
        Fr[Radial Load Fr from Vehicle Weight and Cornering]
        Fa[Axial Load Fa from Clutch or Preload]
    end
    subgraph STANDARDS[Manufacturer Standard Tables]
        Xval[Radial Factor X from Catalogue]
        Yval[Axial Factor Y from Catalogue]
        C0val[Static Load Rating C0 from Catalogue]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Equivalent Load Engine]
        Ratio[Compute Fa over Fr ratio]
        eLookup[Determine e value from Fa over C0]
        Formula[P equals X*Fr + Y*Fa]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output]
        Pval[Dynamic Equivalent Load P in kN]
        Life[Rating Life L10 in hours]
    end
    Fr --> Ratio
    Fa --> Ratio
    C0val --> eLookup
    Ratio --> eLookup
    eLookup --> Formula
    Xval --> Formula
    Yval --> Formula
    Fr --> Formula
    Fa --> Formula
    Formula --> Pval
    Pval --> Life
```

## 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Note

> [!NOTE]
> The two diagrams above map the **logical flow of information** in a real bearing design cycle. The first diagram is the **high-level decision tree**, while the second diagram shows the **dependency graph** between inputs (loads, catalogue constants) and the output (equivalent load). In an industrial setting, this logic is encoded into **CAE tools** and **manufacturer selection software** (e.g., SKF SimPro, NSK ABLE).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Define dynamic equivalent load for a rolling contact bearing. Why is it called "dynamic" and not "static"?**

**Model Answer:**

The dynamic equivalent load $P$ is defined as that constant, stationary load (radial for radial bearings, axial for thrust bearings) which, if applied to the bearing, would give the same fatigue life as the actual variable and/or combined (radial + thrust) loading conditions experienced in service.

It is called **"dynamic"** because:
1. The bearing operates under **rotating, cyclic loading** that produces subsurface fatigue.
2. The concept is intrinsically tied to the **fatigue life equation** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$, which is a *dynamic* (rotational) life model.
3. The *static* equivalent load (for the static rating $C_0$) is used only for **non-rotating or very slowly rotating** applications, where plastic deformation (not fatigue) governs failure.

**Key Term:** The load is computed using **Miner's cumulative damage hypothesis** for variable loads, with $p = 3$ for ball bearings and $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Write the formula for the dynamic equivalent load when a bearing is subjected to (i) combined radial and axial loads, and (ii) stepwise variable loading. Explain the significance of the exponent $p$.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Combined Load Formula:**
$$P = X F_r + Y F_a \quad \text{when} \quad \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} \leq e$$
$$P = F_r \quad \text{when} \quad \dfrac{F_a}{F_r} > e$$

where $X$ = radial factor, $Y$ = axial factor, $e$ = characteristic factor (obtained from $F_a/C_0$).

**(ii) Variable Load Formula:**
$$P = \left[ \dfrac{1}{N_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i P_i^{\,p} \right]^{1/p}$$

**Significance of $p$:**
- $p = 3$ for **ball bearings** (point contact, Hertzian theory)
- $p = 10/3$ for **roller bearings** (line contact, modified Hertzian theory)
- It is the **fatigue life exponent** in the rating life equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$
- It reflects the **non-linear relationship** between load and fatigue damage — life drops as the *cube* of the load ratio for ball bearings.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **INSTRUCTIONS TO STUDENTS (KTU 2024 Pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Show all derivation steps and units.*

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
A deep groove ball bearing (Series 6206) is subjected to a radial load $F_r = 3.5$ kN and an axial load $F_a = 1.2$ kN. The static load rating is $C_0 = 16.6$ kN. The bearing rotates with the inner race. From the manufacturer catalogue:
- $e = 0.27$ when $F_a/C_0 = 0.07$ $\rightarrow$ $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.85$

Compute the **dynamic equivalent load $P$** and the **rating life in hours** if the speed is 1440 rpm and $C = 25.5$ kN.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
The same bearing is now used in a vehicle wheel hub where the load varies cyclically:
- 40% of time: 4 kN
- 35% of time: 5 kN
- 25% of time: 2 kN

The speed is constant at 1440 rpm. Compute the **equivalent dynamic load** and the **new rating life in hours**.

---

#### **Model Solution to B1(a):**

**Step 1: Compute the ratio $F_a/F_r$.**
$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{1.2}{3.5} = 0.343$$

**Step 2: Compare with $e = 0.27$.**

Since $F_a/F_r = 0.343 > e = 0.27$, we use the second branch:
$$\boxed{P = F_r = 3.5 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3: Verify the given $F_a/C_0$ matches the catalogue row.**
$$\dfrac{F_a}{C_0} = \dfrac{1.2}{16.6} = 0.072 \approx 0.07 \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Compute the rating life in millions of revolutions ($p = 3$ for ball).**
$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{3} = \left( \dfrac{25.5}{3.5} \right)^{3} = (7.286)^{3} = 386.7 \text{ million revs}$$

**Step 5: Convert to operating hours.**
$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \times N} \cdot L_{10} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1440} \times 386.7$$
$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{386.7 \times 10^{6}}{86{,}400} \approx \boxed{4{,}475 \text{ hours}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to B1(b):**

**Step 1: Since the load is variable, use the stepwise equivalent load formula with $p = 3$.**

Treating each as a *radial-equivalent* load first:
$$\begin{aligned}
P^{3} &= \dfrac{1}{N_{total}} \left[ n_1 P_1^{3} + n_2 P_2^{3} + n_3 P_3^{3} \right] \\
P^{3} &= 0.40 \times 4^{3} + 0.35 \times 5^{3} + 0.25 \times 2^{3} \\
P^{3} &= 0.40 \times 64 + 0.35 \times 125 + 0.25 \times 8 \\
P^{3} &= 25.6 + 43.75 + 2.0 = 71.35 \text{ (kN)}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Take the cube root.**
$$P = (71.35)^{1/3} \approx \boxed{4.144 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the new rating life.**
$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{25.5}{4.144} \right)^{3} = (6.153)^{3} = 232.97 \text{ million revs}$$

**Step 4: Convert to hours.**
$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{232.97 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1440} = \dfrac{232.97 \times 10^{6}}{86{,}400} \approx \boxed{2{,}696 \text{ hours}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (B1):** The most common error here is using $P = (0.4 \times 4 + 0.35 \times 5 + 0.25 \times 2) = 3.95$ kN as a **linear mean**. This is WRONG. Fatigue damage is **cubic**, not linear. Using the linear mean would give $L_{10h} \approx 3{,}970$ hours — an **overestimate of ~47%**, leading to a *dangerous* under-designed bearing. Always cube the loads first, take the weighted mean, and then the cube root. Marks lost here: typically **4 to 5 marks out of 7** for the life part.

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
A rolling contact bearing has a basic dynamic load rating $C = 50$ kN. It operates at 1000 rpm under a constant radial load of 5 kN. Calculate the rating life in:
(i) Millions of revolutions
(ii) Hours of operation
*(Assume ball bearing, $p = 3$.)*

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
Derive the expression for the **dynamic equivalent load under variable loading conditions** using **Miner's cumulative damage hypothesis**. Clearly state all assumptions and define each variable used.

---

#### **Model Solution to B2(a):**

**Step 1: Identify the equivalent load.**
Since the load is purely radial and constant:
$$P = F_r = 5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Apply the rating life equation.**
$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{p} = \left( \dfrac{50}{5} \right)^{3} = (10)^{3} = 1000 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 3: Convert to hours.**
$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^{6}}{60 \times N} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1000} = \boxed{16{,}667 \text{ hours}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to B2(b):**

This is a **derivation question** — KTU examiners award full 7 marks only if the **assumptions** and **complete step-by-step logic** are written. Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full 7-step derivation.

**Critical Assumptions to State:**
1. The bearing operates at constant speed.
2. The load steps are sequential and non-overlapping.
3. **Miner's linear cumulative damage hypothesis** holds (damage is additive and independent of load sequence).
4. The exponent $p$ is constant (3 for ball, 10/3 for roller).
5. Failure occurs when cumulative damage reaches unity ($D = 1$).

**Final Result to Box:**
$$P = \left[ \dfrac{1}{N_{total}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \cdot P_i^{\,p} \right]^{1/p}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (B2):** In derivation questions, students frequently **write the formula directly without showing the Miner's rule origin**. The examiner expects at least 5 to 6 algebraic steps, starting from the damage per cycle $D_i = 1/L_i$. Skipping the derivation loses **at least 4 marks out of 7**. Also, do **not confuse** $N_{total}$ with $N$ (speed in rpm) — these are different quantities. $N_{total}$ is the total number of revolutions in the loading cycle; $N$ is the rotational speed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Dynamic equivalent load ($P$)** is a *constant* load that gives the *same fatigue life* as the actual variable/combined loading.
- [x] **Two cases**: (i) Combined radial + axial $\rightarrow$ $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (or $P = F_r$ if $F_a/F_r > e$); (ii) Variable load $\rightarrow$ $P = \left[\frac{1}{N_{total}}\sum n_i P_i^p\right]^{1/p}$.
- [x] **Exponent $p$**: $p = 3$ for **ball bearings**, $p = 10/3$ for **roller bearings**. *Always read the problem carefully.*
- [x] **Catalogue lookup order**: First find $F_a/C_0$, then look up $e$, then $X$ and $Y$ from the corresponding row.
- [x] **Miner's hypothesis** is the **theoretical foundation** of the variable-load formula — you must be able to derive it.
- [x] **Arithmetic mean is WRONG** — always cube (or raise to $p$) the loads, take the mean, then take the $p^{th}$ root.
- [x] **Units consistency**: $C$, $P$, $F_r$, $F_a$ must all be in the same units (N or kN). Life comes out in millions of revs; convert to hours using $L_{10h} = L_{10} \times 10^{6} / (60N)$.
- [x] **Rotating race matters**: For ball bearings with **rotating inner race**, the standard $X$/$Y$ formula applies. For **rotating outer race**, the axial contribution drops and $P \approx F_r$.
- [x] **Static equivalent load $P_0$** is a *different* concept (used with $C_0$, not $C$) and is for very slow rotation — do not confuse with dynamic $P$.
- [x] **For KTU exams**: When a problem says *"the bearing is subjected to a constant radial load of X kN"*, the answer is $P = X$ kN. Do not over-engineer.
- [x] **Show all 5 to 7 derivation steps** in 14-mark derivation questions. Direct formula = **-4 marks minimum**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sliding Contact Bearing - Lubrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sliding Contact Bearing - Lubrication # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Sliding Contact Bearings — The Foundation A **Sliding Contact Bearing** (also called a **Plain Bearing** or **Journal Bearing**) is a machine element that supports a rotating shaft (called the **journal**) through a thin film of lubricant, allowing relative sliding motion between two surfaces with minimum friction and wear. In automotive applications, they are used to support the crankshaft, camshaft, connecting rods, and gear shafts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMUT601 Module 2):** > A sliding contact bearing is a bearing in which two mating surfaces — the **journal** (rotating member) and the **bearing** (stationary member) — slide relative to each other, separated by a **lubricant film** that carries the load and minimizes direct metal-to-metal contact. ### 1.2 What is Lubrication? **Lubrication** is the process of introducing a fluid (lubricant) between two sliding surfaces to: - Reduce friction and wear - Carry away generated heat - Prevent corrosion - Dampen vibrations and noise > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Think of a *water slide at a swimming pool*. When dry, your skin scrapes painfully against the plastic surface (high friction, wear). When water flows between you and the surface, you glide effortlessly. The water acts as the *lubricant*, separating the two surfaces. In a journal bearing, the *lubricant film* plays exactly the same role between the shaft and the bearing surface. ## 1.3 Modes of Lubrication in Sliding Bearings The behavior of the lubricant film determines the type of lubrication regime. KTU emphasizes **four primary modes**: | Mode | Description | Load Capacity | Friction | |------|-------------|---------------|----------| | **Hydrodynamic (Thick Film)** | Surfaces completely separated by a fluid film | High | Low | | **Mixed (Partial Film)** | Combination of fluid film + asperity contact | Moderate | Moderate | | **Boundary (Thin Film)** | Surfaces separated by molecular lubricant film only | Low | High | | **Hydrostatic (Externally Pressurized)** | Fluid supplied under external pressure | Very High | Very Low | > [!IMPORTANT] > In **automotive engines**, hydrodynamic lubrication is the **designed operating regime**, while mixed and boundary lubrication occur during **start-up and shut-down** when speeds are low and the fluid film cannot fully develop. ## 1.4 The Lubrication Triangle (Key Concept) The formation and sustenance of a lubricant wedge depends on three critical parameters: 1. **Relative Velocity (N)** — the speed at which the journal drags oil into the converging clearance. 2. **Viscosity (μ or Z)** — the resistance of the lubricant to flow. 3. **Load (W or P)** — the external force trying to squeeze the oil out. ``` VELOCITY (N) △ /│\ / │ \ / │ \ / │ \ / │ \ LOAD (W)───────VISCOSITY (μ) Lubrication Triangle ``` If **N·μ** is too low relative to **W**, the film collapses → boundary contact → wear. If **N·μ** is high, a full hydrodynamic wedge is maintained → safe operation. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Petroff's Equation (The Simplest Hydrodynamic Model) Petroff assumed a **lightly loaded, concentric journal** with the shaft floating centrally in a sleeve, with the lubricant behaving as a **Newtonian fluid** in pure shear (no pressure built up, no taper wedge). ### Key Assumptions of Petroff's Model - Journal is concentric (no eccentricity) → no load-carrying wedge - Bearing is infinitely long - Flow is laminar and viscous - Velocity of the lubricant is linear across the film (Couette flow) - No side leakage - Bearing is **lightly loaded** ### Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Intuition) 1. Imagine a smooth shaft rotating inside a sleeve with a tiny radial clearance `c`. 2. The oil sticks to both surfaces (no-slip condition). 3. The shaft's surface drags the oil with velocity `U = πDN`, and the sleeve holds oil stationary. 4. Across the thin film of thickness `h`, velocity varies linearly (Couette shear). 5. Shear stress τ = μ × (du/dy) = μ × (U/h). 6. Total friction force = τ × (surface area) and friction torque = Friction force × Radius. ### Petroff's Friction Torque Equation $$T_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \, \mu \, N \, r^3 \, L}{c}$$ Where: - $T_f$ = Friction torque (N·mm) - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of lubricant (N·s/mm² or Pa·s) - $N$ = Journal speed (rev/s) - $r$ = Journal radius (mm) - $L$ = Bearing length (mm) - $c$ = Radial clearance (mm) ### Petroff's Coefficient of Friction $$\mu_f = \frac{2 \pi^2 \, \mu \, N \, r}{P \, c}$$ Where $P = W/(L \times 2r)$ = bearing pressure (N/mm²). > [!NOTE] > **Why Petroff Matters in KTU:** Even though it doesn't predict load capacity (since the journal is concentric), it provides the **upper bound on the friction coefficient** and is the **starting point** for the more accurate Reynolds/Hydrodynamic analysis. It is often asked in **2-mark and 3-mark questions**. ## 2.2 Reynolds Equation (Cornerstone of Hydrodynamic Lubrication) Osborne Reynolds (1886) formulated the **Reynolds Equation**, the governing partial differential equation of thin-film lubrication. It describes the **pressure distribution** in the oil film. ### Simplified Form (for steady-state, incompressible, isoviscous flow): $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 \mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$ ### Key Insight > [!IMPORTANT] > The pressure gradient $\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$ is non-zero **only when** the film thickness $h$ varies with $x$ (i.e., a converging wedge). A **converging wedge is essential** to generate hydrodynamic pressure. This is why **eccentricity** (off-center rotation) is what creates load-carrying capacity in a real journal bearing. ## 2.3 Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing — The Practical Model In a real bearing, the journal shifts from the center by an **eccentricity `e`**, creating a **wedge-shaped film** that is thicker on one side and thinner on the other. ### Key Geometric Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | |-----------|--------|------------| | Radial Clearance | $c$ | $c = R - r$ (difference between bearing and journal radii) | | Eccentricity | $e$ | Distance between bearing center and journal center | | Eccentricity Ratio | $\epsilon$ | $\epsilon = e/c$ (dimensionless, ranges 0 to 1) | | Minimum Film Thickness | $h_0$ | $h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon)$ | | Diameter Clearance | $c_d$ | $c_d = 2c = D_B - d$ | | Clearance Ratio | $\psi$ | $\psi = c/r$ (typically 0.001 to 0.002) | ### Film Thickness at any Angle θ (measured from line of centers) $$h = c(1 + \epsilon \cos\theta)$$ > [!NOTE] > At $\theta = 180°$ (point of maximum film thickness): $h_{max} = c(1 + \epsilon)$ > At $\theta = 0°$ (point of minimum film thickness): $h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon)$ ## 2.4 Sommerfeld Number (The Most Important Dimensionless Group in KTU) The **Sommerfeld Number (So or S)** is a dimensionless quantity that characterizes the operating regime of a hydrodynamic bearing. It balances **load, speed, viscosity, and geometry**. ### Sommerfeld Number Formula (Two equivalent forms) $$S_o = \frac{\mu \, N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu \, N}{P \, \psi^2}$$ Where: - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or reyns) - $N$ = Journal speed (rev/s) - $P$ = Bearing pressure = $W/(L \times D)$ (Pa or N/mm²) - $\psi = c/r$ = clearance ratio > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** Always keep units **consistent**. If $\mu$ is in Pa·s and $P$ in Pa, then $N$ must be in rev/s. If $P$ is in N/mm², then $\mu$ must be in N·s/mm² and $N$ in rev/s. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical KTU Use | |---|---------|---------|-----------------| | 1 | $T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$ | Petroff's friction torque | 3-mark direct | | 2 | $\mu_f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{Pc}$ | Petroff's friction coefficient | 3-mark | | 3 | $h = c(1 + \epsilon \cos\theta)$ | Film thickness | Sub-part (a) | | 4 | $h_0 = c(1-\epsilon)$, $h_{max} = c(1+\epsilon)$ | Min/Max film thickness | 7-mark | | 5 | $S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | Sommerfeld number | Every problem | | 6 | $\frac{W}{L} = \frac{6 \pi \mu N r^2 \epsilon}{c^2(2+\epsilon^2)\sqrt{1-\epsilon^2}}$ | Raimondi-Boyd load eqn (short bearing) | 14-mark | | 7 | $Q = r c N \omega L$ | Petroff's oil flow (periphery) | Heat dissipation | | 8 | $H_{gen} = \mu_f W U$ | Heat generated | Heat balance | | 9 | $H_{diss} = h_{CR} A (T_b - T_a)$ | Heat dissipated | Heat balance | | 10 | $\mu_{abs} = \mu_f \cdot W$ | Total friction force | Torque | ### Units Conversion Table (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems) | Quantity | SI Unit | CGS / Practical | |----------|---------|------------------| | Dynamic Viscosity (μ) | Pa·s = N·s/m² | 1 Pa·s = 10 poise = 1000 cP | | Kinematic Viscosity (ν) | m²/s | 1 m²/s = 10⁴ stokes = 10⁶ cSt | | Pressure (P) | Pa = N/m² | 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa = 1 MPa | | Force | N | 1 kgf = 9.81 N | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality Check:** > In passenger car engines, $S_o$ typically ranges from **0.5 to 5.0**, $\epsilon$ from **0.6 to 0.95**, and $\psi$ is held between **0.001 and 0.002** to ensure sufficient film thickness without excessive oil leakage. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Petroff's Equation ### Setup Consider a concentric journal (shaft) of radius $r$ rotating at speed $N$ rev/s inside a stationary bearing of radius $R = r + c$, supported by a lubricant of dynamic viscosity $\mu$. The film thickness is uniform = $c$. ### Step 1 — Velocity Distribution By the **Newtonian viscous flow** assumption and the **no-slip condition**: - At the journal surface (y = 0): velocity = $U = 2\pi r N$ (peripheral speed of shaft) - At the bearing surface (y = c): velocity = 0 (stationary) Assuming laminar Couette flow, velocity varies linearly: $$u(y) = U \left(1 - \frac{y}{c}\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Shear Stress From Newton's law of viscosity: $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \left(-\frac{U}{c}\right) = -\frac{\mu U}{c}$$ The **magnitude** of shear stress is: $$\vert \tau \vert = \frac{\mu U}{c} = \frac{2\pi r N \mu}{c}$$ ### Step 3 — Friction Force The friction force on the journal = shear stress × wetted area ($2\pi r L$): $$F_f = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{2\pi r N \mu}{c} \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{4\pi^2 N \mu r^2 L}{c}$$ ### Step 4 — Friction Torque The friction force acts at radius $r$, so: $$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 N \mu r^3 L}{c}$$ $$\boxed{T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}}$$ ### Step 5 — Coefficient of Friction The applied load is $W = P \cdot 2r L$, where $P$ = bearing pressure. The coefficient of friction is: $$\mu_f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^2 N \mu r^2 L / c}{P \cdot 2rL} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P c}$$ $$\boxed{\mu_f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{Pc}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Sommerfeld Number (from Petroff + Dimensional Insight) Petroff's load equation (modified) for an eccentric journal can be written (after integration of Reynolds equation for an **infinitely long bearing**, Ocvirk approximation) as: $$\frac{W}{L} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 \epsilon}{c^2 (2 + \epsilon^2)} \cdot \text{(correction factor)}$$ Rearranging for the **dimensionless group**: $$\frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{(2+\epsilon^2) \sqrt{1-\epsilon^2}}{6\pi \epsilon} \cdot \text{(function of }\epsilon\text{)}$$ The left side is **defined** as the **Sommerfeld Number** $S_o$, and the right side is a known function of eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$ (tabulated as **Raimondi-Boyd charts**). $$\boxed{S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu N}{P \, \psi^2}}$$ ## 3.3 Heat Balance in a Journal Bearing (Thermal Equilibrium) In steady-state operation, the heat generated by viscous shear must equal the heat dissipated by the lubricant (convective cooling through the bearing housing). ### Step 1 — Heat Generated Power lost to friction = friction force × sliding velocity: $$H_{gen} = \mu_f \cdot W \cdot U = \mu_f \cdot W \cdot (\pi D N)$$ In units of Watts. ### Step 2 — Heat Dissipated Heat carried away by the oil flow and by convection from the housing: $$H_{diss} = \rho Q c_p (T_o - T_i) + h_{CR} A_s (T_b - T_a)$$ Where: - $\rho$ = oil density (kg/m³) - $Q$ = oil flow rate (m³/s) - $c_p$ = specific heat of oil (J/kg·K ≈ 1750 for mineral oil) - $T_o, T_i$ = outlet and inlet oil temperatures - $h_{CR}$ = heat transfer coefficient from housing (W/m²·K) - $A_s$ = housing surface area (m²) - $T_b, T_a$ = bearing surface and ambient temperatures ### Step 3 — Heat Balance Equation $$\boxed{H_{gen} = H_{diss}}$$ $$\mu_f \, W \, \pi D N = \rho Q c_p (T_o - T_i) + h_{CR} A_s (T_b - T_a)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Strategy:** For numericals, KTU often simplifies the heat balance to **just the housing convection term**, ignoring oil temperature rise, i.e., $H_{gen} = h_{CR} A_s \Delta T$. The oil temperature rise is found from the **lumped heat capacity equation** separately. ## 3.4 Raimondi-Boyd Analytical Solution (for Infinite-Length Bearing) The exact solution of Reynolds equation for a journal bearing gives a **closed-form relation** between load, eccentricity, and Sommerfeld number. For an **infinitely long bearing** (no side leakage): $$\frac{W}{L} = \frac{6 \pi \mu N r^2 \epsilon}{c^2 (2 + \epsilon^2) \sqrt{1 - \epsilon^2}}$$ This can be rewritten as: $$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{(2 + \epsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \epsilon^2}}{6 \pi \epsilon} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi}$$ For the more accurate **finite-length (short) bearing** (Gumbel/Sommerfeld): $$\frac{W}{L} = \frac{\mu N L^2 \epsilon \pi}{2 c^2 \sqrt{1 - \epsilon^2}} \left[\frac{\pi^2}{(L/d)^2} - 1\right]^{-1} \cdot \text{(corrections)}$$ > [!NOTE] > In KTU exams, you will be **given** the Raimondi-Boyd chart values or the relation in tabular form. You need to identify $\epsilon$ from $S_o$, then compute $h_0$, friction variable $(r/c)f$, and flow variable $Q/(rcN L)$. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Standard KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A 100 mm diameter journal runs at 1500 rpm in a 100.2 mm diameter bearing. The load is 50 kN and the bearing length is 100 mm. Viscosity of oil = 20 cP. Calculate: (a) Sommerfeld number (b) Coefficient of friction (c) Power loss (d) Minimum film thickness (assume $\epsilon = 0.8$) ### Given - $d = 100$ mm → $r = 50$ mm - $D = 100.2$ mm → $R = 50.1$ mm - $c = R - r = 0.1$ mm - $N = 1500$ rpm = 25 rev/s - $W = 50,000$ N - $L = 100$ mm - $\mu = 20$ cP = $20 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s = $20 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m² ### (a) Sommerfeld Number First, compute bearing pressure: $$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{50{,}000}{100 \times 100} = 5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ Clearance ratio: $$\psi = \frac{c}{r} = \frac{0.1}{50} = 0.002$$ Convert $\mu$ to consistent units: $\mu = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (already consistent with Pa). $$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P \psi^2} = \frac{(20 \times 10^{-3})(25)}{(5 \times 10^6)(0.002)^2}$$ $$S_o = \frac{0.5}{(5 \times 10^6)(4 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{0.5}{20} = 0.025$$ ### (b) Coefficient of Friction For $\epsilon = 0.8$, the Raimondi-Boyd chart gives $(r/c)f \approx 3.22$ (typical value). $$f = \frac{3.22 \times c}{r} = \frac{3.22 \times 0.1}{50} = 0.00644$$ ### (c) Power Loss Sliding velocity: $$U = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.1 \times 25 = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$ Wait — convert D to meters: $D = 0.1$ m, $N = 25$ rev/s. $$U = \pi (0.1)(25) = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$ Friction force: $$F_f = f \cdot W = 0.00644 \times 50{,}000 = 322 \text{ N}$$ Power loss: $$P_{loss} = F_f \cdot U = 322 \times 7.854 = 2529 \text{ W} \approx 2.53 \text{ kW}$$ ### (d) Minimum Film Thickness For $\epsilon = 0.8$: $$h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon) = 0.1 (1 - 0.8) = 0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.02 \text{ mm} = 20 \text{ μm}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Threshold Rule:** The minimum film thickness $h_0$ must exceed the **combined surface roughness** of the journal and bearing ($R_{a,journal} + R_{a,bearing}$). If $h_0 \le R_{a,sum}$, the bearing operates in the **mixed lubrication regime** and accelerated wear will occur. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Journal Bearing Geometric Layout ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["JOURNAL
Radius r, Speed N rev per s"] -->|Rotates about| nodeB["CENTER O_j"] nodeC["BEARING
Radius R, Stationary"] -->|Centered at| nodeD["CENTER O_b"] nodeE["LOAD W
Applied vertically"] -->|Pushes journal down| nodeA nodeF["LUBRICANT FILM
Variable thickness h theta"] -->|Separates| nodeA nodeF -.->|Thickness h = c x 1 + epsilon cos theta| nodeG["WEDGE SHAPE
Converges from h_max to h_0"] nodeG -->|Creates pressure| nodeH["HYDRODYNAMIC PRESSURE p theta"] nodeH -->|Balances| nodeE nodeI["CLEARANCE c = R minus r"] -->|Determines| nodeF nodeJ["ECCENTRICITY e
Distance between O_j and O_b"] -->|Drives wedge| nodeF nodeK["MIN FILM THICKNESS h_0
At theta = 0 degrees"] -->|Critical for wear| nodeL["MIXED OR FULL FILM?"] nodeG -->|Located at| nodeK ``` ## 4.2 Lubrication Regime Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["OPERATING BEARING
Shaft speed N, Load W"] -->|Compute| nodeB["SOMMERFELD NUMBER S_o"] nodeB -->|S_o is large| nodeC["FULL HYDRODYNAMIC
Thick film regime"] nodeB -->|S_o is moderate| nodeD["MIXED LUBRICATION
Partial film regime"] nodeB -->|S_o is small| nodeE["BOUNDARY LUBRICATION
Thin film regime"] nodeB -->|Externally pressurized| nodeF["HYDROSTATIC LUBRICATION
External pump supply"] nodeC -->|h_0 greater than R_a sum| nodeG["SAFE OPERATION"] nodeC -->|h_0 less than R_a sum| nodeD nodeD -->|h_0 less than R_a sum| nodeE nodeE -->|Direct metal contact| nodeH["ACCELERATED WEAR
Risk of seizure"] nodeF -->|Even at zero speed| nodeG ``` ## 4.3 Hydrodynamic Pressure Generation in the Wedge ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["INLET REGION
Thick film, low pressure"] -->|Oil dragged in| nodeB["CONVERGING WEDGE
Film thickness decreases"] nodeB -->|Viscous drag + squeeze| nodeC["PRESSURE PEAK
Maximum at h min"] nodeC -->|Pressure drops| nodeD["DIVIDING LINE
theta = 0 degrees
Min film thickness h_0"] nodeD -->|Film expands| nodeE["EXIT REGION
Thin expanding film, low pressure"] nodeE -->|Sub-atmospheric possible| nodeF["CAVITATION ZONE
Air or vapor released"] nodeF -->|Cycle repeats| nodeA nodeG["SUMMATION OF PRESSURE x AREA"] -->|Equals| nodeH["LOAD W carried by journal"] ``` ## 4.4 Heat Balance Schematic for a Journal Bearing ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["MECHANICAL INPUT
Torque and Speed"] -->|Drives| nodeB["JOURNAL ROTATION"] nodeB -->|Shears oil film| nodeC["VISCOUS FRICTION LOSS
Power = mu_f x W x U"] nodeC -->|Converts to| nodeD["HEAT GENERATION H_gen"] nodeD -->|Split into two paths| nodeE["PATH 1: HEAT IN OIL
Q rho cp delta T"] nodeD -->|Split into two paths| nodeF["PATH 2: HEAT IN HOUSING
h_CR A_s delta T"] nodeE -->|Carried by oil flow to| nodeG["OIL SUMP and COOLER"] nodeF -->|Convected to| nodeH["AMBIENT AIR"] nodeG -->|Returns cooled oil| nodeI["LUBRICANT INLET"] nodeI -->|Re-enters bearing| nodeB nodeH -->|Steady state| nodeJ["EQUILIBRIUM
H_gen = H_diss"] ``` ## 4.5 Lubricant Selection Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["DESIGN REQUIREMENTS
Load, Speed, Temperature"] -->|Step 1| nodeB["DETERMINE S_o TARGET
epsilon between 0.6 and 0.9"] nodeB -->|Step 2| nodeC["COMPUTE REQUIRED VISCOSITY mu
mu = S_o x P x psi squared / N"] nodeC -->|Step 3| nodeD["MATCH TO OIL GRADE
SAE 20, 30, 50, etc"] nodeD -->|Step 4| nodeE["CHECK VISCOSITY INDEX VI
For temperature stability"] nodeE -->|Step 5| nodeF["ADD ADDITIVES
Anti wear, Anti foam, Detergent"] nodeF -->|Step 6| nodeG["FINAL OIL SPECIFICATION
Viscosity grade + Additive package"] nodeG -->|Feeds into| nodeH["ENGINE LUBRICATION SYSTEM"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper] **Differentiate between hydrodynamic and hydrostatic lubrication. Give one example of each from an automotive application.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Hydrodynamic Lubrication | Hydrostatic Lubrication | |--------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | Pressure Source | Created by relative motion of surfaces | Created by external pump | | Speed Dependency | Needs sufficient speed to build film | Works even at zero speed | | Load Capacity | Moderate | Very High | | Example (Auto) | Crankshaft main bearing, camshaft bearing | Heavy diesel engine turbocharger bearing, large rolling mill bearings | | Friction at Rest | High (boundary) | Very Low | > [!NOTE] > **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for example = 3 marks. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the significance of the Sommerfeld number in journal bearing design? What happens if its value is very small?** **Model Answer:** The Sommerfeld number ($S_o$) is a **dimensionless parameter** that characterizes the **operating regime** of a hydrodynamic journal bearing. It expresses the ratio of viscous forces to load-carrying capacity and is used to determine the **eccentricity ratio ($\epsilon$)** and the **minimum film thickness ($h_0$)** from the **Raimondi-Boyd charts**. If $S_o$ is **very small** (e.g., < 0.1), it indicates: - Viscosity is low, or - Speed is low, or - Load is too high This leads to a **small eccentricity** in the charts → very high $\epsilon$ → **small $h_0$** → risk of **metal-to-metal contact**, **boundary lubrication**, and eventual **bearing seizure**. **Significance Summary:** - Determines $\epsilon$ and $h_0$ via Raimondi-Boyd charts. - Ensures $h_0 \ge R_{a,sum}$ for full-film operation. - Predicts friction and power loss. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for significance + 1 mark for "small So consequence" = 3 marks. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing Design (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PCMUT601 Module 2]** A journal bearing has the following specifications: - Journal diameter: $d = 100$ mm - Bearing diameter: $D = 100.2$ mm - Bearing length: $L = 100$ mm - Load: $W = 50$ kN - Speed: $N = 1500$ rpm - Viscosity of lubricant: $\mu = 20$ cP at operating temperature #### Part (a) — Compute the Sommerfeld number and determine the eccentricity ratio, coefficient of friction, and minimum film thickness using Raimondi-Boyd charts. (7 marks) **Step 1 — Compute the clearance ratio $\psi$** $$\psi = \frac{c}{r} = \frac{(D-d)/2}{d/2} = \frac{D-d}{d} = \frac{0.2}{100} = 0.002$$ **Step 2 — Compute bearing pressure $P$** $$P = \frac{W}{L \times D} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ N}}{100 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm}} = 5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 3 — Convert $\mu$ to consistent units** $$\mu = 20 \text{ cP} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$$ (To use with $P$ in Pa: $P = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa) **Step 4 — Convert N to rev/s** $$N = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rev/s}$$ **Step 5 — Compute $S_o$** $$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P \psi^2} = \frac{(20 \times 10^{-3})(25)}{(5 \times 10^6)(0.002)^2} = \frac{0.5}{20} = 0.025$$ **Step 6 — Use Raimondi-Boyd chart for $S_o = 0.025$** From the Raimondi-Boyd chart (or standard table): - Eccentricity ratio $\epsilon \approx 0.81$ - Friction variable $(r/c)f \approx 3.22$ - Flow variable $Q/(rcNL) \approx 3.33$ **Step 7 — Compute coefficient of friction $f$** $$f = \frac{3.22 \times c}{r} = 3.22 \times 0.002 = 0.00644$$ **Step 8 — Compute minimum film thickness $h_0$** $$h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon) = 0.1 (1 - 0.81) = 0.1 \times 0.19 = 0.019 \text{ mm} = 19 \text{ μm}$$ #### Part (b) — Compute the power loss and the total oil flow required, and verify the suitability of the bearing. (7 marks) **Step 1 — Sliding velocity $U$** $$U = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.1 \text{ m} \times 25 = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Friction force $F_f$** $$F_f = f \times W = 0.00644 \times 50{,}000 = 322 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Power loss $P_{loss}$** $$P_{loss} = F_f \times U = 322 \times 7.854 = 2529 \text{ W} \approx 2.53 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Total oil flow $Q$** Using flow variable $Q/(rcNL) = 3.33$: $$Q = 3.33 \times r \times c \times N \times L$$ Convert all to meters: $r = 0.05$ m, $c = 0.0001$ m, $N = 25$ rev/s, $L = 0.1$ m. $$Q = 3.33 \times 0.05 \times 0.0001 \times 25 \times 0.1 = 3.33 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$Q = 4.16 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 41.6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 2.5 \text{ L/min}$$ **Step 5 — Verification** - If combined surface roughness $R_{a,journal} + R_{a,bearing} = 0.8 + 0.6 = 1.4$ μm, - Since $h_0 = 19$ μm $\gg 1.4$ μm, the bearing operates in the **full hydrodynamic regime** — **design is suitable**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** Students often mix $N$ in rpm with $P$ in N/mm². Always convert to consistent units first. > 2. **Forgetting to convert cP to Pa·s (–1 mark):** 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s. The "20 cP" trap catches many students. > 3. **Misreading Raimondi-Boyd chart (–2 marks):** Use the **short-bearing** chart when $L/d < 1$ and **long-bearing** chart when $L/d \ge 1$. Here $L/d = 1$, so it is borderline — typically use the **short-bearing** values as conservative. --- ### Question B — Petroff's Equation & Heat Balance (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]** A hydrodynamic journal bearing is to be designed for a centrifugal pump. The journal has: - Diameter: $d = 80$ mm - Length: $L = 80$ mm - Radial clearance: $c = 0.08$ mm - Speed: $N = 3000$ rpm - Load: $W = 20$ kN - Viscosity at operating temperature: $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s - Ambient temperature: $T_a = 30°C$ - Heat transfer coefficient of housing: $h_{CR} = 30$ W/m²·K - Bearing housing surface area: $A_s = 0.15$ m² #### Part (a) — Using Petroff's equation, compute the friction torque, coefficient of friction, and power loss. (7 marks) **Step 1 — Petroff's friction torque** Convert $N$: $N = 3000/60 = 50$ rev/s. $$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$$ Convert all to SI: $r = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.08$ m, $c = 0.00008$ m. $$T_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \times 0.025 \times 50 \times (0.04)^3 \times 0.08}{0.00008}$$ Compute numerator: $4 \pi^2 \approx 39.478$; $0.025 \times 50 = 1.25$; $(0.04)^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$. $$\text{Numerator} = 39.478 \times 1.25 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.08$$ $$= 39.478 \times 1.25 \times 5.12 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 2.527 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·m}$$ $$T_f = \frac{2.527 \times 10^{-4}}{0.00008} = 3.16 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 2 — Coefficient of friction** Bearing pressure: $$P = \frac{W}{L \times d} = \frac{20{,}000}{0.08 \times 0.08} = 3.125 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\mu_f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P c} = \frac{2 \times 9.87 \times 0.025 \times 50 \times 0.04}{3.125 \times 10^6 \times 0.00008}$$ $$\text{Numerator} = 19.74 \times 0.025 \times 50 \times 0.04 = 19.74 \times 0.05 = 0.987$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 3.125 \times 10^6 \times 0.00008 = 250$$ $$\mu_f = \frac{0.987}{250} = 0.00395$$ **Step 3 — Power loss** Sliding velocity: $U = \pi d N = \pi \times 0.08 \times 50 = 12.566$ m/s. Friction force: $F_f = \mu_f \times W = 0.00395 \times 20{,}000 = 79$ N. $$P_{loss} = F_f \times U = 79 \times 12.566 = 993 \text{ W} \approx 1 \text{ kW}$$ #### Part (b) — Determine the equilibrium bearing temperature and check whether it stays within the safe oil temperature limit. (7 marks) **Step 1 — Heat balance at steady state** $$H_{gen} = H_{diss} = h_{CR} \times A_s \times (T_b - T_a)$$ We have $H_{gen} = 993$ W. **Step 2 — Solve for $T_b$** $$993 = 30 \times 0.15 \times (T_b - 30)$$ $$993 = 4.5 \times (T_b - 30)$$ $$T_b - 30 = \frac{993}{4.5} = 220.67$$ $$T_b = 250.67°C$$ **Step 3 — Check against safe oil limit** Mineral oils typically fail above **150–180°C** (oxidation, coking). Since $T_b = 250.67°C \gg 180°C$, **the bearing will overheat** and the design is **not acceptable**. **Step 4 — Recommended remediation** - Increase housing surface area (add fins). - Use a forced oil cooler. - Switch to synthetic oil with higher viscosity index. - Use water-cooled bearing housing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Using $r$ vs $R$ confusion (–1 mark):** Petroff's equation uses the journal radius $r$, not the bearing radius $R$. Mixing them up will give wrong torque. > 2. **Forgetting to convert c to meters in SI-based numerical (–2 marks):** The clearance is in **mm** in the problem but the formula needs SI units. 0.08 mm = 0.00008 m. > 3. **Not interpreting heat balance result (–2 marks):** Students stop at "T_b = 250°C" without commenting on whether the design is safe. Always provide a **concluding remark**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 2: Sliding Contact Bearing Lubrication — Key Takeaways** ### 🔑 Critical Definitions - [ ] **Sliding Contact Bearing**: Bearing with journal sliding against bearing surface, separated by lubricant. - [ ] **Hydrodynamic Lubrication**: Pressure generated by relative motion of surfaces via a converging wedge. - [ ] **Eccentricity Ratio ($\epsilon$)**: Ratio of eccentricity to radial clearance, $\epsilon = e/c$, ranges 0 (centered) to 1 (metal-to-metal). - [ ] **Sommerfeld Number ($S_o$)**: $\mu N / (P \psi^2)$, determines operating regime. - [ ] **Petroff's Equation**: Applies only to **concentric**, lightly loaded bearings. - [ ] **Raimondi-Boyd Charts**: Graphical solution linking $S_o$, $\epsilon$, friction variable, and flow variable. ### 🔑 Top 10 Formulas (Memorize) 1. $h = c(1 + \epsilon \cos\theta)$ 2. $h_0 = c(1 - \epsilon)$ 3. $S_o = \mu N / (P \psi^2)$ 4. $T_f = 4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L / c$ (Petroff) 5. $\mu_f = 2\pi^2 \mu N r / (P c)$ (Petroff) 6. $P_{loss} = \mu_f W \pi D N$ 7. $U = \pi D N$ (sliding velocity) 8. $H_{gen} = H_{diss} = h_{CR} A_s (T_b - T_a)$ 9. $Q = (\text{flow variable}) \times r c N L$ 10. $P = W / (L \times D)$ (bearing pressure) ### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Pitfalls - [ ] Always convert **rpm → rev/s** before using in $S_o$. - [ ] Convert **cP → Pa·s** (× 10⁻³) and **N/mm² → Pa** (× 10⁶). - [ ] Petroff gives **upper bound on friction**; real bearings have **lower** friction. - [ ] Higher $\epsilon$ → lower $h_0$ → higher wear risk. - [ ] $h_0$ must exceed **combined surface roughness** of journal + bearing. - [ ] Heat balance assumes **steady state**: $H_{gen} = H_{diss}$. - [ ] For **short bearings** ($L/d < 1$), use **Gumbel/Raimondi-Boyd short-bearing chart**. - [ ] For **long bearings** ($L/d \ge 1$), use **Sommerfeld long-bearing approximation**. ### 🔑 Real-World Automotive Applications - **Engine Crankshaft Bearings**: Operating in hydrodynamic regime at cruising speed. - **Camshaft Bearings**: Similar hydrodynamic operation. - **Connecting Rod Big-End**: Critical for engine reliability; uses high-VI oils. - **Wheel Hub Bearings**: Some use hydrodynamic sliding sleeves; others use rolling elements. - **Turbocharger Bearings**: Often hydrostatic to handle high speeds (200,000+ rpm). - **Transmission Shafts**: Hydrodynamic plain bearings in some manual gearboxes. ### 🔑 Numerical Strategy for KTU 14-Mark Questions 1. **List Given Data** (5 parameters) — 1 mark 2. **Convert All Units to SI** — 1 mark 3. **Compute $\psi$ and $P$** — 1 mark 4. **Compute $S_o$** — 1 mark 5. **Read $\epsilon$, friction variable, flow variable from Raimondi-Boyd chart** — 2 marks 6. **Compute $h_0$, $\mu_f$, $P_{loss}$, $Q$** — 4 marks 7. **Verify design safety ($h_0$ vs surface roughness, $T_b$ vs oil limit)** — 2 marks 8. **Conclude with design recommendation** — 2 marks

topics.title: Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — LUBRICANTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubricant (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *lubricant* is any substance (solid, semi-solid, liquid, or gaseous) introduced between two contacting, relatively moving surfaces to **reduce friction, dissipate heat, minimize wear, transmit power, and protect against corrosion** — thereby improving the functional reliability, efficiency, and service life of an automotive component.

In the strict sense used in the KTU **Design of Automotive Components (PCMUT601)** syllabus, a lubricant is a **tribological medium** that maintains a separating film (fluid film, boundary film, or solid film) between interacting asperities of machine elements such as journal bearings, piston rings, cams, gears, and valve trains.

### 1.2 The Five Primary Functions of a Lubricant in an Automobile

| # | Function | Automotive Example |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Friction reduction** | Engine bearings, crankshaft journals |
| 2 | **Wear prevention** | Cam-follower contacts, piston rings |
| 3 | **Heat dissipation** | Engine sump oil, transmission oil |
| 4 | **Contaminant suspension / cleaning** | Detergents in engine oil hold soot |
| 5 | **Corrosion protection & sealing** | Grease in wheel bearings, oil film on cylinder walls |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slippery Floor" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Shopping-Cart Wheel**
> Imagine pushing a heavy shopping cart. The wheels make a horrible squeaking sound when *dry* — that sound is friction destroying the metal. Now imagine a small puddle of oil on the floor where the wheel rolls. Suddenly the wheel glides silently. The oil "got in between" the metal wheel and the metal axle of the floor, preventing the rough peaks (called *asperities*) from touching and grinding against each other.
>
> **That puddle of oil is exactly what a lubricant does inside your car engine** — it gets trapped between the crankshaft journal and the bearing shell, replacing metal-to-metal contact with oil-to-oil contact that has ~1000× less resistance.

### 1.4 Why Lubricants are Critical in Automotive Design

A typical automotive engine produces **~3000 to 6000 friction losses** measured in horsepower. Without proper lubrication:
- Bearing temperatures would exceed **200 °C**, causing seizure
- Wear rates would shorten engine life from **~200,000 km to <5,000 km**
- Fuel efficiency would drop by **8–15 %**
- Emissions of unburnt hydrocarbons would rise due to cylinder wall wear

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Automotive Reference Metrics (must be memorised for KTU):**
> - **Dynamic viscosity of fresh engine oil at 100 °C:** 9.3 – 12.5 cSt (centistokes)
> - **Engine oil operating temperature range:** −30 °C (cold start) to ~150 °C (sump peak)
> - **Bearings designed with radial clearance c/r ratio of order 0.001**
> - **Minimum hydrodynamic oil film thickness target:** ≥ 0.25 µm for automotive bearings
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Lubricants

Lubricants used in automobiles fall into **four physical states** and multiple chemical families.

### 2.1.1 By Physical State

| Physical State | Common Examples | Typical Use in Vehicles |
|----------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| **Liquid (Oils)** | Mineral oil, synthetic PAO, ester | Engine sump, gearboxes, hydraulics |
| **Semi-Solid (Greases)** | Lithium / calcium / sodium soap + base oil | Wheel bearings, chassis points, U-joints |
| **Solid** | Graphite, MoS₂, PTFE, hexagonal BN | High-temp locks, CV joints, dry-film |
| **Gaseous** | Air, nitrogen | Gas bearings (rare in road cars) |

### 2.1.2 By Chemical Origin

- **Mineral oils** — refined from petroleum crude (paraffinic, naphthenic, aromatic fractions).
- **Synthetic oils** — Polyalphaolefin (PAO), diesters, polyalkylene glycol (PAG), silicone.
- **Vegetable oils** — bio-lubricants; e.g. castor oil, rapeseed oil (used in some two-stroke applications).
- **Solid lubricants** — lamellar solids (graphite, MoS₂) that shear easily along basal planes.

## 2.2 Required Properties of an Automotive Lubricant

A lubricant is judged by **physical, chemical, and performance tests**. The KTU syllabus expects the student to know the following properties in detail.

### 2.2.1 Viscosity (μ)

The **single most important property**. Defined as the internal resistance of a fluid to flow. Measured in:

- **Dynamic viscosity μ** → units: Pa·s (SI) or Poise (CGS) → **1 Pa·s = 10 Poise**
- **Kinematic viscosity ν = μ / ρ** → units: m²/s (SI) or Stokes → **1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s = 100 cSt**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One Stoke = 100 centistokes (cSt).** Engine oils are rated in cSt at **40 °C and 100 °C** as per **ISO 3104 / ASTM D445**.

### 2.2.2 Viscosity Index (VI)

A dimensionless number (developed by **Dean & Davis, 1929**) that indicates the *rate at which an oil's viscosity drops with temperature*. Higher VI ⇒ flatter viscosity-temperature curve ⇒ better lubricant.

### 2.2.3 Flash Point & Fire Point

- **Flash point** — lowest temperature at which oil vapour ignites momentarily when a flame is brought near (closed-cup ASTM D93 or open-cup ASTM D92).
- **Fire point** — temperature at which vapour *sustains* burning for ≥ 5 s.
- Engine oils must have **flash point ≥ 200 °C** for safe under-hood operation.

### 2.2.4 Pour Point & Cloud Point

- **Pour point** — lowest temperature at which oil will *just flow* (ASTM D97). Critical for **cold-start** in cold climates.
- **Cloud point** — temperature at which wax crystals first become visible.

### 2.2.5 Neutralisation Number (Acid Number, AN)

Mass of **KOH (in mg) required to neutralise 1 g of oil**. Indicates oil degradation / oxidation. Fresh engine oil: AN < 2 mg KOH/g.

### 2.2.6 Ash Content & Carbon Residue

- **Ash** — incombustible residue (metallic detergents Zn, Ca, Mg). High ash ⇒ increased spark-plug fouling in petrol engines.
- **Carbon residue (Conradson or Ramsbottom)** — tendency of oil to form coke at high T. Important for diesel engine oils.

### 2.2.7 Emulsification, Demulsibility, Foaming

- **Emulsification** — ability to mix with water (steam-cylinder oils WANT this).
- **Demulsibility** — ability to separate from water (turbine oils WANT this).
- **Foam resistance** — controlled by anti-foam additives like **silicone (polydimethylsiloxane)**.

## 2.3 Viscosity Grade Systems Used in Automotive Design

| System | Organisation | Where Used |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| **SAE J300** | Society of Automotive Engineers | Engine oils (0W, 5W, 10W … 20, 30, 40 …) |
| **SAE J306** | SAE | Axle / gear oils (75W, 80W, 90, 140) |
| **ISO 3448** | ISO | Industrial hydraulic oils (ISO VG 32, 46, 68 …) |
| **AGMA 250.04** | American Gear Manufacturers | Industrial gear lubricants |
| **NLGI** | National Lubricating Grease Institute | Grease consistency (000, 0, 1, 2, 3) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **multi-grade** engine oil such as **SAE 5W-30** passes both the 5W cold-test (≤ 6600 cP at −30 °C) AND the 30 hot-test (9.3–12.5 cSt at 100 °C) — made possible by **Viscosity Index Improvers (VII)** which are long-chain polymers (e.g., OCP, PMA) that uncoil at high T.

## 2.4 Lubrication Regimes — The Stribeck Curve

When two surfaces move relative to each other, the *thickness* of the lubricant film determines which regime the bearing operates in.

The **Stribeck curve** plots the **coefficient of friction f** on the y-axis against a dimensionless group — the **Sommerfeld number** or the **Hersey number** (μN/P) — on the x-axis. Three distinct regimes appear:

1. **Boundary lubrication** — at very low speed or very high load. Surfaces are in asperity contact; friction is governed by surface chemistry, not fluid film.
2. **Mixed lubrication** — partial film; some asperities still touch.
3. **Hydrodynamic (full-film) lubrication** — surfaces completely separated by a continuous fluid wedge. Friction is purely viscous.

**Elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)** is a special case occurring in **gear teeth and rolling-element bearings** where contact pressures are so high (GPa range) that the surfaces elastically deform AND the lubricant viscosity rises dramatically with pressure.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table — questions appear in Part A and Part B of KTU ESE every year on these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Petroff's friction torque | $T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L}{c}$ | μ = dynamic viscosity [Pa·s], N = rev/s [1/s], r = journal radius [m], c = radial clearance [m], L = bearing length [m] |
| 2 | Petroff's coefficient of friction | $f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r}{P\,c}$ | P = bearing pressure $\bigl(\frac{W}{2rL}\bigr)$ [Pa] |
| 3 | Petroff's load capacity | $W = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,L\,r^{3}}{c}\cdot\dfrac{1}{f}$ | — |
| 4 | Sommerfeld number | $S_{o} = \dfrac{\mu\,N}{P}\cdot\dfrac{r}{c}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Hersey number | $H_{e} = \dfrac{\mu\,N}{P}$ | [1/s] |
| 6 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | ν in m²/s, μ in Pa·s, ρ in kg/m³ |
| 7 | Viscosity Index (Dean–Davis) | $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H}\times 100$ | U = 40 °C viscosity of test oil, L and H from reference tables |
| 8 | SAE multi-grade W-rating | $CCS \le 6600\ \text{cP at } -30\ \text{°C (for 5W)}$ | Cold Cranking Simulator limit |
| 9 | NLGI worked penetration | $P_{60} = P_{0} + \Delta P$ | Grease consistency classification |
| 10 | Petroff's power loss | $P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{8\pi^{3}\,\mu\,N^{2}\,r^{3}\,L}{c}$ | Watts (using ω = 2πN) |

> **Engineering Utility:** Petroff's equations give the *first-order estimate* of friction and heat generation in a newly designed journal bearing. They **assume a thin concentric film with no load support from the wedge**, hence they *under-predict* load capacity — but they remain the KTU-favourite analytical tool because they allow the student to quickly relate **viscosity, speed, clearance, and load** during a board exam.

## 2.6 Lubricant Additives — The "Performance Chemistry"

Modern automotive oils are blends of a **base stock (70–90 %)** + a **chemical additive package (10–30 %)**.

| Additive Type | Function | Chemical Example |
|---------------|----------|------------------|
| **Anti-wear (AW)** | Forms sacrificial film at asperities | ZDDP (zinc dialkyldithiophosphate) |
| **Detergents** | Neutralise acids, keep surfaces clean | Calcium sulphonate, Mg-phenate |
| **Dispersants** | Suspend soot & sludge | Succinimide |
| **Anti-oxidant** | Slow oil oxidation | ZDDP, hindered phenols |
| **Viscosity Index Improver (VII)** | Flattens μ–T curve | OCP, PMA, styrene-isoprene |
| **Pour-point depressant** | Prevents wax crystallisation | PMA copolymers |
| **Anti-foam** | Releases trapped air | Silicone (PDMS) |
| **Friction modifier (FM)** | Reduces boundary friction | MoDTC, organic fatty acids |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Petroff's Equation for Friction Torque in a Journal Bearing

> **Premise.** Consider a *full, concentric* journal of radius **r** rotating at **N** revolutions per second inside a bearing of length **L**. The radial clearance is **c** (very small compared to r). The space between journal and bearing is filled with a Newtonian lubricant of dynamic viscosity **μ**.

**Step 1 — Velocity of journal surface.**
The journal surface moves tangentially with a linear velocity

$$U = 2\pi r N \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity gradient (shear rate) in the film.**
Because the clearance **c** is very small, the velocity variation across the film is approximately linear (Couette flow assumption). The shear rate is

$$\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{U}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{c} \quad \text{[1/s]}$$

**Step 3 — Shear stress from Newton's law of viscosity.**
For a Newtonian fluid,

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma} = \mu \cdot \dfrac{2\pi r N}{c} \quad \text{[Pa]}$$

**Step 4 — Friction force on the journal.**
The shear stress acts on the entire cylindrical wetted area $A = 2\pi r L$:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot \dfrac{2\pi r N}{c} \cdot 2\pi r L = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{2}\,L}{c} \quad \text{[N]}$$

**Step 5 — Friction torque about the journal axis.**
The force acts at radius **r**, so the resisting torque is

$$\boxed{\,T = F \cdot r = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L}{c} \quad \text{[N·m]}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of friction.**
The bearing pressure is defined as load per unit projected area:

$$P = \dfrac{W}{2rL} \quad \text{[Pa]}$$

The coefficient of friction is the ratio of tangential friction force to applied radial load:

$$f = \dfrac{F}{W} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{2}\,L}{W\cdot c} = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r}{P\cdot c} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}$$

**Step 7 — Power loss due to viscous drag.**
Power dissipated as heat is torque × angular velocity. With $\omega = 2\pi N$,

$$\boxed{\,P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L}{c} \cdot 2\pi N = \dfrac{8\pi^{3}\,\mu\,N^{2}\,r^{3}\,L}{c} \quad \text{[W]}\,}$$

> **Engineering note (KTU Board marker comments):** Petroff's derivation assumes *concentric* (no-load) journal position. Real bearings operate *eccentric* with eccentricity ratio **ε** between 0 and 1. The full hydrodynamic solution uses **Reynolds' equation** and gives higher load capacity. Petroff's formulas are therefore **conservative upper bounds for friction and lower bounds for load capacity**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Viscosity Index (Dean–Davis Method)

> **Premise.** Two reference oils are selected:
> - Oil **A**: VI = 0 (Pennsylvanian paraffinic crude)
> - Oil **B**: VI = 100 (Gulf Coast naphthenic crude)
> Both have the **same kinematic viscosity at 100 °C** as the *test oil* **U**.

**Step 1 — Read the 40 °C viscosities.**
Let:
- $L$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of reference oil A (VI = 0)
- $H$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of reference oil B (VI = 100)
- $U$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of the test oil (also measured at 100 °C same as A and B)

**Step 2 — Linear interpolation between 0 and 100.**
By definition, the VI scale is linear between L and H:

$$\dfrac{VI - 0}{U - L} = \dfrac{100 - 0}{H - L}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for VI.**

$$\boxed{\,VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H}\times 100\,}$$

**Step 4 — Extension to VI > 100.**
If $U < H$, the test oil is "better" than the VI = 100 reference. A modified formula based on logarithmic interpolation is used:

$$VI = 100 + \dfrac{L - U}{D}$$

where **D** is a small correction factor (~0.7) defined in ASTM D2270.

> **Interpretation:** Higher VI means a flatter μ–T curve — the oil is "multigrade-friendly". Typical engine oils: **VI = 95 to 120**; **synthetic PAO**: **VI = 135 to 150**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lubricant Selection for Common Automotive Components

> Use this matrix to answer KTU design questions: "*Select a suitable lubricant grade for a given automotive component and justify.*"

| Component | Operating Condition | Recommended Lubricant | Justification (Property Used) |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Engine sump (petrol)** | 90–110 °C, high load variation | SAE 5W-30 / 10W-40 synthetic | High VI, low cold-crank viscosity, ZDDP AW protection |
| **Crankshaft main bearing** | 5000–7000 rpm, hydrodynamic | Multigrade engine oil (5W-30) | Petroff's: high μ at high T → enough film thickness |
| **Manual gearbox** | High tooth-load, sliding + rolling | SAE 80W-90 GL-4 mineral | EHL film at gear mesh; EP additives for sliding |
| **Differential (hypoid)** | Very high sliding at gear mesh | SAE 85W-140 GL-5 with EP | EP (extreme-pressure) additives: sulphur-phosphorus |
| **Wheel bearing (disc brake side)** | 100–150 °C, sealed | NLGI No. 2 lithium grease | High drop point (~190 °C), water resistance |
| **Wheel bearing (non-brake side)** | Lower T, occasional motion | NLGI No. 3 lithium | Stiffer consistency, longer retention |
| **Constant-velocity (CV) joint** | High angle, shock loads | Grease with MoS₂ | Solid lubricant + high VI base oil |
| **Brake caliper slide pins** | 200–300 °C spikes | Silicone or PFPE grease | High T stability, no rubber swell |
| **Shock absorber** | High shear, low T rise | Hydraulic oil (ISO VG 32) | Low pour point, anti-foam |
| **Power-steering pump** | 50–130 °C, 100+ bar | ATF (Dexron III / VI) | High VI, anti-wear, low foaming |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Petroff's Friction Calculation

> **Problem.** A crankshaft journal in a 4-cylinder petrol engine has the following data:
> - Journal radius $r = 25\ \text{mm} = 0.025\ \text{m}$
> - Bearing length $L = 20\ \text{mm} = 0.020\ \text{m}$
> - Radial clearance $c = 0.050\ \text{mm} = 5\times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$
> - Speed $N = 5000\ \text{rpm} = 83.33\ \text{rev/s}$
> - Oil dynamic viscosity at operating T: $\mu = 0.012\ \text{Pa·s}$
> - Applied bearing load $W = 3500\ \text{N}$

**Step 1 — Bearing pressure.**

$$P = \dfrac{W}{2rL} = \dfrac{3500}{2(0.025)(0.020)} = \dfrac{3500}{0.001} = 3.5\times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 3.5\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Petroff's coefficient of friction.**

$$f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r}{P\cdot c} = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}(0.012)(83.33)(0.025)}{(3.5\times 10^{6})(5\times 10^{-5})}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$2\pi^{2}(0.012)(83.33)(0.025) = 2(9.8696)(0.012)(83.33)(0.025) = 0.4935\ \text{(approx.)}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$(3.5\times 10^{6})(5\times 10^{-5}) = 175$$

Therefore:

$$f = \dfrac{0.4935}{175} = 2.82\times 10^{-3} = 0.00282$$

**Step 3 — Petroff's friction torque.**

$$T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L}{c} = \dfrac{4(9.8696)(0.012)(83.33)(0.025)^{3}(0.020)}{5\times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator:

$$4(9.8696)(0.012)(83.33)(1.5625\times 10^{-5})(0.020) = 1.234\times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:

$$5\times 10^{-5}$$

Therefore:

$$T = \dfrac{1.234\times 10^{-4}}{5\times 10^{-5}} = 2.47\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Power loss in this bearing.**

$$P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = 2.47 \times (2\pi \times 83.33) = 2.47 \times 523.6 = 1293\ \text{W} \approx 1.29\ \text{kW}$$

> **Interpretation for KTU board answer:** A single main bearing in a 4-cylinder engine dissipates about 1.3 kW as heat. Across 5 main bearings, total friction loss in bearings alone is **~6.5 kW** — that is why **oil cooler** and **oil pump flow rate** are critical design parameters in the cooling system.

> **Reading tip:** The chart shows that *automobile applications* cluster in the **B → B1 / B2** and **C → C1A** branches. Students should mentally highlight: *engine oil = B1 (mineral) or B2 (synthetic), wheel-bearing grease = C1A (lithium), CV joint grease = C1A with MoS₂ additive (cross-link with D1B).*

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Engine Lubrication System

A complete engine lubrication system is a *closed-loop* arrangement combining the lubricant (oil), the pump, the filter, the cooler, and the bearings. The block diagram below captures the **flow of lubricant** and the **energy interactions** at the system level.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUMP[OIL SUMP RESERVOIR]
        RES[Oil Reservoir Volume 4 to 6 L]
    end

    PUMP[Oil Pump Gear or Rotor Type] --> FILT
    FILT[Full Flow Oil Filter 10 to 25 micron] --> MAIN[Main Oil Gallery in Block]

    MAIN --> MAINJOURNAL[Main Bearing Journals 1 to 5]
    MAIN --> CAMSHAFT[Cam Shaft Bearings]
    MAIN --> ROCKER[Rocker Arms]
    MAIN --> PISTON[Piston Cooling Jets]

    MAINJOURNAL -->|Through Crankshaft Drillings| CONNROD[Connecting Rod Big End]
    CONNROD --> CONNRODSMALL[Connecting Rod Small End]
    CONNRODSMALL -->|Spray| CYLD[cylinder Wall]
    CYLD -->|Drain by gravity| SUMPBACK[Return to Sump]

    CAMSHAFT --> RETURN
    ROCKER --> RETURN
    PISTON --> RETURN

    SUMP --> PUMP
    MAIN --> BYP[Bypass Relief Valve set at 3 to 5 bar]
    BYP --> SUMP

    subgraph COOL[OIL COOLING LOOP]
        OILCOOL[Engine Oil Cooler 25 to 40 kW Heat Rejection]
        COOLFAN[Cooling Fan / Water Cross Flow]
    end

    SUMP -. Heat path via oil .-> OILCOOL
    OILCOOL --> COOLFAN
    COOLFAN -. Cooled oil .-> SUMP

    RETURN[Return to sump] --> SUMP
```

> **Functional interpretation:**
> - The **pump** pressurises the oil (typically 3–5 bar at idle, up to 6 bar at high speed).
> - The **filter** traps particles ≥ 10 µm. Most filters are *full-flow* (100 % of oil passes through).
> - The **oil cooler** is essential in turbocharged engines — charge-cooled EGR gases raise oil temperature beyond the natural convection limit of the sump.
> - The **bypass relief valve** opens if the filter becomes clogged, protecting the engine from oil starvation.

## 4.3 Stribeck Curve — Lubrication Regimes

A schematic of the Stribeck curve is rendered below as a **state-machine** mapping operating conditions to lubrication regimes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[Increasing Speed or Decreasing Load] --> REG1
    REG1[BOUNDARY LUBRICATION] -->|Friction High and Variable| REG2
    REG2[MIXED LUBRICATION] -->|Friction Decreasing| REG3
    REG3[FULL FILM HYDRODYNAMIC] -->|Stable low friction| REG4
    REG4[Elastohydrodynamic EHL Gear and Rolling Contact]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Question pattern:** "*At what value of the Hersey number does the transition from boundary to mixed lubrication occur for a typical automotive crankshaft bearing?*"
> **Answer:** Approximately $H_{e} = \mu N/P \approx 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$ for boundary→mixed, and $\approx 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$ for mixed→hydrodynamic.
