```mermaid
    classDef alert fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Strain-Energy Decision Matrix (Mermaid Mind Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Strain Energy in Automotive Components))
    Axial Loading
      Formula: U equals P squared L by 2AE
      Application: Connecting Rod Bolt
      Resilience: Ur equals sigma squared by 2E
    Torsional Loading
      Formula: U equals T squared L by 2GJ
      Application: Drive Shaft
      Resilience: Ur_s equals tau squared by 2G
    Bending Loading
      Formula: U equals M squared L by 2EI
      Application: Leaf Spring
      Resilience: Linear elastic recovery
    Multiaxial Loading
      Formula: General 3D expression
      Application: Crankcase bolts
      Resilience: Von Mises equivalent stress
    Design Check
      Compare U with U_p
      Safe or Yield Decision
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The block diagrams above intentionally use **alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels** (per the protocol), avoiding any markdown formatting inside the node text. Engineers reading the chart should follow the arrows from *Road Force* → *Spring* → *Energy Reservoir* → *Proof-Resilience Decision Logic* → *Damper → Smooth Motion*. The **orange decision block** is the critical engineering checkpoint — if the stored strain energy ever exceeds proof resilience, the spring yields and the safety alert is triggered.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms: (a) Modulus of Resilience, and (b) Proof Resilience. State their units.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (a) **Modulus of Resilience** is the strain energy absorbed per unit volume of a material when loaded up to its elastic limit. It is given by $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ and measured in **J/m³ or Pa**. *(1.5 marks)*
- (b) **Proof Resilience** is the maximum strain energy a component can absorb without undergoing permanent deformation, expressed as $U_p = U_r \times V$, measured in **N·m or Joules**. *(1.5 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**A mild steel specimen has $E = 200$ GPa and $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa. Find its modulus of resilience.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(250 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 200 \times 10^9} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{16}}{4 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$U_r = 156\,250 \text{ J/m}^3$$

*Final Answer:* $\boxed{U_r = 0.15625 \text{ MJ/m}^3}$ *(3 marks: 2 for formula+substitution, 1 for final value with units)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **modulus of resilience** for a linear elastic material under uniaxial tension. Hence, compute the proof resilience of a steel rod of length **1.0 m** and diameter **40 mm**, given $E = 210$ GPa and $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*Derivation (4 marks):*
- Strain at elastic limit: $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$ … (i)
- Modulus of resilience = area under $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ triangle up to yield: $U_r = \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_y$ … (ii)
- Substituting (i) into (ii): $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ … (iii) ✓ *(4 marks)*

*Numerical Computation (3 marks):*
- Volume $V = A \cdot L = \pi (0.02)^2 (1.0) = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $U_r = (350 \times 10^6)^2 / (2 \times 210 \times 10^9) = 291.67$ kJ/m³
- $U_p = U_r \cdot V = 291\,666.67 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} = 366.5$ J

*Final Answer:* $\boxed{U_p \approx 366.5 \text{ J}}$ *(3 marks: 1 for substitution, 1 for answer, 1 for units)*

---

**(b)** A torque of **2 kN·m** is suddenly applied to a **solid circular shaft** of length **1.5 m** and diameter **50 mm**. Take $G = 80$ GPa. Compute: (i) the maximum shear stress, and (ii) the strain energy absorbed. Compare with the resilience capacity if $\tau_y = 180$ MPa. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

(i) Maximum shear stress:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2000}{\pi (0.05)^3} = 81.49 \text{ MPa}$$ *(3 marks)*

(ii) Strain energy absorbed:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^4}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

$$U = \frac{T^2 L}{2 G J} = \frac{(2000)^2 \times 1.5}{2 \times 80 \times 10^9 \times 6.136 \times 10^{-7}} = 61.10 \text{ J}$$ *(3 marks)*

Comparison:

$$U_{r,s} = \frac{\tau_y^2}{2G} = \frac{(180 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 80 \times 10^9} = 202.5 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$

Proof resilience $U_p = 202\,500 \times V = 202\,500 \times 2.945 \times 10^{-3} = 596.4$ J. Since $U = 61.1$ J $< U_p = 596.4$ J, the shaft is **safe**. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a labelled stress–strain diagram, explain the difference between **modulus of resilience** and **modulus of toughness**. For an automotive leaf spring, justify why a high modulus of resilience is preferred over a high modulus of toughness. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- **Modulus of Resilience** = area of triangle up to elastic limit $0 \rightarrow \sigma_y$ on $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ diagram. Represents energy stored **elastically** and fully recovered on unloading. *(2 marks)*
- **Modulus of Toughness** = total area under the entire $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve up to fracture. Represents total energy absorbed **including plastic deformation**, of which only a fraction is recovered. *(2 marks)*
- **Automotive Leaf Spring Rationale:** The spring undergoes repeated elastic loading cycles. A high modulus of resilience ensures (i) full recovery after each cycle (no sag), (ii) no fatigue damage from plastic flow, and (iii) predictable spring constant. A high modulus of toughness with high plastic component would cause permanent set, ride-height loss, and eventual failure. Therefore, leaf spring materials (e.g., **55Si2Mn20, 60Si7**) are heat-treated to maximize $U_r$, not $U_t$. *(3 marks)*

---

**(b)** A mild steel component is subjected to a biaxial stress state with $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -50$ MPa, and all shear components equal to zero. Take $E = 200$ GPa, $G = 77$ GPa, and $\nu = 0.30$. Compute the total strain energy density. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Using the isotropic 3-D formula with $\sigma_z = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zx} = 0$:

$$u = \frac{1}{2E}\bigl[\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - 2\nu\,\sigma_x\sigma_y\bigr]$$

$$u = \frac{1}{2 \times 200 \times 10^9}\bigl[(100)^2 + (-50)^2 - 2(0.30)(100)(-50)\bigr] \times 10^{12}$$

$$u = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{11}}\bigl[10\,000 + 2\,500 + 3\,000\bigr] \times 10^{12}$$

$$u = \frac{15\,500 \times 10^{12}}{4 \times 10^{11}} = 38\,750 \text{ J/m}^3$$ *(5 marks)*

Conversion check (Pa equivalent):

$$u = 38\,750 \text{ J/m}^3 = 0.03875 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$ *(1 mark for unit, 1 mark for verification step)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — KTU Board Examiner Reports**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of ½:** Many students write $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / E$ instead of $\sigma_y^2 / (2E)$. This is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in resilience problems. Always remember the *triangular* area formula.
>
> 2. **Mixing up modulus of resilience with modulus of toughness:** Modulus of resilience uses only the **elastic** region (triangle), modulus of toughness uses the **entire** region (general shape). Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** purely for correctly identifying which region applies.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** If $\sigma$ is in MPa and $E$ is in GPa, you must **convert both to the same base unit (Pa)** before substituting. Failure to convert costs **½ to 1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Volumetric vs. Total Energy:** When the problem asks for "proof resilience of a component," give the answer in **Joules** (volume multiplied), not J/m³. Conversely, "modulus of resilience" requires J/m³.
>
> 5. **Neglecting Poisson Effects in 2D/3D Problems:** Under biaxial/multiaxial stress, students often write $u = (\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2)/2E$, ignoring the $-2\nu \sigma_x \sigma_y$ cross-term. This costs a full **2–3 marks**.
>
> 6. **Sudden vs. Gradual Loading:** In sudden loading problems (drop-weight or impact), the strain energy is **doubled** relative to gradual loading of the same peak force, because of the dynamic factor $h_{dyn}$. This is a favourite KTU examiner trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Resilience Module**

- ✅ **Resilience = elastic strain energy** stored per unit volume (Modulus) or in total (Proof).
- ✅ Core formula: $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ — the **most-tested equation** in this module.
- ✅ Proof resilience $U_p = U_r \times V = \sigma_y^2 V / (2E)$ — multiply by volume to get total energy.
- ✅ Torsional analogue: $U_{r,s} = \tau_y^2 / (2G)$.
- ✅ **Volumetric strain energy density:** $u_v = \sigma^2 / (2K)$, $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$.
- ✅ **Distortional strain energy density:** $u_d = (1+\nu)\sigma_{vm}^2 / (3E)$ — basis of von Mises yield.
- ✅ **Hooke's Law is the cornerstone:** Always linear elasticity → triangular area on $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ plot.
- ✅ **Automotive applications:** Suspension springs, leaf springs, drive shafts, clutch springs, bumper mounts, valve springs.
- ✅ **Material choice for springs:** High $\sigma_y / E$ ratio (e.g., spring steels, titanium alloys for racing).
- ✅ **Units discipline:** Stress in Pa, $E$ in Pa → $U_r$ in J/m³ (≡ Pa). Multiply by m³ to get Joules.
- ✅ **Resilience vs Toughness:** Resilience = elastic area (triangle); Toughness = total area (irregular shape including plastic zone).
- ✅ **3-D formula must be remembered** for Part B multiaxial questions; cross-terms $-2\nu(\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x)$ are **frequently tested**.
- ✅ **Conversion rule of thumb:** 1 MPa = $10^6$ Pa; 1 GPa = $10^9$ Pa; 1 kJ/m³ = $10^3$ J/m³.
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** "Stress squared over two times Young's — that is your resilience density per volume."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Fatigue Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static and Fatigue Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Static Loading — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Loading** is defined as the application of a load (or force) whose magnitude remains constant or varies very slowly with time, such that the resulting **inertial forces** can be neglected. The material is assumed to be subjected to a single, sustained application of stress.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (Outcome-Based Education, NEP 2020), the term *static* is reserved for loads where:
- The rate of loading is well below the natural frequency of the component.
- The induced stresses are **time-invariant** or vary at a quasi-static rate.
- Failure, when it occurs, is by a **single, monotonic event** (yielding, brittle fracture, or plastic collapse).

**Standard Static Failure Criteria (Recall — Module-1 Prequel):**

$$n = \frac{S_{y}}{\sigma} \quad \text{(Ductile — yielding)} \qquad n = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma} \quad \text{(Brittle — ultimate)}$$

where the **Factor of Safety (FoS)** $n$ is the *static factor of safety* against first yield or fracture.

---

### 1.2 Fatigue Loading — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue Loading** refers to a **time-varying, dynamic, cyclic (or random) load** of magnitude large enough to cause fracture after a *finite* number of cycles, even though the peak stress is well below the static ultimate strength $S_{ut}$ of the material. This phenomenon is termed **fatigue failure**.

The term was first coined by **J. V. Poncelet (1839)** and experimentally established by **August Wöhler (1858–1871)** while investigating sudden fracture of railway axle journals of the Royal Hanoverian State Railways.

**The Three Pillars of Fatigue (from KTU Module-2 syllabus):**

1. **Cyclic Stress Magnitude:** The peak stress is *below* the static strength, yet the part fails.
2. **Cyclic Count (Cycles to Failure, $N$):** Failure occurs only after a sufficient number of cycles.
3. **Localized Permanent Damage:** Failure is initiated at a *local* microscopic flaw (slip-band extrusions, inclusions) and propagates as a **fatigue crack**.

> [!NOTE]
> **S-N Diagram (Wöhler Curve):** The foundational tool of fatigue analysis. It plots alternating stress amplitude $S_a$ (vertical) against the number of cycles to failure $N$ (horizontal, logarithmic). The horizontal asymptote defines the **Endurance Limit $S_e$** for ferrous metals.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making it Stick

Imagine the following two real-world scenarios:

| Scenario | Loading Type | Outcome |
|----------|--------------|---------|
| Hanging a 50 kg bag from a steel hook overnight. | **Static** | Hook does not fail — stress held at ~ constant. |
| Repeatedly bending a **paperclip** back and forth. | **Fatigue** | Clip snaps at the bend after ~ 15–20 cycles, even though each bend force is far below the ultimate strength. |
| A connecting rod in an engine running at 3000 rpm for 1000 hours. | **Fatigue** | $N = 3000 \times 60 \times 1000 = 1.8 \times 10^{8}$ cycles — fatigue is *guaranteed* unless designed. |
| Pressing a car chassis during a single accidental impact. | **Static (impact, quasi-static)** | Failure by single-event yielding. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *Static = Single Strike. Fatigue = A Million Tiny Strikes, Each Individually Harmless.*

The brittle, often *catastrophic*, snap of the paperclip — which occurred with **no visible warning, no plastic deformation, and no necking** — is the exact signature of fatigue failure. This is why ~80% of all mechanical service failures in automobiles (crankshafts, valve springs, leaf springs, half-shafts) are attributed to fatigue.

---

### 1.4 The Three Canonical Stress Cycles (Syllabus Highlight)

For any fatigue analysis, the time-varying stress $\sigma(t)$ is reduced to its **maximum** $S_{max}$ and **minimum** $S_{min}$. From these two scalars, *all* fatigue quantities are derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Completely Reversed Stress Cycle:** $S_{max} = -S_{min}$ (e.g., rotating shaft under bending, fully reversed tension-compression). Mean stress $S_m = 0$.
> 2. **Repeated Stress Cycle:** $S_{min} = 0$ (or $S_{max} = 0$). Pulse between zero and a peak. (e.g., tension rod loaded from 0 → $P$ → 0.)
> 3. **Fluctuating (Asymmetric) Stress Cycle:** $S_{max}$ and $S_{min}$ are both non-zero and of the **same sign** (e.g., bolt pre-load fluctuating around a tensile mean). $0 < S_{min} < S_{max}$.

---

### 1.5 Key Engineering Constants and Terminology

- **Endurance Limit $S_e$** — Stress below which the material, in principle, has *infinite life*. For steels: $S_e' \approx 0.5 S_{ut}$ when $S_{ut} \le \mathbf{1400 \, MPa}$ (Shigley / ASME).
- **Fatigue Strength $S_f$** — Stress at which failure occurs at a specified finite number of cycles $N$ (commonly $10^{3}$, $10^{4}$, $10^{5}$, $10^{6}$).
- **Cycles to Failure $N$** — The number of stress cycles endured before fracture.
- **Stress Ratio $R$** — $R = S_{min} / S_{max}$. $R = -1$ (reversed), $R = 0$ (zero-to-tension), $R = +1$ (static).
- **Fatigue Life Ratio** — $N / N_f$, where $N_f$ is cycles-to-failure at the applied stress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Fundamental Fatigue Stress Parameters

Given any cyclic load history, the variation is always fully described by **two** of the following six. The other four are deterministically derived:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \text{Maximum (algebraic) stress in the cycle} \\
S_{min} &= \text{Minimum (algebraic) stress in the cycle} \\
S_m    &= \tfrac{1}{2}\left(S_{max}+S_{min}\right) \quad \text{(Mean stress — DC offset)} \\
S_a    &= \tfrac{1}{2}\left(S_{max}-S_{min}\right) \quad \text{(Alternating stress amplitude — AC component)} \\
S_r    &= S_{max}-S_{min} \quad \text{(Stress range)} \\
R      &= S_{min}/S_{max} \quad \text{(Stress ratio)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cyclic stress at any instant can be reconstructed as: $\sigma(t) = S_m + S_a \sin(\omega t)$.

---

### 2.2 Logic of Deriving All Quantities from $S_{max}$ and $S_{min}$

- **Why $S_m$?** Many real cycles (e.g., a preloaded bolt with a fluctuating tensile load) have a *non-zero* static baseline. The mean stress **raises** the average and drastically lowers fatigue life. Two cycles with the same $S_a$ but different $S_m$ produce *very* different damage.
- **Why $S_a$?** It is the *purely reversing* component — exactly the quantity measured on a rotating-beam (R.R. Moore) fatigue testing machine, where $S_m = 0$ by symmetry.
- **Why $R$?** It is dimensionless, independent of magnitude, and uniquely defines the *character* (shape) of the cycle.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield reference** for all Part A & Part B derivations in Module-2 ESE questions. *Memorize the column headers and the boxed units.*

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Boundary / Typical Value | Standard Units |
|---|----------|--------|---------|--------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Maximum stress | $S_{max}$ | — | $> 0$ typically | **MPa** $\vert \textbf{N/mm}^2$ |
| 2 | Minimum stress | $S_{min}$ | — | May be negative | **MPa** $\vert \textbf{N/mm}^2$ |
| 3 | Mean stress | $S_m$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}(S_{max}+S_{min})$ | $0 \le S_m \le S_{ut}$ | **MPa** $\vert \textbf{N/mm}^2$ |
| 4 | Amplitude | $S_a$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\vert S_{max}-S_{min}\vert$ | $0 \le S_a \le S_e$ for infinite life | **MPa** $\vert \textbf{N/mm}^2$ |
| 5 | Range | $S_r$ | $S_{max}-S_{min}$ | $S_r = 2 S_a$ | **MPa** $\vert \textbf{N/mm}^2$ |
| 6 | Stress ratio | $R$ | $S_{min}/S_{max}$ | $-1 \le R \le +1$ (uniaxial) | dimensionless |
| 7 | Amplitude ratio | $A$ | $S_a/S_m$ | — | dimensionless |
| 8 | Endurance limit (uncorrected, steels) | $S_e'$ | $0.5 \, S_{ut}$ | Valid for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa | **MPa** |
| 9 | Endurance limit (cast iron) | $S_e'$ | $0.4 \, S_{ut}$ | Empirical | **MPa** |
| 10 | Corrected endurance limit | $S_e$ | $k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, S_e'$ | $0.5 \le k \le 1$ | **MPa** |
| 11 | Surface factor | $k_a$ | $a S_{ut}^{b}$ | From tables; depends on finish | dimensionless |
| 12 | Size factor (round, axial/torsion) | $k_b$ | $0.879 d^{-0.107}$ (axial) $\vert \, 0.91 d^{-0.157}$ (bending) | $d$ in mm | dimensionless |
| 13 | Load factor (bending) | $k_c$ | $1.00$ | — | dimensionless |
| 14 | Load factor (axial) | $k_c$ | $0.85$ | — | dimensionless |
| 15 | Load factor (torsion) | $k_c$ | $0.59$ | — | dimensionless |
| 16 | Goodman line | — | $\tfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \tfrac{S_m}{S_{ut}} = \tfrac{1}{n}$ | Linear; conservative | dimensionless |
| 17 | Gerber parabola | — | $\left(\tfrac{S_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} + \tfrac{S_a}{S_e} = \tfrac{1}{n}$ | Curved; best fit for ductile | dimensionless |
| 18 | Soderberg line | — | $\tfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \tfrac{S_m}{S_y} = \tfrac{1}{n}$ | Most conservative | dimensionless |

---

### 2.4 The Engineering Significance

**Why does static-design fail in cyclic service?** A connecting rod in a 4-stroke SI engine is loaded only up to ~ $\mathbf{200 \, MPa}$ peak — well below $S_{ut} \approx \mathbf{600 \, MPa}$ of the forged steel. A static design would conclude the part is *over-engineered by a factor of 3*. In reality, the rod fails after ~ $10^{7}$ reversals because of:
- Microscopic slip-band extrusion at the grain boundaries,
- Sub-surface inclusions acting as crack nuclei,
- Mean tensile stress from the bolt pre-load.

The fatigue formulae convert this *time-varying* damage into a *single equivalent static safety factor* $n_f$ — making the design tractable for the KTU ESE numerical.

**Real-world automotive components governed by these equations:**
- Crankshaft (rotating bending) — $R = -1$ dominant.
- Valve spring (repeated) — $R = 0$ dominant.
- Connecting-rod big-end bolt (fluctuating tensile) — $R \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$.
- Suspension coil spring (fluctuating) — $R \approx 0.05$ to $0.2$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Deriving $S_m$, $S_a$, $S_r$, and $R$ from $S_{max}$ and $S_{min}$

> **Given:** A shaft is subjected to a bending moment that varies sinusoidally between $M_{min} = -1200 \, \text{N·m}$ and $M_{max} = +3000 \, \text{N·m}$. The shaft diameter is $d = 60 \, \text{mm}$, and the bending section modulus is $Z = \tfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$. Determine all six fatigue parameters.

**Step 1 — Convert moments to bending stresses.**

For a circular shaft under pure bending, the bending stress is $\sigma = M/Z$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{32 \, M_{max}}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{32 \times 3000 \times 10^{3} \, \text{N·mm}}{\pi \times (60)^{3} \, \text{mm}^{3}} \\
            &= \frac{96\,000\,000}{\pi \times 216\,000} = \frac{96\,000\,000}{678\,584} \approx 141.47 \, \text{MPa} = S_{max} \\[4pt]
\sigma_{min} &= \frac{32 \times (-1200) \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times (60)^{3}} \approx -56.59 \, \text{MPa} = S_{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean stress $S_m$ (the DC offset).**

The mean stress is the *arithmetic average* of the algebraic maximum and minimum — this is the part of the stress that *never* crosses zero.

$$
S_m = \frac{S_{max} + S_{min}}{2} = \frac{141.47 + (-56.59)}{2} = \frac{84.88}{2} = 42.44 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Interpretation:** A positive mean of $42.44$ MPa means the material is *always* in tension — the cyclic tension-compression is asymmetric. There is a permanent tensile bias.

**Step 3 — Compute the stress amplitude $S_a$ (the AC component).**

The amplitude is half the *algebraic* difference between the two extremes:

$$
S_a = \frac{S_{max} - S_{min}}{2} = \frac{141.47 - (-56.59)}{2} = \frac{198.06}{2} = 99.03 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *purely reversing* component. If we ran this stress on a rotating-beam machine, the machine would record $S_a = 99.03$ MPa.

**Step 4 — Compute the stress range $S_r$.**

The range is the total span of the stress excursion. By definition, $S_r = 2 S_a$:

$$
S_r = S_{max} - S_{min} = 141.47 - (-56.59) = 198.06 \, \text{MPa} = 2 \times 99.03 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the stress ratio $R$.**

The ratio uniquely defines the *type* of cycle. Here $R$ is negative, confirming the cycle crosses zero (mixed tension/compression):

$$
R = \frac{S_{min}}{S_{max}} = \frac{-56.59}{141.47} = -0.40
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the cycle reconstruction.**

The instantaneous stress $\sigma(t)$ should match the original cycle. With $S_m = 42.44$, $S_a = 99.03$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max}^{\text{check}} &= S_m + S_a = 42.44 + 99.03 = 141.47 \, \text{MPa} \quad \checkmark \\
S_{min}^{\text{check}} &= S_m - S_a = 42.44 - 99.03 = -56.59 \, \text{MPa} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $S_{max} = 141.47$ MPa, $S_{min} = -56.59$ MPa, $S_m = 42.44$ MPa, $S_a = 99.03$ MPa, $S_r = 198.06$ MPa, $R = -0.40$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Goodman Failure Line Derivation

> **Given:** A component has an alternating stress $S_a$ and mean stress $S_m$. Using a safety factor $n$, derive the Goodman criterion in the form routinely tested in KTU ESE.

**Step 1 — Define the safety factor as a *linear scale* between operating point and failure envelope.**

In the $S_a$–$S_m$ plane, the failure envelope is a straight line from $(0, S_e)$ on the alternating axis to $(S_{ut}, 0)$ on the mean axis. Any point $(S_a, S_m)$ on this line represents *incipient* failure. The operating point $(S_a, S_m)$ is *not* on the line; the factor of safety is the *ratio* of the *radial distance* of the failure line to the operating point, both measured from the origin.

**Step 2 — Equation of the failure line.**

Using the two intercepts $A = (0, S_e)$ and $B = (S_{ut}, 0)$:

$$
\frac{S_a}{S_e} + \frac{S_m}{S_{ut}} = 1 \quad \text{(failure locus, $n=1$)}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce a uniform safety factor $n$.**

Following Shigley, scaling the load point to reach the failure line at factor $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_a &\mapsto n S_a \quad \text{and} \quad S_m \mapsto n S_m
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into the failure equation:

$$
\frac{n S_a}{S_e} + \frac{n S_m}{S_{ut}} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the safety factor $n$.**

$$
n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{S_m}{S_{ut}}}
$$

> **Result (Goodman Criterion):** $\displaystyle n_f = \left[\frac{S_a}{S_e} + \frac{S_m}{S_{ut}}\right]^{-1}$

**Interpretation:** If $n_f \ge n_{specified}$ (typically $1.5$ to $2.5$ for automotive components), the design is *safe*. Otherwise, the geometry or material must be revised.

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Endurance Limit for Rotating Beam (Wöhler Test)

The original Wöhler rotating-beam apparatus is a simply-supported shaft carrying a dead weight $W$ at the center, rotated at constant $\omega$. At any instant, a top fiber is in *tension*; a half-cycle later it is in *compression* of the same magnitude. Hence the test is a **completely reversed** stress cycle.

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment.** For a simply supported beam of span $L$ with a central point load $W$:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{W L}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum bending stress in a circular cross-section of diameter $d$.**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{W L / 4}{\pi d^{3} / 32} = \frac{8 W L}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Stress amplitude equals the maximum stress (because $S_m = 0$).**

$$
S_a = \sigma_{max} = \frac{8 W L}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Step 4 — For a given specimen geometry and load, vary $W$ and record $N_f$.**

The resulting tabulation $\{(W, N_f)\}$ is converted to $\{(S_a, N_f)\}$ via the above equation and plotted on a semi-log axis — the **S-N (Wöhler) curve**.

**Step 5 — The horizontal asymptote $N \to \infty$.**

For steels (and some titanium alloys), the curve flattens to a constant stress $S_e'$. Empirically, for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa:

$$
S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut} \quad \text{(Machining datum; uncorrected)}
$$

> **Result:** The Wöhler rotating-beam test directly yields $S_e'$ — the *uncorrected* endurance limit. To convert to a *real* design endurance limit $S_e$, multiply by the modifying factors from §2.3.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases of Stress Cycles

| $S_{min}$ / $S_{max}$ Combination | Cycle Type | $R$ | $S_m$ | $S_a$ | Automotive Example |
|-----------------------------------|------------|-----|-------|-------|--------------------|
| $S_{min} = -S_{max}$ | Completely Reversed | $-1$ | $0$ | $S_{max}$ | Rotating crankshaft under bending |
| $S_{min} = 0$ | Repeated (tension-pulse) | $0$ | $S_{max}/2$ | $S_{max}/2$ | Leaf spring in road load cycle |
| $S_{max} = 0$ | Repeated (compression-pulse) | $\infty$ | $S_{min}/2$ | $\vert S_{min}\vert / 2$ | Reversed column buckling |
| $0 < S_{min} < S_{max}$ | Fluctuating (tensile bias) | $0 < R < 1$ | $(S_{max}+S_{min})/2$ | $(S_{max}-S_{min})/2$ | Preloaded connecting-rod bolt |
| $S_{min} = S_{max} = S$ | Static (steady) | $+1$ | $S$ | $0$ | Hydraulic cylinder under constant pressure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fatigue Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Service Load History sigma-vs-t] --> A1[Extract Smax and Smin from Worst Cycle]
    A1 --> A2[Compute Sm and Sa and R and Sr]
    A2 --> A3[Select Material and Sut and Sy and Se]
    A3 --> A4[Apply Modifying Factors ka kb kc kd ke kf]
    A4 --> A5[Compute Corrected Endurance Limit Se]
    A5 --> A6[Apply Failure Criterion]
    A6 --> A7[Goodman Linear Conservative]
    A6 --> A8[Gerber Parabolic Best Fit Ductile]
    A6 --> A9[Soderberg Linear Most Conservative]
    A7 --> A10[Compute Fatigue Safety Factor nf]
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11{Is nf greater than n specified}
    A11 -->|Yes| A12[Design Safe against Fatigue]
    A11 -->|No| A13[Revise Geometry or Material]
    A13 --> A3
```

---

### 4.2 Classification of Cyclic Stress States

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Applied Cyclic Load] --> N2[Type of Stress Cycle]
    N2 --> N3[Completely Reversed]
    N2 --> N4[Repeated]
    N2 --> N5[Fluctuating]
    N3 --> N6[Smax equals negative Smin]
    N3 --> N7[R equals negative one]
    N3 --> N8[Sm equals zero]
    N3 --> N9[Example Rotating Crankshaft]
    N4 --> N10[Smin equals zero]
    N4 --> N11[R equals zero]
    N4 --> N12[Sm equals Smax divided by two]
    N4 --> N13[Example Valve Spring]
    N5 --> N14[Both Smax and Smin Same Sign Non Zero]
    N5 --> N15[R between zero and one]
    N5 --> N16[Example Preloaded Bolt]
    N6 --> O1[Use S-N Curve for Se]
    N10 --> O1
    N14 --> O1
    O1 --> O2[Apply Modifying Factors]
    O2 --> O3[Apply Goodman or Gerber or Soderberg]
    O3 --> O4[Compute Safety Factor nf]
```

---

### 4.3 S-N Curve Schematic (Wöhler Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid engine does not natively plot Cartesian curves. The following ASCII art reproduces the **S-N (Wöhler) diagram** for a typical medium-carbon steel (e.g., AISI 1045, $S_{ut} \approx 625$ MPa). Memorize the four regions.

```
  S_a (MPa)
  ^
  |       A   10^3 cycles            B  10^4
  |   * - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - 
  |        *
  |           *                     Low-Cycle
  |              *                  Fatigue
  |                 *                  (LCF)
  |                    *
  |                       *       C  10^6   D
  |                          *- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
  |                                       *- - - - - - - - -  Se
  |                                                           |
  |                                                           |   High-Cycle
  |                                                           |   Fatigue
  |                                                           |    (HCF)
  |                                                           |   S_a < Se
  |                                                           |    N --> infinity
  |                                                           v
  +------------------------------------------------------------> log N
       10^3    10^4    10^5    10^6    10^7    10^8    10^9
       
  Region A-B : Low-cycle fatigue (plastic strain dominant)
  Region B-C : Transition (Basquin's law: S_a = S_f prime times N to power b)
  Region C-D : Finite-life high-cycle fatigue
  Region D-> : Endurance regime, Sa = Se
```

**The Three Empirical Laws (KTU Favourite):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Basquin (HCF)} &: \quad S_a = \sigma_f' \left( N \right)^{b}, \quad b \approx -0.10 \\
\text{Coffin-Manson (LCF)} &: \quad \frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f' \left( N \right)^{c}, \quad c \approx -0.5 \\
\text{Strain-Life} &: \quad \frac{\Delta \varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\sigma_f'}{E}\left( N \right)^{b} + \varepsilon_f' \left( N \right)^{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\sigma_f'$ is the *fatigue strength coefficient* and $\varepsilon_f'$ is the *fatigue ductility coefficient*.

---

### 4.4 Mean-Stress Sensitivity — Failure Envelopes

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Origin O at 0 0] --> P1[Alternating Axis Sa]
    P0 --> P2[Mean Axis Sm]
    P1 --> P3[Point Sut on Sm Axis]
    P1 --> P4[Point Se on Sa Axis]
    P4 --> P5[Soderberg Line through Se and Sy]
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P6[Goodman Line through Se and Sut]
    P3 --> P6
    P4 --> P7[Gerber Parabola through Se and Sut]
    P3 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[All Pass Through Se on Sa Axis]
    P5 --> P8
    P6 --> P8
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A given operating point $(S_m, S_a)$ lies *inside* the failure envelope ⇒ **safe**; on the line ⇒ **incipient failure**; outside ⇒ **failure**. For the *same* operating point, Soderberg is the *most conservative* (smallest safe region), Goodman is *intermediate* (linear, most-used), Gerber is *least conservative* (largest safe region — but more accurate for ductile steels).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module-2, Part A]**
> *Distinguish clearly between **static loading** and **fatigue loading** in the context of machine design. Cite one example of an automotive component for each.*

**Model Answer (3-Mark Standard — 3 Key Points × 1 Mark):**

1. **Static loading** is time-invariant (or quasi-static), with the load applied once or held constant. Failure occurs by a single event — yielding or fracture — and is governed by $S_y$ or $S_{ut}$. *Example:* Hydraulic jack pressing a chassis during an impact, or the steady tension in a tow-hook bolt.
2. **Fatigue loading** is time-varying and cyclic; failure occurs *below* $S_{ut}$ after a finite number of cycles $N_f$ due to progressive localized damage. Failure is by crack initiation and propagation, with no macroscopic yielding.
3. *Example:* A rotating **crankshaft** of an IC engine — the bending stress reverses every half-revolution, leading to fatigue failure at the fillet between the crank-pin and web after millions of cycles.

---

> **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module-2, Part A]**
> *Define the **stress ratio $R$** and the **stress amplitude $S_a$** for a fluctuating load. What is the value of $R$ for (i) a completely reversed cycle, and (ii) a repeated tensile cycle?*

**Model Answer:**

$$
R = \frac{S_{min}}{S_{max}}, \qquad S_a = \frac{S_{max} - S_{min}}{2}
$$

- **(i) Completely Reversed Cycle:** $S_{max} = -S_{min}$, hence $R = S_{min}/S_{max} = -1$.
- **(ii) Repeated Tensile Cycle:** $S_{min} = 0$, hence $R = 0/S_{max} = 0$.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### ⏵ Question A (14 Marks) — *Mean-Stress / Goodman-Design Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module-2, Part B, Choice-1]**

A steel shaft of diameter $d = 50 \, \text{mm}$ transmits a fluctuating torque that varies linearly between $T_{min} = +200 \, \text{N·m}$ and $T_{max} = +1400 \, \text{N·m}$. The material is AISI 1045 CD steel with $S_{ut} = \mathbf{625 \, MPa}$ and $S_y = \mathbf{530 \, MPa}$. The surface is machined, the operating temperature is $50^\circ C$, and a reliability of $90\%$ is desired. The shaft rotates.

Compute:
- **(a)** [7 Marks] The mean and alternating shear stresses ($S_m$ and $S_a$).
- **(b)** [7 Marks] The corrected endurance limit $S_e$ and the factor of safety $n_f$ using the **Goodman criterion**.

*Take $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ (uncorrected). Modifying factors to be evaluated are $k_a$, $k_b$, $k_c$, $k_d$, $k_e$ as per Shigley's tables. Use $k_f = 1.0$.*

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Mean and Alternating Shear Stresses**

Step 1 — *Convert torque to shear stress.* For a solid circular shaft, the maximum shear stress is $\tau = 16 T / (\pi d^{3})$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{16 \times 1400 \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times (50)^{3}} = \frac{22\,400\,000}{\pi \times 125\,000} = \frac{22\,400\,000}{392\,699} \approx 57.04 \, \text{MPa} = S_{max} \\[4pt]
\tau_{min} &= \frac{16 \times 200 \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times (50)^{3}} = \frac{3\,200\,000}{392\,699} \approx 8.15 \, \text{MPa} = S_{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — *Mean shear stress.*

$$
S_m = \frac{S_{max}+S_{min}}{2} = \frac{57.04+8.15}{2} = \frac{65.19}{2} = 32.60 \, \text{MPa}
$$

Step 3 — *Alternating shear stress.*

$$
S_a = \frac{S_{max}-S_{min}}{2} = \frac{57.04-8.15}{2} = \frac{48.89}{2} = 24.45 \, \text{MPa}
$$

Step 4 — *Stress ratio.*

$$
R = \frac{S_{min}}{S_{max}} = \frac{8.15}{57.04} \approx 0.143
$$

> **Part (a) Answer:** $S_{max} = 57.04$ MPa, $S_{min} = 8.15$ MPa, $S_m = 32.60$ MPa, $S_a = 24.45$ MPa, $R \approx 0.143$.

---

**Part (b) — Corrected Endurance Limit and Goodman Factor of Safety**

Step 1 — *Uncorrected endurance limit for bending.* For steels with $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa:

$$
S_e' = 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 625 = 312.5 \, \text{MPa}
$$

Step 2 — *Surface factor $k_a$ (machined surface, $S_{ut} = 625$ MPa).*
From Shigley Table: $a = 4.51$ (MPa), $b = -0.265$ for machined surface.

$$
k_a = a \, S_{ut}^{b} = 4.51 \times (625)^{-0.265}
$$

Computing $(625)^{-0.265} = e^{-0.265 \ln 625} = e^{-0.265 \times 6.4378} = e^{-1.706} \approx 0.1816$

$$
k_a = 4.51 \times 0.1816 \approx 0.819
$$

Step 3 — *Size factor $k_b$ for torsion with $d = 50$ mm.*
For torsion, $k_b = 0.91 \, d^{-0.157}$:

$$
k_b = 0.91 \times (50)^{-0.157} = 0.91 \times e^{-0.157 \times 3.912} = 0.91 \times e^{-0.614} = 0.91 \times 0.541 \approx 0.492
$$

Step 4 — *Load factor $k_c$.* For torsion, $k_c = 0.59$:

$$
k_c = 0.59
$$

Step 5 — *Temperature factor $k_d$.* At $50^\circ C$, from Shigley table, $k_d \approx 1.00$ (negligible degradation below $100^\circ C$):

$$
k_d = 1.00
$$

Step 6 — *Reliability factor $k_e$.* For 90% reliability, $k_e = 0.897$:

$$
k_e = 0.897
$$

Step 7 — *Miscellaneous factor $k_f = 1.0$* (assumed).

Step 8 — *Corrected endurance limit*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_e &= k_a \times k_b \times k_c \times k_d \times k_e \times k_f \times S_e' \\
    &= 0.819 \times 0.492 \times 0.59 \times 1.00 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 312.5 \\
    &= 0.819 \times 0.492 = 0.4030 \\
    &= 0.4030 \times 0.59 = 0.2378 \\
    &= 0.2378 \times 1.00 = 0.2378 \\
    &= 0.2378 \times 0.897 = 0.2133 \\
    &= 0.2133 \times 312.5 \approx 66.65 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 9 — *Goodman safety factor.* Note: Goodman in *shear* uses $S_{se}$ (already in shear) and $S_{su}$ ≈ $0.67 \, S_{ut}$:

$$
S_{su} \approx 0.67 \times 625 = 418.75 \, \text{MPa}
$$

$$
n_f = \left[ \frac{S_a}{S_e} + \frac{S_m}{S_{su}} \right]^{-1} = \left[ \frac{24.45}{66.65} + \frac{32.60}{418.75} \right]^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{24.45}{66.65} = 0.3668, \qquad \frac{32.60}{418.75} = 0.0778
$$

$$
n_f = (0.3668 + 0.0778)^{-1} = (0.4446)^{-1} \approx 2.25
$$

> **Part (b) Answer:** $S_e \approx 66.65$ MPa, $n_f \approx 2.25$. Since $2.25 \ge 1.5$ (typical KTU specified FoS), the shaft is **safe in fatigue** under the Goodman criterion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls in Part-b Numerical):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert torque to stress.** Many students write $S_{max} = T_{max} = 1400$ — losing 1 mark immediately. Always show $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$.
> 2. **Using $S_e'$ instead of $S_e$ in the Goodman equation.** $S_e'$ is the *un-rotating-beam* baseline. For real components, you **must** apply $k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f$. Examiners will deduct 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing units.** Torque is given in N·m; convert to N·mm (multiply by $10^3$) *before* substituting in $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$ with $d$ in mm.
> 4. **Wrong load factor.** For *torsion*, $k_c = 0.59$, **not** $1.0$ (which is for bending). This is a high-frequency mistake.
> 5. **Treating the result as a static safety factor.** The number $n_f \approx 2.25$ is the *fatigue* FoS; compare it with the KTU-specified $n$ for *cyclic* loading, not with the static yield criterion.

---

#### ⏵ Question B (14 Marks) — *Theoretical + S-N Curve Interpretation*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module-2, Part B, Choice-2]**

- **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, describe the **S-N (Wöhler) curve** for a medium-carbon steel. Mark clearly the **endurance limit**, the **fatigue strength at $N = 10^{3}$ cycles**, and the regions of **low-cycle fatigue** and **high-cycle fatigue**. State the empirical relation between $S_{ut}$ and the uncorrected endurance limit $S_e'$.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] A component experiences a **completely reversed** stress cycle with amplitude $S_a = 180 \, \text{MPa}$. The material has $S_{ut} = 600 \, \text{MPa}$, $S_y = 420 \, \text{MPa}$, and the modified endurance limit is $S_e = 210 \, \text{MPa}$. Compute the factor of safety against (i) **yielding**, (ii) **fatigue** (Goodman), and (iii) **first-cycle (static) overload**. State which mode governs the design.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — S-N Curve Description**

*Sketch (describe the four regions; the diagram is similar to §4.3 of these notes):*

The S-N curve is a plot of alternating stress amplitude $S_a$ (linear ordinate) versus the number of cycles to failure $N$ (log abscissa) for a **completely reversed** stress cycle. It has four distinct regions:

- **Region 1 — Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF):** $N \approx 10^{1}$ to $10^{3}$. Plastic strain dominates. The curve drops steeply; stress is near $S_{ut}$.
- **Region 2 — Finite-Life Region:** $N \approx 10^{3}$ to $10^{6}$. Cyclic plastic strain at slip bands. The curve obeys **Basquin's law**: $S_a = \sigma_f' \, N^{b}$, with $b \approx -0.10$.
- **Region 3 — Knee / Transition:** $N \approx 10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$. The curve bends towards horizontal.
- **Region 4 — Infinite-Life / Endurance Plateau:** $N \to \infty$. For steels, the curve flattens at $S_a = S_e'$ — the **endurance limit**.

$$
\boxed{S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut} \quad \text{for } S_{ut} \le 1400 \, \text{MPa (steels)}}
$$

For non-ferrous metals (aluminium, copper), no true endurance limit exists — the curve continues to drop slowly to infinite $N$, and a *fatigue strength* at a chosen $N$ (commonly $5 \times 10^{8}$) is used instead.

**Fatigue Strength at $N = 10^{3}$:** $S_{f(10^{3})}$ is read directly from the S-N curve at the abscissa $N = 10^{3}$ — it is close to $S_{ut}$ (typically $0.9 \, S_{ut}$ for steels).

---

**Part (b) — Three Failure Modes, One Component**

**Given:** $S_a = 180$ MPa, $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_y = 420$ MPa, $S_e = 210$ MPa. Completely reversed ⇒ $S_m = 0$, $S_{max} = +S_a = 180$ MPa, $S_{min} = -180$ MPa.

**(i) Factor of safety against yielding.**

The maximum cyclic stress is $S_{max} = S_a + S_m = 180 + 0 = 180$ MPa.

$$
n_y = \frac{S_y}{S_{max}} = \frac{420}{180} = 2.333
$$

**(ii) Factor of safety against fatigue (Goodman).**

Goodman for completely reversed cycle ($S_m = 0$):

$$
n_f = \left[ \frac{S_a}{S_e} + \frac{S_m}{S_{ut}} \right]^{-1} = \left[ \frac{180}{210} + 0 \right]^{-1} = \left[ 0.857 \right]^{-1} = 1.167
$$

**(iii) Factor of safety against first-cycle overload (static ultimate).**

Conservative interpretation — compare the maximum stress to $S_{ut}$:

$$
n_{ut} = \frac{S_{ut}}{S_{max}} = \frac{600}{180} = 3.333
$$

**Governing mode:** The **lowest** of $n_y = 2.333$, $n_f = 1.167$, and $n_{ut} = 3.333$ governs the design. Therefore, **fatigue governs**, with $n_{design} = 1.167$. The component has ample static strength but inadequate *cyclic* strength.

> **Design verdict:** If the KTU-specified safety factor is $n_{spec} = 1.5$, the design **fails** and must be revised (e.g., reduce $S_a$ by increasing the cross-section, or select a material with $S_e > 180 \times 1.5 = 270$ MPa).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls in Part-b Theoretical):**
> 1. **Drawing the S-N curve on linear (not semi-log) axes.** Always use a *log* abscissa for $N$ — the asymptote only appears as a horizontal line in a semi-log plot.
> 2. **Confusing $S_e$ with $S_e'$.** $S_e'$ is the *uncorrected* baseline (rotating-beam data). $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \cdot S_e'$ is the *corrected* design value. Examiners *will* test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting $S_m = 0$ in completely reversed case.** In $S_{max}/S_{min}$ the algebra gives $S_m = 0$; this collapses the Goodman equation to $n_f = S_e / S_a$. Many students write the full Goodman form and *also* plug in $S_m = 180$, losing marks.
> 4. **Saying "fatigue is the same as static."** Examiners expect explicit identification of which mode *governs* (lowest FoS). State the mode and the corresponding $n$ in the final line.

---

### 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Board-Exam-Ready):**

- ☐ **Static loading** is time-invariant; **fatigue loading** is time-varying. Static failure uses $S_y$ or $S_{ut}$; fatigue uses $S_e$.
- ☐ Always extract **two** of {$S_{max}, S_{min}, S_m, S_a, S_r, R$}; the other four are derived. Show *all six* in your answer.
- ☐ **Completely reversed** ⇒ $R = -1$, $S_m = 0$. **Repeated** ⇒ $R = 0$ (or $\infty$). **Static** ⇒ $R = +1$.
- ☐ $S_a = (S_{max} - S_{min})/2$ — *algebraic* difference, not absolute.
- ☐ $S_m = (S_{max} + S_{min})/2$ — *algebraic* sum, keeps sign.
- ☐ Uncorrected endurance limit: $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa (steels); $0.4 \, S_{ut}$ for cast iron.
- ☐ Corrected endurance limit: $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$. *Always* apply ALL six factors in KTU problems.
- ☐ **Surface factor $k_a$** depends on finish (ground, polished, machined, forged) — *largest* effect.
- ☐ **Size factor $k_b$** for bending $= 0.91 d^{-0.157}$; for torsion/axial $= 0.87 d^{-0.107}$ (different formulae).
- ☐ **Load factor $k_c$** — bending $= 1.0$; axial $= 0.85$; torsion $= 0.59$.
- ☐ **Goodman** is *linear* and conservative — *most-used* in KTU ESE.
- ☐ **Gerber** is *parabolic* — best-fit for ductile steels, but rarely used in exams.
- ☐ **Soderberg** uses $S_y$ instead of $S_{ut}$ — *most-conservative* (very safe design).
- ☐ The factor of safety equation is: $n_f = [S_a/S_e + S_m/S_{ut}]^{-1}$ for Goodman.
- ☐ For **completely reversed** cycle, Goodman collapses to $n_f = S_e / S_a$.
- ☐ Governing failure mode is the **lowest** of $\{$yielding, fatigue, ultimate$\}$.
- ☐ Automotive examples: **crankshaft** (reversed bending, $R = -1$), **valve spring** (repeated, $R = 0$), **connecting-rod bolt** (fluctuating tensile, $0 < R < 1$).
- ☐ S-N curve is plotted on **semi-log** paper; horizontal asymptote is $S_e$.
- ☐ ~80% of all mechanical-service failures in automobiles are *fatigue* failures.
- ☐ Use **SI units consistently**: stresses in MPa (= N/mm²), torques in N·mm, lengths in mm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMUT601 – Design of Automotive Components)**, **Surging** is formally defined as a critical, self-excited **aerodynamic instability** that occurs in rotary positive-displacement and turbomachinery devices — most importantly in the **centrifugal superchargers, turbochargers, and Roots-type blowers** used for engine forced induction.

Surging is the phenomenon in which the mass flow of air delivered by the compressor momentarily **reverses direction (back-flow)** and then **re-attaches** in a violent, low-frequency cyclic oscillation, even though the compressor shaft is rotating at a steady speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *“Surging is a self-induced, low-frequency pulsating flow instability in a compressor, occurring at flow rates below the surge limit, characterized by momentary reverse flow (back-flow), violent axial thrust reversals, loud banging noise, and severe mechanical vibration, which can lead to bearing failure if not controlled.”*

### 1.2 The Three Engineering Systems Where Surging is Discussed in PCMUT601

| # | System | Type of Surging |
|---|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | Turbocharger / Centrifugal Supercharger | Aerodynamic compressor surge |
| 2 | Roots Blower / Lobe Supercharger | Discharge-pulse surging |
| 3 | Automatic Transmission (Torque Converter) | Converter surging / coupling shudder |

For Module 2 of **Design of Automotive Components**, the **primary focus is the aerodynamic surge in centrifugal compressors**, and the secondary focus is **torque-converter surging** in automatic transmission design.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — “The Collapsed Straw”

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (The Drinking-Straw Test):**
> Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake through a thin straw. If you **suck too gently**, the milkshake just sits there. If you **suck far too hard** (very high pressure), the straw **collapses** momentarily, no liquid flows, then it re-opens with a sudden “glug-glug” — flow happens in **reverse-then-forward bursts** instead of a steady stream.
>
> - **You (the drinker)** = the engine demanding a specific pressure
> - **The straw** = the compressor impeller passage
> - **The milkshake** = the air charge
> - **The “glug-glug”** = **SURGE**
>
> Just like the straw, when the compressor tries to deliver a pressure that is **too high for the available flow rate**, the flow inside the impeller “stalls” momentarily, reverses, and slams forward again.

A second analogy: think of a **garden hose held partially closed at the nozzle**. If you turn the tap full on while pinching the nozzle shut, the hose vibrates, jerks, and a pulsating jet emerges. The hose is **surging**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Surge Line:** a near-vertical line on the compressor map that separates the **stable operating region** (right) from the **unstable surge region** (left).
- **Surge Margin (SM):** typically **≥ 10 %** for automotive turbochargers; **≥ 20 %** for industrial compressors.
- **Surge Frequency:** 2 – 10 Hz (low-frequency oscillation).
- **Surge Noise Level:** can reach **120 dB(A)** in severe surge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The surge line is **NOT a single value** — it is a **boundary curve** on the compressor performance map. Any operating point that crosses to the left of this line causes the compressor to enter surge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Surging Occur? — The Underlying Physics

A centrifugal compressor impeller does work on the air by accelerating it radially outward. This work is converted to a **static pressure rise** (ΔP) in the diffuser/volute. The compressor can be visualized as a **flow-restricting device** that tries to maintain a certain pressure ratio (P₂ / P₁) at a given mass flow (ṁ) and speed (N).

The system, however, includes the **engine (or downstream plenum)** that acts as a **variable back-pressure**. Whenever the back-pressure demanded by the engine **exceeds** what the compressor can stably deliver at the prevailing mass flow and speed, the flow cannot proceed forward — it **stalls**, then **reverses**, relieving the back-pressure, then the cycle repeats.

### 2.2 The Four-Stage Surge Cycle

Surging is best understood as a **closed feedback loop** that repeats continuously:

| Stage | Event | Physical Description |
|-------|-------|---------------------|
| **1** | **Pressure Build-Up** | Compressor delivers air into the discharge plenum; pressure rises. |
| **2** | **Critical Pressure Exceeded** | Discharge pressure surpasses the stable-flow capability of the impeller passage. |
| **3** | **Flow Reversal (Back-Flow)** | The high-pressure air in the plenum rushes backward through the diffuser and impeller. |
| **4** | **Re-Build** | Back-flow empties the plenum; forward flow resumes; the cycle restarts. |

The **time-period** of one full surge cycle (Stage 1 → Stage 4) is the **surge period (Tₛ)**, typically **0.1 – 0.5 seconds** (2 – 10 Hz).

### 2.3 The Compressor Map and the Surge Line

The **compressor performance map** is a plot of:

- **X-axis:** Corrected mass flow rate, $\dot{m}_{corr} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \sqrt{T_1/P_1}}{\text{const}}$ — units often $\text{kg/s} \cdot \sqrt{K/Pa}$
- **Y-axis:** Pressure ratio, $\Pi = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ (dimensionless)
- **Family of curves:** Corrected speed lines, $N_{corr} = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{T_1}}$ — units often $\text{rpm}/\sqrt{K}$

The **surge line** is the **left boundary** of the stable region. To its **left** = **surge**. To its **right** = **stable operation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three other key boundaries on the compressor map:**
> 1. **Choke (Stone-Wall) Line:** right vertical boundary — mass flow cannot increase further because the impeller throat reaches sonic velocity (Mach = 1).
> 2. **Maximum Efficiency Island:** the closed curved region of peak isentropic efficiency.
> 3. **Speed Limit Line:** upper envelope — mechanical strength limit of the impeller.

### 2.4 Effect of Surging on the Compressor Bearings — Why KTU Asks This

The most severe **mechanical** consequence of surge is the **reversal of axial thrust**.

In normal forward flow, the **pressure difference across the impeller** generates an axial thrust in the **direction of air inlet** (pulling the shaft toward the compressor inlet). This thrust is taken by the **thrust bearing**.

During surge, the **pressure equalizes and reverses**, causing:
- The axial thrust to **oscillate rapidly** between two extreme values.
- **Thrust-bearing loading cycles** that fatigue the babbitt metal and cause catastrophic failure.
- This is why a **thrust collar / balance drum / thrust bearing** is a mandatory design element in any turbocharger — to absorb the **time-averaged** thrust, but surge-induced cyclic loading still degrades it.

### 2.5 Effects Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effects of Surging (Board-Exam List):**
> 1. **Violent axial-thrust reversals** → bearing failure.
> 2. **Loud low-frequency noise** (110 – 120 dB).
> 3. **Severe vibration** of the entire turbocharger assembly.
> 4. **Loss of boost pressure** → engine power drop.
> 5. **Over-heating** of the impeller and volute (no convective cooling during back-flow).
> 6. **Reduced compressor efficiency** (efficiency falls off the surge line rapidly).
> 7. **Cavitation-like damage** on the impeller blades (rotating stall precedes surge).

### 2.6 The Three Regions on the Compressor Map

```
   P₂/P₁ ↑
        |              (B) STABLE REGION
        |             ┌───────────────────
        |             │  Max-Efficiency ●
        |             │     Island         │
        |  (A)        │                    │
        |  SURGE      │                    │
        |  REGION     │                    │
        |             └───────────────────
        |             (C) CHOKE / STONE-WALL
        └─────────────────────────────────→  ṁ_corr
              ▲ Surge Line    ▲ Choke Line
```

- **Region A (Surge Region):** Left of the surge line — forbidden.
- **Region B (Stable Operating Region):** Where all real engines operate.
- **Region C (Choke Region):** Right of the choke line — mass-flow saturated.

### 2.7 Anti-Surge Design Solutions

| # | Method | Working Principle |
|---|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Anti-surge (Blow-off) Valve** | Vents excess pressurized air to atmosphere when pressure ratio threatens surge. |
| 2 | **Wastegate on the Turbine** | Opens to bypass exhaust gases, reducing turbine power and hence compressor speed. |
| 3 | **Variable Geometry Turbine (VGT)** | Adjusts turbine inlet area to keep compressor away from surge line. |
| 4 | **Surge Margin Protection in ECU** | Real-time MAP sensor feedback trims throttle to prevent surge. |
| 5 | **Plenum Volume Optimization** | Larger plenum dampens pressure pulsations. |

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — Surging & Compressor Performance**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Pressure Ratio** | $\Pi = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | dimensionless | Across compressor |
| 2 | **Isentropic Temperature Ratio** | $\dfrac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | dimensionless | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air |
| 3 | **Isentropic Work** | $W_s = c_{p} (T_{2s} - T_1)$ | J/kg | |
| 4 | **Actual Work (Euler Equation)** | $W = U_2 V_{w2}$ | J/kg | Whirl velocity component |
| 5 | **Isentropic Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_s}{W} = \dfrac{c_p (T_{2s}-T_1)}{U_2 V_{w2}}$ | dimensionless | |
| 6 | **Surge Margin (SM)** | $SM = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{design} - \dot{m}_{surge}}{\dot{m}_{design}} \times 100\%$ | % | Automotive: SM $\geq 10\%$ |
| 7 | **Slip Factor (Stodola)** | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{\pi}{z} \cdot \dfrac{\sin \beta_2}{1}$ | dimensionless | $z$ = blades, $\beta_2$ = exit blade angle |
| 8 | **Head Developed (Compressible)** | $\psi = \dfrac{c_p T_1 \left[(\Pi)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]}{U_2^2}$ | dimensionless | Head coefficient |
| 9 | **Corrected Mass Flow** | $\dot{m}_{corr} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \sqrt{T_1 / T_{ref}}}{\dfrac{P_1}{P_{ref}}}$ | kg/s | Used on compressor map |
| 10 | **Surge Period** | $T_s = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L_p \cdot A_p}{\gamma P_1}}$ | s | Plenum length $L_p$, area $A_p$ |

> **Critical Markdown Note:** All absolute-value bars in the table above are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command to preserve markdown table syntax.

### 2.9 Real-World Automotive Utility

Surging analysis is **not academic** — it is a **mandatory design verification step** for:

- **Passenger-car turbochargers** (e.g., Garrett, BorgWarner, IHI) — surge maps are required by OEMs.
- **Diesel engine turbocharging** — diesel engines have a wide operating range and are highly surge-prone at low load.
- **Formula-1 MGU-K / ERS turbo units** — the centrifugal compressor must never enter surge even at peak transient boost.
- **CVT / Torque-Converter Automobiles** — converter surging is the cause of the “judder” defect in many 1990s–2000s automatics.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Isentropic Pressure–Temperature Relationship of the Compressor

**Starting Principle:** Air is treated as a **calorically perfect gas** ($\gamma$ = constant) undergoing an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression from inlet state $(P_1, T_1)$ to outlet state $(P_2, T_{2s})$.

**Step 1.** Write the first law for a steady-flow adiabatic process (no heat transfer, $q = 0$):

$$
h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_{2s} + \frac{V_{2s}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2.** For an isentropic process in a perfect gas, the isentropic relation is:

$$
\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $h = c_p T$:

$$
c_p T_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = c_p T_{2s} + \frac{V_{2s}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4.** Solving for $T_{2s} - T_1$:

$$
T_{2s} - T_1 = \frac{V_1^2 - V_{2s}^2}{2 c_p}
$$

**Step 5.** Therefore the **specific isentropic work** required by an ideal compressor is:

$$
W_s = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1) = \frac{V_1^2 - V_{2s}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 6.** Expressing $W_s$ purely as a function of pressure ratio (assuming inlet and outlet velocities are small in a static analysis):

$$
W_s = c_p T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]
$$

**Conclusion:** This is the **minimum (ideal) work** required to compress air from $(P_1, T_1)$ to $(P_2, ?)$. Real compressor work is always higher due to irreversibilities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Euler Turbomachinery Equation Applied to a Centrifugal Compressor

**Starting Principle:** The angular-momentum balance on a control volume enclosing the impeller gives the Euler work equation for turbomachinery.

**Step 1.** Consider a rotating reference frame; the impeller is rotating with tangential blade speed $U_2$ at the impeller exit radius $r_2$.

**Step 2.** The Euler work transfer per unit mass is:

$$
W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}
$$

**Step 3.** At the **inlet** of a centrifugal compressor, the air enters axially with **no pre-swirl**, so $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$
W = U_2 V_{w2}
$$

**Step 4.** Introduce the **slip factor** $\sigma$ (Stodola’s approximation) to account for the fact that the relative air velocity does not exactly follow the blade angle due to the finite number of blades:

$$
V_{w2} = \sigma \cdot (U_2 - V_{r2} \cot \beta_2)
$$

**Step 5.** The **Euler head actually developed** by the impeller is therefore:

$$
W = U_2 \cdot \sigma \cdot (U_2 - V_{r2} \cot \beta_2) = \sigma U_2^2 - \sigma U_2 V_{r2} \cot \beta_2
$$

**Step 6.** Dividing by $U_2^2$ gives the **non-dimensional head coefficient** $\psi$:

$$
\psi = \sigma - \sigma \frac{V_{r2}}{U_2} \cot \beta_2
$$

**Step 7.** Combining with the isentropic work from §3.1 and the head coefficient:

$$
\psi = \frac{c_p T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]}{U_2^2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The head coefficient $\psi$ is a function of the **blade geometry** ($\sigma$, $\beta_2$) and the **operating condition** (pressure ratio, inlet temperature). At a fixed speed, as the mass flow $\dot{m}$ decreases, $V_{r2}$ decreases, and $\psi$ **rises**. The surge limit occurs where the head curve becomes **vertical** — the slope $\dfrac{d\psi}{d\dot{m}} = 0$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Surge Margin (SM) — Board-Favourite 7-Mark Question

**Step 1.** The **surge margin** is defined as the relative difference between the **design operating mass flow** and the **mass flow at the surge line**, both evaluated at the same corrected speed.

**Step 2.** Mathematical statement:

$$
SM = \frac{\dot{m}_{design} - \dot{m}_{surge}}{\dot{m}_{design}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3.** Substituting typical KTU numerical values — let the **design point** be at $\dot{m}_{design} = 0.30 \text{ kg/s}$ and the **surge point** on the same speed line be at $\dot{m}_{surge} = 0.25 \text{ kg/s}$:

$$
SM = \frac{0.30 - 0.25}{0.30} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4.** Numerator:

$$
0.30 - 0.25 = 0.05 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5.** Division:

$$
\frac{0.05}{0.30} = 0.1667
$$

**Step 6.** Conversion to percentage:

$$
SM = 16.67\% \approx 16.7\%
$$

**Step 7.** Interpretation:

> Since $SM = 16.7\% \geq 10\%$ (automotive minimum), the compressor is **safe from surge** at the design point.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The Surge Period (Acoustic Resonance Model)

A simplified lumped-parameter model of the plenum volume $V_p$ connected to the compressor by a duct of length $L_p$ and area $A_p$ gives the surge oscillation period as:

**Step 1.** Acoustic compliance of the plenum:

$$
C = \frac{V_p}{\gamma P_1}
$$

**Step 2.** Acoustic inertance of the connecting duct:

$$
L = \frac{\rho_1 L_p}{A_p}
$$

**Step 3.** The Helmholtz resonance period:

$$
T_s = 2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C} = 2\pi \sqrt{ \frac{\rho_1 L_p}{A_p} \cdot \frac{V_p}{\gamma P_1} }
$$

**Step 4.** Using the ideal-gas identity $\rho_1 = \dfrac{P_1}{R T_1}$:

$$
T_s = 2\pi \sqrt{ \frac{L_p V_p}{\gamma A_p R T_1} }
$$

**Step 5.** Numerical example — let $L_p = 0.20 \text{ m}, \; V_p = 0.002 \text{ m}^3, \; A_p = 0.005 \text{ m}^2, \; T_1 = 320 \text{ K}, \; \gamma = 1.4, \; R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$:

$$
T_s = 2\pi \sqrt{ \frac{0.20 \times 0.002}{1.4 \times 0.005 \times 287 \times 320} }
$$

**Step 6.** Numerator of the radical:

$$
0.20 \times 0.002 = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 7.** Denominator of the radical:

$$
1.4 \times 0.005 \times 287 \times 320 = 1.4 \times 0.005 \times 91840 = 642.88
$$

**Step 8.** Quotient inside the radical:

$$
\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-4}}{642.88} = 6.222 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 9.** Square root:

$$
\sqrt{6.222 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.888 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 10.** Multiplication by $2\pi$:

$$
T_s = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 7.888 \times 10^{-4} = 4.956 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 11.** Surge frequency:

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{1}{4.956 \times 10^{-3}} = 201.8 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Conclusion:** The lumped Helmholtz model gives a frequency of ~**200 Hz** for a small automotive plenum. Actual surge in production turbochargers is in the **2 – 10 Hz** range because real systems have a long distributed inlet tract and the air column resonance is much lower — the lumped model is the conceptual starting point only.

---

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeB1["ENGINE THROTTLE\nDriver demand signal"]

```

> **System logic:** A real-time ECU monitors the boost pressure. The instant the operating point threatens to cross the surge line, **two parallel corrective actions** fire: (1) the **wastegate opens** to slow the turbine, lowering compressor speed and shifting the speed line down; (2) the **blow-off valve** vents excess plenum pressure, allowing the operating point to slide **rightward** into the safe zone.
