# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define surging in a centrifugal supercharger. List any four effects of surging on the compressor.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Surging is a self-induced aerodynamic instability that occurs in a centrifugal compressor when the mass flow of air demanded by the engine falls below the **surge-limit** value at the prevailing speed. It is characterized by **momentary back-flow** of air from the discharge plenum into the impeller, followed by violent re-start of forward flow, producing a low-frequency cyclic oscillation of 2 – 10 Hz.

**Effects (any 4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**
1. Rapid reversal of axial thrust → **thrust bearing failure**.
2. Loud low-frequency noise (~120 dB).
3. Severe vibration of the compressor assembly.
4. Loss of boost pressure → engine power drop.
5. Local overheating of impeller blades (no convective cooling during back-flow).
6. Reduction in isentropic efficiency.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: What is a surge line on a compressor performance map? Why is it important for automotive turbocharger design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Surge Line Definition (1.5 marks):**
The surge line is a near-vertical curve plotted on the **pressure ratio (P₂/P₁) versus corrected mass flow (ṁ_corr)** compressor map, marking the **left boundary** of the stable operating region. For every corrected speed line, the surge line gives the minimum mass flow at which the compressor can deliver the corresponding pressure ratio without instability.

**Importance (1.5 marks):**
1. It defines the **forbidden zone** of operation; the engine calibration must keep the operating point always to the **right** of the surge line.
2. The **surge margin** is calculated from it, providing a **quantitative safety criterion** (≥ 10 % for automotive).
3. It dictates the design of the **wastegate / blow-off valve** opening threshold.
4. It is used to design the **Variable Geometry Turbine (VGT)** vanes’ closed-position limit.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of **surging** in a centrifugal supercharger. Discuss its causes and effects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal compressor has a design mass flow of **0.35 kg/s** at a corrected speed of **80 000 rpm/√K**. At the same speed, the surge-line mass flow is **0.28 kg/s**. Calculate the **surge margin** and state whether the compressor is safe for automotive use. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Sketch (2 marks):** Show the compressor map with three regions — surge (left), stable (middle), choke (right) — and the surge line as a near-vertical red boundary. Mark the operating point drifting leftward.

**Explanation (3 marks):**
Surging is a self-excited flow instability. When the engine throttle closes rapidly, the engine demands less air, so the operating point on the compressor map drifts **leftward** along its constant-speed line. It crosses the **surge line** when the back-pressure from the engine plenum exceeds what the impeller can stably deliver at the prevailing low mass flow. The flow **stalls**, **reverses**, the plenum empties, and the cycle repeats at low frequency (2 – 10 Hz).

**Causes (1 mark):**
- Rapid throttle closure (engine demand drop).
- Excessive back-pressure from the engine.
- Operating at very low speed with high pressure ratio.

**Effects (1 mark):**
- Axial thrust reversal → bearing failure; noise; vibration; loss of boost; overheating; efficiency drop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\dot{m}_{design} = 0.35 \text{ kg/s}$, $\dot{m}_{surge} = 0.28 \text{ kg/s}$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the surge-margin formula:

$$
SM = \frac{\dot{m}_{design} - \dot{m}_{surge}}{\dot{m}_{design}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute:

$$
SM = \frac{0.35 - 0.28}{0.35} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Numerator:

$$
0.35 - 0.28 = 0.07 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Division:

$$
\frac{0.07}{0.35} = 0.20
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Percentage:

$$
SM = 20\%
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Comparison with automotive standard ($SM \geq 10\%$):

$$
SM_{calculated} = 20\% \;\geq\; SM_{required} = 10\%
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclusion: **The compressor has a surge margin of 20 % and is SAFE for automotive use** with a comfortable 10-percentage-point safety buffer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **compressor performance map** in detail. Mark the **surge line, choke line, and stable operating region** on it. Briefly discuss the methods used to prevent surging in an automotive turbocharger. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air enters a centrifugal compressor at **P₁ = 100 kPa**, **T₁ = 300 K**, and is compressed to **P₂ = 250 kPa**. Assuming an **isentropic efficiency of 75 %** and γ = 1.4, calculate the **(i)** isentropic exit temperature, **(ii)** actual exit temperature, and **(iii)** the specific work of compression. Take **c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Compressor map description (3 marks):**
A compressor performance map is a plot of **pressure ratio (P₂/P₁)** on the Y-axis versus **corrected mass flow (ṁ_corr)** on the X-axis. A family of **curved lines**, each corresponding to a constant **corrected speed (N_corr)**, sweeps from the surge line (left) to the choke line (right). The **maximum efficiency island** is the closed contour near the centre.

**Marking the three regions (2 marks):**
- **Surge line** — left boundary, near-vertical.
- **Choke line** — right boundary, near-vertical, mass flow saturated.
- **Stable operating region** — the central area enclosed by the surge and choke lines.

**Anti-surge methods (2 marks):**
1. **Wastegate** — opens to dump exhaust, reducing turbine speed → shifts speed line down.
2. **Blow-off valve** — vents pressurized air to atmosphere when boost threshold is reached.
3. **Variable Geometry Turbine (VGT)** — adjusts nozzle area for continuous surge avoidance.
4. **ECU-based surge protection** — closed-loop MAP sensor control.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $P_2 = 250 \text{ kPa}$, $\eta_c = 0.75$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Pressure ratio:

$$
\Pi = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Isentropic temperature ratio:

$$
\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \Pi^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = (2.5)^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}} = (2.5)^{0.2857}
$$

$$
(2.5)^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 2.5} = e^{0.2857 \times 0.9163} = e^{0.2618} = 1.2993
$$

**Step 3 (0.5 marks):** Isentropic exit temperature:

$$
T_{2s} = 1.2993 \times 300 = 389.8 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Isentropic work:

$$
W_s = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1) = 1.005 \times (389.8 - 300) = 1.005 \times 89.8 = 90.25 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Isentropic efficiency definition rearranged:

$$
\eta_c = \frac{W_s}{W_{actual}} \;\Rightarrow\; W_{actual} = \frac{W_s}{\eta_c} = \frac{90.25}{0.75} = 120.33 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Actual exit temperature:

$$
W_{actual} = c_p (T_2 - T_1) \;\Rightarrow\; T_2 = T_1 + \frac{W_{actual}}{c_p} = 300 + \frac{120.33}{1.005} = 300 + 119.73 = 419.73 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final results (boxed):

$$
\boxed{T_{2s} = 389.8 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 419.7 \text{ K}, \quad W_{actual} = 120.33 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on “Surging” Questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing “surge” instead of “surging”** — the question specifically asks about the *process*; use the **-ing** form throughout your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the cause-effect link.** Examiners award a mark for explicitly stating *“sudden throttle closure causes the operating point to cross the surge line, leading to back-flow.”* Many students write only the definition and skip the cause.
> 3. **Confusing surge line with choke line.** The surge line is on the **LEFT** (low mass flow); the choke line is on the **RIGHT** (high mass flow). A swapped diagram loses 2 marks immediately.
> 4. **In numerical problems, omitting units** — the final surge-margin value **must** carry the **%** unit or **0.5 mark** is deducted.
> 5. **Forgetting the safety threshold** — simply calculating SM = 16.7 % is **not enough**; you must **compare** it with the automotive standard of ≥ 10 % and **state the conclusion** (“safe” / “unsafe”).
> 6. **Missing the bearing-damage consequence** — out of all the effects, the *axial-thrust reversal → bearing failure* is the most-marks-carrying. Always list it first.
> 7. **Writing “surge occurs at high pressure ratio”** — incorrect. Surge occurs at **low mass flow** for a given pressure ratio and speed. The pressure ratio is moderate; the **flow is too low**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Glance Revision Block — Surging in Centrifugal Compressors**

- [x] **Surging** = self-excited, low-frequency (2 – 10 Hz) **back-flow oscillation** in a centrifugal compressor.
- [x] Triggered when the operating point on the compressor map drifts **left of the surge line** (mass flow too low for the demanded pressure ratio).
- [x] The **surge line** is the **left** boundary on the compressor map; the **choke line** is the **right** boundary.
- [x] **Surge Margin (SM)** $= \dfrac{\dot{m}_{design} - \dot{m}_{surge}}{\dot{m}_{design}} \times 100\%$. Automotive minimum = **10 %**.
- [x] **Primary cause:** rapid throttle closure → engine demand drops faster than compressor can spool down.
- [x] **Primary effect:** cyclic **reversal of axial thrust** → **thrust-bearing failure** is the dominant failure mode.
- [x] Secondary effects: noise (~120 dB), vibration, boost loss, impeller overheating, efficiency drop, blade damage.
- [x] **Surge period** $T_s = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L_p V_p}{\gamma A_p R T_1}}$ — Helmholtz resonance model of plenum-duct system.
- [x] **Anti-surge methods:** (1) Wastegate, (2) Blow-off valve, (3) Variable Geometry Turbine (VGT), (4) ECU-based MAP-sensor feedback, (5) Larger plenum volume for pulsation damping.
- [x] **Isentropic relations:** $\dfrac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$, $\;W_s = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1)$, $\;\eta_c = \dfrac{W_s}{W_{actual}}$.
- [x] **Euler turbomachinery equation:** $W = U_2 V_{w2}$ (centrifugal compressor, no inlet pre-swirl).
- [x] **Surge line is a curve, not a single number** — the most common single-point mistake in KTU answers.
- [x] **Stodola slip factor:** $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{\pi}{z} \sin \beta_2$ — appears in head-coefficient derivation.
- [x] **Surge → rotating stall → full surge** — the progression of aerodynamic instability as flow rate decreases.
- [x] In **torque converters**, “surge” = coupling shudder, cured by **lock-up clutch** and **damping springs**.
- [x] In **Roots blowers**, surging manifests as **discharge-pulse hammering**, mitigated by **vent ports** in the lobe clearance pockets.
- [x] **For KTU board exam:** always **draw the compressor map** with three regions and **mark the surge line in red**; this is worth a guaranteed **2 marks** in the diagram allotment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Critical Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Critical Frequency – Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design of Automotive Components**, the term **Critical Frequency** refers to the natural (resonant) frequency of a vibrating system — typically the sprung mass (vehicle body), unsprung mass (wheel–axle assembly), or a rotating shaft — at which the system undergoes **resonance** with a small periodic excitation. At this frequency, the amplitude of vibration becomes theoretically infinite (in an undamped system) and practically damaging in a damped system, leading to failure of components, loss of ride comfort, and possible accidents.

For an **automotive suspension system**, the critical frequency is commonly expressed in three distinct forms:

| Vibration Mode | Symbol | Governing Mass |
|---|---|---|
| **Bouncing frequency** | $f_b$ | Sprung mass ($m_s$) |
| **Pitching frequency** | $f_p$ | Sprung mass moment of inertia ($I_y$) |
| **Wheel-hop frequency** | $f_w$ | Unsprung mass ($m_u$) |

Mathematically, the generic **Critical Frequency** of a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{eq}}{m_{eq}}}$$

where $k_{eq}$ is the equivalent stiffness (N/m) and $m_{eq}$ is the equivalent mass (kg).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PCMUT601 Module 2, *Critical Frequency* is the gateway concept that connects **suspension stiffness**, **static deflection ($\delta_{st}$)**, and **ride comfort**. Expect numerical problems asking you to compute $f_b$, $f_p$, and $f_w$ from given vehicle parameters.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Bridge and the Soldiers"

Imagine a group of soldiers marching across a suspension bridge. If they march in **unison** (step frequency = bridge's natural frequency), the bridge begins to sway violently and may collapse. This is **resonance**. The bridge has a *critical frequency* (its natural frequency) at which it absolutely must NOT be excited.

In an automobile:
- The **road** is the "periodic excitation source" (bumps, undulations at a certain frequency $v/L$ where $v$ is vehicle speed and $L$ is wavelength of road profile).
- The **suspension** is the "bridge" with a critical frequency $f_c$.
- If road excitation frequency $\approx f_c$ → the car body bounces uncontrollably → **resonance** → poor ride, possible structural failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Golden Rule:** A well-designed vehicle suspension should have its critical frequency **far away** (typically lower) from the dominant road-excitation frequency range (≈ 1–10 Hz) encountered at normal driving speeds.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (**bold** for emphasis in calculations)
- **Typical acceptable bouncing frequency for passenger cars:** $f_b \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ Hz}$
- **Typical wheel-hop frequency:** $f_w \approx 8 \text{ to } 12 \text{ Hz}$
- **Pitching frequency:** $f_p \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.6 \text{ Hz}$ (must be close to $f_b$ to avoid simultaneous resonance)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Remember **1 Hz for the body, 10 Hz for the wheel**. This 1:10 ratio is a hallmark of well-designed automotive suspensions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup – Quarter-Car Two-Mass Model

The automotive suspension is modelled as a **two-mass, two-spring, one-damper** system:
- $m_s$ = Sprung mass (car body) supported by spring $k$ and damper $c$.
- $m_u$ = Unsprung mass (wheel/axle/tire) supported by tyre stiffness $k_t$.
- Static equilibrium produces a static deflection $\delta_{st}$ under the weight $W = m_s g$.

The system has **two degrees of freedom** and consequently **two natural frequencies** for the body (bounce & pitch) plus one for the wheel (hop).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

### Step 1: Static Equilibrium of Sprung Mass
At rest, the suspension spring $k$ deflects by $\delta_{st}$ such that it balances the weight $W$.

$$k \cdot \delta_{st} = m_s g$$

This allows us to express stiffness purely in terms of $\delta_{st}$:

$$k = \frac{m_s g}{\delta_{st}}$$

### Step 2: Equation of Motion (Free Vibration, Vertical)
For small vertical displacement $x$ of the sprung mass about equilibrium, applying Newton's second law:

$$m_s \ddot{x} + k x = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve the Characteristic Equation
Assuming $x = X \sin(\omega_n t)$, the characteristic equation gives:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_s}}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Frequency in Hz
Since $\omega_n = 2\pi f_n$:

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_s}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

> This elegant form shows that **the bouncing critical frequency depends ONLY on static deflection**, not on the absolute mass!

### Step 5: Pitching Mode (Rotation about Y-axis)
When the front and rear suspensions compress unequally, the body rotates. The pitching critical frequency is:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k \cdot L^2}{I_y}}$$

where $L$ is the effective wheelbase participating in pitch and $I_y$ is the mass moment of inertia of the body about the pitch axis.

### Step 6: Wheel-Hop Mode (Unsprung Mass)
The wheel/tyre system vibrates on its own stiffness $k_t$ and mass $m_u$:

$$f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_t}{m_u}}$$

Typical tyre stiffness $k_t \approx 200{,}000 \text{ to } 400{,}000$ N/m.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table – it covers 90% of KTU numerical questions on Critical Frequency.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Static deflection** | $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{m_s g}{k}$ | m | Bridge between mass and stiffness |
| 2 | **Bouncing critical freq.** | $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | Hz | Body resonance – most asked |
| 3 | **Pitching critical freq.** | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k L^2}{I_y}}$ | Hz | Must $\approx f_b$ to decouple modes |
| 4 | **Wheel-hop critical freq.** | $f_w = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_t}{m_u}}$ | Hz | Tyre-stiffness controlled |
| 5 | **Critical speed of shaft** | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | rpm | For rotating automotive shafts |
| 6 | **Stiffness from $\delta_{st}$** | $k = \dfrac{4\pi^2 m_s f_b^2}{1}$ | N/m | Rearranged form for design |
| 7 | **Design ratio (Pitch/Bounce)** | $\dfrac{f_p}{f_b} \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.1$ | — | Avoids coupled resonance |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (for any future tables):** All absolute-value-like symbols use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ride Comfort Engineering:** Automobile OEMs (Toyota, BMW, Tata Motors) tune $\delta_{st}$ to target $f_b \approx 1.2$ Hz for luxury cars and $\approx 1.5$ Hz for sporty cars.
- **NVH (Noise–Vibration–Harshness):** Critical frequencies feed into CAE simulations (ADAMS, MATLAB/Simulink) before prototyping.
- **Off-Road Vehicles:** Lower $f_b$ is chosen (0.8–1.0 Hz) to handle rough terrain without bottoming out.
- **Race Cars:** Higher $f_b$ (1.8–2.0 Hz) for stiffer response and better road feel.
- **Heavy Trucks & Buses:** Special attention to $f_p$ to avoid "porpoising" (front-rear pitch oscillation).

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Connection:** Tata Motors' Indica and Mahindra Scorpio use $\delta_{st} \approx 0.15$–$0.20$ m which yields $f_b \approx 1.1$–$1.3$ Hz — a sweet spot for Indian road conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Bouncing Critical Frequency

**Starting Equation (from static equilibrium):**
The suspension spring carries the weight of the sprung mass. At static equilibrium:

$$k \cdot \delta_{st} = W = m_s \cdot g$$

Therefore, the spring stiffness can be expressed as:

$$k = \frac{m_s g}{\delta_{st}}$$

---

**Free Body Diagram Analysis (during oscillation):**
Let the sprung mass be displaced by a small vertical distance $x$ from its equilibrium position. The spring force (restoring) acting upward is $k(\delta_{st} - x)$ and gravity $m_s g$ acts downward.

**Applying Newton's Second Law:**

$$m_s \ddot{x} = m_s g - k(\delta_{st} - x)$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$m_s \ddot{x} = m_s g - k\delta_{st} + kx$$

---

**Using the Static Equilibrium Condition $k\delta_{st} = m_s g$:**

The first two terms on the right cancel out:

$$m_s \ddot{x} = kx$$

Rearranging into standard form $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$:

$$m_s \ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

---

**Solving the Differential Equation:**
Assume a harmonic solution of the form:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t)$$

Differentiating twice:

$$\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega_n^2 X \sin(\omega_n t) = -\omega_n^2 x$$

Substituting back into the equation of motion:

$$m_s(-\omega_n^2 x) + kx = 0$$

$$x(k - m_s \omega_n^2) = 0$$

For a non-trivial solution ($x \neq 0$):

$$k - m_s \omega_n^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m_s}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_s}} \text{ rad/s}$$

---

**Conversion to Frequency in Hz:**
Using $f = \omega_n / (2\pi)$:

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_s}}$$

---

**Substituting $k = m_s g / \delta_{st}$:**

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\cancel{m_s} \cdot g}{\delta_{st} \cdot \cancel{m_s}}}$$

$$\boxed{f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}}$$

> **This is the final, mass-independent, KTU-exam-ready formula for Bouncing Critical Frequency.**

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Wheel-Hop Critical Frequency

**Setup:** The unsprung mass $m_u$ (wheel + tyre + part of axle) is supported by the tyre acting as a spring of stiffness $k_t$. The suspension spring $k$ and damper $c$ act on it from above.

**Free Vibration Equation (decoupled approximation):**
For the wheel-hop mode at its natural frequency, the suspension mass $m_s$ is assumed quasi-static, and the wheel alone oscillates:

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u + k_t x_u = 0$$

**Solving identically to the sprung mass case:**

$$\omega_w^2 = \frac{k_t}{m_u} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_t}{m_u}}$$

**Typical numerical substitution for an Indian sedan (e.g., Maruti Suzuki Dzire):**
- $m_u \approx 40$ kg
- $k_t \approx 250{,}000$ N/m

$$f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{250{,}000}{40}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{6250} = \frac{79.06}{6.283} \approx 12.58 \text{ Hz}$$

> This confirms the design rule: **wheel hop is roughly 10× the body bounce frequency.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement (Sample):**
A passenger car has a sprung mass of $m_s = 350$ kg at each wheel, a suspension stiffness of $k = 22{,}000$ N/m, and a static deflection of $\delta_{st} = 0.156$ m. Calculate:
1. The bouncing critical frequency $f_b$.
2. The pitching critical frequency $f_p$ if $I_y = 1200$ kg·m² and effective wheelbase $L = 2.4$ m.
3. The wheel-hop frequency $f_w$ if $k_t = 280{,}000$ N/m and $m_u = 45$ kg.

**Solution:**

**Part (1) – Bouncing Frequency:**

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.156}}$$

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{62.885}$$

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 7.930$$

$$\boxed{f_b \approx 1.262 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

**Part (2) – Pitching Frequency:**

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k L^2}{I_y}}$$

Note: We use the total stiffness $k_{total} = 4k = 4 \times 22{,}000 = 88{,}000$ N/m when accounting for all four wheels acting on pitch.

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{88{,}000 \times (2.4)^2}{1200}}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{88{,}000 \times 5.76}{1200}}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{506{,}880}{1200}}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{422.4}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 20.55$$

$$\boxed{f_p \approx 3.27 \text{ Hz}}$$

*Observation:* In a single-wheel model this gives a higher value; the true car pitch is much closer to $f_b$ in practice. The above calculation represents the simplified single-corner pitch.

---

**Part (3) – Wheel-Hop Frequency:**

$$f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_t}{m_u}}$$

$$f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{280{,}000}{45}}$$

$$f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{6222.22}$$

$$f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 78.88$$

$$\boxed{f_w \approx 12.55 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

## 3.4 Critical Speed of a Shaft (Rotating Component Extension)

For an automotive **propeller shaft** or **crankshaft** treated as a simply-supported beam with a central disc, the critical speed is:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

where $\delta_{st}$ is the **static deflection at the centre of the shaft due to its own weight** plus the attached components.

> **Derivation Insight:** The shaft equation of motion reduces mathematically to the same $k/m$ form once the lateral deflection is cast in terms of the equivalent spring rate $k = W / \delta_{st}$ where $W = mg$.

$$\boxed{N_c = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \text{ rpm} \quad \text{(since } \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 9.55\text{)}}$$

**Example:** If $\delta_{st} = 0.5$ mm = $0.0005$ m for a propeller shaft:

$$N_c = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.0005}} = 9.55 \sqrt{19{,}620} = 9.55 \times 140.07 \approx 1338 \text{ rpm}$$

This means the shaft must NOT operate near 1338 rpm — the engine's idle or a particular gear ratio could excite this resonance if not designed properly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Mass Quarter-Car Model (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Road Excitation Profile] -->|Periodic Force| B(Unsprung Mass Mu)
    B -->|Tyre Stiffness Kt| C[Ground Contact]
    B -->|Spring K and Damper C| D(Sprung Mass Ms)
    D -->|Pitching Rotation Iy| E[Vehicle Body CG]
    E -->|Outputs| F[Vibration Amplitude]
    F -->|Compare with| G[Critical Frequency Fc]
    G -->|Resonance If Equal| H[Loud Warning Resonance Damage]
    G -->|Far from Fc| I[Smooth Ride Comfort]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Bottom path: tyre → unsprung mass (12 Hz resonance).
- Middle path: spring/damper → sprung mass (1.2 Hz resonance).
- Top path: road provides periodic excitation at $f_{road} = v / L_{wavelength}$.
- **If** $f_{road} \approx f_b$ **or** $\approx f_w$ → Resonance (left outcome).
- **Else** → Comfortable ride (right outcome).

---

## 4.2 Critical Frequency Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Sprung Mass Ms] --> S2[Step 2: Identify Static Deflection Delta]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply Fb equals 1 over 2 pi times sqrt g over delta]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Numerical Value in Hz]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compare with Road Excitation Frequency]
    S5 -->|Match| R1[Redesign Delta Stiffness or Damper]
    S5 -->|No Match| G1[Acceptable Design]
```

---

## 4.3 Resonance Avoidance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Is Fb between 1.0 and 1.5 Hz?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Good Passenger Car Design]
    Q1 -->|No| R1[Recompute Delta St]
    Q2{Is Fp close to Fb within 10 percent?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P2[Avoids Coupled Resonance]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Adjust Stiffness Distribution Front Rear]
    Q3{Is Fw between 8 and 12 Hz?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Optimal Tyre Comfort]
    Q3 -->|No| R3[Change Tyre Pressure or Kt]
    P1 --> END[Design Validated]
    P2 --> END
    P3 --> END
    R1 --> Q1
    R2 --> Q2
    R3 --> Q3
```

---

## 4.4 Concept Map: Critical Frequency in Automotive Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Critical Frequency] --> M1[Bouncing Mode]
    ROOT --> M2[Pitching Mode]
    ROOT --> M3[Wheel Hop Mode]
    ROOT --> M4[Critical Speed of Shaft]
    M1 --> D1[Governed by Static Deflection]
    M2 --> D2[Governed by Wheelbase and Inertia]
    M3 --> D3[Governed by Tyre Stiffness]
    M4 --> D4[Governed by Shaft Deflection]
    D1 --> APP1[Ride Comfort]
    D2 --> APP1
    D3 --> APP2[Road Holding]
    D4 --> APP3[Driveline Durability]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define critical frequency of a vehicle suspension system. State its significance in ride comfort.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Critical frequency is the natural frequency of a vibrating automotive system (sprung or unsprung mass) at which resonance occurs. Mathematically, $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_{eq}}{m_{eq}}}$.
- **Significance (1 mark):** It must be kept away from the dominant road-excitation frequency (1–10 Hz) to prevent resonance, ensure ride comfort, and avoid structural damage.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A car has a static suspension deflection of 200 mm. Calculate the bouncing critical frequency. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.200}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{49.05}$$

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 7.004 = 1.115 \text{ Hz} \approx \mathbf{1.12 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the bouncing critical frequency of a vehicle suspension in terms of static deflection. Explain why this frequency is mass-independent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car has the following data:
- Sprung mass per wheel: $m_s = 400$ kg
- Suspension stiffness per wheel: $k = 25{,}000$ N/m
- Unsprung mass: $m_u = 50$ kg
- Tyre stiffness: $k_t = 300{,}000$ N/m
- Effective pitch wheelbase: $L = 2.5$ m
- Pitch moment of inertia: $I_y = 1500$ kg·m²

Calculate the bouncing, pitching, and wheel-hop critical frequencies and comment on the design. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) – Derivation (7 marks)
*Step 1 (1.5 marks):* State the static equilibrium: $k\delta_{st} = m_s g \Rightarrow k = m_s g / \delta_{st}$.

*Step 2 (1.5 marks):* Write the free vibration equation: $m_s \ddot{x} + kx = 0$.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Solve: assume $x = X\sin\omega_n t$, get $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m_s}$.

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Convert to Hz: $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m_s}$.

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Substitute $k$: $\boxed{f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}}$ — mass $m_s$ cancels, hence **mass-independent**.

#### Part (b) – Numerical (7 marks)

**Bouncing frequency:**

$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.1568}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{62.56} = \frac{7.91}{6.283} \approx \mathbf{1.26 \text{ Hz}}$$

(Here $\delta_{st} = m_s g / k = 400 \times 9.81 / 25{,}000 = 0.1568$ m)

**Pitching frequency (using 4-wheel stiffness $4k = 100{,}000$ N/m):**

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{100{,}000 \times (2.5)^2}{1500}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{625{,}000}{1500}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{416.67}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 20.41 \approx \mathbf{3.24 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Wheel-hop frequency:**

$$f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{300{,}000}{50}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{6000} = \frac{77.46}{6.283} \approx \mathbf{12.33 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Design Comment (1 mark):**
- $f_b = 1.26$ Hz → **excellent** for a passenger car (range 1.0–1.5 Hz).
- $f_p / f_b = 3.24 / 1.26 = 2.57$ → **too high**; pitch and bounce are decoupled but pitch may resonate with road undulations around 3 Hz.
- $f_w / f_b = 12.33 / 1.26 = 9.78$ → close to the ideal 10:1 ratio → **good design**.

**Recommendation:** Reduce effective pitch stiffness distribution or increase $I_y$ by lowering CG to bring $f_p$ closer to $f_b$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the quarter-car two-mass model for vehicle suspension. Derive the expression for wheel-hop critical frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A propeller shaft of a rear-wheel-drive car is supported by two bearings 1.2 m apart. The central disc weighs 50 N and produces a static deflection of 0.6 mm at the centre. Calculate:
1. The critical speed of the shaft in rpm.
2. The safe operating speed range.
3. Comment on what happens if the engine idles at 850 rpm. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) – Quarter-Car Model (7 marks)

*Sketch description (2 marks):* Two masses — $m_s$ (sprung) above, $m_u$ (unsprung) below — connected by spring $k$ and damper $c$. The unsprung mass rests on the road through tyre stiffness $k_t$.

*Wheel-hop derivation (5 marks):*

For the unsprung mass in free vibration (suspension assumed stationary):

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u + k_t x_u = 0$$

Characteristic equation: $\omega_w^2 = k_t / m_u$

$$\boxed{f_w = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_t}{m_u}}}$$

#### Part (b) – Propeller Shaft (7 marks)

**Given:** $W = 50$ N, $\delta_{st} = 0.6$ mm $= 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute $f_c$ first:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{6 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{16{,}350} = \frac{127.87}{6.283} \approx 20.35 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Convert to critical speed:

$$N_c = f_c \times 60 = 20.35 \times 60 \approx \mathbf{1221 \text{ rpm}}$$

(Alternative form: $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{g/\delta_{st}} = 9.55 \times 127.87 \approx 1221$ rpm ✓)

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Safe operating range: typically $0.8 N_c < N < 0.8 N_c$ to avoid, i.e., the shaft should NOT run between **~1220 rpm ± 15%** to stay clear of resonance.

$$N_{safe} < 0.8 \times 1221 = 977 \text{ rpm} \quad \text{or} \quad N_{safe} > 1.2 \times 1221 = 1465 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Engine idle at 850 rpm < 977 rpm → **safe from resonance**, but barely. Whipping vibrations could still be observed; engineering recommendation is to either stiffen the shaft (reduce $\delta_{st}$) or add a damping element.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in static deflection — costs 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up $f_b$ and $f_w$ formulas** — bouncing uses $g/\delta_{st}$; wheel-hop uses $k_t/m_u$. These are NOT interchangeable.
> 3. **Not mentioning "mass independence"** of $f_b$ when asked — easy 1-mark gift that students skip.
> 4. **Using 3.14 instead of full precision for $\pi$** — use 3.14159 or the calculator's $\pi$ key.
> 5. **Forgetting to compare $f_p$ with $f_b$** in design comments — examiners specifically look for the **decoupling analysis**.
> 6. **Units in final answer** — always write **Hz** or **rpm** explicitly to avoid losing the final 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Critical Frequency (PCMUT601 Module 2)**

- ✅ **Bouncing critical frequency** $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ — **mass independent**, depends only on static deflection.
- ✅ **Wheel-hop critical frequency** $f_w = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_t}{m_u}}$ — controlled by tyre stiffness & unsprung mass.
- ✅ **Pitching critical frequency** $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k L^2}{I_y}}$ — must be **close to $f_b$** (within 10%) to avoid coupled resonance.
- ✅ **Critical speed of shaft** $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ in **rpm**.
- ✅ **Design target:** $f_b \approx 1.0$–$1.5$ Hz (passenger cars), $f_w \approx 8$–$12$ Hz, $f_p \approx f_b$.
- ✅ **Static deflection** $\delta_{st} = m_s g / k$ is the *key design knob*.
- ✅ **1 Hz body : 10 Hz wheel** rule of thumb.
- ✅ **Avoid operating** rotating components within ±15% of $N_c$.
- ✅ **Resonance condition:** excitation frequency $\approx$ natural frequency → infinite/dangerous amplitude.
- ✅ Always carry $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $\frac{60}{2\pi} = 9.55$ in your mental toolkit.
- ✅ For 14-mark problems, structure answer as: Given Data → Formula → Substitution → Numerical Result → Design Comment.

> **End of Notes — Critical Frequency, PCMUT601 Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: End Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** End Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the design of automotive pressure vessels, hydraulic accumulators, compressed air reservoirs, and oil/air tanks, **End Construction** refers to the structural geometry and thickness calculation of the *closing caps (heads)* attached to the open ends of a cylindrical shell. These heads must safely transmit the internal pressure loads into the shell wall while providing a leak-proof closure, and they must conform to **IS 2825 (Unfired Pressure Vessels)** and **ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code Section VIII** standards.

The four principal industrial classifications of end constructions are:

1. **Flat Ends** – Plain circular discs bolted or welded to the shell flange.
2. **Hemispherical Ends** – Half-sphere geometry offering the most efficient pressure containment.
3. **Ellipsoidal Ends** – An oblate ellipsoid (2:1 ratio is standard) – the most common industrial choice.
4. **Torispherical Ends** – Composed of a central spherical knuckle transitioning into a cylindrical straight flange (flued ends).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 2 outcome emphasizes design of cylindrical and spherical pressure vessels with special attention to **head design efficiency** — comparing material economy, manufacturing ease, and stress distribution across different end geometries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blowing up a **balloon** (a cylinder analogy) and then pinching off the open end. The rubber stretches most at the **poles** (the ends) where the curvature changes abruptly. If you instead used a **fully inflated sphere**, the rubber would be uniformly stretched everywhere.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in automobiles:** A car fuel tank or hydraulic accumulator is essentially a "stretched balloon." The hemispherical end is mathematically the most efficient (least material for a given pressure), but it is hard to manufacture and wastes interior space. Therefore, automotive engineers choose ellipsoidal or torispherical ends as a *practical compromise* between **strength, space, and cost**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Internal Design Pressure ($p$)** : typically **0.5 MPa to 35 MPa** in automotive hydraulic systems.
- **Allowable Stress ($S$)** : derived from **$S = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{n}$** with safety factor **$n \geq 4$** for ductile steels.
- **Joint Efficiency ($\eta$)** : **0.7 to 1.0** depending on weld inspection (radiographed vs. spot-checked).
- **Corrosion Allowance ($C_A$)** : typically **1 mm to 3 mm** added to calculated thickness.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Each End Type

The fundamental design philosophy behind end construction is governed by the **Membrane Stress Theory** for thin shells. For a shell subjected to internal pressure $p$, the membrane stress is inversely proportional to the **mean radius of curvature** at that point:

$$\sigma_m \propto \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

**Thus, to minimize stress (and hence required thickness), we want the smallest possible radius of curvature at every point of the head.**

#### A. Flat End
- **Geometry:** Plain disc clamped against the flange of the shell.
- **Stress State:** Pure bending — the entire internal pressure load must be resisted as a bending moment across the disc diameter.
- **Thickness Formula:**
$$t = D \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.3 \cdot p}{S}}$$
- **Pros:** Cheapest, easiest to manufacture, provides access for inspection/cleaning.
- **Cons:** Thickest (≈ 4× heavier than equivalent hemispherical head); induces severe flange bending stresses.

#### B. Hemispherical End
- **Geometry:** Perfect half-sphere welded to the cylindrical shell.
- **Thickness Formula:**
$$t = \frac{p \cdot R}{2 \cdot S \cdot \eta - 0.2 \cdot p}$$
- **Pros:** Highest strength-to-weight ratio; uniform biaxial membrane stress state.
- **Cons:** Deep dome consumes useful interior space; difficult deep-drawing manufacturing.

#### C. Ellipsoidal End (2:1 Standard)
- **Geometry:** Surface of revolution of an ellipse where **major axis $a = 2 \cdot$ minor axis $b$**.
- **Thickness Formula:**
$$t = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot \eta - 0.2 \cdot p}$$
where $D$ is the shell outside diameter.
- **Pros:** Standard industrial choice; balanced stress distribution.
- **Cons:** Slightly more material than hemispherical at apex.

#### D. Torispherical End (Flued)
- **Geometry:** Central spherical cap (knuckle radius $R$) merging via a toroidal fillet into a cylindrical straight flange (crown radius $r$).
- **Design Rule:** $R = D$ and $r = 0.06D$ (typical) or $r = 0.1D$ (for high pressure).
- **Thickness Formula:**
$$t = \frac{0.885 \cdot p \cdot R}{S \cdot \eta - 0.1 \cdot p}$$
- **Pros:** Cheaper to manufacture than ellipsoidal (formed by spinning); good for large diameters.
- **Cons:** Stress concentration at the knuckle-to-flange transition.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| End Type | Thickness Formula | Stress State | Design Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat (Bolted)** | $t = D \sqrt{\dfrac{0.3\,p}{S}}$ | Pure bending | Lowest |
| **Flat (Welded)** | $t = D \sqrt{\dfrac{C \cdot p}{S}}$ with $C \approx 0.25$ | Bending + membrane | Low |
| **Hemispherical** | $t = \dfrac{p\,R}{2\,S\,\eta - 0.2\,p}$ | Pure membrane (biaxial equal) | Highest |
| **Ellipsoidal (2:1)** | $t = \dfrac{p\,D}{2\,S\,\eta - 0.2\,p}$ | Membrane + slight bending | High |
| **Torispherical** | $t = \dfrac{0.885\,p\,R}{S\,\eta - 0.1\,p}$ | Membrane + knuckle bending | Medium-High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Automotive Utility:** These formulas are used to design CNG vehicle cylinders (torispherical), hydraulic brake accumulators (hemispherical), and air receiver tanks (ellipsoidal 2:1) in passenger cars, trucks, and earth-moving equipment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thickness of a Flat Head (Unstayed, Welded)

Consider a flat circular disc of diameter $D$ and thickness $t$, simply supported along its periphery and subjected to uniform pressure $p$ on one face.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Force on Half Disc**
Cut the disc along a diametral plane. The net hydrostatic thrust pushing one half of the disc away from the other half is:

$$F = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment at the Supported Edge**
This thrust acts at the centroid of the semicircle, which lies at a distance $\dfrac{4D}{3\pi}$ from the center. The bending moment at the clamped/supported edge is therefore:

$$M = F \cdot \frac{4D}{3\pi} = \frac{p \pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{4D}{3\pi}$$

After simplifying:

$$M = \frac{p D^3}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus of the Disc Edge**
For a rectangular cross-section at the edge (width $= 1 \cdot t$, depth $= 1$ unit along the circumference effectively):

$$Z = \frac{1 \cdot t^2}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Equate Bending Stress to Allowable Stress**
By the flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$:

$$S = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{p D^3 / 3}{t^2 / 6} = \frac{2 p D^3}{t^2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Thickness $t$**

$$t^2 = \frac{2 p D^3}{S} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = D \sqrt{\frac{2 p}{S}}$$

However, this is the elastic bending stress result. For a **flat end welded to a shell**, the edge is partially constrained (not free to rotate), so the empirical coefficient is reduced. The **IS 2825 recommended formula** is:

$$t = D \sqrt{\frac{0.3 \, p}{S}}$$

This empirical factor **0.3** accounts for the partial edge fixity and stress redistribution in welded flat heads.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thickness of a Hemispherical Head

For a thin spherical shell of inner radius $R$ and thickness $t$, cut the sphere into two equal hemispheres by a diametral plane.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Force from Internal Pressure**

$$F = p \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 2 — Resisting Force from Membrane Stress**
The membrane stress $\sigma$ acts tangentially along the cut circumference of length $2\pi R$, distributed over two halves, each of thickness $t$:

$$F_{resist} = 2 \cdot \sigma \cdot \pi R \cdot t = 2 \pi R t \, \sigma$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium $F = F_{resist}$**

$$p \pi R^2 = 2 \pi R t \, \sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = \frac{p R}{2 t}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Allowable Stress with Joint Efficiency**
Including joint efficiency $\eta$ and corrosion allowance $C_A$:

$$\frac{p R}{2 t} = S \cdot \eta$$

Solving for thickness:

$$t = \frac{p R}{2 S \eta - 0.2 p}$$

where the **$0.2 p$** term in the denominator accounts for the secondary stress due to pressure acting on the unsupported thickness itself (added per **IS 2825** to keep stress from exceeding allowable even when nominal stress equals allowable).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thickness of a 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head

For a 2:1 ellipsoidal head, the maximum stress occurs at the **apex (crown)** where the meridional radius of curvature is smallest. By applying the **Membrane Theory of Shells of Revolution**, the meridional stress at the apex is:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{p D}{2 t}$$

where $D$ is the *diameter of the cylindrical shell* to which the head is welded.

Following the same allowable-stress methodology with joint efficiency and correction term:

$$\frac{p D}{2 t} = S \cdot \eta \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{p D}{2 S \eta - 0.2 p}$$

Note: **The ellipsoidal head formula is identical in form to the hemispherical formula**, except that the diameter $D$ is used in place of the spherical radius $R$. Since for a 2:1 ellipse the meridional radius at the crown equals $D/2$, the numerical values coincide.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of End-Construction Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressure Vessel Design Starts] --> B{Operating Pressure p}
    B -->|Low p ≤ 1 MPa| C[Flat End Candidate]
    B -->|Medium p 1-10 MPa| D[Ellipsoidal 2:1 Candidate]
    B -->|High p > 10 MPa| E[Torispherical or Hemispherical Candidate]
    
    C --> F{Is access required?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Bolted Flat Head]
    F -->|No| H[Welded Flat Head]
    
    D --> I{Diameter D}
    I -->|Small D ≤ 500 mm| J[Deep Drawn Ellipsoidal]
    I -->|Large D > 500 mm| K[Spin Formed Ellipsoidal]
    
    E --> L{Space Constraint}
    L -->|Tight| M[Torispherical]
    L -->|Loose| N[Hemispherical]
    
    G --> O[Final Design Check]
    H --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    
    O --> P{Thickness t > Corrosion Allowance CA?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Add Corrosion Allowance and Finalize]
    P -->|No| R[Increase t or Change Material]
    
    Q --> S[IS 2825 Compliance Check]
    R --> S
    S --> T[Drawing Released for Manufacture]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Description of End Geometries

Since the physical cross-sections of pressure vessel heads involve complex curved profiles, the **block diagram** above captures the engineering decision logic. The visual cross-sectional comparison is summarized below:

| Geometry | Cross-Section Sketch (Textual) | Relative Depth |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat** | `|------|` (vertical line attached to flat plate) | Zero |
| **Hemispherical** | `|---⌒---|` (half circle) | $D/2$ |
| **Ellipsoidal 2:1** | `|---◠---|` (oblate ellipse, shallow dome) | $D/4$ |
| **Torispherical** | `|---⏛---|` (small central dome with cylindrical flange) | $D/8$ to $D/4$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** A shallow ellipsoidal/torispherical head uses **less axial space** inside the vehicle chassis than a hemispherical head — this is a critical automotive packaging constraint.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List the four major types of end constructions used in pressure vessels and state which one provides the highest design efficiency.*

**Model Answer:**
The four types are: (i) Flat ends, (ii) Hemispherical ends, (iii) Ellipsoidal ends, and (iv) Torispherical ends.
The **hemispherical end** provides the highest design efficiency because it offers a uniform biaxial membrane stress state with the minimum thickness-to-diameter ratio for a given pressure.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the term "Joint Efficiency" ($\eta$) in the context of end construction. What values are typically used?*

**Model Answer:**
Joint efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of the strength of a welded joint to the strength of the unwelded parent metal. It depends on the degree of radiographic inspection:
- **100% radiographed** : $\eta = 1.0$
- **Spot-checked radiography** : $\eta = 0.85$
- **No radiography, but properly welded** : $\eta = 0.70$

It is incorporated in end-construction thickness formulas to penalize designs using lower-quality welds.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> A cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter $D = 600$ mm is to operate at an internal pressure of $p = 2.5$ MPa. The material has an ultimate tensile strength of 450 MPa, with a factor of safety of 4. The welded joint efficiency is 0.85. Design a suitable **ellipsoidal (2:1)** end for this vessel.

**(a)** [7 Marks] — Determine the required thickness of the ellipsoidal end and explain the role of joint efficiency in the calculation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] — Redesign the end as a **torispherical** head and compare the mass of material required for both designs.

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Ellipsoidal End Design**

**Step 1 — Allowable Stress Calculation**
$$S = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{n} = \frac{450}{4} = 112.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Thickness of 2:1 Ellipsoidal Head**
Using the IS 2825 formula:
$$t = \frac{p \, D}{2 S \eta - 0.2 p}$$

Substituting:
$$t = \frac{2.5 \times 600}{2 \times 112.5 \times 0.85 - 0.2 \times 2.5}$$

$$t = \frac{1500}{191.25 - 0.5} = \frac{1500}{190.75}$$

$$t = 7.86 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Adding Corrosion Allowance**
Take $C_A = 2$ mm (typical):
$$t_{final} = 7.86 + 2.0 = 9.86 \text{ mm} \approx 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Role of Joint Efficiency:** $\eta$ reduces the effective allowable stress, forcing a thicker head to compensate for the weakened weld zone. Lower $\eta$ → thicker head.

**(b) Torispherical End Design**

For a torispherical head, typical proportions are $R = D = 600$ mm and $r = 0.06 D = 36$ mm.

**Step 1 — Thickness of Torispherical Head**
$$t = \frac{0.885 \, p \, R}{S \eta - 0.1 p}$$

$$t = \frac{0.885 \times 2.5 \times 600}{112.5 \times 0.85 - 0.1 \times 2.5}$$

$$t = \frac{1327.5}{95.625 - 0.25} = \frac{1327.5}{95.375}$$

$$t = 13.92 \text{ mm} \approx 14 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Adding Corrosion Allowance**
$$t_{final} = 13.92 + 2.0 = 15.92 \text{ mm} \approx 16 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Comparison**
Approximating both heads as discs of effective diameter $D + 2t$ and a circular plate density of $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³:

- **Ellipsoidal mass:** $\pi (0.30 + 0.005)^2 \times 0.010 \times 7850 \approx 22.6$ kg (using approximate projected area)
- **Torispherical mass:** $\pi (0.30 + 0.008)^2 \times 0.016 \times 7850 \approx 36.4$ kg (approximately)

**Conclusion:** The ellipsoidal head requires roughly **38% less material**, confirming its higher design efficiency.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

> A welded flat-end cap is to be designed for a vessel of internal diameter $D = 400$ mm operating at $p = 1.2$ MPa. The allowable stress of the material is $S = 100$ MPa.

**(a)** [7 Marks] — Calculate the thickness of the flat end using IS 2825 recommendations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] — If the same vessel were redesigned with a hemispherical end, compute the thickness and comment on which design is more economical in terms of material used.

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Flat End Thickness**

Using:
$$t = D \sqrt{\frac{0.3 \, p}{S}}$$

$$t = 400 \sqrt{\frac{0.3 \times 1.2}{100}} = 400 \sqrt{0.0036} = 400 \times 0.06$$

$$t = 24 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Hemispherical End Thickness**

Here, the spherical radius $R \approx D/2 = 200$ mm.

$$t = \frac{p \, R}{2 S \eta - 0.2 p}$$

Taking $\eta = 1.0$ (assume full radiographic inspection for the sphere):

$$t = \frac{1.2 \times 200}{2 \times 100 \times 1.0 - 0.2 \times 1.2} = \frac{240}{200 - 0.24}$$

$$t = \frac{240}{199.76} = 1.20 \text{ mm}$$

**Adding corrosion allowance $C_A = 2$ mm:**
$$t_{final} = 1.20 + 2.0 = 3.20 \text{ mm} \approx 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Comment on Economy:**
- **Flat end:** $t = 24$ mm, mass per head $\approx 23.7$ kg
- **Hemispherical end:** $t = 4$ mm, mass per head $\approx 4.0$ kg (ignoring the deeper dome volume)

The hemispherical head uses **~83% less material**, demonstrating vastly superior efficiency. However, flat ends are still chosen when **easy access for cleaning/inspection** is essential (e.g., filters in automotive fuel systems).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $0.2p$ or $0.1p$ correction term** in the denominator — this costs **2 marks** typically.
> 2. **Using $\sigma_{uts}$ directly instead of allowable stress $S = \sigma_{uts}/n$** — verify factor of safety is given.
> 3. **Confusing knuckle radius $R$ and crown radius $r$** in torispherical formulas.
> 4. **Omitting the corrosion allowance $C_A$** in final thickness — KTU awards 1 mark specifically for this addition.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Ensure $p$, $S$, and dimensions are all in **consistent SI units** (MPa and mm).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flat ends** are cheap but inefficient — used where access matters more than weight.
- **Hemispherical ends** give uniform biaxial membrane stress — most material-efficient but space-hungry.
- **Ellipsoidal 2:1 ends** are the *industrial standard* — balanced choice for automotive pressure vessels.
- **Torispherical ends** are economical to manufacture by spinning — common in CNG cylinders.
- The **$0.2p$** and **$0.1p$** terms in the denominator are **mandatory** per IS 2825.
- **Joint efficiency $\eta$** depends on weld inspection quality (0.7 to 1.0).
- **Corrosion allowance $C_A$** must always be added to the calculated membrane thickness.
- **Allowable stress $S = \sigma_{uts}/n$**, with **$n \geq 4$** for ductile materials.
- **Membrane stress formula** $\sigma = \dfrac{p r}{t}$ is the foundation of all thin-shell end design.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Mnemonic for End Types (FAST → HET):**
> **F**lat (bending)
> **A**ccessible (cheap)
> **S**tress concentration (knuckle in torispherical)
> **T**hickest of all types
> **H**emispherical (uniform, efficient, deep)
> **E**llipsoidal (balanced, standard)
> **T**orispherical (manufacturable, knuckle stress)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Leaf Springs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Leaf Springs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — DESIGN OF LEAF SPRINGS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Leaf Spring (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **leaf spring** is a simple form of mechanical suspension spring, commonly used in automotive vehicles, manufactured from a stack of carefully shaped flat, rectangular, or curved metal strips (leaves) of nearly equal width and varying lengths. The leaves are held together by means of a **center bolt**, **rebound clips**, and **U-bolts**, and they deflect under load to absorb shock energy through elastic bending deformation.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2 — *Suspension Systems*), leaf springs are classified as **resilient suspension elements** that perform three critical functions simultaneously:

1. **Load-bearing** — Support the static weight of the vehicle chassis and payload.
2. **Shock absorption** — Dissipate vibrational energy transmitted from road irregularities.
3. **Locating the axle** — Transmit forces (braking, driving, cornering) between axle and chassis.

### 1.2 Nomenclature of Leaf Spring Components

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Master Leaf** | The longest leaf with an *eye* at each end; carries the maximum bending moment and is subjected to greatest stress. |
| **Full-Length Leaves** | Leaves extending from eye to eye (full length) along with master leaf. |
| **Graduated Leaves** | Progressively shorter leaves stacked above the master leaf; they carry load only over their reduced effective length. |
| **Eye** | A circular loop at the end of master leaf for pin/shackle connection. |
| **Center Bolt** | Holds all leaves in proper alignment and resists lateral displacement. |
| **U-Bolts** | Clamp the entire leaf assembly to the axle housing. |
| **Rebound Clips** | Prevent excessive separation of leaves during rebound (unloading). |
| **Shackle** | Provides swinging/pivoting action at the rear eye to permit length change during deflection. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Leaf is also called *Main Leaf* or *Eye Leaf*** — it is the most critically stressed leaf and must always be designed first.

### 1.3 Types of Leaf Springs (KTU Classification)

Based on **geometric configuration**, leaf springs are classified into:

**1. Semi-Elliptical Leaf Spring** *(Most widely used in automobiles)*
- Both ends are free to pivot (one end fixed to chassis via spring eye, other end via shackle).
- Load is applied at the **center (U-bolt location)**.
- Treated as a *simply supported beam of effective length L* with central load W.

**2. Full-Elliptical Leaf Spring**
- Two halves of the spring act in opposite directions; used in older passenger cars and railway buffers.
- Acts as two semi-elliptical springs joined back-to-back.

**3. Quarter-Elliptical Leaf Spring**
- One end fixed (cantilever), other end supported on shackle.
- Used in light commercial vehicles and as auxiliary springs.

**4. Three-Quarter Elliptical Leaf Spring**
- Combination of semi- and quarter-elliptical designs.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Diving Board with Friends on It":**
> Imagine a *flexible diving board* mounted between two pivots (like a see-saw). When one person stands at the middle, the board bends downward, storing energy that is released when the person steps off. A leaf spring works exactly the same way — except instead of one board, multiple thinner boards (leaves) are *stacked together* to share the load. The longest board (master leaf) acts like the diver's main lever, while shorter boards (graduated leaves) "help out" near the center where bending is greatest and bow out (like a *fan*) toward the ends where bending is less.

Another excellent analogy is the **bow and arrow** — the bow stores elastic strain energy when the string is pulled; the leaf spring stores strain energy when the vehicle load deflects it.

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants (Bold Emphasis)

- **Young's Modulus of Spring Steel (E):** **200 – 210 GPa** (typically **E = 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm²**)
- **Permissible Bending Stress (σ_b):** **300 – 500 MPa** for automotive applications
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength of Spring Steel:** **1200 – 1800 MPa**
- **Density of Spring Steel:** **7.85 g/cm³**
- **Width-to-Thickness Ratio (b/t):** Typically **6 ≤ b/t ≤ 12** for optimum stress distribution.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis of Leaf Spring Design

The design of a leaf spring rests on **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory** combined with the **principle of equal stress distribution** among the leaves. The fundamental assumptions are:

1. All leaves are of **uniform width and thickness**.
2. The leaves are initially **curved (cambered)** such that the tip load leaves the master leaf without any initial gap.
3. The ends of the spring (eye centers) act as **simple supports**.
4. Load W is applied at the **center of the spring** through the axle (via U-bolts).
5. **No inter-leaf friction** (lubrication between leaves allows free slippage).
6. All leaves deflect equally under load.

### 2.2 Why "Multiple Leaves" Instead of One Thick Leaf?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy:**
> A single thick leaf would store the same energy as n thinner leaves of equivalent section modulus, BUT it would have **very high inter-laminar shear stresses** and **no compliance**. By splitting one thick leaf into *n thinner leaves*, we achieve:
> - Better load distribution across the width
> - Reduction of peak stress concentration
> - Built-in *fail-safe redundancy* (failure of one leaf does not collapse the spring)
> - Reduced weight for the same stiffness (because **deflection ∝ 1/n**)

### 2.3 Camber — The Initial Curvature

The leaves are not manufactured straight; they are pre-curved upward with an initial curvature called **camber**. Under maximum load, the spring becomes *almost flat*. This ensures:
- **Even stress distribution** across all leaves at full load
- **Tip load distribution** (master leaf tip meets the second leaf tip with no gap only at full load)
- **Smooth ride characteristic** without sudden load pickup

Mathematically, camber is related to maximum deflection:

$$\text{Initial Camber} = \frac{\delta_{\max}}{2}$$

where $\delta_{\max}$ is the maximum static deflection under full load.

### 2.4 Nipping of Leaf Springs

> [!NOTE]
> **Nipping** is a deliberate pre-stressing technique where the shorter leaves are pre-bent such that they sit *tightly* between the master leaf and the second full-length leaf. This ensures:
> - Graduated leaves begin to carry load **immediately** upon application of load (no slack period).
> - Reduces stress on the master leaf by transferring load to upper leaves sooner.
> - Stress due to nipping is given by: $\sigma_{nip} = \dfrac{3WL}{n_b \cdot b \cdot t^2}$ where $n_b$ is the number of leaves at the section under consideration.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Leaf Spring Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below MUST be memorized for KTU examinations.** Note: use `\vert` instead of `\vert` for clarity (no LaTeX delimiters break tables).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Maximum Bending Moment** (simply supported, central load) | $M = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{4}$ | N·mm | Maximum moment at center of spring |
| 2 | **Section Modulus** (for n leaves, each b × t) | $Z = \dfrac{n \cdot b \cdot t^2}{6}$ | mm³ | Bending resistance of leaf stack |
| 3 | **Moment of Inertia** (for n leaves) | $I = \dfrac{n \cdot b \cdot t^3}{12}$ | mm⁴ | Flexural stiffness parameter |
| 4 | **Maximum Bending Stress** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^2}$ | N/mm² (= MPa) | Stress in master leaf at center |
| 5 | **Maximum Deflection** | $\delta = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{2 \cdot n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$ | mm | Vertical deflection under load W |
| 6 | **Stiffness (Spring Rate)** | $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{2 \cdot n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{3 \cdot L^3}$ | N/mm | Load per unit deflection |
| 7 | **Strain Energy Stored** | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot W \cdot \delta$ | N·mm (= mJ) | Energy absorbed by spring |
| 8 | **Thickness of Leaf** (from stress eqn.) | $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b}}$ | mm | Designed leaf thickness |
| 9 | **Number of Leaves** (from stress eqn.) | $n = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_b}$ | dimensionless | Required leaves |
| 10 | **Number of Leaves** (from deflection eqn.) | $n = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{2 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3 \cdot \delta}$ | dimensionless | Leaves required for stiffness |
| 11 | **Effective Number of Leaves** (full + 2/3 graduated) | $n_{eff} = n_f + \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot n_g$ | dimensionless | Used in deflection calc. |
| 12 | **Initial Camber** | $C = \dfrac{\delta}{2}$ | mm | Pre-curvature of leaves |
| 13 | **Leaf Length (full)** | $L_f = 2L$ (between eye centers) | mm | Total spring length |
| 14 | **Shear Stress at Eye** (master leaf) | $\tau = \dfrac{3W}{4 \cdot b \cdot t}$ | N/mm² | Stress near eye hole |
| 15 | **Bending Stress at Section x** (for graduated leaves) | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot (L - x)}{2 \cdot n_x \cdot b \cdot t^2}$ | N/mm² | Variable stress along leaf |

### 2.6 Where Does the Formula σ = (3WL)/(2nbt²) Come From? — Logic Flow

> **Why "3WL"?** Because we model the spring as a *simply supported beam* with central load W:
> - Bending moment at center: M = WL/4
> - Section modulus of n leaves: Z = nbt²/6
> - $\sigma = M/Z = (WL/4) \cdot (6/(nbt^2)) = (3WL)/(2nbt^2)$

> **Why "n" appears in denominator?** Because the load is shared between all leaves (each leaf has section modulus bt²/6; total = nbt²/6).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Leaf springs are the *backbone* of commercial vehicle suspension systems because:
- **Load-carrying capacity** ranges from 5 kN (motorcycles) to over 200 kN (heavy trucks).
- **Maintenance-friendly** — individual leaves can be replaced without discarding the entire assembly.
- **Excellent lateral stability** — the wide, flat stack resists side loads during cornering.
- **Cost-effective** — no precision machining needed; simple hot-forming and heat treatment.
- **Material-efficient** — fiber stresses dominate; volume is utilized optimally.

They are widely used in **Mahindra, Tata, Ashok Leyland, Eicher trucks**, and almost all commercial vehicles and railway wagons in India.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Rigorous Derivation — Bending Stress in Leaf Spring

**Step 1 — Beam Model Setup**

Consider a semi-elliptical leaf spring as a *simply supported beam*:
- Effective length between U-bolt centers (or between shackle pin and U-bolt) = **L**
- Total load applied at the center = **W**
- Total number of leaves = **n** (each of width **b** and uniform thickness **t**)

**Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment**

For a simply supported beam with central point load, the maximum bending moment occurs *at the center* and equals:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus of n leaves (assumed stacked rigidly)**

Each leaf has a rectangular cross-section b × t. Section modulus of one leaf:

$$Z_1 = \frac{I_1}{c} = \frac{(b t^3 / 12)}{(t/2)} = \frac{b t^2}{6}$$

For *n* leaves stacked together (assuming equal stress sharing — nipping ensures this):

$$Z_{\text{total}} = n \cdot Z_1 = \frac{n \cdot b \cdot t^2}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Flexural Formula**

By Euler-Bernoulli bending theory: $\sigma_b = M_{\max} / Z_{\text{total}}$

Substituting the values:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \frac{M_{\max}}{Z_{\text{total}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(W L / 4)}{(n \cdot b \cdot t^2 / 6)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{W L}{4} \times \frac{6}{n \cdot b \cdot t^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{4 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{3 W L}{2 n b t^2}}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU equation** for the maximum bending stress in a leaf spring.

---

## 3.2 Rigorous Derivation — Maximum Deflection of Leaf Spring

**Step 1 — Apply Standard Beam Deflection Formula**

For a *simply supported beam* of span L with central point load W, the maximum deflection (occurring at the center) from classical beam theory is:

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{W \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Moment of Inertia of n Leaves**

Moment of inertia for n leaves (each b × t):

$$I_{\text{total}} = n \cdot \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} = \frac{n \cdot b \cdot t^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Combine**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\max} &= \frac{W \cdot L^3}{48 \cdot E \cdot (n b t^3 / 12)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{W \cdot L^3 \cdot 12}{48 \cdot E \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{W \cdot L^3}{4 \cdot E \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Leaf Spring Loading Correction**

In practice, leaf springs experience *additional deflection* due to:
- Curvature effects (camber)
- Non-uniform load distribution along graduated leaves
- Effective length being less than total length between eyes

The *commonly used KTU/standard formula* (used in V.B. Bhandari and most Indian engineering textbooks) incorporates these factors and is given as:

$$\boxed{\delta_{\max} = \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{2 \cdot n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference Between Rigorous and Standard Formulae:**
> The rigorous derivation gives $\delta = WL^3/(4Enbt^3)$. The standard textbook formula $\delta = 3WL^3/(2nEbt^3)$ accounts for the **effective span** being different from the *eye-to-eye length* and the *load distribution* along the spring. In KTU exam problems, students should **use the standard formula** unless otherwise stated.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:**
Design a leaf spring for a road vehicle with the following specifications:

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Maximum load on spring | W | 25,000 N |
| Effective length of spring | L | 1000 mm |
| Width of leaf | b | 75 mm |
| Permissible bending stress | σ_b | 350 N/mm² |
| Permissible deflection | δ | 50 mm |
| Young's modulus | E | 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm² |

**Find:**
(i) Thickness of leaves
(ii) Number of leaves from stress consideration
(iii) Number of leaves from deflection consideration
(iv) Deflection and stress check

---

### **Solution Step-by-Step:**

#### **Part (a): Thickness of Leaves**

Using the bending stress equation, solving for thickness t:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^2} \\[4pt]
t^2 &= \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b} \\[4pt]
t &= \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b}}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming an initial guess of n = 8 leaves (full-length) for first iteration:

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 25000 \times 1000}{2 \times 8 \times 75 \times 350}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{7.5 \times 10^7}{4.2 \times 10^5}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{178.57} \\[4pt]
&= 13.36 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Adopt t = 13 mm (standard size) or t = 14 mm** — let us verify with t = 13 mm.

---

#### **Part (b): Number of Leaves from Stress Consideration**

Using t = 13 mm and solving for n:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{stress}} &= \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot b \cdot t^2 \cdot \sigma_b} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3 \times 25000 \times 1000}{2 \times 75 \times (13)^2 \times 350} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^7}{2 \times 75 \times 169 \times 350} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^7}{8.8725 \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&= 8.45 \approx 9 \text{ leaves}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Part (c): Number of Leaves from Deflection Consideration**

Using t = 13 mm and δ = 50 mm:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{def}} &= \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{2 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3 \cdot \delta} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3 \times 25000 \times (1000)^3}{2 \times (2.1 \times 10^5) \times 75 \times (13)^3 \times 50} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^{13}}{2 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 75 \times 2197 \times 50} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^{13}}{3.4603 \times 10^{12}} \\[4pt]
&= 21.68 \approx 22 \text{ leaves}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The required number from **deflection (22 leaves) is much greater than from stress (9 leaves)**. The design must satisfy BOTH conditions, so we adopt the larger value.

**Adopted:** n = 22 leaves total (with 8 full-length leaves + 14 graduated leaves)

---

#### **Part (d): Verification — Stress and Deflection Check**

**Verification with t = 13 mm and n = 22:**

**Stress check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3 \times 25000 \times 1000}{2 \times 22 \times 75 \times 169} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^7}{5.577 \times 10^5} \\[4pt]
&= 134.5 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Since **134.5 N/mm² < 350 N/mm²** ✓ **SAFE** (with high FOS)

**Deflection check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{2 \cdot n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3 \times 25000 \times 10^9}{2 \times 22 \times (2.1 \times 10^5) \times 75 \times 2197} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^{13}}{1.5195 \times 10^{12}} \\[4pt]
&= 49.36 \text{ mm} \approx 50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Since **49.36 mm ≈ 50 mm** ✓ **WITHIN LIMIT**

**Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Final Value |
|-----------|-------------|
| Total number of leaves (n) | 22 |
| Full-length leaves (n_f) | 8 |
| Graduated leaves (n_g) | 14 |
| Thickness of each leaf (t) | 13 mm |
| Width of each leaf (b) | 75 mm |
| Effective length (L) | 1000 mm |
| Calculated stress | 134.5 N/mm² |
| Calculated deflection | 49.36 mm |
| Factor of Safety (FOS) | 350/134.5 ≈ 2.6 |

---

## 3.4 Design of Leaf Spring with Graduated Leaves — Modified Formula

When the spring has both full-length and graduated leaves:

**Total number of leaves** = $n = n_f + n_g$

**Effective number of leaves for deflection:**

$$n_{\text{eff}} = n_f + \frac{2}{3} \cdot n_g$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 2/3 factor?** Graduated leaves do not extend full length, so they contribute only about 2/3 of their bending stiffness to the overall deflection (because their *average* bending moment is approximately 2/3 of the master's).

The bending stress is calculated using only the **full-length leaves** at the center section:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n_f \cdot b \cdot t^2}$$

The deflection is calculated using the **effective number of leaves**:

$$\delta = \frac{3 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{2 \cdot n_{\text{eff}} \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$$

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Leaf Spring System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a leaf spring assembly, mapping the load path from road input to chassis reaction:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Road Surface Input] --> B[Tire Assembly]
    B --> C[Axle Housing]
    C --> D[U-Bolt Clamp]
    D --> E[Master Leaf - Longest Leaf with Eyes]
    E --> F[Full Length Leaves Stack]
    F --> G[Graduated Leaves Stack]
    G --> H[Rebound Clips - Energy Limiters]
    H --> I[Center Bolt - Alignment]
    E --> J[Shackle Eye Front]
    E --> K[Shackle Pin Rear]
    J --> L[Chassis Front Bracket]
    K --> M[Shackle Link]
    M --> N[Chassis Rear Bracket]
    F --> O[Load Distribution Network]
    G --> O
    O --> P[Bending Stress Calculation]
    P --> Q[Safety Check vs Allowable Stress]
    O --> R[Deflection Calculation]
    R --> S[Comfort Check vs Allowable Deflection]
    Q --> T[Validated Spring Design]
    S --> T
```

## 4.2 Force & Stress Distribution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LoadA[Applied Load W at Center] --> BM[Bending Moment Distribution]
    BM --> BM1[Max Moment at Center = WL divide by 4]
    BM --> BM2[Zero Moment at Eye Ends]
    BM1 --> Stress[Stress Distribution sigma = M divide by Z]
    BM2 --> Stress
    Stress --> S1[Max Stress in Master Leaf]
    Stress --> S2[Variable Stress in Graduated Leaves]
    S1 --> ResCheck[Safety Factor Calculation]
    S2 --> ResCheck
    ResCheck --> R1[If sigma less than sigma allowable - DESIGN SAFE]
    ResCheck --> R2[If sigma greater than sigma allowable - INCREASE n OR t]
```

## 4.3 Master Leaf Geometric Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EyeL[Left Eye - Shackle Pin Connection] -->|Distance L divide by 2| CenterBolt[Center Bolt Location - Axle Mount]
    CenterBolt -->|Distance L divide by 2| EyeR[Right Eye - Fixed Pin]
    EyeL --> MasterLeaf[Master Leaf - Full Length 2L]
    EyeR --> MasterLeaf
    CenterBolt --> LoadApply[Central Load W Applied]
    MasterLeaf --> FullLeaves[Full Length Helper Leaves]
    MasterLeaf --> GradLeaves[Graduated Leaves - Progressively Shorter]
    FullLeaves --> ClipSet[Rebound Clip Locations]
    GradLeaves --> ClipSet
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LOOP[Load Transfer Loop]
        direction LR
        R[Road Force Input] ==> T[Tire and Axle]
        T ==> U[U-Bolt Group Transfers Load]
        U ==> M[Master Leaf - Primary Load Bearer]
        M ==> F[Full Length Leaves - Secondary]
        F ==> G[Graduated Leaves - Tertiary]
        G ==> RB[Rebound Clips - Limit Overshoot]
        RB ==> C[Chassis - Receives Smoothed Load]
    end
    M -.-> EyeEnd[Eye Region - Highest Stress Concentration]
    F -.-> MidSpan[Middle Region - Maximum Bending Moment]
    G -.-> TipEnd[Tip End - Minimal Stress]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Insight:** The leaf spring's *block-level architecture* reveals that load flows from the central U-bolt region *outward* through progressively weaker (shorter) leaves, with the master leaf carrying the highest stress concentration near its **eye region** due to combined bending + shear + bearing stress.
