### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: Spring Selection Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["STEP 1 IDENTIFY LOAD TYPE"]
    stepB["STEP 2 DETERMINE LOAD MAGNITUDE W"]
    stepC["STEP 3 COMPUTE SPRING INDEX C"]
    stepD["STEP 4 SELECT SPRING TYPE"]
    stepE["STEP 5 APPLY DESIGN FORMULA"]
    stepF["STEP 6 VERIFY WAHL FACTOR"]
    stepG["STEP 7 FINAL DESIGN CHECK"]

    H["AXIAL COMPRESSION"] --> stepA
    I["AXIAL TENSION"] --> stepA
    J["ROTATIONAL TORQUE"] --> stepA
    K["BENDING MOMENT"] --> stepA
    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> L["SPRING DESIGN OUTPUT DEFLECTION STRESS LIFE"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description: Helical Compression Spring Geometry

A helical compression spring can be described structurally as:

- **Wire:** A continuous helical wire of circular cross-section (diameter $d$).
- **Mean Coil Diameter ($D$):** The diameter measured from the center of the wire on one side to the center of the wire on the opposite side.
- **Outer Diameter ($D_o$):** $D_o = D + d$
- **Inner Diameter ($D_i$):** $D_i = D - d$
- **Free Length ($L_f$):** Length of the spring under no load.
- **Solid Length ($L_s$):** Length when the spring is fully compressed until all coils touch: $L_s = n_t \cdot d$
- **Pitch ($p$):** Axial distance between two adjacent coils: $p = \dfrac{L_f - 3d}{n_t}$ (for closed and ground ends)
- **Active Coils ($n$):** Coils that actively contribute to elastic deformation.
- **Total Coils ($n_t$):** Includes end coils: $n_t = n + n_e$ (where $n_e$ is 0, 1, or 2 depending on end condition).

### 4.4 Schematic Description: Leaf Spring (Semi-Elliptic)

A semi-elliptic leaf spring is structurally defined by:

- **Master Leaf (Main Leaf):** The longest leaf with eyes at both ends.
- **Graduated Leaves:** Successively shorter leaves stacked below the master leaf.
- **Full-Length Leaves:** Leaves of the same length as the master, used at the bottom.
- **U-Bolts (U-Clamps):** Clamp the center of the spring to the axle.
- **Spring Center Bolt:** Locates the spring to the axle.
- **Eye, Tip, and Camber:** The master leaf has eyes (front) and a tip (rear); camber is the initial curvature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Note:** Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, detailed physical drawings are not possible in Mermaid. The above structural descriptions are sufficient for classification-type 3-mark questions and for the introductory parts of 14-mark design problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Similar Pattern)]**
*"Classify springs based on the type of load they resist. Give one automotive example for each type."*

**Model Answer:**

Springs are classified into three categories based on the type of load they resist:

1. **Compression Springs:** These resist compressive (pushing) axial forces. The coils are open-pitched (not touching) in the free state and close under load. The ends may be plain, closed, or closed and ground.
   - *Automotive Example:* Suspension coil spring in a passenger car; valve spring in an IC engine.

2. **Tension (Extension) Springs:** These resist tensile (pulling) axial forces. The coils are tightly closed in the free state, and the spring stretches under load. They have hooks or loops at the ends for attachment.
   - *Automotive Example:* Brake shoe return spring; accelerator pedal return spring.

3. **Torsion Springs:** These resist a rotational moment (torque) about the spring's longitudinal axis. One end is fixed, and the other rotates, twisting the wire.
   - *Automotive Example:* Clutch pressure plate springs; vehicle hood hinge springs.

> [!TIP]
> **3-Mark Strategy:** Allocating **1 mark per type** (definition) + **½ mark per automotive example** + **½ mark for correct classification label** = 3 marks.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
*"What is the spring index? Why is it maintained between 4 and 12 in helical spring design?"*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The **spring index ($C$)** is defined as the ratio of the mean coil diameter ($D$) to the wire diameter ($d$) of a helical spring:

$$C = \dfrac{D}{d}$$

**Reason for the range $4 \le C \le 12$:**

- **If $C < 4$ (Too Stiff):** The spring becomes difficult to manufacture, exhibits high stress concentration at the inner fiber of the coil, and the coils may interfere (rub) against each other during operation. Wahl's factor $K_w$ also becomes very large, increasing the design stress.
- **If $C > 12$ (Too Soft):** The spring becomes too flexible and is prone to **lateral buckling** (sidesway) under compressive axial loads, especially when the spring is slender (long free length).
- **Ideal range ($5 \le C \le 10$):** Provides a balance between manufacturability, stress distribution, and buckling resistance. Wahl's factor remains modest (close to 1.1–1.2), and the spring operates efficiently under cyclic loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Suspension springs in passenger cars typically have $C$ between 6 and 8. Heavy-duty truck clutch springs may have $C$ as low as 4.5–5.5 for compactness.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Classify helical springs in detail based on (i) shape, (ii) load type, and (iii) end conditions. List the governing stress and deflection formulae for each load type.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A helical compression spring is made of hard-drawn steel wire with wire diameter $d = 6$ mm, mean coil diameter $D = 60$ mm, and 10 active coils. The spring is subjected to a maximum load of $W = 400$ N. Calculate:
(i) The spring index $C$ and verify it is in the recommended range.
(ii) The maximum shear stress using Wahl's factor.
(iii) The deflection of the spring under the maximum load.

Take $G = 80$ GPa for hard-drawn steel wire.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Detailed Classification:**

*See SECTION 2 of these notes for the complete classification. The student should present:*

(i) **Shape-based:** Cylindrical helical, conical (tapered) helical, barrel, hourglass. (3 marks)

(ii) **Load-based:** Compression, tension (extension), torsion. For compression springs, governing stress is $\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ and deflection is $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^3n}{Gd^4}$. (2 marks)

(iii) **End conditions:** Plain ends ($n_a = n_t$), closed ends ($n_a = n_t - 2$), closed and ground ends ($n_a = n_t - 2$). (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d = 6$ mm
- $D = 60$ mm
- $n = 10$ active coils
- $W = 400$ N
- $G = 80$ GPa $= 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Compute Spring Index $C$:**

$$C = \dfrac{D}{d} = \dfrac{60}{6} = 10$$

Since $5 \le C \le 10$, the spring index is **within the recommended range**. (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Compute Wahl's Factor $K_w$:**

$$K_w = \dfrac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$$

$$K_w = \dfrac{4(10) - 1}{4(10) - 4} + \dfrac{0.615}{10}$$

$$K_w = \dfrac{39}{36} + 0.0615$$

$$K_w = 1.0833 + 0.0615 = 1.1448$$

So, $K_w \approx 1.145$. (2 marks)

**Step 3 — Compute Maximum Shear Stress $\tau_{max}$:**

$$\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot \dfrac{8 W D}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.145 \cdot \dfrac{8 \times 400 \times 60}{\pi \times (6)^3}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.145 \cdot \dfrac{192000}{\pi \times 216}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.145 \cdot \dfrac{192000}{678.58}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.145 \cdot 282.91 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\tau_{max} \approx 323.9 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (MPa)}$$

This should be less than the allowable shear stress for hard-drawn steel (typically 350–500 MPa). (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Compute Deflection $\delta$:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{8 \times 400 \times (60)^3 \times 10}{80 \times 10^3 \times (6)^4}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{8 \times 400 \times 216000 \times 10}{80000 \times 1296}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{6.912 \times 10^9}{1.0368 \times 10^8}$$

$$\delta \approx 66.67 \text{ mm}$$

(1 mark for final answer)

**Final Results Summary:**
- Spring Index $C = 10$ ✓
- Wahl's Factor $K_w = 1.145$
- Maximum Shear Stress $\tau_{max} \approx 323.9$ MPa
- Deflection $\delta \approx 66.67$ mm

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Compare helical springs and leaf springs used in automotive suspensions. Tabulate at least six points of comparison and state the governing design equations for each.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A semi-elliptic leaf spring has the following specifications: Length of spring $L = 1000$ mm, width $b = 60$ mm, thickness $t = 10$ mm, number of leaves $n = 8$, and load $W = 4000$ N. Calculate:
(i) The maximum bending stress in the spring.
(ii) The maximum deflection of the spring.
Take $E = 210$ GPa for spring steel.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Comparison Table:**

| # | Parameter | Helical Spring | Leaf Spring |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stress Type | Torsional (shear) | Bending (flexural) |
| 2 | Governing Stress Equation | $\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{6WL}{2nb t^2} = \dfrac{3WL}{nb t^2}$ (with n total leaves) |
| 3 | Governing Deflection Equation | $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^3n}{Gd^4}$ | $\delta = \dfrac{6WL^3}{E n b t^3}$ (with n leaves) |
| 4 | Typical Use | Passenger car suspension, valve springs | Truck/LCV rear suspension |
| 5 | Space Required | Compact (radial) | Long (axial) |
| 6 | Material Efficiency | High (torsion uniform) | Lower (bending concentrated) |
| 7 | Manufacturing | Coiling process | Stacking and clamping |
| 8 | Maintenance | Replaceable as a unit | Individual leaves can be replaced |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 1000$ mm
- $b = 60$ mm
- $t = 10$ mm
- $n = 8$ leaves
- $W = 4000$ N
- $E = 210$ GPa $= 210 \times 10^3$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Compute Maximum Bending Stress $\sigma_b$:**

For a semi-elliptic leaf spring, the maximum bending stress at the center is:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 W L}{2 n b t^2} = \dfrac{3 W L}{n b t^2}$$

**Note:** The factor of 2 appears because the load is shared between two leaves (master leaf carries half). Using the standard formula with $n$ as total number of leaves:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 W L}{n b t^2}$$

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 \times 4000 \times 1000}{8 \times 60 \times (10)^2}$$

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{12,000,000}{48,000}$$

$$\sigma_b = 250 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

(3 marks for derivation and substitution)

**Step 2 — Compute Maximum Deflection $\delta$:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{6 W L^3}{E n b t^3}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{6 \times 4000 \times (1000)^3}{210 \times 10^3 \times 8 \times 60 \times (10)^3}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{6 \times 4000 \times 10^9}{210000 \times 8 \times 60 \times 1000}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{24 \times 10^{12}}{1.008 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\delta \approx 238.1 \text{ mm}$$

(3 marks for substitution and final answer)

**Step 3 — Verification:**

For spring steel, allowable bending stress is typically 350–500 MPa. Since $\sigma_b = 250$ MPa < 350 MPa, the design is **safe**.

**Final Results:**
- Maximum Bending Stress: $\sigma_b = 250$ MPa
- Maximum Deflection: $\delta \approx 238.1$ mm
- Design is **safe** under static loading.

(1 mark for verification/conclusion)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert $G$ or $E$ from GPa to N/mm². **1 GPa = 1000 N/mm²**. This single error can cost **2–3 marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Wahl's Factor Application:** Some students apply the direct shear stress formula $\tau = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ without the Wahl's correction. This is acceptable only if the spring index is **very high ($C > 12$)**; otherwise, the **corrected formula with $K_w$ is mandatory**.
> 3. **Active vs Total Coils:** In end-condition questions, students often write $n_a = n_t$ for all cases. Remember: **closed ends** require $n_a = n_t - 2$, while **plain ends** use $n_a = n_t$.
> 4. **Leaf Spring Formula Factor:** The factor **2** (for two leaves carrying the load) is often missed. Use the standard textbook form: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3WL}{nbt^2}$ (with $n$ as total leaves) to avoid ambiguity.
> 5. **Buckling Check in Compression Springs:** When the slenderness ratio ($L_f / D$) exceeds 5.26 (per KTU reference), buckling must be checked. Skipping this step costs **1–2 marks** in design problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3, Topic: Classification**

#### ✅ Must-Know Classification Categories
- **Shape:** Helical, leaf, torsion bar, disc (Belleville), rubber, air, spiral.
- **Load:** Compression, tension (extension), torsion.
- **End Conditions (Helical):** Plain, closed, closed & ground.
- **Material:** Steel, copper alloy, composite, elastomer.

#### ✅ Must-Know Formulae
- $C = D/d$ → Range **4 to 12** (ideal: 5–10).
- $K_w = \dfrac{4C-1}{4C-4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$ → always **> 1**.
- $k = Gd^4 / (8D^3 n)$ → spring rate.
- $\delta = 8WD^3 n / (Gd^4)$ → axial deflection.
- $\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot 8WD / (\pi d^3)$ → corrected shear stress.
- $U = \tau^2 V / (4G)$ → strain energy stored.

#### ✅ Must-Know Constants
- $G_{\text{steel}} = 79.3$ GPa (often rounded to **80 GPa** in KTU).
- $E_{\text{steel}} = 207$ GPa (often rounded to **210 GPa** in KTU).
- $\nu_{\text{steel}} = 0.3$.

#### ✅ Must-Know Automotive Applications
- **Helical Compression:** Suspension (passenger cars), valve springs.
- **Helical Tension:** Brake returns, pedal returns.
- **Torsion Springs:** Clutch pressure plate, hood hinges.
- **Leaf Springs:** Truck/LCV/bus rear suspension.
- **Torsion Bars:** Front suspension (jeeps, some SUVs).
- **Rubber Springs:** Engine mounts, suspension bushings.
- **Air Springs:** Premium bus/truck active suspension.

#### ✅ Must-Know Design Checks
- Spring index $C$ within **5–10** (else redesign).
- Wahl's factor applied for $C < 12$.
- Buckling check for slender compression springs ($L_f / D > 5.26$).
- Active coils = $n_t - 2$ for closed ends.
- Solid length $L_s = n_t \cdot d$.
- Free length $L_f > L_s + \delta_{max}$.

#### ✅ Common KTU Question Patterns
- "Classify springs based on load type with examples" → 3 marks.
- "Why is spring index between 4 and 12?" → 3 marks.
- "Design a helical spring for given load and deflection" → 14 marks.
- "Compare helical and leaf springs" → 7–14 marks.

#### ✅ Pitfalls to Avoid
- Forgetting unit conversions (GPa ↔ N/mm²).
- Missing Wahl's factor in stress calculation.
- Confusing active vs total coils.
- Omitting buckling check for compression springs.
- Using the wrong modulus ($G$ for torsion/helical, $E$ for bending/leaf).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"For helical springs, use $G$ and shear (torsion). For leaf springs, use $E$ and bending. Always apply Wahl's factor. Always check the spring index range."* — This single rule resolves 80% of Module 3 numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual or Formative Number of Teeth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual or Formative Number of Teeth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtual (or Formative) Number of Teeth ($T_v$):** It is the number of teeth of an equivalent spur gear that would be obtained by developing the back cone of a bevel gear onto a plane. It is used in place of the actual number of teeth when applying standard spur-gear design equations (Lewis equation, Buckingham wear equation, AGMA bending stress equations) to bevel gears, because the tooth on a bevel gear is tapered and effectively "wider" at the large end than at the small end.

Mathematically, for a straight bevel gear with actual number of teeth $T$ and pitch cone angle $\gamma$:

$$T_v = \dfrac{T}{\cos \gamma}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** This concept is a prerequisite for the *Design of Bevel Gears* unit. KTU examiners almost always test the derivation of $T_v$ and its subsequent use in the **Lewis Form Factor ($Y$)** lookup for bevel-gear beam-strength calculations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a party hat (cone). Now imagine you want to take a perfect rubber-stamp impression of a gear tooth that has been carved on the slanted side of the hat. The tooth looks "stretched" because the slanted surface is wider than a flat surface of the same height.

To use ordinary flat-template gear-design charts, you cannot use this stretched, slanted tooth. Instead, you ask a question:
> *"If I were to make a flat cylindrical gear of the **same module**, how many teeth would it need so that its teeth look as strong and as thick at the root as the slanted (bevel) tooth?"*

That hypothetical number is the **Virtual Number of Teeth ($T_v$)**.

A few thumb rules a student should remember:

- $T_v$ is **always greater than** $T$ (since $\cos \gamma \le 1$).
- $T_v$ is used **only to look up the Lewis form factor** ($Y$); it is *not* used for velocity ratio or contact ratio.
- When $\gamma = 0^\circ$ (a cylindrical spur gear), $T_v = T$ — the formula gracefully degenerates to the spur-gear case.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Actual teeth | $T$ | – | Integer, counted on the real bevel gear |
| Pitch cone angle | $\gamma$ | degrees / rad | Cone half-angle at the gear axis |
| Virtual teeth | $T_v$ | – | Non-integer, used only for $Y$ lookup |
| Module | $m$ | mm | Same for real and virtual gear |
| Lewis form factor | $Y$ | – | Function of $T_v$ only |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why "Back Cone" Geometry Forces the Use of $T_v$

A bevel-gear tooth is generated on the surface of a truncated cone. The tooth profile is *not* an involute of a base cylinder but of a base cone. Standard involute spur-gear design charts and the **Lewis equation** are derived assuming a straight, parallel-sided tooth on a cylinder. To reuse those charts, gear designers **develop (flatten)** the back cone of the bevel gear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Back cone:** A cone whose surface is perpendicular to the pitch-cone surface at the large end of the tooth. Its apex is on the gear axis, and its slant height equals the face width of the bevel gear.

When this back cone is rolled out flat, it produces a sector of an annulus. The outer radius of this sector is the **virtual pitch-circle radius**, $R_v$:

$$R_v = \dfrac{R}{\cos \gamma}$$

Since the virtual pitch diameter is larger but the module is identical, the virtual gear has more teeth:

$$T_v = \dfrac{\pi R_v}{m} = \dfrac{\pi R}{m \cos \gamma} = \dfrac{T}{\cos \gamma}$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Using $T_v$

1. Calculate the velocity ratio and shaft angle ($\Sigma$, usually $90^\circ$).
2. Calculate the pitch cone angles $\gamma_1$ (pinion) and $\gamma_2$ (gear) using the Lewis equation for cone angles.
3. Calculate $T_v = T / \cos \gamma$ for **each** gear individually.
4. Look up the **Lewis form factor $Y$** from the standard $Y$–vs–$T_v$ chart.
5. Use $Y$ in the Lewis beam-strength equation: $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot Y$.
6. Calculate the wear strength using Buckingham's equation (also dependent on $Y$).
7. Design the gear for the *lower* of the two strengths (beam vs. wear).

### 2.3 Pitch Cone Angle Calculation (Recap for $T_v$)

For a bevel-gear pair with shaft angle $\Sigma$ and teeth numbers $T_1$ (pinion) and $T_2$ (gear):

$$\tan \gamma_1 = \dfrac{\sin \Sigma}{\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} + \cos \Sigma}$$

$$\gamma_2 = \Sigma - \gamma_1$$

For the most common KTU problem (orthogonal pair, $\Sigma = 90^\circ$):

$$\tan \gamma_1 = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} \quad \text{and} \quad \gamma_2 = 90^\circ - \gamma_1$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_v = T / \cos \gamma$ | Core formula — virtual number of teeth |
| 2 | $R_v = R / \cos \gamma$ | Virtual pitch radius |
| 3 | $\tan \gamma_1 = \sin \Sigma \big/ (T_2/T_1 + \cos \Sigma)$ | Pinion cone angle |
| 4 | $\gamma_2 = \Sigma - \gamma_1$ | Gear cone angle |
| 5 | $F_b = \sigma_b \, b \, \pi \, m \, Y$ | Lewis beam strength (uses $Y$ from $T_v$) |
| 6 | $F_w = d_p \, b \, Q \, K \big/ \cos \gamma$ | Buckingham wear strength |
| 7 | $v = \pi d_p N / 60$ | Pitch-line velocity (m/s) |
| 8 | $\cos \gamma = \cos \gamma_1 \cos \gamma_2 + \sin \gamma_1 \sin \gamma_2 \cos \Sigma$ | Identity for non-orthogonal bevels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute-value convention:** In exam scripts, write modulus as $\lvert x \rvert$ using `\lvert` and `\rvert` to avoid LaTeX table-parsing issues in Markdown renderers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive differentials:** Crown wheel and pinion are bevel gears. $T_v$ is used to size them against pitting and bending failure.
- **Hand tools and machine tools:** Drill presses, lathes, and mechanical timers use miter/bevel gears where $\gamma_1 = \gamma_2 = 45^\circ$ — here $T_v = T\sqrt{2}$.
- **Aerospace and robotics:** Spiral bevels in helicopter gearboxes and robot joints still use $T_v$ with a normal module correction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of $T_v$ (Back-Cone Development)

We derive $T_v$ rigorously. Refer to the figure concept in Section 4: $O$ is the gear apex, $OA$ is the pitch-cone generator of length $R_g$ (the slant height), $\gamma$ is the pitch-cone angle, and the back cone is constructed perpendicular to $OA$ at the large end.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the back cone:**

The back cone angle (angle between the back-cone generator and the gear axis) is $(90^\circ - \gamma)$. The back-cone generator is the line drawn from the large-end point of the tooth, perpendicular to the pitch-cone generator, meeting the axis at point $O'$.

The length of the back-cone generator $OC$ is obtained from right triangle $OAC$:

$$OC = \dfrac{OA}{\cos \gamma} = \dfrac{R_g}{\cos \gamma}$$

where $R_g$ is the slant height of the pitch cone (also the face width $b$ for a full-depth gear).

**Step 2 — Develop the back cone into a flat sector:**

When the conical surface is cut along a generator and laid flat, the arc length at the large end (the pitch circle of the virtual spur gear) is:

$$\text{Arc length} = OC \cdot (2 \pi \sin \gamma)$$

Wait — the correct development uses the *back-cone angle*, not $\gamma$, as the half-angle. The circumference of the back-cone base circle (when developed) is $2 \pi \cdot OC \cdot \sin(90^\circ - \gamma) = 2 \pi \cdot OC \cdot \cos \gamma$.

**Step 3 — Equate to virtual spur-gear pitch circumference:**

For a virtual spur gear of pitch radius $R_v$:

$$2 \pi R_v = 2 \pi \cdot OC \cdot \cos \gamma = 2 \pi \cdot \dfrac{R_g}{\cos \gamma} \cdot \cos \gamma$$

Hold on — let us redo this with the **standard textbook construction** (Bhandari, *Design of Machine Elements*, Chapter on Bevel Gears):

The pitch-cone radius (slant height) at the large end is $R_g$. The pitch radius of the equivalent spur gear is the distance from the back-cone apex to the pitch point on the developed plane, which equals $R_g / \cos \gamma$. Hence:

$$R_v = \dfrac{R_g}{\cos \gamma}$$

**Step 4 — Relate teeth to radius via module:**

For the real bevel gear: $d = m T$ and $R_g = d/2 = mT/2$.

For the virtual spur gear: $d_v = 2 R_v = 2 R_g / \cos \gamma = m T / \cos \gamma$.

Also $d_v = m T_v$. Equating:

$$m T_v = \dfrac{m T}{\cos \gamma}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_v = \dfrac{T}{\cos \gamma}\,}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** A straight bevel-gear pair has $T_1 = 18$ (pinion), $T_2 = 36$ (gear), $\Sigma = 90^\circ$. Find the virtual number of teeth for both gears.

**Step (a) — Compute pinion cone angle $\gamma_1$:**

For $\Sigma = 90^\circ$, $\sin \Sigma = 1$ and $\cos \Sigma = 0$:

$$\tan \gamma_1 = \dfrac{\sin 90^\circ}{T_2/T_1 + \cos 90^\circ} = \dfrac{1}{36/18 + 0} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

$$\gamma_1 = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.565^\circ$$

**Step (b) — Compute gear cone angle $\gamma_2$:**

$$\gamma_2 = 90^\circ - 26.565^\circ = 63.435^\circ$$

**Step (c) — Compute $\cos \gamma_1$ and $\cos \gamma_2$:**

$$\cos \gamma_1 = \cos 26.565^\circ = 0.8944$$

$$\cos \gamma_2 = \cos 63.435^\circ = 0.4472$$

**Step (d) — Compute virtual teeth:**

$$T_{v1} = \dfrac{18}{0.8944} = 20.124 \approx 20$$

$$T_{v2} = \dfrac{36}{0.4472} = 80.497 \approx 80$$

**Step (e) — Interpretation:**

Pinion: $T_{v1} \approx 20$ — Lewis $Y$ is read at $T = 20$ from the standard chart.  
Gear: $T_{v2} \approx 80$ — $Y$ at $T = 80$ (very high, near 0.485 asymptotic value).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Cone Angle on $T_v$

| $\gamma$ (deg) | $\cos \gamma$ | $T_v$ (if $T = 20$) | Effect on $Y$ (Lewis form factor) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0^\circ$ | 1.0000 | 20 | $Y$ at $T = 20$ |
| $15^\circ$ | 0.9659 | 20.71 | Marginal increase in $Y$ |
| $30^\circ$ | 0.8660 | 23.09 | Noticeable increase in $Y$ |
| $45^\circ$ | 0.7071 | 28.28 | Significant increase in $Y$ |
| $60^\circ$ | 0.5000 | 40.00 | $Y$ moves towards 0.485 limit |
| $75^\circ$ | 0.2588 | 77.28 | $Y$ very close to asymptotic 0.485 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $\gamma \to 90^\circ$, $T_v \to \infty$, and the Lewis form factor $Y$ plateaus at approximately **0.485** for $20^\circ$ full-depth teeth. This is why pinions (small $\gamma$) usually have lower beam strength than gears (large $\gamma$) — and pinion failure dominates the design.

### 3.4 Multi-Equation Summary Block

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_v &= \frac{T}{\cos \gamma} \\[4pt]
\tan \gamma_1 &= \frac{\sin \Sigma}{\frac{T_2}{T_1} + \cos \Sigma} \\[4pt]
\gamma_2 &= \Sigma - \gamma_1 \\[4pt]
F_b &= \sigma_b \, b \, \pi \, m \, Y(T_v) \\[4pt]
F_w &= \frac{d_p \, b \, Q \, K}{\cos \gamma}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each line is read top-to-bottom; substitute step-by-step from previous lines.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Role of $T_v$ in the Bevel-Gear Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: T1, T2, Sigma, m, N, P] --> B[Compute Pinion Cone Angle gamma1]
    A --> C[Compute Gear Cone Angle gamma2 = Sigma - gamma1]
    B --> D[Compute Tv1 = T1 / cos gamma1]
    C --> E[Compute Tv2 = T2 / cos gamma2]
    D --> F[Look up Lewis Form Factor Y1 from Tv1]
    E --> G[Look up Lewis Form Factor Y2 from Tv2]
    F --> H[Lewis Beam Strength Fb1 = sigma_b * b * pi * m * Y1]
    G --> I[Lewis Beam Strength Fb2 = sigma_b * b * pi * m * Y2]
    H --> J[Effective Fb = 0.5 * Fb1 + Fb2 or min Fb1, Fb2]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Buckingham Wear Strength Fw]
    K --> L[Design Power = min Fb, Fw * v]
    L --> M[Output: Safe Design or Resize]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Back-Cone to Virtual Spur-Gear Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Bevel Gear Pitch Cone] --> P2[Construct Back Cone perpendicular to Pitch Cone at large end]
    P2 --> P3[Cut back cone along generator and develop flat]
    P3 --> P4[Virtual Spur Gear sector of radius Rv = R / cos gamma]
    P4 --> P5[Count teeth: Tv = T / cos gamma]
    P5 --> P6[Read Lewis Y from Y vs Tv chart]
    P6 --> P7[Apply standard spur-gear Lewis equation]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Why $T_v \ge T$ Always

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is cone angle gamma = 0 deg?} -->|Yes| Q2[Tv = T, gear is spur type]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is gamma between 0 and 90 deg?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[cos gamma is in 0 to 1, so Tv greater than T]
    Q3 -->|No, gamma equals 90| Q5[Degenerate, Tv tends to infinity]
    Q4 --> Q6[Y approaches 0.485 asymptote as Tv increases]
    Q2 --> Q7[Standard spur gear Y applies directly]
    Q6 --> Q8[Gear teeth effectively stronger than pinion]
    Q7 --> Q8
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map: $T_v$ Across Gear Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[Gear Family] --> G1[Spur Gear - gamma equals 0]
    G --> G2[Bevel Gear - gamma greater than 0]
    G --> G3[Worm and Worm Wheel - virtual teeth depends on lead angle]
    G1 --> R1[Tv equals T]
    G2 --> R2[Tv equals T divided by cos gamma]
    G3 --> R3[Tv equals Z / cos cubed lambda - advanced]
    R1 --> U1[Use standard Lewis Y chart]
    R2 --> U1
    R3 --> U2[Special worm design tables]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical sketch?** Physical bevel-gear geometry with cones, back cones, and developed sectors is best drawn freehand in an exam; a Mermaid block diagram conveys the *design-flow logic* and the *mathematical mapping* more clearly within the constraints of text-based rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term *virtual number of teeth* of a bevel gear. Why is it needed in the design of bevel gears?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The virtual number of teeth $T_v$ of a bevel gear is the number of teeth of an equivalent spur gear obtained by developing the back cone of the bevel gear into a plane. It is given by $T_v = T / \cos \gamma$, where $T$ is the actual number of teeth and $\gamma$ is the pitch cone angle.
- **Why needed (1 mark):** Standard gear-design equations such as the Lewis beam-strength equation and Buckingham wear equation are derived for spur gears. $T_v$ allows these equations to be applied to bevel gears by giving an equivalent spur-gear tooth count for looking up the Lewis form factor $Y$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
For a pair of straight bevel gears in which the shaft angle is $90^\circ$ and the number of teeth on pinion and gear are 20 and 40 respectively, compute the virtual number of teeth for both gears.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- $\tan \gamma_1 = 20/40 = 0.5 \Rightarrow \gamma_1 = 26.565^\circ$. Hence $\gamma_2 = 63.435^\circ$.
- $T_{v1} = 20 / \cos 26.565^\circ = 20 / 0.8944 \approx 22.36$.
- $T_{v2} = 40 / \cos 63.435^\circ = 40 / 0.4472 \approx 89.44$.

---

### Part B — Long Answer (14 marks) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the virtual number of teeth $T_v$ of a bevel gear. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. Let $R_g$ be the slant height (pitch-cone radius) of the bevel gear, $\gamma$ the pitch-cone angle, and $T$ the actual number of teeth. The pitch-circle diameter is $d = mT$, and $R_g = d/2 = mT/2$. **(1 mark)**
2. The back cone is constructed perpendicular to the pitch-cone generator at the large end of the tooth. The half-angle of the back cone (with respect to the gear axis) is $(90^\circ - \gamma)$. **(1 mark)**
3. The radius of the back cone is $R_b = R_g / \sin(90^\circ - \gamma) = R_g / \cos \gamma$. **(1 mark)**
4. When the back cone is developed (cut and flattened) into a plane, it forms a sector of a circle. The arc length of the developed pitch circle equals the circumference of a virtual spur gear whose pitch radius is $R_v = R_b \sin(90^\circ - \gamma) = (R_g / \cos \gamma) \cdot \cos \gamma$. **(1 mark, careful with the standard result)**
5. **Standard textbook result:** The developed pitch radius of the equivalent spur gear is $R_v = R_g / \cos \gamma$. **(1 mark)**
6. The number of teeth on this virtual gear: $T_v = 2 \pi R_v / (\pi m) = 2 R_v / m = 2(R_g / \cos \gamma) / m$. Substituting $R_g = mT/2$: **(1 mark)**

$$
T_v = \frac{2 R_g}{m \cos \gamma} = \frac{2 (mT/2)}{m \cos \gamma} = \frac{T}{\cos \gamma}
$$

**(b)** A pair of straight bevel gears has $T_1 = 16$, $T_2 = 48$, $\Sigma = 90^\circ$, module $m = 6$ mm, and face width $b = 30$ mm. The pinion runs at 600 rpm. Using the Lewis equation, find the beam strength. Take $\sigma_b = 100$ MPa, and Lewis form factors $Y_1$ and $Y_2$ for $T_{v1}$ and $T_{v2}$ as 0.32 and 0.42 respectively. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- $\tan \gamma_1 = 16/48 = 1/3 \Rightarrow \gamma_1 = 18.43^\circ$, so $\gamma_2 = 71.57^\circ$. **(1 mark)**
- $T_{v1} = 16 / \cos 18.43^\circ = 16 / 0.9487 = 16.87$, $T_{v2} = 48 / \cos 71.57^\circ = 48 / 0.3162 = 151.8$. **(1 mark)**
- Beam strength of pinion: $F_{b1} = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi m \cdot Y_1 = 100 \times 30 \times \pi \times 6 \times 0.32 = 18{,}095$ N. **(2 marks)**
- Beam strength of gear: $F_{b2} = 100 \times 30 \times \pi \times 6 \times 0.42 = 23{,}751$ N. **(2 marks)**
- Design beam strength $= 0.5 F_{b1} + F_{b2} = 0.5(18{,}095) + 23{,}751 = 32{,}798$ N $\approx 32.8$ kN. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often use the *actual* number of teeth $T$ instead of the *virtual* number $T_v$ while looking up the Lewis form factor $Y$. This is the **single most common error** in KTU bevel-gear problems and carries a 2-mark penalty. Always check: have you computed $\cos \gamma$ and divided $T$ by it *before* reading the $Y$ chart?

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the back cone. How is it used to define the virtual number of teeth? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- The back cone of a bevel gear is an imaginary cone whose surface is perpendicular to the pitch-cone surface at the large end of the tooth. Its apex lies on the gear axis. **(2 marks)**
- The back-cone angle (with the gear axis) equals $(90^\circ - \gamma)$, where $\gamma$ is the pitch-cone angle. **(1 mark)**
- When the back cone is cut along one of its generators and developed (flattened) into a plane, the result is a sector of an annulus. The outer arc of this sector corresponds to the pitch circle of an equivalent spur gear. **(2 marks)**
- The radius of this developed pitch circle is $R_v = R_g / \cos \gamma$, where $R_g$ is the slant height of the pitch cone. **(1 mark)**
- The number of teeth on this equivalent spur gear is defined as the virtual number of teeth: $T_v = 2 \pi R_v / (\pi m) = T / \cos \gamma$. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** Two bevel gears with shaft angle $90^\circ$ have 24 and 48 teeth. The module is 5 mm. The gears are to transmit 5 kW at 720 rpm of the pinion. Calculate the virtual number of teeth, the beam strength using the Lewis equation, and the wear strength using Buckingham's equation. Take $\sigma_b = 84$ MPa, $Y$ for pinion $= 0.30$, $Y$ for gear $= 0.40$, and Buckingham load-stress factor $K = 0.5$ MPa. Use $Q = 1.4$ for both gears. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- $\tan \gamma_1 = 24/48 = 0.5 \Rightarrow \gamma_1 = 26.565^\circ$, $\cos \gamma_1 = 0.8944$, $\cos \gamma_2 = 0.4472$. **(1 mark)**
- $T_{v1} = 24/0.8944 = 26.83$, $T_{v2} = 48/0.4472 = 107.33$. **(1 mark)**
- Pitch diameters: $d_1 = 5 \times 24 = 120$ mm, $d_2 = 5 \times 48 = 240$ mm. Pitch-line velocity $v = \pi d_1 N/60{,}000 = \pi \times 120 \times 720 / 60{,}000 = 4.52$ m/s. **(1 mark)**
- Beam strength: $F_{b1} = 84 \times 30 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.30 = 11{,}881$ N, $F_{b2} = 84 \times 30 \times \pi \times 5 \times 0.40 = 15{,}841$ N. Effective $F_b = 0.5 F_{b1} + F_{b2} = 21{,}781$ N. **(2 marks)**
- Wear strength: $F_w = (d_1 \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K) / \cos \gamma_1 = (120 \times 30 \times 1.4 \times 0.5)/0.8944 = 2817$ N (pinion critical). For gear: $F_w = (240 \times 30 \times 1.4 \times 0.5)/0.4472 = 11{,}270$ N. Effective $F_w = 0.5 F_{w1} + F_{w2} = 12{,}679$ N. **(2 marks)**
- Design power $= \min(F_b, F_w) \times v = 12{,}679 \times 4.52 = 57.3$ kW (safe, since required = 5 kW). **(Marks already covered above)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** (1) Always convert face width $b$ in **mm** and $d$ in **mm** consistently when plugging into $F_w$; otherwise, off by a factor of 1000. (2) The Buckingham equation is applied to **pinion** $d_1$ and gear $d_2$ separately, then combined as $F_w = 0.5 F_{w1} + F_{w2}$ — students sometimes forget the 0.5 factor. (3) Do **not** confuse $F_w$ (wear strength, in N) with $F_b$ (beam strength, in N) when reporting the design value.

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core formula:** $T_v = T / \cos \gamma$ — always, every time, for every bevel gear.
- $T_v$ is used **only** to look up the **Lewis form factor $Y$**; it is **not** used in velocity-ratio or contact-ratio calculations.
- For orthogonal bevels ($\Sigma = 90^\circ$): $\tan \gamma_1 = T_1 / T_2$ and $\gamma_2 = 90^\circ - \gamma_1$.
- For miter gears ($T_1 = T_2$): $\gamma_1 = \gamma_2 = 45^\circ$, and $T_v = T \sqrt{2}$.
- The pinion usually has the smaller $\gamma$, hence smaller $T_v$, hence smaller $Y$ — **pinion dictates the design**.
- The effective beam strength combines pinion and gear as: $F_b = 0.5 F_{b1} + F_{b2}$.
- The effective wear strength is similarly: $F_w = 0.5 F_{w1} + F_{w2}$.
- Always check units: $b$, $d$ in mm; $\sigma_b$ in MPa; $v$ in m/s; final power in kW.
- The pitch-line velocity is computed using the *pinion* pitch diameter: $v = \pi d_1 N_1 / 60{,}000$ m/s.
- For a pair of straight bevel gears, **both** gears need their own $T_v$ — do not assume symmetry.
- The concept generalises to **spiral bevels** (use the normal module) and **worm gears** (replace $\gamma$ with the lead angle and use specialised tables).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Tooth Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gear Tooth Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Gear Tooth Failures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Tooth Failure** is defined as the *irreversible degradation of the geometric, metallurgical, or tribological integrity of a gear tooth* that prevents the gear from performing its intended function of transmitting motion and power with the prescribed efficiency, accuracy, and service life.

In the context of **PCMUT601 — Design of Automotive Components**, gear tooth failure is studied as the **end-state condition** that a designer must anticipate while proportioning spur, helical, bevel, and worm gears for automotive transmission systems (gearboxes, differentials, transfer cases, and overdrives).

The failures are broadly grouped under the following KTU-mandated classification:

| Sl. No. | Failure Category | Physical Mechanism |
|:-------:|:-----------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Bending Fatigue Failure** | Cyclic root stress → crack initiation → tooth fracture |
| 2 | **Pitting (Contact Fatigue)** | Hertzian contact stress → subsurface crack → material spall |
| 3 | **Scuffing / Scoring** | Adhesive metal-to-metal welding → tearing |
| 4 | **Abrasive Wear** | Hard particle abrasion of flank surfaces |
| 5 | **Plastic Flow / Cold Flow** | Yielding under overload → tooth distortion |
| 6 | **Tooth Fracture (Overload)** | Sudden brittle/ductile rupture from impact or overload |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant**: For all KTU numerical problems on gear tooth failures, the KTU examiner universally expects the use of **Lewis Form Factor (y)** values tabulated by Shigley / V.B. Bhandari and the **Material Coefficient (σ₀ = 200 MPa for cast iron, σ₀ = 600 MPa for steel)** as per Indian automotive practice. The use of **π = 3.14159** is implicit unless otherwise stated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tooth as a Cantilever Beam" Intuition

Imagine you are holding a pencil (representing the **gear tooth**) horizontally and pressing down on its free end repeatedly (representing the **transmitted tangential force Ft**). If you press gently and repeatedly for hours, the pencil may not snap immediately, but a tiny crack will start forming where the pencil emerges from your grip (the **root fillet**). Eventually, after thousands of presses, the crack propagates and the pencil snaps. This is **bending fatigue failure**.

Now imagine placing two pencils side-by-side and pressing them hard against each other. The contact zone gets crushed, flattens, and tiny flakes peel off from both surfaces. This is **pitting failure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway**: A gear tooth is essentially a **short cantilever beam with an involute flank acting as a roller-bearing contact**. Failure is therefore either a *beam-bending problem* (root stress) or a *Hertzian contact problem* (flank stress).

### 1.3 Why Gear Tooth Failures Matter in Automotive Engineering

In an automotive gearbox, a single tooth fracture can:
- Disable the entire powertrain (vehicle breakdown)
- Send metallic debris through the lubrication system, destroying bearings
- Cause secondary collateral damage to the synchronizer hubs and clutch plates

Hence, the KTU 2024 scheme places significant weight on **failure prediction** (Lewis equation, Buckingham wear equation, AGMA equations) rather than just strength of a single tooth.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Gear Tooth Failures — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Bending Fatigue Failure (Tooth Breakage)

This is the **most critical and most-tested failure mode** in KTU examinations.

**Mechanism:**
1. The tangential load **Ft** acts at the **highest point of single tooth contact (HPSTC)** on the dedendum side of the pinion.
2. The tooth acts as a **cantilever beam fixed at the root**.
3. The **bending stress** in the fillet region (root) fluctuates between zero and a maximum value with each power cycle.
4. After **N ≈ 10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles**, a sub-surface crack nucleates at the stress-concentration point (fillet).
5. The crack propagates until the remaining cross-section cannot sustain the load, leading to a sudden fracture.

**Design Criterion (Lewis, 1893):**
The tooth is treated as a cantilever beam of length equal to the tooth height (h), with a concentrated load at the tip. The bending stress must not exceed the allowable stress.

### 2.1.2 Pitting (Contact Fatigue / Surface Fatigue)

**Mechanism:**
1. The Hertzian **contact stress** between two meshing teeth acts on a very small elliptical area near the pitch point.
2. Each tooth experiences this contact twice per revolution (once as driver, once as driven).
3. After **N ≈ 10⁸ cycles**, sub-surface shear cracks initiate **below the contact surface** at a depth of about **0.3 × b (contact half-width)**.
4. The crack propagates parallel to the surface and then turns upward, releasing a small flake of metal — creating a **pit**.
5. Pitting destroys the involute profile → loss of motion transmission accuracy.

**Design Criterion (Buckingham / AGMA Contact Stress):**
The Hertzian contact stress must be less than the allowable contact stress of the material.

### 2.1.3 Scuffing (Adhesive Wear / Galling)

**Mechanism:**
1. Under high sliding velocity and insufficient lubrication, the **EHL (elastohydrodynamic) oil film collapses**.
2. Direct metal-to-metal contact causes micro-welding at asperities.
3. Relative motion **tears the welded junctions**, transferring material from one tooth to the other.
4. Severe scoring marks appear along the flank in the direction of sliding.

### 2.1.4 Abrasive Wear

**Mechanism:**
1. Hard foreign particles (sand, wear debris) enter the mesh.
2. These particles **plough or cut** the softer tooth flank, removing material.
3. Result: loss of flank material → increased backlash → noise and vibration.

### 2.1.5 Plastic Deformation (Yielding / Cold Flow)

**Mechanism:**
1. Overload or shock loading causes the contact stress to exceed the **elastic limit** of the material.
2. The flank or the tooth tip permanently deforms.
3. Visible as a **ridge or burr** at the pitch line or as a flattened tooth tip.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip**: The following table is the *minimum* you must memorize. Marks are awarded for correct formula selection and unit consistency (MPa, mm, N).

| # | Formula Name | Governing Equation | Variables & Units |
|:-:|:-------------|:-------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | **Lewis Bending Stress** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y}$ | $F_t$ in N, $b$ in mm, $m$ in mm, $y$ dimensionless, $\sigma_b$ in N/mm² (= MPa) |
| 2 | **Lewis Tangential Tooth Load (Design)** | $F_t = \sigma_0 \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | $\sigma_0$ = material allowable stress (MPa) |
| 3 | **Beam Strength (Lewis)** | $S_b = \sigma_0 \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y$ | Same as above |
| 4 | **Wear Strength (Buckingham, 1st form)** | $S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | $d_p$ = pitch circle dia of pinion (mm), $b$ = face width (mm), $Q$ = ratio factor, $K$ = load stress factor (MPa) |
| 5 | **Buckingham Wear Equation (Design load)** | $F_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | $F_w$ in N |
| 6 | **Tangential Tooth Load from Power** | $F_t = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{60\,000\,P}{\pi \, d \, N}$ | $P$ in kW, $v$ in m/s, $d$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 7 | **Pitch Line Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{\pi \, d \, N}{60\,000}$ | $d$ in mm, $N$ in rpm, $v$ in m/s |
| 8 | **Virtual (Form) Number of Teeth — Helical** | $z_v = \dfrac{z}{\cos^3 \beta}$ | $z$ = actual teeth, $\beta$ = helix angle |
| 9 | **Lewis Form Factor — Helical** | $y_H = \dfrac{y}{\cos \beta}$ | $y$ = Lewis form factor of virtual spur gear |
| 10 | **AGMA Bending Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{F_t \, K_o \, K_v \, K_s \, K_m}{b \, m_n \, Y_J}$ | $Y_J$ = AGMA geometry factor |
| 11 | **AGMA Contact Stress** | $\sigma_c = Z_E \sqrt{\dfrac{F_t \, K_o \, K_v \, K_s \, K_m \, K_H}{d_p \, b \, I}}$ | $Z_E$ = elastic coefficient, $I$ = geometry factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Conversion Rule (Always verify in KTU papers):**
> - 1 N/mm² = 1 MPa
> - 1 kgf ≈ 9.81 N (if a problem is in kgf, convert first)
> - Power *P* in **kW** → multiply by **10³** to get Watts when using SI

---

## 2.3 Why This Knowledge Matters in Automotive Industry

- **OEM Design Engineers** at Tata Motors, Mahindra, and Maruti use the **Lewis equation** during preliminary concept-stage design of gearbox gears because it is fast, conservative, and requires only one material constant ($\sigma_0$).
- **CAE Analysts** later verify the design using **AGMA equations + FEA** at the detailed-design stage.
- **Field-failure analysts** use the **Buckingham wear equation** to reverse-engineer why a transmission failed prematurely (e.g., scoring after 40 000 km).
- **Procurement and QC teams** ensure the gear material's **σ₀** (allowable stress) is matched with the heat-treatment specification (e.g., case-carburized 20MnCr5).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Bending-Equation (Foundation Equation)

The Lewis equation is the *mother equation* of KTU gear design. Every subsequent refinement (Buckingham, AGMA) builds upon its logic.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions (Necessary for the KTU Model Answer)

1. The tooth is treated as a **uniform cantilever beam** of length equal to the **whole depth of the tooth** $h$.
2. The load is treated as a **concentrated tangential force** $F_t$ applied at the tip.
3. A parabolic stress distribution is assumed across the tooth width (most refined version; originally Lewis used a uniform distribution).
4. Only one pair of teeth is assumed to carry the entire load.
5. The tooth is in pure bending — no axial or shear contribution is considered.

### 3.1.2 Free-Body Diagram (Conceptual)

We model the tooth as a cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section $b \times t$, fixed at the root fillet. The tangential force $F_t$ acts at the tooth tip at a height $h$ above the root.

### 3.1.3 Bending Moment at the Root

The bending moment at the root section (where failure is assumed to initiate) is:

$$
M = F_t \times h
$$

where $h$ is the **whole depth of the tooth** measured from tip to root.

### 3.1.4 Section Modulus of the Tooth (Parabolic Profile)

The tooth has a trapezoidal cross-section. At the root, the thickness is $t$, and the face width is $b$. The section modulus is:

$$
Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{b \, t^{2}}{6}
$$

where $c = t/2$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the outermost fibre.

### 3.1.5 Bending Stress at the Root

Applying the flexure formula $\sigma = M / Z$:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{F_t \cdot h}{\dfrac{b \, t^{2}}{6}} = \frac{6 \, F_t \, h}{b \, t^{2}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Introduction of the Lewis Form Factor

Lewis observed that the geometry of an involute tooth forces a specific relationship between $h$, $t$, and the module $m$. He defined the **Lewis form factor** as:

$$
y = \frac{t^{2}}{6 \, h}
$$

This factor is **dimensionless** and depends only on the **number of teeth** and the **pressure angle (typically 20°)**. Tabulated values:

| Teeth (z) | $y$ (20° PA, full depth) |
|:---------:|:------------------------:|
| 18 | 0.308 |
| 20 | 0.320 |
| 25 | 0.352 |
| 30 | 0.380 |
| 40 | 0.389 |

### 3.1.7 Final Lewis Equation

Substituting $y$ into the bending stress expression:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The module $m$ is used instead of the actual tooth dimensions because for standard full-depth gears, $h \propto m$ and $t \propto m$. The form factor $y$ absorbs the geometric proportionality constant.

### 3.1.8 Design Form (Beam Strength)

For design, the tangential load is rewritten as the **beam strength** of the tooth:

$$
S_b = \sigma_0 \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y
$$

where the factor $\pi$ enters because the full Lewis derivation uses $\pi \, m$ as the effective face length for the parabolic profile (this is a standard convention used in Indian textbooks such as V.B. Bhandari and Shigley).

The **design condition** is:

$$
F_t \leq S_b
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Buckingham Wear Equation

While Lewis addressed bending, **Buckingham (1934)** addressed flank wear / pitting. His derivation is based on Hertzian contact theory.

### 3.2.1 Hertzian Contact Setup

Two meshing involute teeth make line contact along the line of action. At the pitch point, the line contact is equivalent to two cylinders pressed together with a normal force $F_n$.

For two cylinders of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ (radius of curvature of the involute flanks), the **equivalent radius** is:

$$
\frac{1}{r_{eq}} = \frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{r_2}
$$

### 3.2.2 Hertzian Contact Stress

The maximum compressive stress at the contact is:

$$
\sigma_c^{2} = \frac{F_n}{\pi \, b} \cdot \frac{1}{r_{eq}} \cdot \frac{2}{\left(\dfrac{1-\nu_1^{2}}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\nu_2^{2}}{E_2}\right)}
$$

### 3.2.3 Buckingham's Simplification

Buckingham combined all the elastic constants and geometric constants into two empirical factors:
- $Q$ = **Ratio Factor** (depends on the gear ratio and addendum)
- $K$ = **Load-Stress Factor** (depends on the material elastic modulus)

He obtained the **wear strength** of the gear tooth:

$$
S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K
$$

where $d_p$ is the pitch circle diameter of the **pinion** (the smaller gear, which always fails first in wear because it meshes more frequently).

### 3.2.4 Design Equation

The design condition for wear is:

$$
F_w = S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K
$$

The **wear load $F_w$** must be ≥ the actual transmitted tangential force $F_t$ for safe design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip**: For a standard 20° full-depth system with steel gears, $Q = \dfrac{2 \, z_g}{z_g + z_p}$ (approximate), and $K \approx 0.16 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$ for cast iron, $K \approx 0.35 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$ for steel. Always check the table values from the textbook you studied.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix (Bending vs. Wear)

| Parameter | Bending (Lewis) | Wear (Buckingham) |
|:----------|:----------------|:------------------|
| **Failure Mode Addressed** | Tooth fracture | Pitting / scoring |
| **Critical Stress** | Bending stress at root | Hertzian contact stress |
| **Critical Section** | Root fillet | Pitch point flank |
| **Critical Gear** | Pinion (smaller, more cycles) | Pinion |
| **Design Equation** | $F_t = \sigma_0 \, b \, \pi \, m \, y$ | $F_w = d_p \, b \, Q \, K$ |
| **Material Parameter** | $\sigma_0$ (allowable bending stress) | $K$ (load-stress factor) |
| **Geometric Parameter** | $y$ (form factor) | $Q$ (ratio factor) |
| **Failure Cycle Range** | $10^{6}$ cycles | $10^{8}$ cycles |
| **KTU Typical Exam Weight** | 50–60% of gear problems | 40–50% of gear problems |

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (Derivation of Tangential Load from Power)

**Given:** Pinion rotates at $N_p = 1000$ rpm, pitch diameter $d_p = 100$ mm, transmitted power $P = 7.5$ kW.

**Find:** Tangential tooth load $F_t$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert power to consistent units. $P = 7.5 \, \text{kW} = 7500 \, \text{W}$.

Step 2 — Compute the pitch-line velocity.

$$
v = \frac{\pi \, d_p \, N_p}{60 \, 000} = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times 1000}{60 \, 000} = \frac{314\,159.27}{60\,000} = 5.236 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the tangential force.

$$
F_t = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{7500}{5.236} = 1432.4 \, \text{N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $F_t$ is then compared against both the **beam strength** $S_b$ and the **wear strength** $S_w$. The **minimum** of the two governs the design (i.e., the gear is sized to satisfy *both* failure modes).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any six modes of gear tooth failure and state the governing design equation for the most critical one.**

**Model Answer:**

The six principal modes of gear tooth failure are:
1. Bending fatigue (root fracture)
2. Pitting (contact fatigue)
3. Scuffing / scoring
4. Abrasive wear
5. Plastic deformation (cold flow)
6. Overload fracture

The most critical mode is **bending fatigue**, governed by the **Lewis equation**:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y}
$$

The design condition requires this stress to be less than the allowable stress $\sigma_0$ for the chosen material, which gives the **beam strength**:

$$
S_b = \sigma_0 \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y
$$

**(3 marks: 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for identifying critical mode, 1 mark for correct formula)**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between pitting failure and scuffing failure in gears.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pitting | Scuffing |
|:--------|:--------|:---------|
| Mechanism | Subsurface fatigue crack → surface flake | Adhesive welding → tearing |
| Stress Type | Hertzian contact | Local welding-shear |
| Appearance | Small craters on flank | Longitudinal scoring marks |
| Location | Below pitch line (subsurface) | Above and along pitch line |
| Lubrication Dependency | Lower (stress controlled) | High (film collapse is cause) |
| Governing Equation | Buckingham wear equation | Flash temperature / EHL film thickness |

**(3 marks: 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for appearance, 1 mark for governing principle)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) follows an **internal choice** pattern: the student answers **EITHER Question A OR Question B** in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Lewis equation for gear tooth design. State clearly the assumptions involved and the significance of the form factor $y$.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A pair of spur gears with 20° full-depth involute teeth is required to transmit 15 kW at 1440 rpm of the pinion. The pinion has 20 teeth and the gear has 60 teeth. The allowable static stress for the material is 100 MPa and the face width is 10 times the module. Determine the module based on the Lewis equation. Use $y = 0.320$ for 20 teeth.**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

#### Part (a) Model Answer

**Assumptions of the Lewis Equation:**
1. The tooth is a cantilever beam fixed at the root.
2. Load $F_t$ acts at the tip as a concentrated tangential force.
3. Stress distribution at the root is parabolic (full section participation).
4. Only one tooth carries the entire load.
5. Effects of stress concentration at the fillet, dynamic load, and lubrication are neglected.

**Derivation Outline (as in §3.1):**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{F_t}{b \cdot m \cdot y}
$$

**Significance of Form Factor $y$:**
- $y$ is a **dimensionless geometric factor** that captures the ratio of tooth thickness at the root to the tooth height.
- It **depends only on the number of teeth and pressure angle**, not on module size.
- It allows the Lewis equation to be **scaled for any module** — a critical convenience for designers.
- For a 20-tooth pinion at 20° PA, $y = 0.320$; for fewer teeth $y$ is smaller (weaker tooth), for more teeth $y$ is larger (stronger tooth). This explains the **minimum teeth rule** (typically 17–18 teeth) in gear design.

**(7 marks: 2 marks for assumptions, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for $y$ significance)**

---

#### Part (b) Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Extract the given data.**

$P = 15 \, \text{kW}$, $N_p = 1440 \, \text{rpm}$, $z_p = 20$, $z_g = 60$, $\sigma_0 = 100 \, \text{MPa}$, $b = 10m$, $y = 0.320$.

**Step 2 — Select the Lewis design equation.**

For design, we equate the transmitted tangential load $F_t$ to the **beam strength**:

$$
F_t = S_b = \sigma_0 \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y
$$

**Step 3 — Express $F_t$ in terms of $m$.**

The pitch diameter of the pinion: $d_p = m \cdot z_p = 20m$.

The pitch-line velocity:

$$
v = \frac{\pi \, d_p \, N_p}{60 \, 000} = \frac{\pi \times 20m \times 1440}{60\,000} = \frac{28\,800 \pi \, m}{60\,000} = 1.5080 \, m \, \text{m/s}
$$

The transmitted tangential force:

$$
F_t = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{15\,000}{1.5080 \, m} = \frac{9947.18}{m} \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the beam strength equation.**

$$
\frac{9947.18}{m} = 100 \times (10m) \times \pi \times 0.320
$$

$$
\frac{9947.18}{m} = 1005.31 \, m
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $m$.**

$$
9947.18 = 1005.31 \, m^{2}
$$

$$
m^{2} = \frac{9947.18}{1005.31} = 9.8945 \, \text{mm}^{2}
$$

$$
m = \sqrt{9.8945} = 3.146 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Standardize the module.**

Round up to the nearest **standard module** (IS 2535): $m = 4 \, \text{mm}$.

**Final Answer: Module $m = 4 \, \text{mm}$** (use standard).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Forgetting to convert **kW to W** ($P = 15\,000 \, \text{W}$) → lose 1 mark
> - Using $b = 10m$ but forgetting to substitute this back into the equation → lose 1 mark
> - Forgetting to round up to a standard module → lose 0.5 mark
> - Using $y = 0.320$ for the gear (60 teeth) instead of the **pinion (20 teeth)** → **2 marks lost** (this is the most common error)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain Buckingham's wear equation for gear tooth design. State clearly the significance of the ratio factor $Q$ and the load-stress factor $K$.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A pinion with 18 teeth meshes with a gear of 72 teeth. Both gears are made of steel with $K = 0.35 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$. The face width is 50 mm and the module is 6 mm. The pinion speed is 720 rpm. Compute the wear strength of the gear tooth pair.**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

#### Part (a) Model Answer

**Buckingham's Wear Equation (Concept):**

Buckingham recognised that **pitting failure** is governed by **Hertzian contact stress** at the pitch point. He derived the **wear strength** as:

$$
S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K
$$

**Significance of Ratio Factor $Q$:**
- $Q$ is dimensionless and **depends on the gear ratio** $i = z_g / z_p$.
- For standard 20° full-depth teeth:

$$
Q = \frac{2 i}{i + 1}
$$

- As the ratio $i$ increases (large gear reduction), $Q \to 2$. As $i \to 1$ (equal gears), $Q \to 1$.
- $Q$ represents the **combined geometry effect** of pinion curvature and gear curvature on the contact ellipse size.

**Significance of Load-Stress Factor $K$:**
- $K$ has units of N/mm² and depends on the **elastic modulus of the material pair**.
- For common materials:
  - Cast iron: $K \approx 0.16 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$
  - Steel: $K \approx 0.35 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$
  - Bronze: $K \approx 0.12 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$
- A **higher $K$** means the material can resist more load without pitting.

**(7 marks: 2 marks for equation, 2 marks for $Q$, 2 marks for $K$, 1 mark for design implication)**

---

#### Part (b) Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the pitch diameter of the pinion.**

$$
d_p = m \cdot z_p = 6 \times 18 = 108 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gear ratio and ratio factor.**

$$
i = \frac{z_g}{z_p} = \frac{72}{18} = 4
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2 i}{i + 1} = \frac{2 \times 4}{4 + 1} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6
$$

**Step 3 — List all input values for the wear equation.**

$d_p = 108 \, \text{mm}$, $b = 50 \, \text{mm}$, $Q = 1.6$, $K = 0.35 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the wear strength.**

$$
S_w = d_p \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K
$$

$$
S_w = 108 \times 50 \times 1.6 \times 0.35
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the multiplication step-by-step.**

$$
108 \times 50 = 5400
$$

$$
5400 \times 1.6 = 8640
$$

$$
8640 \times 0.35 = 3024
$$

**Final Answer:** $S_w = 3024 \, \text{N}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):**
> - Using **$d_g$ (gear pitch diameter)** instead of **$d_p$ (pinion)** in the equation → **lose 2 marks** (the pinion always fails first because it has more load cycles)
> - Forgetting that $i = z_g / z_p$ (gear over pinion) → lose 1 mark
> - Mixing up the $K$ value (using cast iron's $K$ instead of steel's) → lose 1 mark
> - Forgetting to mention the **unit (N)** in the final answer → lose 0.5 mark

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PCMUT601 Module 3 — Gear Tooth Failures**

- ✅ Gear tooth failure has **6 main modes**: bending fatigue, pitting, scuffing, abrasive wear, plastic deformation, overload fracture.
- ✅ The **Lewis equation** is the design check for **bending failure**; it uses the **form factor $y$** (depends only on tooth count and pressure angle).
- ✅ The **Buckingham wear equation** is the design check for **pitting failure**; it uses the **ratio factor $Q$** and the **load-stress factor $K$**.
- ✅ For design: **$F_t \leq S_b$** (bending) AND **$F_t \leq S_w$** (wear). The **smaller** of $S_b$ and $S_w$ governs.
- ✅ The **pinion** is always the critical gear because:
  - It has **fewer teeth** → lower $y$ → lower beam strength.
  - It rotates **more times** → more load cycles → fails first.
- ✅ Standard $\sigma_0$ values for KTU: **Cast Iron = 56 MPa**, **Steel (plain) = 100 MPa**, **Alloy Steel (case-hardened) = 200 MPa** (always check the table provided in the question paper).
- ✅ Always convert **kW → W** and ensure units of $b$, $d$, and $m$ are all in **mm**.
- ✅ The **pitch-line velocity** $v$ must be computed **before** $F_t = P/v$.
- ✅ **Helical gears** use the **virtual number of teeth** $z_v = z / \cos^{3}\beta$ for form-factor lookup.
- ✅ **Standard modules (IS 2535)** to remember: 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm.
- ✅ **Minimum teeth** for pinion to avoid undercutting at 20° PA: **17 teeth (full depth)**, **21 teeth (with profile shift)**.
- ✅ The **AGMA equations** are a refined version of Lewis + Buckingham — they include dynamic, overload, size, load-distribution, and reliability factors.
- ✅ **Scuffing** is mostly a **lubrication problem**, not a stress problem — addressed by **EHL film thickness** and **flash temperature** calculations.
- ✅ KTU exam tip: Draw a **free-body diagram of the tooth as a cantilever beam** at the start of every bending-stress problem to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beam Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Beam Strength ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Beam Strength (Lewis Beam Strength):** In the context of automotive gear design, *Beam Strength* refers to the maximum tangential load that a gear tooth can transmit at the pitch circle without causing **bending (flexural) failure** at the tooth root. It is computed using the classical **Lewis Equation (1892)**, which idealizes the gear tooth as a **cantilever beam of varying cross-section** fixed at the root circle and loaded tangentially at the tip. Mathematically, the Lewis Beam Strength is expressed as: $$ F_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y $$ where each term carries a precise engineering meaning defined in Section 2. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Diving Board:** > Imagine a person standing at the very end of a diving board. The board is **rigidly clamped** at one end (the pool deck) and **loaded** at the free end (the diver's feet). The board bends, and the maximum bending stress develops at the **fixed root** — exactly where the wood meets concrete. > > A **gear tooth behaves identically**: > - The **tooth root** is analogous to the **fixed clamp** (where fracture usually initiates). > - The **tooth tip** is analogous to the **diver's feet** (where the tangential force is applied). > - The **Lewis Form Factor (Y)** captures the **tapering shape** of the tooth (narrow at root, broad at tip), which reduces stress concentration. > > Hence, "beam strength" = the load-carrying capacity of this natural cantilever before the tooth snaps off at its weakest cross-section — the root fillet. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Lewis Form Factor (Y):** A dimensionless geometric factor, typically in the range **0.28 to 0.485** for standard 20° full-depth teeth. > - **Module (m):** Expressed in **millimeters (mm)** — the fundamental size parameter of a gear. > - **Face Width (b):** Expressed in **mm**. > - **Allowable Bending Stress (σ_b):** Typically **100 MPa to 200 MPa** for plain carbon steel gears, per **AGMA / IS 4460** standards. > - **Pressure Angle (φ):** Standard values are **14.5°** (old), **20°** (current standard), and **25°** (high-load). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Lewis Cantilever Assumption — Why and How The Lewis equation rests on **three core idealizations**: 1. **Cantilever Model:** The tooth is treated as a **rectangular cantilever beam** projecting from the rim. The load acts **tangentially at the tip**. 2. **Worst-Cross-Section:** Bending moment is maximum, and the section modulus is minimum, at the **root of the tooth**. Failure initiates here. 3. **Static Load Assumption:** Dynamic effects and stress concentration are lumped into a single empirical factor — the **velocity factor (Cv)** or **service factor (Cs)**. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation Flow - **Step 1 — Resolve the Tangential Load:** The transmitted tangential force at the pitch circle is decomposed into components along and perpendicular to the tooth centerline. - **Step 2 — Bending Moment at Root:** The component perpendicular to the tooth axis creates a bending moment at the root equal to **Force × Length of tooth as cantilever**. - **Step 3 — Bending Stress:** Using the flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$, where **Z** is the section modulus of the weakest tooth cross-section. - **Step 4 — Introduce Lewis Form Factor:** The complex parabolic tooth profile is replaced by an equivalent rectangular beam, and a geometric **form factor (Y)** is empirically introduced to absorb profile effects. - **Step 5 — Multiply by Face Width:** The 2D analysis is extended to the 3D tooth by multiplying by face width **b**. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Value** | **Units** | |------------|--------------|---------------------|-----------| | $F_b$ | Beam Strength (Tangential Load) | $F_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y$ | **N** | | $m$ | Module of gear | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ (Pitch Dia / Teeth) | **mm** | | $b$ | Face width | Typically $b = 6m$ to $10m$ | **mm** | | $\sigma_b$ | Allowable bending stress | 100 – 200 (steel), per IS 4460 | **N/mm² (MPa)** | | $Y$ | Lewis Form Factor | Read from standard tables for given $\phi$ and teeth | **Dimensionless** | | $P_{design}$ | Design tangential load | $\dfrac{P_{transmitted}}{C_v}$ or $P \cdot C_s$ | **N** | | $F_s$ | Factor of Safety | $\dfrac{F_b}{P_{design}}$ | **Dimensionless** | | $t$ | Tooth thickness at pitch circle | $t = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m}{2}$ | **mm** | | $Y$ empirical | For 20° full-depth stub | $Y = 0.485 - \dfrac{2.87}{Z}$ (approx.) | — | | $\phi$ | Pressure angle | **20°** (current KTU standard) | **Degrees** | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Notation Warning for KTU Valuation:** > In KTU answer scripts, examiners **deduct marks** if students confuse **module (m)** with **mass (m)** or **face width (b)** with **Lewis Form Factor (Y)**. Always underline symbols and define them before use. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Gearboxes:** Beam strength governs the design of spur gears in **transmission systems** (e.g., constant-mesh gears, synchromesh hubs). - **Differential Gears:** In rear-axle differentials, beam strength ensures **ring and pinion gears** survive shock loading during torque transitions. - **Electric Vehicle (EV) Reducers:** Even in EVs, reduction gears (single or multi-stage) must be checked for beam strength against transient torque spikes. - **Failure Diagnosis:** "**Tooth breakage at root fillet**" is the **classic beam-strength failure signature** in forensic gear analysis. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Lewis Beam Strength Equation #### Step 1 — Tangential Force at Pitch Circle Let the tangential load transmitted by the gear be **$P_t$** (in Newtons). This is the force we wish to compare against the beam strength. #### Step 2 — Resolve Forces on the Tooth When force $P_t$ acts tangentially at the pitch circle, it is resolved into: - A **radial component** $P_r = P_t \tan\phi$ - A **tangential component along tooth centerline** $P_t$ The **bending-causing component** is the tangential force $P_t$ applied at a distance equal to the length of the tooth acting as a cantilever. #### Step 3 — Bending Moment at Tooth Root The bending moment at the tooth root (taken as the weakest section) is: $$ M = P_t \cdot h $$ where **$h$** is the height of the tooth from the root circle to the point of load application. #### Step 4 — Bending Stress Using Flexure Formula By Euler-Bernoulli beam theory: $$ \sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} $$ Substituting $M = P_t \cdot h$: $$ \sigma_b = \dfrac{P_t \cdot h}{Z} $$ #### Step 5 — Express Section Modulus (Z) Geometrically At the root, the tooth has an approximately **parabolic (narrowing toward tip)** profile. The section modulus is empirically written as: $$ Z = b \cdot t^2 / 6 \cdot Y $$ where: - $b$ = face width - $t$ = tooth thickness at the **root** (where bending is critical) - $Y$ = **Lewis Form Factor** (a dimensionless ratio that corrects the rectangular-beam idealization for the actual tooth shape) #### Step 6 — Solve for Beam Strength Rearranging $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P_t \cdot h}{Z}$: $$ P_t = \dfrac{\sigma_b \cdot Z}{h} $$ Substituting Z and using $t \propto m$ (since $t = \pi m / 2$ at the pitch circle), and recognizing that the ratio $t^2/(6h)$ collapses to the product $m \cdot Y$: $$ \begin{aligned} F_b &= m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y \end{aligned} $$ This is the celebrated **Lewis Beam Strength Equation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Standard Form (must memorize for KTU):** > $$\boxed{F_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y}$$ > where $F_b$ is in **Newtons**, $m$ and $b$ in **mm**, $\sigma_b$ in **N/mm² (MPa)**, and $Y$ is dimensionless. ### 3.2 The Lewis Form Factor — Empirical Geometry The form factor **Y** depends on: - **Number of teeth (Z)** on the pinion (and the gear pair) - **Pressure angle ($\phi$)** - **Whether the system is stub or full-depth** For **20° full-depth teeth**, the AGMA-recommended empirical expression is: $$ Y = 0.30 - \dfrac{1.77}{Z} \quad \text{(approx., for Z = 20 to 100)} $$ Or, more conservatively used in KTU textbooks (Khurmi/Gupta): $$ Y = 0.485 - \dfrac{2.87}{Z} $$ ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format) **Problem:** A spur pinion with **18 teeth** transmits **5 kW** at **900 rpm**. The teeth are **20° full-depth**, module = **4 mm**, and face width = **40 mm**. The allowable bending stress is **100 MPa**. The Lewis form factor for 18 teeth is given as **0.323**. Determine the **factor of safety** against beam strength failure. Take velocity factor $C_v = \dfrac{3}{3 + v}$ where $v$ is in m/s. #### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Pitch Circle Diameter** $$ D = m \cdot Z = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm} = 0.072 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Pitch Line Velocity** $$ v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.072 \times 900}{60} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} v &= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 64.8}{60} \\ v &= \dfrac{203.58}{60} \\ v &\approx 3.393 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Velocity Factor** $$ C_v = \dfrac{3}{3 + v} = \dfrac{3}{3 + 3.393} = \dfrac{3}{6.393} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_v &= 0.4693 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Tangential Load Transmitted** $$ P_t = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6 \cdot \text{Power}}{\pi \cdot D \cdot N} $$ $$ P_t = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6 \times 5}{\pi \times 72 \times 900} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_t &= \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{203{,}575.2} \\ P_t &\approx 1473.5 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Design (Effective) Load** $$ P_d = \dfrac{P_t}{C_v} = \dfrac{1473.5}{0.4693} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_d &\approx 3139.6 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Beam Strength** $$ F_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y $$ $$ F_b = 4 \times 40 \times 100 \times 0.323 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} F_b &= 16000 \times 0.323 \\ F_b &= 5168 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Factor of Safety** $$ F_s = \dfrac{F_b}{P_d} = \dfrac{5168}{3139.6} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} F_s &\approx 1.646 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** Since $F_s \approx 1.65$, which is **less than the KTU-recommended minimum of 1.5–2.0** for moderate shock loads, the gear design is **borderline acceptable**. For high-shock automotive applications, a **larger module** or **case-hardened material** (with higher $\sigma_b$) is recommended. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Beam Strength Computational Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Parameters:
Power P, Speed N, Module m] --> B[Compute Pitch Diameter:
D = m x Z] B --> C[Compute Pitch Line Velocity:
v = pi x D x N / 60] C --> D[Compute Velocity Factor:
Cv = 3 / 3 + v] D --> E[Compute Tangential Load:
Pt = Power / v] E --> F[Compute Design Load:
Pd = Pt / Cv] F --> G[Compute Beam Strength:
Fb = m x b x sigma_b x Y] G --> H{Compare} H -->|Fb greater than Pd| I[Safe Design
Factor of Safety greater than 1] H -->|Fb less than Pd| J[Failure Risk
Increase module or face width] G --> K[Read Lewis Form Factor Y
from Tables for given Z and phi] K --> G ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tooth-as-Cantilever Physical Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Tooth Tip
Free End] -->|Tangential Force Pt| B[Tooth Profile
Cantilever Beam] B --> C[Tooth Root
Fixed Clamp] C --> D[Bending Stress
Maximum at Root Fillet] D --> E[Lewis Form Factor Y
Shape Correction] E --> F[Beam Strength Fb] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Description — Stress Distribution at Tooth Root > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stress Profile Visualization (textual schematic):** > ``` > ↑ Tangential Load (Pt) > | > ____|____ > / \ ← TOOTH TIP (low stress) > / \ > / \ > | | ← TOOTH MID-FLANK (moderate stress) > | | > \ / ← STRESS RISES toward root > \ / > \_________/ > | > ▔▔▔▔▔▔ ← TOOTH ROOT (MAX bending stress) > ════════════════ ← Gear Rim > ``` > The stress concentration **always peaks at the root fillet** — this is why Lewis evaluated bending **exactly at the root circle**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Define *Beam Strength* of a gear tooth and state the Lewis equation. **Model Answer:** > Beam strength is the **maximum tangential load** a gear tooth can transmit at the pitch circle **without causing bending failure at the tooth root**. It is given by the **Lewis equation**: > $$F_b = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y$$ > where $m$ = module, $b$ = face width, $\sigma_b$ = allowable bending stress, $Y$ = Lewis form factor (a dimensionless geometric correction factor that accounts for the actual parabolic tooth profile). --- #### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** What is the *Lewis Form Factor* and what factors does it depend on? **Model Answer:** > The Lewis Form Factor **Y** is a dimensionless empirical factor that corrects the assumption of a rectangular cantilever beam to account for the **actual parabolic (tapered) tooth profile**. It depends on: > 1. **Number of teeth (Z)** on the pinion, > 2. **Pressure angle ($\phi$)**, and > 3. **System of teeth** (full-depth or stub). > > For 20° full-depth teeth, $Y$ decreases as Z increases, asymptotically approaching **0.30**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Lewis equation for beam strength of a spur gear tooth, clearly stating all assumptions. **Model Solution:** > **Assumptions of Lewis Equation:** > 1. The tooth acts as a **cantilever beam** fixed at the root. > 2. The load acts **tangentially at the tip** of the tooth. > 3. The tangential load is the **only component causing bending** (radial component produces compressive stress, neglected). > 4. The bending stress is **maximum at the root** (smallest section modulus). > 5. Stress concentration and dynamic effects are absorbed by a **velocity factor** $C_v$. > > **Derivation:** > Consider the tooth as a cantilever of length **$h$** with face width **$b$**, loaded tangentially by force **$P_t$** at the tip. > > Bending moment at the root: > $$M = P_t \cdot h$$ > > Bending stress using flexure formula: > $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{P_t \cdot h}{Z}$$ > > Section modulus at the parabolic root (Lewis correction): > $$Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^2}{6Y}$$ > > where $t$ is the tooth thickness at the root and $Y$ is the form factor. > > Substituting: > $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \cdot P_t \cdot h \cdot Y}{b \cdot t^2}$$ > > Solving for $P_t$ and noting that for standard 20° teeth, $\dfrac{t^2}{6h} = m \cdot Y$ (Lewis empirical collapse): > > $$F_b = P_t = m \cdot b \cdot \sigma_b \cdot Y$$ --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A spur pinion has **20 teeth**, module = **5 mm**, face width = **50 mm**, and rotates at **1440 rpm**. It meshes with a gear of **60 teeth**. The material has an allowable bending stress of **120 MPa**, and the Lewis form factor for 20 teeth is **0.320**. The pinion transmits **7.5 kW**.
