> **Module (m)** = Pitch circle diameter (d) / Number of teeth (z), measured in **mm**. The constant **m = d/z = p/π** is the standard metric system used in automotive gear design (especially European and Indian specifications).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Tooth Terminology

The geometry of a gear tooth is defined by several key linear measurements. Let us break these down logically:

### 2.1.1 Reference (Pitch) Circle
- The reference circle is an **imaginary circle** on which the tooth thickness and space width are theoretically equal.
- **Diameter:** $d = m \cdot z$
- **Radius:** $r = \dfrac{m \cdot z}{2}$

### 2.1.2 Addendum (a)
- **Definition:** The radial distance from the **pitch circle** to the **tip (outside) of the tooth**.
- **Standard value:** $a = 1.0 \, m$ (for 20° full depth)
- **Physical meaning:** This is the "working height" that engages with the meshing gear.

### 2.1.3 Dedendum (b)
- **Definition:** The radial distance from the **pitch circle** to the **root (bottom) of the tooth**.
- **Standard value:** $b = 1.25 \, m$ (for 20° full depth)
- **Physical meaning:** Provides space for the addendum of the meshing gear.

### 2.1.4 Clearance (c)
- **Definition:** The radial distance from the **tip of one tooth** to the **bottom of the mating tooth space**, when the teeth are engaged at the pitch point.
- **Standard value:** $c = 0.25 \, m$ (for 20° full depth)
- **Relationship:** $c = b - a = 1.25m - 1.0m = 0.25m$

### 2.1.5 Working Depth (hw)
- **Definition:** The radial distance from the **addendum circle** to the **dedendum circle** of the *meshing gear* (i.e., engagement depth).
- **Value:** $h_w = 2a = 2.0 \, m$

### 2.1.6 Whole Depth (ht)
- **Definition:** The total radial distance from the **tip of the tooth** to the **root of the tooth** of the *same gear*.
- **Value:** $h_t = a + b = 2.25 \, m$

### 2.1.7 Tooth Thickness (t) and Space Width (w)
- At the pitch circle, by definition: $t = w = \dfrac{p}{2} = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$
- The actual measured tooth thickness is on the **circular arc** at the pitch circle.

### 2.1.8 Circular Pitch (p)
- **Definition:** The arc length measured on the pitch circle between corresponding points on adjacent teeth.
- **Value:** $p = \pi m$

### 2.1.9 Fillet Radius (rf)
- A small radius at the tooth root to **reduce stress concentration**.
- **AGMA Standard:** $r_f = 0.30 \, m$ (maximum) for 20° full-depth gears.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula (20° Full Depth) | Formula (20° Stub Tooth) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Module | $m$ | $m = d / z$ | $m = d / z$ | mm |
| Pitch Circle Diameter | $d$ | $d = m \cdot z$ | $d = m \cdot z$ | mm |
| Circular Pitch | $p$ | $p = \pi m$ | $p = \pi m$ | mm |
| Addendum | $a$ | $a = 1.0 \, m$ | $a = 0.8 \, m$ | mm |
| Dedendum | $b$ | $b = 1.25 \, m$ | $b = 1.0 \, m$ | mm |
| Clearance | $c$ | $c = 0.25 \, m$ | $c = 0.2 \, m$ | mm |
| Whole Depth | $h_t$ | $h_t = 2.25 \, m$ | $h_t = 1.8 \, m$ | mm |
| Working Depth | $h_w$ | $h_w = 2.0 \, m$ | $h_w = 1.6 \, m$ | mm |
| Outside Diameter | $D_o$ | $D_o = m(z + 2)$ | $D_o = m(z + 1.6)$ | mm |
| Root Diameter | $D_r$ | $D_r = m(z - 2.5)$ | $D_r = m(z - 2.0)$ | mm |
| Tooth Thickness (at P.C.) | $t$ | $t = \pi m / 2$ | $t = \pi m / 2$ | mm |
| Tooth Space Width | $w$ | $w = \pi m / 2$ | $w = \pi m / 2$ | mm |
| Base Circle Diameter | $D_b$ | $D_b = d \cos \phi$ | $D_b = d \cos \phi$ | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU:** The module **m** must always be selected from **standard values**: 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10... mm (AGMA/ISO preferred numbers). Non-standard modules are discouraged in automotive practice.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Standardized Tooth Proportions

In automotive contexts, standard tooth proportions serve critical engineering purposes:

1. **Interchangeability** — Standardized gears from any manufacturer worldwide can mesh properly (e.g., aftermarket replacement gears in a Maruti or Tata transmission).
2. **Predictable Strength** — Engineers can use standardized Lewis, Buckingham, or AGMA bending stress equations with confidence.
3. **Manufacturing Economy** — Standard cutter modules (hobs, rack cutters) are available only for standard modules and pressure angles.
4. **Inspection Simplification** — Chordal tooth thickness measurements (a key inspection method) rely on standard proportions.

> [!NOTE]
> The **20° full-depth system** dominates modern automotive gear design due to its balance of strength, smooth action, and reduced interference. The **14.5° system** is mostly obsolete and is restricted to legacy machinery and some older automotive applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Outside and Root Diameters

### 3.1.1 Outside Diameter (Dₒ) — 20° Full Depth

The outside diameter is the pitch circle diameter plus **two addenda** (one on each side of the pitch circle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= d + 2a \\
D_o &= m \cdot z + 2(1.0 \, m) \\
D_o &= m \cdot z + 2m \\
D_o &= m(z + 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Steps:**
1. The pitch diameter is $d = m z$.
2. The addendum extends outward by $a = 1.0 \, m$ from the pitch circle.
3. Adding two addenda (top + bottom symmetry) yields $d + 2a$.

### 3.1.2 Root Diameter (Dr) — 20° Full Depth

The root diameter is the pitch circle diameter minus **two dedenda**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= d - 2b \\
D_r &= m \cdot z - 2(1.25 \, m) \\
D_r &= m \cdot z - 2.5m \\
D_r &= m(z - 2.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Steps:**
1. The dedendum extends inward by $b = 1.25 \, m$ from the pitch circle.
2. Two dedenda must be subtracted to reach the root circle.
3. Substituting $b = 1.25m$ gives the final form.

## 3.2 Derivation of Tooth Thickness at Pitch Circle

By definition, the **tooth thickness equals the space width** at the pitch circle, and together they constitute the **circular pitch**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t + w &= p \\
\text{But} \quad t &= w \quad \text{(by definition of pitch circle)} \\
\therefore \quad 2t &= p \\
t &= \frac{p}{2} = \frac{\pi m}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Steps:**
1. The circular pitch $p$ is the arc distance between corresponding points on adjacent teeth.
2. This arc must be shared equally between one tooth and one space.
3. Therefore, each occupies half the circular pitch.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 20° Full Depth vs. 20° Stub Tooth

Let us perform a **direct numerical comparison** for a gear with **z = 20 teeth** and **m = 5 mm**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Given:} \quad z = 20, \quad m = 5 \text{ mm} \\
&\textbf{Step 1: Pitch Diameter} \\
&d = m \cdot z = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ mm} \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Circular Pitch} \\
&p = \pi m = \pi \times 5 = 15.708 \text{ mm} \\
&\textbf{Step 3: Tooth Thickness at P.C.} \\
&t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = 7.854 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compare proportions for both systems:

| Parameter | 20° Full Depth | 20° Stub Tooth | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addendum | $1.0 \times 5 = 5.0$ mm | $0.8 \times 5 = 4.0$ mm | −1.0 mm |
| Dedendum | $1.25 \times 5 = 6.25$ mm | $1.0 \times 5 = 5.0$ mm | −1.25 mm |
| Clearance | $0.25 \times 5 = 1.25$ mm | $0.2 \times 5 = 1.0$ mm | −0.25 mm |
| Whole Depth | $2.25 \times 5 = 11.25$ mm | $1.8 \times 5 = 9.0$ mm | −2.25 mm |
| Working Depth | $2.0 \times 5 = 10.0$ mm | $1.6 \times 5 = 8.0$ mm | −2.0 mm |
| Outside Dia. | $5(20+2) = 110$ mm | $5(20+1.6) = 108$ mm | −2.0 mm |
| Root Dia. | $5(20−2.5) = 87.5$ mm | $5(20−2.0) = 90$ mm | +2.5 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Stub-tooth gears have a **shorter, stronger tooth** (less bending stress) but **lower contact ratio** (fewer teeth in contact). They are used in **aerospace and high-speed automotive applications** where space and weight are critical.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Base Circle Diameter

The base circle is the circle from which the involute tooth profile is mathematically generated. Its diameter is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_b &= d \cos \phi \\
\text{where} \quad \phi &= \text{pressure angle} = 20° \\
\therefore \quad D_b &= m \cdot z \cdot \cos 20° \\
D_b &= 0.9397 \, m \cdot z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Steps:**
1. The base circle is where the involute curve begins.
2. The involute is generated by unwinding a string from the base circle.
3. The base circle is always smaller than the pitch circle, related by the cosine of the pressure angle.

## 3.5 Chordal Tooth Thickness and Chordal Addendum (For Inspection)

For gear inspection, the **chordal tooth thickness** is measured using a gear-tooth vernier caliper. The chordal measurements ensure the correct tooth shape, independent of any tooth-to-tooth spacing errors.

### 3.5.1 Chordal Tooth Thickness (tc)

The tooth thickness measured as a **straight-line chord** at the **chordal position** (where the tooth has a definite reference point):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_c &= r \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2z}\right) \cdot 2 \quad \text{(approximation for small angles)} \\
t_c &\approx \dfrac{\pi m}{2} - \dfrac{t^3}{6 d^2} \\
\text{Simplified:} \quad t_c &\approx p \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{6}\left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2\right] \\
t_c &\approx p \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{\pi^2}{24 z^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Steps:**
1. The arc tooth thickness at the pitch circle is $p/2 = \pi m / 2$.
2. The chord is slightly shorter than the arc, with a small-angle correction.
3. The correction term depends on the ratio $p/d$ or $1/z$.

### 3.5.2 Chordal Addendum (ac)

The chordal addendum is the height from the **chord (top of the tooth)** to the **tip circle**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_c &= a + \dfrac{t^2}{4d} \\
a_c &= m + \dfrac{(\pi m / 2)^2}{4 m z} \\
a_c &= m + \dfrac{\pi^2 m}{16 z} \\
a_c &= m \left[1 + \dfrac{\pi^2}{16 z}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Steps:**
1. The chordal addendum is slightly more than the standard addendum.
2. The extra amount accounts for the curvature of the tooth top.
3. The correction depends inversely on the number of teeth $z$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standard Gear Tooth Geometry — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Since gear tooth profiles involve complex involute curves that cannot be directly drawn in Mermaid, we map the **functional architecture** showing the relationships between the proportional elements:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gear Tooth Geometry] --> B[Pitch Circle Diameter d]
    A --> C[Tooth Tip Region]
    A --> D[Tooth Root Region]
    A --> E[Pressure Angle phi]

    B --> B1[Module m = d slash z]
    B --> B2[Circular Pitch p = pi m]

    C --> C1[Addendum a = 1.0 m]
    C --> C2[Outside Diameter Do = m z plus 2m]
    C --> C3[Chordal Addendum ac]

    D --> D1[Dedendum b = 1.25 m]
    D --> D2[Root Diameter Dr = m z minus 2.5m]
    D --> D3[Clearance c = 0.25 m]
    D --> D4[Fillet Radius rf = 0.30 m]

    E --> E1[Base Circle Db = d cos phi]
    E --> E2[Standard 20 deg]
    E --> E3[Force Transmission Direction]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Flow: 20° Full Depth vs. 20° Stub Tooth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Select Gear System] --> B{Pressure Angle Decision}

    B -->|20 deg Full Depth| C[Addendum equals 1.0 m]
    B -->|20 deg Stub Tooth| D[Addendum equals 0.8 m]

    C --> E[Dedendum equals 1.25 m]
    C --> F[Clearance equals 0.25 m]
    C --> G[Whole Depth equals 2.25 m]

    D --> H[Dedendum equals 1.0 m]
    D --> I[Clearance equals 0.2 m]
    D --> J[Whole Depth equals 1.8 m]

    G --> K[Stronger Tooth - Higher Contact Ratio]
    J --> L[Shorter Tooth - Lower Bending Stress]

    K --> M[Use in Standard Automotive Transmissions]
    L --> N[Use in High Speed and Aerospace Gears]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Visualization of Tooth Proportions (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG[Gear Tooth Cross Section Profile]
        S1[Addendum Circle Do] 
        S2[Pitch Circle d]
        S3[Root Circle Dr]
        S4[Base Circle Db]
        S5[Tooth Thickness at PC equals p slash 2]
        S6[Space Width at PC equals p slash 2]
    end

    S1 -->|Addendum a = 1.0 m| S2
    S2 -->|Dedendum b = 1.25 m| S3
    S4 -->|Cos phi = 0.9397| S2
    S5 --- S6
```

## 4.4 Mating Gear Pair — Functional Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Driving Gear Pinion] -->|Teeth engage on P.C.| P2[Driven Gear]
    P1 --> P3[Pinion Outside Diameter Do1]
    P1 --> P4[Pinion Root Diameter Dr1]
    P2 --> P5[Gear Outside Diameter Do2]
    P2 --> P6[Gear Root Diameter Dr2]

    P3 --> Q1[Center Distance C equals m times z1 plus z2 over 2]
    P5 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q2[Working Depth hw equals 2a]
    P6 --> Q2

    Q1 --> R[Meshing Engagement]
    Q2 --> R
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following terms related to gear tooth proportions:**
**(a)** Addendum, **(b)** Dedendum, **(c)** Clearance.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Addendum (a):** It is the radial distance of a tooth from the **pitch circle** to the **tip (outside) of the tooth**. For 20° full-depth gears, $a = 1.0 \, m$, where $m$ is the module.

**(b) Dedendum (b):** It is the radial distance from the **pitch circle** to the **root (bottom) of the tooth**. For 20° full-depth gears, $b = 1.25 \, m$.

**(c) Clearance (c):** It is the difference between the dedendum of one gear and the addendum of the mating gear. It allows for lubricant film, thermal expansion, and manufacturing tolerances. $c = b - a = 0.25 \, m$ for 20° full-depth gears.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between 20° full-depth and 20° stub-tooth gear systems? Mention any two key proportional differences.**

**Model Answer:**

The 20° full-depth and 20° stub-tooth systems differ in the **length of the tooth** (whole depth) for the same pressure angle (20°) and module:

| Parameter | 20° Full Depth | 20° Stub Tooth |
|---|---|---|
| Addendum | $1.0 \, m$ | $0.8 \, m$ |
| Whole Depth | $2.25 \, m$ | $1.8 \, m$ |

The stub-tooth gear has a **shorter and stronger tooth**, leading to lower bending stress but a reduced contact ratio. It is used in high-speed applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length Design Problem**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3 Choice]**

**(a)** A pair of mating spur gears has a module of **4 mm** and a pressure angle of **20°**. The pinion has **18 teeth** and the gear has **72 teeth**. Calculate:
   **(i)** Pitch circle diameters of pinion and gear
   **(ii)** Addendum, dedendum, and clearance
   **(iii)** Outside and root diameters
   **(iv)** Center distance

**(b)** If the same pair is to be redesigned using the **20° stub-tooth system** (keeping the same module and tooth count), determine the new values of addendum, dedendum, clearance, and whole depth. Comment on the engineering significance.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — 20° Full-Depth System

**Given:** $m = 4$ mm, $\phi = 20°$, $z_1 = 18$ (pinion), $z_2 = 72$ (gear).

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= m \cdot z_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm} \\
d_2 &= m \cdot z_2 = 4 \times 72 = 288 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Addendum, Dedendum, and Clearance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= 1.0 \, m = 1.0 \times 4 = 4.0 \text{ mm} \\
b &= 1.25 \, m = 1.25 \times 4 = 5.0 \text{ mm} \\
c &= b - a = 5.0 - 4.0 = 1.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Outside and Root Diameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{o1} &= m(z_1 + 2) = 4(18 + 2) = 80 \text{ mm} \\
D_{o2} &= m(z_2 + 2) = 4(72 + 2) = 296 \text{ mm} \\
D_{r1} &= m(z_1 - 2.5) = 4(18 - 2.5) = 62 \text{ mm} \\
D_{r2} &= m(z_2 - 2.5) = 4(72 - 2.5) = 278 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Center Distance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{72 + 288}{2} = \frac{360}{2} = 180 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — 20° Stub-Tooth System

For the same module $m = 4$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a' &= 0.8 \, m = 0.8 \times 4 = 3.2 \text{ mm} \\
b' &= 1.0 \, m = 1.0 \times 4 = 4.0 \text{ mm} \\
c' &= b' - a' = 4.0 - 3.2 = 0.8 \text{ mm} \\
h_t' &= a' + b' = 3.2 + 4.0 = 7.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Significance:** The stub-tooth system reduces the tooth height by approximately **20%** compared to the full-depth system. This results in:
1. **Lower bending stress** in the tooth (shorter lever arm),
2. **Reduced weight and material**,
3. **Lower contact ratio** (fewer teeth in simultaneous contact),
4. **Suitability for high-speed, lightweight automotive applications** such as racing transmissions and aerospace gears.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3 Choice]**

**(a)** Derive expressions for the **outside diameter** and **root diameter** of a spur gear in the **20° full-depth system**. Given: $m = 5$ mm, $z = 25$ teeth, compute all standard proportions.

**(b)** Explain why a **chordal tooth thickness** measurement is preferred over an arc tooth thickness measurement during gear inspection. Compute the **chordal tooth thickness** and **chordal addendum** for the given gear.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation and Calculations

**Derivation of Outside Diameter:**

The outside diameter is the pitch diameter plus two addenda (one on each side of the pitch circle):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_o &= d + 2a \\
D_o &= m z + 2(1.0 m) \\
D_o &= m(z + 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of Root Diameter:**

The root diameter is the pitch diameter minus two dedenda:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_r &= d - 2b \\
D_r &= m z - 2(1.25 m) \\
D_r &= m(z - 2.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Calculation** for $m = 5$ mm, $z = 25$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= m z = 5 \times 25 = 125 \text{ mm} \\
a &= 1.0 \times 5 = 5.0 \text{ mm} \\
b &= 1.25 \times 5 = 6.25 \text{ mm} \\
c &= 0.25 \times 5 = 1.25 \text{ mm} \\
h_t &= 2.25 \times 5 = 11.25 \text{ mm} \\
h_w &= 2.0 \times 5 = 10.0 \text{ mm} \\
D_o &= 5(25 + 2) = 135 \text{ mm} \\
D_r &= 5(25 - 2.5) = 112.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Chordal Measurements

**Why Chordal Thickness is Preferred:**
The arc tooth thickness varies along the tooth height due to the involute profile. A **chord** provides a unique, unambiguous measurement at a defined reference circle position, independent of any index or pitch errors in adjacent teeth. Hence, gear-tooth vernier calipers measure **chordal thickness** and **chordal addendum** for quality inspection.

**Chordal Tooth Thickness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_c &= p \left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{\pi^2}{24 z^2}\right] \\
p &= \pi m = \pi \times 5 = 15.708 \text{ mm} \\
t_c &= 15.708 \left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{\pi^2}{24 \times 25^2}\right] \\
t_c &= 15.708 \left[0.5 - \frac{9.8696}{15000}\right] \\
t_c &= 15.708 \left[0.5 - 0.000658\right] \\
t_c &= 15.708 \times 0.499342 \\
t_c &\approx 7.844 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Chordal Addendum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_c &= m \left[1 + \frac{\pi^2}{16 z}\right] \\
a_c &= 5 \left[1 + \frac{9.8696}{16 \times 25}\right] \\
a_c &= 5 \left[1 + \frac{9.8696}{400}\right] \\
a_c &= 5 \left[1 + 0.024674\right] \\
a_c &= 5 \times 1.024674 \\
a_c &\approx 5.123 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up addendum and dedendum formulas:** Students often write $a = 1.25m$ and $b = 1.0m$, which is **incorrect** for the 20° full-depth system. Remember: **Addendum is smaller than dedendum** in standard proportions.
> 2. **Forgetting clearance:** $c$ is not just a definition — it is **0.25 m for 20° full-depth** and is essential for manufacturing and lubrication.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Module **m must always be in mm** when other dimensions are in mm. Mixing units is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Outside vs. Root Diameter confusion:** $D_o = m(z+2)$ is the **largest** circle, and $D_r = m(z-2.5)$ is the **smallest** circle for the same gear.
> 5. **Stub-tooth substitution:** When switching to stub-tooth, students often forget that the **module and pressure angle remain the same** — only the addendum/dedendum coefficients change.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 3 Revision Sheet — Tooth Proportions**

- [x] **Module (m):** $m = d / z$ — the foundation of all proportional calculations. Always use **standard preferred values** (1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm).
- [x] **Circular Pitch (p):** $p = \pi m$ — the arc distance between adjacent teeth on the pitch circle.
- [x] **Tooth Thickness at P.C.:** $t = \pi m / 2$ — equal to the space width at the pitch circle.
- [x] **20° Full-Depth System:** $a = 1.0m$, $b = 1.25m$, $c = 0.25m$, $h_t = 2.25m$, $h_w = 2.0m$.
- [x] **20° Stub-Tooth System:** $a = 0.8m$, $b = 1.0m$, $c = 0.2m$, $h_t = 1.8m$, $h_w = 1.6m$.
- [x] **Outside Diameter:** $D_o = m(z + 2)$ for full depth; $D_o = m(z + 1.6)$ for stub.
- [x] **Root Diameter:** $D_r = m(z - 2.5)$ for full depth; $D_r = m(z - 2.0)$ for stub.
- [x] **Base Circle:** $D_b = d \cos \phi$, where $\phi = 20°$ standard. This is where the involute begins.
- [x] **Center Distance:** $C = m(z_1 + z_2) / 2$ — for a pair of mating gears.
- [x] **Chordal Inspection:** Chordal tooth thickness $t_c$ and chordal addendum $a_c$ are measured for quality control, using the **gear-tooth vernier caliper**.
- [x] **Engineering Trade-off:** Full-depth teeth give higher contact ratio (smoother); stub-tooth teeth give higher strength (shorter lever arm).
- [x] **Automotive Relevance:** Tooth proportions govern the design of **transmission gears, differential pinions, timing gears, and starter motor gears** in vehicles.
- [x] **AGMA Standard:** The 20° full-depth system with $a = 1.0m$, $b = 1.25m$ is the **default in modern automotive gear design**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beam Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Beam Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BEAM STRENGTH (LEWIS BEAM STRENGTH)**: In the context of automotive gear design, **Beam Strength** refers to the load-carrying capacity of a **gear tooth** when it is idealized and analyzed as a **short cantilever beam** of varying cross-section, fixed at the root (dedendum circle) and loaded at the tip by the tangential component of the meshing force. This concept was formalized by **Wilfred Lewis (1892)** and remains the foundational criterion for evaluating **bending fatigue failure** of gear teeth.

Mathematically, it represents the **maximum tangential load** that a gear tooth can transmit at the pitch circle without inducing a bending stress greater than the **allowable bending stress** at the root of the tooth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flag Pole on a Wall" Intuition

Imagine a **flagpole rigidly cemented into a brick wall** (the wall = the gear body / root circle, the pole = the gear tooth). If you push the flagpole horizontally at its tip, the point where it enters the wall experiences the **maximum bending moment** and is most likely to snap.

A gear tooth behaves identically:

- **Root of the tooth** → the "wall" (fixed support)
- **Tip of the tooth** → the "end of the pole" (load application point)
- **Tangential force at pitch circle** → the horizontal wind force

The flagpole's "strength to resist breaking" is its **Beam Strength**. For a gear, the tooth must be strong enough so that the **bending stress at the weakest section (the fillet at the root)** does not exceed the material's permissible bending fatigue limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students**: A gear tooth is NOT a uniform beam — it is a **tapered cantilever** (wider at root, narrower at tip). This is precisely why **Wilfred Lewis** introduced the famous **"Lewis Form Factor (Y)"** to account for the geometry-varying cross-section.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Quantities (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit |
|----------|--------|------------------|
| Tangential load at pitch circle | $W_t$ | **N (Newton)** |
| Module of gear | $m$ | **mm** |
| Face width of gear | $b$ | **mm** |
| Pressure angle | $\phi$ | **degrees (°)** |
| Lewis form factor | $Y$ | **dimensionless** |
| Allowable bending stress | $\sigma_b$ | **MPa (N/mm²)** |
| Beam strength | $S_b$ or $F_b$ | **N (Newton)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of Lewis Beam Theory

Wilfred Lewis made the following simplifying assumptions to enable closed-form analysis:

1. The **tangential load** $W_t$ is applied at the **pitch point** of the tooth (acting as a concentrated load).
2. The **radial component** $W_r$ of the meshing force is **neglected** (it only creates compressive stress, which is small).
3. The gear tooth is treated as a **cantilever beam of uniform strength** — its cross-section at every height is proportional to the bending moment at that section (this assumption gives the famous **parabolic tooth profile**, known as the **Lewis Profile**).
4. The **maximum bending stress** occurs at the **root fillet** of the tooth.
5. Stress concentration at the fillet is lumped into the **Lewis Form Factor** $Y$.
6. The load is **purely static-equivalent** for the analysis (dynamic effects handled via separate velocity factor $C_v$).

## 2.2 Why Is Beam Strength Important in Automotive Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility**:
> - **Transmission gears** in cars (synchromesh, differential gears) are sized primarily using beam strength.
> - **Gear failure modes**: Bending fatigue (tooth root crack) vs. Pitting vs. Scuffing. Beam strength addresses the **bending fatigue** mode.
> - **Design equation used in KTU**: Design tangential load = **Beam Strength / Velocity Factor**, ensuring the gear operates below the bending fatigue limit.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\mid` or `\vert` for absolute values — NEVER use bare `|` inside table cells.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Use |
|---|--------------|----------|-----|
| 1 | Tangential tooth load | $W_t = \frac{2T}{d}$ where $d = m \cdot z$ | Relates torque to load |
| 2 | Lewis Bending Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{W_t \cdot \cos\phi}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}$ | Stress at tooth root |
| 3 | **Lewis Beam Strength** (Force) | $S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$ | Maximum safe tangential load |
| 4 | Lewis Beam Strength (DP version) | $S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot p \cdot Y$ | When using diametral pitch $p$ |
| 5 | Lewis Form Factor (20° full-depth) | $Y = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P + \dfrac{h}{r}}$ | Classical empirical form |
| 6 | Design tangential load (Lewis) | $W_t^{design} = \dfrac{S_b}{C_v}$ | Incorporates dynamic factor |
| 7 | Lewis Equation (compact form) | $W_t = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y \cdot C_v$ | **MOST USED KTU FORMULA** |
| 8 | Tooth proportions (full-depth, 20°) | Addendum $a = m$, Dedendum $h = 1.25m$, Clearance $c = 0.25m$ | Standard AGMA gear geometry |
| 9 | Number of teeth vs form factor | $Y = 0.154 - \dfrac{0.912}{z}$ (approx, 20° PA) | Empirical shortcut for $z \geq 20$ |
| 10 | Wear strength (Buckingham, comparison) | $S_w = d \cdot b \cdot Q \cdot K$ | Used to compare with beam strength |

## 2.4 The Concept of "Lewis Form Factor (Y)"

The form factor $Y$ is **not a material property** — it is purely a **geometric factor** that captures:

- The shape of the tooth (full-depth vs stub)
- The pressure angle (20° vs 14.5°)
- The number of teeth (smaller $z$ → weaker tooth → lower $Y$)
- The stress concentration at the root fillet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher $Y$ → Stronger tooth**. For 20° full-depth teeth, $Y$ rises from ~0.32 (at 18 teeth) to ~0.485 (at ∞ teeth). **Never use $Y$ values from 14.5° PA gears with 20° PA design problems — they are incompatible.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why Beam Strength Matters |
|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Manual Transmission Gears** | Tooth root fracture is the primary failure mode → beam strength governs design |
| **Differential Gears** | High shock loads during cornering → beam strength gives fatigue safety |
| **Electric Vehicle Reduction Gears** | High RPM, high torque density → tooth bending is critical |
| **Motorcycle Transmission** | Compact, high-power → every gram of material must be justified by beam strength |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lewis Beam Strength Equation

### Step 1: Idealize the Gear Tooth as a Cantilever Beam

Consider a gear tooth fixed at the **root (dedendum circle)** and loaded at the **pitch circle** by a tangential force $W_t$. The pressure angle $\phi$ decomposes the normal force $F_n$ at the pitch point into:

- **Tangential component**: $W_t = F_n \cos\phi$
- **Radial component**: $W_r = F_n \sin\phi$ (neglected in Lewis analysis)

### Step 2: Bending Moment at the Root Section

The bending moment at any section at height $h_1$ from the root is:

$$M = W_t \cdot h_1$$

The **maximum bending moment** occurs at the **root**, where $h_1$ equals the total tooth height from root to pitch circle. Since addendum $= m$ and half the dedendum clearance adds to the cantilever:

$$M_{max} = W_t \cdot m \quad \text{(to a first approximation)}$$

### Step 3: Bending Stress Using the Section Modulus

For a **rectangular cross-section** at the root with width $b$ (face width) and thickness $t$ (tooth thickness at root):

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{M}{\frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}}$$

Lewis observed that real teeth fail along a diagonal line — the **weakest line** is not horizontal but inclined at approximately **30° to the tooth profile tangent**. The **effective tooth thickness** at the weakest section is taken as the **chordal thickness** $t_c$, not the circular thickness.

### Step 4: Introduce the Form Factor

Since deriving the exact section modulus for the complex curved tooth profile is impractical, Lewis introduced an **empirical form factor** $Y$ such that the bending stress becomes:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{W_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}$$

The form factor $Y$ effectively **lumps together** the effects of:
- Tooth geometry
- Load point location
- Section shape
- Stress concentration at the fillet

### Step 5: Equate to Allowable Stress & Rearrange

Setting the bending stress equal to the allowable bending stress $\sigma_b$:

$$\sigma_{allowable} = \frac{W_t}{b \cdot m \cdot Y}$$

Solving for the maximum safe tangential load (the **Beam Strength**):

$$\boxed{W_{t,max} = S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot m \cdot Y}$$

### Step 6: Inclusion of the Circular Pitch Factor (Final Lewis Form)

In the original AGMA convention, the module $m$ is replaced by the **circular pitch** $p_c = \pi m$, giving the standard textbook form:

$$S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y$$

In the diametral-pitch (DP) system:

$$S_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot p \cdot Y \quad \text{where } p = \frac{25.4}{m_{DP}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: A 20° full-depth spur gear with 25 teeth, module 4 mm, face width 30 mm, is made of steel with allowable bending stress **100 MPa**. Determine the **beam strength**.

**Step A — Identify given data:**

$$z = 25, \quad m = 4 \text{ mm}, \quad b = 30 \text{ mm}, \quad \phi = 20°, \quad \sigma_b = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step B — Determine the Lewis Form Factor $Y$:**

For 20° full-depth teeth with $z = 25$, from standard AGMA tables (or empirical formula):

$$Y = 0.485 - \frac{1.291}{z} \cdot (\text{correction terms}) \approx 0.354$$

Using the empirical approximation:

$$Y \approx 0.154 - \frac{0.912}{25} + \frac{1.275}{25^2} \approx 0.154 - 0.0365 + 0.00204 \approx 0.350$$

We will adopt $Y = 0.350$ (from standard tables).

**Step C — Apply the Lewis Beam Strength equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_b &= \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot Y \\
&= 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 30 \text{ mm} \times \pi \times 4 \text{ mm} \times 0.350 \\
&= 100 \times 30 \times 3.1416 \times 4 \times 0.350 \\
&= 100 \times 30 \times 3.1416 \times 1.4 \\
&= 100 \times 30 \times 4.3982 \\
&= 100 \times 131.95 \\
&= 13195 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Result:**

$$\boxed{S_b = 13{,}195 \text{ N} \approx 13.2 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step E — Interpretation for KTU Exam:**

This means the gear can safely transmit **13.2 kN of tangential load** at the pitch circle before the tooth root bending stress reaches the allowable 100 MPa. To find the safe **torque**:

$$T = \frac{W_t \cdot d}{2} = \frac{13195 \times (4 \times 25)}{2} = \frac{13195 \times 100}{2} = 6.6 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·mm} = 660 \text{ N·m}$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Beam Strength vs Wear Strength

| Criterion | Beam Strength (Lewis) | Wear Strength (Buckingham) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Failure mode addressed | Tooth root bending fatigue | Surface pitting / wear |
| Governing stress | Bending stress $\sigma_b$ | Contact stress (Hertzian) |
| Key factor | Form factor $Y$ | Load-stress factor $Q$ |
| Material property used | $\sigma_b$ (allowable bending) | Surface endurance limit |
| Typical governing criterion for | **High-speed, low-load** gears | **Slow-speed, high-load** gears |
| Dynamic factor | $C_v$ included separately | $C_v$ included separately |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: The actual design tangential load is:

$$W_{t,design} = \min\!\left(\frac{S_b}{C_v},\; \frac{S_w}{C_v}\right)$$

You compute **both** beam and wear strengths, then use the **smaller** as the design limit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Lewis Beam Strength Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Gear Parameters] --> B[Number of Teeth z]
    A --> C[Module m]
    A --> D[Face Width b]
    A --> E[Pressure Angle phi]
    A --> F[Allowable Bending Stress sigma_b]
    
    B --> G[Look up or compute Lewis Form Factor Y]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Calculate Beam Strength Sb]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    F --> H
    
    H --> I{Sb Computation}
    I --> J[Apply Velocity Factor Cv]

    M --> N[Check Bending Safety]
    M --> O[Check Wear Safety]
```
