# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Torque Capacity of a Multi-Plate Clutch under Uniform Pressure

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU Module 3. The examiner expects 5–6 clear logical steps.

### Statement
Prove that for a multi-plate clutch with $n$ contact surfaces, inner radius $R_i$ and outer radius $R_o$, under the assumption of **uniform pressure** $p$ over the friction face, the torque capacity is

$$
T = \mu \cdot p \cdot \pi \cdot n \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2) \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of an elemental ring:**
Consider an elemental annular ring of radius $r$ and width $\mathrm{d}r$ on the friction surface. The contact area of this ring is

$$
\mathrm{d}A = 2 \pi r \, \mathrm{d}r
$$

**Step 2 — Elemental normal force on the ring:**
Under uniform pressure $p$, the normal force on this ring is

$$
\mathrm{d}F = p \cdot \mathrm{d}A = 2 \pi p \, r \, \mathrm{d}r
$$

**Step 3 — Elemental friction force (tangential):**
The friction force opposing sliding is

$$
\mathrm{d}F_f = \mu \cdot \mathrm{d}F = 2 \pi \mu p \, r \, \mathrm{d}r
$$

**Step 4 — Elemental torque (about axis):**
The elemental torque transmitted through this ring is

$$
\mathrm{d}T = r \cdot \mathrm{d}F_f = 2 \pi \mu p \, r^2 \, \mathrm{d}r
$$

**Step 5 — Integration over the contact annulus:**
Integrate from $R_i$ to $R_o$ for **one** friction surface:

$$
T_{1} = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2 \pi \mu p \, r^2 \, \mathrm{d}r
$$

$$
T_{1} = 2 \pi \mu p \left[ \dfrac{r^3}{3} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o}
$$

$$
T_{1} = 2 \pi \mu p \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Account for n contact surfaces and total axial clamping force:**
For uniform pressure, the **total axial clamping force** is

$$
F = p \cdot \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)
$$

For $n$ friction surfaces,

$$
T = n \cdot T_1 = n \cdot 2 \pi \mu p \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{3}
$$

Substituting $p = F / [\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)]$:

$$
T = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}
$$

**Hence proved.** ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Capacity under Uniform Wear (Real Operating Condition)

In a real clutch, as the lining wears, the pressure redistributes so that $p \cdot r = \text{constant} = k$ (this is the **uniform wear hypothesis**, also called the **real operating condition**).

**Step 1 — Pressure distribution:**
$$
p(r) = \dfrac{k}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Elemental normal force:**

$$
\mathrm{d}F = p(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, \mathrm{d}r = 2 \pi k \, \mathrm{d}r
$$

**Step 3 — Total clamping force (integrate):**

$$
F = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2 \pi k \, \mathrm{d}r = 2 \pi k (R_o - R_i)
$$

Therefore,

$$
k = \dfrac{F}{2 \pi (R_o - R_i)}
$$

**Step 4 — Elemental torque:**

$$
\mathrm{d}T = \mu \cdot \mathrm{d}F \cdot r = \mu \cdot 2 \pi k \cdot r \, \mathrm{d}r
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate:**

$$
T_1 = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2 \pi \mu k \, r \, \mathrm{d}r = 2 \pi \mu k \cdot \dfrac{R_o^2 - R_i^2}{2} = \pi \mu k (R_o^2 - R_i^2)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $k$:**

$$
T_1 = \pi \mu \cdot \dfrac{F}{2 \pi (R_o - R_i)} \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2) = \mu F \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — For n surfaces:**

$$
\boxed{T = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison of design assumptions:**
>
> | Assumption | Use it when… | Gives… |
> |---|---|---|
> | Uniform Pressure | New clutch, no wear, conservative design | $T = n \mu F \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$ |
> | Uniform Wear | Steady-state, partially worn lining | $T = n \mu F \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ |
>
> For the same $F$, the **uniform wear** capacity is always **less** than uniform pressure → the wear assumption is more conservative for $R_o / R_i > 1$ ratio checks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Self-Energising Effect in a Single Leading-Shoe Drum Brake

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **second-most-tested** in KTU Module 3. It explains why rear drum brakes in small cars require less pedal effort than disc brakes of the same lining area.

### Setup
A pivoted shoe of length $L$ is actuated by a force $F$ at the heel. The lining pivots at distance $a$ from the pivot and ends at distance $b$ from the pivot, so $a + b = L$. The drum rotates such that friction at the lining **adds** to the normal force (leading shoe).

**Step 1 — Normal force distribution on lining:**
Let $p$ be the pressure at the lining. For a small element of length $\mathrm{d}x$ at distance $x$ from the pivot, the normal force is

$$
\mathrm{d}N = p \cdot r_{drum} \cdot \mathrm{d}x
$$

where $r_{drum}$ is the drum radius. The friction force on this element is

$$
\mathrm{d}F_f = \mu \cdot p \cdot r_{drum} \cdot \mathrm{d}x
$$

**Step 2 — Moment equilibrium about the pivot:**
Sum of moments about the pivot = 0. The actuating moment $F \cdot a$ is balanced by the friction moment (which is **augmented** by the normal-force moment of the lining):

$$
F \cdot a = \int_0^L (p \cdot r_{drum}) \cdot x \cdot \mathrm{d}x - \int_0^L \mu \cdot (p \cdot r_{drum}) \cdot x \cdot \mathrm{d}x \cdot \dfrac{r_{drum}}{r_{drum}}
$$

For uniform pressure (simplest case) where $p$ is constant, the integrals evaluate to

$$
\int_0^L x \, \mathrm{d}x = \dfrac{L^2}{2}
$$

For a lining that only contacts from $x = a$ to $x = a + b$ (i.e. $L = b$ when $a$ is the lever arm to actuation):

$$
F \cdot a = p \cdot r_{drum} \cdot \dfrac{b^2}{2} - \mu \cdot p \cdot r_{drum} \cdot \dfrac{b^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the lining pressure $p$:**

$$
p = \dfrac{2 F a}{r_{drum} \cdot b \cdot (b - \mu a)}
$$

**Step 4 — Self-energising condition:**
The lining force grows when the denominator shrinks, i.e. when $\mu a \to b$. The amplification factor is

$$
\boxed{\text{Self-Energy Factor (SEF)} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot a}{b}}
$$

If $\mu a > b$, the denominator becomes negative — the shoe is **over-energised** and locks (grabs) — this is unstable, and $a / b$ must satisfy $a / b < 1/\mu$.

**Step 5 — Resulting brake torque:**
The total tangential friction force integrated over the lining, multiplied by the drum radius, gives

$$
T_b = \mu \cdot p \cdot r_{drum}^2 \cdot b = \mu \cdot r_{drum} \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{2 a^2}{b - \mu a}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leading vs Trailing Shoe (KTU favourite):**
> - **Leading shoe** (rotation drags lining INTO the shoe) → friction force **adds** to clamping → **self-energised**, less pedal effort.
> - **Trailing shoe** (rotation drags lining AWAY) → friction force **opposes** clamping → **not** self-energised, more pedal effort, but **stable** and fade-resistant.
> Modern dual-servo drum brakes use **two leading shoes** for high brake torque, with a small wheel cylinder between them.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Application → Design Choice → Governing Equation

| Application | Geometry Choice | Clutch/Brake Type | Primary Equation | Critical Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Scooter CVT | Small diameter, high n | Multi-plate wet | $T = n \mu F (R_o + R_i)/2$ | n (number of plates) |
| Maruti Alto (passenger car) | Single plate, dry | Diaphragm spring | $F = p \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ | $R_o / R_i = 1.4$ |
| TATA LPT 1109 truck | Twin plate, dry | Coil spring + pneumatic booster | $T_{design} = T_e \cdot C_s$ | $C_s = 1.75$ |
| Tractor (Mahindra 575) | Single plate, dry, large | Heavy-duty organic lining | $p = 0.15$ MPa max | $T = 350$ Nm |
| Royal Enfield (350 cc) | Multi-plate wet | Hand-operated, 5 plates | $T = n \mu F (R_o + R_i)/2$ | $\mu = 0.1$ (wet) |
| F1 race car | Triple plate, sintered | Paddle-shift, hydraulic | $T_e \cdot C_s$ with $C_s = 2.5$ | Heat dissipation |
| Disc brake (passenger car) | Floating calliper, vented disc | Hydraulic, single piston | $T = 2 \mu F r_{eff}$ | $\Delta T \le 350$ °C |
| Drum brake (rear) | Leading + trailing | Hydraulic, duo-servo | $T_b = \mu p r_d^2 b$ | $SEF < 1$ |
| Air brake (truck) | S-cam drum | Air-actuated | $F_{push} = p_{air} \cdot A_{cyl}$ | $p_{air} = 0.6$–0.8 MPa |
| Earth-mover parking | Spring-applied, hydraulic-release | Dry disc, fail-safe | $F_{spring} \ge M g \sin\theta$ | $\theta = 20°$ min |

## 3.5 Numerical Solved Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

### Question
A multi-plate wet clutch for a 250 cc motorcycle has **6 friction plates** (so $n = 6$ contact surfaces), inner radius $R_i = 40$ mm, outer radius $R_o = 65$ mm. The coefficient of friction for the oil-bath lining is $\mu = 0.10$, and the maximum allowable axial clamping force is $F = 1800$ N. Calculate the torque capacity assuming (a) uniform pressure and (b) uniform wear. State which is the safer design value and why.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $n = 6$, $R_i = 0.040$ m, $R_o = 0.065$ m, $\mu = 0.10$, $F = 1800$ N

**Part (a) — Uniform pressure:**

$$
T_{UP} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}
$$

Compute $R_o^3 = 0.065^3 = 2.7463 \times 10^{-4}$ m³ and $R_i^3 = 0.040^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$ m³.

$$
R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 2.7463 \times 10^{-4} - 0.64 \times 10^{-4} = 2.1063 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3
$$

Compute $R_o^2 = 0.004225$ m² and $R_i^2 = 0.0016$ m².

$$
R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 0.002625 \, \text{m}^2
$$

The ratio:

$$
\dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2} = \dfrac{2.1063 \times 10^{-4}}{0.002625} = 0.08024 \, \text{m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{UP} = 6 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 1800 \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.08024
$$

$$
T_{UP} = 1080 \cdot 0.6667 \cdot 0.08024 \approx 57.76 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Part (b) — Uniform wear:**

$$
T_{UW} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}
$$

$$
T_{UW} = 6 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 1800 \cdot \dfrac{0.065 + 0.040}{2}
$$

$$
T_{UW} = 1080 \cdot 0.0525 = 56.7 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Conclusion:**
- $T_{UP} = 57.76$ N·m
- $T_{UW} = 56.7$ N·m
- The **uniform wear** value is the **safer** (lower) and is the recommended value for KTU board examination answers. The engine torque of a 250 cc motorcycle (~22 N·m) is well within capacity, leaving a service factor of $56.7 / 22 \approx 2.58$, which is acceptable for a wet multi-plate clutch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common mistake:** Students often forget the factor $n$ (number of contact surfaces) and write $T = \mu F (R_o + R_i)/2$ — this loses 2–3 marks per KTU valuation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Clutch Application Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vehicle Specification] --> B{Engine Peak Torque}
    B -- Less than 350 Nm --> C[Passenger Car Category]
    B -- Between 350 and 1000 Nm --> D[Light Commercial Vehicle]
    B -- Greater than 1000 Nm --> E[Heavy Commercial Vehicle]

    C --> C1[Single Plate Dry Clutch]
    C1 --> C2[Diaphragm Spring Actuation]
    C2 --> C3[Hydraulic or Cable Release]

    D --> D1{Twin or Single Plate}
    D1 -- Single --> D2[Coil Spring Clutch]
    D1 -- Twin --> D3[Twin Plate Clutch]
    D2 --> D4[Mechanical Linkage]
    D3 --> D4

    E --> E1[Single or Twin Plate Dry]
    E1 --> E2[Air Actuated Pneumatic Booster]
    E2 --> E3[Heavy Duty Organic Lining]

    C3 --> F[Application Validated]
    D4 --> F
    E3 --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Brake Application Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Vehicle Category] --> Q{Pedal Effort Required}
    Q -- Less than 100 N --> R[Power Assisted Required]
    Q -- Less than 200 N --> S[Manual Hydraulic OK]
    Q -- More than 300 N --> T[Air Brake System Needed]

    R --> R1[Vacuum or Hydraulic Booster]
    R1 --> R2[Disc Front and Disc Rear]
    R2 --> R3[High Performance Cars]

    S --> S1{Energy Dissipation per Stop}
    S1 -- Less than 200 kJ --> S2[Disc Front plus Drum Rear]
    S1 -- Less than 100 kJ --> S3[Drum Front and Drum Rear]
    S2 --> S4[Passenger Hatchback]
    S3 --> S4

    T --> T1[S Cam Drum Brake]
    T1 --> T2[Truck and Bus Application]
    T2 --> T3[Air Over Hydraulic Disc Option for Bus]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Interaction of Clutch and Brake in a Driveline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Block] --> CLU[Clutch Assembly]
    CLU -- Engaged --> GB[Manual Gearbox]
    GB -- Output Shaft --> PROP[Propeller Shaft]
    PROP --> DIFF[Differential Unit]
    DIFF --> WHL[Wheel Hub]
    WHL -- Rolling --> BRK[Brake Friction Element]
    BRK -- Friction Torque --> HUB[Hub Stops Wheel]

    CLU -. Driver disengages .-> PDL[Clutch Pedal]
    BRK -. Driver presses .-> BPL[Brake Pedal]
    BPL -. Vacuum assist .-> BST[Booster Unit]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Friction Material Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Operating Temperature Range] --> Y{Peak Temperature}
    Y -- Below 250 degree C --> Z1[Organic Resin Bonded]
    Y -- Between 250 and 400 degree C --> Z2[Semi Metallic]
    Y -- Between 400 and 600 degree C --> Z3[Sintered Metallic]
    Y -- Above 600 degree C --> Z4[Carbon Ceramic]

    Z1 --> APP1[Passenger Car Clutch Lining]
    Z2 --> APP2[Truck Brake Pad and Clutch Facing]
    Z3 --> APP3[Racing Clutch and High Performance Brake]
    Z4 --> APP4[Formula 1 and Aerospace Brake]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams and not physical drawings:**
> Mermaid cannot physically render 3D friction plates, drum geometry, or shoe actuation. Block diagrams are the KTU-endorsed substitute because they (1) show the **interaction** between components, (2) reveal the **selection logic** in a single view, and (3) make the **governing parameters** explicit at each branch.

## 4.5 Annotated Schematic (Text-Based) of a Single-Plate Diaphragm Clutch

```
                     ┌──────────────── Pressure Plate ─────────────────┐
                     │              (Diaphragm Spring)                │
                     │   ┌── Pivot Ring ──┐  ┌── Springs ──┐         │
                     │   │                │  │             │         │
                     │   │   ◯ ◯ ◯ ◯ ◯ ◯  │  │  | | | | |  │         │
                     │   └────────────────┘  └─────────────┘         │
                     └──────────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
                                            │ F_clamp (axial)
                                            ▼
        ┌───────────────────── Friction Disc (Driven Plate) ─────────────────┐
        │   ╔══════════════════╗   ╔══════════════════╗                     │
        │   ║  Friction Lining ║   ║  Friction Lining ║                     │
        │   ╚══════════════════╝   ╚══════════════════╝                     │
        │   R_i = 110 mm    Hub Spline    R_o = 165 mm                      │
        └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                            ▲
                                            │ Mounted on
                                            │
        ┌─────────────────── Flywheel (Driving Member) ───────────────────┐
        │   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
        │   │   Engine Crankshaft Flange + Ring Gear (Starter)     │      │
        │   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
        └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- **F_clamp** = $p \cdot \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ (axial clamping force)
- **Torque** = $\mu \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$ (uniform pressure)
- **Energy per engagement** = $\dfrac{1}{2} I_{engine} \omega^2$
- **Pedal effort** = $\dfrac{F_{clamp}}{\text{mechanical advantage} \cdot \eta_{hydraulic}}$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Each Part A question is a short-answer conceptual or definitional question worth 3 marks, model-answered in 60–80 words as per KTU ESE pattern.**

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PCMUT601]
**"Differentiate between a leading shoe and a trailing shoe in a drum brake with a neat sketch."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **leading shoe** is one in which the direction of drum rotation tends to **drag the friction lining into the shoe**, increasing the normal force and hence the braking torque. This is called the **self-energising** effect. A **trailing shoe** is one in which the rotation tends to **drag the lining away** from the shoe, decreasing the normal force — it is **non-self-energising**. A leading shoe provides higher brake torque for the same actuation force, while a trailing shoe is **stable and fade-resistant**. Most passenger-car rear drum brakes use a duo-servo (two leading shoe) configuration for high torque with low pedal effort.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PCMUT601]
**"State the uniform wear hypothesis in clutch design. Why is it preferred over uniform pressure for rating a used clutch?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **uniform wear hypothesis** (also called the **uniform wear theory**) states that after initial running-in, the wear rate at any radius is proportional to the work done per unit area, and the steady-state condition is such that **$p \cdot r = \text{constant}$** (where $p$ is the local pressure and $r$ is the radius from the axis). It is preferred over the uniform-pressure assumption because: (i) it represents the actual operating condition of a worn clutch more accurately, (ii) it gives a **lower and more conservative** torque capacity for the same clamping force when $R_o / R_i > 1$, and (iii) it yields a simple closed-form expression $T = n \mu F (R_o + R_i)/2$ that is easy to use in design.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Pattern

> **Part B questions in KTU 2024 ESE carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The model answers below follow the exact KTU valuation key format.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** A single-plate dry clutch is to be designed for a passenger car with the following data:
- Maximum engine torque $T_e = 180$ N·m at 3200 rpm
- Service factor $C_s = 1.5$
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.30$ (organic lining)
- Outer radius $R_o = 1.5 \times$ inner radius $R_o = 1.5 R_i$
- Permissible pressure $p = 0.20$ MPa
- Uniform pressure assumption is to be used.

Calculate (i) the design torque, (ii) the inner and outer radii in mm, and (iii) the axial clamping force required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **self-energising phenomenon** in a single leading-shoe drum brake with a labelled sketch. Derive the expression for the self-energising factor and state the stability condition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Design torque:**
$$
T_{design} = T_e \times C_s = 180 \times 1.5 = 270 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque formula under uniform pressure with $n = 1$:**
$$
T = \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $R_o = 1.5 R_i$:**
$$
R_o^3 = 3.375 R_i^3, \quad R_o^2 = 2.25 R_i^2
$$

$$
\dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2} = \dfrac{2.375 R_i^3}{1.25 R_i^2} = 1.9 R_i
$$

So $T = \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 1.9 R_i = \mu F \cdot 1.2667 R_i$

**Step 4 — Clamping force in terms of $R_i$:**
$$
F = p \cdot \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2) = p \cdot \pi \cdot 1.25 R_i^2
$$

With $p = 0.20 \times 10^6$ Pa:
$$
F = 0.20 \times 10^6 \cdot \pi \cdot 1.25 R_i^2 = 7.854 \times 10^5 R_i^2 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into torque equation and equate to $T_{design}$:**
$$
270 = 0.30 \cdot (7.854 \times 10^5 R_i^2) \cdot 1.2667 R_i
$$

$$
270 = 2.984 \times 10^5 R_i^3
$$

$$
R_i^3 = \dfrac{270}{2.984 \times 10^5} = 9.049 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
R_i = 0.0966 \, \text{m} = 96.6 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Outer radius:**
$$
R_o = 1.5 \times 96.6 = 144.9 \, \text{mm} \approx 145 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Clamping force:**
$$
F = 7.854 \times 10^5 \times (0.0966)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^5 \times 9.33 \times 10^{-3} = 7329 \, \text{N} \approx 7.33 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Final Answers (Part a):**
- (i) $T_{design} = 270$ N·m
- (ii) $R_i = 96.6$ mm, $R_o = 144.9$ mm
- (iii) $F = 7329$ N $\approx 7.33$ kN

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Sketch (text representation):**

```
              Drum rotates → (leading direction)
              ┌─────────────────────────┐
              │     ┌─── Lining ───┐    │
              │     │              │    │  ← Drum
              │     └─────▲────────┘    │
              │       ╔═══╧═══╗         │
              │       ║ Piv   ║ ← F·a   │  (actuation force × lever arm)
              │       ╚══════╝          │
              │  Pivot ●                │
              └─────────────────────────┘
              Lining length = b, lever arm = a
```

**Derivation (already given in Section 3.3):**

The actuating moment is $F \cdot a$. The normal force on the lining creates a counter-moment $p \cdot r_{drum} \cdot b^2/2$ about the pivot, and the friction force creates an additional moment $\mu \cdot p \cdot r_{drum} \cdot b^2/2$ that **adds to** the clamping action (since it is a leading shoe).

$$
F \cdot a = \dfrac{p \cdot r_{drum} \cdot b^2}{2} - \mu \cdot \dfrac{p \cdot r_{drum} \cdot b^2}{2}
$$

$$
p = \dfrac{2 F a}{r_{drum} \cdot b \cdot (b - \mu a)}
$$

**Self-energising factor:**
$$
SEF = \dfrac{\mu \cdot a}{b}
$$

**Stability condition:** For the brake to be stable (not lock up), the denominator $(b - \mu a)$ must remain positive, i.e.

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{a}{b} < \dfrac{1}{\mu}}
$$

If $\mu = 0.35$, then $a/b < 2.857$ — easily satisfied. But if $\mu$ rises to **0.50** (wet, hot, fading), then $a/b < 2.0$ — the margin shrinks, and the brake may **grab**. **(7 marks — derivation + condition + sketch + explanation)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** A heavy commercial vehicle uses a **twin-plate dry clutch** with the following design data:
- Engine torque $T_e = 850$ N·m
- Service factor $C_s = 1.75$
- Inner radius $R_i = 110$ mm, outer radius $R_o = 175$ mm
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.32$
- Number of contact surfaces $n = 4$ (twin plate = 2 driven + 1 driving × 2 sides)

Determine (i) the design torque, (ii) the axial clamping force required using the **uniform wear** assumption, and (iii) the resulting average lining pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **applications** of the following brake systems, justifying each choice with one practical example:
- (i) Disc brake vs Drum brake
- (ii) Air brake vs Hydraulic brake
- (iii) ABS vs Non-ABS braking

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Design torque:**
$$
T_{design} = 850 \times 1.75 = 1487.5 \, \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Uniform wear formula with $n = 4$:**
$$
T = n \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $F$:**
$$
F = \dfrac{2 T}{n \mu (R_o + R_i)} = \dfrac{2 \times 1487.5}{4 \times 0.32 \times (0.175 + 0.110)}
$$

$$
F = \dfrac{2975}{4 \times 0.32 \times 0.285} = \dfrac{2975}{0.3648} = 8154.5 \, \text{N} \approx 8.15 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Average lining pressure:**
$$
p_{avg} = \dfrac{F}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)} = \dfrac{8154.5}{\pi (0.175^2 - 0.110^2)}
$$

$$
p_{avg} = \dfrac{8154.5}{\pi (0.030625 - 0.0121)} = \dfrac{8154.5}{\pi \times 0.018525} = \dfrac{8154.5}{0.05820}
$$

$$
p_{avg} = 1.401 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 0.140 \, \text{MPa}
$$

This is within the allowable limit of 0.1–0.35 MPa for a heavy-duty dry clutch.

**Final Answers (Part a):**
- (i) $T_{design} = 1487.5$ N·m
- (ii) $F \approx 8.15$ kN
- (iii) $p_{avg} = 0.140$ MPa ✓

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Disc vs Drum brake:**

| Aspect | Disc Brake | Drum Brake |
|---|---|---|
| Heat dissipation | Excellent (open to air) | Poor (enclosed drum) |
| Fade resistance | High | Low (expanding drum) |
| Self-energising | None (purely friction-based) | Yes (leading shoe) |
| Weight | Lighter | Heavier |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Application | **Front wheels of passenger cars** (e.g., Maruti Swift) | **Rear wheels of budget hatchbacks** (e.g., Maruti Alto) |
| Pedal effort | Higher without booster | Lower (self-energised) |

**Justification:** Front brakes handle ~70 % of stopping energy, so heat dissipation is critical — disc brake is used. Rear brakes are low-energy and benefit from lower cost and self-energising — drum brake is used.

**(ii) Air vs Hydraulic brake:**

| Aspect | Hydraulic Brake | Air Brake |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Brake fluid (incompressible) | Compressed air |
| Response time | Fast (~30 ms) | Slower (~300 ms) |
| Force multiplication | Moderate | Very high (large cylinder area) |
| Failure mode | Self-energising residual; needs dual-circuit | Spring-applied, fail-safe |
| Application | **Cars, two-wheelers, LCVs** (e.g., Hyundai i20) | **Trucks, buses, heavy vehicles** (e.g., TATA LPT) |

**Justification:** Trucks have GVW > 12 tonnes requiring pedal effort of > 500 N if hydraulic — physically impossible. Air pressure at 0.6–0.8 MPa provides the necessary clamping force with low pedal effort.

**(iii) ABS vs Non-ABS:**

| Aspect | Non-ABS | ABS |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel lock on panic stop | Yes (skid) | No (modulated) |
| Steering control during braking | Lost | Retained |
| Stopping distance on dry road | Slightly shorter | Slightly longer |
| Stopping distance on wet/loose road | Longer (locked wheel slides) | Shorter (rolling wheel) |
| Application | **Older two-wheelers, budget cars** (entry-level variants) | **All BS-VI cars, premium bikes, mandated for all new 4-wheelers from 2019** |

**Justification:** ABS modulates brake pressure 15–25 times per second, preventing lock-up and retaining steering control. It is mandatory for all new passenger cars sold in India since 2019. **(7 marks — comparative table + one example + justification each)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Module 3 Applications:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $n$ in multi-plate clutch torque equations.** A twin-plate clutch has $n = 4$, not 2. Many students write $T = \mu F (R_o + R_i)/2$ and lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing uniform pressure and uniform wear in clutch design.** The examiner expects you to **state the assumption explicitly** before using the formula. Marks are awarded for the assumption, not just the final number.
>
> 3. **In drum brake self-energising derivation, students forget the term $\mu \cdot p \cdot r_d \cdot b^2/2$ (the friction-augmented moment).** This is the heart of the derivation. Losing it = losing 4 marks.
>
> 4. **In Part B, students write generic applications (e.g., "disc brake is used in cars") without naming a specific model or vehicle.** Always anchor your answer in a real KTU-relevant example (Maruti, TATA, Royal Enfield, Mahindra).
>
> 5. **Not specifying units in the final answer.** $T = 270$ gets zero; $T = 270$ N·m gets full marks.
>
> 6. **In Part A (3 marks), students exceed 100 words and run out of time for Part B.** Keep Part A crisp: definition + one numerical value or example.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **The following 14 points are the most important takeaways for a KTU 2024 ESE aspirant. Tick them off before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Clutch torque — uniform pressure:** $T = n \mu F \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$
- [ ] **Clutch torque — uniform wear:** $T = n \mu F \cdot \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$
- [ ] **Uniform wear is the conservative (and exam-recommended) design assumption** for an operating clutch.
- [ ] **Service factor $C_s$**: Passenger car = 1.25–1.5, Truck = 1.5–2.0, Tractor = 1.75–2.25.
- [ ] **Multi-plate wet clutch** is used in two-wheelers and earth-movers (small diameter, high $n$, oil cooling).
- [ ] **Single-plate dry clutch with diaphragm spring** is the default for passenger cars.
- [ ] **Twin-plate dry clutch** is used when engine torque > 600 Nm (mid-range LCVs and SUVs).
- [ ] **Drum brake leading shoe = self-energised**; **trailing shoe = stable but non-self-energised**.
- [ ] **Self-energising factor $SEF = \mu a / b$; stability requires $a/b < 1/\mu$.**
- [ ] **Disc brake**: front wheels of cars — high heat dissipation, no self-energising, requires booster.
- [ ] **Air brake**: trucks and buses — spring-applied fail-safe parking, low pedal effort.
- [ ] **Hydraulic brake fluid is incompressible**; air brakes use compressed air at 0.6–0.8 MPa.
- [ ] **Centrifugal clutch** is used in scooters (CVT input) — engagement is automatic at a preset rpm.
- [ ] **ABS** modulates brake pressure 15–25 times/sec to prevent wheel lock — **mandatory for all new 4-wheelers in India since 2019 (AIS-018 / GSR 209(E))**.

> **Final mnemonic to remember the design order:**
> **"T = n·μ·F·R_mean, F = p·π·(R_o²−R_i²), T_design = T_engine·C_s"**
> *(Torque = friction × force × mean radius, Force = pressure × contact area, Design torque = engine torque × safety margin.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terms in Worm Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Terms in Worm Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Worm Gear** is a special class of crossed-axis helical gear pair used to transmit motion and power between two non-intersecting, perpendicular shafts (typically at 90°). It consists of two elements:

- **Worm (Driver)**: A screw-like cylindrical gear with one or more helical threads (starts) cut on its pitch cylinder.
- **Worm Wheel (Driven)**: A helical gear (essentially similar to a helical gear) that meshes with the worm, resembling a spur gear in appearance but with a curved flank profile (usually of conjugate form, e.g., ZN1, ZI, ZK, ZA from the AGMA/ISO classification).

The worm essentially acts as a **screw** whose rotational motion is converted into the rotational/linear motion of the mating wheel through sliding contact, similar in principle to a rack-and-pinion sliding along a screw shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The terms defined here form the *geometrical foundation* for all design calculations in worm gear pairs — including beam strength, wear strength, thermal rating, and efficiency.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine turning a **carpenter's wood screw** (the worm) using a screwdriver. As the screw rotates one full turn, it advances a fixed distance — called the *lead*. Now imagine placing a **gear with curved teeth** around that screw so that its teeth engage with the screw thread. Each time the screw turns, it pushes the gear teeth sideways, making the gear rotate by exactly one tooth pitch.

- The **screw** = Worm
- The **gear around it** = Worm Wheel
- The **distance the screw moves in one turn** = **Lead (L)**
- The **angle the thread makes with the axis** = **Lead Angle (λ)**

### 1.3 Real-World Automotive Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Steering gearboxes** in passenger cars (e.g., Maruti, Tata, Mahindra worm-and-roller/worm-and-sector steering).
> - **Power window lift mechanisms**.
> - **Windshield wiper gear trains** (older generation vehicles).
> - **Electric vehicle (EV) reduction gearboxes** (some use multi-start worms for compactness).
> - **Gear-shift quadrants** and **parking brake mechanisms**.
> - **Lubrication oil pumps** in heavy-duty trucks.

Because a worm gear provides a very large speed reduction in a single stage (typically 5:1 to 75:1) and is **self-locking**, it is heavily favoured in safety-critical automotive sub-systems.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standards govern worm gear geometry in KTU-evaluated problems:

- **Pressure Angle (α)** = **20°** (standard ISO/AGMA full-depth system).
- **Normal Module (m_n)** = Transverse Module ($m_t$) × cos(λ). This is the *standard* module on which the entire geometry is based.
- **Standard Centre Distance (a)** is generally preferred and calculated as:

$$a = 0.5 \times (d_w + D_w)$$

where **d_w** = worm pitch diameter and **D_w** = worm wheel pitch diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU problem solving, always quote the **axial module (m_x)** when referring to a worm — because the module is conventionally measured on the worm's axial plane, since gear cutters are not used on the worm; the worm itself *acts* as the cutter for the wheel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometry of the Worm — Step by Step Logic

A worm is essentially a **helical gear with a very large helix angle**. If we "unwrap" the pitch cylinder of the worm, the thread appears as a straight line wrapped around the cylinder, exactly like the thread of a screw.

**Why does this matter for design?**
Because when we calculate strength, sliding velocity, or efficiency, we always work on this "unwrapped" pitch cylinder — the geometry becomes a simple right triangle:

- **Vertical side** = π × d_w = circumference of worm pitch cylinder
- **Horizontal side** = Lead (L)
- **Hypotenuse** = Length of thread helix on one full revolution
- **Angle opposite to L** = Lead Angle (λ)

This right-triangle model is the foundation of all worm gear kinematics.

### 2.2 The 18 Most Critical Terms in Worm Gear (KTU High-Yield List)

Below is a structured breakdown of the essential nomenclature that examiners expect KTU students to define and use in calculations.

#### A. Terms on the Worm

| # | Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|------|--------|------------|
| 1 | Axial Module | $m_x$ | Module measured in the axial plane of the worm. Equal to the normal module $m_n \div \cos(\lambda)$ |
| 2 | Axial Pitch | $p_x$ | Distance between adjacent thread profiles measured along the axis. $p_x = \pi \cdot m_x$ |
| 3 | Lead | $L$ | Distance the worm thread advances axially in one full revolution. $L = z_w \cdot p_x$ |
| 4 | Number of Threads/Starts | $z_w$ | Number of independent helical threads on the worm |
| 5 | Lead Angle | $\lambda$ | Angle between the thread helix and a plane perpendicular to the worm axis |
| 6 | Pitch Diameter of Worm | $d_w$ | Diameter of the worm pitch cylinder. $d_w = m_x \cdot q$ |
| 7 | Diametral Quotient | $q$ | Ratio of worm pitch diameter to axial module. $q = d_w \div m_x$ |
| 8 | Outside Diameter of Worm | $d_{ao}$ | $d_{ao} = d_w + 2 \cdot m_x$ |
| 9 | Root Diameter of Worm | $d_{rf}$ | $d_{rf} = d_w - 2.4 \cdot m_x$ |
| 10 | Length of Worm | $l_w$ | Minimum bearing face length of the worm |

#### B. Terms on the Worm Wheel

| # | Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|------|--------|------------|
| 11 | Transverse Module | $m_t$ | Module in the transverse plane of the wheel. $m_t = m_n \div \cos(\lambda)$ |
| 12 | Circular Pitch | $p_c$ | $p_c = \pi \cdot m_t = p_x$ |
| 13 | Number of Teeth | $z_g$ | Number of teeth on the worm wheel |
| 14 | Pitch Diameter of Wheel | $D_w$ | $D_w = m_t \cdot z_g$ |
| 15 | Outside Diameter of Wheel | $D_{oe}$ | $D_{oe} = D_w + 2 \cdot m_t$ |
| 16 | Throat Diameter of Wheel | $D_{th}$ | Diameter of the wheel rim at the throat. $D_{th} = d_{ao}$ |
| 17 | Face Width of Worm | $b$ | Length of worm that engages the wheel |
| 18 | Throat Angle | $\phi$ | Half-angle subtended by the worm at the wheel centre (typically $\leq 60°$) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** you will need for any worm gear problem in the KTU 2024 scheme examination. Memorise it thoroughly.

| Formula No. | Equation | Description |
|-------------|----------|-------------|
| **F-1** | $L = z_w \cdot p_x$ | Lead = number of starts × axial pitch |
| **F-2** | $p_x = \pi \cdot m_x$ | Axial pitch |
| **F-3** | $\tan(\lambda) = \dfrac{L}{\pi \cdot d_w}$ | Lead angle from unwrapped geometry |
| **F-4** | $d_w = m_x \cdot q$ | Worm pitch diameter |
| **F-5** | $D_w = m_t \cdot z_g$ | Wheel pitch diameter |
| **F-6** | $m_t = \dfrac{m_x}{\cos(\lambda)}$ | Relation between axial and transverse module |
| **F-7** | $i = \dfrac{z_g}{z_w}$ | Velocity ratio (speed reduction) |
| **F-8** | $a = 0.5 \cdot (d_w + D_w)$ | Centre distance |
| **F-9** | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_w}{60 \cdot \cos(\lambda)}$ | Sliding velocity (m/s) |
| **F-10** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_w \cdot N_g}{60}$ | Pitch line velocity of wheel (m/s) |

> **Note on Units (KTU Convention):**
> - Linear dimensions: **mm**
> - Diametral quotient ($q$): dimensionless, typically between **8 and 16** for power transmission.
> - Lead angle ($\lambda$): typically between **4° and 25°** for power worms.
> - Sliding velocity ($V_s$): **m/s**.

### 2.4 Why These Terms Matter in Real Engineering

Each term is not just a textbook label — it has direct engineering consequences:

- **Lead Angle (λ)** controls **efficiency**. Higher λ → higher efficiency but lower self-locking ability. Below ~6° the worm becomes self-locking (a critical safety feature in steering gears and lifts).
- **Sliding Velocity ($V_s$)** governs **heat generation** and **lubricant selection**. High $V_s$ requires EP (extreme pressure) oils and forced lubrication.
- **Diametral Quotient (q)** controls **worm rigidity** and avoids interference. Small $q$ → flexible worm; large $q$ → strong but bulky gear pair.
- **Number of Starts ($z_w$)** dictates **speed reduction ratio** directly. Single-start ($z_w = 1$) gives maximum reduction; multi-start gives smoother but less reduction per stage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Lead (L) and its Relationship to Axial Pitch

**Statement to Prove:**
The lead of a worm equals the product of the number of starts ($z_w$) and the axial pitch ($p_x$).

**Step 1 — Visualising the Unwrapped Pitch Cylinder:**
If we cut the pitch cylinder of the worm along its axis and lay it flat, the helical thread becomes a straight inclined line. This produces a right triangle with:

- **Base** = circumference of pitch cylinder = $\pi \cdot d_w$
- **Height** = Lead (L)
- **Inclination** = Lead Angle ($\lambda$)

**Step 2 — Counting Threads:**
Each individual helical thread on the worm is a separate, parallel helix. For a single-start worm, one full revolution moves the thread forward by exactly one axial pitch (the distance between adjacent thread crests measured along the axis).

**Step 3 — Generalising to Multi-Start Worms:**
For a worm with $z_w$ starts, each revolution advances all $z_w$ threads simultaneously, each by $p_x$. Therefore, the total axial advance per revolution is:

$$L = z_w \cdot p_x$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Definition of Axial Pitch:**
The axial pitch is the linear distance along the axis between corresponding points on adjacent threads, which equals the circular pitch of the mating wheel:

$$p_x = \pi \cdot m_x$$

**Step 5 — Final Form:**

$$L = z_w \cdot \pi \cdot m_x$$

This is **Equation F-1** in the cheat sheet above and is the foundation of all speed-ratio calculations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Lead Angle (λ)

**Statement to Prove:**
$\tan(\lambda) = \dfrac{L}{\pi \cdot d_w}$

**Step 1 — Refer to the Unwrapped Geometry:**
From Section 3.1, the right triangle on the unwrapped pitch cylinder has:
- Opposite side (rise) = Lead (L)
- Adjacent side (run) = Circumference = $\pi \cdot d_w$

**Step 2 — Apply Trigonometric Definition:**
By definition of the tangent function:

$$\tan(\lambda) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{L}{\pi \cdot d_w}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $L = z_w \cdot p_x = z_w \cdot \pi \cdot m_x$:**

$$\tan(\lambda) = \frac{z_w \cdot \pi \cdot m_x}{\pi \cdot d_w} = \frac{z_w \cdot m_x}{d_w}$$

**Step 4 — Use $d_w = q \cdot m_x$:**

$$\tan(\lambda) = \frac{z_w \cdot m_x}{q \cdot m_x} = \frac{z_w}{q}$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\tan(\lambda) = \frac{z_w}{q}$$

This is the **master equation** that KTU examiners love. Given any two of {$z_w$, $q$, $\lambda$}, the third is immediately obtainable.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Centre Distance (a)

**Step 1 — Define Centre Distance:**
For two shafts at 90°, the centre distance is the perpendicular distance between the worm axis and the wheel axis.

**Step 2 — Geometrically Add the Two Pitch Radii:**

$$a = \frac{d_w}{2} + \frac{D_w}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Standard Definitions:**
Using $d_w = m_x \cdot q$ and $D_w = m_t \cdot z_g$:

$$a = \frac{m_x \cdot q}{2} + \frac{m_t \cdot z_g}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Express $m_t$ in terms of $m_x$** using $m_t = m_x \div \cos(\lambda)$:

$$a = \frac{m_x}{2}\left[q + \frac{z_g}{\cos(\lambda)}\right]$$

This is the **complete centre distance equation** used in design problems where the centre distance must be checked against a standardised value (typically rounded to whole mm or to 0.5 mm).

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Velocity Ratio (i) and Sliding Velocity (V_s)

**Velocity Ratio (Speed Reduction):**

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity Relation:**
For a gear pair in mesh, the linear pitch line velocity must be equal at the point of contact:

$$\pi \cdot m_t \cdot N_w \cdot \cos(\lambda) = \pi \cdot m_t \cdot N_g$$

The $\cos(\lambda)$ factor on the worm side accounts for the helical (axial) component of the worm's linear velocity. After cancelling $\pi \cdot m_t$:

$$N_w \cdot \cos(\lambda) = N_g$$

**Step 2 — Apply Definition of Speed Ratio:**

$$i = \frac{N_w}{N_g} = \frac{1}{\cos(\lambda)} \cdot \frac{z_g}{z_w}$$

For KTU 2024 examinations, a simpler equivalent form is often used (assuming small corrections):

$$i = \frac{z_g}{z_w}$$

**Sliding Velocity ($V_s$):**

**Step 1 — Pitch Line Velocity of Worm (Tangential component):**

$$V_{tw} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_w}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Add Axial Component of Wheel (which slides past the worm thread):**
The wheel's pitch line velocity acts perpendicular to the worm axis. The total sliding velocity is the vector sum:

$$V_s = \sqrt{V_{tw}^2 + V_{tw}^2 \cdot \tan^2(\lambda)} = \frac{V_{tw}}{\cos(\lambda)}$$

**Step 3 — Final Form:**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_w}{60 \cdot \cos(\lambda)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sliding velocity $V_s$ is **always greater than the worm pitch line velocity** $V_{tw}$ because the wheel contributes a perpendicular sliding component. This is why worm gears run hot and require special lubricants.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU 2-Mark Type)

**Problem:**
A worm gear pair has the following data:
- Number of starts on worm, $z_w = 4$
- Diametral quotient, $q = 10$
- Axial module, $m_x = 5$ mm
- Number of teeth on wheel, $z_g = 30$

**Find:** Lead angle (λ), Centre distance (a), and Velocity ratio (i).

**Solution:**

**Step A — Lead Angle:**
Using the derived compact formula:

$$\tan(\lambda) = \frac{z_w}{q} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}(0.4) = 21.8°$$

**Step B — Worm Pitch Diameter:**

$$d_w = m_x \cdot q = 5 \times 10 = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step C — Wheel Pitch Diameter:**

$$\cos(\lambda) = \cos(21.8°) = 0.9285$$

$$m_t = \frac{m_x}{\cos(\lambda)} = \frac{5}{0.9285} = 5.385 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_w = m_t \cdot z_g = 5.385 \times 30 = 161.55 \text{ mm}$$

**Step D — Centre Distance:**

$$a = 0.5 \times (d_w + D_w) = 0.5 \times (50 + 161.55) = 105.78 \text{ mm}$$

**Step E — Velocity Ratio:**

$$i = \frac{z_g}{z_w} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5$$

> **Result Summary:**
> $\lambda = 21.8°$, $a = 105.78$ mm, $i = 7.5 : 1$

This kind of 4-step problem is the *exact format* expected in KTU Part A (3-mark) and as the first sub-part of a 14-mark Part B question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Worm Gear Pair Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Driver Shaft] --> nodeB[Worm with z_w starts]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Lead Angle lambda}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sliding Contact Zone]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Worm Wheel with z_g teeth]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Driven Shaft at 90 degrees]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Pitch Diameter d_w]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Pitch Diameter D_w]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Centre Distance a]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Design Verification]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Velocity Ratio i = z_g / z_w]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Sliding Velocity V_s]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Strength and Thermal Check]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Unwrapped Worm Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Pitch Cylinder Unwrapped] --> nodeQ[Right Triangle Model]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Vertical Side = pi times d_w]
    nodeQ --> nodeS[Horizontal Side = Lead L]
    nodeQ --> nodeT[Hypotenuse = Helix Length]
    nodeQ --> nodeU[Angle lambda = Lead Angle]
    nodeR --> nodeV[Module m_x selected]
    nodeS --> nodeW[Number of starts z_w]
    nodeV --> nodeX[Tan lambda = z_w divided by q]
    nodeW --> nodeX
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Worm Gear System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput[Power Input from Engine or Motor] --> nodeCoupling[Flexible Coupling]
    nodeCoupling --> nodeWormShaft[Worm Shaft with Bearings]
    nodeWormShaft --> nodeWormBody[Worm with Helical Threads]
    nodeWormBody --> nodeMesh[Mesh Zone - Sliding Contact]
    nodeMesh --> nodeWheelTeeth[Worm Wheel Teeth Curved Profile]
    nodeWheelTeeth --> nodeWheelShaft[Worm Wheel Shaft with Bearings]
    nodeWheelShaft --> nodeOutput[Output to Differential or Load]
    nodeMesh --> nodeLube[Lubricant Splash System]
    nodeLube --> nodeCooling[Heat Dissipation via Housing]
```

### 4.4 Identification of Key Dimensions (ASCII Visual Map)

```
       Z-axis (Worm axis)
       |
       |     OUTSIDE d_ao
       |    /==========\
       |   /            \
       |  |   WORM       |
       |  |   PITCH d_w  |  <-- d_w = m_x * q
       |   \            /
       |    \==========/
       |     ROOT d_rf
       |
       |<-------------->|
           Length l_w
```

### 4.5 Schematic of Worm-Wheel Mesh (Top View)

```
              Worm (along X-axis)
        ===============================
        ||| ||| ||| ||| ||| ||| ||| |||
        ===============================
                       |
                       |  Throat region
                       v
                  .---.---.---.
                /  / | / | / | /
              /  /  |/  |/  |/
            +--+---+---+---+---   Worm Wheel (in YZ-plane)
            |  |   |   |   |   |
            +--+---+---+---+---+
              \  \  |\  |\  |\
                \  \ | \ | \ | \
                  '---'---'---'
```

> [!NOTE]
> For complex physical drawings beyond Mermaid capability, students should practice drawing:
> 1. The unwrapped pitch cylinder showing the right triangle (λ, L, πd_w).
> 2. The cross-section of the mesh showing the throat and face width.
> 3. The line sketch showing addendum, dedendum, and pitch circles of both elements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
Define the following terms as applied to a worm gear pair:
(a) Lead
(b) Lead angle
(c) Diametral quotient

**Model Answer:**

> (a) **Lead (L):** The axial distance advanced by a worm thread in one complete revolution. It is given by $L = z_w \cdot p_x$ where $z_w$ is the number of starts and $p_x$ is the axial pitch.
>
> (b) **Lead Angle (λ):** The angle made by the worm thread helix with a plane perpendicular to the worm axis. It is given by $\tan(\lambda) = \dfrac{L}{\pi \cdot d_w} = \dfrac{z_w}{q}$.
>
> (c) **Diametral Quotient (q):** The dimensionless ratio of the worm pitch diameter to the axial module, $q = \dfrac{d_w}{m_x}$. Typical values lie between 8 and 16 for power transmission worms.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
Explain the terms "axial pitch" and "circular pitch" as applied to worm gears. Why are they equal in magnitude?

**Model Answer:**

> The **axial pitch ($p_x$)** of a worm is the distance measured along the worm axis between corresponding points on two adjacent thread profiles. It is given by $p_x = \pi \cdot m_x$.
>
> The **circular pitch ($p_c$)** of the worm wheel is the arc distance measured along the pitch circle of the wheel between corresponding points on two adjacent teeth, given by $p_c = \pi \cdot m_t$.
>
> Since the worm and wheel are conjugate (the worm acts as the cutter for the wheel), their respective modules must satisfy $m_x = m_t \cdot \cos(\lambda)$, ensuring that $p_x = p_c$. This equality is essential for proper meshing and continuous transmission of motion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to convert **rpm to rps** (divide by 60) when calculating sliding velocity — this is the **#1 mistake** in KTU answer sheets.
> - Confusing **axial module ($m_x$)** with **transverse module ($m_t$)**. By KTU convention, **all modules quoted in problems are axial** unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> - Reporting **λ in radians** instead of **degrees** — examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> - Using **centre distance in mm** without converting to **m** when calculating torques (a common dimensional mismatch).

---

**Q3A.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between lead angle (λ), number of starts ($z_w$), and diametral quotient (q) for a worm gear. State the typical range of lead angles used for power transmission worms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-start worm has a pitch diameter of 60 mm and a lead of 18.85 mm. Calculate:
1. The axial module
2. The diametral quotient
3. The lead angle
4. The centre distance if the worm wheel has 40 teeth **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *See Section 3.2 derivation.*
From the unwrapped pitch cylinder:

$$\tan(\lambda) = \frac{z_w}{q}$$

Typical range for power transmission worms: **4° to 25°**. For self-locking worms (used in lifts and steering), $\lambda \leq 6°$.

**(b) Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Axial Module:**
For a single-start worm, $z_w = 1$, so $L = p_x = \pi \cdot m_x$.

$$m_x = \frac{L}{\pi} = \frac{18.85}{\pi} = 6.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Diametral Quotient:**

$$q = \frac{d_w}{m_x} = \frac{60}{6} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Lead Angle:**

$$\tan(\lambda) = \frac{z_w}{q} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}(0.1) = 5.71°$$

**Step 4 — Centre Distance:**
Wheel pitch diameter:

$$\cos(\lambda) = \cos(5.71°) = 0.995$$

$$m_t = \frac{m_x}{\cos(\lambda)} = \frac{6}{0.995} = 6.03 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_w = m_t \cdot z_g = 6.03 \times 40 = 241.2 \text{ mm}$$

Centre distance:

$$a = 0.5 \times (d_w + D_w) = 0.5 \times (60 + 241.2) = 150.6 \text{ mm}$$

> **Final Answer:** $m_x = 6$ mm, $q = 10$, $\lambda = 5.71°$, $a = 150.6$ mm.

---

**Q3B.** **[Internal Choice — Alternative Question for 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the geometry of a worm and worm wheel pair. Define all the essential terms used in worm gear design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A worm gear reducer has the following specifications:
- Axial module, $m_x = 8$ mm
- Number of starts, $z_w = 2$
- Diametral quotient, $q = 12$
- Number of teeth on wheel, $z_g = 48$
- Worm speed, $N_w = 1440$ rpm

Calculate:
1. Centre distance
2. Velocity ratio
3. Worm wheel speed
4. Sliding velocity **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Refer to Section 4.4 and Section 2.2 tables for the answer.* The sketch should show the perpendicular shaft arrangement, throat region, face width, pitch diameters, and the unwrapped right triangle.

**(b) Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Worm Pitch Diameter:**

$$d_w = m_x \cdot q = 8 \times 12 = 96 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Lead Angle:**

$$\tan(\lambda) = \frac{z_w}{q} = \frac{2}{12} = 0.1667$$

$$\lambda = \tan^{-1}(0.1667) = 9.46°$$

**Step 3 — Wheel Pitch Diameter:**

$$\cos(9.46°) = 0.9863$$

$$m_t = \frac{8}{0.9863} = 8.11 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_w = 8.11 \times 48 = 389.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Centre Distance:**

$$a = 0.5 \times (96 + 389.4) = 242.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Velocity Ratio:**

$$i = \frac{z_g}{z_w} = \frac{48}{2} = 24$$

**Step 6 — Worm Wheel Speed:**

$$N_g = \frac{N_w}{i} = \frac{1440}{24} = 60 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 7 — Sliding Velocity:**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_w}{60 \cdot \cos(\lambda)}$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \times 0.096 \times 1440}{60 \times 0.9863}$$

$$V_s = \frac{434.07}{59.18} = 7.33 \text{ m/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** $a = 242.7$ mm, $i = 24$, $N_g = 60$ rpm, $V_s = 7.33$ m/s.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before Exam:**

- **Core identity:** $\tan(\lambda) = \dfrac{z_w}{q}$ — *the single most tested equation*.
- **Lead formula:** $L = z_w \cdot \pi \cdot m_x$ — *define before any numerical problem*.
- **Centre distance:** $a = 0.5 \cdot (d_w + D_w)$ — *always in mm*.
- **Sliding velocity:** $V_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot N_w}{60 \cdot \cos(\lambda)}$ — *watch the units: $d_w$ in metres, $N_w$ in rpm*.
- **Module identity:** $m_x \neq m_t$ except when $\lambda = 0$. Use $m_t = m_x / \cos(\lambda)$.
- **Self-locking condition:** $\lambda < 6°$ → worm cannot back-drive the wheel. Critical for lifts and steering.
- **Efficiency ordering:** Higher $\lambda$ → Higher efficiency (up to ~95% at $\lambda = 25°$ with proper lubrication).
- **Standard pressure angle:** $\alpha = 20°$ unless otherwise stated in the problem.
- **Typical diametral quotient range:** $q = 8$ to $16$ for power transmission; $q = 6$ to $8$ only for instrument worms.
- **Velocity ratio:** Always integer-friendly; $i = z_g / z_w$.
- **Rule of thumb:** For 90° shaft angle, $z_g \geq 6 \cdot z_w$ to avoid undercutting on the worm wheel.
- **Addendum / Dedendum:** Addendum = $1.0 \cdot m_x$, Dedendum = $1.25 \cdot m_x$ (full-depth system).
- **Standard centre distance preference:** Round $a$ to nearest 0 mm or 5 mm as per design data book.
- **Two competing standards to remember:** (1) AGMA — uses circular pitch on worm wheel, and (2) ISO/KSB — uses normal module. KTU predominantly follows the **ISO system** (use $m_n$ and $m_x$).
- **Examiners always check:** Units, conversion from rpm to rps, and correct angle units (degrees, not radians).

> **Final Tip:** When a question gives "worm speed in rpm" and asks for sliding velocity, the most common trap is to forget the $\cos(\lambda)$ in the denominator. Always draw the right triangle from the unwrapped cylinder — this geometric visual will *never* let you forget the formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strength of Worm Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strength of Worm Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Worm Gear** is a specialized gear pair consisting of a **worm** (a screw-like gear with one or more helical threads) meshing with a **worm wheel** (a standard helical gear resembling a spur gear). It is used to transmit motion and power between non-intersecting, non-parallel shafts, typically at right angles (90°). The **strength of a worm gear** refers to its load-carrying capacity against two primary failure modes:

1. **Bending Strength of Worm Wheel Teeth** — Resistance to tooth fracture at the root.
2. **Wear Strength (Surface Durability)** — Resistance to pitting and surface deterioration caused by sliding contact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Strength of worm gear is NOT evaluated only by static load; it requires analysis of **sliding velocity**, **dynamic effects**, and **thermal dissipation**, since worm gears operate under sliding (not rolling) contact.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **wood screw** (the worm) driving into a **notched wooden gear** (the worm wheel). Each turn of the screw advances the gear by one tooth. Because the contact is *sliding* rather than *rolling*, friction is high, generating heat. The "strength" we engineer is the gear wheel's ability to resist:
- The screw's tooth breaking the wheel's tooth (**bending**).
- The wheel's surface wearing out like a brake pad (**wear**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Automotive Application:** Worm gears are used in **steering gearboxes of cars (rack-and-pinion alternatives)**, **window lift mechanisms**, **seat adjusters**, and **power tailgates**. In each, the strength design ensures the gear lasts the vehicle's lifetime (~200,000 km).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Materials

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Lead angle | $\gamma$ | 4°–25° |
| Coefficient of friction | $\mu$ | **0.04 to 0.06** (with phosphor bronze) |
| Centre distance | $a$ | Standard series (mm) |
| Module | $m$ | 1 to 25 mm |
| Worm material | — | Hardened steel (HRC 58–62) |
| Wheel material | — | Phosphor Bronze (Grade 2) or Aluminium Bronze |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Failure Modes of Worm Gear

The two governing strength criteria are:

### A. **Bending Strength of Worm Wheel Teeth (Lewis Equation)**
The tangential load on the worm wheel tooth must not exceed the bending stress capacity.

### B. **Wear Strength (Buckingham's Equation)**
The sliding contact causes compressive surface stress; pitting must be avoided.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Compute Sliding Velocity** ($V_s$) — drives friction and heat.
2. **Compute Efficiency** ($\eta$) — determines power loss as heat.
3. **Compute Tangential Force on Worm** ($P_{t_1}$) — from power input.
4. **Compute Tangential Force on Worm Wheel** ($P_{t_2}$) — equal to axial force on worm.
5. **Apply Lewis Equation** to check bending of wheel teeth.
6. **Apply Buckingham's Wear Equation** to check surface durability.
7. **Apply Heat Dissipation Equation** to check thermal capacity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = \dfrac{V_1}{\cos \gamma} = \dfrac{\pi \, d_1 \, n_1}{60 \, \cos \gamma}$ | Sliding velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \gamma}{\tan(\gamma + \phi)}$ | Worm gear efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{t_1} = \dfrac{60 \, P}{2\pi \, n_1}$ | Tangential force on worm | N |
| 4 | $P_{t_2} = \dfrac{P_{t_1} \cdot \cos(\gamma + \phi)}{\cos \gamma}$ | Tangential force on wheel | N |
| 5 | $F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \, m \cdot y$ | **Lewis Bending Equation** | N |
| 6 | $F_w = K \cdot b \cdot d_1$ | **Buckingham Wear Equation** | N |
| 7 | $H = \dfrac{P \, (1-\eta)}{A_s}$ | Heat dissipation rate | W/m²·K |
| 8 | $\text{Power loss} = P \, (1-\eta)$ | Heat generated | W |
| 9 | $a = \dfrac{d_1 + d_2}{2}$ | Centre distance | mm |
| 10 | $L = \pi \, m \, z_1$ | Lead of worm | mm |

**Where:**
- $\sigma_b$ = allowable bending stress for wheel material (N/mm²)
- $b$ = face width of worm wheel (mm)
- $m$ = module (mm)
- $y$ = Lewis form factor (depends on tooth profile)
- $K$ = wear load-stress factor (from material tables)
- $d_1$ = pitch diameter of worm
- $P$ = power transmitted (W)
- $A_s$ = surface area for heat dissipation (m²)
- $z_1$ = number of threads (starts) on worm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Units:** When using Lewis equation, **forces in N**, **lengths in mm**, **stresses in N/mm²**. For Buckingham, K is tabulated in **N/mm²**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The strength analysis directly determines:
- **Gearbox life** in automotive steering systems.
- **Torque capacity** in window lift actuators.
- **Safety factor** in industrial lifting worm hoists.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Worm Gear Efficiency ($\eta$)

**Step 1 — Free Body Setup:**
Consider the worm thread as an inclined plane with lead angle $\gamma$. The worm wheel tooth experiences a tangential force $P_{t_2}$, an axial force equal to $P_{t_1}$, and a radial force $P_r$.

**Step 2 — Resolve Forces on the Worm Thread:**
The normal reaction $R_N$ acts perpendicular to the thread surface. The friction force $\mu R_N$ acts opposite to the direction of sliding.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Along Sliding Direction:**
The component of applied force along the sliding direction must overcome the friction component plus the useful tangential component.

$$
P_{t_1} \cdot \cos\gamma = P_{t_2} \cdot \cos\gamma + \mu \, R_N
$$

**Step 4 — Normal Reaction Calculation:**
From geometry, the normal to the inclined thread makes angle $(\gamma + \phi)$ with the horizontal. The normal reaction:

$$
R_N = \dfrac{P_{t_2} \cdot \cos\gamma}{\cos(\gamma + \phi)}
$$

Where $\phi$ is the friction angle: $\tan\phi = \mu$.

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{t_1} \cdot \cos\gamma &= P_{t_2} \cdot \cos\gamma + \mu \cdot \dfrac{P_{t_2} \cdot \cos\gamma}{\cos(\gamma + \phi)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing through by $\cos\gamma$:

$$
P_{t_1} = P_{t_2} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{\mu}{\cos(\gamma + \phi)} \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Express Efficiency:**
Efficiency = (Power Output) / (Power Input)

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{t_2} \cdot V_2}{P_{t_1} \cdot V_1} = \dfrac{P_{t_2} \cdot V_2}{P_{t_1} \cdot V_2 \cdot \cos\gamma / \cos\gamma}
$$

Since $\dfrac{P_{t_2}}{P_{t_1}} = \dfrac{\tan\gamma}{\tan(\gamma + \phi)}$, we obtain:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\tan\gamma}{\tan(\gamma + \phi)}
$$

> This is the **master efficiency equation** for worm gears.

## 3.2 Derivation of Buckingham's Wear Equation

**Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Concept:**
At the worm-wheel contact, the worm acts like an **indenter** pressing on a curved cylinder (the wheel tooth). Hertz's contact theory applies.

**Step 2 — Curvature Radius:**
The equivalent radius of curvature at the contact point involves the radius of the worm $r_1$ and the radius of the worm wheel tooth at the pitch point.

**Step 3 — Substitute Hertz Contact Stress:**

$$
\sigma_c^2 \propto \dfrac{F \cdot E}{b \cdot R_{\text{eq}}}
$$

Where $E$ is effective elastic modulus and $R_{\text{eq}}$ is equivalent radius.

**Step 4 — Empirical Simplification (Buckingham):**
Buckingham experimentally combined constants and curvature into a single load-stress factor $K$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K \cdot F_w}{b \cdot d_1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Equating to Allowable Stress:**

$$
\sigma_c \leq \sigma_c^{\text{allow}} = K
$$

This yields the **governing wear equation**:

$$
F_w = K \cdot b \cdot d_1
$$

**Where:**
- $K$ is obtained from empirical tables (N/mm²), dependent on materials and sliding velocity.
- For **phosphor bronze vs hardened steel**: $K$ ranges from **1.5 to 4.0 N/mm²**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Lewis (Bending) | Buckingham (Wear) |
|---|---|---|
| Failure mode | Tooth root fracture | Surface pitting |
| Critical stress | Bending | Hertzian contact |
| Governing variable | Module $m$ | Centre distance via $d_1$, $b$ |
| Typical controlling case | High-speed, lightly loaded | Slow-speed, heavily loaded |
| Material of importance | Worm wheel | Both (matched pair) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Worm Gear Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Power P and Speed n1] --> B[Calculate Sliding Velocity Vs]
    B --> C[Determine Lead Angle gamma]
    C --> D[Compute Efficiency eta]
    D --> E[Calculate Tangential Force Pt1 on Worm]
    E --> F[Calculate Tangential Force Pt2 on Wheel]
    F --> G{Strength Check}
    G --> H[Lewis Bending Equation]
    G --> I[Buckingham Wear Equation]
    G --> J[Heat Dissipation Check]
    H --> K{Design Safe?}
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K -->|Yes| L[Accept Design]
    K -->|No| M[Iterate: Increase b or Change Material]
    M --> C
```

## 4.2 Force Resolution on Worm Thread (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Applied Tangential Force Pt1] --> B[Resolve along sliding direction]
    B --> C[Useful Force Component]
    B --> D[Friction Force mu times Rn]
    C --> E[Output Tangential Force Pt2]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Output Torque on Worm Wheel]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Component Architecture

| Block | Function | Inputs | Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Module | Source | Vehicle engine / motor | $P$, $n_1$ |
| Worm Shaft | Drives rotation | Torque | Sliding motion |
| Worm Wheel | Receives motion | Sliding contact | $n_2$, torque |
| Bearing Housing | Supports shafts | Loads | Reactions |
| Heat Sink / Casing | Dissipates heat | Power loss $P(1-\eta)$ | Temperature rise |
| Lubricant Reservoir | Reduces friction | Splash oil | Cool film |

---
