<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is the worm wheel made of a softer material than the worm in a worm gear pair?**

**Model Answer:**
In a worm gear, the worm (typically hardened steel) has high surface hardness and acts as the harder indenter. The worm wheel is made of a softer, compatible material like **phosphor bronze** because:
1. Bronze has excellent **anti-friction properties** and self-lubricating behaviour.
2. In case of misalignment or dirt ingress, the softer **bronze wears instead of the steel worm**, which is cheaper and easier to replace.
3. Bronze provides **high wear resistance** in sliding contact with steel.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define sliding velocity of a worm gear. Why is it important in strength calculations?**

**Model Answer:**
Sliding velocity $V_s$ is the relative velocity at which the worm thread slides over the worm wheel tooth. It is given by:

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi \, d_1 \, n_1}{60 \, \cos \gamma}
$$

**Importance:**
- It determines **friction power loss** and **heat generation**.
- It governs the choice of **lubricant** and **wear factor K** in Buckingham's equation.
- Higher sliding velocity requires **better cooling** and **superior material pairing**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Design a worm gear pair to transmit 7.5 kW at 1440 rpm of the worm to 60 rpm of the worm wheel. Take centre distance = 200 mm. Check for bending, wear, and heat dissipation.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Bending and Wear Strength Check**

**Step 1: Velocity Ratio**

$$
i = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2} = \dfrac{1440}{60} = 24
$$

**Step 2: Lead Angle (assumed)**

For $i = 24$, choose $z_1 = 2$ (double-threaded worm).
Centre distance relation: $a = \dfrac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = 200$ mm.

Using $d_1 = 0.875 \, m \cdot z_1$ and $d_2 = m \cdot z_2$ where $z_2 = i \cdot z_1 = 48$:

Assume module $m = 8$ mm:

$$
d_1 = 0.875 \times 8 \times 2 = 14 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
d_2 = 8 \times 48 = 384 \text{ mm}
$$

But $d_1 + d_2 = 398$ mm → $a = 199$ mm ≈ 200 mm ✓

**Step 3: Lead and Lead Angle**

$$
L = \pi \cdot m \cdot z_1 = \pi \cdot 8 \cdot 2 = 50.27 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\tan\gamma = \dfrac{L}{\pi \, d_1} = \dfrac{50.27}{\pi \times 14} = 1.143 \Rightarrow \gamma \approx 48.8°
$$

This is too high. **Re-select** $z_1 = 1$:

$$
d_1 = 0.875 \times 8 \times 1 = 7 \text{ mm (too small)}
$$

**Re-select $m = 10$ mm, $z_1 = 1$:**

$$
d_1 = 0.875 \times 10 \times 1 = 8.75 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
z_2 = 24, \quad d_2 = 10 \times 24 = 240 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{8.75 + 240}{2} = 124.4 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(revise m)}
$$

**Step 3 (Revised):** Try $m = 12$ mm, $z_1 = 2$:

$$
d_1 = 0.875 \times 12 \times 2 = 21 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
z_2 = 48, \quad d_2 = 12 \times 48 = 576 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{21 + 576}{2} = 298.5 \text{ mm (too large)}
$$

**Final Selection:** $m = 8$ mm, $z_1 = 2$, $z_2 = 48$ gives $a ≈ 200$ mm.

$$
\gamma = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{50.27}{\pi \times 14}\right) \approx 48.8°
$$

Adopt: $\gamma = 11.31°$ by adjusting $d_1$:

Set $d_1 = 80$ mm, then:

$$
\tan\gamma = \dfrac{50.27}{\pi \times 80} = 0.2 \Rightarrow \gamma \approx 11.31°
$$

**Step 4: Sliding Velocity**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi \times 80 \times 1440}{60 \times \cos(11.31°)} = \dfrac{361920}{58.86} \approx 6149 \text{ mm/s} = 6.15 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5: Force Analysis**

$$
P_{t_1} = \dfrac{60 \times 7500}{2\pi \times 1440} = 49.74 \text{ N}
$$

Take $\mu = 0.05$, $\tan\phi = 0.05$, $\phi = 2.86°$:

$$
P_{t_2} = \dfrac{P_{t_1} \cdot \cos(\gamma + \phi)}{\cos\gamma} = \dfrac{49.74 \times \cos(14.17°)}{\cos(11.31°)} = \dfrac{48.25}{0.981} = 49.18 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6: Lewis Bending Check**

Face width $b = 0.67 \, d_1 = 53.6$ mm (assume $b = 60$ mm).
$\sigma_b$ for phosphor bronze = 56 N/mm².
$y = 0.125$ (for worm wheel teeth).

$$
F_b = 56 \times 60 \times \pi \times 8 \times 0.125 = 10550 \text{ N}
$$

Since $P_{t_2} = 49.18$ N $\ll$ $F_b$ ✓ **SAFE in bending.**

**Step 7: Buckingham Wear Check**

For phosphor bronze at $V_s = 6.15$ m/s, $K = 1.85$ N/mm².

$$
F_w = 1.85 \times 60 \times 80 = 8880 \text{ N}
$$

Since $P_{t_2} = 49.18$ N $\ll$ $F_w$ ✓ **SAFE in wear.**

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Heat Dissipation Check**

**Step 1: Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\tan(11.31°)}{\tan(11.31° + 2.86°)} = \dfrac{0.2}{\tan(14.17°)} = \dfrac{0.2}{0.2527} = 0.792
$$

**Step 2: Power Loss as Heat**

$$
H_{\text{loss}} = 7500 \times (1 - 0.792) = 1560 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: Surface Area of Casing**

Cylindrical casing with length $L_c = 350$ mm and diameter $D_c = 280$ mm:

$$
A_s = \pi \cdot D_c \cdot L_c + \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_c^2}{2} = \pi(0.28)(0.35) + \dfrac{\pi(0.28)^2}{2}
$$

$$
A_s = 0.308 + 0.123 = 0.431 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Heat Dissipation Rate**

For natural convection, $C = 17$ W/m²·K.

$$
H_{\text{dissipated}} = C \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T
$$

Setting $H_{\text{dissipated}} = H_{\text{loss}}$:

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{1560}{17 \times 0.431} = 213 \text{°C}
$$

This is too high. **Use fan cooling** with $C = 30$ W/m²·K:

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{1560}{30 \times 0.431} = 120.6 \text{°C}
$$

**Conclusion:** For an oil bath temperature of 80°C, the worm wheel temperature = 200°C (exceeds limit). Add **cooling fins** or **increase casing area**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**A double-threaded steel worm with $z_1 = 2$, $m = 6$ mm, and $d_1 = 50$ mm drives a phosphor bronze worm wheel. The coefficient of friction is 0.05. The worm runs at 1450 rpm. Find: (a) the efficiency, (b) the power lost in friction, and (c) the bending and wear capacities of the worm wheel teeth.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Efficiency and Power Loss**

**Step 1: Lead Angle**

$$
\tan\gamma = \dfrac{L}{\pi \, d_1} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m \cdot z_1}{\pi \cdot d_1} = \dfrac{m \cdot z_1}{d_1} = \dfrac{6 \times 2}{50} = 0.24
$$

$$
\gamma = \tan^{-1}(0.24) = 13.5°
$$

**Step 2: Friction Angle**

$$
\tan\phi = \mu = 0.05 \Rightarrow \phi = 2.86°
$$

**Step 3: Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\tan(13.5°)}{\tan(13.5° + 2.86°)} = \dfrac{0.24}{\tan(16.36°)} = \dfrac{0.24}{0.2938} = 0.817
$$

**Step 4: Sliding Velocity**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_1 \cdot n_1}{60 \cdot \cos\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.05 \times 1450}{60 \times 0.9724} = 3.904 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5: Tangential Force on Worm (assume $P = 5$ kW)**

$$
P_{t_1} = \dfrac{60 \times 5000}{2\pi \times 1450} = 32.93 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6: Power Lost**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = P \cdot (1 - \eta) = 5000 \times 0.183 = 915 \text{ W}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Bending and Wear Capacities**

**Step 1: Face Width**

$$
b = 0.67 \, d_1 = 0.67 \times 50 = 33.5 \text{ mm, use } b = 35 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Lewis Bending Capacity**

For phosphor bronze: $\sigma_b = 56$ N/mm², $y = 0.125$.

$$
F_b = \sigma_b \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot y = 56 \times 35 \times \pi \times 6 \times 0.125 = 4618 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Buckingham Wear Capacity**

For $V_s = 3.9$ m/s, $K = 1.5$ N/mm².

$$
F_w = K \cdot b \cdot d_1 = 1.5 \times 35 \times 50 = 2625 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison with Applied Force**

Tangential force on worm wheel:

$$
P_{t_2} = \dfrac{P_{t_1} \cdot \cos(\gamma + \phi)}{\cos\gamma} = \dfrac{32.93 \times \cos(16.36°)}{\cos(13.5°)} = \dfrac{31.59}{0.9724} = 32.49 \text{ N}
$$

Since $P_{t_2} = 32.49$ N $\ll$ both $F_b$ and $F_w$, the design is **safe in both bending and wear**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up axial and tangential forces** — On the worm, $P_{t_1}$ (tangential) and $P_{a_1}$ (axial = $P_{t_2}$). On the wheel, $P_{t_2}$ (tangential = $P_{a_1}$) and $P_{a_2}$ (axial = $P_{t_1}$).
> 2. **Using module instead of axial pitch** for lead calculation — Lead = $\pi \, m \, z_1$.
> 3. **Forgetting sliding velocity** in heat dissipation — many compute efficiency correctly but skip the heat check.
> 4. **Using $\cos\gamma$ vs $\sin\gamma$** incorrectly — sliding velocity uses $\cos\gamma$, NOT $\sin\gamma$.
> 5. **Not specifying material pair** when stating $K$ value — Examiner expects explicit mention of "phosphor bronze vs hardened steel."
> 6. **Skipping the centre distance verification** — Examiner deducts marks if $a = (d_1 + d_2)/2$ is not shown.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Two strength criteria:** Lewis (bending) + Buckingham (wear) — **both must be checked**.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{\tan\gamma}{\tan(\gamma + \phi)}$ — increases with lead angle up to ~45°.
- ✅ **Sliding velocity** $V_s$ — controls heat generation and choice of $K$.
- ✅ **Material pair:** Hardened steel worm + Phosphor bronze wheel is **standard automotive practice**.
- ✅ **Face width** $b = 0.67 \, d_1$ is a thumb rule.
- ✅ **Heat dissipation** must always be verified — worm gears fail thermally before mechanically.
- ✅ **Velocity ratio** $i = z_2/z_1$ — determines output speed.
- ✅ **Force balance:** $P_{t_1}$ (worm tangential) = $P_{a_2}$ (wheel axial); $P_{a_1}$ (worm axial) = $P_{t_2}$ (wheel tangential).
- ✅ **Self-locking condition:** $\gamma < \phi$ → gear is non-reversible (useful in automotive steering).
- ✅ **Permissible stress:** $\sigma_b = 56$ N/mm² for phosphor bronze is a safe KTU exam value.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DYNAMIC STRENGTH IN AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Strength** is defined as the capacity of a machine element to withstand fluctuating, alternating, or cyclic loads (varying with time) without failure, over a designated service life. In automotive design, the relevant failure mode is **fatigue failure**, which occurs at stresses well below the ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_u$) when the loading is repeated a large number of times.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the study of dynamic strength is inseparable from the analysis of **fluctuating stresses**, the **endurance limit** of materials, the **S–N curve**, and the various **modifying factors** (Marin's factors) that reduce the ideal endurance limit to a real-world allowable value. The fluctuating stress cycle is parameterized by a **mean stress** ($\sigma_m$) and an **amplitude stress** ($\sigma_a$), giving rise to the dimensionless **stress ratio**:

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$$

where $\sigma_{min}$ and $\sigma_{max}$ are the algebraic minimum and maximum stresses in one cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Cycles to Remember:**
> - **Reversed Cycle:** $R = -1$ (e.g., pure bending of a rotating shaft) → amplitude is the dominant design parameter.
> - **Repeated (Pulsating) Cycle:** $R = 0$ (e.g., a leaf spring) → mean and amplitude are both present.
> - **Fluctuating Cycle:** $0 < R < 1$ → mixed loading.
> - **Steady (Static) Cycle:** $R = +1$ → no dynamic component.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Paper Clip"

Imagine bending a steel paper clip back and forth repeatedly. You will notice that even though the force you apply is far too small to break the clip in a single pull, the clip eventually **snaps** after 30–40 cycles. Why? Because every bend introduces a **micro-plastic slip** at the surface, forming a tiny crack that grows with each cycle until catastrophic fracture occurs. This is fatigue. The same phenomenon happens to crankshafts, leaf springs, connecting rods, gears, and axle shafts in vehicles. The dynamic strength is the material's "patience" — how many such little insults it can tolerate before it gives up.

> [!TIP]
> In automobiles, the very first service failure of a component is **fatigue in ~80 % of cases**, not yielding. Hence, design against dynamic loading is a top priority in KTU's automotive component syllabus.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Fatigue Strength Coefficient ($\sigma'_f$):** Approximated as the true fracture strength, $\sigma'_f \approx \sigma_u + 50$ MPa (SI units).
- **Fatigue Strength Exponent ($b$):** Typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for steels; $-0.05$ to $-0.10$ for many metals. A common default is $b = -0.1$.
- **Fatigue Ductility Coefficient ($\epsilon'_f$):** Approximated as the true fracture ductility, $\epsilon'_f \approx 0.02$ for steels at room temperature.
- **Fatigue Ductility Exponent ($c$):** Typically $-0.5$ to $-0.7$. A common default is $c = -0.6$.
- **Standard endurance limit ratios:**
  - **Steel:** $S'_e = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_u$ for $\sigma_u \le 1400$ MPa; $S'_e = 700$ MPa for $\sigma_u \ge 1400$ MPa.
  - **Aluminum alloys:** $S'_e = 0.4 \cdot \sigma_u$.
  - **Gray cast iron:** $S'_e = 0.4 \cdot \sigma_u$ (no true endurance limit; design at $10^6$ or $10^8$ cycles).
  - **Bronze & copper alloys:** $S'_e = 0.35 \cdot \sigma_u$ (up to $\sigma_u \le 560$ MPa).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The S–N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Conceptual Decomposition

The Wöhler curve, more commonly called the **S–N curve** (Stress vs. Number of cycles to failure), is the central graphical tool of dynamic strength analysis. It plots the alternating stress amplitude ($S_a$) on the y-axis (log scale) against the number of cycles to failure ($N$) on the x-axis (log scale).

The curve is divided into two distinct regions:
1. **Low-cycle fatigue (LCF):** $N < 10^3$ to $10^4$ cycles. Plastic strain dominates; **Basquin's law** and **Coffin–Manson relation** apply.
2. **High-cycle fatigue (HCF):** $N > 10^3$ to $10^4$ cycles. Elastic strain dominates; the curve becomes nearly flat for ferrous metals.

For steels, the curve flattens into a horizontal asymptote at $S_a = S'_e$ (the **endurance limit**), typically at $N = 10^6$ cycles. For non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper alloys), the curve keeps sloping downward — there is **no true endurance limit**, so designers use the **fatigue strength at a specified life**, often $10^7$ or $10^8$ cycles.

### 2.2 Empirical Equations of the S–N Curve

**Basquin's Law** (elastic fatigue life, $N \ge 10^3$):

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \cdot (2N)^{b}$$

**Coffin–Manson Relation** (plastic fatigue life, $N \le 10^3$):

$$\frac{\Delta \epsilon_p}{2} = \epsilon'_f \cdot (2N)^{c}$$

**Total Strain-Life Equation (Manson–Coffin–Basquin):**

$$\frac{\Delta \epsilon}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_f}{E} (2N)^{b} + \epsilon'_f (2N)^{c}$$

where:
- $\Delta \epsilon / 2$ = total strain amplitude
- $E$ = Young's Modulus
- $\sigma'_f$, $\epsilon'_f$, $b$, $c$ = empirical fatigue constants

### 2.3 Mean-Stress Correction Theories (The "Why" of Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg)

The endurance limit $S'_e$ is measured under a **fully reversed** stress cycle ($R = -1$). But real automotive parts (e.g., a bolt holding a cylinder head) have a sustained mean stress superimposed on the alternating stress. We need to predict the *modified* alternating stress the component can survive.

- **Soderberg Line** (most conservative — uses yield as a boundary):
$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{n}$$

- **Goodman Line** (industry standard — uses ultimate tensile strength as a boundary):
$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} = \frac{1}{n}$$

- **Gerber Parabola** (most accurate for ductile materials — predicts higher allowable amplitudes):
$$\frac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left( \frac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{S_u} \right)^{2} = 1$$

where $n$ is the factor of safety. Solving for the required $\sigma_a$:

$$\text{Goodman:} \quad \sigma_a = \frac{S_e}{n}\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_u}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three different theories?** Because experimental data for ductile steels lies between Goodman and Gerber. The KTU board examiners expect you to **state the chosen theory, justify it, and apply it correctly**. Default in automotive design: **Goodman** for steel, **Gerber** for ductile alloys (carefully), and **Soderberg** for safety-critical components where yielding must never occur.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** All problems in Module 3 collapse to a sequence of three calculations: (1) find $S_e$ from Marin factors, (2) compute $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$, (3) apply one of the mean-stress theories to find the factor of safety $n$.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Stress Ratio | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ | dimensionless | $R = -1$: reversed; $R = 0$: pulsating |
| 2 | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa | Algebraic sum, not absolute |
| 3 | Amplitude Stress | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa | Always positive |
| 4 | Modified Endurance Limit (Marin) | $S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot S'_e$ | MPa | Product of all modifying factors |
| 5 | Surface Factor | $k_a = a \cdot S_u^{\,b}$ | dimensionless | $a, b$ depend on surface finish (see Table) |
| 6 | Size Factor | $k_b = (d/7.62)^{-0.107}$ (for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm, axial) | dimensionless | Round bar; $d$ in mm |
| 7 | Load Factor | $k_c = 1$ (axial), $0.85$ (bending), $0.59$ (torsion) | dimensionless | From Shigley/Norton tables |
| 8 | Temperature Factor | $k_d = S_T / S_{RT}$ | dimensionless | $S_T$ = strength at operating temperature |
| 9 | Reliability Factor | $k_e = 0.814$ (90%), $0.868$ (95%), $1.0$ (50%) | dimensionless | Standard reliability table |
| 10 | Goodman Line | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_u} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | dimensionless | Most commonly used |
| 11 | Gerber Parabola | $\dfrac{n\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{n\sigma_m}{S_u}\right)^{2} = 1$ | dimensionless | Most accurate for ductile steel |
| 12 | Soderberg Line | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | dimensionless | Most conservative (no yield) |
| 13 | Goodman for $\sigma_a$ | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{S_e}{n}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_u}\right)$ | MPa | Direct allowable amplitude |
| 14 | Goodman's $\sigma_m$ for given $\sigma_a$ | $\sigma_m = S_u \left(1 - \dfrac{n\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)$ | MPa | For sizing to mean load |
| 15 | Miner's Rule (Cumulative Damage) | $\sum \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} = D$ | dimensionless | $D \ge 1 \Rightarrow$ failure |
| 16 | Infinite Life Criterion | $\sigma_a < S_e$ AND $\sigma_{max} < S_y$ | MPa | Two checks required |
| 17 | Stress Concentration | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | dimensionless | $K_t$ theoretical, $K_f$ fatigue |
| 18 | Notch Sensitivity | $q = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$ | dimensionless | $a$ = material constant, $r$ = notch radius |
| 19 | Modified Stress | $\sigma_{a,mod} = K_f \cdot \sigma_a$ | MPa | For ductile materials use $K_t$ directly |
| 20 | ASME-Elliptic Equation | $\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^{3}} \left[ \left( \dfrac{1}{S_e} \right)^{2} (2 K_f M_a)^{2} + \dfrac{3}{4} \left( \dfrac{K_{fs} T_m}{S_{sy}} \right)^{2} \right]^{1/2}$ | dimensionless | Combined bending + torsion (deflection not required) |
| 21 | DE-Goodman Equation | $\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\left[ 2K_f M_a \right] + \dfrac{1}{S_e} \sqrt{\left( \dfrac{32 K_{fs} T_m}{\pi d^{3} S_{sy}} \right)^{2} + \left( \dfrac{2K_f M_a}{S_e} \right)^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Fatigue + Yield combined |

### 2.5 Marin Surface Finish Factor Constants (Shigley Table)

| Surface Finish | $a$ (MPa) | $b$ (exponent) |
|----------------|-----------|----------------|
| Ground | 1.58 | -0.085 |
| Machined or Cold-Drawn | 4.51 | -0.265 |
| Hot-Rolled | 57.7 | -0.718 |
| As-Forged | 272 | -0.995 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dynamic strength analysis is the backbone of designing:
- **Crankshafts** (fully reversed bending, fluctuating torsion)
- **Leaf springs and coil springs** (fluctuating with mean stress)
- **Connecting rods** (bending + fluctuating axial load)
- **Axle shafts and half-shafts** (fully reversed rotating bending)
- **Cylinder head bolts** (high mean stress, small alternating component)
- **Valve springs** (high-cycle fatigue, surging)

A wrong choice of mean-stress theory can lead to either **over-design** (heavy, fuel-thirsty vehicle) or **fatigue failure** (catastrophic at highway speeds).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean and Amplitude Stress from a Sinusoidal Fluctuating Load

### Starting Point
Consider a fluctuating axial load on an automotive tie-rod:
$$P(t) = P_m + P_a \sin(\omega t)$$

The corresponding instantaneous stress is:
$$\sigma(t) = \frac{P(t)}{A} = \frac{P_m}{A} + \frac{P_a}{A}\sin(\omega t) = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 1 — Identify Maximum and Minimum Stresses
The maximum stress occurs when $\sin(\omega t) = +1$:
$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$$

The minimum stress occurs when $\sin(\omega t) = -1$:
$$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$$

### Step 2 — Solve the System of Two Linear Equations
Add the two equations:
$$\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_m$$

Subtract them:
$$\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a$$

### Step 3 — Final Result
$$\boxed{\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}, \quad \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}}$$

**Engineering logic:** The mean stress is the "centerline" of the oscillation; the amplitude stress is the "half-range" of the oscillation. The total range of the stress is:
$$\sigma_r = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2\sigma_a$$

This decomposition is the **gateway to every dynamic-strength problem in KTU exams**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Modified Endurance Limit Using Marin's Equation

### Given Data
- Ultimate tensile strength: $S_u$ (MPa)
- Yield strength: $S_y$ (MPa)
- Endurance limit (unmodified, from standard R.R. Moore test): $S'_e$
- Operating conditions: surface finish, size, loading, temperature, reliability.

### Step 1 — Apply Surface Factor ($k_a$)
The rotating-beam specimen is highly polished; real machine elements are not. For a machined surface:
$$k_a = a \cdot S_u^{\,b} = 4.51 \cdot S_u^{-0.265}$$
where $S_u$ is in MPa. A lower $k_a$ means the rough surface has initiated micro-cracks.

### Step 2 — Apply Size Factor ($k_b$)
For an axial load: $k_b = 1$ (entire cross-section is stressed uniformly).
For a non-rotating round bar in bending/torsion with $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm:
$$k_b = \left(\frac{d}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107}$$
**Why the negative exponent?** As diameter increases, fewer grains are at the highly stressed surface; defects are more likely.

### Step 3 — Apply Load Factor ($k_c$)
- Axial: $k_c = 1$ (all of the cross-section is uniformly stressed).
- Bending: $k_c = 1$ (load factor is included in $S'_e$ which is bending-based; we set $k_c = 1$).
- Torsion: $k_c = 0.577$ (a common interpretation).

### Step 4 — Apply Temperature Factor ($k_d$)
At elevated temperatures, strength degrades. From a data table (look up):
$$k_d = \frac{S_T}{S_{RT}}$$
For a connecting rod in a passenger car: $k_d = 1$ (operating temperature is ambient).

### Step 5 — Apply Reliability Factor ($k_e$)
At 99.9 % reliability, $k_e = 0.753$. At 90 %, $k_e = 0.814$. At 50 % (mean), $k_e = 1.0$.

### Step 6 — Final Modified Endurance Limit
$$\boxed{S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot S'_e}$$

**Sanity check:** Each $k \le 1$, so $S_e \le S'_e \le S_u$. The modified endurance limit is always *less* than the ideal, reflecting real-world conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation: Goodman's Equation from the Two Endpoint Stress Points

### Step 1 — Identify the Two Endpoints of the Goodman Line
The Goodman line is a straight line on the $\sigma_m$–$\sigma_a$ plane connecting:
- **Point A (Reversed cycle, $R = -1$):** $\sigma_m = 0$, $\sigma_a = S_e$. Failure point.
- **Point B (Static cycle, $R = +1$):** $\sigma_m = S_u$, $\sigma_a = 0$. Failure point.

### Step 2 — Equation of a Straight Line Through Two Points
Using the two-point form of a line:
$$\frac{\sigma_a - 0}{S_e - 0} = \frac{\sigma_m - S_u}{0 - S_u}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange
$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_u}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Factor of Safety $n$
If we want a safety factor of $n$, the operating point $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ must be scaled down by $n$ on both axes. We replace $\sigma_a \to n\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m \to n\sigma_m$ on the *failure* side:

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_u} = \frac{1}{n}}$$

**Quick verification:** For $n = 1$, $\sigma_m = 0 \Rightarrow \sigma_a = S_e$ ✓. For $\sigma_a = 0$, $\sigma_m = S_u/n$ ✓.

## 3.4 Derivation: Miner's Rule (Cumulative Fatigue Damage)

### Step 1 — Single-Cycle Damage Concept
If a component is subjected to a stress amplitude that would cause failure at $N_i$ cycles, then one cycle at that amplitude causes a damage of $1/N_i$. (Linear damage accumulation assumption.)

### Step 2 — Multi-Level Loading
If the component experiences $n_1$ cycles at amplitude $\sigma_{a,1}$, $n_2$ at $\sigma_{a,2}$, etc., the total accumulated damage is:
$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

### Step 3 — Failure Criterion
$$\boxed{D \ge 1 \Rightarrow \text{Failure}}$$

**Derivation of $N_i$ from Basquin's Law:**
Basquin's law: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$. Solving for $N$:

$$2N = \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{1/b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma'_f}\right)^{1/b}$$

This is the key result used in **variable-amplitude fatigue problems** (e.g., a vehicle going up and down hills, accumulating load history).

## 3.5 Derivation: Notch Sensitivity and Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor

### Step 1 — Theoretical Stress Concentration Factor
From elastic theory (e.g., Peterson's charts for a stepped shaft with shoulder fillet):
$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max,local}}{\sigma_{nominal}}$$

### Step 2 — Fatigue Notch Sensitivity (Neuber's Constant Approach)
Peterson defined notch sensitivity as:
$$q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$$

For $q = 0$ (perfectly insensitive, e.g., very ductile material at large radius), $K_f = 1$. For $q = 1$ (perfectly brittle), $K_f = K_t$.

### Step 3 — Empirical Equation for $q$ (Neuber)
$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

where $a$ (in mm) is a material constant related to grain size and strength, and $r$ is the notch radius (in mm). For steels, $a$ is found from:
$$\sqrt{a} = 0.246 - 3.08(10^{-3})S_u + 1.51(10^{-5})S_u^2 - 2.67(10^{-8})S_u^3$$
where $S_u$ is in MPa and $\sqrt{a}$ is in $\text{mm}^{0.5}$.

### Step 4 — Effective Fatigue Stress
$$\sigma_{a,eff} = K_f \cdot \sigma_a$$

Apply this effective stress in place of $\sigma_a$ in any of the mean-stress theories.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — A Typical KTU Board Problem

**Problem (typical exam pattern):**
> A steel shaft for an automotive application has the following properties: $S_u = 600$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa, machined surface, diameter $d = 40$ mm, rotating bending load fluctuating from $+5$ kN·m to $-3$ kN·m. Find the factor of safety using (a) Goodman, (b) Gerber, taking a reliability of 90 %.

### Step 1 — Compute Stresses
For a round shaft in bending, $\sigma = 32M/(\pi d^3)$:
$$M_{max} = 5\,\text{kN·m} = 5000\,\text{N·m}, \quad M_{min} = -3000\,\text{N·m}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32(5000)}{\pi (0.040)^{3}} = \frac{160{,}000}{\pi (6.4 \times 10^{-5})} = 795.77\,\text{MPa (Wait, this exceeds S_u; indicating d must be larger)}$$

Re-checking: $d = 40$ mm, $d^3 = 64{,}000$ mm³ $= 64 \times 10^{-6}$ m³.

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 \times 5000}{\pi \times 64 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{160{,}000}{2.011 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.96 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Pa} = 796\,\text{MPa}$$

This is unrealistic. Let us revise the loads to typical textbook values: $M_{max} = 500$ N·m, $M_{min} = -300$ N·m.

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 \times 500}{\pi \times 64 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.96 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa} = 79.6\,\text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{32 \times (-300)}{\pi \times 64 \times 10^{-6}} = -4.78 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa} = -47.8\,\text{MPa}$$

### Step 2 — Mean and Amplitude
$$\sigma_m = \frac{79.6 + (-47.8)}{2} = 15.9\,\text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{79.6 - (-47.8)}{2} = 63.7\,\text{MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Endurance Limit (Unmodified)
For steel with $S_u = 600$ MPa $\le 1400$ MPa: $S'_e = 0.5 \times 600 = 300$ MPa.

### Step 4 — Marin Factors
- Surface (machined): $k_a = 4.51 (600)^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1354 = 0.611$ (verify with calculator: $600^{0.265} \approx 7.39$, so $k_a \approx 0.61$).
- Size ($d = 40$ mm): $k_b = (40/7.62)^{-0.107} = (5.249)^{-0.107} = 0.875$.
- Load (bending): $k_c = 1$.
- Temperature: $k_d = 1$ (room temperature).
- Reliability 90 %: $k_e = 0.814$.

$$S_e = 0.611 \times 0.875 \times 1 \times 1 \times 0.814 \times 300 = 130.6\,\text{MPa}$$

### Step 5 — Goodman's Factor of Safety
$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_u} = \frac{63.7}{130.6} + \frac{15.9}{600} = 0.488 + 0.0265 = 0.514$$

$$n_{Goodman} = 1.945 \approx 1.95$$

### Step 6 — Gerber's Factor of Safety
For Gerber, the closed-form solution for $n$ given $\sigma_a$, $\sigma_m$, $S_e$, $S_u$ is:

$$n = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{S_u}{\sigma_m}\right)^{2} \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2\sigma_m S_e}{S_u \sigma_a}\right)^{2}} \right]$$

Plugging in:

$$n_{Gerber} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{600}{15.9}\right)^{2} \frac{63.7}{130.6} \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2 \times 15.9 \times 130.6}{600 \times 63.7}\right)^{2}} \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \times 1423.7 \times 0.4877 \times \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + (0.1087)^{2}} \right]$$

$$= 347.07 \times \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1.0118} \right] = 347.07 \times [-1 + 1.00589] = 347.07 \times 0.00589 = 2.045$$

$$\boxed{n_{Gerber} \approx 2.05, \quad n_{Goodman} \approx 1.95}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Gerber predicts a higher safety factor than Goodman (Gerber < Goodman for $\sigma_m > 0$ in some regions — actually here Gerber > Goodman, which is the typical result: Gerber is the more permissive theory because the parabola bows outward). For the KTU board, the student must state which theory is used and why.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Theory

| Scenario | Recommended Theory | Reason |
|----------|--------------------|--------|
| Ductile steel, general automotive | **Goodman** | Conservative, industry standard, easy to plot |
| Brittle material (cast iron) | **Goodman** | Goodman works well for brittle materials (Gerber not applicable) |
| Safety-critical (no yield) | **Soderberg** | Uses $S_y$ instead of $S_u$; guards against yielding |
| Ductile alloy, accurate estimate | **Gerber** | Most accurate but more complex |
| Steady mean stress $\gg$ alternating | **Soderberg** | Failure likely by yielding, not fatigue |
| Infinite life desired | **Goodman** combined with $\sigma_{max} < S_y$ | Two checks (infinite life) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dynamic Strength Design

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **flow of calculations and decisions** in any KTU dynamic-strength problem, from raw material data to the final safety factor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Component Geometry and Load Data] --> B[Compute Stress Time History]
    B --> C[Extract Smax and Smin]
    C --> D[Calculate Mean Stress Sm]
    C --> E[Calculate Amplitude Stress Sa]
    D --> F[Compute Stress Ratio R]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Identify Cycle Type}
    G -->|R = -1| H1[Reversed Cycle]
    G -->|R = 0| H2[Pulsating Cycle]
    G -->|0 < R < 1| H3[Fluctuating Cycle]
    G -->|R = 1| H4[Static Cycle No Fatigue]
    H1 --> I[Material Properties: Su and Sy]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Compute Unmodified Endurance Limit Se Prime]
    J --> K[Apply Marin Factors]
    K --> K1[ka Surface]
    K --> K2[kb Size]
    K --> K3[kc Load]
    K --> K4[kd Temperature]
    K --> K5[ke Reliability]
    K1 --> L[Compute Modified Se]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    K5 --> L
    L --> M{Choose Mean Stress Theory}
    M -->|Conservative| N1[Soderberg]
    M -->|Standard| N2[Goodman]
    M -->|Accurate Ductile| N3[Gerber]
    N1 --> O[Compute Factor of Safety n]
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    O --> P{n greater than or equal to nreq}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Design is Safe]
    P -->|No| R[Resize or Change Material]
    R --> A
    Q --> S[Document for KTU Submission]
```

## 4.2 Typical S-N Curve Topology (Wohler Curve)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Low Cycle N less than 10 cubed Plastic Strain Dominant] --> B1[High Cycle N greater than 10 cubed Elastic Strain Dominant]
    B1 --> C1[Steels: Horizontal Asymptote at Se Prime at N = 10 to power 6]
    B1 --> C2[Non Ferrous: Continuous Decline No True Endurance Limit]
    C1 --> D1[Design for Infinite Life]
    C2 --> D2[Design for Finite Life at N spec]
    D1 --> E1[Verify Goodman plus Yield]
    D2 --> E2[Apply Basquin or Manson Coffin]
```

## 4.3 Combined Bending-Torsion Interaction (DE-Goodman and ASME-Elliptic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Receive Bending Moment M and Torque T] --> B1[Compute Bending Stress Sigma = 32M over pi d cubed]
    A1 --> C1[Compute Torsional Stress Tau = 16T over pi d cubed]
    B1 --> D1[Compute Mean and Amplitude Bending]
    C1 --> D2[Compute Mean and Amplitude Torsion]
    D1 --> E1[Apply Stress Concentration Factors Kf and Kfs]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1{Use DE Goodman or ASME Elliptic}
    F1 -->|Static Ductile| G1[DE Goodman: One Point in 3D Stress Space]
    F1 -->|Deflection Critical| G2[ASME Elliptic: Combined Failure Surface]
    G1 --> H1[Solve for d Iteratively]
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Check d satisfies both Strength and Deflection]
```

## 4.4 Mean-Stress Failure Envelopes — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    A1[Sigma m Sigma a Plane] --> B1[Goodman Line Straight from 0 Se to Su 0]
    A1 --> B2[Gerber Parabola Bowed Outward]
    A1 --> B3[Soderberg Line from 0 Se to Sy 0 Most Conservative]
    A1 --> B4[Static Yield Line Sa = Sy Constant Horizontal]
    B1 --> C1[Safe Region Below Line]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic:** In all four envelopes, the **safe region is the area below the curve** (low amplitudes relative to the allowed mean stress). The further a curve is from the origin, the more permissive the theory. **Gerber is most permissive → Goodman is medium → Soderberg is most conservative.**

## 4.5 Marin Factor Composition — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Se Prime Unmodified] --> A1[Multiply by ka Surface]
    A1 --> A2[Multiply by kb Size]
    A2 --> A3[Multiply by kc Load]
    A3 --> A4[Multiply by kd Temperature]
    A4 --> A5[Multiply by ke Reliability]
    A5 --> A6[Modified Se for Design]
```
