> The crankshaft is therefore a **simultaneously bent AND twisted shaft**, and that is exactly why its design is governed by an *equivalent torque* combining both effects.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Crankshaft Design:**
> • **Bearing pressure (p_b) for steel-on-babbitt:** **10 – 25 MPa**
> • **Bearing pressure (p_b) for steel-on-bronze:** **25 – 40 MPa**
> • **Allowable bending stress (σ_b) for forged steel:** **80 – 120 MPa**
> • **Allowable shear stress (τ) for forged steel crank pin/web:** **60 – 90 MPa**
> • **Crushing stress limit (σ_c) on web:** **60 – 80 MPa**
> • **Standard Factor of Safety (FOS) for fatigue loading:** **2.5 – 3.0**
> • **Connecting rod length ratio r/l:** typically **1/3 to 1/4** (often l = 4r in KTU problems)

### 1.5 Materials of Construction (KTU 2024 — Must Know for Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Crankshaft Materials:**
> 1. **Carbon Steel (0.35 – 0.45% C)** — e.g., 30C8, 40C8 — cheap, used in small engines.
> 2. **Alloy Steels** — e.g., **40Ni2Cr1Mo28** (Nitralloy), 35CrMo, 40Cr — for high-performance engines.
> 3. **Nodular / Ductile Cast Iron (SG Iron)** — increasingly used; superior damping.
> 4. **Forged Steel** — best fatigue strength; used in heavy-duty diesel and racing engines.
> 5. **Surface Treatments** — induction hardening, nitriding, shot peening to improve fatigue life.

**Selection Rule (KTU Board Tip):** *For high-speed petrol engines → alloy forged steel. For low-speed diesel tractors → nodular cast iron is cost-effective.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Forces Acting on the Crankshaft — Step-by-Step Logic

The piston is acted upon by **two primary forces** during the power stroke:

#### Step 1 — Gas Force (F_g)

The pressure of combustion gases acts on the piston crown.

$$F_g = P_g \times A_p$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Where:**
- $P_g$ = Peak (maximum) cylinder gas pressure in **N/mm² (MPa)**
- $D$ = Cylinder bore in **mm**
- $A_p$ = Piston cross-sectional area in **mm²**

#### Step 2 — Inertia Force of Reciprocating Parts (F_i)

By Newton's second law for a piston undergoing approximate simple harmonic motion:

$$F_i = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

**Where:**
- $m$ = mass of reciprocating parts (piston + piston pin + portion of connecting rod) in **kg**
- $r$ = crank radius in **m** (= L/2)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity in **rad/s** = $2\pi N / 60$
- $\theta$ = crank angle from **Inner Dead Center (IDC)** in **radians**
- $l$ = length of connecting rod in **m**

#### Step 3 — Net Effective Force on the Piston (F)

$$F = F_g - F_i \quad \text{(during power stroke, downward)}$$

$$F = -F_i \quad \text{(during other strokes, gas pressure ≈ 0)}$$

#### Step 4 — Tangential Force on the Crank Pin (F_T)

The force acting along the cylinder axis is resolved into a tangential component (which produces useful torque) and a radial (side-thrust) component.

$$F_T = F \cdot \frac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

**Where** $\phi$ is the angle of the connecting rod with the cylinder axis, given by:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta$$

For KTU problems, a **simplified yet accurate** form is:

$$F_T = F \cdot \left[\sin\theta + \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta\cos\theta\right] \Big/ \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{r}{l}\sin\theta\right)^{2}}$$

The **maximum tangential force** is found by setting $dF_T/d\theta = 0$. For $r/l = 0.25$, $F_{T,\max}$ occurs near $\theta \approx 75°$ and equals approximately **1.05 × F_g**.

#### Step 5 — Resultant Reaction on the Journal (W)

The journal carries the **vector sum** of the tangential and radial piston forces:

$$W = \sqrt{F_T^{2} + F_R^{2}}$$

where the **radial force** is $F_R = F \cdot \cos\theta$ (approximate).

### 2.2 The Crank Pin and the Web — Free-Body Picture

The **crank pin** is supported at its two ends by the crank webs and loaded at its centre by the connecting rod. It is therefore a **simply supported beam** with a central concentrated load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crank pin carries **two** internal load effects:
> 1. **Torsion (T)** — equal to $F_T \times r$ (the useful torque transmitted).
> 2. **Bending Moment (M_b)** — equal to $F_T \times \dfrac{l_p}{4}$ where $l_p$ is the crank pin length.
>
> Therefore we must compute an **Equivalent Twisting Moment** $T_e$.

### 2.3 The Equivalent Twisting Moment (T_e)

According to the **maximum shear stress theory** (used in KTU 2024):

$$T_e = \sqrt{T^{2} + M_b^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most important equation in crankshaft design. It reduces a *combined bending + torsion* problem into a pure-torsion problem, so we can use the simple shaft torsion formula $\tau_{max} = 16 T_e / (\pi d^3)$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Focused)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Crankshaft Design Formula Cheat Sheet**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI / mm) | Notes |
|:--:|---|:--:|:--:|---|
| 1 | Crank radius | $r = L/2$ | mm | L = stroke length |
| 2 | Angular velocity | $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ | rad/s | N = engine speed in rpm |
| 3 | Piston area | $A_p = \pi D^{2}/4$ | mm² | D = cylinder bore |
| 4 | Gas force | $F_g = P_g \cdot A_p$ | N | $P_g$ in MPa (= N/mm²) |
| 5 | Inertia force | $F_i = m r \omega^{2}(\cos\theta + (r/l)\cos 2\theta)$ | N | m in kg, r in m |
| 6 | Max torque on pin | $T_{max} = F_{T,\max} \cdot r$ | N·mm | $F_{T,\max}$ ≈ 1.05 $F_g$ |
| 7 | Bending moment on pin | $M_b = F_T \cdot l_p/4$ | N·mm | $l_p$ = pin length |
| 8 | Equivalent twisting moment | $T_e = \sqrt{T^{2} + M_b^{2}}$ | N·mm | Max-shear-stress theory |
| 9 | Crank pin diameter | $d_p = \left(\dfrac{16 T_e}{\pi \,\tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}$ | mm | Torsion formula |
| 10 | Web bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \, F_T \, r}{t \, w^{2}}$ | MPa | Rectangular web |
| 11 | Web crushing stress | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{F_T}{t \, d_p}$ | MPa | Direct bearing |
| 12 | Web shear stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{F_T}{t \, w}$ | MPa | Direct shear |
| 13 | Journal bearing pressure | $p_b = \dfrac{W}{l_j \cdot d_j}$ | MPa | $p_b$ ≤ 10–25 MPa |
| 14 | Journal length | $l_j = \dfrac{W}{p_b \cdot d_j}$ | mm | l/d ratio ≈ 1.0 to 1.5 |
| 15 | Factor of Safety | $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | — | Target 2.5 – 3.0 |

> **Note on the table:** Vertical bars for absolute value are not used in formulas; all quantities are squared or cubed. Always convert **N/mm² ↔ MPa** (they are identical).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> • **Passenger cars (1500–3000 rpm):** Crankpin fatigue is the limiting factor; design is **fatigue-driven**, not static strength.
> • **Heavy-duty diesel (locomotives, marine):** Static strength and **torsional vibration** dominate — critical speeds must be checked.
> • **Formula-1 engines (>18,000 rpm):** Crankshaft stiffness controls **journal oil-film collapse**; designs use hollow crankpins and titanium.
> • **Hybrid & electric powertrains:** Crankshaft design is **simplified** because reciprocating loads are lower — but torsional smoothness matters for NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness).

### 2.6 Why the Equivalent Torque Works (Physical "Why")

When a shaft is loaded by **bending (M) and torsion (T)** simultaneously, the **principal stresses** in a surface fibre are:

$$\sigma_1 = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\left(M + \sqrt{M^{2}+T^{2}}\right)/2, \quad \sigma_2 = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\left(-M + \sqrt{M^{2}+T^{2}}\right)/2$$

The **maximum shear stress** at the surface is half the difference, and it equals **$16 \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}/(\pi d^3)$** — which is exactly the torsion formula with $T_e = \sqrt{M^2+T^2}$. Hence the substitution is mathematically rigorous under the maximum-shear-stress (Tresca) failure theory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Equivalent Twisting Moment for the Crank Pin

The crank pin acts as a **simply-supported beam of length $l_p$** carrying a central concentrated load $F_T$ from the connecting rod.

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment at the Centre of the Pin**

For a simply supported beam with a central point load:

$$M_{max} = \frac{F_T \cdot l_p}{4}$$

This is the standard result from strength of materials (reactions at each support = $F_T/2$; moment at centre = $(F_T/2)\cdot(l_p/2) = F_T l_p/4$).

**Step 2 — Torque Transmitted by the Crank Pin**

The torque on the pin equals the tangential force times the crank radius:

$$T = F_T \cdot r$$

**Step 3 — Combining Bending and Torsion Using Max Shear Stress Theory**

For a circular shaft of diameter $d_p$, the **direct (bending) stress** at the surface is:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d_p^{3}}$$

The **torsional shear stress** at the surface is:

$$\tau = \frac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d_p^{3}}$$

According to the **maximum shear stress theory (Tresca criterion)**, the equivalent torsion is:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$$

**Derivation Logic:** Substituting the two equations above into the principal-stress expression for combined loading yields, after simplification:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d_p^{3}} \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, d_p^{3}}$$

This matches the simple torsion formula with $T_e$ replacing $T$.

**Step 4 — Diameter of the Crank Pin**

Setting the maximum shear stress equal to the allowable shear stress $\tau_{allow}$:

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, d_p^{3}}$$

Solving for $d_p$:

$$d_p = \left(\frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}$$

This is the **fundamental crank-pin design equation** (Formula 9 in the cheat sheet).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Stresses in the Crank Web (Cheek)

The web is a **rectangular plate** of thickness $t$ and width $w$. It is loaded at the crankpin by the tangential force $F_T$ and the radial force $F_R$. For a typical KTU design, the **tangential force is dominant** because it produces the **largest bending moment** at the web.

**Step 1 — Bending Moment on the Web**

The crank pin is offset from the main journal by the crank radius $r$. The tangential force at the crank pin thus exerts a bending moment on the web equal to:

$$M_{web} = F_T \cdot r$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus of the Rectangular Web**

For a rectangular cross-section with thickness $t$ (axial) and width $w$ (radial), the section modulus about the neutral axis is:

$$Z_{web} = \frac{t \cdot w^{2}}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Stress**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M_{web}}{Z_{web}} = \frac{F_T \cdot r \cdot 6}{t \cdot w^{2}} = \frac{6 \, F_T \, r}{t \, w^{2}}$$

Solving for the web width $w$:

$$w = \sqrt{\frac{6 \, F_T \, r}{t \cdot \sigma_{b,allow}}}$$

**Step 4 — Crushing Stress (Bearing Stress at Pin-Web Interface)**

The tangential force $F_T$ is transmitted from the crank pin into the web as a **compressive load** over the projected area $t \cdot d_p$:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{F_T}{t \cdot d_p}$$

The web is safe if $\sigma_c \leq \sigma_{c,allow}$ (typically 60–80 MPa for steel).

**Step 5 — Maximum Shear Stress in the Web**

The web is sheared along the radial plane of width $w$ and thickness $t$:

$$\tau_{web} = \frac{F_T}{t \cdot w}$$

The web is safe if $\tau_{web} \leq \tau_{allow}$ for the material.

**Step 6 — Check All Three Stresses**

The **final web thickness $t$ and width $w$** must satisfy **simultaneously**:

$$\sigma_b \leq \sigma_{b,allow}, \quad \sigma_c \leq \sigma_{c,allow}, \quad \tau_{web} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU pitfall** — students often check only bending and forget crushing or shear. Examiners in 2024 have specifically marked this.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Journal (Main Bearing) Length from Bearing Pressure

The **main journal** carries the **resultant force** from the gas and inertia loads:

$$W = \sqrt{F_T^{2} + F_R^{2}}$$

**Step 1 — Define the Bearing Pressure**

The bearing pressure is the projected load divided by the projected area:

$$p_b = \frac{W}{l_j \cdot d_j}$$

**Where:**
- $l_j$ = axial length of the journal bearing
- $d_j$ = journal diameter

**Step 2 — Solve for the Journal Length**

$$l_j = \frac{W}{p_b \cdot d_j}$$

The **l/d ratio** for crankshaft journals is typically **0.8 to 1.5**. If the calculated $l_j/d_j$ is outside this range, the **diameter $d_j$** must be **iteratively adjusted** until both constraints are satisfied:

1. $l_j/d_j$ in the allowable range,
2. $p_b \leq p_{b,allow}$ for the chosen bearing material.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
> *Design a crankshaft for a single-cylinder, four-stroke, horizontal IC engine with the following data:*
> - Cylinder bore $D = 100$ **mm**
> - Stroke $L = 120$ **mm**
> - Maximum gas pressure $P_g = 5$ **N/mm² (MPa)**
> - Engine speed $N = 1500$ **rpm**
> - Mass of reciprocating parts $m = 1.5$ **kg**
> - Connecting rod length $l = 240$ **mm** (so $r/l = 0.25$)
> - Material: **30C8 steel**, $\sigma_t = 500$ MPa, $\tau = 350$ MPa
> - Allowable bearing pressure $p_b = 10$ MPa
> - Length of crank pin: assume $l_p = 1.5 \, d_p$
> - Factor of safety (overall) = 3

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Geometric Quantities**

$$r = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 100^{2}}{4} = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Forces on the Piston**

Gas force:

$$F_g = P_g \cdot A_p = 5 \times 7853.98 = 39269.9 \text{ N}$$

Angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

Inertia force at $\theta = 0$ (worst case for KTU — approximate):

$$F_{i,0} = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^{2} \left(1 + \frac{r}{l}\right) = 1.5 \cdot 0.060 \cdot 157.08^{2} \cdot 1.25$$

Let us evaluate this carefully:

$$F_{i,0} = 1.5 \times 0.060 \times 24673.2 \times 1.25 = 2775.7 \text{ N}$$

So inertia is **~7% of gas force** and can often be neglected in rough KTU design. For conservative design, we **include** it.

Net force at top dead centre:

$$F = F_g - F_{i,0} = 39269.9 - 2775.7 = 36494.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Tangential Force on Crank Pin**

Using the simplified formula and the empirical peak at $\theta \approx 75°$ for $r/l = 0.25$:

$$F_{T,\max} \approx 1.05 \times F = 1.05 \times 36494.2 = 38319 \text{ N}$$

(For KTU 2024, it is **acceptable** to use $F_{T,\max} = F$ and place the analysis at the worst-case angle; both yield diameters in the same range.)

**Step 4 — Torque Transmitted**

$$T = F_{T,\max} \cdot r = 38319 \times 60 = 2299140 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5 — Allowable Stresses (with FOS = 3)**

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{\tau_{ult}}{FOS} = \frac{350}{3} = 116.67 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{b,allow} = \frac{\sigma_t}{FOS} = \frac{500}{3} = 166.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — First Estimate of Crank Pin Diameter (Torsion Only)**

For a first cut, neglect bending:

$$d_p = \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16 \times 2299140}{\pi \times 116.67}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{36786240}{366.52}\right)^{1/3} = (100375)^{1/3} \approx 46.4 \text{ mm}$$

Try $d_p = 50$ mm (round up to a standard size).

**Step 7 — Refined Calculation Including Bending**

With $d_p = 50$ mm, the pin length is:

$$l_p = 1.5 \, d_p = 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ mm}$$

Bending moment on the pin:

$$M_b = \frac{F_{T,\max} \cdot l_p}{4} = \frac{38319 \times 75}{4} = 718481 \text{ N·mm}$$

Equivalent twisting moment:

$$T_e = \sqrt{T^{2} + M_b^{2}} = \sqrt{2299140^{2} + 718481^{2}} = \sqrt{5.286 \times 10^{12} + 5.162 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{5.802 \times 10^{12}} = 2.409 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

Refined pin diameter:

$$d_p = \left(\frac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16 \times 2.409 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 116.67}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{3.854 \times 10^{7}}{366.52}\right)^{1/3} = (105160)^{1/3} = 47.2 \text{ mm}$$

So **standardise $d_p = 50$ mm** (covers both iterations).

**Step 8 — Crank Web Design**

Assume web thickness $t = 25$ mm and solve for width $w$ from the bending stress:

$$w = \sqrt{\frac{6 \, F_{T,\max} \cdot r}{t \cdot \sigma_{b,allow}}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 38319 \times 60}{25 \times 166.67}} = \sqrt{\frac{13794840}{4166.75}} = \sqrt{3311} = 57.5 \text{ mm}$$

Standardise to $w = 60$ mm.

**Verify all three web stresses:**

Bending:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{6 \, F_T \, r}{t \, w^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 38319 \times 60}{25 \times 60^{2}} = \frac{13794840}{90000} = 153.3 \text{ MPa} \leq 166.67 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

Crushing:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{F_T}{t \, d_p} = \frac{38319}{25 \times 50} = 30.66 \text{ MPa} \leq 80 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

Shear:

$$\tau_{web} = \frac{F_T}{t \, w} = \frac{38319}{25 \times 60} = 25.55 \text{ MPa} \leq 116.67 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 9 — Journal (Main Bearing) Design**

Resultant force on the journal (worst case at $\theta \approx 75°$):

$$F_R = F \cdot \cos\theta = 36494.2 \times 0.259 = 9452 \text{ N}$$

$$W = \sqrt{F_T^{2} + F_R^{2}} = \sqrt{38319^{2} + 9452^{2}} = \sqrt{1.469 \times 10^{9} + 8.93 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{1.558 \times 10^{9}} = 39471 \text{ N}$$

Journal diameter (assume same as crank pin, $d_j = 50$ mm):

$$l_j = \frac{W}{p_b \cdot d_j} = \frac{39471}{10 \times 50} = 78.94 \text{ mm}$$

Standardise $l_j = 80$ mm.

**Step 10 — Final Design Summary**

| Component | Final Dimensions |
|---|:---:|
| Crank pin diameter, $d_p$ | **50 mm** |
| Crank pin length, $l_p$ | **75 mm** |
| Web thickness, $t$ | **25 mm** |
| Web width, $w$ | **60 mm** |
| Journal diameter, $d_j$ | **50 mm** |
| Journal length, $l_j$ | **80 mm** |

> All stresses are within allowable limits with FOS = 3, satisfying **KTU 2024 design-check criteria**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Equation

| Design Situation | Applicable Equation | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|
| Need crank pin size | $d_p = (16 T_e / \pi \tau_{allow})^{1/3}$ | "Design the crank pin…" |
| Need web dimensions | All three web stresses checked | "Design the crank web…" |
| Need main journal length | $l_j = W / (p_b d_j)$ | "Find the bearing length…" |
| Check fatigue | Use Soderberg / Goodman line | "Check for infinite life…" |
| Find max torque | $T = F_{T,\max} \cdot r$ | "Compute the torque…" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The crankshaft is a 3-D physical object with offset features that are difficult to render as a flat Mermaid drawing. Therefore, the following is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the *interactions of forces, components, and design checks* on the crankshaft.

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Crankshaft Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs: Bore D, Stroke L, Pressure P_g, Speed N, Mass m, Rod length l, Material] --> B[Compute Geometry: r equals L by 2]
    B --> C[Compute Forces: F_g, F_i, F_net]
    C --> D[Find Maximum Tangential Force F_T at critical angle theta]
    D --> E[Compute Torque T equals F_T times r]
    E --> F[Crank Pin Module]
    E --> G[Crank Web Module]
    E --> H[Main Journal Module]
    F --> F1[Bending Moment M_b equals F_T times l_p over 4]
    F1 --> F2[Equivalent Torque T_e equals sqrt of T squared plus M_b squared]
    F2 --> F3[Pin Diameter d_p equals cube root of 16 T_e over pi tau_allow]
    G --> G1[Bending Stress equals 6 F_T r over t w squared]
    G --> G2[Crushing Stress equals F_T over t d_p]
    G --> G3[Shear Stress equals F_T over t w]
    G1 --> G4[Web Width w from bending equation]
    G2 --> G5[Verify all three web stresses within limits]
    G3 --> G5
    G4 --> G5
    H --> H1[Resultant Force W equals sqrt of F_T squared plus F_R squared]
    H1 --> H2[Journal Length l_j equals W over p_b times d_j]
    H2 --> H3[Check l_j over d_j ratio between 0.8 and 1.5]
    F3 --> I[Final Design Check: Factor of Safety at least 2.5]
    G5 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Standardise All Dimensions to KTU Board Norms]
    J --> K[Output: d_p, l_p, t, w, d_j, l_j]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Load Flow on a Crankpin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Piston with Gas Pressure P_g] --> A2[Connecting Rod at Angle theta]
    A2 --> A3[Big End Bearing]
    A3 --> A4[Crank Pin Center]
    A4 --> A5[Force F_T tangential, produces TORSION T]
    A4 --> A6[Force F_R radial, produces BENDING on web]
    A5 --> A7[Crankpin transmits T to web]
    A6 --> A8[Crankpin experiences BENDING M_b]
    A7 --> A9[Equivalent T_e equals sqrt of T squared plus M_b squared]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Crankpin design diameter d_p]
```

### 4.3 Text Schematic — Standard KTU Multi-Cylinder Inline Crankshaft Layout

```
              Timing Gear End                    Flywheel End
                     |                                  |
    [M.J.]---[Web]---[P1]---[Web]---[M.J.]---...---[Flywheel Flange]
                     |
                  Crankpin P1 (connects to Cyl 1 connecting rod)
              M.J. = Main Journal
              Web = Crank Web (Cheek)
              P1  = Crankpin for Cylinder 1
              φ   = Crank-pin offset (angle between cylinders)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For V-engines and multi-cylinder inline engines**, the angle between crank throws (φ) determines the firing order and balance. KTU 2024 questions on multi-cylinder design will often specify the firing order and ask for **journal sizing** given the **number of cylinders** and the **largest resultant force** in any one cylinder.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the materials commonly used for IC engine crankshafts and state one reason for the choice of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Material | Reason for Selection |
|---|---|
| **30C8 / 40C8 Carbon Steel** | Low cost; adequate strength for small/medium engines (1 mark) |
| **35CrMo / 40NiCr Alloy Steel** | High fatigue strength; used in high-performance petrol and diesel engines (1 mark) |
| **Nodular Cast Iron (SG Iron)** | Excellent damping; cheaper to cast in complex multi-throw shapes (1 mark) |

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What are the different stresses induced in a crankshaft? Write the expression for the equivalent twisting moment used in crank pin design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The crankshaft is subjected to the following stresses:
1. **Bending stress** — due to the radial component of the piston force acting at the crank pin offset (1 mark).
2. **Torsional shear stress** — due to the tangential force times the crank radius, which transmits the engine torque (1 mark).
3. **Shear and crushing stresses in the crank web** — at the pin–web interface (1 mark).

The **equivalent twisting moment**, by the maximum-shear-stress theory, is:

$$T_e = \sqrt{T^{2} + M_b^{2}}$$

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Crankshaft Design:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the equivalent torque $T_e$** — using only $T$ for the crank pin diameter underestimates the size. **Loss: 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Including inertia force at TDC even on the return strokes** — inertia acts *upward* on the return stroke, reducing the net force. Always specify "power stroke" or compute both cases. **Loss: 1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Using a single $F_T = F_g$** without verifying the critical angle — for high $r/l$ ratios (e.g. 1/3), the peak $F_T$ is ~1.05 $F_g$. Neglecting this under-designs the shaft. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting crushing or shear in web design** — checking only bending is a frequent error. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 5. **Not standardising to the next available size** — examiners mark down if the calculated $d = 47.2$ mm is not rounded to the standard 50 mm. **Loss: 1 mark**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Crankpin and Web Design for Single-Cylinder Engine**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

*Design a crankshaft for a single-cylinder, four-stroke, water-cooled diesel engine with the following data:*

- Cylinder bore $D = 120$ mm
- Stroke $L = 150$ mm
- Maximum gas pressure $P_g = 6$ N/mm²
- Engine speed $N = 1200$ rpm
- Mass of reciprocating parts $m = 2.0$ kg
- Length of connecting rod $l = 300$ mm
- Crank-pin length: $l_p = 1.5 \, d_p$
- Material: **40C8 steel** with $\sigma_t = 540$ MPa, $\tau = 380$ MPa
- Factor of safety = 4

**(a) Compute the gas force, the tangential force, and the maximum torque transmitted by the crank pin. (7 marks)**

**(b) Design the crank pin diameter and the crank web dimensions. (7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Force and Torque Calculation (7 marks)**

Crank radius:

$$r = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{150}{2} = 75 \text{ mm}$$

Piston area:

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 120^{2}}{4} = 11309.7 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

Gas force (1 mark):

$$F_g = P_g \cdot A_p = 6 \times 11309.7 = 67858.4 \text{ N}$$

Angular velocity (0.5 mark):

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

Inertia force at TDC (1 mark):

$$F_i = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^{2} \left(1 + \frac{r}{l}\right)$$

With $r = 0.075$ m, $l = 0.300$ m, $r/l = 0.25$:

$$F_i = 2.0 \times 0.075 \times 125.66^{2} \times 1.25 = 2.0 \times 0.075 \times 15791.4 \times 1.25 = 2960.9 \text{ N}$$

Net force on piston (1 mark):

$$F = F_g - F_i = 67858.4 - 2960.9 = 64897.5 \text{ N}$$

Maximum tangential force — for $r/l = 0.25$, the empirical factor is $F_{T,\max} \approx 1.05 \, F$ (1 mark):

$$F_{T,\max} = 1.05 \times 64897.5 = 68142.4 \text{ N}$$

Maximum torque on the crank pin (1.5 marks):

$$T_{max} = F_{T,\max} \cdot r = 68142.4 \times 75 = 5110680 \text{ N·mm} = 5.11 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Part (b) — Crank Pin and Web Design (7 marks)**

Allowable stresses with FOS = 4 (1 mark):

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{380}{4} = 95 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{b,allow} = \frac{540}{4} = 135 \text{ MPa}$$

**Crank Pin Diameter (3 marks):**

First iteration (torsion only):

$$d_p = \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16 \times 5110680}{\pi \times 95}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{81.77 \times 10^{6}}{298.45}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$d_p = (274000)^{1/3} = 65.0 \text{ mm}$$

Try $d_p = 65$ mm. Then $l_p = 1.5 \times 65 = 97.5$ mm.

Bending moment:

$$M_b = \frac{F_T \cdot l_p}{4} = \frac{68142.4 \times 97.5}{4} = 1660797 \text{ N·mm}$$

Equivalent twisting moment:

$$T_e = \sqrt{T^{2} + M_b^{2}} = \sqrt{5110680^{2} + 1660797^{2}} = \sqrt{26.12 \times 10^{12} + 2.76 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{28.88 \times 10^{12}} = 5.374 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

Refined diameter:

$$d_p = \left(\frac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16 \times 5.374 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 95}\right)^{1/3} = (287960)^{1/3} = 66.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardise $d_p = 70$ mm** to keep a comfortable margin.

**Crank Web Dimensions (3 marks):**

Assume web thickness $t = 30$ mm. From bending-stress equation:

$$w = \sqrt{\frac{6 \, F_T \, r}{t \cdot \sigma_{b,allow}}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 68142.4 \times 75}{30 \times 135}} = \sqrt{\frac{30664080}{4050}} = \sqrt{7570} = 87 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardise $w = 90$ mm**.

Verify all three web stresses:

- Bending: $\sigma_b = (6 \times 68142.4 \times 75)/(30 \times 90^{2}) = 30.66 \times 10^{6}/243000 = 126.2$ MPa $\leq 135$ MPa ✓
- Crushing: $\sigma_c = F_T/(t d_p) = 68142.4/(30 \times 70) = 32.4$ MPa $\leq 80$ MPa ✓
- Shear: $\tau_{web} = F_T/(t w) = 68142.4/(30 \times 90) = 25.2$ MPa $\leq 95$ MPa ✓

**Final Design Values:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|:---:|
| Crank pin diameter, $d_p$ | **70 mm** |
| Crank pin length, $l_p$ | **105 mm** |
| Web thickness, $t$ | **30 mm** |
| Web width, $w$ | **90 mm** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice on Journal Design + Material Selection**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

*(a) List the desirable properties of a crankshaft material. For a high-speed petrol engine, justify the selection of forged alloy steel over nodular cast iron. (7 marks)*

*(b) For the crank pin of diameter 60 mm designed in a multi-cylinder engine, the journal diameter is 60 mm and the resultant load on the main journal is 60 kN. Determine the journal length if the allowable bearing pressure is 12 MPa. Verify the $l/d$ ratio. (7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Material Properties and Selection (7 marks)**

**Desirable properties of a crankshaft material (4 marks):**
1. **High fatigue strength** — because the crankshaft undergoes $10^{7}$–$10^{9}$ stress cycles over its life.
2. **High wear resistance** — at the journal and pin surfaces.
3. **Good toughness / impact resistance** — to absorb sudden torsional shocks.
4. **High stiffness** — to maintain alignment between cylinders.
5. **Good machinability** — for the complex profile of crank webs and oil holes.
6. **Damping capacity** — to absorb torsional vibrations.

**Forged alloy steel vs. Nodular cast iron (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Forged Alloy Steel | Nodular Cast Iron |
|---|---|---|
| Fatigue strength | **Higher** (450–600 MPa) | Lower (250–350 MPa) |
| Stiffness (Young's Modulus) | **Higher** (210 GPa) | Lower (170 GPa) |
| Damping capacity | Lower | **Higher** |
| Cost | Higher | **Lower** |
| Weight | Higher | **Lighter** (~10%) |

**Justification for high-speed petrol engine:**
- High-speed engines run at **4000–7000 rpm**, generating **large inertia forces** and **high cycle counts** — fatigue strength is paramount.
- Forged alloy steel (e.g., 40Cr, 35CrMo) offers **30–40% higher fatigue strength** than nodular cast iron.
- The **higher stiffness** of forged steel minimises journal deflection, maintaining the oil-film thickness.
- Hence, **forged alloy steel is preferred for high-speed petrol engines**, whereas nodular cast iron is acceptable for low-speed diesel engines where the cycle count is lower and damping is desired.

**Part (b) — Journal Length Calculation (7 marks)**

Given:
- Journal diameter $d_j = 60$ mm
- Resultant load $W = 60$ kN = 60 000 N
- Allowable bearing pressure $p_b = 12$ MPa

**Step 1 — Journal length from bearing pressure (4 marks):**

$$l_j = \frac{W}{p_b \cdot d_j} = \frac{60000}{12 \times 60} = \frac{60000}{720} = 83.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Standardise journal length (1 mark):**

Take $l_j = 85$ mm (next standard size).

**Step 3 — Verify $l/d$ ratio (2 marks):**

$$\frac{l_j}{d_j} = \frac{85}{60} = 1.417$$

This is **within the standard range of 0.8 to 1.5** for IC engine main journals. ✓

**Conclusion:** The journal length is **$l_j = 85$ mm**, and the $l/d$ ratio of 1.42 satisfies KTU design norms.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Crankshaft Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Geometry:** $r = L/2$; pin length $l_p = 1.4$ to $1.7 \, d_p$ is a standard assumption.
- **Gas force** dominates inertia in most KTU problems; still **include inertia at TDC** for safety.
- **Tangential force** peaks near $\theta = 75°$ for $r/l = 0.25$; use $F_{T,\max} \approx 1.05 \, F_g$ as a quick rule.
- **Equivalent torque** $T_e = \sqrt{T^2 + M_b^2}$ — never use $T$ alone for crank pin diameter.
- **Three web stresses** must be checked: **bending, crushing, shear**. The widest of these often governs.
- **Bearing pressure** $p_b$ for **steel on babbitt = 10–25 MPa**; for **steel on bronze = 25–40 MPa**.
- **Journal $l/d$ ratio** must lie in **0.8 to 1.5**; otherwise, re-iterate on $d_j$.
- **Factor of Safety 2.5 – 3.0** is the KTU board expectation; FOS = 4 is conservative and used for heavy-duty diesels.
- **Materials:** *Forged alloy steel* for high-speed petrol; *nodular cast iron* for low-speed diesel (cost-effective).
- **Failure modes to remember:** *Fatigue* at the fillet between pin and web (most common); *Bearing wear* at the journal; *Whip and vibration* at critical speeds.
- **Always standardise** your final answer to a **whole-number millimetre** (50 mm, 70 mm, etc.) — KTU examiners expect this.
- **Units discipline:** Convert N/mm² ↔ MPa (identical); kg·m/s² = N; m·rad/s² ≠ m/s² — keep units consistent in every step.
- **Final answer table** with $d_p$, $l_p$, $t$, $w$, $d_j$, $l_j$ is the **expected deliverable** in any 14-mark KTU design question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piston
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piston

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piston (as per KTU 2024 Design of Automotive Components syllabus):**
> A **piston** is a cylindrical component that reciprocates inside the cylinder bore of an internal combustion (I.C.) engine. It transmits the force of combustion gases to the connecting rod via the gudgeon (wrist) pin, converts reciprocating motion of the gas column into useful mechanical work, and seals the combustion chamber against gas leakage by means of piston rings.

The piston operates under one of the most punishing mechanical, thermal, and tribological environments in any machine — high cyclic pressure, extreme temperature gradients, and sliding contact with the cylinder wall — making it a **critical fatigue-loaded component** in engine design.

### 1.2 Functional Objectives of a Piston

A well-designed piston must simultaneously satisfy the following functional requirements:

| S. No. | Primary Function | Engineering Consequence |
|:------:|:-----------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | Seal combustion gases without leakage | Determines compression ratio and engine efficiency |
| 2 | Transmit gas force to the connecting rod | Dictates thrust load and bending stresses |
| 3 | Guide the connecting rod in linear motion | Influences side-thrust and skirt wear |
| 4 | Dissipate heat from combustion chamber | Prevents scuffing, knock, and thermal failure |
| 5 | Accommodate thermal expansion | Maintains clearance at all operating temperatures |
| 6 | Reduce reciprocating mass | Lowers inertia loads and bearing stresses |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hero Inside a Combustion Engine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Closed-Fist Hammer Inside a Tube:**
> Imagine a heavy metal cylinder that slides up and down inside a hollow steel tube (the cylinder liner). A controlled explosion at the top of this tube pushes it downward — exactly like placing a fist inside a pipe and striking the top of your fist with a hammer. The "fist" (piston) is forced down, but instead of doing nothing, it pushes a rod (connecting rod) attached to a crank, which turns the whole linear force into rotation.
>
> The challenges: your fist must **not** let any gas (hammer blow) escape around the sides — that's why rings are fitted. The fist also gets **red hot** on top and must shed heat through its body to oil spray below — that's why the piston has internal ribs. The fist also **wobbles slightly** sideways because the connecting rod is angled — that's why the **skirt** is long enough to prevent knocking against the cylinder wall.

### 1.4 Standard Materials Used (KTU High-Yield Data)

| Material | Composition / Type | Typical Application |
|:---------|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| **Cast Iron** | Gray / Alloyed cast iron | Older medium-duty engines |
| **Aluminum Alloys** | Al-Si (e.g., LM 13, LM 26) | Most modern automotive pistons |
| **Forged Aluminum** | Al-Si-Cu-Mg forged alloys | High-performance petrol/diesel pistons |
| **Invar Insert** | Fe-Ni 36% alloy | Strut-type pistons for diesel engines |
| **Cast Steel (rare)** | For very large slow-speed engines | Marine / stationary engines |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Aluminum dominates:** Density ≈ **2.7 g/cm³** (vs. cast iron ≈ **7.2 g/cm³**) → lower inertia force. Thermal conductivity ≈ **150–200 W/m·K** (vs. cast iron ≈ **50 W/m·K**) → superior heat dissipation. The penalty is a lower fatigue strength, so the design must be more careful.

### 1.5 Types of Pistons (KTU Classification)

```
Pistons (by construction)
 ├── Solid / Underslung skirt (old design)
 ├── Slotted / Split skirt
 ├── Autothermic (controlled expansion) pistons
 │      ├── Strut-type (invar strut insert)
 │      └── Cam-ground (elliptical) pistons
 ├── Articulated pistons
 └── Aluminum-steel bi-metal pistons (Morse, Salvadori)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The syllabus module emphasizes **stresses in the piston head, piston rings, gudgeon pin, and skirt**. The *Autothermic* (controlled thermal expansion) piston is the industry standard in modern multi-cylinder engines.

### 1.6 Anatomy of a Typical Autothermic Piston (Sectioned View Vocabulary)

- **Crown / Head** — flat, dished, or piston-bowl shaped; faces combustion directly.
- **Ring Zone** — carries compression rings (top) and oil-control ring (bottom).
- **Skirt** — guides the piston; absorbs side-thrust.
- **Gudgeon Pin Bosses** — house the gudgeon/wrist pin.
- **Internal Ribs** — connect the head to the pin bosses (load path).
- **Gudgeon Pin (Wrist Pin)** — pivots to allow rod articulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design Considerations — The "Engineering Trade-off" Viewpoint

Designing a piston is essentially a multi-constraint optimization problem with **seven** dominant design drivers. KTU examiners expect you to identify *which* stress governs a particular region of the piston.

| Region of Piston | Dominant Loading | Governing Criterion |
|:-----------------|:-----------------|:--------------------|
| **Piston Head** | Gas pressure (uniform), thermal | Bending + heat dissipation |
| **Ring Grooves** | Tangential hoop stress from ring tension | Tensile (hoop) failure |
| **Piston Skirt** | Side-thrust (inertia + gas) | Wear & Hertzian contact |
| **Gudgeon Pin** | Cyclic bending + bearing pressure | Fatigue + crushing |
| **Pin Bosses** | Bearing pressure on pin | Local compressive yield |
| **Crown** | Direct gas + thermal | Creep at high temperature |
| **Ribs (internal)** | Combined bending + shear | Bending stress |

### 2.2 Forces & Stresses Acting on a Piston (Decomposed)

A piston simultaneously experiences:

1. **Gas force (F_g)** = `P_g × (π/4) × D²` — fluctuates from near-zero (intake) to peak pressure (combustion).
2. **Inertia force (F_i)** = `m_r × ω² × r × (cos θ + (r/l)·cos 2θ)` — fluctuating reciprocating mass reaction.
3. **Side thrust (F_s)** = Normal reaction on the cylinder wall from the angular connecting rod.
4. **Frictional force (F_f)** = `μ × F_n` — dissipative, opposes motion.
5. **Ring tension** — preloaded radial contact pressure `P_r` between ring and cylinder wall.
6. **Thermal gradient** — temperature drop from crown (≈ **300 °C**) to skirt (≈ **120 °C**) induces **thermal stress**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities are in **N, mm, N/mm², W, °C, kg, m/s²** unless explicitly stated. Use `\vert` notation for magnitudes.

| S. No. | Parameter | Formula | Meaning of Symbols |
|:------:|:----------|:--------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Gas force on crown | $F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot P_{\max}$ | $D$ = bore, $P_{\max}$ = peak gas pressure |
| 2 | Piston head thickness (strength) | $t_h = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot D^2}{16 \cdot \sigma_t}}$ | $\sigma_t$ = allowable tensile stress |
| 3 | Piston head thickness (heat dissipation) | $t_h = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{H \cdot (D^2 - d^2)}{k \cdot C \cdot (D + d)}}$ | $H$ = heat flow rate, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $C$ = allowable temp. gradient |
| 4 | Approx. empirical (Khurmi) | $t_h = 0.032\,D + 1.5$ (mm) | Quick design estimate |
| 5 | Bending moment on unit strip | $M = F_s \cdot \dfrac{t_h}{2}$ | Per unit circumferential length |
| 6 | Bending stress in head | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 \cdot F_s}{t_h^{\,2}}$ | $F_s$ taken per unit length |
| 7 | Thermal stress in head | $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | $A_1, A_2$ = restraint factors |
| 8 | Number of compression rings | $n \approx 0.0072 \cdot D + 0.3$ | Khurmi's empirical |
| 9 | Radial ring thickness | $t_r = D \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \cdot P_w \cdot w}{\sigma_t}}$ | $P_w$ = wall pressure, $w$ = ring width |
| 10 | Side thrust force | $F_s = 0.1 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot D \cdot L$ | $L$ = cylinder length |
| 11 | Gudgeon pin bending stress (both ends free) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{8 \cdot F \cdot l}{\pi \cdot d_0^{\,3}}$ | $F$ = load on pin, $l$ = pin length |
| 12 | Gudgeon pin bending stress (cantilever) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{4 \cdot F \cdot l}{\pi \cdot d_0^{\,3}}$ | For non-floating pin |
| 13 | Bearing pressure on gudgeon pin | $P_b = \dfrac{F}{d_0 \cdot l_1}$ | $l_1$ = bearing length |
| 14 | Inertia force (TDC max) | $F_i = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{l}\right)$ | $m$ = reciprocating mass |
| 15 | Allowable bearing pressure (pin) | $P_b \le 25\text{–}30$ N/mm² | Empirical limit |
| 16 | Elastic modulus (Al alloy) | $E = 70$–$80$ GPa | Material property |

> [!WARNING]
> **|Absolute Value Rule|** — In any derivation, magnitudes must use `\vert x \vert` (LaTeX) — never the bare pipe `|` — to keep markdown tables intact.

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real-World Engineering

- The **head thickness** is dual-bounded because in diesel engines, mechanical strength *and* heat transfer are equally critical. A thinner head is desirable thermally but causes plastic collapse under peak pressure.
- The **ring radial thickness** equation comes from Barlow's hoop-stress formula applied to a thin ring under internal pressure `P_w` (≈ **0.25 N/mm²** typical wall pressure).
- The **gudgeon pin** is fatigue-critical — its bending stress can swing from +ve to –ve every revolution; design must satisfy a finite fatigue life (typically **10⁷** cycles).
- **Aluminum vs. Cast Iron** selection is governed by inertia force (Al wins at high rpm) and peak temperature (cast iron wins above ≈ **400 °C**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Piston Head Thickness (Strength Criterion)

**Assumption:** The piston head is a **circular plate of uniform thickness**, rigidly clamped at the rim (by the cylinder wall) and loaded by uniformly distributed gas pressure $P_{\max}$. For analysis we consider a unit circumferential strip of width 1 mm — this reduces the problem to a **fixed-fixed beam** of span $D/2$ loaded by a uniform load $w = P_{\max}$ per unit length.

#### Step 1 — Bending Moment at the Fixed Edge

For a uniformly loaded fixed beam of span $\ell = D/2$:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{w \cdot \ell^2}{12} = \frac{P_{\max} \cdot (D/2)^2}{12} = \frac{P_{\max} \cdot D^2}{48}$$

#### Step 2 — Section Modulus of Unit Strip

For a rectangular strip of width $b = 1$ mm and thickness $t_h$:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot t_h^{\,2}}{6} = \frac{t_h^{\,2}}{6}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Bending Equation

$$\sigma_t = \frac{M}{Z} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{P_{\max} \cdot D^2}{48} = \sigma_t \cdot \frac{t_h^{\,2}}{6}$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for $t_h$

$$\sigma_t \cdot \frac{t_h^{\,2}}{6} = \frac{P_{\max} \cdot D^2}{48}$$

$$t_h^{\,2} = \frac{6 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot D^2}{48 \cdot \sigma_t} = \frac{P_{\max} \cdot D^2}{8 \cdot \sigma_t}$$

But because the radial ribs reduce the effective clamping radius and the actual maximum moment is approximately **3/2** of the simple clamped value (Filon's correction for circular plate), the empirical working formula becomes:

$$t_h = \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot D^2}{16 \cdot \sigma_t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor 3/2?** A circular plate clamped at the rim develops a moment coefficient of **3/16** instead of **1/8** for a strip — the additional radial constraint amplifies the moment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Piston Head Thickness (Heat Dissipation Criterion)

**Assumption:** Steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction through the head from the hot crown (temperature $T_1$) to the cooler inner surface (temperature $T_2$). The heat flows radially inward to the ring zone / pin-boss region.

#### Step 1 — Heat Flow Rate

For a flat circular plate of inner radius $d/2$ and outer radius $D/2$, with thickness $t_h$, the heat flow rate $H$ (W) per unit temperature gradient is given by:

$$H = \frac{2 \pi k \cdot t_h}{\ln(D/d)} \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\Delta T = T_1 - T_2$.

#### Step 2 — Equate to the Allowable Heat Flux

A permissible thermal gradient $C$ (in °C per unit area) is established to avoid crown seizure and ring-groove coking:

$$\frac{H}{\frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)} \le C \cdot k$$

#### Step 3 — Solve for $t_h$

Rearranging the heat-flow equation:

$$H = \frac{2 \pi k t_h \, \Delta T}{\ln(D/d)}$$

Substituting $\Delta T = C \cdot (D-d)/2$ (linear gradient approximation) and solving for $t_h$ gives the KTU working formula:

$$t_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H \cdot (D^2 - d^2)}{k \cdot C \cdot (D + d)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU interpretation:** The thickness is the **larger** of the two criteria (strength-based and heat-based). Designers then choose $t_h = \max\{t_{h,\text{strength}},\; t_{h,\text{heat}}\}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bending Stress in the Piston Head (Side-Thrust Effect)

#### Step 1 — Side-Thrust Force

Approximate side-thrust from gas load and inertia:

$$F_s = 0.1 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot D \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the cylinder stroke length.

#### Step 2 — Per-Unit-Length Side Thrust

The thrust is distributed over a circumference $\pi D$, so the per-unit-length side force is:

$$f_s = \frac{F_s}{\pi D} = \frac{0.1 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot L}{\pi}$$

#### Step 3 — Bending Moment Per Unit Length

Assuming a worst-case cantilever action over the head thickness $t_h$:

$$M = f_s \cdot \frac{t_h}{2} = \frac{0.1 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot L \cdot t_h}{2 \pi}$$

#### Step 4 — Section Modulus & Bending Stress

$$Z = \frac{1 \cdot t_h^{\,2}}{6} \;\Rightarrow\; \sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{0.1 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot L \cdot t_h / 2\pi}{t_h^{\,2}/6}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{0.3 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot L}{\pi \cdot t_h}$$

For $P_{\max}$ in MPa, $L$ in mm, and $t_h$ in mm, this gives $\sigma_b$ in MPa.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gudgeon Pin Bending Stress (Floating Pin, Both Ends Supported)

**Assumption:** The floating gudgeon pin transfers load to two bearing surfaces — the connecting rod small end (centre) and the piston pin-bosses (each end). The pin behaves as a beam simply supported at the rod-eye (centre) and loaded at the two ends (bosses).

#### Step 1 — Load Distribution

Let $F$ be the total gas force on the pin (essentially $F_g$ corrected for inertia at TDC). Each end-support reacts with $F/2$.

#### Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment

With span $l$ (length of pin between boss inner faces) and load $F/2$ at each end:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{F}{2} \cdot \frac{l}{2} = \frac{F \cdot l}{4}$$

#### Step 3 — Section Modulus of Solid Pin

$$Z = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot d_0^{\,3}$$

#### Step 4 — Bending Stress

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{F \cdot l / 4}{(\pi / 32) \cdot d_0^{\,3}}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{8 \cdot F \cdot l}{\pi \cdot d_0^{\,3}}$$

For non-floating (fixed) gudgeon pins (each end restrained in the piston boss), the maximum moment is halved, giving:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{4 \cdot F \cdot l}{\pi \cdot d_0^{\,3}}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Inertia Force on the Piston (KTU TDC Condition)

The piston and the upper end of the connecting rod constitute a reciprocating mass $m_r$. Using the exact slider-crank kinematics, the instantaneous acceleration is:

$$a = \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \left( \cos \theta + \frac{r}{l} \cdot \cos 2\theta \right)$$

#### Step 1 — Maximum Value at TDC

At $\theta = 0$: $\cos 0 = 1$, $\cos 0 = 1$:

$$a_{\max} = \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{r}{l} \right)$$

#### Step 2 — Inertia Force

$$F_i = m_r \cdot a_{\max} = m_r \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{r}{l} \right)$$

#### Step 3 — Net Force on Gudgeon Pin

The pin carries the **algebraic sum** of gas force and inertia force:

$$F_{\text{pin}} = F_g \pm F_i$$

- At TDC (firing stroke): $F_{\text{pin}} = F_g + F_i$ (max compression)
- At TDC (overlap): $F_{\text{pin}} = F_g - F_i$ (least loading)

The critical design value is the **maximum**, i.e., the **firing** TDC value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip — Most-missed step:** Students often forget the $(r/l) \cdot \cos 2\theta$ term. Always include it — in slow-speed engines $r/l$ is small, but in high-speed engines ($r/l \approx 1/3.5$) it can be 30 % of the primary term.

---

### 3.6 Design Algorithm — Summary Flow (for KTU numerical problems)

```
STEP 1: Take FOS = 5 to 8 (for fatigue loading on aluminum piston)
STEP 2: σ_t(allow) = σ_u / FOS
STEP 3: Compute t_h(strength) = √(3 P_max D² / 16 σ_t)
STEP 4: Compute t_h(heat) using heat-dissipation formula
STEP 5: t_h = max(STEP 3, STEP 4)
STEP 6: Compute side thrust F_s and bending stress σ_b
STEP 7: Verify σ_b + σ_t(thermal) ≤ σ_t(allow)
STEP 8: Design piston rings (n, t_r, width)
STEP 9: Design gudgeon pin (d_0, l)
STEP 10: Verify pin bearing pressure P_b ≤ 25–30 N/mm²
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> A Mermaid block-level functional architecture has been used to map piston sub-systems and the **flow of force, heat, and stress** through the structure — this is a faithful representation of the energy & load pathways and is fully safe to render.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crown / Head] --> B[Internal Ribs]
    B --> C[Gudgeon Pin Bosses]
    C --> D[Gudgeon Wrist Pin]
    D --> E[Connecting Rod Small End]
    E --> F[Crank Pin Journal]

    A -->|Gas force P_g| A
    A -->|Heat flow H| A

    A -->|Combines into| G[Piston Head Stresses]
    G --> G1[Compressive]
    G --> G2[Bending]
    G --> G3[Thermal]

    A2[Ring Zone] -->|Tension T_0| H[Piston Ring Stresses]
    H --> H1[Hoop]
    H --> H2[Radial contact]

    A3[Skirt] -->|Side thrust F_s| I[Skirt Stresses]
    I --> I1[Hertzian contact]
    I --> I2[Wear]

    D -->|Bearing load F| J[Gudgeon Pin Stresses]
    J --> J1[Bending sigma_b]
    J --> J2[Bearing p_b]
    J --> J3[Shear tau]

    G --> K[Total Failure Check]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L{sigma_total < sigma_allow ?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Design Accepted]
    L -- No --> N[Revise t_h or d_0]
```

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Subsystem Roles

| Subsystem | Functional Role | Dominant Design Parameter |
|:----------|:----------------|:--------------------------|
| Crown | Seals combustion chamber | $P_{\max}$, $T_{\text{crown}}$ |
| Head | Transfers gas force to ribs | $t_h$ (head thickness) |
| Ribs | Conduct force crown → pin-boss | Rib orientation, count |
| Ring Zone | Maintains gas seal | $n$, $t_r$, ring tension |
| Skirt | Guides piston, absorbs thrust | $L_{\text{skirt}}$, ovality |
| Pin Bosses | House gudgeon pin | Bearing pressure $P_b$ |
| Gudgeon Pin | Pivots relative to rod & piston | $d_0$, $l$, fatigue life |

### 4.2 Schematic Load-Path Map (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Combustion Gas P_max] -->|Pressure| Crown
    Crown -->|Force| Ribs
    Ribs -->|Concentrated| Bosses
    Bosses -->|Shear and Bearing| Pin
    Pin -->|Push Pull| RodSmall
    RodSmall -->|Axial| RodBig
    RodBig -->|Torque| Crank
    Crown -.->|Heat Q| HCrown
    HCrown -->|Conduction| Ribs
    Ribs -->|Conduction| Pin
    Pin -->|Conduction| OilSpray
    OilSpray -->|Convection| Sump
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1. List any three functions of a piston in an I.C. engine.**

**Model Answer:**
1. It transmits the force of combustion gases to the connecting rod.
2. It forms a seal with the cylinder walls (via piston rings) to prevent gas leakage into the crankcase.
3. It guides the connecting rod in its linear motion and dissipates a major portion of the combustion heat to the lubricating oil.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**Q2. Why is aluminum alloy preferred over cast iron for high-speed automotive pistons?**

**Model Answer:**
Aluminum alloy has one-third the density of cast iron (**2.7 vs. 7.2 g/cm³**), which reduces the reciprocating inertia force and hence the dynamic bearing loads. Its thermal conductivity is also 3–4 times higher (**150–200 W/m·K vs. 50 W/m·K**), enabling better heat dissipation from the crown. These advantages outweigh its lower fatigue strength in modern high-speed engines.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Piston Head Design**

The cylinder of a four-stroke diesel engine has bore **D = 100 mm**, stroke **L = 125 mm**, and peak gas pressure **P_max = 7 N/mm²**. The piston head is to be designed in aluminum alloy with ultimate tensile strength **σ_u = 200 N/mm²** and a factor of safety of **6**. The thermal conductivity is **k = 175 W/m·K**, and the allowable temperature gradient is **C = 5 °C per mm**. The cylinder wall is **w = 25 mm** thick and the piston pin boss diameter is **d = 30 mm**. The heat flow rate from the crown is **H = 1.2 × 10⁴ W**.

**(a)** Determine the thickness of the piston head from strength considerations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the thickness of the piston head from heat dissipation considerations and select the design value. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution — Strength Criterion**

Given: $D = 100$ mm, $P_{\max} = 7$ N/mm², $\sigma_u = 200$ N/mm², FOS = 6.

$$\sigma_t = \frac{\sigma_u}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{200}{6} = 33.33 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Apply the strength formula:

$$t_h = \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot D^2}{16 \cdot \sigma_t}}$$

$$t_h = \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot 7 \cdot (100)^2}{16 \cdot 33.33}}$$

$$t_h = \sqrt{\frac{21 \cdot 10\,000}{533.28}} = \sqrt{393.94}$$

$$\boxed{t_h \approx 19.85 \text{ mm} \;\;\text{(strength-based)}}$$

---

**(b) Model Solution — Heat Dissipation Criterion**

Given: $H = 1.2 \times 10^4$ W, $k = 175$ W/m·K, $C = 5$ °C/mm, $D = 100$ mm, $d = 30$ mm.

$$t_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H \cdot (D^2 - d^2)}{k \cdot C \cdot (D + d)}}$$

$$t_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.2 \times 10^4 \cdot (100^2 - 30^2)}{175 \cdot 5 \cdot (100 + 30)}}$$

$$t_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.2 \times 10^4 \cdot 9100}{175 \cdot 5 \cdot 130}}$$

$$t_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.092 \times 10^8}{113\,750}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{960}$$

$$t_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 30.98 \approx 15.49 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Design Selection:** $t_h = \max(19.85,\; 15.49) = \mathbf{19.85 \text{ mm}}$, rounded up to **20 mm**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Gudgeon Pin Design**

A four-cylinder petrol engine has a bore **D = 80 mm**, maximum gas pressure **P_max = 5 N/mm²**, and a floating gudgeon pin of length **l = 60 mm** with mean pin-boss bearing length **l₁ = 30 mm**. The pin is made of alloy steel with $\sigma_u = 600$ N/mm² and FOS = 4.

**(a)** Calculate the bending stress in the gudgeon pin and verify against the allowable value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the bearing pressure on the gudgeon pin and check whether it is within the safe limit (25 N/mm²). **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution — Bending Stress**

Gas force on the piston:

$$F_g = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot P_{\max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 80^2 \cdot 5 = 25\,133 \text{ N}$$

For a floating gudgeon pin (load shared equally between piston boss and rod small end), each end-support carries $F/2$, and the load on the pin (per boss reaction) is half the gas force:

$$F_{\text{end}} = \frac{F_g}{2} = 12\,566 \text{ N}$$

Allowable bending stress:

$$\sigma_b^{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_u}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{600}{4} = 150 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

The required pin diameter for this stress (using $\sigma_b = 8Fl/(\pi d_0^3)$):

$$d_0^3 = \frac{8 \cdot 12\,566 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot 150} = \frac{6.03 \times 10^6}{471.24} = 12\,799 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d_0 = (12\,799)^{1/3} \approx 23.5 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt $d_0 = \mathbf{25}$ mm (next standard size).

Check actual bending stress:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{8 \cdot 12\,566 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot 25^3} = \frac{6.03 \times 10^6}{49\,087} \approx 122.8 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_b = 122.8 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 150 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\;\text{Safe}}$$

---

**(b) Model Solution — Bearing Pressure**

The bearing pressure on the pin-boss interface (projected area $d_0 \times l_1$):

$$P_b = \frac{F_{\text{end}}}{d_0 \cdot l_1} = \frac{12\,566}{25 \cdot 30} = \frac{12\,566}{750}$$

$$\boxed{P_b = 16.75 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 25 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\;\text{Safe}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the FOS in the gas force** — Always reduce the ultimate strength; examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if the design is checked against raw $\sigma_u$.
> 2. **Forgetting inertia force at TDC** — The KTU module 4 question bank often includes the term $(1 + r/l)$; a missing $r/l$ term loses **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Using wrong formula variant for gudgeon pin** — *Floating* pin and *fixed* pin have different moment coefficients ($8$ vs. $4$ in the numerator). Mixing them up costs **3 marks**.
> 4. **Not selecting the max** of the two head-thickness criteria — examiners explicitly look for $\max\{t_{h,s},\; t_{h,h}\}$.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** — $H$ in W, $k$ in W/m·K, dimensions in mm — students must convert **m → mm** in the thermal formula. A 10³ error in thickness is a common killer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this on your wall before the KTU exam:**

- **Piston** is a reciprocating component performing **sealing, force transmission, heat dissipation, and guidance**.
- Preferred material: **Al-Si alloy** (LM 13, LM 26) — low density, high thermal conductivity.
- **Piston head thickness** has TWO criteria: **strength** ($t_h \propto \sqrt{P \cdot D^2 / \sigma_t}$) and **heat** ($t_h \propto \sqrt{H/(k \cdot C)}$); pick the **larger**.
- **Bending stress** in the head is caused by **side thrust** $F_s = 0.1 \cdot P_{\max} \cdot D \cdot L$.
- **Thermal stress** arises from the temperature drop across the crown: $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T \cdot (\text{restraint factor})$.
- **Number of compression rings**: $n \approx 0.0072 D + 0.3$.
- **Radial ring thickness**: $t_r = D \sqrt{3 P_w w / \sigma_t}$.
- **Gudgeon pin** is **fatigue-critical** — design with FOS = 3 to 4.
  - Floating pin: $\sigma_b = 8 F l / (\pi d_0^3)$
  - Fixed pin: $\sigma_b = 4 F l / (\pi d_0^3)$
- **Bearing pressure on pin** $P_b \le 25$–$30$ N/mm² for aluminum bosses.
- **Inertia force at TDC**: $F_i = m \cdot \omega^2 r (1 + r/l)$ — never omit the $r/l$ term.
- **Skirt** absorbs side-thrust; **oval grinding** (cam-grinding) gives clearance at running temperature.
- **Autothermic (controlled expansion) piston** uses an **Invar (Fe-36%Ni) strut** to control thermal expansion at the boss region.
- The final design FOS check: $\sigma_{\text{combined}} = \sigma_b + \sigma_t + \sigma_{th} \le \sigma_t^{\text{allowable}}$.
- **Use `\vert` for magnitudes** in all written notes; never raw pipe `|` (it breaks LaTeX tables).
- **Always state the FOS** explicitly in the first step of any numerical solution.
- **Verify all three**: (i) strength of head, (ii) bearing pressure on pin, (iii) combined stress $\le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylinder
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cylinder

# Cylinder — Module 4 Premium Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cylinder (Automotive Context):** The cylindrical bore within the engine block (or cylinder liner) in which the piston reciprocates, converting combustion pressure into linear mechanical work. In design terminology, it is a **thick/finite cylindrical pressure vessel** subjected to internal combustion gas pressure ($p_g$) and external cooling-jacket water pressure ($p_w$), with closed ends (head and crankcase).

In KTU 2024 terminology, the cylinder is treated as a **finite-length thick cylinder** because:
- The wall thickness ($t$) is comparable to the bore diameter ($D$).
- It experiences **both internal pressure (combustion gases)** and **external pressure (cooling water)** simultaneously.
- The ends are integral with the head and crankcase — hence not free to expand radially.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pressure Cooker with an Agitator" Analogy:**
> Imagine a heavy-duty pressure cooker (the cylinder) clamped from top (head) and bottom (crankcase). Inside, an agitator rod (piston) violently pushes down, creating pressure of ~60–80 bar — that's your cylinder during combustion. Around it, cold water continuously flows to keep it from melting. The cylinder wall must therefore resist being **pushed outward (internal pressure)** and **crushed inward (cooling water pressure)** simultaneously — while also sliding against the piston thousands of times per minute.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range (IC Engine) |
|---|---|
| Combustion Pressure ($p_g$) | **40 – 80 bar** (Gasoline), **60 – 100 bar** (Diesel) |
| Cooling Water Pressure ($p_w$) | **1 – 3 bar** |
| Bore Diameter ($D$) | **60 – 150 mm** (passenger), up to **600 mm** (marine) |
| Wall Thickness ($t$) | **6 – 25 mm** |
| Liner Thickness | **2 – 8 mm** |
| Operating Temperature (inner wall) | **200 – 300 °C** |
| Linear Thermal Gradient | **60 – 120 °C** across wall |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Concept Breakdown

The design of a cylinder involves understanding **three coupled load systems**:

- **Mechanical Loads:** Combustion gas pressure ($p_g$) pushes radially outward → produces **circumferential (hoop) tensile stress** and **longitudinal (axial) tensile stress**.
- **Thermal Loads:** Inner wall is hot ($\approx 250°\text{C}$), outer wall is cooled ($\approx 90°\text{C}$) → produces **compressive thermal stress** that *partially counteracts* tensile mechanical stress.
- **Friction Loads:** Sliding contact with piston rings → produces **wear** at the bore surface.

**Why is this important for design?**
- Cylinder failure = catastrophic engine seizure.
- We need to ensure the wall doesn't burst (mechanical), doesn't buckle (thermal/mechanical), and doesn't wear out prematurely (tribological).
- Hence the design checks **stress, deformation, temperature, and wear simultaneously**.

### 2.2 Cylinder Construction Philosophies

1. **Monoblock Cylinder:** Cylinder is integral with the block. *Cheaper, stiffer, but heavier.* Common in small 2/3-wheelers.
2. **Dry Liner:** A thin sleeve is press-fitted into the block. Does NOT contact coolant. *Lower heat transfer, but easier replacement.*
3. **Wet Liner:** Sleeve contacts coolant directly. *Better cooling, replaceable, but needs O-ring sealing.*

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Dry liner — no direct contact with coolant; Wet liner — surrounded by coolant on outer surface." This single line is worth 3 marks.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Validity / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_{g,\text{net}} = p_g - p_w$ | Net effective pressure across wall | Wet liner / water-cooled cylinder |
| 2 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2t}$ | Thin cylinder hoop stress | $t < D/20$ |
| 3 | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4t}$ | Thin cylinder longitudinal stress | $t < D/20$ |
| 4 | $\sigma_r = -p$ (inner), $\sigma_r = 0$ (outer) | Radial stress (Lamé solution) | Thick cylinder |
| 5 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{p \cdot a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \dfrac{b^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Tangential stress (Lamé, external pressure) | Thick cylinder under external $p$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{-p \cdot a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{b^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Radial stress (Lamé, external pressure) | Thick cylinder under external $p$ |
| 7 | $\Delta T \cdot \alpha \cdot E$ | Free thermal expansion strain | Temperature gradient |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{2(1-\nu)}$ | Constrained thermal stress | $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 9 | $p_w = \dfrac{\sigma_t \cdot (b^2 - a^2)}{a^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{b^2}{a^2}\right)}$ | Maximum allowable external (water) pressure | Buckling check |
| 10 | $t = \dfrac{D}{2}\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_t - 2p}} - 1\right)$ | Required wall thickness (thick cyl.) | Cylinder / Liner design |
| 11 | $t = \dfrac{D \cdot p}{2\sigma_t}$ | Wall thickness (thin cylinder) | $D/t > 20$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** The allowable tensile stress $\sigma_t$ for **cast iron** = **30 – 40 N/mm²**; for **alloy cast iron** = **50 N/mm²**; for **aluminum alloys** = **60 – 80 N/mm²**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Passenger Vehicles:** Monoblock aluminum block with thin cast-iron dry liners.
- **Heavy-Duty Diesels:** Wet liners in nodular cast iron for easy service.
- **Motorcycles:** Nikasil-plated aluminum cylinders (no separate liner).
- **Racing Engines:** Steel cylinder sleeves for maximum wear resistance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Wall Thickness of a Cylinder Liner (Thick Cylinder Form)

**Problem Setup:** A dry cylinder liner is subjected to gas pressure $p_g$ on the inside. Outer surface is in contact with the cylinder block (zero external pressure). Find the thickness $t$ given bore $D$ and allowable stress $\sigma_t$.

**Lamé's Equations for thick cylinder (internal pressure only):**

Tangential stress at the inner radius $r = a$:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p \cdot b^2}}{b^2 - a^2} \left( 1 + \frac{b^2}{a^2} \right)
$$

**Substitute** $b = a + t$, $a = D/2$, and solve for $t$:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p \cdot (a + t)^2}{(a + t)^2 - a^2} \left(1 + \frac{(a + t)^2}{a^2}\right)
$$

**Simplify** the term $\dfrac{(a + t)^2}{(a + t)^2 - a^2}$:

$$
\frac{(a + t)^2}{(a + t)^2 - a^2} = \frac{(a + t)^2}{(a + t - a)(a + t + a)} = \frac{(a + t)^2}{t(2a + t)}
$$

Now set the inner tangential stress equal to the allowable stress $\sigma_t$ and solve for $t$:

$$
\sigma_t = p \cdot \frac{(a + t)^2}{t(2a + t)} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{(a + t)^2}{a^2}\right)
$$

For a **first engineering approximation** (commonly used in KTU textbooks — Khurmi/Gupta style), assuming thin cylinder regime ($D/t > 20$), the simpler form is used:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p \cdot D}{2t}
$$

Therefore, the **required thickness is**:

$$
\boxed{\; t \;=\; \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \, \sigma_t} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Comment:** Khurmi recommends using $p$ = **maximum gas pressure** (not mean effective pressure) for conservative design. This ensures the cylinder never bursts even under detonation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Effective Pressure for Wet Liner

When the cylinder liner is a **wet liner**, the outer surface is subjected to cooling-water pressure $p_w$. The wall must therefore resist the **algebraic difference** of pressures.

Force balance on a small cylindrical element of radius $r$ and unit length, considering both inner pressure $p_g$ (acting outward) and outer pressure $p_w$ (acting inward):

$$
\sigma_t \cdot t = (p_g - p_w) \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\; t \;=\; \frac{(p_g - p_w) \cdot D}{2 \, \sigma_t} \;}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical External Pressure (Collapse) Check

A wet liner can **collapse inward** if water pressure exceeds the critical buckling pressure. Lamé's solution for tangential stress under **external pressure only**:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p_w \cdot a^2}{b^2 - a^2} \left(1 + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)
$$

At collapse, $\sigma_t = \sigma_c$ (compressive allowable). Solving for maximum allowable $p_w$:

$$
p_{w,\max} = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot (b^2 - a^2)}{a^2 \left(1 + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)}
$$

Substituting $b = a + t$:

$$
p_{w,\max} = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot t \cdot (2a + t)}{(a + t)^2}
$$

For typical liner ($t \ll a$), this simplifies to:

$$
p_{w,\max} \approx \frac{2 \, \sigma_c \, t}{a} = \frac{4 \, \sigma_c \, t}{D}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermal Stress in Cylinder Wall

The temperature difference between inner and outer wall causes constrained thermal expansion. Strain compatibility:

Total strain = Mechanical strain + Thermal strain = Constrained (zero net expansion):

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mech}} + \varepsilon_{\text{th}} = 0
$$

Substituting Hooke's law $\varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = \sigma / E$ and $\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$:

$$
\frac{\sigma_{\text{th}}}{E} + \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 0
$$

Including the biaxial plane-strain correction $(1 - \nu)$:

$$
\boxed{\; \sigma_{\text{th}} \;=\; \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{2(1 - \nu)} \;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention:** Thermal stress is **compressive** at the hot inner wall (it wants to expand but is restrained). This *partially cancels* the tensile gas-pressure hoop stress — a beneficial effect.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Dry Liner | Wet Liner | Monoblock |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling efficiency | Moderate | **Highest** | Moderate |
| Replacement ease | Difficult | **Easy** | Not replaceable |
| Sealing complexity | Low | **High (O-rings)** | None |
| Weight | Medium | High | **Lowest** |
| Cost | Medium | Highest | **Lowest** |
| Heat dissipation path | Block → Air | **Direct to coolant** | Block → Coolant |
| Thermal gradient | Large | **Small** | Large |
| Typical application | Petrol engines | **Diesel engines** | Small 2W/4W |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
