## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a dry liner and a wet liner in an IC engine.**

**Model Answer:**
- A **dry liner** is a thin sleeve press-fitted into the cylinder block such that its outer surface does **not directly contact** the cooling water. Heat transfer occurs through the block material.
- A **wet liner** is a cylinder sleeve whose outer surface is in **direct contact** with the cooling water. It requires O-ring sealing at the top and bottom flanges to prevent coolant leakage.
- Wet liners provide **better cooling** and are **replaceable**, but are more expensive and prone to cavitation damage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by the "net effective pressure" acting on a wet cylinder liner?**

**Model Answer:**
The **net effective pressure** is the algebraic difference between the internal combustion gas pressure and the external cooling-water pressure acting on the liner wall:

$$
p_{\text{net}} = p_g - p_w
$$

This is the pressure used in design calculations for wet liner thickness. Typical values: $p_g = 40$ bar, $p_w = 1.5$ bar, so $p_{\text{net}} \approx 38.5$ bar. It represents the **actual pressure trying to burst the wall outward**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**A four-cylinder petrol engine has the following data:**
- Bore, $D = 80$ mm
- Maximum gas pressure, $p_g = 4.2$ N/mm²
- Allowable tensile stress for cylinder material, $\sigma_t = 35$ N/mm²
- The cylinder is **water-cooled with a wet liner**; water pressure $p_w = 0.15$ N/mm²

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **required thickness of the cylinder liner** assuming it behaves as a thin cylinder.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the liner is **3 mm thick**, calculate the **maximum allowable water pressure** to avoid collapse, using the Lamé equation for thick cylinder under external pressure. Take $\sigma_c = 60$ N/mm² (compressive allowable).

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Required Liner Thickness

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $p_g = 4.2$ N/mm², $\sigma_t = 35$ N/mm², $p_w = 0.15$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Compute net effective pressure:**

$$
p_{\text{net}} = p_g - p_w = 4.2 - 0.15 = 4.05 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply thin cylinder hoop stress formula:**

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p_{\text{net}} \cdot D}{2t}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$:**

$$
t = \frac{p_{\text{net}} \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_t} = \frac{4.05 \times 80}{2 \times 35}
$$

$$
t = \frac{324}{70} = 4.63 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Answer (a):** Required liner thickness $t \approx \mathbf{4.63 \text{ mm}}$.

**Verification of thin-cylinder assumption:** $D/t = 80/4.63 = 17.3$ → Just outside thin-cylinder regime (>20 required). For higher accuracy, Lamé equations should be used.

---

#### Part (b) — Maximum Allowable Water Pressure

**Given:** $t = 3$ mm, $a = D/2 = 40$ mm, $b = a + t = 43$ mm, $\sigma_c = 60$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Lamé tangential stress at inner radius $r = a$ under external pressure $p_w$:**

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p_w \cdot a^2}{b^2 - a^2} \left(1 + \frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p_w \cdot (40)^2}{(43)^2 - (40)^2} \left(1 + \frac{(43)^2}{(40)^2}\right)
$$

Compute $(43)^2 - (40)^2 = 1849 - 1600 = 249$ mm²:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p_w \cdot 1600}{249} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1849}{1600}\right)
$$

Compute the bracket: $1 + 1849/1600 = 1 + 1.1556 = 2.1556$:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{p_w \cdot 1600}{249} \cdot 2.1556 = p_w \cdot \frac{3449}{249} = p_w \cdot 13.85
$$

**Step 3 — Equate to allowable compressive stress and solve:**

$$
p_w \cdot 13.85 = 60
$$

$$
\boxed{\; p_{w,\max} = \frac{60}{13.85} \approx 4.33 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;}
$$

> **Answer (b):** Maximum allowable water pressure ≈ **4.33 N/mm²** (= 43.3 bar). This is far above normal cooling system pressure (~1.5 bar), so collapse is not a concern in operation.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **thermal stresses developed in a cylinder wall** due to temperature gradient. Derive the expression for thermal stress in terms of temperature difference $\Delta T$, modulus $E$, coefficient of expansion $\alpha$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylinder wall has $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\alpha = 1.1 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C, $\nu = 0.25$, and $\Delta T = 180$ °C (inner hotter). Calculate the **thermal hoop stress**. If the mechanical hoop stress due to gas pressure is 25 N/mm² (tensile), find the **effective stress**.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Thermal Stress

**Concept:** The inner surface of the cylinder is at high temperature ($T_i \approx 250°\text{C}$) and the outer surface is cooled ($T_o \approx 90°\text{C}$). This produces a temperature gradient $\Delta T = T_i - T_o$ across the wall thickness.

**If the cylinder were free to expand**, the inner layer would expand more than the outer:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{free}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**However**, the layers are bonded — so the inner is **restrained** from expanding fully, and the outer is stretched to match. This creates:
- **Compressive** stress at the hot inner surface (wants to expand, cannot).
- **Tensile** stress at the cold outer surface (forced to expand).

**Compatibility condition (zero net strain in plane):**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mechanical}} + \varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = 0
$$

**Substituting** Hooke's law for biaxial plane strain:

$$
\frac{\sigma_{\text{th}}}{E} + \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 0
$$

For plane strain (radial stress zero), include Poisson's ratio:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{th}}}{E} - \nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_{\text{th}}}{E} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{th}}(1 - \nu)}{E}
$$

Setting equal to negative thermal strain:

$$
\sigma_{\text{th}}(1 - \nu) = -E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Final expression (magnitude):**

$$
\boxed{\; \sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU textbooks (Khurmi) use the form $\sigma_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{E \alpha \Delta T}{2(1 - \nu)}$ for **fully constrained** thick cylinder (both ends clamped). Use the form consistent with your course module.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\alpha = 1.1 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C, $\nu = 0.25$, $\Delta T = 180$ °C

**Step 1 — Compute thermal stress:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 180}{1 - 0.25}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5 \times 1.98 \times 10^{-3}}{0.75}
$$

Compute numerator: $1.2 \times 10^5 \times 1.98 \times 10^{-3} = 237.6$ N/mm²:

$$
\sigma_{\text{th}} = \frac{237.6}{0.75} = 316.8 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Effective stress (thermal is compressive, mechanical is tensile):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{effective}} = \sigma_{\text{mech}} - \sigma_{\text{th}} = 25 - 316.8 = -291.8 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
The effective stress is **compressive** with magnitude ≈ **291.8 N/mm²**. This is *beneficial* — it counteracts tensile stress and reduces the net bursting tendency.

> **Answer (b):** Thermal stress = **316.8 N/mm²** (compressive); Effective stress = **−291.8 N/mm²** (net compressive).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error:** Forgetting that thermal stress is **compressive at the hot (inner) wall** — losing 2 marks in 7-mark problems.
> 2. **Mixing Wet/Dry formulas:** Using thin-cylinder formula for wet liners without subtracting $p_w$ — common mistake.
> 3. **Using $p_{\text{meP}}$ instead of $p_{\max}$:** Design must use **maximum (peak) gas pressure**, not brake mean effective pressure (BMEP).
> 4. **Units Inconsistency:** Converting bar → N/mm² incorrectly. Remember: **1 bar = 0.1 N/mm²**.
> 5. **Thin Cylinder Check:** Always verify $D/t > 20$ before using the simple formula. If $D/t < 20$, you **must** use Lamé equations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- **Three loads on cylinder:** Mechanical (gas pressure), Thermal (temperature gradient), Tribological (piston ring wear).
- **Wet liner** = surrounded by coolant; **Dry liner** = press-fitted, no coolant contact.
- **Net effective pressure:** $p_{\text{net}} = p_g - p_w$ (used for wet liner thickness).
- **Thin cylinder thickness:** $t = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \sigma_t}$ — valid only when $D/t > 20$.
- **Thick cylinder (Lamé — external pressure):**
  - $\sigma_t = \dfrac{p \cdot a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \dfrac{b^2}{r^2}\right)$
  - $\sigma_r = \dfrac{-p \cdot a^2}{b^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{b^2}{r^2}\right)$
- **Thermal stress (magnitude):** $\sigma_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{E \alpha \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$ (Khurmi form uses $2(1-\nu)$ for fully constrained).
- **Thermal stress is compressive** at inner wall → reduces effective tensile stress.
- **Standard allowables:** Cast iron 30–40 N/mm²; Alloy cast iron 50 N/mm²; Aluminum alloy 60–80 N/mm².
- **Conversion:** 1 bar = 0.1 N/mm² = 100 kPa.
- **Use $p_{\max}$ (peak combustion pressure) for cylinder design** — NOT BMEP or IMEP.
- **Cylinder materials:** Gray cast iron (most common), Alloy cast iron, Aluminum alloys with Ni-SiC plating.
- **Failure modes:** Burst (mechanical), Collapse (external water pressure), Wear (tribological), Thermal fatigue (cyclic heating).
- **Cooling passages:** Designed for heat flux ~ 0.3–0.5 MW/m² in diesel engines.
- **Crown thickness:** Head design typically uses 6–8% of bore as a starting estimate.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connecting Rod
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Connecting Rod

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Connecting Rod** is a critical reciprocating engine component that serves as the mechanical linkage between the **piston** (via the piston pin at the small end) and the **crankshaft** (via the crankpin at the big end). Its primary function is to transmit the reciprocating linear motion of the piston into the rotational motion of the crankshaft while withstanding enormous, dynamically fluctuating forces.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the connecting rod is classified as a **dynamically loaded, slender, two-force member** that must be designed against three principal failure modes:
- **Compressive Buckling** (column instability)
- **Fatigue Failure** (alternating tensile-compressive stresses)
- **Bearing Failure at Big End & Small End**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight**: The connecting rod is the ONLY component in the engine that experiences both **tension (during the intake/exhaust stroke)** and **compression (during the power/compression stroke)** in every working cycle. This bi-directional loading makes it the textbook example for fatigue analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a cyclist pedaling hard against the pedal. The **thigh-bone (femur)** of the cyclist behaves exactly like a connecting rod:

- The **knee joint** (where femur meets shin) = **Small End of the connecting rod** (where it joins the piston pin).
- The **hip joint** (where femur meets pelvis) = **Big End of the connecting rod** (where it wraps around the crankpin).
- The **cyclist's torso pushing down** = **Combustion Pressure pushing the piston down**.
- The **femur is slender yet strong** because it is hollow — like the **I-section of the connecting rod**, which is strong in bending but lightweight.

Just like a cyclist's femur can **bend or buckle sideways** if pushed too hard, the connecting rod must be designed so it does not buckle under peak cylinder pressure or snap due to repeated stress reversals.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

| Metric | Typical Value (Petrol Engine) | Typical Value (Diesel Engine) |
|--------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Peak Cylinder Pressure | **2.5 MPa – 4.0 MPa** | **5.0 MPa – 10.0 MPa** |
| Rod Length-to-Bore Ratio (L/D) | **1.5 – 2.2** | **1.4 – 1.8** |
| Standard Materials | EN-18, EN-24, SAE-4140 | EN-24, SAE-4340, Titanium Alloys |
| Fatigue Safety Factor | **2.0 – 3.0** | **2.5 – 4.0** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Design Rule (Constant for KTU Problems)**: The **section modulus** of the rod must be **constant along its length** to maintain uniform stress distribution. The I-section achieves this with **minimum material (minimum weight)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The connecting rod operates under three distinct mechanical actions during every engine cycle:

### Step 1: Force Transmission (Two-Force Member Concept)
- The rod is treated as a **straight, prismatic, two-force member**.
- All forces act only at the **small end (piston pin)** and the **big end (crankpin)**.
- The internal force along the rod axis alternates between **tensile (pull)** and **compressive (push)**.

### Step 2: Compressive Buckling Assessment
- During the power stroke, the rod is compressed by peak gas pressure.
- Since L/k ratio is significant (typically **40–100**), Euler's pure elastic buckling formula becomes invalid. We use **Rankine's Empirical Formula** which combines yield and elastic buckling behavior.

### Step 3: Fatigue (Endurance) Assessment
- Because stress reverses every cycle, the rod is designed against **fluctuating stresses**.
- The standard KTU approach uses the **Soderberg Criterion** or the **Modified Goodman Diagram** with the maximum stress theory.

### Step 4: Big End Cap Design (C-Frame Analysis)
- The big end is split to allow assembly around the crankpin.
- The two **big-end cap bolts** must be designed for both **clamping load** and **fatigue**.
- The C-frame region experiences **bending** due to the offset between the cap bolts and the crankpin centerline.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Connecting Rod Design

| S.No. | Formula | Application | Units |
|:-----:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $F = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot p_{max}$ | Maximum force on piston (where $D$ = bore, $p_{max}$ = peak pressure) | N |
| 2 | $P_c = \dfrac{f_c \cdot A}{1 + a\left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2}$ | **Rankine's Critical Load** (for column buckling) | N |
| 3 | $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot A}{\left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2}$ | **Euler's Critical Load** (slender columns only) | N |
| 4 | $I = \dfrac{B H^3}{12} - \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ | Moment of Inertia of **I-section** | m⁴ |
| 5 | $A = B H - b h$ | Cross-sectional Area of I-section | m² |
| 6 | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Least Radius of Gyration | m |
| 7 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{2I}{H}$ | Section Modulus (neutral axis at mid-height) | m³ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{B}{H} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7}{33}}$ | Optimum I-section proportion (minimum area for given $I$) | — |
| 9 | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{P_c}{A}$ | Direct compressive stress | Pa |
| 10 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{Z}$ | Bending stress due to buckling | Pa |
| 11 | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_d + \sigma_b$ | Maximum combined compressive stress | Pa |
| 12 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F_t}{A}$ | Maximum tensile stress (inertia force component) | Pa |
| 13 | $\dfrac{1}{F.S.} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_{ut}} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_e}$ | **Soderberg Fatigue Criterion** | — |
| 14 | $P_b = 0.78 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot A_t$ | Proof load for big-end cap bolts | N |
| 15 | $d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot P_b}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}}$ | Core diameter of big-end cap bolt | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rankine's Constant 'a' (Standard KTU Values)**:
> - For **mild steel**: $a = \dfrac{1}{7500}$
> - For **cast iron**: $a = \dfrac{1}{1600}$
> - For **wrought iron**: $a = \dfrac{1}{9000}$
> - For **EN-series alloy steel**: $a = \dfrac{1}{7500}$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles used in connecting rod design are directly applied in:
- **Aerospace push-pull tubes** (control rods, landing gear struts)
- **Hydraulic press columns**
- **Civil structural columns** (tall building frames)
- **Offshore drilling derricks**

The I-section with constant section modulus is the same principle used in **railway rail design, I-beams in buildings, and aircraft wing spars**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum I-Section Proportion

The connecting rod's **I-section** must have minimum area for a given moment of inertia (mass optimization). The two web thicknesses are assumed equal: $b = B - 2t$, and total height is $H$.

**Step 1**: Express the area:
$$A = B H - b h$$

**Step 2**: Express moment of inertia:
$$I = \dfrac{B H^3}{12} - \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$$

**Step 3**: To minimize $A$ for a given $I$, we set $\dfrac{dA}{dB} = 0$ while keeping $I$ constant (Lagrange multiplier / calculus minimization).

**Step 4**: Using the relation between web and flange thickness ($B \approx 0.6H$, $b \approx 0.4H$), and after substitution, we obtain:
$$\dfrac{B}{H} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7}{33}}$$

**Step 5**: Numerically:
$$\dfrac{B}{H} = 0.4606 \approx 0.46$$

For practical design, KTU uses:
$$\dfrac{B}{H} = 0.5 \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{b}{h} = 0.4$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation appears regularly in KTU Board exams as a **7-mark sub-question** in Part B.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combined Stress Expression for Buckling Check

The connecting rod, when treated as a column with eccentricity or lateral loading, must satisfy both compressive stress and bending stress criteria simultaneously.

**Step 1**: The direct compressive stress using Rankine's formula:
$$\sigma_d = \dfrac{P_c}{A} = \dfrac{f_c}{1 + a\left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2}$$

**Step 2**: The bending moment that tends to buckle the rod is:
$$M = P \cdot L$$

**Step 3**: The bending stress at the extreme fiber:
$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{Z}$$

**Step 4**: For maximum compressive stress in the rod:
$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_d + \sigma_b$$

**Step 5**: For the rod to be safe under buckling:
$$\sigma_{max} \leq \dfrac{f_c}{\text{Factor of Safety}}$$

This forms the **Master Equation** that KTU examiners expect you to write before any substitution.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Big End Bolt Design (Preloaded Bolt)

The big end cap is held by **two bolts** (typically) that must clamp the cap against the rod body.

**Step 1**: Total separating force on the cap due to gas pressure acting on the projected area:
$$F_{sep} = p_{max} \cdot A_{projected}$$

**Step 2**: Load per bolt (for $n$ bolts):
$$P_{bolt} = \dfrac{F_{sep}}{n}$$

**Step 3**: Using a tightening factor of 1.5 (to maintain clamping under vibration):
$$P_{clamp} = 1.5 \cdot P_{bolt}$$

**Step 4**: Proof load capacity of bolt (using yield):
$$P_b = 0.78 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot A_t$$

**Step 5**: Equating proof load to clamp load for safety:
$$0.78 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot A_t \geq 1.5 \cdot P_{bolt}$$

**Step 6**: Solving for core cross-sectional area:
$$A_t = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot P_{bolt}}{0.78 \cdot \sigma_y}$$

**Step 7**: Core diameter of the bolt:
$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot A_t}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot P_{bolt}}{0.78 \cdot \pi \cdot \sigma_y}}$$

## 3.4 Master Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem Statement**: Design a connecting rod for a single-cylinder petrol engine with the following data:
- Cylinder bore, $D = 80$ mm
- Stroke length, $L_s = 110$ mm
- Length of connecting rod, $L = 250$ mm
- Peak pressure, $p_{max} = 3.5$ MPa
- Material: EN-24 steel, $\sigma_y = 660$ MPa, $\sigma_{ut} = 850$ MPa, $E = 210$ GPa
- Rankine's constant, $a = \dfrac{1}{7500}$
- Factor of safety = 5

**Step 1 — Maximum Force on Piston**:
$$F = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot p_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 \cdot (3.5 \times 10^6)$$

$$F = 0.7854 \times 0.0064 \times 3.5 \times 10^6$$

$$F = 17{,}592 \text{ N} \approx 17.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Section Proportions**:
Assume optimum I-section: $H = 4t$ and $B = 6t$ (where $t$ is web/flange thickness).

$$I = \dfrac{B H^3}{12} - \dfrac{b h^3}{12} = \dfrac{6t (4t)^3}{12} - \dfrac{4t (2t)^3}{12}$$

$$I = \dfrac{6t \cdot 64t^3}{12} - \dfrac{4t \cdot 8t^3}{12} = 32t^4 - 2.667t^4 = 29.33 t^4$$

$$A = 6t \cdot 4t - 4t \cdot 2t = 24t^2 - 8t^2 = 16 t^2$$

$$k^2 = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{29.33 t^4}{16 t^2} = 1.833 t^2$$

$$k = 1.354 \cdot t$$

$$Z = \dfrac{2I}{H} = \dfrac{2 \times 29.33 t^4}{4t} = 14.667 t^3$$

**Step 3 — Rankine's Critical Load Equation**:
$$P_c = \dfrac{f_c \cdot A}{1 + a\left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2} = \dfrac{f_c \cdot 16 t^2}{1 + \dfrac{1}{7500} \left(\dfrac{0.250}{1.354 t}\right)^2}$$

$$\dfrac{L}{k} = \dfrac{0.250}{1.354 t} = \dfrac{0.1846}{t}$$

$$\left(\dfrac{L}{k}\right)^2 = \dfrac{0.03407}{t^2}$$

$$P_c = \dfrac{660 \times 10^6 \cdot 16 t^2}{1 + \dfrac{0.03407}{7500 \cdot t^2}} = \dfrac{1.056 \times 10^{10} \cdot t^2}{1 + 4.543 \times 10^{-6}/t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Safety Factor (Load on rod = F)**:
The rod must support $F \times F.S. = 17{,}592 \times 5 = 87{,}960$ N

$$P_c \geq 87{,}960 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Iterative Solution**:
Try $t = 12$ mm = 0.012 m:
$$P_c = \dfrac{1.056 \times 10^{10} \times (0.012)^2}{1 + 4.543 \times 10^{-6}/(0.012)^2}$$

Numerator: $1.056 \times 10^{10} \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5206 \times 10^6$

Denominator: $1 + 4.543 \times 10^{-6}/1.44 \times 10^{-4} = 1 + 0.0316 = 1.0316$

$$P_c = \dfrac{1.5206 \times 10^6}{1.0316} = 1.474 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1474 \text{ kN}$$

This is well above 87.96 kN. **Check bending stress**:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{Z} = \dfrac{17{,}592 \times 0.250}{14.667 \times (0.012)^3}$$

$$Z = 14.667 \times 1.728 \times 10^{-6} = 2.535 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{4398}{2.535 \times 10^{-5}} = 173.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_d = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{17{,}592}{16 \times (0.012)^2} = \dfrac{17{,}592}{2.304 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.64 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 173.5 + 7.64 = 181.1 \text{ MPa}$$

Compare to allowable: $\dfrac{f_c}{F.S.} = \dfrac{660}{5} = 132$ MPa

**Stress exceeds allowable** → increase $t$.

Try $t = 14$ mm:
$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{4398}{14.667 \times (0.014)^3} = \dfrac{4398}{4.025 \times 10^{-5}} = 109.3 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_d = \dfrac{17{,}592}{16 \times (0.014)^2} = \dfrac{17{,}592}{3.136 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.61 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{max} = 109.3 + 5.61 = 114.9 \text{ MPa} \leq 132 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

**Final Dimensions**:
- Web/flange thickness: $t = 14$ mm
- Total depth: $H = 4t = 56$ mm
- Total width: $B = 6t = 84$ mm
- Inner cavity: $h = 2t = 28$ mm, $b = 4t = 56$ mm

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four functions of a connecting rod in an IC engine.**

**Model Answer**:
1. Transmits the reciprocating motion of the piston to the rotating crankshaft, converting linear motion to rotary motion.
2. Withstands the fluctuating gas and inertia loads without buckling or fatigue failure.
3. Maintains proper piston alignment through its small-end bearing.
4. Provides a lubrication passage from the big end to the small end for bearing lubrication.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Rankine's and Euler's column formulas. When is each used?**

**Model Answer**:
| Aspect | Rankine's Formula | Euler's Formula |
|:-------|:------------------|:----------------|
| Failure Mode | Combined yielding + buckling | Pure elastic buckling only |
| Applicable Slenderness | Short to medium columns | Long, slender columns (L/k > 100) |
| Empirical Constant | Uses constant 'a' from material tests | No empirical constants |
| Use Case | Connecting rod, machine columns | Long struts, masts |

Euler's formula is used only when the slenderness ratio exceeds the critical value (transition slenderness ratio). For connecting rods, **Rankine's formula is universally applied** since L/k is between 40–100.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A connecting rod is to be designed for a single-cylinder four-stroke petrol engine with the following data:**
- Cylinder bore: $D = 100$ mm
- Stroke length: $L_s = 125$ mm
- Length of connecting rod: $L = 300$ mm
- Maximum gas pressure: $p_{max} = 4.0$ MPa
- Material: EN-18 steel with $\sigma_y = 450$ MPa, $\sigma_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $E = 210$ GPa
- Rankine's constant $a = 1/7500$
- Factor of safety = 6

**(a) Design the I-section of the connecting rod shank. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Check the design for buckling and bending stresses. (7 Marks)**

### Question A — Model Solution:

**Part (a) Solution**:

**Step 1 — Peak Force**:
$$F = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot p_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 \times 4 \times 10^6 = 31{,}416 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — I-Section Proportions** (Assume $B = 6t$, $H = 4t$):
- Area: $A = 16 t^2$
- Moment of inertia: $I = 29.33 t^4$
- Section modulus: $Z = 14.667 t^3$
- Radius of gyration squared: $k^2 = 1.833 t^2$

**Step 3 — Rankine's Critical Load Equation**:
$$P_c = \dfrac{\sigma_y \cdot A}{1 + a (L/k)^2} = \dfrac{450 \times 10^6 \times 16 t^2}{1 + \dfrac{1}{7500} \times \dfrac{(0.300)^2}{1.833 t^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Factor of Safety**:
Required load capacity = $F \times FOS = 31{,}416 \times 6 = 188{,}496$ N

**Step 5 — Solve for thickness by iteration**:
Try $t = 15$ mm:
- $A = 16 \times (0.015)^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $(L/k)^2 = (0.300)^2 / (1.833 \times (0.015)^2) = 0.09 / 4.125 \times 10^{-4} = 218.18$
- $P_c = (450 \times 10^6 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3}) / (1 + 218.18/7500) = 1.62 \times 10^6 / 1.029 = 1.574 \times 10^6$ N
- This is far greater than 188,496 N ✓

**Step 6 — Adopt $t = 15$ mm**:
- $H = 4t = 60$ mm
- $B = 6t = 90$ mm
- $h = 2t = 30$ mm
- $b = 4t = 60$ mm

**Part (b) Solution**:

**Direct compressive stress**:
$$\sigma_d = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{31{,}416}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.73 \text{ MPa}$$

**Bending stress**:
$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{Z} = \dfrac{31{,}416 \times 0.300}{14.667 \times (0.015)^3} = \dfrac{9424.8}{4.95 \times 10^{-5}} = 190.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Maximum combined stress**:
$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_d + \sigma_b = 8.73 + 190.4 = 199.1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Allowable stress**:
$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FOS} = \dfrac{450}{6} = 75 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **199.1 MPa > 75 MPa — Design FAILS at first iteration!**

**Re-iterate with $t = 22$ mm**:
- $A = 16 \times (0.022)^2 = 7.744 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $Z = 14.667 \times (0.022)^3 = 1.561 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
- $\sigma_d = 31{,}416 / 7.744 \times 10^{-3} = 4.06$ MPa
- $\sigma_b = (31{,}416 \times 0.300) / 1.561 \times 10^{-4} = 60.4$ MPa
- $\sigma_{max} = 4.06 + 60.4 = 64.46$ MPa ≤ 75 MPa ✓

**Final Adopted Dimensions**:
- $H = 88$ mm, $B = 132$ mm
- $h = 44$ mm, $b = 88$ mm
- $t = 22$ mm

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (Internal Choice)

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the different types of connecting rods based on cross-section. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A connecting rod has a circular section big end. Design the big-end cap bolts for the following data: (7 Marks)**
- Diameter of crankpin = 80 mm
- Thickness of big-end bearing = 25 mm
- Maximum gas pressure = 3.5 MPa
- Number of bolts = 2
- Bolt material: alloy steel with $\sigma_y = 800$ MPa
- Factor of safety = 4

### Question B — Model Solution:

**Part (a) Solution**:

The connecting rod cross-sections used in automotive applications are:

**Type 1 — Rectangular Section**: Simple to manufacture, used in low-speed engines. Low strength-to-weight ratio.

**Type 2 — I-Section**: Most common for high-speed engines. Provides maximum moment of inertia for minimum weight. Optimum proportion $B/H = \sqrt{7/33}$.

**Type 3 — Circular Section**: Used in light two-wheelers and small engines. Easy to forge, but lower stiffness.

**Type 4 — Tubular Section**: Used in racing engines and aircraft engines. Highest strength-to-weight ratio using titanium alloys.

**Type 5 — Box Section**: Used in heavy-duty diesel engines. Combines high rigidity with good fatigue life.

**Part (b) Solution**:

**Step 1 — Total Separating Force on Big End Cap**:
The gas pressure acts on the projected area of the crankpin:
$$F_{sep} = p_{max} \times D_{cp} \times t_{bearing}$$
$$F_{sep} = 3.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.080 \times 0.025 = 7000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Load per Bolt** (with 2 bolts and tightening factor 1.5):
$$P_{bolt} = \dfrac{1.5 \times F_{sep}}{2} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 7000}{2} = 5250 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Allowable Shear/Yield Stress for Bolts**:
$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FOS} = \dfrac{800}{4} = 200 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Core Cross-sectional Area Required**:
Using the proof load formula: $P_b = 0.78 \times \sigma_y \times A_t$

For safe design: $P_b \geq P_{bolt}$
$$A_t = \dfrac{P_{bolt}}{0.78 \times \sigma_y} = \dfrac{5250}{0.78 \times 800 \times 10^6} = 8.41 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Core Diameter of Bolt**:
$$d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot A_t}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 8.41 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi}} = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Adopt standard core diameter: $d_c = 4$ mm (M6 bolt nominal)**

Nominal bolt diameter using standard thread series:
- For M6: $d_c = 4.019$ mm ✓
- Adopt **M6 alloy steel bolts**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5 tightening factor** in bolt load → design becomes unsafe in service.
> 2. **Using nominal instead of core diameter** in bolt strength calculations → 15–20% overestimation of strength.
> 3. **Confusing "factor of safety" application**: It applies to the yield stress, NOT to the ultimate stress.
> 4. **Ignoring the projected area principle** for big-end cap force calculation.
> 5. **Mixing up B/H ratios**: The optimum is $\sqrt{7/33}$, NOT $\sqrt{7/11}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Connecting Rod** = Two-force, dynamically loaded, slender member subjected to alternating tension and compression.
- **Three failure modes**: Buckling (compression), Fatigue (tension), Bearing wear (at small & big ends).
- **I-section** is preferred over rectangular section for **highest strength-to-weight ratio**.
- **Optimum I-section proportion**: $B/H = \sqrt{7/33} \approx 0.46$ → practically use $B = 6t$, $H = 4t$.
- **Rankine's formula** is used (not Euler's) because slenderness ratio is intermediate (40–100).
- **Rankine's constant 'a'**:
  - Mild steel: 1/7500
  - Cast iron: 1/1600
  - Wrought iron: 1/9000
- **Master Equation**: $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_d + \sigma_b = \dfrac{F}{A} + \dfrac{F \cdot L}{Z}$
- **Soderberg criterion**: $\dfrac{1}{FOS} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_{ut}} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_e}$ — used for fatigue design.
- **Big end cap** uses two bolts with a **tightening factor of 1.5** to maintain clamping under vibration.
- **Big end cap bolt diameter formula**: $d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot P_{bolt}}{\pi \cdot \sigma_y \cdot 0.78}}$
- **Section modulus constant along length** → ensures uniform stress distribution and prevents localized failure.
- **Material selection**: EN-18, EN-24, SAE-4140, SAE-4340; for racing applications, titanium alloys Ti-6Al-4V.
- **Forging direction must be parallel to load** to maximize fatigue strength.
- **Oil hole in shank** provides lubrication pathway from big end to small end.
- **Fillet radii** at small-end and big-end transitions reduce stress concentration (mandatory design feature).
- **Standard factors of safety**: 5 for static buckling, 2-4 for fatigue.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Trick for KTU Viva**:
> *"Connecting rod = I-section + Rankine + Soderberg + 1.5-tightening"* — these four keywords cover 90% of all viva and exam questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flywheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flywheel (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> A **flywheel** is a heavy, rotating mechanical energy-storage device mounted on the crankshaft of an Internal Combustion (I.C.) engine between the crankshaft and the clutch (or transmission input shaft). Its primary design function is to **store kinetic energy during the power stroke** and **release it during the idle strokes** (suction, compression, exhaust), thereby smoothing out the inherently fluctuating torque of a single-cylinder (or low-cylinder) reciprocating engine and ensuring a near-uniform rotational speed at the output shaft.

In the context of **PCMUT601 – Design of Automotive Components**, the flywheel is treated as a **mechanical energy reservoir**, where the design objective is to determine the **minimum required mass moment of inertia (I)** and the **geometric dimensions of the rim, arms, and shaft**, so that the prescribed **coefficient of fluctuation of speed ($C_s$)** is satisfied under all operating load conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology (Module 4 Highlight):**
> - **Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E$):** The difference between the maximum and minimum kinetic energy stored in the flywheel per cycle.
> - **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_s$):** The ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed.
> - **Turning Moment Diagram (TMD):** The graph of crank-shaft torque versus crank angle; the *geometric area* under the TMD is directly proportional to the energy developed per cycle.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Tower of a City"

Imagine a small town where water demand (water used by homes/factories) varies drastically throughout the day — high in the morning, low at night. A water pump supplies water at a **constant rate**, but demand is **unsteady**.

To bridge this gap, the town installs a **water tower (overhead tank)**:
- When **demand is low**, the pump fills the tower.
- When **demand is high**, the tower releases water to supplement the pump.

The flywheel works **exactly the same way** for an engine:
- The **pump = the power stroke** (provides bursts of energy).
- The **town demand = the idle strokes** (consume energy but produce none).
- The **water tower = the flywheel** (stores kinetic energy in rotation and returns it when needed).

Without the "tower," the engine would **stall** every time it tried to compress a fresh charge of air-fuel mixture.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following standard metrics are conventionally adopted in KTU board examinations and textbook problems:

- **Mass of the flywheel ($m$)**: expressed in **kg**.
- **Radius of gyration ($k$)**: expressed in **m** (typically 0.95 × mean rim radius for thin rim).
- **Mean angular speed ($\omega$)**: expressed in **rad/s**.
- **Mean coefficient of fluctuation of speed ($C_s$)**: dimensionless; typical values **0.01 to 0.05** for I.C. engines.
- **Density of cast iron ($\rho$)**: typically **$7200 \text{ kg/m}^3$**; for cast steel **$7860 \text{ kg/m}^3$**.
- **Poisson's ratio ($\nu$)** for rim material: **0.25** (cast iron), **0.30** (steel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Flywheel is Critical in Automotive Design:**
> 1. Smoothens the **angular velocity fluctuations** of the crankshaft.
> 2. Reduces **vibration and NVH** (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) in the driveline.
> 3. Provides a **mounting flange for the clutch** and acts as an **inertia mass for starting** the vehicle.
> 4. Balances the **reciprocating mass** of pistons/connecting rods to a large extent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Broken Down Step-by-Step

Let us dissect the working principle of a flywheel into six logical layers:

- **Step 1 — Energy Generation by the Crank:**
  The net force on the piston (gas force – inertia force) acts at the crankpin through the connecting rod. This produces a **crank torque (T)** which varies with the crank angle $\theta$.

- **Step 2 — The Mean Torque ($T_{mean}$):**
  For one complete working cycle (two revolutions for a 4-stroke engine), the *average* torque is:
  $$T_{mean} = \frac{\text{Net work done per cycle}}{4\pi}$$
  (since $4\pi$ rad = 2 revolutions).

- **Step 3 — Maximum and Minimum Torque ($T_{max}$ and $T_{min}$):**
  From the **Turning Moment Diagram (TMD)**, identify the **highest peak** ($T_{max}$) and the **lowest valley** ($T_{min}$).
  - **Mean torque line** is drawn horizontally on the TMD equal to $T_{mean}$.
  - Areas **above** the mean line = energy supplied *to* the flywheel.
  - Areas **below** the mean line = energy taken *from* the flywheel.

- **Step 4 — Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E$):**
  The maximum area bounded between the TMD curve and the mean torque line is the **fluctuation of energy** ($\Delta E$). It is the energy the flywheel must alternately store and release.

- **Step 5 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_s$):**
  $$C_s = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$$
  where $N_1$, $N_2$ are the maximum and minimum speeds and $N$ is the mean speed.

- **Step 6 — Required Mass Moment of Inertia ($I$):**
  Using $E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2$ and $E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2$, the **maximum fluctuation of energy** is:
  $$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$$
  Factoring this gives the **master flywheel equation**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Mandatory High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Sheet — KTU Board Examination (Module 4)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{mean} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{4\pi}$ | Mean torque for 4-stroke engine | N·m |
| 2 | $C_s = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$ | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ | Max fluctuation of energy | J |
| 4 | $\Delta E = I \cdot \omega^2 \cdot C_s$ | (valid when $C_s \ll 1$) | J |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\omega^2 \cdot C_s}$ | Required mass moment of inertia | kg·m² |
| 6 | $I = m \cdot k^2$ | $k$ = radius of gyration of flywheel rim | kg·m² |
| 7 | $\sigma_t = \rho \cdot v^2$ | Tangential (hoop) stress in rim due to centrifugal action | Pa (N/m²) |
| 8 | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{3 + \nu}{4} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2$ | Radial (compressive) stress in thin rim (Lamé equation) | Pa |
| 9 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | Peripheral velocity of rim | m/s |
| 10 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2 \cdot Z}$ | Bending stress in arm (for 6-arm wheel) | Pa |
| 11 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{W}{A \cdot \cos\alpha}$ | Tensile stress in arm | Pa |
| 12 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \sigma_t^2}$ | Maximum principal stress in arm | Pa |
| 13 | $d_s = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{16 \cdot T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau_{max}}}$ | Shaft diameter from equivalent torque | m |
| 14 | $T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T_t^2}$ | Equivalent twisting moment (combined loading) | N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Note (KTU Pitfall):**
> In Formula 8, the radial stress $\sigma_r$ exists **only** if the flywheel rim is *not* solid — i.e., it has a **flat web/disc web** joining the rim to the hub. For a solid disc flywheel, $\sigma_r = 0$.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the automotive industry, flywheel design directly impacts:

- **Passenger Car Smoothness:** Modern multi-cylinder engines use *small* flywheels (low $C_s$ allowed since firing is more uniform).
- **Heavy Commercial Vehicles & Tractors:** Single-cylinder diesel engines (tractors, gensets) need *massive* flywheels to handle the violent torque pulse from each power stroke.
- **Hybrid Vehicles (Modern Relevance):** The flywheel is being reinvented as the **Kinetic Energy Recovery System (KERS)** in Formula 1 cars — a high-rpm carbon-fiber flywheel stores braking energy and re-injects it for acceleration.
- **Engine NVH Tuning:** Engineers tune $C_s$ to balance ride smoothness against engine responsiveness — high $C_s$ = sluggish acceleration; low $C_s$ = jerky cruising.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — The Master Flywheel Equation ($\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$)

This is the single most important derivation in Module 4. Every flywheel sizing problem depends on it.

**Starting Point:**
The kinetic energy of the rotating flywheel at any angular speed $\omega$ is:
$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

**Step A — Identify Boundary States:**
During one cycle, the speed varies between:
- $\omega_1$ = maximum angular velocity (peak torque being absorbed)
- $\omega_2$ = minimum angular velocity (peak torque being released)

So:
$$E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2$$
$$E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2$$

**Step B — Subtraction (Fluctuation of Energy):**
$$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$$

**Step C — Factor Using Difference of Squares:**
$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1 + \omega_2)(\omega_1 - \omega_2)$$

**Step D — Introduce Mean Speed and $C_s$:**
Define $\omega = \dfrac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$ and $C_s = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$.

Therefore:
$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \cdot (2\omega) \cdot (\omega \cdot C_s)$$
$$\boxed{\Delta E = I \cdot \omega^2 \cdot C_s}$$

This is the **master equation** used universally in KTU problems. **Memorise it.**

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Required Mass Moment of Inertia ($I$)

Direct rearrangement of the master equation gives the **design requirement** for the flywheel:

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{\omega^2 \cdot C_s}$$

For a rim-type flywheel, the mass is concentrated near the rim, so:
$$I = m \cdot k^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{I}{k^2} = \frac{\Delta E}{\omega^2 \cdot C_s \cdot k^2}$$

If the rim is approximated as a thin circular ring of mean radius $R$:
$$I \approx m \cdot R^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{\Delta E}{\omega^2 \cdot C_s \cdot R^2}$$

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Rim Tangential (Hoop) Stress Due to Centrifugal Force

Consider an elemental arc of the rim subtending angle $d\theta$ at the centre. The centrifugal force acting radially outward on this element is:
$$dF = dM \cdot \omega^2 \cdot R = (\rho \cdot A \cdot R \cdot d\theta) \cdot \omega^2 \cdot R$$

where $\rho$ is the density of rim material and $A$ is the rim cross-sectional area.

**Step A — Total Centrifugal Force on a Half-Rim:**
For a half rim ($\pi$ rad), the two halves pull away from each other. The balancing tangential (hoop) tension $T$ in the cross-section satisfies:
$$2T = \int_0^\pi dF \cdot \sin\theta$$

(The $\sin\theta$ arises because only the radial component of $dF$ contributes to the pull at the cut section.)

**Step B — Evaluate the Integral:**
$$2T = \rho \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot \omega^2 \int_0^\pi \sin\theta \, d\theta = \rho \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot \omega^2 \cdot 2$$

So:
$$T = \rho \cdot A \cdot R^2 \cdot \omega^2$$

**Step C — Hoop (Tangential) Stress:**
$$\sigma_t = \frac{T}{A} = \rho \cdot R^2 \cdot \omega^2 = \rho \cdot v^2$$

where $v = R \cdot \omega = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ is the rim peripheral velocity.

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \rho \cdot v^2}$$

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Radial Stress in a Thin Rim (Lamé's Equation Approach)

For a **thin rim** (rim thickness $t \ll$ rim radius $R$) attached to a web:
$$\sigma_r = \frac{3 + \nu}{4} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2$$

This is derived by assuming the rim behaves as a thin rotating cylinder. For a solid disc rim, the radial stress is zero at the outer periphery and grows inward; for design simplicity in KTU problems, use the above formula when explicitly stated.

---

### 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — Stresses in Flywheel Arms

Each arm acts like a **cantilever beam** loaded by the rim weight (during rotation, the effective outward pull is $W$ per arm where $W = m_{rim} \omega^2 R / n_{arms}$).

**Bending moment at the arm root (hub end):**
$$M_b = W \cdot L$$
(where $L$ = arm length)

For an arm of rectangular cross-section $b \times h$:
- **Section modulus:** $Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6}$
- **Bending stress:** $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_b}{Z} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{b h^2 / 6} = \dfrac{6 W L}{b h^2}$

The arm is also subjected to **direct tension** due to the tangential pull from the rim:
$$\sigma_t = \frac{W}{b \cdot h \cdot \cos\alpha}$$
($\alpha$ = arm taper angle from the hub).

**Combined maximum principal stress (using principal stress formula):**
$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \sigma_t^2}}$$

This is the **critical stress checked against the allowable stress** of the rim material.

---

### 3.6 DERIVATION 6 — Flywheel Shaft Diameter

The shaft transmits the mean torque $T_{mean}$ and also experiences a **bending moment** $M_b$ due to flywheel weight (between two bearings). Using the **equivalent twisting moment** concept:
$$T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T_t^2}$$

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d_s$:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \cdot T_e}{\pi \cdot d_s^3}$$

Solving for $d_s$:
$$\boxed{d_s = \left(\frac{16 \cdot T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau_{max}}\right)^{1/3}}$$

---

### 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Design Output | Governing Formula | Key Inputs |
|---------------|-------------------|------------|
| Required $I$ | $I = \Delta E / (\omega^2 C_s)$ | $\Delta E$, $N$, $C_s$ |
| Flywheel Mass $m$ | $m = I / k^2$ | $I$, assumed $k$ |
| Rim Tangential Stress | $\sigma_t = \rho v^2$ | $\rho$, $N$, $D$ |
| Arm Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = 6WL/(bh^2)$ | $W$, $L$, $b$, $h$ |
| Arm Maximum Stress | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_b/2 + \sqrt{(\sigma_b/2)^2 + \sigma_t^2}$ | $\sigma_b$, $\sigma_t$ |
| Shaft Diameter | $d_s = \left[16 T_e / (\pi \tau_{max})\right]^{1/3}$ | $T_e$, allowable $\tau$ |

### 4.1 Block Diagram A — Design Logic Flow (From Problem Statement to Final Dimensions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given Engine Data N, P, Cs"] --> B["Compute Mean Torque Tmean = W net / 4 pi"]

    C --> D["Identify Tmax and Tmin from TMD"]
    D --> E["Draw Mean Torque Line on TMD"]
    E --> F["Compute Max Area Delta E Above Below Line"]
    F --> G["Calculate Required Inertia I = Delta E over omega squared Cs"]
    G --> H["Assume Rim Radius R and Rim Cross Section"]
    H --> I["Verify Rim Tangential Stress sigma t equals rho v squared"]
    I --> J{"Check sigma t Less Than Allowable"}
    J -- Yes --> K["Proceed to Arm Design"]
    J -- No --> H
    K --> L["Compute Centrifugal Load Per Arm W"]
    L --> M["Assume Arm Cross Section b h"]
    M --> N["Compute Bending Stress sigma b and Tensile sigma t"]
    N --> O["Compute Max Principal Stress sigma max"]
    O --> P{"sigma max Less Than Allowable"}
    P -- Yes --> Q["Compute Shaft Diameter d s from T e"]
    P -- No --> M
    Q --> R["Final Flywheel Geometry Output"]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram B — Force Flow Inside a Spinning Flywheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Crank Pin"] -- Torque T mean --> SH["Input Shaft"]
    SH -- Transmits --> FW["Flywheel Hub"]
    FW -- Centrifugal Pull --> ARMS["6 Arms Cantilever"]
    ARMS -- Distribute Load --> RIM["Outer Rim"]
    RIM -- Stores Kinetic Energy E equals half I omega squared --> RIM
    RIM -- Releases Energy During Idle Strokes --> FW
    FW -- Smooth Output --> OUT["Clutch Disc Mounting Flange"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description (For Student's Notebook Reproduction)

**Description of a Rim-Arm-Hub Flywheel Cross-Section (Textual Schematic):**

```
        _______________
       /               \
      /     [RIM]       \      ← Outer Rim (heavy, mass concentrated)
     |    ___________   |
     |   /           \  |
     |  /   [ARMS x6] \ |     ← Six arms (elliptical cross-section preferred)
     |  \             / |
     |   \___________/  |
     |        | |       |
     |     [HUB]        |     ← Central hub (bolted to crankshaft)
      \       |       /
       \______|______/
              |
         [CRANKSHAFT]        ← Mounting on tapered crankshaft flange
```

**Description of a Typical Turning Moment Diagram (TMD) for a 4-stroke Single-Cylinder Engine:**

```
Torque (N·m)
   ↑          Tmax
   |         /\
   |        /  \
   |       /    \  ____ Tmean (horizontal line)
   |      /      \/    \
   |     /      /\      \
   |    /      /  \      \
   |   /  A1  / A2 \  A3  \
   |  /______/______\______\___ → Crank angle θ
   |      Tmin
   |
   0°    90°   180°   270°   360°   540°   720°
        (one complete 4-stroke cycle = 720°)

   A1 = Energy absorbed by flywheel (above Tmean line)
   A2 = Energy released by flywheel (below Tmean line)
   Delta E = Maximum of (A1) or (A2) — the fluctuation of energy
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Board Exam:**
> When a TMD is given in a problem, always draw the **mean torque line first**. The fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$ is the **largest enclosed area** between the TMD curve and this mean line — *not* the area between $T_{max}$ and $T_{min}$.
