# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define an internal expanding shoe brake. Mention its two main classifications based on the length of the shoe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An internal expanding shoe brake is a friction brake in which one or more curved shoes carrying friction linings are forced *radially outward* against the inner cylindrical surface of a rotating drum, converting kinetic energy into heat through friction.

**Two main classifications based on shoe length:**
1. **Short shoe (block-type):** The lining subtends a small angle (≈ 30°–60°). It can be analysed as a block brake acting on the inside of a drum. Reactions are concentrated and the shoe is typically non-pivoted.
2. **Long shoe:** The lining subtends a large angle (≈ 60°–120°). It is analysed as a pivoted member with a defined anchor pin. The long shoe exhibits **self-energization** and is the basis of most automotive drum brakes.

> **Marking key:** (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for short shoe, 1 mark for long shoe with angle range.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State and explain the uniform wear hypothesis (Reye's hypothesis) used in shoe brake analysis. How does it differ from the uniform pressure assumption?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Uniform wear hypothesis (Reye, 1858):** The rate of wear of the lining at any point is proportional to the friction work done at that point. For steady-state operation, this implies the **normal pressure p is inversely proportional to the rubbing radius r**, i.e.

$$p \cdot r = \text{constant} = c$$

**Differences from uniform pressure:**

| Aspect | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure distribution | Constant *p* | p ∝ 1/r (max at edges, min at centre) |
| Realism | Conservative, for new rigid shoes | Realistic for worn-in brakes |
| Torque formula (short shoe) | $T = 2\mu p b r^{2}\theta$ | $T = 2\mu c b r \theta$ |
| When preferred | Initial design check | Final design verification |

> **Marking key:** (1 mark statement, 1 mark derivation p·r = c, 1 mark comparison table.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Read this carefully):**
> The most common marks lost in 14-mark shoe brake problems are:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert angles from degrees to radians** in the torque integral (the factor *2θ* must be in radians).
> 2. **Mixing up the two pressure assumptions** — if a problem gives you a "maximum allowable pressure p_max," it is the *uniform wear* case, not uniform pressure.
> 3. **Sign errors in the moment equation** — always take clockwise moments as positive (or consistently) and write the sign in the equation.
> 4. **Forgetting the (1 − μ tan θ) denominator** in the long-shoe formula — this is what makes self-energization visible.
> 5. **Not specifying the moment arm of F** in pivoted shoes — a partial-credit trap.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the actuating force of a **single pivoted long shoe** in an internal expanding brake, assuming **uniform pressure distribution**. Clearly state the self-energization condition.

**(b) [7 marks]** A double-shoe internal expanding brake has each shoe of width **b = 60 mm** acting on a drum of radius **r = 200 mm**. Each shoe subtends an angle of **120°** at the drum centre. The coefficient of friction is **μ = 0.30**. If the maximum allowable lining pressure is **0.8 MPa** (uniform wear), find:
- (i) the braking torque per shoe,
- (ii) the total braking torque, and
- (iii) the actuating force per shoe (assume symmetric pivoted configuration).

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The expected answer structure is:

1. **Sketch (1 mark):** Show drum, shoe, pivot, actuator, elemental arc dφ, elemental forces dN and μ dN.
2. **Elemental normal force (1 mark):** $dN = p \cdot b \cdot r \cdot d\phi$
3. **Moment equation (2 marks):** $\sum M_{\text{pivot}} = 0$ with the geometric projection of dN and μdN at angle φ.
4. **Integration (2 marks):** Carry out the integration cleanly, arriving at the final boxed expression.
5. **Self-energization condition (1 mark):** $\tan\theta > \mu$, equivalently $\mu \tan\theta < 1$ for stable (non-self-locking) operation.

**Final expression to be derived:**

$$F = \dfrac{p b r (\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: b = 0.060 m, r = 0.200 m, 2θ = 120° → θ = 60°, μ = 0.30, p_max = 0.8 × 10⁶ Pa.

Uniform wear: c = p_max · r = 0.8 × 10⁶ × 0.200 = **1.6 × 10⁵ N/m**

**(i) Torque per shoe (uniform wear):**

$$T_{\text{shoe}} = 2\mu c b r \theta \quad \text{(θ in radians)}$$

θ = 60° = π/3 rad = **1.0472 rad**

$$T_{\text{shoe}} = 2 \times 0.30 \times 1.6 \times 10^{5} \times 0.060 \times 0.200 \times 1.0472$$

$$= 0.60 \times 1.6 \times 10^{5} \times 0.0120 \times 1.0472$$

$$= 1152 \times 1.0472 \approx 1206.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Total braking torque** (two shoes, both leading for the conservative case — note: if one is leading and one trailing, the trailing shoe gives lower torque; the *minimum* total is the leading-shoe torque plus the trailing-shoe torque with **−μtanθ** in denominator):

$$T_{\text{total, both leading}} = 2 \times 1206.4 \approx 2412.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Actuating force per shoe:**

$$F = \dfrac{c b (\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}$$

- sin 60° = 0.8660
- cos 60° = 0.5000
- tan 60° = 1.7321

Numerator: $1.6 \times 10^{5} \times 0.060 \times (0.8660 - 0.30 \times 0.5000)$
$= 9600 \times (0.8660 - 0.1500) = 9600 \times 0.7160 = 6873.6$ N

Denominator: $1 - 0.30 \times 1.7321 = 1 - 0.5196 = 0.4804$

$$F = \dfrac{6873.6}{0.4804} \approx 14{,}307 \text{ N} \approx 14.31 \text{ kN}$$

> **Mark allocation for Part (b):** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **leading–trailing shoe** configuration. Show that the **torque ratio of the leading shoe to the trailing shoe is:**

$$\dfrac{T_{L}}{T_{T}} = \dfrac{1 + \mu \tan\theta}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}$$

**(b) [7 marks]** A short shoe inside a brake drum has: r = 0.125 m, b = 0.040 m, 2θ = 60°, μ = 0.35, p = 0.6 MPa (uniform pressure). Calculate:
- (i) the actuating force required,
- (ii) the braking torque, and
- (iii) the heat generated per second if the drum rotates at **1500 rpm** and braking is continuous.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Sketch (2 marks):** Show the two shoes in the same drum, with arrows indicating drum rotation direction. Label the leading shoe (friction rotates it *into* the drum — i.e., moment assists actuation) and the trailing shoe (friction rotates it *away* from the drum — moment opposes actuation).

**For the leading shoe** (assume pivoted, symmetric), the moment equilibrium about the pivot gives:

$$F \cdot a = M_{N} - M_{f} \quad \text{(friction moment OPPOSES rotation, so subtracts)}$$

Wait — for the *leading* shoe, friction tries to rotate the shoe *into* the drum, which *adds* to the pressing action, reducing the required F. The moment balance is:

$$F \cdot a + M_{f} = M_{N} \quad \text{(leading)}$$

Solving for F:

$$F_{L} = \dfrac{M_{N} - M_{f}}{a}$$

For the *trailing* shoe, friction tries to rotate it *away*, *adding* to the required force:

$$F_{T} = \dfrac{M_{N} + M_{f}}{a}$$

The braking torque is proportional to the friction moment, **T = μ · N · r = M_f / r** (in the linearized case). So:

$$\dfrac{T_{L}}{T_{T}} = \dfrac{F_{L}^{-1}}{F_{T}^{-1}} \quad \text{after inverting,}$$

and using the standard KTU result (derived from the long-shoe force expressions with the appropriate sign of μ in the denominator):

$$\dfrac{T_{L}}{T_{T}} = \dfrac{1 + \mu \tan\theta}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}$$

> **Mark allocation:** (Sketch 2, moment balance 3, final ratio 2.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: r = 0.125 m, b = 0.040 m, 2θ = 60° → θ = 30° = 0.5236 rad, μ = 0.35, p = 0.6 × 10⁶ Pa.

**(i) Actuating force (short shoe, uniform pressure):**

For a *short, non-pivoted* shoe, the actuating force equals the total normal force resolved:

$$F = N (\cos\theta - \mu\sin\theta) = p b r (2\theta)(\cos\theta - \mu\sin\theta)$$

(Alternative form: $F = p b r \cdot 2\theta (\cos\theta - \mu\sin\theta)$.)

- 2θ = 60° = 1.0472 rad
- cos 30° = 0.8660, sin 30° = 0.5000
- cos θ − μ sin θ = 0.8660 − 0.35 × 0.5000 = 0.8660 − 0.1750 = 0.6910

$$F = 0.6 \times 10^{6} \times 0.040 \times 0.125 \times 1.0472 \times 0.6910$$

$$= 3000 \times 1.0472 \times 0.6910 = 3141.6 \times 0.6910 \approx 2170.8 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Braking torque:**

$$T = 2\mu p b r^{2} \theta = 2 \times 0.35 \times 0.6 \times 10^{6} \times 0.040 \times (0.125)^{2} \times 0.5236$$

$$= 0.70 \times 0.6 \times 10^{6} \times 0.040 \times 0.015625 \times 0.5236$$

$$= 16{,}800 \times 0.015625 \times 0.5236 = 262.5 \times 0.5236 \approx 137.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Heat generated per second:**

Power dissipated = Torque × Angular velocity

ω = 2π × N / 60 = 2π × 1500 / 60 = **157.08 rad/s**

$$P = T \cdot \omega = 137.4 \times 157.08 \approx 21{,}583 \text{ W} \approx 21.58 \text{ kW}$$

> **Mark allocation for Part (b):** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks (with proper unit handling).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section before every KTU exam on this module.**

- [ ] **Two pressure assumptions** — Uniform pressure (Coulomb) and uniform wear (Reye, p·r = c). Default to uniform wear for design.
- [ ] **Short shoe, uniform pressure torque:** $T = 2\mu p b r^{2} \theta$ (θ in radians).
- [ ] **Short shoe, uniform wear torque:** $T = 2\mu c b r \theta$, with c = p_max · r.
- [ ] **Long shoe (pivoted) actuating force (symmetric):** $F = \dfrac{p b r (\sin\theta - \mu\cos\theta)}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}$.
- [ ] **Self-energization threshold:** $\mu \tan\theta < 1$ for stable operation; $\mu \tan\theta = 1$ is self-locking; $\mu \tan\theta > 1$ is unstable / self-de-energizing.
- [ ] **Leading vs Trailing shoe:** Leading has friction *assisting* the actuator (μ tanθ adds to denominator numerator favourably); trailing has friction *opposing*. Torque ratio: $T_{L}/T_{T} = (1+\mu\tan\theta)/(1-\mu\tan\theta)$.
- [ ] **Angle conversion:** Always convert degrees → radians in integrals and the formula *2θ*.
- [ ] **Typical μ values:** 0.25 – 0.40 for organic / semi-metallic linings; 0.10 – 0.15 for sintered metal; 0.50+ for ceramic (race applications).
- [ ] **Typical automotive wrap angles:** 2θ = 90° to 120° for rear drum brakes.
- [ ] **Units consistency:** Pressures in Pa (N/m²), lengths in metres, torques in N·m.
- [ ] **Heat dissipation:** $P = T \cdot \omega$ — calculate for fade and lining-wear checks.
- [ ] **Common student error:** Confusing the moment-arm factor (2r sin(θ/2) or 2r cos(θ/2)) in the pivoted long-shoe derivation.
- [ ] **Self-locking danger:** If you design a brake with μ tan θ = 0.9, a small increase in μ (e.g., from lining heat-up) can cause **brake grab** — a safety hazard.
- [ ] **Duo-servo caution:** Both-shoes-leading gives 2× the friction effect, but is unstable in reverse and incompatible with ABS; not used in front brakes.
- [ ] **Symbol glossary:** r = drum radius; b = lining width (axial); θ = half-wrap angle; 2θ = total wrap angle; p = normal pressure; c = p·r; μ = coefficient of friction; F = actuating force; T = braking torque; a = moment arm of F.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disc Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT601 - DESIGN OF AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disc Brakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Disc Brake** is a friction-based automotive braking system in which a flat, circular metallic (or composite) disc — called the **rotor** — is gripped axially on one or both faces by a pair of friction pads housed inside a **caliper assembly**. The clamping force is generated by a hydraulic, mechanical, pneumatic, or electromechanical actuator. The resulting frictional contact converts the **kinetic energy** of the moving vehicle into **thermal energy** (heat), which is then dissipated to the surrounding atmosphere.

The braking torque generated in a disc brake is governed by the fundamental tribological relation:

$$T_b = \mu \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot r_{eff}$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between the pad and rotor, $F_{clamp}$ is the axial clamping force, and $r_{eff}$ is the effective friction radius.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, the primary design focus is on **internal expanding shoe mechanisms of internal drum brakes**, but the design philosophy (uniform pressure, uniform wear, torque capacity, heat dissipation, fade) is directly analogous and extends to **disc brake pad pressure distribution analysis**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pressing a spinning bicycle wheel rim between your two palms — the harder you squeeze (greater **clamping force**), the quicker the wheel slows down. The **roughness of your palms** is the **coefficient of friction** ($\mu$), and the **distance of your palms from the hub** is the **effective friction radius** — palms placed closer to the rim produce far more braking effect than palms near the hub. A disc brake is essentially this concept, engineered with metal pads (your palms), a steel rotor (the wheel rim), and a hydraulic cylinder (your bicep) replacing human muscle.

> [!NOTE]
> - Standard rotor diameter for passenger cars: **280 mm to 355 mm**
> - Standard pad area: **30 to 80 cm² per face**
> - Standard coefficient of friction (sintered/semi-metallic pads): **0.30 to 0.45**
> - Maximum permissible disc surface temperature: **600 °C to 750 °C** (cast iron)

## 1.3 Classification of Disc Brakes

| Basis of Classification | Type | Typical Application |
|--------------------------|------|----------------------|
| Mounting | Floating (Sliding) Caliper / Fixed Caliper | Passenger cars (floating), sports cars (fixed) |
| Ventilation | Solid Disc / Vented Disc / Drilled / Slotted | Two-wheelers (solid), cars (vented) |
| Number of discs | Single Disc / Twin Disc | Bikes (single), heavy trucks (twin) |
| Actuation | Hydraulic / Mechanical / Pneumatic / Electric | Cars (hydraulic), trucks (pneumatic), EVs (electric) |
| Pad contact | Opposed Piston / Opposed Pad / Floating | Most passenger cars |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Driver Input:** The brake pedal is depressed, producing a **master cylinder pressure** ($P_m$).
2. **Hydraulic Amplification:** This pressure is transmitted (undiminished, per Pascal's Law) through brake fluid to the caliper piston(s).
3. **Clamping Force Generation:** The caliper piston(s) convert fluid pressure into an axial mechanical force:
   - $F_{clamp} = P_c \cdot A_p$ where $P_c$ is the caliper pressure and $A_p$ is the piston cross-sectional area.
4. **Friction Engagement:** The brake pads, backed by the caliper, press against the rotating rotor. The pad–rotor interface develops a tangential friction force.
5. **Energy Conversion:** Kinetic energy of the vehicle → thermal energy at the pad–rotor interface → conduction into the rotor → convection to ambient air.
6. **Self-energization check:** Unlike drum brakes with leading/trailing shoes, disc brakes exhibit **no self-energization** — $\mu$ does not amplify the clamping force. This is a key design consideration.
7. **Release:** When the pedal is released, the square-cut seal in the caliper bore retracts the piston, allowing the pad to lift off the rotor by a controlled clearance (~0.05–0.15 mm).

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why hydraulic?** Hydraulic fluid is incompressible, allowing precise force transmission without mechanical linkages.
- **Why vented rotors?** Vented rotors increase surface area (and thus convective heat transfer coefficient), reducing thermal fade.
- **Why a fixed caliper with opposed pistons?** Cancels out bending moment on the rotor, allowing a stiffer mounting and better pedal feel.
- **Why no self-energization?** It results in a more **linear, predictable, and stable** brake feel — preferred for ABS integration.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Clamping force | $F_c = P_c \cdot A_p \cdot n_p$ | N | $n_p$ = number of pistons |
| 2 | Effective friction radius | $r_{eff} = (R+r) \div 2$ | m | $R$ = outer pad radius, $r$ = inner pad radius |
| 3 | Braking torque (uniform pressure) | $T = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot r_{eff}$ | N·m | Most commonly used |
| 4 | Braking torque (uniform wear) | $T = \dfrac{\mu \cdot F_c}{2} \cdot (R+r)$ | N·m | Identical to uniform pressure result |
| 5 | Equivalent (effective) radius for design | $r_{eq} = \dfrac{2(R^3 - r^3)}{3(R^2 - r^2)}$ | m | Used in thermal/stress analysis |
| 6 | Friction work rate (heat generation) | $\dot{Q} = T \cdot \omega$ | W | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 7 | Total braking energy | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$ | J | Energy that must be dissipated |
| 8 | Disc surface temperature rise (lumped) | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m_{disc} \cdot c_p}$ | K or °C | Lumped capacitance approximation |
| 9 | Convective heat loss (rotor) | $Q_{conv} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | $h \approx 50$–$250$ W/m²K for air |
| 10 | Pad wear rate (Archard) | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{k \cdot F_c \cdot v_s}{H}$ | m³/s | $H$ = pad hardness, $k$ = wear coefficient |
| 11 | Stopping time | $t_s = \dfrac{v_1}{a}$ | s | $a$ = deceleration |
| 12 | Stopping distance | $s = \dfrac{v_1^2}{2a}$ | m | For constant deceleration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** For automotive disc brakes, the *uniform pressure* theory and *uniform wear* theory yield the **same torque expression** because the pad contact area is essentially a flat annular ring pressed uniformly by a rigid caliper. This is unlike a long external shoe where the pressure distribution varies.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger Cars:** Hydraulic floating caliper, single vented rotor per wheel.
- **Motorcycles:** Single piston floating caliper with solid or petal rotor.
- **Heavy Commercial Vehicles (HCVs):** Pneumatic actuation, often with **foundation drum brakes** but increasingly using air-actuated disc brakes for fade resistance.
- **Formula 1 / High Performance:** Carbon-carbon composite rotors, operating temperatures up to **1000 °C**, with active air ducting.
- **EVs (Regenerative + Friction blending):** Smaller friction brakes supplemented by regenerative braking, demanding precise friction material selection to avoid corrosion-induced noise.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Braking Torque by Uniform Pressure Theory

**Given:** An annular friction pad of inner radius $r$ and outer radius $R$ presses against a rotating disc with a uniformly distributed normal pressure $p$.

**Goal:** Derive the braking torque $T$ as a function of the clamping force $F_c$ and friction coefficient $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Define an elemental ring.** Consider a small ring of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$ on the pad surface.

The area of this elemental ring is:

$$dA = 2\pi x \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Compute the normal force on the ring.** With uniform pressure $p$:

$$dF_N = p \cdot dA = 2\pi p \, x \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Compute the friction force on the ring.** The tangential friction force is:

$$dF_f = \mu \cdot dF_N = 2\pi \mu p \, x \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Compute the elemental torque.** Torque is force times moment arm:

$$dT = dF_f \cdot x = 2\pi \mu p \, x^2 \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $r$ to $R$:**

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \int_{r}^{R} x^2 \, dx$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \dfrac{x^3}{3} \right]_{r}^{R} = \dfrac{2\pi \mu p}{3} \left( R^3 - r^3 \right)$$

**Step 6 — Relate $p$ to the total clamping force $F_c$.** Total normal force on the pad equals $F_c$:

$$F_c = p \cdot A_{pad} = p \cdot \pi (R^2 - r^2)$$

Solving for $p$:

$$p = \dfrac{F_c}{\pi (R^2 - r^2)}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $p$ into the torque expression:**

$$T = \dfrac{2\pi \mu}{3} \cdot \dfrac{F_c}{\pi (R^2 - r^2)} \cdot (R^3 - r^3)$$

Simplifying:

$$T = \dfrac{2 \mu F_c}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2}$$

**Step 8 — Define the equivalent (effective) radius:**

$$r_{eq} = \dfrac{2(R^3 - r^3)}{3(R^2 - r^2)}$$

Therefore, the compact form is:

$$\boxed{T = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot r_{eq}}$$

This is the **KTU standard result** for uniform pressure distribution on a disc brake pad.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Braking Torque by Uniform Wear Theory

**Premise (Uniform Wear Hypothesis):** For long-life friction systems, the *wear rate* is constant over the pad surface. Since wear is proportional to $p \cdot v = p \cdot \omega x$, the condition $p \cdot x = \text{constant}$ holds.

**Step 1 — Write the wear condition:**

$$p(x) = \dfrac{k}{x}$$

where $k$ is a constant of proportionality.

**Step 2 — Express the clamping force in terms of $k$:**

$$F_c = \int_{r}^{R} p(x) \cdot 2\pi x \, dx = 2\pi k \int_{r}^{R} dx = 2\pi k (R - r)$$

So:

$$k = \dfrac{F_c}{2\pi (R - r)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the elemental torque:**

$$dT = \mu \cdot p(x) \cdot 2\pi x \, dx \cdot x = 2\pi \mu k \, x \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $r$ to $R$:**

$$T = 2\pi \mu k \int_{r}^{R} x \, dx = 2\pi \mu k \cdot \dfrac{R^2 - r^2}{2} = \pi \mu k (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $k$:**

$$T = \pi \mu \cdot \dfrac{F_c}{2\pi (R - r)} \cdot (R^2 - r^2) = \mu F_c \cdot \dfrac{R + r}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{T = \mu F_c \cdot r_{mean}, \quad \text{where } r_{mean} = \dfrac{R + r}{2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Uniform Pressure Theory | Uniform Wear Theory |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Pressure distribution | $p = \text{const}$ | $p \cdot x = \text{const}$ |
| Maximum pressure location | Anywhere on pad | At inner radius $r$ |
| Braking torque form | $T = \mu F_c r_{eq}$ | $T = \mu F_c \cdot (R+r)/2$ |
| Typical application | New pads, cold brakes | Worn-in pads, steady-state |
| Validity for automotive discs | **Highly valid** (rigid caliper) | Valid as a check, not primary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Decision Rule:** For KTU 2024 problems, use **uniform pressure theory** for steady-state friction torque and **uniform wear theory** for predicting *pad life* and *local wear variation*.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Energy Balance and Temperature Rise

**Step 1 — Total heat generated in a stop** from initial velocity $v_1$ to $v_2$ (with vehicle mass $m$):

$$Q = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 2 — Heat absorbed by the rotor** (lumped capacitance assumption, since the rotor is metallic with high thermal conductivity):

$$Q = m_{disc} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

where $m_{disc}$ is the rotor mass and $c_p$ is specific heat (cast iron: $c_p \approx 460$ J/kg·K).

**Step 3 — Equating and solving for $\Delta T$:**

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m_{disc} \cdot c_p} = \dfrac{m(v_1^2 - v_2^2)}{2 m_{disc} c_p}$$

**Step 4 — Convection cooling check** during a stop of duration $t_s$:

$$Q_{conv} = h \cdot A_{rotor} \cdot \overline{\Delta T} \cdot t_s$$

For accurate transient analysis, the **finite element method (FEM)** or **Duplication Method** (analogy to RC circuits) is recommended.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** A 1500 kg car stopping from 100 km/h absorbs approximately **577 kJ** — enough to fully melt **1.7 kg of cast iron** if isolated. This is why rotor thermal capacity is critical.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]`

**Q: List any three advantages of a disc brake over a drum brake.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Better heat dissipation:** The exposed rotor surface allows direct convective cooling, drastically reducing **brake fade** during repeated or prolonged braking.
2. **Self-cleaning action:** The wiping action of the pads against the rotor expels dust, water, and debris, restoring friction quickly after immersion (e.g., wet brakes).
3. **No self-energization:** This produces a **linear, predictable pedal feel** and uniform pad wear, which is critical for ABS and electronic stability control integration.
4. *Bonus:* Easier inspection without wheel removal in some designs, and consistent performance regardless of direction of rotation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Cycle]`

**Q: Define the term "uniform wear" in the context of friction pad design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **uniform wear hypothesis** states that for a properly designed friction interface, the volumetric wear rate is constant over the entire contact area. Mathematically, since wear is proportional to the product of normal pressure $p$ and sliding velocity $v = \omega x$, the condition for uniform wear becomes $p \cdot x = \text{constant}$. This hypothesis is the basis for predicting long-term pad life and for designing **internal expanding shoe** and **disc pad** geometry such that the thinnest point of the pad wears out evenly across its radial extent.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the braking torque of a single disc brake assuming **uniform pressure distribution** over the pad. State all assumptions clearly.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Pressure $p$ is uniformly distributed over the entire pad face (annular area).
2. The coefficient of friction $\mu$ is constant over the pad–rotor interface.
3. The rotor is rigid and the caliper does not deflect appreciably under load.
4. The contact is perfectly flat (no coning or distortion of the pad).

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):**

Consider an elemental ring of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$. Then:

$$dA = 2\pi x \, dx$$

$$dF_N = p \cdot 2\pi x \, dx$$

$$dF_f = \mu \cdot dF_N = 2\pi \mu p \, x \, dx$$

$$dT = dF_f \cdot x = 2\pi \mu p \, x^2 \, dx$$

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \int_{r}^{R} x^2 \, dx = \dfrac{2\pi \mu p}{3} (R^3 - r^3)$$

The total clamping force is:

$$F_c = p \cdot \pi (R^2 - r^2) \Rightarrow p = \dfrac{F_c}{\pi (R^2 - r^2)}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{2 \mu F_c}{3} \cdot \dfrac{R^3 - r^3}{R^2 - r^2} = \mu F_c \cdot r_{eq}}$$

where $r_{eq} = \dfrac{2(R^3 - r^3)}{3(R^2 - r^2)}$ is the **equivalent friction radius**.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A single disc brake has an outer pad radius of **100 mm** and an inner pad radius of **50 mm**. The coefficient of friction is **0.35** and the actuating force on each pad is **2.5 kN**. Calculate (i) the average pressure on the pad, and (ii) the braking torque.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Average pressure on the pad:**

Pad area:

$$A = \pi (R^2 - r^2) = \pi (0.100^2 - 0.050^2) = \pi (0.01 - 0.0025) = \pi \times 0.0075 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A = 0.02356 \text{ m}^2 = 23562 \text{ mm}^2$$

Average pressure:

$$p = \dfrac{F_c}{A} = \dfrac{2500}{0.02356} \approx 1.061 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.1061 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 0.106 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(ii) Braking torque:**

Compute the equivalent radius:

$$r_{eq} = \dfrac{2(R^3 - r^3)}{3(R^2 - r^2)} = \dfrac{2(0.100^3 - 0.050^3)}{3(0.100^2 - 0.050^2)}$$

$$r_{eq} = \dfrac{2(0.001 - 0.000125)}{3(0.01 - 0.0025)} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.000875}{3 \times 0.0075} = \dfrac{0.00175}{0.0225}$$

$$r_{eq} \approx 0.0778 \text{ m} = 77.8 \text{ mm}$$

Braking torque:

$$T = \mu F_c r_{eq} = 0.35 \times 2500 \times 0.0778 \approx 68.07 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 68.07 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **floating (sliding) caliper disc brake** used in passenger cars. List two advantages and one disadvantage.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**

A floating caliper disc brake consists of:

- **Rotor (Disc):** A cast iron (or composite) disc bolted to the wheel hub, rotating with the wheel.
- **Caliper Assembly:** A single-piston caliper that is mounted on a **sliding pin / guide mechanism**. The caliper can slide axially (inboard-outboard) along the pins.
- **Single Piston:** Located on the inboard side of the caliper, facing the rotor.
- **Brake Pads:** Two pads — an **inboard pad** (between piston and rotor) and an **outboard pad** (on the opposite side of the rotor).
- **Dust Seal & Square-cut Seal:** Around the piston bore.

**Working:**

1. Hydraulic pressure acts on the piston, pushing the inboard pad against the rotor.
2. The reaction force tries to push the caliper body in the opposite direction, but the caliper slides on its pins.
3. The sliding caliper body pushes the outboard pad against the rotor's other face with equal force (since the system is a Class 1 lever).
4. Equal and opposite clamping forces grip the rotor, generating friction torque.

**Advantages:**
1. Lower cost and weight compared to a fixed caliper (single piston, simpler casting).
2. Self-centering effect of sliding pins; tolerates small rotor run-out.

**Disadvantage:**
1. Higher pistons force needed (compared to opposed-piston fixed caliper) for the same clamping force; also slight friction in the slide pins can affect pedal feel.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A vehicle of mass **1200 kg** is travelling at **90 km/h** and is brought to rest in **3 seconds** by the disc brakes. Assuming all four wheels contribute equally and the rotor has an effective friction radius of **0.12 m**, calculate: (i) the braking force per wheel, and (ii) the required coefficient of friction at the pad–rotor interface if the clamping force per wheel is **3 kN**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 1200$ kg
- $v_1 = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, $v_2 = 0$
- $t = 3$ s
- $r_{eff} = 0.12$ m
- $F_{c,per\,wheel} = 3000$ N

**(i) Braking force per wheel:**

Deceleration:

$$a = \dfrac{v_1 - v_2}{t} = \dfrac{25}{3} \approx 8.333 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Total braking force:

$$F_{total} = m \cdot a = 1200 \times 8.333 = 10000 \text{ N}$$

Per wheel (4 wheels):

$$F_{b,per\,wheel} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{4} = \dfrac{10000}{4}$$

$$\boxed{F_{b,per\,wheel} = 2500 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Required coefficient of friction:**

The braking torque per wheel must equal the friction force times the effective radius:

$$T = F_{b,per\,wheel} \cdot r_{eff} = 2500 \times 0.12 = 300 \text{ N·m}$$

Also, the torque generated by friction at the pad interface is:

$$T = \mu \cdot F_{c,per\,wheel} \cdot r_{eff}$$

Equating:

$$\mu = \dfrac{F_{b,per\,wheel}}{F_{c,per\,wheel}} = \dfrac{2500}{3000}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 0.833}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** A common mistake is forgetting to convert 90 km/h to m/s. The correct conversion factor is $5/18$, not $3.6$.
> 2. **Wrong sign in deceleration:** Deceleration is positive when treated as the magnitude of rate of change of velocity. Use $a = v_1 / t_s$.
> 3. **Failing to divide by 4 wheels:** The "per wheel" stipulation is critical. Students often use total force and total clamping force, which inflates the answer by a factor of 4.
> 4. **Using $r_{mean}$ instead of $r_{eff}$ in design problems:** For design *calculations* (especially thermal), use $r_{eq} = 2(R^3 - r^3) / [3(R^2 - r^2)]$. For simpler steady-state torque problems with given $r_{eff}$, use it directly.
> 5. **Mixing up uniform pressure vs. uniform wear formulas:** These yield different effective radii. Always read the problem statement carefully — *uniform pressure* gives $r_{eq} = 2(R^3-r^3)/[3(R^2-r^2)]$, while *uniform wear* gives $r_{mean} = (R+r)/2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Disc Brake = Axial clamping of a rotor by friction pads** — fundamentally different from the *radial expansion* of internal drum brake shoes.
- **Key design radii to remember:**
  - $r_{eq}$ (uniform pressure): $r_{eq} = \dfrac{2(R^3 - r^3)}{3(R^2 - r^2)}$
  - $r_{mean}$ (uniform wear): $r_{mean} = \dfrac{R + r}{2}$
- **No self-energization** in disc brakes → predictable, linear brake feel → ideal for ABS.
- **Friction coefficient $\mu$** is the *most sensitive* parameter; design uses $\mu = 0.30$–$0.45$ (semi-metallic pads).
- **Heat dissipation** dominates the design of high-performance / heavy-vehicle disc brakes; use vented or drilled rotors.
- **Fade resistance** depends on:
  1. Pad material (sintered metallic, ceramic, carbon-composite)
  2. Rotor mass and specific heat
  3. Cooling airflow
- **Energy check:** A 1500 kg car stopping from 100 km/h must dissipate $\approx$ 577 kJ.
- **Stopping distance** for a vehicle with deceleration $a$: $s = v_1^2 / (2a)$.
- **Wear law (Archard):** $\dot{V} = k F_c v_s / H$ — useful for predicting pad life.
- **Pneumatic actuation** is preferred for heavy commercial vehicles; **hydraulic** for passenger cars; **electromechanical** for EVs and brake-by-wire systems.
- **Disc material:** Gray cast iron (most common, cost-effective), carbon-ceramic (high performance, expensive), carbon-carbon (F1).
- **Standard KTU assumption:** For disc brake design, use **uniform pressure** unless the problem explicitly states "uniform wear."
- **Thermal limit:** Cast iron discs must be kept below ~750 °C to prevent phase change and thermal cracking.
- **Pedal feel** depends on caliper stiffness, fluid bulk modulus, and absence of air in brake lines (bleeding).
- **Hydraulic pressure calculation:** $F_c = P_c \cdot A_p \cdot n_p$ where $A_p$ is piston area and $n_p$ is number of pistons acting on the pad.
- **Floating caliper = 1 piston, 2 pads (equal clamp force via sliding); Fixed caliper = 2 to 6 pistons for opposed-pad clamping.**
- **Disc Brake Test (DT) and Fade Test** on dynamometers are standard KTU lab/industry protocols to validate thermal performance.
